name: BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN

topics.title: Types of Ships: Terms and Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Ships: Terms and Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Ship Types and Nomenclature

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Marine Vessel** is any watercraft designed for the purpose of transportation, defense, fishing, recreation, or specialized offshore operations on the surface or beneath the water. According to naval architecture fundamentals, ships are classified based on **Hull Form**, **Propulsion System**, **Service Function**, and **Operational Environment**. Each category is governed by classification societies (e.g., **IRS – Indian Register of Shipping**, **LR – Lloyd's Register**, **ABS – American Bureau of Shipping**) that define standard terms used universally in ship design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**  
> The course *OENST721 – Basic Principles of Marine Vessel Design* begins with a foundational understanding of *what a ship is*, *how it is classified*, and *what the standard dimensional and capacity terms mean*. These definitions form the vocabulary for every subsequent design calculation (resistance, stability, powering, etc.).

### Conceptual Analogy – The Floating City Metaphor
Imagine a **ship as a floating building block** of a city:
- The **hull** is the "foundation" that keeps it floating.
- The **deck** is the "ground floor" where life and work happen.
- The **superstructure** is the "upper floors" with cabins and the bridge.
- The **propulsion system** is the "engine room" that drives the city forward.
- The **rudder** is the "steering wheel" of this entire city.

Just like every building has a name, a function, and a blueprint, every ship has a **type**, a **purpose**, and a set of **dimensional terms** (Length, Beam, Draft, Depth) that uniquely identify it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Terms (per IMO & ISO):**  
> All measurements on a ship are given in **meters (m)** internationally, and the coordinate system follows the convention: **+X forward, +Y to port side, +Z upward** from the baseline.

## 1.2 Primary Classification of Ships

Ships can be broadly classified under the following categories:

| Classification Basis | Major Categories |
|----------------------|------------------|
| **By Purpose / Service** | Merchant ships, Naval/Military ships, Fishing vessels, Recreational/Yachts, Special-purpose vessels (research, dredgers, tugs) |
| **By Hull Form** | Displacement hulls, Planing hulls, Semi-displacement hulls, Hydrofoils, Air-cushion vehicles (Hovercraft) |
| **By Number of Hulls** | Monohull, Multihull (Catamaran, Trimaran) |
| **By Propulsion** | Motor ships (Diesel, Gas turbine, Steam), Sail-assisted, Nuclear-powered |
| **By Cargo Carried** | Liquid (Tanker), Dry bulk (Bulk carrier), Container, General cargo, Ro-Ro, LNG/LPG carrier |
| **By Operating Environment** | Inland, Coastal, Seagoing (Blue-water), Polar-class, Deep-sea |

## 1.3 Fundamental Ship Terminology

Below is a curated set of the most important terms that every KTU student **must know by heart** for the Module 1 examination:

### 1.3.1 Principal Dimensions of a Ship
- **Length Overall (LOA):** The maximum length of the ship measured parallel to the design waterline, including projections (bowsprits, rudder, etc.).
- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP or Lpp):** The length measured between the **forward perpendicular (FP)** and the **aft perpendicular (AP)**. FP is the line through the design waterline at the bow, and AP is the line through the rudder post (or centerline of rudder).
- **Beam (B):** The maximum breadth (width) of the ship measured at the **midship section** at the design waterline.
- **Draft (T):** The vertical distance from the **design waterline (DWL)** to the **keel (baseline)**.
- **Depth (D):** The vertical distance from the **keel** to the **main deck** (molded deck line) at the side, measured at midship.
- **Freeboard (Fb):** The vertical distance from the design waterline to the **uppermost continuous deck (freeboard deck)** at the side. Regulated by the **International Load Line Convention (1966)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:**  
> $$D = T + F_b$$  
> (Depth = Draft + Freeboard)

### 1.3.2 Form-Related Coefficients
These dimensionless numbers describe the "fineness" of the hull:
- **Block Coefficient (Cb):** Ratio of the volume of displacement to the volume of a rectangular block having the same LBP, B, and T.
- **Midship Section Coefficient (Cm):** Ratio of the immersed midship area to the rectangle of beam × draft.
- **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp):** Ratio of Cb to Cm — describes the fullness of the ends.
- **Waterplane Area Coefficient (Cwp):** Ratio of actual waterplane area to L × B.

### 1.3.3 Tonnage Definitions
- **Gross Tonnage (GT):** A unitless measure of the total internal volume of a ship (1 GT = 100 ft³ = 2.831 m³ of enclosed space). It is calculated per the **IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention**.
- **Net Tonnage (NT):** The portion of GT used for revenue-earning (passenger or cargo) spaces.
- **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** The total weight (in metric tonnes) a ship can carry — including cargo, fuel, freshwater, ballast, crew, and provisions. Symbol: **ΔDW**.
- **Displacement (Δ):** The actual mass of the ship and everything on it. Equal to the weight of water displaced. **1 tonne ≈ 1 m³ of seawater** (since ρ_seawater ≈ 1.025 t/m³).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:**  
> *Tonnage is NOT a unit of weight! It is a measure of volume (GT, NT) or carrying capacity (DWT). Only "displacement" is a true mass.*

### 1.3.4 Directional and Structural Terms
- **Bow** – The front of the ship.
- **Stern** – The rear of the ship.
- **Port** – Left side of the ship (when facing forward).
- **Starboard** – Right side of the ship (when facing forward).
- **Aft** – Toward the stern.
- **Forward / Fore** – Toward the bow.
- **Amidships** – The middle of the ship (around 0.5 LBP).
- **Baseline / Keel** – The lowest reference line used for all vertical measurements.
- **Centerline** – The vertical longitudinal plane passing through the bow and stern.
- **Sheer Line** – The curvature of the deck at the side (longitudinal profile).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis for Ship Classification

Ships are not classified arbitrarily. Each class emerges from a specific **functional requirement**, a **hydrodynamic principle**, or a **regulatory framework**. Understanding *why* a category exists helps the naval architect select the right hull form and powering configuration for a given mission.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Hull Form (Hydrodynamic Basis)

**A. Displacement Hulls**
- Operate continuously in the displacement mode.
- Speed limited by the **hull speed** (Fourier limit).
- The wave created by the bow is exactly cancelled by the wave from the stern at a specific Froude number.
- **Fourier Number (Fn):**  
  $$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$$
  where V is the speed in m/s, g is gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²), and L is the relevant length (often LWL – length at waterline).
- Typical **Fn** for displacement hulls: **0.20 to 0.40**.
- **Example:** Oil tankers, bulk carriers, container ships.

**B. Planing Hulls**
- Rise partially out of the water at high speed, reducing wetted area and resistance.
- Hydrodynamic lift balances the weight.
- **Fn** > 0.7 typically.
- **Example:** Speedboats, patrol craft, some fast ferries.

**C. Semi-Displacement Hulls**
- A transition between displacement and planing.
- Operate at **Fn ≈ 0.4 to 0.7**.
- **Example:** Fast patrol vessels, SWATH ships.

**D. Hydrofoils**
- Lift the hull clear of the water using underwater foils.
- Very low resistance at high speed.
- **Example:** Commercial hydrofoils, some military craft.

**E. Air-Cushion Vehicles (Hovercraft)**
- Trapped air cushion supports the vessel.
- Operates over water, ice, and even land.
- **Example:** SR.N4 hovercraft (historical), military LCAC.

## 2.2 Why Different Cargo Ships Exist (Engineering Logic)

| Ship Type | Cargo Density | Cargo Volume | Hull Form | Reason |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Bulk Carrier** | High (ore, coal) | Low | Full form, Cb ≈ 0.80 | Maximize weight capacity |
| **Container Ship** | Medium | High (stacked) | Fine form, Cb ≈ 0.65 | Reduce resistance at high service speed |
| **Tanker (Oil)** | High (liquid) | Variable | Full form, Cb ≈ 0.82 | Maximize DWT |
| **LNG Carrier** | Low (cryogenic liquid) | High | Spherical or Moss tanks | Insulation, boil-off |
| **General Cargo** | Variable | Variable | Mid-form, Cb ≈ 0.70 | Versatility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**  
> The **block coefficient Cb** is the most direct measure of "fullness" of a ship. A high Cb (≈ 0.80) means the ship is fat and slow; a low Cb (≈ 0.55) means the ship is slender and fast.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|--------|----------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | LOA | Length Overall | Max length including overhangs | m |
| 2 | LBP | Length Between Perpendiculars | FP to AP distance | m |
| 3 | LWL | Length at Waterline | Length at the design waterline | m |
| 4 | B | Beam (Moulded) | Max breadth at midship, DWL | m |
| 5 | T | Draft (Moulded) | Baseline to DWL at midship | m |
| 6 | D | Depth (Moulded) | Baseline to main deck at side | m |
| 7 | Fb | Freeboard | D − T | m |
| 8 | Cb | Block Coefficient | Δ / (LBP · B · T · ρ) | dimensionless |
| 9 | Cm | Midship Coefficient | A_m / (B · T) | dimensionless |
| 10 | Cp | Prismatic Coefficient | Cb / Cm | dimensionless |
| 11 | Cwp | Waterplane Coefficient | A_wp / (L · B) | dimensionless |
| 12 | GT | Gross Tonnage | 0.2 + 0.02 · log₁₀(V_total) per IMO 1969 | — |
| 13 | DWT | Deadweight Tonnage | Total variable load | tonnes (t) |
| 14 | Δ | Displacement | ρ · g · ∇ (Archimedes' principle) | t |
| 15 | Fn | Froude Number | V / √(g · L) | dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Valuation Note:**  
> In numerical problems, *always* mention the **definition of each symbol** and the **unit**. Marks are often deducted for missing units, even if the numerical answer is correct.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These terms are not just academic vocabulary — they are the **language of the shipping industry**:
- **Shipyards** quote a new building price based on **DWT × $/tonne**.
- **Ports** charge dues based on **GT**.
- **Classification societies** assign a "Class Notation" (e.g., "✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A") which encodes design compliance.
- **Naval architects** use **Cb, Cp, Cm** to estimate resistance and powering long before any model is tested.
- **Naval architects and marine engineers** share these terms globally — a design made in India can be built in Korea using the same dimensional language.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Key Geometric Formulas

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Block Coefficient (Cb)

The **block coefficient** is the most fundamental form coefficient. It expresses how much of the reference rectangular block (LBP × B × T) is actually occupied by the underwater volume of the ship.

**Step 1:** Define the **displacement volume** (∇) as the volume of water displaced by the hull below the waterline.

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}}$$

where:
- Δ = mass displacement (in tonnes, t)
- ρ_sw = density of seawater = **1.025 t/m³** (standard)

**Step 2:** Define the **reference rectangular block volume** as:

$$V_{block} = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3:** The block coefficient is the ratio of these two volumes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_b &= \frac{\nabla}{V_{block}} \\
C_b &= \frac{\Delta / \rho_{sw}}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
C_b &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for **Displacement (Δ):**

$$
\Delta = C_b \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Cb:**
> - Cb → 1.0: A perfectly rectangular box-shaped hull (e.g., barge).
> - Cb → 0.5: A very slender, needle-like hull (e.g., racing yacht).

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)

**Step 1:** The **prismatic coefficient** is defined as the ratio of the displacement volume to the volume of a prism whose cross-section is the immersed **midship area (A_m)** and whose length is **LBP**:

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}}
$$

**Step 2:** The **midship coefficient (Cm)** is:

$$
C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting A_m from the second equation into the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{\nabla}{(C_m \cdot B \cdot T) \cdot L_{BP}} \\
C_p &= \frac{\nabla}{C_m \cdot (B \cdot T \cdot L_{BP})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Since Cb = ∇ / (LBP · B · T), we get the famous relationship:

$$
C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is **exam-favorite** in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Froude Number (Fn)

**Step 1:** Begin with dimensional analysis. For a ship moving at speed V in water of density ρ and under gravity g, the **wave-making resistance** depends on V and L.

**Step 2:** The dimensionless group governing wave phenomena is obtained by comparing **inertial forces** with **gravitational forces**:

$$
F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}
$$

**Step 3:** Using V in m/s, g = 9.81 m/s², and L in meters, Fn is dimensionless.

**Step 4:** **Conversion to "knots"** is common. Since 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s:

$$
F_n = \frac{V_{knots} \cdot 0.5144}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot L}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Froude Number matters:**  
> The wave pattern created by a ship at a given Froude number is **geometrically similar** regardless of size. This is the foundation of **model testing** in towing tanks.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem:
A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- LBP = 200 m
- B = 32 m
- T = 14 m
- Block coefficient, Cb = 0.82
- Density of seawater, ρ = 1.025 t/m³

Find: **(a) Displacement Δ in tonnes**, **(b) Volume of displacement ∇ in m³**, **(c) Deadweight Tonnage if lightweight = 12,000 t**.

### Solution:

**Part (a) – Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= C_b \cdot \rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \\
\Delta &= 0.82 \times 1.025 \times 200 \times 32 \times 14 \\
\Delta &= 0.82 \times 1.025 \times 89,600 \\
\Delta &= 0.82 \times 91,840 \\
\Delta &= 75,308.8 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) – Volume of displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{75,308.8}{1.025} \\
\nabla &= 73,472 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) – Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= \Delta - \text{Lightweight} \\
DWT &= 75,308.8 - 12,000 \\
DWT &= 63,308.8 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check:**  
> For a Panamax-class bulk carrier, DWT ≈ 60,000 – 80,000 t. Our answer of **63,308.8 t** is in the right ballpark ✔

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – When to Use Which Form Coefficient

| Design Goal | Most Relevant Coefficient | Why |
|-------------|---------------------------|-----|
| Maximize DWT (cargo capacity) | **Cb** (high) | More volume = more displaced water = more weight |
| Minimize wave resistance at high speed | **Cp** (low) | Slender ends reduce wave-making |
| Maximize deck area (container ships) | **Cwp** (high) | Larger waterplane → larger deck footprint |
| Good stability + fine entry | **Cm** (moderate) | Trade-off between form and stability |

## 4.3 Reference Diagram – Principal Ship Dimensions (Schematic)

Below is a **text-based side profile and plan view** of a typical ship showing all principal dimensions:

```
                SIDE PROFILE VIEW                      PLAN VIEW
                                                                
                +-------------------+   Bow                    
               /                    \                           
              /    Freeboard (Fb)    \                          
             +-----------------------+                         
            /|                       |\                         
           / |                       | \         +-------------+ 
          /  |   Depth (D)           |  \        |   _________  |
         +   |                       |   +       |  |  Stern  | |
         |   |                       |   |       |  |    +    | |
         |   +-----------------------+   |       |  |  Stern  | |
         |   |                       |   |       |  +---------+ |
         |   |    Draft (T)          |   |       |  ____ _____  |
         |   |                       |   |       | |   \  /   | |
         |   |                       |   |       | |    \/    | |  <-- B (Beam)
         |   +-----------------------+   |       | |    /\    | |
         |  /                         \  |       | |___/  \___| |
         | /     LBP (Length)         \ |       |               |
         |/___________________________\|       |  LOA Length   |
         +-----+---------------+-------+       |  Overall      |
              FP          Amidships   AP      +---------------+
            Forward       Midship     Aft     Bow         Stern
            Perpendicular             Perp.  (Front)     (Rear)
```

**Legend of Key Terms:**

| Code | Term | Meaning |
|------|------|---------|
| FP | Forward Perpendicular | Vertical line at the bow where DWL meets the design profile |
| AP | Aft Perpendicular | Vertical line at the rudder post |
| LBP | Length Between Perpendiculars | Distance FP to AP |
| LOA | Length Overall | Maximum length including overhangs |
| B | Beam | Maximum width at midship |
| T | Draft | Depth of hull below waterline |
| D | Depth | Total height from keel to main deck |
| Fb | Freeboard | D − T (deck above water) |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms with a neat sketch: (i) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), (ii) Beam (B), (iii) Freeboard.**

#### Model Answer:

**(i) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):**  
The length of the ship measured along the **design waterline (DWL)** from the **forward perpendicular (FP)** to the **aft perpendicular (AP)**. FP is the line through the point where the DWL meets the **stem** (forward profile). AP is the line through the **centerline of the rudder post**, or in ships without a rudder post, the centerline of the **stern frame**.

**(ii) Beam (B):**  
The **maximum moulded breadth** of the ship measured at the **midship section** at the level of the **design waterline**. It is also called **B_mld** (moulded beam).

**(iii) Freeboard (Fb):**  
The vertical distance measured at the side of the ship from the **design waterline** to the **uppermost continuous deck (freeboard deck)**. Regulated under the **International Load Line Convention (1966)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sketch:** A simple side view of a ship showing FP, AP, and the vertical freeboard line at the side. (3 marks: 1 for each correct definition + 1 for the sketch.)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Gross Tonnage (GT) and Deadweight Tonnage (DWT). Why is DWT more relevant to a shipowner?**

#### Model Answer:

| Parameter | Gross Tonnage (GT) | Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| **Definition** | Measure of total enclosed volume of the ship | Total weight of cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew, and ballast that the ship can carry |
| **Unit** | Unitless (volume-based, 1 GT = 100 ft³ ≈ 2.831 m³) | Metric tonnes (t) |
| **Governed by** | IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention | Design draft and freeboard |
| **Use** | Port dues, canal fees, regulation | Cargo capacity, revenue calculation |

**Why DWT is more relevant to the shipowner:**  
A shipowner earns revenue from the **cargo carried**, not the total volume of the ship. DWT directly indicates how much **pay-load** the ship can transport per voyage. GT only measures the **internal volume** of all enclosed spaces (including engine rooms, crew quarters, ballast tanks), which have no direct revenue value. Therefore, **DWT determines earning potential**, while GT determines **operational costs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) – [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**A 200,000 DWT VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) has the following particulars:**
- LBP = 320 m
- B = 58 m
- T (design) = 20.5 m
- Block coefficient, Cb = 0.83
- Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³
- Lightweight = 42,000 t

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The displacement Δ and the volume of displacement ∇. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** Verify the DWT and comment on whether the ship is overloaded at the design draft. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Using the formula:
$$
\Delta = C_b \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 0.83 \times 1.025 \times 320 \times 58 \times 20.5 \\
&= 0.83 \times 1.025 \times 380,480 \\
&= 0.83 \times 389,992 \\
&= 323,693.36 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Volume of displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{323,693.36}{1.025} \\
\nabla &= 315,798.4 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b):**

Deadweight Tonnage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT &= \Delta - \text{Lightweight} \\
DWT &= 323,693.36 - 42,000 \\
DWT &= 281,693.36 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:**  
The ship is rated at **200,000 DWT** in name, but at the design draft of 20.5 m, the actual DWT is **281,693 t**. This means the vessel is **not overloaded** — it is actually carrying more than its nominal DWT because the **scantling draft** (the maximum permissible draft set by the class society) is typically **less than the design draft**. The ship is operating **below its maximum permissible draft** at the design condition, which is normal and safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Do NOT confuse **DWT (capacity)** with **Displacement (actual mass)**.  
> - Always specify the **unit (tonnes)** in the final answer.  
> - Skipping the comment on overloading loses the last 2 marks.  
> - Using ρ = 1.0 (freshwater) instead of 1.025 (seawater) costs 1 mark.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) – [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Define Block Coefficient, Midship Coefficient, and Prismatic Coefficient. Show the relationship between them. (7 marks)**

**(b) A container ship has LBP = 280 m, B = 40 m, T = 14.5 m, displacement Δ = 145,000 t, and immersed midship area A_m = 540 m². Calculate Cb, Cm, and Cp. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Definitions and Relationship:**

**Block Coefficient (Cb):**  
The ratio of the **volume of displacement (∇)** of the ship to the volume of a **rectangular block** having the same length (LBP), beam (B), and draft (T).

$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}
$$

**Midship Coefficient (Cm):**  
The ratio of the **immersed cross-sectional area at the midship section (A_m)** to the product of beam and draft at the same section.

$$
C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}
$$

**Prismatic Coefficient (Cp):**  
The ratio of the **volume of displacement (∇)** to the volume of a **prism** whose base is the immersed midship area A_m and length is LBP.

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}}
$$

**Relationship between them:**

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}{C_m \cdot B \cdot T \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{C_b}{C_m}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m}}
$$

**Part (b) – Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1: Calculate Cb**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{145,000}{1.025} = 141,463.4 \text{ m}^3 \\
C_b &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{141,463.4}{280 \times 40 \times 14.5} \\
C_b &= \frac{141,463.4}{162,400} \\
C_b &= 0.871
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_m &= \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} = \frac{540}{40 \times 14.5} = \frac{540}{580} \\
C_m &= 0.931
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cp (two ways — verification)**

**Method 1: Direct formula**

$$
C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{141,463.4}{540 \times 280} = \frac{141,463.4}{151,200} = 0.9356
$$

**Method 2: Using relationship**

$$
C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{0.871}{0.931} = 0.9356
$$

Both methods give **Cp ≈ 0.936** ✔

**Comment:**  
A container ship typically has **Cb ≈ 0.60 – 0.70**. Our calculated value of **Cb = 0.871** is unusually high for a container ship — it suggests the ship is in **ballast condition** (lightly loaded) or that the example values are illustrative. The values, however, satisfy the **invariant relationship** Cp = Cb/Cm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> - Forgetting to convert **Δ → ∇** (using density) costs 2 marks.  
> - Using ρ = 1.0 t/m³ instead of **1.025 t/m³** (seawater) costs 1 mark.  
> - Failing to **state the relationship** Cp = Cb/Cm costs 2 marks.  
> - Not showing **two methods** for Cp calculation (verification) loses 1 mark in advanced valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 1: Types of Ships & Definitions**

### 🔑 Key Definitions to Memorize
- [ ] **LOA** vs **LBP** vs **LWL** — the three "lengths" of a ship.
- [ ] **B, T, D, Fb** — the four principal vertical/lateral dimensions.
- [ ] **FP and AP** — where LBP begins and ends.
- [ ] **Cb, Cm, Cp, Cwp** — the four form coefficients.
- [ ] **GT, NT, DWT, Δ** — the four tonnage-related quantities.
- [ ] **Port, Starboard, Bow, Stern, Forward, Aft, Amidships** — directional terms.

### ⚙️ Key Formulas to Master
- [ ] $C_b = \frac{\Delta}{\rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$
- [ ] $C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$
- [ ] $C_p = \frac{C_b}{C_m} = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{BP}}$
- [ ] $D = T + F_b$
- [ ] $F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$
- [ ] $\Delta = C_b \cdot \rho \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$

### 🚢 Ship Classification Mnemonic
**"H-P-P-N"** — **H**ull form, **P**urpose, **P**ropulsion, **N**umber of hulls.

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Calling "tonnage" a unit of weight — it isn't (except DWT).
- ❌ Using ρ = 1.0 for seawater (it is **1.025 t/m³**).
- ❌ Confusing **displacement** with **DWT**.
- ❌ Forgetting to mention the **density** of seawater when using the Cb formula.
- ❌ Mixing up **port (left)** and **starboard (right)**.

### 📚 KTU Exam Pattern Tips
- Definitions and sketches carry **3 marks each** in Part A.
- Numerical problems in Part B usually follow the **"Given → Find → Formula → Substitute → Answer"** structure.
- Always end with a **comment / interpretation** to grab the last 1–2 marks.
- **Mermaid diagrams** are not required in answer sheets — but labeled **sketches** are!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Ships (General Cargo Ships)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cargo Ships (General Cargo Ships)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **General Cargo Ship** is a multi-deck, single- or twin-screw commercial vessel specifically designed to transport **break-bulk, packaged, palletized, bagged, drummed, and unitized cargo** that is loaded and discharged in parcels (lots) at multiple ports. These vessels are characterized by **tweendecks, hatch covers, and onboard cargo-handling gear** (derricks or cranes), making them highly versatile workhorses of the global tramp and liner trade.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** General Cargo Ships are classified under the broad category of **Dry Cargo Ships**, which include General Cargo, Bulk Carriers, Container Ships, and Multi-Purpose Vessels. Within Module 1, they form the *base reference vessel* for studying form coefficients, tonnage, and hull form design.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Classification Society Notation:** Most modern general cargo vessels are built to the rules of **IACS** (International Association of Classification Societies) members such as Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas (BV), DNV, or ABS, with a typical service notation like **✠ 1A General Cargo Ship**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a **floating multi-storey warehouse mounted on a ship's hull**.

- The **floors** of this warehouse are called **tweendecks** (horizontal platforms inside the hold).
- The **walls between rooms** are called **bulkheads** (watertight vertical partitions).
- The **roof** of each room is a **hatch cover** that can slide or lift open to drop cargo inside.
- The **gantry on top** represents the **cranes/derricks** used to load and unload cargo.
- The **bridge cabin** is the captain's office at the top.

Just as a warehouse stores mixed goods (boxes, sacks, drums) for different customers, a general cargo ship carries **mixed commodities** (textiles, machinery, food grains, steel coils) for multiple consignees across different ports. The "truck" carrying this warehouse moves at a service speed of typically **14–18 knots** (1 knot = 1.852 km/h).

---

## 1.3 Key Operational Features

A typical **single-island general cargo ship** has the following structural and operational features:

- **Multi-deck hull** with 1–3 tweendecks.
- **Flush deck** or **well-deck** design with large rectangular hatches.
- **MacGregor-type or pontoon hatch covers** for weather protection.
- **4 to 6 cargo holds** separated by watertight bulkheads.
- **Fore-and-aft bridge superstructure** (single island) with engine room either aft or amidships.
- **3–10 tonnes SWL (Safe Working Load)** cargo cranes or derricks per hatch.
- **Deadweight capacity (DWT)** ranging from **3,000 t to 30,000 t** (typical Handy/Mid-size range).
- **Service speed:** 14–18 knots.
- **Endurance:** 10,000–20,000 nautical miles.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Metric — Block Coefficient (C_b):** General cargo ships have a **block coefficient between 0.60 and 0.72**, balancing cargo-carrying capacity with a moderate speed. Lower C_b means a slimmer, faster hull; higher C_b means a fuller, higher-capacity hull.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural & Functional Logic of a General Cargo Ship

The design of a general cargo ship is governed by **four engineering trade-offs** that must be simultaneously satisfied:

### Step 1 — Cargo Volume vs. Ship Size
- The **stowage factor (SF)** of mixed cargoes typically lies in the range **1.2 m³/t to 2.5 m³/t**.
- Required **cargo volume = Mass × SF**.
- Required **hold volume (V_h)** must be greater than cargo volume to allow for broken stowage and dunnage.

### Step 2 — Hull Form Selection
- The **block coefficient (C_b)** determines the fullness of the hull.
- For general cargo: **C_b ≈ 0.60 to 0.72** (medium-full hull form).
- Prismatic coefficient (C_p) typically **0.65 to 0.75**.

### Step 3 — Stability & Freeboard
- The ship must satisfy **IMO Load Line Convention** freeboard requirements.
- **GM (metacentric height)** must be ≥ 0.15 m for intact stability.

### Step 4 — Tonnage & Regulatory Compliance
- **Gross Tonnage (GT)** and **Net Tonnage (NT)** are computed using the **IMO Tonnage Convention (1969)**.
- **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** is computed under **DWT Convention**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------------|------|
| 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_b$ | $\nabla \, / \, (L \times B \times T)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p$ | $\nabla \, / \, (A_m \times L)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Midship Coefficient | $C_m$ | $A_m \, / \, (B \times T)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Waterplane Area Coefficient | $C_w$ | $A_w \, / \, (L \times B)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Displacement | $\Delta$ | $C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$ | tonnes |
| 6 | Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT$ | $\Delta_{load} - \Delta_{light}$ | tonnes |
| 7 | Lightweight (Lightship Weight) | $LW$ | $\Delta - DWT$ | tonnes |
| 8 | Tonnes per Centimetre | $TPC$ | $A_w \times \rho \, / \, 100$ | t/cm |
| 9 | Stowage Factor | $SF$ | $V_{cargo} \, / \, W_{cargo}$ | m³/t |
| 10 | Broken Stowage | $BS$ | $1 - (V_{cargo} \, / \, V_{hold})$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Cargo Density | $\rho_c$ | $W_{cargo} \, / \, V_{cargo}$ | t/m³ |
| 12 | Length between Perpendiculars | $LBP$ | $L_{OA} - L_{overhang}$ | metres |
| 13 | Froude Number | $F_n$ | $V \, / \, \sqrt{g \times L}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Reserve Buoyancy | $RB$ | $1 - F$ (where F = freeboard ratio) | fraction |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Universal Conversion:** Seawater density **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³**; Freshwater density **ρ_fw = 1.000 t/m³**. Always use **1.025 t/m³** unless explicitly stated otherwise in KTU problems.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design principles of general cargo ships form the **foundational template** for all subsequent vessel types in naval architecture:

- **Bulk Carriers** evolved by removing tweendecks and adding larger hatches.
- **Container Ships** evolved by adding cellular guides and cell-castles.
- **Ro-Ro Ships** evolved by adding stern/ramp doors and internal ramps.
- **Multi-Purpose Vessels** are direct upgrades with heavy-lift cranes (up to 750 t SWL).

Thus, mastering the form coefficients, tonnage, and stowage logic of a general cargo ship is **the gateway** to designing any modern merchant vessel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Block Coefficient (C_b)

The block coefficient is the ratio of the **displaced volume of the ship (∇)** to the volume of a **rectangular box** that tightly encloses the ship (Length × Beam × Draft).

**Step 1 — Statement from Archimedes' Principle**

A floating ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the weight of the ship.

$$\Delta = \nabla \times \rho$$

where Δ is the displacement (in tonnes), ∇ is the displaced volume (in m³), and ρ is the water density (in t/m³).

**Step 2 — Definition of Enclosing Box**

The smallest rectangular box that encloses the ship has dimensions:
- Length = L (Length between Perpendiculars, LBP)
- Breadth = B (Moulded Breadth)
- Depth immersed = T (Moulded Draft)

The volume of this box is:

$$V_{box} = L \times B \times T$$

**Step 3 — Defining C_b**

By definition, the block coefficient is the volumetric efficiency of the hull:

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{V_{box}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Expression**

Substituting the values of ∇ and V_box:

$$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times T}$$

**Step 5 — Expressing Displacement**

Rearranging the Archimedes equation:

$$\Delta = C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Insight:** For a fixed L, B, T, the displacement is **linearly proportional** to C_b. A 10% increase in C_b produces a 10% increase in cargo capacity — but at the cost of higher resistance and lower speed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of TPC (Tonnes per Centimetre)

TPC is the **mass (in tonnes) required to increase the ship's draft by exactly 1 centimetre (0.01 m)**.

**Step 1 — Volume Increase per cm of Draft**

If the waterplane area at the load waterline is $A_w$ (in m²), then immersing the ship by 0.01 m adds a slab of water:

$$\Delta V = A_w \times 0.01 \quad (\text{m}^3)$$

**Step 2 — Mass Equivalent**

The mass of this additional displaced water is:

$$\Delta m = \Delta V \times \rho = A_w \times 0.01 \times \rho \quad (\text{tonnes})$$

**Step 3 — Defining TPC**

By definition, this mass is the Tonnes Per Centimetre:

$$TPC = A_w \times 0.01 \times \rho$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 100 to remove the decimal:

$$TPC = \frac{A_w \times \rho}{100} \quad (\text{tonnes/cm})$$

> [!NOTE]  
> For **seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³)**, this simplifies to: $TPC = A_w / 97.56$. For **freshwater (ρ = 1.000 t/m³)**, $TPC = A_w / 100$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A general cargo ship has the following particulars:
- $L = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, $T = 6.5$ m
- $C_b = 0.66$
- $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ (seawater)
- $C_w = 0.71$
- $LW = 4800$ t

**Find:** (i) Displacement Δ, (ii) TPC, (iii) DWT, (iv) Cargo capacity (assume consumables = 600 t).

**Solution:**

**(i) Displacement**

$$\Delta = C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$$

$$\Delta = 0.66 \times 120 \times 18 \times 6.5 \times 1.025$$

$$\Delta = 0.66 \times 14310 \times 1.025$$

$$\Delta = 0.66 \times 14667.75$$

$$\Delta = 9680.715 \text{ tonnes} \approx 9680.7 \text{ t}$$

**(ii) TPC**

$$A_w = C_w \times L \times B = 0.71 \times 120 \times 18 = 1533.6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$TPC = \frac{A_w \times \rho}{100} = \frac{1533.6 \times 1.025}{100} = \frac{1571.94}{100}$$

$$TPC = 15.72 \text{ t/cm}$$

**(iii) DWT**

$$DWT = \Delta - LW = 9680.7 - 4800 = 4880.7 \text{ t}$$

**(iv) Cargo Capacity**

$$W_{cargo} = DWT - \text{Consumables} = 4880.7 - 600 = 4280.7 \text{ t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Result Validation:** DWT/TPC = 4880.7 / 15.72 ≈ 310.5 cm ≈ 3.10 m of "draft-to-deadweight" sensitivity. This is realistic for a 4800 DWT general cargo ship.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cargo Ship Types

| Feature | General Cargo | Bulk Carrier | Container Ship | Tanker |
|---------|--------------|--------------|----------------|--------|
| Block Coeff. (C_b) | 0.60 – 0.72 | 0.78 – 0.85 | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.78 – 0.85 |
| Tweendecks | Yes (1–3) | No | No | No |
| Hatch Covers | Yes | Yes | Yes (removable) | Deck-mounted |
| Cargo Handling Gear | Cranes/Derricks | Cranes only | None (shore) | Pipeline |
| Typical DWT | 3,000–30,000 t | 30,000–400,000 t | 10,000–240,000 t | 30,000–550,000 t |
| Service Speed | 14–18 knots | 12–15 knots | 18–25 knots | 12–16 knots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Single-Island General Cargo Ship

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture and inter-component flow** of a typical single-island general cargo ship, viewed in profile (longitudinal section).

```mermaid
graph TD
    hullA["General Cargo Ship Hull"]
    bowA1["Forecastle Deck"]
    bowA2["Bow Thruster Compartment"]
    holdB1["Cargo Hold 1"]
    holdB2["Cargo Hold 2"]
    holdB3["Cargo Hold 3"]
    bhdC1["Watertight Bulkhead 1"]
    bhdC2["Watertight Bulkhead 2"]
    tweenD1["Tween Deck Level 1"]
    tweenD2["Tween Deck Level 2"]
    hatchE1["Hatch Cover Hatch 1"]
    hatchE2["Hatch Cover Hatch 2"]
    hatchE3["Hatch Cover Hatch 3"]
    mainF["Main Deck Stringer"]
    superG["Superstructure Island"]
    bridgeH1["Navigation Bridge"]
    accomH2["Crew Accommodation"]
    engineI["Engine Room"]
    funnelJ["Funnel Exhaust"]
    gearK1["Crane 1 SWL 30t"]
    gearK2["Crane 2 SWL 30t"]
    gearK3["Crane 3 SWL 30t"]
    sternL1["Poop Deck"]
    sternL2["Stern Frame Rudder"]
    shaftM["Propeller Shaft Line"]

    hullA --> bowA1
    hullA --> bowA2
    hullA --> holdB1
    hullA --> holdB2
    hullA --> holdB3
    holdB1 --> bhdC1
    bhdC1 --> holdB2
    holdB2 --> bhdC2
    bhdC2 --> holdB3
    holdB1 --> tweenD1
    tweenD1 --> tweenD2
    holdB2 --> hatchE1
    holdB3 --> hatchE2
    tweenD2 --> hatchE3
    hatchE1 --> mainF
    hatchE2 --> mainF
    hatchE3 --> mainF
    mainF --> superG
    superG --> bridgeH1
    superG --> accomH2
    superG --> engineI
    engineI --> funnelJ
    mainF --> gearK1
    mainF --> gearK2
    mainF --> gearK3
    hullA --> sternL1
    sternL1 --> sternL2
    engineI --> shaftM
    shaftM --> sternL2
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram:** The hull is partitioned into **3 cargo holds** (Hold 1, 2, 3) separated by watertight bulkheads. Each hold is sub-divided vertically by **tweendecks** to allow segregation of cargo types. The **superstructure island** (aft) houses the bridge, accommodation, and engine room. **Cranes** straddle the main deck hatches to enable self-loading/discharging at any port without shore equipment.

---

## 4.2 Cargo Loading & Stowage Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    cargoA["Incoming Cargo at Quay"]
    craneB["Shipboard Crane SWL 30t"]
    hatchC["Hatch Cover Opened"]
    holdD["Cargo Hold Volume"]
    tweenE["Tween Deck Compartments"]
    lashF["Lashing and Securing"]
    trimG["Stability Trim Check"]
    sailH["Sailing Draft Condition"]

    cargoA --> craneB
    craneB --> hatchC
    hatchC --> holdD
    holdD --> tweenE
    tweenE --> lashF
    lashF --> trimG
    trimG --> sailH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Logic:** Every cargo load operation must end with a **trim and stability check** to ensure IMO intact stability criteria are met. A general cargo ship typically allows **10–15 days of cargo work** per port, depending on parcel size and crane productivity.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Midship Region)

```mermaid
graph TD
    shipA["Cross Section View"]
    deckA1["Main Deck Plate"]
    stringerA2["Deck Stringer"]
    sideA3["Side Shell Plate"]
    tweenA4["Tween Deck Plate"]
    holdA5["Cargo Space"]
    bilgeA6["Bilge Hopper Tank"]
    keelA7["Flat Plate Keel"]
    floorA8["Bottom Transverse Frame"]
    bracketA9["Tank Top Bracket"]

    shipA --> deckA1
    shipA --> sideA3
    shipA --> tweenA4
    shipA --> holdA5
    shipA --> keelA7
    deckA1 --> stringerA2
    sideA3 --> floorA8
    tweenA4 --> holdA5
    holdA5 --> bilgeA6
    bilgeA6 --> keelA7
    floorA8 --> bracketA9
    bracketA9 --> tweenA4
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Structural Logic:** The **transverse frame system** carries vertical load; the **tweendeck brackets** transfer cargo weight to the side shell; the **bilge hopper** drains water to a sump for the bilge pump.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a General Cargo Ship. List any six distinguishing features. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **General Cargo Ship** is a multi-deck merchant vessel designed to carry **mixed break-bulk, packaged, and unitized dry cargoes** at multiple ports. Six distinguishing features:

1. **Multi-deck hull** with 1–3 tweendecks for cargo segregation.
2. **Hatch covers** (MacGregor or pontoon type) for weather-tight cargo protection.
3. **Onboard cargo-handling gear** — derricks or cranes (3–10 t SWL each).
4. **Watertight transverse bulkheads** dividing hull into 3–6 holds.
5. **Block coefficient (C_b) between 0.60 and 0.72** — medium-full form.
6. **Deadweight range of 3,000 t to 30,000 t** — Handy to Mid-size category.

---

### Question 2: Define Stowage Factor. A consignment of 12,000 t of bagged rice has a stowage factor of 1.45 m³/t. Find the required hold volume if broken stowage is 15%. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Stowage Factor is the **volume occupied by one tonne of cargo**, expressed in m³/t.

$$SF = \frac{V_{cargo}}{W_{cargo}}$$

**Given:** $W = 12{,}000$ t, $SF = 1.45$ m³/t, Broken Stowage = 15% (i.e., 0.15)

**Step 1 — Cargo Volume:**

$$V_{cargo} = W \times SF = 12{,}000 \times 1.45 = 17{,}400 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Required Hold Volume (accounting for 15% broken stowage):**

$$V_{hold} = \frac{V_{cargo}}{1 - BS} = \frac{17{,}400}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{17{,}400}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{V_{hold} = 20{,}470.59 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the choice given.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Discuss the classification of general cargo ships based on (i) number of decks, (ii) superstructure position, and (iii) cargo-handling gear. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification by Number of Decks:**

- **Single-deck ships:** Single cargo hold level; suitable for heavy machinery, steel products, and project cargo.
- **Tweendeck ships:** One or two intermediate decks; provides **compartmentalization** for segregation of cargoes (e.g., grain below, machinery above). Most common in liner trade.
- **Multi-deck / Cellular ships:** Three or more decks; used in older **combination passenger-cargo** vessels.

**(ii) Classification by Superstructure Position:**

- **Single-island (aft) layout:** Engine room and bridge at the stern; cargo holds occupy the full forward and midship region. Maximizes continuous cargo length.
- **Single-island (midship) layout:** Bridge amidships; engine room below; cargo in forward and aft holds. Provides good visibility but splits cargo space.
- **Twin-island layout:** Bridge forward, engine room aft. Common in **reefer/general cargo** combined vessels.
- **Flush-decker:** Minimal superstructure; all accommodation forward in a forecastle; cargo holds run nearly full length.

**(iii) Classification by Cargo-Handling Gear:**

- **Geared vessels:** Equipped with 4–10 shipboard cranes (3–30 t SWL). Can load/discharge at any port without shore equipment. Higher capital cost.
- **Gearless vessels:** No onboard gear; depend entirely on shore cranes. Cheaper to build but require port infrastructure.
- **Heavy-lift / Multi-purpose:** Equipped with 1–2 heavy-lift cranes (100–750 t SWL) for project cargo. Hybrid between general cargo and heavy-lift.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conclusion:** Modern general cargo ships are predominantly **single-island tweendeckers with geared cranes**, balancing flexibility, cargo volume, and turnaround time.

---

**(b) A general cargo ship has the following particulars: $L = 130$ m, $B = 19.5$ m, $T = 7.2$ m, $C_b = 0.68$, $C_w = 0.72$, $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $LW = 5500$ t. Calculate: (i) Displacement, (ii) TPC, (iii) DWT, (iv) Cargo capacity assuming consumables of 850 t. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Displacement:**

$$\Delta = C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$$

$$\Delta = 0.68 \times 130 \times 19.5 \times 7.2 \times 1.025$$

$$\Delta = 0.68 \times 18252 \times 1.025$$

$$\Delta = 0.68 \times 18708.3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta = 12{,}721.64 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**(ii) TPC:**

$$A_w = C_w \times L \times B = 0.72 \times 130 \times 19.5 = 1825.2 \text{ m}^2$$

$$TPC = \frac{A_w \times \rho}{100} = \frac{1825.2 \times 1.025}{100}$$

$$\boxed{TPC = 18.71 \text{ t/cm}}$$

**(iii) Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$DWT = \Delta - LW = 12{,}721.64 - 5500$$

$$\boxed{DWT = 7221.64 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**(iv) Cargo Capacity:**

$$W_{cargo} = DWT - \text{Consumables} = 7221.64 - 850$$

$$\boxed{W_{cargo} = 6371.64 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the midship section structural arrangement of a general cargo ship. Explain the function of each structural member. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

**Midship Section — Key Structural Members:**

```
   ┌─────────────── Main Deck Plate ───────────────┐
   │  Side Stringer  ┊              ┊ Side Stringer│
   ├═══════════════ Tween Deck Plate ══════════════┤
   │  Side Shell     ┊  Hold Space  ┊ Side Shell   │
   │  (Top)          ┊              ┊ (Top)        │
   │                 ┊              ┊  ─── Bilge   │
   │  Longitudinal   ┊              ┊   Hopper     │
   │  Bulkhead       ┊  Cargo       ┊   (Sloped)   │
   │  Frame          ┊  Stowage     ┊              │
   │  Side Shell     ┊  Region      ┊  Side Shell  │
   │  (Bottom)       ┊              ┊  (Bottom)    │
   └════════════════ Tank Top Plate ═══════════════┘
       Double Bottom Tank                    Keel Plate
```

**Function of Each Member:**

1. **Main Deck Plate:** Topmost horizontal plating; carries weather, crane, and hatch cover loads.
2. **Side Stringer:** Reinforced plate strip at the deck edge; resists shear and bending.
3. **Tween Deck Plate:** Intermediate horizontal plating; subdivides cargo hold vertically.
4. **Side Shell Plate:** Vertical outer hull plating; resists hydrostatic and wave pressure.
5. **Longitudinal Bulkhead/Frame:** Reduces panel span and increases hull girder strength.
6. **Bilge Hopper (sloped plate):** Funnels water/drainage to the bilge well; increases cargo volume.
7. **Tank Top Plate:** Inner bottom plating above double bottom tanks.
8. **Double Bottom Tank:** Provides buoyancy reserve, fuel oil storage, and fresh water ballast.
9. **Keel Plate:** Lowest longitudinal strength member of the hull girder.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Exam Tip:** A common mistake is omitting the **transverse frame spacing** mention. KTU expects mention of **transverse framing at 600–800 mm** spacing for general cargo ships (per IACS UR S21).

---

**(b) A ship carries 8,000 t of general cargo with an average stowage factor of 1.40 m³/t. Calculate: (i) Total volume of cargo, (ii) Required cargo hold volume if utilization factor is 0.85, (iii) Volume per hold if the ship has 4 holds of equal capacity. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total Cargo Volume:**

$$V_{cargo} = W \times SF = 8000 \times 1.40$$

$$\boxed{V_{cargo} = 11{,}200 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(ii) Required Hold Volume:**

$$V_{hold} = \frac{V_{cargo}}{\text{Utilization Factor}} = \frac{11{,}200}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{V_{hold} = 13{,}176.47 \text{ m}^3}$$

**(iii) Volume Per Hold (4 equal holds):**

$$V_{per\,hold} = \frac{V_{hold}}{4} = \frac{13{,}176.47}{4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{per\,hold} = 3294.12 \text{ m}^3}$$

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Using ρ = 1.000 instead of 1.025 t/m³** in seawater problems → causes ~2.4% error in displacement. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract consumables** from DWT when finding cargo capacity. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up LBP with LOA** in C_b or TPC formulas. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion** of freeboard or draft from feet to metres. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 5. **Omitting broken stowage factor** in cargo hold volume calculation. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 6. **Not labelling the Mermaid/sketch** when the question says "with neat sketch" → (Loss: 1–2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cargo Ships (General Cargo Ships)**

- **Definition:** Multi-deck vessel for mixed break-bulk dry cargoes; transported in parcels to multiple ports.
- **Typical DWT Range:** 3,000 t to 30,000 t (Handy → Mid-size class).
- **Service Speed:** 14–18 knots.
- **Block Coefficient (C_b):** 0.60 to 0.72.
- **Prismatic Coefficient (C_p):** 0.65 to 0.75.
- **Midship Coefficient (C_m):** 0.90 to 0.98 (high; full hull form).
- **Waterplane Coefficient (C_w):** 0.70 to 0.80.
- **Number of Holds:** 3 to 6 (separated by watertight bulkheads).
- **Tweendecks:** 1 to 3 levels for cargo segregation.
- **Cargo Gear:** Derricks or hydraulic cranes, typically 3–30 t SWL per unit.
- **Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
  - $C_b = \nabla / (L \times B \times T)$
  - $\Delta = C_b \times L \times B \times T \times \rho$
  - $TPC = A_w \times \rho / 100$
  - $DWT = \Delta_{load} - \Delta_{light}$ or $DWT = \Delta - LW$
  - $SF = V_{cargo} / W_{cargo}$
  - $V_{hold} = V_{cargo} / (1 - BS)$
- **Standard Density:** Seawater = 1.025 t/m³ (always unless specified).
- **Classification Society Notation:** ✠ 1A General Cargo Ship.
- **Operational Limitation:** Subject to **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) and **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution) regulations.
- **Historical Note:** General cargo ships dominated world trade until the **1960s containerization revolution**; today they survive as **multi-purpose/heavy-lift vessels** and **regional short-sea traders**.
- **Key Distinction from Container Ships:** General cargo has **variable hold shapes and tweendecks**; container ships have **standardized cellular holds with cell guides**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulk Carriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bulk Carriers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Carrier:** A single-deck, multi-purpose dry-cargo ship equipped with top-side tanks and hopper tanks within the cargo space area, designed primarily to carry homogeneous dry-bulk cargoes such as grain, coal, iron ore, cement, sugar, and similar unpackaged goods in bulk form.

According to the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)** and the **SOLAS Chapter XII** (Regulations for Bulk Carriers), a bulk carrier is specifically defined as:

$$\text{Bulk Carrier} = \left\{ \text{Ship} \mid \text{Single deck}, \, \text{Top-side tanks present}, \, \text{Hopper tanks in cargo space}, \, \text{Designed for dry bulk cargo} \right\}$$

A bulk carrier is fundamentally a **single-deck vessel** with the following defining characteristics:
- **Single continuous deck** running the entire length of the cargo area
- **Hopper tanks** (sloped tanks) on the port and starboard sides at the bottom
- **Top-side tanks** (wing tanks) located on the upper portions of the cargo holds
- **Cargo holds** that are open from the deck for loading/discharging
- **Hatch covers** that can be opened for vertical loading (gravity drops)
- **Self-trimming** design (in some classes) to allow cargo to flow evenly

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike general cargo ships or container ships, bulk carriers are **single-deck** vessels and are NOT permitted by IACS to carry liquid cargoes in their cargo holds. They are also prohibited from carrying containers on deck in certain configurations under revised SOLAS regulations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **bulk carrier as a giant floating bathtub designed to scoop up raw materials**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Floating Train Hopper"**
> 
> Imagine a long freight train hopper car — that wide, open-top wagon used to haul coal or wheat. Now stretch it to about 200-300 meters, give it a sloped bottom on both sides (hopper tanks), install watertight walls on top (top-side tanks), put a steel lid on top that opens up (hatch covers), and float it on the ocean. That is essentially a bulk carrier!
> 
> - The **cargo holds** are the giant open containers in the middle.
> - The **hopper tanks** (sloped V-shaped tanks on the sides at the bottom) act like sand timers — they help the cargo slide to the center for grab discharge.
> - The **top-side tanks** act as the buoyancy "shoulders" that keep the ship from rolling over when cargo is uneven.
> - The **hatch covers** are like giant sliding or rolling lids that protect the cargo from seawater.
> - The **ballast tanks** (double bottom + hopper + top-side) act like adjustable water bags that help balance the ship during the empty return voyage.

When you visualize a bulk carrier, picture a **long rectangular barge with a flat top, sloped sides at the bottom, and a few wide open holes in the middle** — the cargo holds, sealed by enormous movable steel lids.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Class Notation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Bulk Carrier Metrics (Industry References):**
> - **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage):** Primary sizing metric; ranges from 5,000 DWT (Handysize) to 400,000 DWT (VLOC)
> - **LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars):** 100 m (small) to 360 m (Capesize/VLOC)
> - **Beam (B):** Typically 0.14 L to 0.18 L
> - **Draft (T):** 7 m (Handysize) to 23 m (VLOC fully loaded)
> - **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** **0.75 to 0.85** (full-form, slow-speed hull)
> - **Service Speed (V):** 12 to 15 knots
> - **Cargo Density (ρ_cargo):** 0.5 t/m³ (grain) to 3.0 t/m³ (iron ore)
> - **Young's Modulus of Ship Steel (E):** 2.06 × 10⁵ N/mm²
> - **Yield Stress (σ_y):** 235 N/mm² for Grade A steel (HTS), 315 N/mm² for Grade AH
> - **Density of Seawater (ρ_sw):** **1.025 t/m³**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** 9.81 m/s²

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Society Notation (Common):** DNV ✪1A Bulk Carrier ESP, LR ✪100A1 Bulk Carrier, ABS ✪A1 Bulk Carrier (CSR), BV ✪HULL ✪Mach Bulk Carrier CSR

---

## 1.4 Historical Context & Why Bulk Carriers Matter

The bulk carrier emerged as a specialized vessel class in the **late 19th century** when global industrialization required economical transport of massive quantities of raw materials (coal, iron ore, grain). Before bulk carriers, such cargoes had to be loaded in bags, barrels, or crates, making transport inefficient and expensive.

Today, bulk carriers transport approximately **30% of all seaborne trade by volume** and are the **backbone of industrial supply chains** for steel mills, power plants, and food distributors worldwide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Major Bulk Cargoes (in order of global tonnage):**
> 1. **Iron ore** (~1.5 billion tonnes/year) — main cargo of Capesize vessels
> 2. **Coal** (~1.2 billion tonnes/year) — both thermal and coking
> 3. **Grain** (wheat, corn, soybeans) — ~450 million tonnes/year
> 4. **Bauxite, alumina, phosphate rock** — minor bulks
> 5. **Cement, sugar, salt, fertilizers** — minor bulks
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bulk Carriers (By Size & Route)

Bulk carriers are classified primarily by their **DWT capacity** and the **shipping route** they are designed to serve. This classification is critical because each size class has distinct structural, regulatory, and operational characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table (Must Memorize):**

| Size Class | DWT Range (t) | Length (m) | Draft (m) | Typical Trade Route |
|------------|---------------|------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Handysize** | 15,000 – 35,000 | 120 – 150 | 7 – 10 | Worldwide, minor ports |
| **Handymax / Supramax** | 35,000 – 60,000 | 150 – 200 | 10 – 12 | Worldwide, versatile |
| **Ultramax** | 60,000 – 65,000 | 190 – 200 | 11 – 12.5 | Efficient Supramax variant |
| **Panamax** | 60,000 – 80,000 | 220 – 230 | 12 – 14 | Old Panama Canal locks |
| **New Panamax (Neopanamax)** | 80,000 – 120,000 | 230 – 250 | 14 – 16 | Expanded Panama Canal |
| **Capesize** | 100,000 – 200,000 | 230 – 290 | 16 – 18.5 | Brazil/China iron ore |
| **Very Large Ore Carrier (VLOC)** | 200,000 – 400,000 | 300 – 360 | 20 – 23 | Long-haul ore routes |

---

## 2.2 Structural Anatomy of a Bulk Carrier

A bulk carrier consists of several major structural zones, each with a specific engineering function:

### 2.2.1 Cargo Hold Region
- **Holds:** Typically 5 to 9 large open box-shaped compartments
- **Bulkheads:** Transverse corrugated watertight walls separating holds
- **Hatch Coamings:** Raised lips (~1.5 m) on deck to prevent water ingress
- **Hatch Covers:** Movable steel lids sealing the holds

### 2.2.2 Double Bottom
- Located below the cargo hold bottom
- Typically 1.5 m to 2.5 m deep
- Functions as both a **ballast tank** and structural strength member

### 2.2.3 Hopper Tanks (Sloped Side Tanks)
- V-shaped tanks at the lower corners of each cargo hold
- Slope angle typically **30° to 45°** from horizontal
- Used for ballast water and to assist cargo discharge by gravity

### 2.2.4 Top-Side Tanks (Wing Tanks)
- Located on the upper portions of the cargo hold (above the load waterline)
- Form a "U" or "double side" cross-section
- Provide reserve buoyancy and improve damage stability

### 2.2.5 Forecastle & Poop Deck
- Raised forecastle (forward) improves seakeeping in head seas
- Poop deck (aft) protects accommodation and machinery spaces

---

## 2.3 Self-Trimming Bulk Carriers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Self-Trimming Concept:**
> A self-trimming bulk carrier has hopper tanks that allow cargo to flow freely toward the center of the hold during loading. This is critical for grain cargoes, which must comply with the **International Grain Code** — a vessel must be able to trim itself to within a specific angle to prevent excessive heeling moments from shifting grain.

The hopper angle θ is the key design parameter:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h_{\text{hopper}}}{w_{\text{hopper}}}\right)$$

Where:
- $h_{\text{hopper}}$ = vertical height of hopper slope (m)
- $w_{\text{hopper}}$ = horizontal projection of hopper slope (m)

> **Typical hopper angle:** 30° to 45° (steeper = better self-trimming)

---

## 2.4 Cargo Hold Volume & Stowage Factor

The **stowage factor (SF)** is the volume (m³) required to stow one tonne of cargo. It varies inversely with cargo density:

$$SF = \frac{V_{\text{cargo hold}}}{M_{\text{cargo}}} = \frac{1}{\rho_{\text{cargo}}}$$

Where:
- $V_{\text{cargo hold}}$ = available cargo volume (m³)
- $M_{\text{cargo}}$ = mass of cargo (t)
- $\rho_{\text{cargo}}$ = cargo density (t/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Light cargoes** (grain, 0.75 t/m³) require large holds → SF ≈ 1.33 m³/t
> **Heavy cargoes** (iron ore, 2.5–3.0 t/m³) need smaller holds → SF ≈ 0.33–0.40 m³/t

---

## 2.5 Strength Considerations — Hogging & Sagging

A bulk carrier is essentially a **box girder** floating on water. Its longitudinal strength is governed by two critical conditions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hogging Condition:** The ship is supported at the ends (by buoyancy) but loaded in the middle (cargo weight). The hull bends **upward in the middle** — the deck is in **tension** and the bottom is in **compression**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sagging Condition:** The ship is supported in the middle (buoyancy) but loaded at the ends (or when ballasted with heavy bow/stern weights). The hull bends **downward in the middle** — the deck is in **compression** and the bottom is in **tension**.

The bending moment at midship under hogging is approximated by:

$$BM_{\text{hog}} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{24}$$

And under sagging:

$$BM_{\text{sag}} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{12}$$

Where:
- $w$ = distributed load (weight or buoyancy) per unit length (t/m)
- $L$ = length of cargo region (m)

> **Note:** In practice, sagging is often the more severe condition for bulk carriers because heavy cargo concentrates in the holds, but buoyancy is distributed more uniformly along the hull.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Stowage Factor | $SF = V/M$ | m³/t | Inversely proportional to ρ_cargo |
| 2 | Cargo Density | $\rho_c = M/V$ | t/m³ | Light <1.0, Medium 1.0–1.5, Heavy >1.5 |
| 3 | Hopper Angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(h/w)$ | degrees | 30°–45° typical |
| 4 | Hogging BM | $BM = wL^2/24$ | t·m | Cargo concentrated amidships |
| 5 | Sagging BM | $BM = wL^2/12$ | t·m | Ballast in peaks, buoyancy uniform |
| 6 | Section Modulus | $Z = I/y_{\text{max}}$ | cm³ | Must exceed minimum per IACS UR S11 |
| 7 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | – | 0.75 to 0.85 for bulkers |
| 8 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M \cdot L)$ | – | 0.80 to 0.85 typical |
| 9 | Displacement | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | t | $\nabla$ = volumetric displacement |
| 10 | Deadweight | $DWT = \Delta - LWT$ | t | LWT = lightweight |
| 11 | Free Surface Effect | $GMT_{\text{eff}} = GMT - \frac{\sum i \cdot \rho_i}{\Delta}$ | m | Critical in ballast tanks |
| 12 | Allowable Shear Stress | $\tau_{\text{allow}} = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ | N/mm² | Von Mises criterion |

> **Use \vert x \vert, \mid x \mid, or \lVert x \rVert instead of \vert x \vert in text — it renders cleaner in LaTeX.**

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

Understanding bulk carrier design is essential for:
- **Naval Architects:** Structural design, hydrostatic analysis, FEA modelling
- **Marine Engineers:** Cargo handling systems, ballast management
- **Ship Surveyors:** Compliance with IACS UR S21/S22/S23 (CSR-BC)
- **Charterers & Operators:** Selection of vessel size for trade route optimization
- **Port Engineers:** Compatibility of vessel with port infrastructure (draft, cranes)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Section Modulus Requirement (IACS UR S11)

The IACS Unified Requirement S11 specifies the minimum longitudinal section modulus for ships. For a bulk carrier, the section modulus at midship must be sufficient to resist the maximum still-water bending moment, considering both hogging and sagging.

### Step 1: Compute the Design Bending Moment

The design hogging moment is:

$$M_{H,\text{design}} = f_{\text{dyn}} \cdot M_{H,\text{SWB}} \cdot 10^{-3} \quad [\text{kN·m}]$$

Where:
- $f_{\text{dyn}}$ = dynamic factor (typically 1.0 to 1.2 for sea conditions)
- $M_{H,\text{SWB}}$ = still water hogging bending moment (t·m)

### Step 2: Compute Minimum Section Modulus

The minimum section modulus for the strength deck or bottom (whichever is critical) is:

$$Z_{\min} = \frac{M_{\text{design}} \cdot 10^3}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}} \quad [\text{cm}^3]$$

Where:
- $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ = allowable bending stress (N/mm²)
- $M_{\text{design}}$ = maximum design bending moment (kN·m)

### Step 3: IACS Empirical Formula (Direct)

For ships where direct calculation is not feasible, IACS provides an empirical minimum:

$$Z_{\min} = C \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7) \cdot K \quad [\text{cm}^3]$$

Where:
- $L$ = rule length (m)
- $B$ = moulded breadth (m)
- $C_B$ = block coefficient
- $C$ = coefficient (e.g., 0.9 for tankers/bulkers)
- $K$ = material factor (1.0 for HTS, 0.78 for HTS32, etc.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Bulk Carrier Stowage Calculation

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**

A Handymax bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- 5 cargo holds, each with length 28 m, breadth 28 m, depth (cargo) 16 m
- Hopper tank height = 3.5 m, hopper width = 4.0 m
- Cargo to be loaded: Wheat, density = 0.78 t/m³ (maximum)
- Permissible draft = 11.0 m
- Lightweight = 8,500 t
- Density of seawater = 1.025 t/m³

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate the **effective cargo volume** per hold (subtract hopper wedge volume).
2. Calculate the **maximum cargo tonnage** for 5 holds.
3. Calculate the **displacement** and **DWT** at permissible draft.
4. Check if the cargo tonnage fits within the DWT.
5. Calculate the **hopper slope angle**.

---

### Solution (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Effective Cargo Volume per Hold**

The cargo hold cross-section is rectangular except for the two V-shaped hoppers at the bottom corners. Each hopper forms a triangular wedge.

**Volume of one hopper wedge (triangular prism along hold length):**

$$V_{\text{hopper}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot w_{\text{hopper}} \cdot h_{\text{hopper}} \cdot L_{\text{hold}}$$

Substituting:
- $w_{\text{hopper}} = 4.0$ m
- $h_{\text{hopper}} = 3.5$ m
- $L_{\text{hold}} = 28$ m

$$V_{\text{hopper}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 4.0 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 28$$

$$V_{\text{hopper}} = 2.0 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 28$$

$$V_{\text{hopper}} = 7.0 \cdot 28 = 196 \text{ m}^3$$

**Each hold has 2 hoppers (port + starboard), so total hopper volume per hold:**

$$V_{\text{hoppers,hold}} = 2 \cdot 196 = 392 \text{ m}^3$$

**Total rectangular cargo volume per hold (without hopper deduction):**

$$V_{\text{rect}} = L_{\text{hold}} \cdot B_{\text{hold}} \cdot D_{\text{hold}} = 28 \cdot 28 \cdot 16$$

$$V_{\text{rect}} = 784 \cdot 16 = 12{,}544 \text{ m}^3$$

**Effective cargo volume per hold:**

$$V_{\text{eff,hold}} = V_{\text{rect}} - V_{\text{hoppers,hold}}$$

$$V_{\text{eff,hold}} = 12{,}544 - 392 = 12{,}152 \text{ m}^3$$

---

**Step 2: Total Cargo Volume for 5 Holds**

$$V_{\text{total}} = 5 \cdot V_{\text{eff,hold}} = 5 \cdot 12{,}152 = 60{,}760 \text{ m}^3$$

---

**Step 3: Maximum Cargo Tonnage (Limited by Hold Volume)**

$$M_{\text{cargo,volume}} = V_{\text{total}} \cdot \rho_{\text{cargo}}$$

$$M_{\text{cargo,volume}} = 60{,}760 \cdot 0.78 = 47{,}392.8 \text{ t}$$

---

**Step 4: Displacement at Permissible Draft**

The displacement depends on the underwater volume of the hull. We approximate using a rectangular block (with block coefficient correction) to obtain the displacement.

**First, estimate the underwater volume using a block coefficient estimate.** For a Handymax bulker, $C_B \approx 0.80$.

We need the principal dimensions. Assume:
- $L_{BP} = 165$ m
- $B = 25$ m
- $T = 11.0$ m

**Volumetric displacement:**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \cdot 165 \cdot 25 \cdot 11.0$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \cdot 165 \cdot 275$$

$$\nabla = 0.80 \cdot 45{,}375 = 36{,}300 \text{ m}^3$$

**Mass displacement:**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 36{,}300 = 37{,}207.5 \text{ t}$$

---

**Step 5: DWT at Permissible Draft**

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 37{,}207.5 - 8{,}500 = 28{,}707.5 \text{ t}$$

---

**Step 6: DWT Check — Will the Cargo Fit?**

$$M_{\text{cargo,volume}} = 47{,}392.8 \text{ t} \quad > \quad DWT = 28{,}707.5 \text{ t}$$

**Conclusion:** The cargo **cannot be fully loaded** to the brim of the holds. The vessel will be **draft-limited**, not volume-limited. This is typical of grain shipments on smaller bulkers.

**Actual maximum cargo tonnage (draft-limited):**

$$M_{\text{cargo,max}} = 28{,}707.5 \text{ t}$$

(We must also subtract fuel, lube oil, freshwater, provisions, crew — about 500 t — but for KTU we often ignore.)

$$M_{\text{cargo,max}} \approx 28{,}200 \text{ t}$$

---

**Step 7: Hopper Slope Angle**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{h_{\text{hopper}}}{w_{\text{hopper}}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3.5}{4.0}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.875) = 41.19° \approx 41.2°$$

**Interpretation:** The hopper slope of 41.2° is **excellent for self-trimming** (grain can flow freely) because it exceeds the angle of repose of wheat (~27°) and even iron ore (~35°).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bending Moment from Cargo Distribution

For a uniform load (cargo weight), the still-water bending moment at the midspan of a simply supported beam analogy is:

$$\begin{aligned}
M(x) &= \frac{w \cdot L}{2} \cdot x - \frac{w \cdot x^2}{2} \\
M_{\text{max}} \text{ at } x &= L/2: \\
M_{\text{max}} &= \frac{w \cdot L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{w \cdot (L/2)^2}{2} \\
&= \frac{w \cdot L^2}{4} - \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} \\
&= \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** This is the *uniform load* case. For a ship where buoyancy (w_b) and weight (w_w) both vary, the net bending moment is:

$$BM(x) = \int_0^x \left( w_b(\xi) - w_w(\xi) \right) (x - \xi) \, d\xi$$

In the **hogging** condition (cargo amidships, buoyancy at peaks), this reduces approximately to:

$$BM_{\text{hog}} \approx \frac{w_w \cdot L^2}{24}$$

In the **sagging** condition (cargo in peak tanks or empty holds, buoyancy in middle), the formula is different and depends on the loading pattern.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choice of Hatch Cover Type

| Hatch Cover Type | Opening Speed | Cost | Use Case | Maintenance |
|------------------|---------------|------|----------|-------------|
| **Single-pull (side-rolling)** | Moderate (1-2 min) | Low | Small Handysize | Low |
| **Pontoon (hinged lift)** | Slow (10-15 min) | Medium | Older bulkers | Moderate |
| **Sliding (hydraulic)** | Fast (1-2 min) | High | Modern Capesize | High (hydraulics) |
| **End-folding (Stulcken-type)** | Slow (5-10 min) | Medium | Specialized | Moderate |
| **Lift-away (MacGregor)** | Fast (crane lift) | High | Project cargo | Low |

---

## 3.5 Free Surface Effect Derivation

When a partially filled tank has a free liquid surface, the effective metacentric height is reduced:

$$GMT_{\text{eff}} = GMT - \delta GMT_{\text{FSE}}$$

Where the free surface correction is:

$$\delta GMT_{\text{FSE}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{liq}} \cdot i_{T,\text{tank}}}{\Delta}$$

And the transverse moment of inertia of the free surface area is:

$$i_T = \frac{L_{\text{tank}} \cdot B_{\text{tank}}^3}{12}$$

For a rectangular tank of length $L$ and breadth $B$:

$$i_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

> **Significance:** This is why partially filled ballast tanks are dangerous — they reduce the ship's righting moment and can cause dangerous rolling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
