<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define a Bulk Carrier. List the four main types of cargoes transported by bulk carriers.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (2 marks):** A bulk carrier is a single-deck, multi-purpose dry-cargo ship equipped with top-side tanks and hopper tanks within the cargo space area, designed primarily to carry unpackaged homogeneous dry bulk cargoes (per IACS and SOLAS Ch. XII).
> 
> **Four main cargoes (1 mark, 0.25 each):**
> 1. **Iron ore** (major dry bulk)
> 2. **Coal** (thermal and coking)
> 3. **Grain** (wheat, corn, soybean, barley)
> 4. **Bauxite / Alumina / Phosphate** (minor bulks)

---

### **Question 2: What is a "hopper tank" in a bulk carrier? State its primary functions.** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** A hopper tank is a V-shaped or sloped tank located at the lower port and starboard corners of a bulk carrier's cargo hold, typically inclined at **30° to 45°** from horizontal.
> 
> **Primary Functions (2 marks, 1 each):**
> 1. **Self-trimming of cargo:** The sloped geometry allows bulk cargo (especially grain) to flow freely toward the center of the hold, ensuring even distribution and compliance with the International Grain Code.
> 2. **Gravity-assisted discharge:** Cargo slides down the hopper slopes to the central hatch square, where grab cranes can easily extract it.
> 3. **Ballast capacity:** Hopper tanks also serve as ballast water tanks during the empty return voyage.
> 4. **Structural strength:** Hopper geometry increases the local section modulus at the lower corners, resisting bending stresses.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: Bulk Carrier Classification, Structural Features & Stability** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify bulk carriers by size with their DWT ranges, typical lengths, and trade routes. Draw a labeled cross-section of a single-deck bulk carrier showing all major structural elements.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- 5 holds, each of length 26 m, breadth 30 m, depth 18 m
- Hopper height = 3.0 m, hopper width = 4.5 m
- Cargo: Iron ore, density = 2.8 t/m³
- Draft = 14.5 m, LBP = 175 m, B = 26 m, C_B = 0.81
- LWT = 9,200 t, seawater density = 1.025 t/m³

**Calculate:**
(i) Effective cargo volume per hold
(ii) Total cargo tonnage (volume-limited)
(iii) Displacement and DWT
(iv) Identify which parameter (volume or DWT) limits the cargo loading

---

### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (a):**

**Classification Table (4 marks):**

| Class | DWT (t) | Length (m) | Trade |
|-------|---------|------------|-------|
| Handysize | 15k–35k | 120–150 | Minor ports |
| Handymax/Supramax | 35k–60k | 150–200 | Versatile |
| Panamax | 60k–80k | 220–230 | Old Panama Canal |
| Capesize | 100k–200k | 230–290 | Deep water |
| VLOC | 200k–400k | 300–360 | Long-haul ore |

**Cross-Section Diagram (3 marks):** A typical bulk carrier cross-section includes:
- Top-side tank (port + starboard)
- Hopper tank (port + starboard) at lower corners
- Double bottom tank
- Cargo hold (central rectangular space)
- Strength deck and inner bottom plating
- Side shell and bottom shell

*(See SECTION 4.2 for the equivalent Mermaid diagram — credit is given for the labeled schematic showing all 6+ elements: strength deck, top-side tank, hopper tank, double bottom, inner bottom, side shell.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A, Part (b):**

**Step (i) — Effective Cargo Volume per Hold (3 marks):**

Rectangular cargo volume per hold:
$$V_{\text{rect}} = 26 \times 30 \times 18 = 14{,}040 \text{ m}^3$$

Single hopper wedge volume:
$$V_{\text{hopper}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.5 \times 3.0 \times 26 = 0.5 \times 13.5 \times 26 = 175.5 \text{ m}^3$$

Two hoppers per hold:
$$V_{\text{hoppers}} = 2 \times 175.5 = 351 \text{ m}^3$$

Effective cargo volume per hold:
$$V_{\text{eff,hold}} = 14{,}040 - 351 = 13{,}689 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step (ii) — Total Cargo Tonnage (Volume-Limited) (1.5 marks):**

$$V_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 13{,}689 = 68{,}445 \text{ m}^3$$

$$M_{\text{cargo,vol}} = 68{,}445 \times 2.8 = 191{,}646 \text{ t}$$

**Step (iii) — Displacement and DWT (2 marks):**

Volumetric displacement:
$$\nabla = C_B \times L \times B \times T = 0.81 \times 175 \times 26 \times 14.5$$
$$\nabla = 0.81 \times 175 \times 377 = 0.81 \times 65{,}975 = 53{,}439.75 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass displacement:
$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 53{,}439.75 = 54{,}775.74 \text{ t}$$

DWT:
$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 54{,}775.74 - 9{,}200 = 45{,}575.74 \text{ t}$$

**Step (iv) — Limiting Parameter (0.5 marks):**

Since $M_{\text{cargo,vol}} = 191{,}646 \text{ t} \gg DWT = 45{,}575.74 \text{ t}$, the vessel is **DWT-limited** (draft-limited). The actual maximum cargo tonnage is approximately **45,000 t** (after subtracting consumables ~500 t).

> **Engineering Insight:** Iron ore is a **heavy bulk** (high density), so a Capesize-equivalent vessel is needed to make full use of the DWT. The holds are typically only partially filled with iron ore because the hold is enormous compared to the DWT capacity.

---

### **Question B: Bulk Carrier Strength & Hatch Cover Systems** [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the concepts of hogging and sagging in a bulk carrier. Why is sagging often the governing design condition for a bulk carrier? List the four main types of hatch covers used on bulk carriers with their relative advantages.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A Panamax bulk carrier has:
- Rule length L = 225 m, B = 32.2 m
- Block coefficient C_B = 0.82
- Lightweight = 11,500 t
- Design draft T = 14.0 m, ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³
- Required IACS section modulus coefficient C = 0.9
- Material factor K = 1.0 (HTS)

**Calculate:**
(i) The displacement at design draft
(ii) The minimum required section modulus at midship using the IACS empirical formula
(iii) Comment on whether this vessel meets typical Capesize structural requirements.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (a):**

**Hogging (1.5 marks):** When the ship is supported at the ends (by buoyancy in the bow and stern regions) but loaded in the middle (cargo concentrated amidships), the hull bends upward in the middle. The **deck is in tension** and the **bottom plating is in compression**.

**Sagging (1.5 marks):** When the ship is supported in the middle (buoyancy) but loaded at the ends (e.g., ballast in fore and aft peak tanks with empty holds), the hull bends downward in the middle. The **deck is in compression** and the **bottom is in tension**.

**Why Sagging Governs Bulk Carriers (2 marks):**
- Bulk carriers often sail in **ballast** with water in fore and aft peak tanks and possibly topside tanks
- Empty cargo holds reduce middle buoyancy, while heavy ballast weights at the ends create large sagging moments
- The deck is highly stressed in compression during sagging, which can cause **buckling** of the deck plating
- Sagging is generally the design condition for the **Hatch Coaming and Deck Plating** integrity

**Four Types of Hatch Covers (2 marks, 0.5 each):**
1. **Side-rolling (single pull)** — Simple, low cost, moderate speed (1-2 min). Used on small Handysize vessels.
2. **Pontoon (hinged lift-up)** — Old technology, slow (10-15 min), moderate cost. Used on older bulkers.
3. **Sliding panels (multi-panel, hydraulic)** — Fast opening (1-2 min), high cost. Used on modern Capesize and VLOC.
4. **End-folding (Stulcken telescopic)** — Compact storage, specialized use. Limited to specific vessel types.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B, Part (b):**

**Step (i) — Displacement at Design Draft (2 marks):**

Volumetric displacement:
$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T = 0.82 \times 225 \times 32.2 \times 14.0$$
$$\nabla = 0.82 \times 225 \times 450.8$$
$$\nabla = 0.82 \times 101{,}430 = 83{,}172.6 \text{ m}^3$$

Mass displacement:
$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 83{,}172.6 = 85{,}251.9 \text{ t}$$

**Step (ii) — Minimum Section Modulus (3 marks):**

IACS Empirical Formula:
$$Z_{\min} = C \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7) \cdot K$$

Substituting:
$$Z_{\min} = 0.9 \times (225)^2 \times 32.2 \times (0.82 + 0.7) \times 1.0$$

$$Z_{\min} = 0.9 \times 50{,}625 \times 32.2 \times 1.52$$

$$Z_{\min} = 0.9 \times 50{,}625 \times 48.944$$

$$Z_{\min} = 0.9 \times 2{,}477{,}790 = 2{,}230{,}011 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\min} \approx 2.23 \times 10^6 \text{ cm}^3}$$

**Step (iii) — Compliance Comment (2 marks):**

A Panamax bulker of L = 225 m typically has a **built section modulus of 2.5 to 3.0 × 10⁶ cm³** at midship. The minimum required value of 2.23 × 10⁶ cm³ is therefore **comfortably met** (with a margin of 10-35% above minimum). This margin is necessary because:
- Corrosion allowance (typically 1-2 mm)
- Higher stresses in seaway (dynamic wave bending moments can be 30-50% higher than still water)
- Fatigue considerations
- Future re-classification or service life extension

If $Z_{\text{built}} < Z_{\min}$, the vessel **does not meet** IACS class requirements and would not be permitted to operate with a Class certificate.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Bulk Carrier Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the hopper wedge volume deduction:** Many students compute the rectangular cargo hold volume ($L \times B \times D$) and use it directly, ignoring the V-shaped hopper deduction. This **overstates** cargo volume and **inflates** tonnage estimates. Always subtract $V_{\text{hoppers}}$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing ρ_cargo and ρ_seawater:** When calculating displacement, use ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³. When calculating cargo tonnage, use ρ_cargo (grain = 0.78, iron ore = 2.8, coal = 0.85). Mixing these up is a **classic 1-2 mark deduction**.
> 
> 3. **Wrong direction of hogging/sagging stress:** Deck is in **tension during hogging** and **compression during sagging**. Bottom is **compression during hogging** and **tension during sagging**. Reversed direction = 0 marks.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up free surface correction sign:** The free surface effect **reduces** GMT. It is subtracted: $GMT_{\text{eff}} = GMT - \delta GMT_{\text{FSE}}$. Adding instead of subtracting = 0 marks.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting the dynamic factor:** IACS bending moment calculations require multiplying still-water BM by a dynamic factor (typically 1.1-1.3) for wave-induced moments. Using only still-water BM gives non-conservative section modulus.
> 
> 6. **Unit errors in section modulus:** IACS formulas yield results in **cm³**, not m³. A student who reports 2.23 m³ instead of 2.23 × 10⁶ cm³ loses full credit.
> 
> 7. **Ignoring consumables:** When computing usable cargo tonnage from DWT, deduct ~500-1500 t for fuel, freshwater, lube oil, provisions, and crew effects. Forgetting this gives a slightly overestimated cargo figure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Bulk Carriers**
> 
> ✅ **Definition:** Single-deck ship with top-side tanks, hopper tanks, designed for dry bulk cargo (IACS / SOLAS Ch. XII)
> 
> ✅ **Five Size Classes:** Handysize, Handymax/Supramax, Panamax, Capesize, VLOC (memorize DWT ranges)
> 
> ✅ **Cross-Section Elements (in order from top to bottom):** Strength deck → Top-side tank → Cargo hold → Hopper tank → Double bottom → Inner bottom → Bottom shell → Keel
> 
> ✅ **Hopper Angle:** 30°–45° for self-trimming; must exceed angle of repose of cargo
> 
> ✅ **Stowage Factor:** $SF = V/M = 1/\rho_c$ (m³/t) — light cargoes have high SF, heavy cargoes have low SF
> 
> ✅ **Hogging:** Buoyancy at ends, weight in middle → deck in TENSION, bottom in COMPRESSION
> 
> ✅ **Sagging:** Buoyancy in middle, weight at ends → deck in COMPRESSION, bottom in TENSION
> 
> ✅ **Free Surface Effect:** Reduces GMT; formula $\delta GMT = \rho i_T / \Delta$
> 
> ✅ **IACS UR S11:** Empirical minimum section modulus formula $Z_{\min} = C \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7) \cdot K$
> 
> ✅ **Hatch Cover Types:** Single-pull side-rolling, Pontoon (hinged), Sliding multi-panel (hydraulic), End-folding (Stulcken)
> 
> ✅ **Major Cargoes:** Iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite, cement, sugar
> 
> ✅ **Block Coefficient:** 0.75 – 0.85 (full-form hull, slow speed)
> 
> ✅ **Service Speed:** 12 – 15 knots
> 
> ✅ **IACS Common Notations:** DNV, LR, ABS, BV, KR, Class NK — all require CSR-BC compliance
> 
> ✅ **Critical Regulations:** SOLAS Ch. XII, IACS UR S21 (CSR-BC), IACS UR S22 (Hold Mass), IACS UR S23 (Hatch Cover Loading)
> 
> ✅ **Limiting Conditions for Cargo Loading:** Either (a) hold volume limit OR (b) DWT/draft limit — whichever is lower governs
> 
> ✅ **Self-Trimming:** Essential for grain cargoes to comply with International Grain Code angle-of-heel criteria
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Container Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Container Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Container Ship** is a specialized cargo vessel designed to transport standardized intermodal containers (typically **20 ft** and **40 ft** ISO units) in dedicated cellular slots arranged both below deck (in holds) and on deck (stacked vertically). The vessel is optimized for high cargo density, rapid port turnaround, and minimal manual handling through the use of shore-based or onboard gantry cranes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** A container ship is a *cellular* vessel — its hull is subdivided into vertical guide cells that precisely fit ISO containers, eliminating the need for internal lashing in holds and enabling fast, mechanical stowage/unstowage.

**Standardised unit of measurement:**
- **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = 1 × 20 ft container (≈ **6.06 m long**)
- **FEU (Forty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = 1 × 40 ft container (≈ **12.19 m long**)
- **1 FEU = 2 TEU**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Floating Lego Truck":**
> Imagine a giant multi-storey parking garage built onto a ship's hull, where every "parking slot" is engineered to perfectly fit a rectangular steel box (the container). The ship doesn't carry loose cargo anymore — it carries *uniform building blocks* that can be lifted straight off by cranes and dropped onto trucks or trains. This is exactly how a container ship works: it is essentially a *floating, sea-going, multi-storey garage for standardized steel boxes*.

## 1.3 Standard ISO Container Dimensions (Reference Data)

| Container Type | Length | Width | Height | Nominal Mass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1 TEU (20 ft)** | **6.06 m** | 2.44 m | 2.59 m | ~ 24 t (max payload ~ 21 t) |
| **1 FEU (40 ft)** | **12.19 m** | 2.44 m | 2.59 m | ~ 30 t (max payload ~ 26 t) |
| **40 ft HC (High Cube)** | 12.19 m | 2.44 m | **2.90 m** | ~ 30 t |
| **45 ft HC** | 13.72 m | 2.44 m | 2.90 m | ~ 27 t |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For all KTU numerical problems, always use **TEU** as the unit of capacity. Capacity (in TEU) is the *primary* descriptor of a container ship (not DWT, which is secondary for tankers/bulkers).

## 1.4 Ship Size Classification (KTU Convention)

- **Feeder:** up to **1,000 TEU**
- **Feedermax:** 1,000 – **2,000 TEU**
- **Panamax:** 2,000 – **5,100 TEU** (constrained by old Panama Canal locks)
- **Post-Panamax:** **5,100 – 10,000 TEU**
- **New Panamax (Neo-Panamax):** 10,000 – **14,000 TEU** (fits expanded Panama locks)
- **ULCV (Ultra Large Container Vessel):** **14,000 TEU and above**
- **Megamax / Triple-E class:** **18,000 – 24,000+ TEU**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Container Ships

A container ship exists because of the **intermodal transport revolution**. Three engineering drivers govern its design:

1. **Standardisation (ISO 668):** Containers are uniform, so holds can be shaped as vertical *cells* of fixed dimensions.
2. **Speed of cargo handling:** Crane moves replace gangs of longshoremen. Ship earns more if it spends more time at sea, less in port.
3. **Economy of scale:** Larger ships have lower TEU-mile cost, but are constrained by channel depth, port cranes, and hull strength.

## 2.2 Principal Particulars of a Container Ship

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify and relate the following particulars:

- **LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars):** design length of hull
- **LWL (Length at Waterline):** actual waterline length
- **B (Breadth, moulded):** maximum internal width — *critical for stowage* (rows of containers are placed athwartships)
- **D (Depth, moulded):** distance from keel to main deck
- **T (Design Draft):** maximum loaded draft
- **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage):** cargo + fuel + water + crew + stores
- **TEU Capacity:** nominal container slots
- **Service Speed (V):** design cruising speed (typical: **18 – 25 knots**)

## 2.3 Cellular Hold Geometry — The "How"

- Containers in a hold are stacked in **transverse bays**.
- Each bay is typically **20 ft long** (i.e. 1 TEU wide longitudinally), so the cargo hold length = (number of 20-ft bays) × **6.06 m**.
- Transversely, containers are placed side-by-side, separated by **vertical corner posts** of the container itself.
- Cell guides (steel angles/T-sections welded vertically in the hold) guide containers into position and prevent transverse shifting.

> [!NOTE]
> **On-deck stacks** are usually lashed with **twist locks, bridges, and lashing rods** to withstand roll accelerations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula the examiner may ask in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions on container ships.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TEU Capacity (rows × tiers × bays) | $C_{TEU} = N_{rows} \times N_{tiers} \times N_{bays}$ | TEU | Each bay = 1 TEU longitudinally |
| 2 | Bay length | $L_{bay} = 6.06\,\text{m}$ (nominal 20 ft) | m | ISO standard |
| 3 | Number of 20-ft bays | $N_{bays} = \dfrac{L_{BP} - L_{engine\,room}}{L_{bay}}$ | — | Subtract engine room length |
| 4 | Slot utilisation factor | $\eta_{slot} = \dfrac{\text{Loaded TEU}}{\text{Nominal TEU capacity}}$ | — | Typically 0.80 – 0.95 |
| 5 | Deadweight to TEU ratio (typical) | $\dfrac{DWT}{TEU} \approx 12\ \text{to}\ 16\ \text{t/TEU}$ | t/TEU | Used to estimate capacity from DWT |
| 6 | Slot weight (homogeneous stow) | $W_{slot} = \dfrac{DWT_{cargo}}{N_{TEU}}$ | t/TEU | Average payload per slot |
| 7 | Stowage plan check | $\sum m_{containers} \le DWT_{cargo}$ | t | Total mass must not exceed DWT |
| 8 | Block coefficient (typical) | $C_B = 0.55\ \text{to}\ 0.65$ | — | Lower than tankers (high speed) |
| 9 | Length-to-beam ratio | $L/B \approx 6\ \text{to}\ 8$ | — | For container ships |
| 10 | Beam-to-depth ratio | $B/D \approx 1.8\ \text{to}\ 2.4$ | — | Wide beam to fit many rows |
| 11 | Transverse metacentre above KG (stability) | $GM = KM - KG$ | m | Must remain **> 0.15 m** positive |
| 12 | Free surface effect correction | $\Delta GM = -\dfrac{\rho_{liq} \cdot i}{W}$ | m | $i$ = moment of inertia of free surface |
| 13 | Hull steel weight (approx) | $W_{steel} = k \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot D$ | t | $k \approx 0.045$ to $0.08$ |
| 14 | Number of containers athwartships (rows) | $N_{rows} = \dfrac{B - 2\,b_{margin}}{b_c + 2\,t_{cell\,guide}}$ | — | $b_c$ = container breadth = 2.44 m |

**Critical KTU formula — Transverse stability of a container stack:**

The heeling moment from a stack of containers of total mass $M$ stacked with their centers of gravity at average height $\bar{h}$ above the keel is:

$$M_{heel} = M \cdot g \cdot \bar{h} \cdot \sin\phi$$

The righting moment of the ship is $W \cdot GM \cdot \sin\phi$. For equilibrium (or stability limit):

$$M \cdot g \cdot \bar{h} \le W \cdot GM \quad \Rightarrow \quad \bar{h} \le \dfrac{W \cdot GM}{M \cdot g}$$

This is why **high stacks of containers** (e.g. on deck) demand either **ballast** or **KG reduction** by lowering the stack base.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Shipyards use these formulae to size the engine room, hatch covers, and cell guides.
- Naval architects use the **DWT/TEU ratio** to classify the trade route a ship is suited for.
- Port authorities use **beam and air-draft limits** to decide which berths a vessel can use (e.g. **Panamax beam ≤ 32.31 m**).
- Stability software (e.g. **POSEIDON, NAUT-S, Maxsurf**) uses the **GM** criterion for intact stability per **IMO A.749**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TEU Capacity from Principal Particulars

**Problem (KTU-style, 14 marks):**  
A container ship has LBP = 240 m, breadth B = 32.2 m, depth D = 19.5 m, design draft T = 12.0 m, and an engine room of length 30 m located aft. The hold bay length is 6.06 m. The number of container rows athwartships is 17, and containers are stacked up to 4 tiers below deck plus 5 tiers on deck (on-deck stack rests on hatch covers). Calculate:
- (a) The number of 20-ft longitudinal bays in the cargo region (7 marks)
- (b) The total nominal TEU capacity (7 marks)

### Solution (a): Number of 20-ft Bays

The cargo region excludes the engine room at the stern.

$$L_{cargo} = LBP - L_{engine\,room} = 240\,\text{m} - 30\,\text{m} = 210\,\text{m}$$

Each 20-ft bay occupies $L_{bay} = 6.06\,\text{m}$.

$$N_{bays} = \dfrac{L_{cargo}}{L_{bay}} = \dfrac{210\,\text{m}}{6.06\,\text{m}} = 34.65$$

Since we cannot have a fractional bay in a cellular ship, we take the floor (the last partial bay is unusable for standard 20-ft containers):

$$N_{bays} = \lfloor 34.65 \rfloor = 34\ \text{bays}$$

### Solution (b): Total Nominal TEU Capacity

Each bay can hold one row of containers per tier. With **17 rows athwartships**:

$$\text{TEU per tier (below deck)} = 17 \times 34 = 578\ \text{TEU}$$

With 4 tiers below deck:

$$TEU_{below} = 578 \times 4 = 2312\ \text{TEU}$$

On deck, the on-deck stack typically covers the full hatch length but with **1 row fewer** (for lashing walkways) — assume 16 rows:

$$TEU_{on\,deck} = 16 \times 34 \times 5 = 2720\ \text{TEU}$$

Total:

$$TEU_{total} = 2312 + 2720 = 5032\ \text{TEU}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: ~5,032 TEU** — this places the vessel in the **Panamax** class (just under the 5,100 TEU Panama Canal limit). Reducing on-deck rows to 16 is consistent with realistic hatch-coverage.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — TEU Capacity from DWT (Cross-check)

Use the rule of thumb $\dfrac{DWT}{TEU} \approx 14\ \text{t/TEU}$.

Assume a DWT of **70,000 t**:

$$TEU \approx \dfrac{70{,}000}{14} = 5{,}000\ \text{TEU}$$

This matches the geometric result above (5,032 TEU). The **5 – 10 % difference** is acceptable; in real design, the average slot payload is lower than the maximum.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Stability Check (Conceptual, 7 marks)

A container ship of displacement $W = 80{,}000\,\text{t}$ has KG = 12 m and KM = 12.20 m. A deck stack of 500 TEU has average stack height $\bar{h} = 28\,\text{m}$ above keel, total mass $M = 500 \times 14 = 7{,}000\,\text{t}$.

$$GM = KM - KG = 12.20 - 12.00 = 0.20\,\text{m}$$

The righting moment per unit heel angle ≈ $W \cdot GM = 80{,}000 \times 0.20 = 16{,}000\,\text{t·m}$.

The heeling moment per unit heel angle from the on-deck stack:

$$M_{heel} = M \cdot \bar{h} = 7{,}000 \times 28 = 196{,}000\,\text{t·m}$$

**The stack heeling moment is far larger than the ship's righting moment — instability!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design mitigation:** Lower the stack (reduce $\bar{h}$), add ballast water, or redistribute containers in lower holds to raise effective GM. This is *exactly* why loading computer software is mandatory on container ships (per SOLAS Ch. VI / IMO CSS Code).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Design Variable

| Design Variable | Effect on TEU Capacity | Effect on Stability | Effect on Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Length (LBP) | ↑ Strongly | Slight ↑ | ↑ Power needed |
| ↑ Beam (B) | ↑↑ (more rows) | ↑ (wider waterplane) | ↑ but allows speed |
| ↑ Draft (T) | ↑ (more tiers in hold) | Slight ↑ | ↑ Skin friction |
| ↑ Block coefficient | ↓ (slower ships slimmer) | — | ↑ Resistance at speed |
| ↑ On-deck tiers | ↑↑ Capacity | ↓↓ **Severe loss of GM** | Negligible |
| ↑ Container mass per slot | — | ↓ (heavier high stack) | ↓ (deeper draft) |
| ↑ Freeboard | ↑ Deck tiers possible | ↑ But raises windage | ↑ Wind resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Container Ship Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Container Ship System] --> B[Hull Structure]
    A --> C[Propulsion Plant]
    A --> D[Cargo Containment System]
    A --> E[Auxiliary Systems]

    B --> B1[Double Bottom]
    B --> B2[Side Shell]
    B --> B3[Deck Plate]
    B --> B4[Bulkheads]

    C --> C1[Main Engine]
    C --> C2[Gearbox]
    C --> C3[Propeller]
    C --> C4[Shaft Line]

    D --> D1[Cargo Holds]
    D --> D2[Cell Guides]
    D --> D3[Hatch Covers]
    D --> D4[On Deck Stack]
    D --> D5[Bay Structure]

    D1 --> D1a[Below Deck TEU]
    D4 --> D4a[On Deck TEU]
    D5 --> D5a[Number of 20 ft Bays]

    E --> E1[Bunker Fuel System]
    E --> E2[Ballast System]
    E --> E3[Cargo Crane or Shore Crane Interface]
    E --> E4[Ventilation Reefer Sockets]
```

## 4.2 Container Bay Layout (Transverse View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1[Tier 1 Hold] --> H2[Tier 2 Hold]
    H2 --> H3[Tier 3 Hold]
    H3 --> H4[Tier 4 Hold]
    H4 --> DK[Hatch Cover]
    DK --> T1[Tier 5 On Deck]
    T1 --> T2[Tier 6 On Deck]
    T2 --> T3[Tier 7 On Deck]
    T3 --> T4[Tier 8 On Deck]
    T4 --> T5[Tier 9 On Deck]
    TT[Top of Stack Bridge Fittings] --- T5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each box is a single 20-ft or 40-ft container stacked vertically. The hatch cover is the **water-tight boundary** between below-deck tiers and on-deck tiers. On-deck stacks are exposed to wind, weather, and roll accelerations — therefore they use **automatic twist-locks and lashing bridges** instead of cell guides.

## 4.3 Logical Stowage Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Stability Check] --> S2{Stability OK}
    S2 -- No --> S3[Adjust Ballast or Re Stow]
    S3 --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S4[Allocate Containers to Bay Plan]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Slot Weights from BAPLIE File]
    S5 --> S6[Output Loading Plan]
    S6 --> S7[Master Approves Plan]
    S7 --> S8[Loading Computer Final Check]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a container ship. What is meant by the term "TEU"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **container ship** is a cargo vessel specifically designed with cellular holds and guide structures to carry **ISO-standard intermodal containers** stowed both below deck and on deck, loaded and discharged by crane with minimal manual handling. (2 marks)

**TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** is the standard unit of measure for a container ship's capacity, equal to the volume of one **20 ft × 8 ft × 8 ft 6 in** ISO container. Container ship capacity is quoted in TEU, where **1 FEU = 2 TEU**. (1 mark)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any four principal particulars of a container ship and state the typical block coefficient range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Principal particulars: **LBP, B (breadth), D (depth), T (draft), DWT, TEU capacity, service speed**. (2 marks)

Typical **block coefficient $C_B$** for container ships = **0.55 to 0.65**, lower than tankers because container ships operate at higher Froude numbers and need finer hull forms for speed. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE 2024 scheme: Part B carries 14 marks. Two alternatives are offered — students answer **one**. Each alternative here is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** with full model solutions.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A(a). Explain with a sketch the cellular hold structure of a container ship. List the main longitudinal and transverse members used. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **cellular hold** of a container ship is a vertical guide system built of transverse **bulkheads spaced at 6.06 m (1 TEU bay length)** and longitudinal **corrugated bulkheads / cell guides** spaced to fit the **2.44 m breadth of a container plus a small clearance (≈ 50 mm)** for placement. (2 marks)

**Main structural members (Longitudinal):**  
- Keel plate  
- Bottom longitudinals (in double bottom)  
- Side shell longitudinals  
- Deck longitudinals  
- Inner bottom plating  
- Hatch coaming longitudinals  (2.5 marks)

**Main structural members (Transverse):**  
- Transverse web frames in holds  
- Transverse bulkheads (form the cell walls)  
- Transverse deck beams  
- Hold end bulkheads (collision bulkhead forward)  (2.5 marks)

**Functional purpose:** Cell guides align and support the containers in the hold; no lashing is required below deck because each container is restrained by 4 corner posts locked into neighbouring corner castings. This makes stowage rapid and structurally efficient.

**Diagram (sketch description):** Transverse section through hold showing 4 rows of 20-ft containers restrained by vertical cell guides, with hatch cover on top and double bottom below.

---

**A(b). A Panamax container ship has the following particulars:**  
LBP = 245 m, B = 32.2 m, D = 19.5 m, T = 12.0 m, $C_B$ = 0.60, engine room length 28 m, fuel + stores mass 5,000 t, crew + provisions 200 t. The number of 20-ft bays in cargo region is 36, with 16 athwartship rows. 4 tiers below deck, 5 tiers on deck (16 rows on deck). ISO container slot weight limit = 24 t (TEU) and 30 t (FEU). 

**Calculate:**  
(i) Total nominal TEU capacity (4 marks)  
(ii) Maximum cargo DWT available (3 marks)  
(iii) Average slot weight if loaded to 90 % capacity with homogeneous 20-ft containers (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total TEU Capacity (4 marks):**

Below deck TEU: $16 \times 36 \times 4 = 2304\ \text{TEU}$  
(We use 16 rows for below deck as well — the 17th row would interfere with cell guide clearances; designer's choice — accept either if justified.)

On deck TEU: $16 \times 36 \times 5 = 2880\ \text{TEU}$

$$TEU_{total} = 2304 + 2880 = 5184\ \text{TEU}$$

(Acceptable band: 5,000 – 5,200 TEU)

**(ii) Maximum Cargo DWT (3 marks):**

Cargo DWT = Lightweight margin is implicit; here DWT is *not* given directly, so compute from displacement:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot LBP \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 1.025 \times 245 \times 32.2 \times 12.0 \times 0.60$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 56{,}834 = 58{,}255\ \text{t}$$

$$DWT_{total} = \Delta - LWT$$

Assuming LWT (lightweight) ≈ 15 % of Δ: $LWT \approx 8{,}740\ \text{t}$.

$$DWT \approx 58{,}255 - 8{,}740 = 49{,}515\ \text{t}$$

Maximum cargo DWT = DWT − fuel − stores − crew:

$$DWT_{cargo} = 49{,}515 - 5{,}000 - 200 = 44{,}315\ \text{t}$$

**(iii) Average slot weight (3 marks):**

Loaded TEU = $0.90 \times 5184 = 4666\ \text{TEU}$

$$W_{slot} = \dfrac{DWT_{cargo}}{TEU_{loaded}} = \dfrac{44{,}315}{4{,}666} \approx 9.50\ \text{t/TEU}$$

**Sanity check:** This is well below the 24 t ISO limit, indicating a *light-cargo* stow (typical for consumer goods).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**B(a). With a suitable sketch, describe the general arrangement of a container ship, indicating the locations of: forecastle, engine room, accommodation block, cellular holds, hatch covers, and lashing bridges on deck. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

A modern container ship has a distinctive general arrangement that differs markedly from a conventional cargo ship:

1. **Forecastle (bow):** Long, flared forward section often with bulbous bow. (0.5 mark)
2. **Cargo region (midships + aft):** Long cellular holds (typically 4–6 holds) covered by **hatch covers**; containers stacked on deck above. (1.5 marks)
3. **Hatch covers:** Large steel covers spanning the hold openings; sized to accommodate a row of containers; modern designs include **pontoon-type or folding covers**. (1 mark)
4. **Aft region:** Engine room, funnel, and **accommodation block** raised high to give a clear view over the foredeck containers. The accommodation is typically **forward of the funnel but aft of the holds** (or, in some designs, right aft). (1.5 marks)
5. **Lashing bridges:** Steel walkways and platforms mounted on top of on-deck container stacks to allow lashing crews to reach twist-locks. (1 mark)
6. **Steering gear and rudder:** Far aft, behind the engine room. (0.5 mark)
7. **Bulbous bow:** Reduces wave-making resistance at service speed. (1 mark)

**Sketch (description):** A profile view of the ship with bow on the left and stern on the right, marking the above features with arrows and labels.

---

**B(b). A 6,000-TEU container ship has displacement Δ = 70,000 t and KG = 11.5 m. The metacentre KM = 12.0 m. The ship is to load 1,000 TEU on deck with average stack height (centroid above keel) $\bar{h}$ = 26 m and slot weight 12 t/TEU. Neglect free-surface effect. (7 marks)**

**Determine:**  
(i) Initial GM (2 marks)  
(ii) Heeling moment per unit angle from the on-deck stack (3 marks)  
(iii) Is the ship stable against this moment? Justify with calculation. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Initial GM (2 marks):**

$$GM = KM - KG = 12.0 - 11.5 = 0.5\ \text{m}$$

**(ii) Heeling moment from on-deck stack (3 marks):**

$$M_{stack} = m \cdot \bar{h} \cdot g$$

$$m = 1{,}000 \times 12 = 12{,}000\ \text{t}$$

$$M_{stack} = 12{,}000 \times 26 = 312{,}000\ \text{t·m per unit sin φ}$$

Equivalent virtual rise in KG due to the on-deck stack (the "free surface" of solids in stability analysis):

$$\Delta KG_{virtual} = \dfrac{m \cdot \bar{h}}{W} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \times 26}{70{,}000} = 4.46\ \text{m}$$

**(iii) Stability check (2 marks):**

$$GM_{effective} = 0.5 - 4.46 = -3.96\ \text{m}$$

Since $GM_{effective} < 0$, the ship is **unstable** with this on-deck load. (0.5 mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **Real design action:** Add ballast water, or load more containers below deck first (to lower KG), or reduce stack height, or refuse the booking. The **loading computer** is mandatory and will block this stow. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in container-ship problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong unit — TEU vs FEU confusion:** A 40-ft container is 2 TEU, not 1. Always state the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the engine room:** Number of bays is *not* $LBP / 6.06$ — you must subtract the engine room length.
> 3. **Block coefficient used as DWT:** $C_B$ is a hull-form coefficient, **not** a tonnage. Use $\Delta = \rho LBT C_B$.
> 4. **On-deck rows ≠ below-deck rows:** Designers often drop 1 row on deck for lashing walkways — read the problem carefully.
> 5. **Stability question: forgetting the virtual-KG correction.** The on-deck stack effectively raises KG by $\dfrac{m \bar{h}}{W}$, which is the most heavily tested item in Part B.
> 6. **Confusing LBP with LOA:** Length Overall (LOA) includes the bow overhang; use **LBP** in $C_B$ formula.
> 7. **Mixing LWT and DWT:** DWT includes *all* variable load (cargo + fuel + water + crew). Cargo DWT = DWT − (fuel + water + stores + crew).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Container Ships**
>
> - ☐ A container ship is a **cellular vessel** designed for **ISO intermodal containers**.
> - ☐ Capacity is always quoted in **TEU** (1 TEU = 1 × 20 ft container; 1 FEU = 2 TEU).
> - ☐ ISO container dimensions: 20 ft = 6.06 m, 40 ft = 12.19 m, breadth = 2.44 m, height = 2.59 m (or 2.90 m HC).
> - ☐ Bay length = 6.06 m; number of 20-ft bays = (LBP − L_engine_room) / 6.06.
> - ☐ TEU = rows × tiers × bays.
> - ☐ Below-deck tiers use cell guides; on-deck tiers use twist-locks + lashing bridges.
> - ☐ Block coefficient $C_B$ ≈ 0.55 – 0.65 (lower than tankers, higher than fast ferries).
> - ☐ Typical L/B ≈ 6 – 8; B/D ≈ 1.8 – 2.4 (wide beam to fit many rows).
> - ☐ DWT/TEU ratio ≈ 12 – 16 t/TEU is a useful rule of thumb.
> - ☐ **Stability is the critical design constraint:** on-deck stacks raise virtual KG by $\dfrac{m \bar{h}}{W}$.
> - ☐ $GM_{effective} = GM_{initial} - \dfrac{m \bar{h}}{W} - \dfrac{\rho i}{W}$ — must remain positive.
> - ☐ Panamax beam limit ≈ 32.31 m; ULCV class ≥ 14,000 TEU; Megamax ≥ 18,000 TEU.
> - ☐ Container ship loading is governed by **IMO CSS Code (Code of Safe Practice for Cargo Stowage and Securing)** and **SOLAS Ch. VI**.
> - ☐ Loading computers are **mandatory** for any vessel carrying containers on deck.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ro-Ro Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ro-Ro Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Roll-on/Roll-off (Ro-Ro) Ship:** A specialized merchant vessel designed for the carriage of **wheeled cargo** (cars, trucks, trailers, tractors, military vehicles) in which the cargo is **rolled on and rolled off the vessel** using built-in ramps, rather than being lifted on and off by cranes. The cargo space is a multi-deck box-like superstructure with internal ramps connecting the weather deck to the lower holds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ro-Ro ships belong to the family of "special-purpose vessel design" and require distinct treatment of **stability**, **internal ramp geometry**, **vehicle securing**, and **fire safety** (SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 20) — none of which apply to conventional dry cargo ships.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey floating car park** that has a giant **garage door at the back**. Instead of drivers parking in the building, the entire building *floats across the ocean* and the cars simply drive in and out under their own power.

- **Cars drive IN** → Stern door opens → internal ramps → lower decks → parked.
- **Cars drive OUT** → Reverse process at the destination port.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Conventional cargo ships need expensive shore cranes and longshoremen. Ro-Ro ships eliminate crane dependency, drastically reducing **port turnaround time** from days to hours. This is why every modern car export/import operation (e.g., Hyundai from South Korea to Europe) uses Ro-Ro ships.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deadweight | DWT | 5,000 – 70,000 t | Car carriers = upper end |
| Number of car decks | — | 8 – 12 | Lifts/ramps connect them |
| Service speed | V | 18 – 22 knots | PCTCs are fast |
| Stern ramp capacity | — | 60 – 200 t | For tracked vehicles |
| Lane length | LL | ~ 4.5 m per car | One lane = 1 row of cars |
| Lane width | LW | ~ 2.1 – 2.5 m | Per standard car |
| Cargo deck height | — | 1.9 – 2.1 m | Internal clearance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unit **lane-metre (lm)** is the KTU-standard metric for measuring Ro-Ro capacity: $1 \text{ lm} = 1 \text{ metre of lane length}$. Total lane length = number of lanes × length of lane.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ro-Ro Ships

Ro-Ro vessels are not a single type — KTU expects you to know the **four major sub-categories**:

1. **Pure Car Carriers (PCCs)** – Carry only cars; small, fast, often multi-deck (8–12 decks).
2. **Pure Car & Truck Carriers (PCTCs)** – Carry cars **and** trucks/trailers; the most common type in global trade.
3. **Con-Ro Ships** – Hybrid **Container + Ro-Ro**; cellular holds aft for containers and Ro-Ro decks forward.
4. **Ro-Pax Ferries** – Carry **passengers and Ro-Ro cargo** simultaneously (e.g., cross-Channel ferries).

## 2.2 Fundamental Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Ro-Ro ship is, in essence, a **box on a hull**. The hull form is optimized for **propulsion efficiency**, while the **box superstructure** is maximized for **cargo volume**. The design conflict (stability vs. tall box) is the central engineering challenge.

The design follows these hierarchical steps:
- **Step 1 — Lane Area Determination:** Estimate cargo volume from trade route analysis. Number of vehicles × lane area per vehicle.
- **Step 2 — Deck Configuration:** Decide number of decks (typically 4–6 fixed + 2–6 hoistable). Hoistable decks use **lifts** to maximize flexibility.
- **Step 3 — Ramp Selection:** Choose **stern ramp**, **side ramp**, or **bow ramp** based on port infrastructure.
- **Step 4 — Stability Check:** Verify **GM (metacentric height)** against IMO weather criterion.
- **Step 5 — Securing System:** Design **lashing points**, **D-rings**, and **wheel chocks** as per CSS Code.

## 2.3 Types of Access Ramps

- **Stern Ramp (Quarter Ramp):** Most common; pivots about a hinge at the upper deck edge.
- **Bow Ramp:** Used where the vessel berths bow-first (e.g., some PCTCs); can be a visor or split.
- **Side Ramp (Side Door):** Used on small ferries where quay height matches deck level.
- **Internal Ramps:** Fixed or adjustable, connecting successive decks inside the ship.
- **Lift / Elevator Deck:** Hydraulic platform raises cars to upper decks (eliminates fixed ramps).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-world utility:** Stern ramps are usually designed for a **quay-deck height of 1.5–3.5 m above waterline** and must be capable of **rapid deployment** (full open/close cycle in ~ 2 minutes for commercial Ro-Ro).

## 2.4 Critical Stability & Safety Considerations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Unlike a normal ship where GM is the only stability parameter, a Ro-Ro ship must satisfy **SOLAS II-1 Regulation 22** (heeling moment from vehicle loads), **II-1 Reg 23** (transverse stability with free surface), and **II-2 Reg 20** (fire safety with water-spray system). Examiner WILL test these.

Key hazards:
- **Free Surface Effect (FSE):** Vehicles are loose masses; liquid in partially-filled tanks adds to FSE. Combined effect can reduce **GM by 30–50%**.
- **Vehicle Heeling Moment:** If all trucks load on one side, an unbalanced moment is created.
- **Fires:** Vehicle fires are catastrophic; hence **water-spray deluge systems** and **smoke detectors** every 20 m are mandatory.
- **Watertight Integrity:** Bow/side/stern doors must seal against a **head of water** of up to the bulkhead deck.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{\text{deck}} = N_c \times a_c$ | Required deck area (cars × area per car) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | $LL_{\text{total}} = N_L \times L_L$ | Total lane length = lanes × lane length | lm (lane-metre) |
| 3 | $\theta_{\text{ramp}} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_{\text{deck}} - H_{\text{quay}}}{L_{\text{ramp}}}\right)$ | Stern ramp angle to quay | degrees |
| 4 | $\Delta GM = -\dfrac{\rho_s \cdot i}{\Delta}$ | Reduction in GM from free surface | m |
| 5 | $M_{\text{heel}} = W \times b$ | Heeling moment from off-centre load | t·m |
| 6 | $P_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{veh}}}{A_{\text{axle}}}$ | Deck load pressure (axle load) | $\text{t/m}^2$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\text{ramp}} = \dfrac{M_{\max}}{Z}$ | Bending stress in ramp structure | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 8 | $t_{\text{drain}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{water}}}{Q_{\text{pump}}}$ | Time to drain accumulated water | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All structural members of a Ro-Ro ramp must satisfy **classification society rules** (DNV, LR, BV, ABS). For KTU problems, use a **uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 3.5 t/m²** unless otherwise specified.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Logistics:** PCTCs transport ~ 25 million cars annually worldwide.
- **Military Sealift:** Ro-Ros carry tanks, helicopters, and troops; the **US Military Sealift Command** operates large Ro-Ros.
- **Ferry Industry:** Cross-Channel ferries (Dover–Calais) are Ro-Pax units carrying 200+ cars per voyage.
- **Short-Sea Shipping:** EU promotes Ro-Ro as an eco-friendly alternative to trucks (Ro-La, "rolling highway").
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stern Ramp Angle to Quay

**Problem Setup:** A Ro-Ro ship is berthed at a quay. The **main deck (vehicle deck) is at height $H_d$** above the waterline, and the **quay top is at height $H_q$** above the waterline. The **stern ramp** has a horizontal projection of length $L_r$ from the ship's transom to the quay face. Find the **ramp angle** $\theta$.

**Geometric Model:**

```
       Ship (stern)                 Quay
   |--------L_r--------|  <-- quay face
   *------------------+____
                       \
                        \  ramp
                         \
                          \____
   H_d  =================  <- main deck level
                          |\
   H_q  -----------------  <- quay level
   WL   -----------------  <- waterline
```

The ramp forms a **right-angled triangle** with:
- Horizontal side = $L_r$
- Vertical side = $H_d - H_q$
- Ramp angle = $\theta$

By trigonometry of the right triangle:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \dfrac{H_d - H_q}{L_r}
$$

Solving for the angle:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left( \dfrac{H_d - H_q}{L_r} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Bench-Mark Result:** A typical Ro-Ro has $H_d = 8 \text{ m}$, $H_q = 3 \text{ m}$, $L_r = 10 \text{ m}$, giving $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57^\circ$. Anything above **15°** is a steep ramp and **slows cargo handling** significantly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Free Surface Effect on GM

**Problem Setup:** A Ro-Ro ship has a **partially filled ballast tank** with free surface length $l$ and breadth $b$. Find the **virtual loss in metacentric height** $\Delta GM$.

**Starting Point — Principle of Virtual Mass:**

When a ship rolls, a partially filled liquid sloshes. The sloshing mass can be modeled as a **virtual rise in the center of gravity** $\Delta GG_1$. This rise, by the standard naval architecture relation, **reduces GM by exactly the same amount**.

For a rectangular free surface of length $l$ and breadth $b$, the **transverse moment of inertia** of the free surface is:

$$
i_T = \dfrac{l \cdot b^3}{12}
$$

The **virtual rise of G** is:

$$
\Delta GG_1 = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot i_T}{\Delta}
$$

where:
- $\rho_s$ = mass density of the liquid (tonnes per m³, e.g., **1.025 t/m³** for seawater, **1.0 t/m³** for freshwater)
- $\Delta$ = ship displacement (tonnes)

Because $GM_{eff} = GM_{rigid} - \Delta GG_1$, we get:

$$
\boxed{\;GM_{eff} = GM_{rigid} - \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot i_T}{\Delta}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** If multiple tanks have free surface (e.g., bilge wells, sewage tanks, partially filled freshwater tanks), the losses are **additive**:
> $$\Delta GM_{total} = \sum_k \dfrac{\rho_{s,k} \cdot i_{T,k}}{\Delta}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Vehicle Heeling Moment

**Problem Setup:** A Ro-Ro ship is being loaded. A convoy of trucks of total weight $W$ tonnes is parked at a transverse distance $b$ metres from the **centerline** (C/L) of the ship, all on the same side. Find the **transverse heeling moment**.

**Moment Definition:**

A heeling moment is the product of a weight and its **horizontal lever** from the centerline of flotation (which, in still water, is the C/L).

$$
M_{\text{heel}} = W \times b
$$

where:
- $W$ = total vehicle weight in tonnes
- $b$ = transverse distance from C/L in metres
- $M_{\text{heel}}$ has units of **t·m** (tonne-metres)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Loading is balanced by **two equal opposing moments** or by **ballast**. A single-sided load of 500 t at 4 m off-center creates $M_{\text{heel}} = 2000$ t·m. SOLAS requires that this moment be neutralized or compensated within **30 minutes** of the event.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Deck Plate Load Distribution

**Problem Setup:** A truck of total laden weight $W_{\text{truck}} = 32$ t is supported on a 3-axle bogie. The contact patch of each axle is $A_c = 0.4 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m}$. Find the **local deck pressure**.

**Logic:** Load is distributed to **3 axles** (assumed equal share for static analysis). Per-axle load:

$$
W_{\text{axle}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{truck}}}{3} = \dfrac{32}{3} \approx 10.667 \text{ t}
$$

Per-axle contact area:

$$
A_c = 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.24 \text{ m}^2
$$

Local pressure on deck:

$$
P_{\text{deck}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{axle}}}{A_c} = \dfrac{10.667}{0.24} \approx 44.44 \text{ t/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Converting to standard SI units for material strength checks:** $1 \text{ t/m}^2 = 9.81 \text{ kPa}$. So $P_{\text{deck}} \approx 435.8 \text{ kPa}$. The deck plate must be checked against this local pressure plus the **dynamic amplification factor (DAF ≈ 1.3 to 1.8)** for ship motions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A Ro-Ro ship has a main deck height of 9.0 m above the waterline. It berths at a quay of height 3.0 m above the waterline. The stern ramp has a horizontal projection of 12 m from the transom to the quay face. Find: (a) the ramp angle, and (b) whether a car with a 15° approach angle can climb it.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{9.0 - 3.0}{12}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{6.0}{12}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.5)
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta = 26.57^\circ}
$$

**Solution (b):**

A car with a 15° approach angle **CANNOT** climb a 26.57° ramp. The ramp is too steep.

**Remedy:** Use a **twin-link (articulated) ramp** that hinges mid-length, halving the effective angle. New effective angle = $\tan^{-1}(3.0/6.0) = 26.57°$ per half — still too steep. Hence **quay must be raised or deck must be lowered** for such cars.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!IMPORTANT]

Following the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Fallback Rule**, the diagrams below are **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flows** mapping the interactions of major Ro-Ro subsystems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** *Define a Roll-on/Roll-off (Ro-Ro) ship. List **any two** advantages it has over a conventional general cargo ship.*

**Model Answer:**
A Ro-Ro ship is a vessel designed to carry **wheeled cargo** (cars, trucks, trailers) which is **driven on and off** the ship using **built-in ramps**, eliminating the need for shoreside cranes.

Advantages:
1. **Faster port turnaround** — Cargo is self-loading; no crane dependency.
2. **Lower cargo damage** — Vehicles are not lifted; handling is gentler.
3. **Reduced labour cost** at ports.
4. **No need for specialized port infrastructure** (cranes, lift-on lift-off gear).
5. **Higher packing density** for wheeled cargo.

*(Any two well-explained advantages earn 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** *What is meant by the term **"lane-metre"** in Ro-Ro ship design? Why is it used as a unit of capacity?*

**Model Answer:**
A **lane-metre (lm)** is the unit of Ro-Ro ship capacity defined as **1 metre of usable lane length** for parking vehicles.

- It is used because Ro-Ro ships carry a **mixed fleet of vehicles** (cars, trucks, trailers) of widely varying sizes; a single unit like "number of cars" is meaningless.
- A Ro-Ro ship of, say, **3000 lane-metres** can carry 300 cars (each ~ 10 m) or 100 trucks (each ~ 30 m), with the operator optimizing mix to fill the deck.
- It is the **standard commercial unit** used in charter parties and freight quotations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain, with neat sketches, the **different types of access ramps** used in Ro-Ro ships. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ro-Ro ship has a main deck **7.5 m above the waterline** and berths at a quay **2.5 m above the waterline**. The stern ramp has a **horizontal projection of 8 m** from the transom. Calculate: (i) the ramp angle, and (ii) the **vertical rise** the ramp must traverse. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types of access ramps** (Full-marks answer should cover):

1. **Stern Quarter Ramp:**
   - Pivots from upper deck transom.
   - **Merits:** Most common; supports heavy loads; quay-independent.
   - **Demerits:** Occupies valuable deck space; vulnerable to stern wave slamming.

2. **Bow Door / Visor:**
   - Folds upward to seal the bow.
   - **Merits:** Vehicle can drive straight through the ship; efficient drive-through.
   - **Demerits:** Complex sealing; vulnerable to green-sea loading on bow.

3. **Side Door (Side Ramp):**
   - Sideways-opening door on ship's side.
   - **Merits:** Vehicle drives sideways off; protects deck from weather.
   - **Demerits:** Limited ramp width; quay height must match.

4. **Internal Ramp / Adjustable Ramp:**
   - Connects fixed and hoistable decks.
   - **Merits:** Saves deck area; flexible cargo configuration.
   - **Demerits:** Adds structural weight; reduces headroom.

5. **Hoistable Deck (Lift):**
   - Platform lifted by hydraulic cylinders.
   - **Merits:** Maximizes cargo flexibility; no ramp geometry issue.
   - **Demerits:** Slow; expensive; mechanical failure risk.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) Ramp angle:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_d - H_q}{L_r}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{7.5 - 2.5}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{5.0}{8}\right)
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.625) = 32.0^\circ
$$

(ii) Vertical rise = $H_d - H_q = 7.5 - 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$

**Conclusion:** The ramp is **steeper than 30°**, which is **marginal for cars** but acceptable for low-bed trucks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 20** requirements for fire safety on Ro-Ro ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ro-Ro ship has a partially filled **freshwater ballast tank** with free surface length $l = 12$ m and breadth $b = 6$ m. The ship has a **rigid GM = 1.5 m** and **displacement $\Delta = 12{,}000$ t**. Find: (i) the loss in GM due to free surface, and (ii) the **effective GM**. Take $\rho_s = 1.0 \text{ t/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SOLAS II-2 Reg 20 (Ro-Ro Fire Safety):**

1. **Water-Spray (Deluge) System:** A fixed water-spray system must cover **every vehicle deck**, capable of delivering **3.5 L/min per m²** of protected area.
2. **Smoke Detection:** Smoke detectors must be installed at intervals **not exceeding 20 m** longitudinally and **on every deck**.
3. **Ventilation Cut-off:** Remote-controlled **fire dampers** to shut off mechanical ventilation within **1 minute** of fire detection.
4. **CO₂ System:** A fixed CO₂ system for the **engine room, pump room, and any space containing internal combustion machinery**.
5. **Means of Escape:** Marked escape routes, **every 20 m** along the deck, leading to a muster station or survival craft embarkation.
6. **Structural Fire Integrity:** Bulkheads and decks separating Ro-Ro spaces from accommodation must have **A-60 fire rating**.
7. **CCTV / Patrol:** Continuous watch with thermal imaging or patrol every 30 minutes.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) Moment of inertia of the free surface:

$$
i_T = \dfrac{l \cdot b^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 6^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 216}{12} = 216 \text{ m}^4
$$

Virtual rise of G:

$$
\Delta GG_1 = \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot i_T}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 216}{12{,}000} = 0.018 \text{ m}
$$

(ii) Effective GM:

$$
GM_{eff} = GM_{rigid} - \Delta GG_1 = 1.5 - 0.018 = 1.482 \text{ m}
$$

> **Note:** The free-surface loss is small for this tank, but if **many tanks are partially filled**, the cumulative effect is significant. SOLAS requires $GM_{eff} \geq 0.15 \text{ m}$ for normal operations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **convert units**. If $\Delta$ is given in **tonnes**, $\rho_s$ in **t/m³**, and $i$ in **m⁴**, the result $\Delta GG_1$ comes out in **metres** directly. Mixing kg and tonnes causes a 1000× error.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Treating Ro-Ro as a "normal ship" and **ignoring free-surface effect** altogether. The examiner will deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In ramp-angle problems, students use **radians instead of degrees** or vice-versa. Always specify.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** In SOLAS questions, students list **SOLAS II-1 (Stability)** items when the question asked for **II-2 (Fire Safety)**. Read carefully.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting to **add the height of the quay above water** when computing the effective vertical rise. Many students compute $H_d / L_r$ instead of $(H_d - H_q) / L_r$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Definition:** Ro-Ro = wheeled cargo rolled on/off via ramps; **NOT lifted** by cranes.
- **Types:** PCC, PCTC, Con-Ro, Ro-Pax.
- **Capacity Unit:** **Lane-metre (lm)**.
- **Ramp Angle Formula:** $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{H_d - H_q}{L_r}\right)$.
- **Free Surface Effect:** $GM_{eff} = GM_{rigid} - \dfrac{\rho_s \cdot i_T}{\Delta}$.
- **Heeling Moment:** $M_{\text{heel}} = W \times b$ (off-centre weight).
- **SOLAS II-2 Reg 20:** Water-spray 3.5 L/min·m²; smoke detectors every 20 m; CO₂ for machinery spaces.
- **Design Heel Cautions:**
  - **Always load symmetrically** about C/L.
  - **Pump out partially filled tanks** before departure to minimize FSE.
  - **Secure every vehicle** with lashings, D-rings, and wheel chocks.
  - **Watertight doors** must seal against the **head of water** to the bulkhead deck.
- **Differences from General Cargo Ship:**
  - No cargo gear (cranes, derricks).
  - Multi-deck box superstructure.
  - Bow/side/stern access doors.
  - Mandatory deluge + CO₂ fire system.
- **Ramp Angle Heuristic:** Cars need ≤ 15°; trucks can handle up to 30°; tracked vehicles can handle up to 40°.
- **Critical Hazard:** Vehicle fires escalate rapidly → **mandatory detection + fixed firefighting within 1 minute**.
- **KTU Favourite Question Types:**
  - "Compute ramp angle given heights and length."
  - "Find loss of GM due to free surface."
  - "List SOLAS fire-safety features for a Ro-Ro."
  - "Differentiate Ro-Ro from container ship in 5 points."

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Ro-Ro Ships. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barge Carriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Barge Carriers**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Barge Carriers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Barge Carrier** is a specialized oceangoing vessel designed to transport pre-loaded, standardized river or sea-going **barges (lighters)** as cargo. The defining engineering feature is a self-contained **onboard gantry crane or elevator system** that allows the mother ship to load and discharge its barge cargo independently, **without any external port or shore-based lifting equipment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Barge carriers are a sub-class of **"Combination Carriers"** (along with OBO - Ore/Bulk/Oil carriers and O/O carriers). They were historically developed to solve the "first-mile/last-mile" problem of cargo delivery to shallow inland ports and river systems that conventional deep-draft ships cannot access.

### 1.2 Classification of Barge Carriers (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The four principal engineering systems recognized by KTU naval architecture syllabus are:

| Acronym | Full Form | Lifting Principle |
|---|---|---|
| **LASH** | Lighter Aboard SHip | Aft-mounted revolving gantry (1300-ton capacity) |
| **Seabee** | Sea Barge | Stern elevator + flooded dock chamber |
| **BOB** | Barge On Board | Deck-mounted gantry pair (twin towers) |
| **BACO** | Barge Aboard Catamaran | Twin-hull gantry lift system |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: "The Floating Mother Truck"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a giant floating **multi-level car-carrier truck** that doesn't just carry cars — it carries **entire smaller boats already loaded with cargo**. Instead of unloading each box at a port, the truck simply drops a fully-loaded mini-boat (barge) into a river and drives away. This is exactly how a barge carrier works. The mother ship is the "truck" and the lighters are the "mini-boats" that continue their journey inland via shallow waterways.

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Standard LASH Lighter:** 18.75 m × 9.50 m × 3.96 m (61.5 ft × 31.2 ft × 13 ft) — **Deadweight 367 tonnes**
- **Standard Seabee Barge:** 29.87 m × 10.67 m × 6.10 m — **Deadweight ~1,000 tonnes**
- **Standard BACO Barge:** 30.5 m × 10.7 m × 4.9 m — **Deadweight ~820 tonnes**
- **LASH Vessel Capacity:** Typically **73 to 89 lighters** across multiple decks
- **Seabee Vessel Capacity:** Exactly **3 large barges** in a flooded hold

### 1.5 Operational Definition of the Lighter (Barge)

A **lighter** is a flat-bottomed, rectangular, unmanned floating pontoon with a watertight hatch cover on top. It is **portable cargo packaging** — the marine equivalent of a standard intermodal shipping container, but floatable and waterborne rather than truckable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disambiguation:** In KTU terminology, "barge" and "lighter" are often used interchangeably. The technically precise distinction is that a **lighter** is a non-self-propelled vessel, while **barge** is the broader term. For Module 1, treat them as synonymous as per the LASH/Seabee original design literature.

### 1.6 Historical Engineering Context

The barge carrier concept emerged in the **late 1960s** as a response to the inadequacy of post-WWII inland waterway infrastructure:
- **LASH system:** Patented 1966, first vessel *Acadia Forest* (1970)
- **Seabee system:** Developed by Stena Line and Sun Shipbuilding, *Seabee* class entered service 1973
- **BACO system:** Commissioned by Combi Lift, the *Combi Lift I* and *Combi Lift II* built 1984
- **BOB system:** US Navy developed for sealift logistics, *Military Sealift Command* operation

The unifying engineering problem these vessels solved: **breaking the port-bottleneck** by transferring cargo to land via barges that could navigate rivers as shallow as **3 m draught**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Engineering Systems: Architectural Logic

#### 2.1.1 LASH (Lighter Aboard SHip) System

**Architectural layout:** A U-shaped well deck runs along the longitudinal centerline of the ship, open at the stern. Above the well deck, a **revolving gantry crane** (mounted on a turntable) operates on rails.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. Mother ship positions in sheltered water.
2. Gantry crane lifts lighter from the **stern hatch** (the only entry point).
3. Lighter is **lowered through** the well deck to a designated tier.
4. Multiple tiers stack vertically — typically **3 to 5 high**.
5. Discharge reverses the process — gantry lowers lighter into the sea, where tugs tow them away.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the U-shaped well deck?** It allows loading without listing the ship excessively. The well deck creates a "trough" that is below the waterline of the loaded barge but above the bilge of the mother ship, providing **gravity-fed loading** with a clear longitudinal water channel for the gantry.

#### 2.1.2 Seabee System

**Architectural layout:** A **floodable stern hold** (dock chamber) that can be filled with seawater to allow the barges to float in and out. A **stern-mounted elevator** (effectively a giant platform lift) raises each barge from the water-filled chamber to a storage position.

**Operational Sequence:**
1. Ship backs into sheltered water.
2. Stern gate opens, chamber is **flooded** to a depth equal to the dock waterline.
3. Floating barges are pushed into the chamber by pusher tugs.
4. Pumping system **de-floods** the chamber, grounding the barges on elevator cradles.
5. Elevator raises barge to upper deck storage; process repeats for the next barge.

#### 2.1.3 BOB (Barge On Board) System

**Architectural layout:** A **flat reinforced upper deck** (the "tank top deck") with two **tower gantries** that can lift a barge in tandem and place it on the upper deck. Barges float alongside the mother ship at the loading station.

#### 2.1.4 BACO (Barge Aboard Catamaran) System

**Architectural layout:** A **catamaran mother ship** with a **transverse gantry** spanning the two hulls. The gantry can lift barges out of the water and stack them between the hulls — a "bridge crane" arrangement.

### 2.2 Core Engineering Trade-offs

| Parameter | LASH | Seabee | BOB | BACO |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Number of barges** | 73–89 | 3 | 25–40 | 35–50 |
| **Lighter size (DWT)** | 367 t | 1,000 t | 600 t | 820 t |
| **Loading time (all barges)** | 24–36 h | 8–10 h | 12–18 h | 14–20 h |
| **Capital cost (1975 USD)** | $28M | $24M | $30M | $35M |
| **Surviving in service (2024)** | 5–8 | 1–2 | 10+ | 3–4 |
| **Best use case** | Multi-port riverine | Heavy lift | Military/civil | Tropical inland |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following formulas appear routinely in KTU 2024 Module 1 questions. Master each one — the KTU examiner often converts a derivation into a **3-mark Part A question**.

| Formula | Expression | Variables | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lighter deadweight | $\Delta_L = \rho_w \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T_{\max} \cdot C_{b,L}$ | L=length, B=beam, T=draught, $C_{b,L}$=block coeff (≈0.85) | tonnes | Sizing lighters |
| Total cargo capacity | $W_{cargo} = N_L \cdot \Delta_L \cdot \eta_{pack}$ | $N_L$ = number of lighters, $\eta_{pack}$ = packaging factor (0.95) | tonnes | Vessel payload |
| Gantry cycle time | $t_{cycle} = 2 t_{lift} + t_{sway} + t_{place}$ | $t_{lift}$ = hoisting time, $t_{sway}$ = slewing time | seconds/hour | Throughput |
| Productivity index | $P = \dfrac{W_{cargo}}{t_{port} \cdot DWT}$ | $t_{port}$ = port stay | t/h | Operational efficiency |
| Block coefficient (mother ship) | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | $\nabla$ = displaced volume | dimensionless | Hull form |
| Lightweight margin | $\eta_{LW} = \dfrac{DWT - W_{cargo}}{DWT}$ | — | % | Cargo vs. ship weight |
| Barge stowage factor | $S_f = \dfrac{V_{hold}}{N_L \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | $V_{hold}$ = total hold volume | — | Stowage check |
| Elevator lift power | $P_{lift} = \dfrac{m_{barge} \cdot g \cdot v_{lift}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | $v_{lift}$ = lift speed, $\eta_{mech}$ = mech. efficiency | kW | Crane sizing |
| Gantry hook load | $F_{hook} = m_{barge} \cdot g + F_{buoy} + F_{slack}$ | $F_{buoy}$ = buoyancy at water, $F_{slack}$ = dynamic slack | kN | Crane SWL |
| Barge stability (in hold) | $GZ_{min} = \dfrac{BM \cdot \sin\theta - GG' \cdot \sin\theta}{...}$ | GM metacentric | m·rad | Damage stability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern maritime commerce, barge carriers are critical for:
- **Amazon River trade** (Brazil) — BOB and BACO systems transport containers from Belem to Manaus
- **Mekong River trade** (Vietnam/Cambodia) — lighters feed inland ports
- **US Military Sealift** — BOB ships deploy to Pacific theaters without port access
- **North Sea offshore** — Seabee barges transport oil rig modules

> [!NOTE]
> The **port-bypass capability** is the central economic justification: a single barge carrier call can deliver cargo to **10+ inland riverine ports** that would otherwise require a fleet of small feeder vessels, each with separate bunker, crew, and insurance costs.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: LASH Lighter Deadweight from First Principles

**Problem statement:** A LASH lighter is 18.75 m long, 9.50 m beam, with a maximum loaded draught of 3.96 m. Compute its maximum deadweight assuming a block coefficient of 0.85 and seawater density of 1.025 t/m³.

**Step 1 — State the buoyancy equation:**

For a floating body in static equilibrium, by **Archimedes' principle**, the displaced water weight equals the total weight of the vessel (lightweight + deadweight). The displaced volume is:

$$
V_{disp} = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_{b,L}
$$

where $C_{b,L}$ accounts for the fact that the lighter is not a perfect rectangular prism but has a tapered bow and rounded bilge.

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{disp} &= 18.75 \cdot 9.50 \cdot 3.96 \cdot 0.85 \\
&= 178.125 \cdot 3.96 \cdot 0.85 \\
&= 705.375 \cdot 0.85 \\
&= 599.57 \;\; \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass using seawater density:**

The displacement mass is volume times density:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_L &= \rho_w \cdot V_{disp} \\
&= 1.025 \;\; \text{t/m}^3 \cdot 599.57 \;\; \text{m}^3 \\
&= 614.56 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract the lightweight to obtain deadweight:**

A standard LASH lighter has a lightweight (empty mass) of approximately **247 tonnes** (hatch covers, structural steel, ballast piping). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DWT_L &= \Delta_L - LW_L \\
&= 614.56 - 247.0 \\
&\approx 367.56 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the industry standard of **~367 tonnes** per LASH lighter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The block coefficient $C_{b,L} = 0.85$ for a lighter is higher than a typical ship's $C_b$ (≈0.60–0.75). This is because lighters are essentially **slab-sided, near-rectangular** vessels optimized for cargo stowage, not for hydrodynamic efficiency.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: LASH Vessel Total Cargo Throughput

**Problem statement:** A LASH vessel carries 83 lighters, each with a 367 t deadweight. The ship's port stay is 32 hours for full loading and 28 hours for discharge. Compute the **Productivity Index** $P$.

**Step 1 — Compute total cargo mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cargo} &= N_L \cdot DWT_L \cdot \eta_{pack} \\
&= 83 \cdot 367 \cdot 0.95 \\
&= 28,932.95 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total port time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{port} &= t_{load} + t_{discharge} \\
&= 32 + 28 \\
&= 60 \;\; \text{h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Productivity Index:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{W_{cargo}}{t_{port}} \\
&= \dfrac{28{,}932.95}{60} \\
&= 482.22 \;\; \text{t/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A LASH vessel can move roughly **482 tonnes per hour** through a single port call. Compare this to a conventional geared bulker at **~200 t/h** with shore cranes — a **2.4× throughput advantage** that justifies the higher capital cost of the gantry system.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Gantry Crane Hook Load Calculation

**Problem statement:** A Seabee crane lifts a 1,000 t barge at 5 m/min with 0.70 mechanical efficiency. The gantry's own weight is 280 t. Compute the **hoist motor power** required.

**Step 1 — Total mass to be lifted:**

The hoist must lift both the barge and the gantry (acting as a counterweight-rigging assembly):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= m_{barge} + m_{gantry} \\
&= 1{,}000 + 280 \\
&= 1{,}280 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lifting force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{lift} &= m_{total} \cdot g \\
&= 1{,}280 \cdot 9.81 \\
&= 12{,}556.8 \;\; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert lift speed to SI units:**

$$
v_{lift} = 5 \;\; \text{m/min} = \dfrac{5}{60} = 0.0833 \;\; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute required mechanical power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= F_{lift} \cdot v_{lift} \\
&= 12{,}556.8 \cdot 0.0833 \\
&= 1{,}046.40 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for mechanical losses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{mech}} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}046.40}{0.70} \\
&= 1{,}494.86 \;\; \text{kW} \\
&\approx 1.50 \;\; \text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to horsepower for engineering practice:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{HP} &= 1{,}494.86 \cdot 1.341 \\
&\approx 2{,}005 \;\; \text{HP}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** This is a continuous-duty power. The actual installed motor is typically **2,200 kW** to provide a 30% service margin for transient loads, wind heeling moments, and acceleration peaks. This is why Seabee vessels have very large auxiliary engine rooms — they are essentially **floating cranes as much as they are cargo ships.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Design Parameter Comparison

| Design Aspect | LASH | Seabee | BOB | BACO | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gantry lifting capacity | 1,300 t | 2,200 t | 800 t | 1,500 t | High (3-mark) |
| Lighter accessibility | Stern only | Stern only | Side flooding | Side-by-side | Medium |
| Number of holds | 1 (U-shaped) | 1 (flooded) | 3–4 (multi-deck) | 1 (between hulls) | High |
| Total cargo DWT | 27,000 t | 22,500 t | 24,000 t | 26,500 t | High (14-mark) |
| Lighter count | 73–89 | 3 | 25–40 | 35–50 | Direct recall |
| Stern gate required | No | **Yes** | No | No | Concept question |
| Need for shore crane | No | No | No | No | Concept question |
| Typical service speed | 18 knots | 18 knots | 16 knots | 16 knots | Direct recall |
| Hull form (C_b) | 0.65 | 0.60 | 0.62 | 0.58 | Calculation |
| Inland waterway access | Yes (river) | Limited | Yes | Yes (Amazon) | Conceptual |
| Barge deployment time | Hours | Hours | Hours | Hours | Direct recall |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Set B]**
Define the term **"LASH vessel"** and explain how it differs from a conventional break-bulk cargo ship with respect to cargo handling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A LASH (Lighter Aboard SHip) vessel is a specialized oceangoing ship designed to carry fully loaded **river/sea lighters (barges)** as cargo. The defining feature is an **onboard revolving gantry crane** (typically 1,300 t SWL) mounted on a turntable above a **U-shaped well deck**, allowing the vessel to **load and discharge its own barges without any shore-side crane assistance**.

Difference from break-bulk: A break-bulk ship uses **port-based gantry cranes** and a derrick/kingpost system to lift individual cargo items, packages, or pallets. The LASH vessel bypasses port infrastructure entirely — the cargo unit is a **whole barge (~367 t)** and the entire lighter is lifted as one unit. This makes LASH ideal for **shallow-draft riverine ports** that cannot accommodate the mother ship. *(3 marks — full credit)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Set A]**
List **any four types of barge carrier systems** and state the **lifting mechanism** used in each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **LASH (Lighter Aboard SHip):** Revolving stern-mounted gantry crane lifting 367 t lighters. (0.75 marks)
2. **Seabee:** Stern elevator + floodable dock chamber lifting 1,000 t barges. (0.75 marks)
3. **BOB (Barge On Board):** Twin tower deck gantries lifting 600 t barges. (0.75 marks)
4. **BACO (Barge Aboard Catamaran):** Transverse gantry on catamaran hull lifting 820 t barges. (0.75 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 marks):**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Set 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **LASH system** in detail. Include in your answer: the layout of the well deck, the gantry crane, the standard lighter dimensions, and the loading sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LASH vessel carries **78 lighters**, each of deadweight **367 t**. The vessel loads in 30 hours and discharges in 24 hours. Calculate:
- (i) Total cargo deadweight carried. (2 marks)
- (ii) Total port time. (1 mark)
- (iii) Productivity Index in t/h. (2 marks)
- (iv) Discuss how this productivity compares to a **geared bulk carrier** working with a shore gantry. (2 marks)

---

#### Model Answer for Question A:

**(a) LASH System Description (7 marks):**

**Layout of Well Deck (2 marks):**
The LASH vessel has a **U-shaped well deck** running longitudinally along the centerline of the ship from forward to aft, open at the stern. The well is divided by internal decks into **3 to 5 tiers** for lighter storage. The bottom of the well extends to within 1.5 m of the keel. This arrangement allows the gantry to lower lighters vertically through the open stern.

**Gantry Crane (2 marks):**
A **revolving gantry crane** (turntable-mounted) sits on rails running the length of the ship above the well deck. The crane has a **Safe Working Load (SWL) of 1,300 t**, hoisting speed of 30 m/min, and can slew 360° to position lighters over any tier. The gantry's own mass (~280 t) is counterweighted and supported by 8 bogies on the rails.

**Standard Lighter Dimensions (1 mark):**
A LASH lighter is **18.75 m long × 9.50 m beam × 3.96 m maximum draught** with a cargo deadweight of **367 t** and a hatch cover of the full deck area.

**Loading Sequence (2 marks):**
1. Ship anchors in sheltered water. (0.5)
2. Gantry positions over stern hatch. (0.5)
3. Lighter is hooked by the gantry spreader. (0.25)
4. Lighter is lifted clear of the water. (0.25)
5. Gantry slews and travels to assigned tier. (0.25)
6. Lighter is lowered into the tier and locked. (0.25)

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Total cargo deadweight (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cargo} &= N_L \cdot DWT_L \\
&= 78 \cdot 367 \\
&= 28{,}626 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total port time (1 mark):**

$$
t_{port} = 30 + 24 = 54 \;\; \text{h}
$$

**(iii) Productivity Index (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \dfrac{W_{cargo}}{t_{port}} \\
&= \dfrac{28{,}626}{54} \\
&= 530.11 \;\; \text{t/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comparison with geared bulk carrier (2 marks):**
A geared bulk carrier using a shore gantry typically achieves a productivity of **150–200 t/h** due to the inherent downtime of multiple-cycle grab operations. The LASH vessel's productivity of **530 t/h** is approximately **2.6× higher** because each lift moves a complete 367 t cargo package in a single gantry cycle, eliminating intermediate handling. The trade-off is that LASH requires **sheltered deep anchorage** (not a dock wall), which is acceptable in well-developed trade lanes.

---

**Question B (14 marks):**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Seabee system** with the help of a labeled block diagram. Discuss the **flooding/de-flooding cycle** and its purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Seabee vessel has a deadweight of **22,500 t** and carries **3 barges** of 1,000 t each. The vessel takes 9 hours to load and 7 hours to discharge. The gantry hoists a barge plus 280 t of rigging at 5 m/min with mechanical efficiency 0.70. Calculate:
- (i) Total cargo carried and the lightweight of the ship. (2 marks)
- (ii) Productivity Index in t/h. (1 mark)
