- (iii) The required hoist motor power in kW. (3 marks)
- (iv) State **two reasons** why Seabee systems are less common than LASH in modern service. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Answer for Question B:

**(a) Seabee System Description (7 marks):**

**General Architecture (2 marks):**
The Seabee system is a barge carrier that uses a **floodable stern hold** combined with a **giant elevator** to load and discharge **three large 1,000 t barges**. The barges are nearly twice the size of LASH lighters and are loaded in a flooded chamber, then raised to storage by an elevator.

**Flooding/De-flooding Cycle (3 marks):**
The procedure is:
1. **Stern gate opens.** A hydraulically-operated gate seals the rear of the hold; when opened, it gives a 20 m × 12 m opening. (0.5)
2. **Chamber floods.** Four ballast pumps of 2,000 m³/h each draw seawater into the chamber; the chamber fills until the water level inside equals the dock waterline. (1.0)
3. **Barge floats in.** A pusher tug nudges the 1,000 t barge into the chamber; it floats because the chamber is now at equal level with the surrounding water. (0.5)
4. **Chamber de-floods.** The pumps reverse and expel water, lowering the water level and **grounding the barge onto elevator cradles** positioned in the chamber floor. (1.0)

**Purpose of the cycle (2 marks):**
The flooding/de-flooding cycle allows the barge to be **transferred between dock water and storage position without ever being lifted by a crane**. This avoids the need for a 1,000 t SWL crane and reduces the structural demands on the gantry. It also minimizes **list and trim** changes because the buoyancy of the water in the chamber stabilizes the ship during barge handling.

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Total cargo and ship lightweight (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{cargo} &= 3 \cdot 1{,}000 = 3{,}000 \;\; \text{t} \\
LW_{ship} &= DWT - W_{cargo} = 22{,}500 - 3{,}000 = 19{,}500 \;\; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Productivity Index (1 mark):**

$$
P = \dfrac{3{,}000}{9 + 7} = \dfrac{3{,}000}{16} = 187.5 \;\; \text{t/h}
$$

**(iii) Hoist motor power (3 marks):**

Total lifted mass = 1,000 + 280 = 1,280 t. Lift speed = 5/60 = 0.0833 m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{lift} &= 1{,}280 \cdot 9.81 = 12{,}556.8 \;\; \text{kN} \\
P_{mech} &= 12{,}556.8 \cdot 0.0833 = 1{,}046.4 \;\; \text{kW} \\
P_{motor} &= \dfrac{1{,}046.4}{0.70} = 1{,}494.9 \;\; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Two reasons Seabee is less common than LASH (1 mark):**
1. Seabee vessels carry only **3 barges** versus LASH's **73–89**, severely limiting the trade economics.
2. The Seabee requires a **floodable chamber and stern gate** — a complex and maintenance-heavy system vulnerable to corrosion and seal failure.
3. (Any one of the above — 0.5 each)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing DWT of lighters vs. DWT of mother ship.** The lighter DWT (367 t) is the cargo per unit. The mother ship's DWT is the sum across all lighters. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the gantry's own weight** in hoist power calculations. Many students lift only the barge and forget the rigging mass. This is a recurring **-1 mark** error in Part B numerical problems.
> 3. **Confusing LASH and Lighter.** LASH is the *ship*; Lighter is the *barge it carries*. Examiners commonly ask "explain the difference" — losing **1 mark** for mixing up the terminology.
> 4. **Block coefficient misuse.** Do not use $C_b = 0.6$ (ship's value) for the lighter; use $C_b = 0.85$ for the lighter. Common error — **-1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting unit conversions in productivity problems.** Always convert port time to hours consistently. Mixing minutes and hours is a classic **-0.5 mark** deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This Twice Before the Exam:**

- **Definition:** Barge carrier = ship that carries barges/lighters as cargo using **onboard gantry** (no shore crane).
- **Four systems:** **LASH, Seabee, BOB, BACO** — know each one's lifting mechanism.
- **LASH capacity:** 73–89 lighters × 367 t = ~30,000 t DWT mother ship.
- **Seabee capacity:** 3 barges × 1,000 t = 3,000 t DWT mother ship.
- **BOB capacity:** 25–40 barges × 600 t = ~24,000 t DWT.
- **BACO capacity:** 35–50 barges × 820 t = ~26,500 t DWT.
- **Lighter dimensions:** 18.75 m × 9.50 m × 3.96 m; $C_{b,L} = 0.85$.
- **Lighter mass calculation:** $\Delta_L = \rho_w \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_{b,L}$ with $\rho_w = 1.025$ t/m³.
- **Productivity index:** $P = W_{cargo} / t_{port}$ (units: t/h).
- **Gantry power:** $P_{motor} = (m_{total} \cdot g \cdot v_{lift}) / \eta_{mech}$ — always include gantry weight in $m_{total}$.
- **LASH system:** U-shaped well deck, stern gantry, vertical lift through stern hatch.
- **Seabee system:** Floodable stern hold, elevator, pump-driven barge float-in/ground.
- **BOB system:** Twin tower deck gantries, barges stored on upper tank top deck.
- **BACO system:** Catamaran hull with transverse gantry, designed for tropical rivers.
- **Key advantage:** **Port independence** — can work in sheltered anchorages without dock infrastructure.
- **Key advantage:** **Inland access** — barges navigate rivers as shallow as 3 m draught.
- **Key disadvantage:** **High capital cost** of gantry and hull modifications.
- **Key disadvantage:** **Lighter loss risk** — lighters can be lost at sea in storms.
- **Key disadvantage:** **Weather sensitivity** — gantry operations require calm conditions.
- **Modern relevance:** Used in Amazon, Mekong, Mississippi river trade; US military sealift.
- **KTU weight:** This topic carries **~12%** of Module 1 marks in the End Semester Exam.
- **Examiner tip:** Always show units in calculations; KTU deducts **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
- **Examiner tip:** Draw labeled block diagrams in 14-mark questions — they earn **0.5–1 mark** for visual presentation.
- **Examiner tip:** When asked to "compare," use a **table format** with at least 4 comparison parameters.
- **Most-tested 3-mark question:** "List the types of barge carriers and their capacities."
- **Most-tested 14-mark question:** "Describe a specific system + numerical calculation on productivity or gantry power."
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Do not confuse the **LASH system** with the **LASH lighter** — they are different things.
- **Industry status (2024):** ~25–30 LASH, ~2 Seabee, ~10 BOB, ~4 BACO vessels in active service worldwide.
- **Future trend:** Barge carriers are being phased out for **container feeder ships** in many trades, but remain dominant on the Amazon and Mekong where river barges are essential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tankers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Tankers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tanker (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *tanker* is a specialized merchant ship engineered and constructed with a flush deck hull form, longitudinal bulkheads, and a system of segregated cargo tanks primarily intended for the bulk transportation of liquid cargoes — such as crude oil, refined petroleum products, chemicals, edible oils, or liquefied gases — across oceanic and coastal waterways. The fundamental design intent distinguishes a tanker from a dry-cargo ship: the entire hull volume is dedicated to liquid containment, eliminating the need for holds, hatch covers, or onboard cargo-handling gear. Cargo is loaded and discharged through a **marine loading manifold** connected to an intricate network of cargo pumps, pipelines, and valves routed through a **pump room**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > Think of a tanker as a *giant floating industrial pipeline*, sliced vertically into long rectangular compartments, like a chocolate bar with deep grooves running along its length. Instead of carrying chocolate squares, each groove holds thousands of tonnes of crude oil. The ship is engineered to keep the liquid stable (so the ship doesn't roll excessively), sealed (so nothing leaks into the ocean), and thermally safe (so volatile cargoes don't explode). The "grooves" in this analogy are the **cargo tanks**, separated by **longitudinal bulkheads** and transverse **swash bulkheads**, all enclosed by the **double hull** — a modern safety requirement that acts as a sacrificial buffer in case of grounding or collision. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics - **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT):** Total weight a tanker can carry, including cargo, fuel, freshwater, ballast, stores, and crew. Standard metric: **tonnes (t)**. - **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** Distance between the forward and aft perpendiculars, used for hydrodynamic calculations. - **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Ratio of displaced volume to the product of LBP × Beam × Draft. Typical VLCC: **0.80 – 0.85**. - **MARPOL 73/78 Annex I:** The international regulation governing oil tanker design — mandates **double-hull construction** for newbuilds since 1996. - **IMO:** International Maritime Organization, the UN body that sets global tanker safety standards. - **1 Barrel (oil):** Standard unit ≈ **0.159 m³** or **42 US gallons**. ## 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot | Era | Dominant Hull Form | Maximum DWT (approx.) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Pre-1950s | Single-deck riveted hulls | ~20,000 DWT (T2 Tanker) | | 1950s – 1970s | Single-hull welded, central stringer | ~300,000 DWT (Ultra-Large Crude Carrier era begins) | | 1996 onwards | Double-hull mandatory (MARPOL) | 320,000+ DWT (VLCC, ULCC) | | 2020s | Hybrid double-hull + alternative fuels ready | 320,000 DWT (with LNG-ready retrofits) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically expects students to differentiate between *single-hull* and *double-hull* configurations and explain the regulatory driver (MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19) that forced the phase-out of single-hull tankers worldwide. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Tankers (Structured Logic) Tankers are classified along **three primary axes** that KTU examiners consistently test: ### Axis 1: By Cargo Type - **Crude Oil Tankers (Crude Carriers):** Transport unrefined crude oil from extraction terminals to refineries. - **Product Tankers:** Carry refined petroleum products (naphtha, gasoline, jet fuel, diesel). - **Chemical Tankers:** Carry liquid chemicals; require **stainless steel** or **coated** tanks (IMO Type 1/2/3 classification). - **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) Carriers:** Carry natural gas at **−162 °C** in spherical or membrane tanks. - **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) Carriers:** Transport propane/butane under pressure or refrigeration. - **Edible Oil / Wine / Fruit Juice Tankers:** Coated tanks, often stainless steel, with strict hygiene. ### Axis 2: By Size (DWT Bands) - **General Purpose (GP):** up to 25,000 DWT - **Handysize:** 25,000 – 40,000 DWT - **Handymax / MR (Medium Range):** 40,000 – 60,000 DWT - **Aframax (AFRA):** 80,000 – 120,000 DWT - **Suezmax:** 120,000 – 200,000 DWT (Suez Canal-capable) - **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier):** 200,000 – 320,000 DWT - **ULCC (Ultra Large Crude Carrier):** > 320,000 DWT ### Axis 3: By Hull Form - **Single-hull:** Cargo tanks form part of the hull boundary (largely phased out). - **Double-hull (Double Bottom + Double Side):** Mandatory under MARPOL Annex I; ballast tanks occupy the void space. - **Mid-deck / Hybrid designs:** Used in some chemical and product carriers. ## 2.2 Core Hull Geometry & Hydrostatics > [!NOTE] > A tanker is a *form-optimized vessel* — every dimension of the principal particulars is dictated by the cubic capacity required for the target DWT and the port/restriction constraints (Suez, Panama, length-limiting terminals). ### Fundamental Design Parameters Let: - $L$ = Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), in metres - $B$ = Moulded Breadth, in metres - $D$ = Moulded Depth, in metres - $T$ = Design Draft, in metres - $\nabla$ = Displaced volume (m³) - $C_B$ = Block coefficient (dimensionless) The **displaced volume** equals: $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T $$ The **displacement (mass)** is then: $$ \Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla $$ where $\rho_{sw}$ is the density of seawater (typically **1.025 t/m³** for standard ocean conditions, **1.000 t/m³** for freshwater). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Displaced Volume | $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | | 2 | Displacement (mass) | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | | 3 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.80 – 0.85 (VLCC) | | 4 | Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$ | tonnes | | 5 | Lightweight (Lightship) | $LWT = \Delta_{light}$ | tonnes | | 6 | Freeboard (Load Line) | $F = D - T$ | metres | | 7 | Cargo Capacity (approx.) | $V_{cargo} \approx C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot 0.92$ | m³ (≈ 92% of $\nabla$) | | 8 | Tonnes per cm immersion (TPC) | $TPC = \dfrac{A_W}{100} \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | t/cm | | 9 | Bunker Consumption (VLCC at service speed) | — | 60 – 90 t/day HFO | | 10 | 1 Tonne of Oil | $\approx 6.29$ barrels | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always quote the **block coefficient** in the range **0.80 – 0.85** for a fully loaded VLCC. Values below 0.75 are typical of container ships, NOT tankers. KTU examiners specifically test whether students confuse these. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The block coefficient governs **resistance and powering**. A higher $C_B$ means: - A "fatter" hull → more cargo capacity for the same length - Higher frictional + form resistance - Lower service speed - Optimized for **long-haul, slow-steaming crude transport** VLCCs typically operate at **13 – 16 knots** with $C_B \approx 0.83$. By contrast, chemical tankers operate at **14 – 16 knots** with $C_B \approx 0.78$ (slightly finer form to allow port flexibility). ## 2.5 Double-Hull Geometry (MARPOL-Compliant) The mandatory double hull consists of: - **Double Bottom (DB):** Height typically **B/15** or **2 m**, whichever is greater (per MARPOL Reg. 19). - **Double Side (DS):** Width typically **B/10** or **2 m**, whichever is greater. - **Wing tanks, Cofferdams, Slop tanks** occupy these voids. > [!NOTE] > **Why double hull?** Statistical analysis of oil spills (Exxon Valdez 1989, Erika 1999, Prestige 2002) showed that single-hull breaches caused catastrophic pollution. The double hull ensures a **buffer zone** that can survive moderate grounding/contact without cargo release. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Block Coefficient from Principal Particulars > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently tests this derivation. Memorize the logical chain, not just the formula. ### Step 1 — Define the Lifting Volume When a tanker floats at draft $T$ in seawater, it displaces a volume of water equal in weight to the vessel's total mass. By Archimedes' principle: $$ \Delta_{mass} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla $$ ### Step 2 — Define the Geometric Prism The bounding box of length $L$, breadth $B$, and draft $T$ has a volume: $$ V_{prism} = L \cdot B \cdot T $$ ### Step 3 — Form the Ratio The block coefficient is defined as the ratio of the actual displaced volume to the geometric prism: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $\nabla$ Multiplying both sides by $L \cdot B \cdot T$: $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T $$ ### Step 5 — Express Displacement Substituting into Archimedes: $$ \Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T $$ ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style) > **Problem:** A Suezmax tanker has $L = 280$ m, $B = 48$ m, $T = 20$ m, $C_B = 0.83$. Seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³. Compute displacement. **Step A — Compute the prism volume:** $$ V_{prism} = L \cdot B \cdot T = 280 \times 48 \times 20 = 268{,}800 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step B — Compute the actual displaced volume:** $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot V_{prism} = 0.83 \times 268{,}800 = 223{,}104 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step C — Convert to mass (displacement in tonnes):** $$ \Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 223{,}104 = 228{,}681.6 \text{ t} $$ **Step D — State the answer with units:** $$ \boxed{\Delta \approx 228{,}682 \text{ tonnes (or DWT, neglecting lightweight)}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Deadweight and Cargo Carrying Capacity The **lightweight** (LWT) is the empty-ship mass (steel, machinery, outfit). The **deadweight** is everything variable added to make the ship operational. $$ DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - LWT $$ For a tanker, DWT is further decomposed as: $$ DWT = Cargo + Bunker + Fresh\ Water + Ballast\ (in\ loaded\ condition) + Stores + Crew $$ In the **ballast voyage** (ship returns empty after discharging cargo), the ballast water occupies dedicated **segregated ballast tanks (SBTs)** within the double hull — this is a MARPOL requirement to prevent oily ballast discharge. ## 3.3 Derivation: Freeboard and Load Line The **freeboard** $F$ is the vertical distance from the waterline to the **deck line** (usually the upper deck at the side). It is governed by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966)**. $$ F = D - T $$ For tankers, the **load line** (Plimsoll mark) is set considering: - **Reserve buoyancy** (volume above waterline) - **Weather-tight integrity** - **Shear and bending moment limits** > [!NOTE] > A common KTU pitfall: confusing **depth** $D$ (moulded, keel to upper deck) with **draft** $T$ (keel to waterline). Always remember $D = T + F$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tanker Type vs. Cargo Density | Tanker Type | Typical Cargo Density (t/m³) | Tank Material | Slop Tank Required? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Crude Carrier (VLCC) | 0.85 – 0.95 (heavy crude) | Mild steel, coated | Yes (MARPOL) | | Product Tanker (MR) | 0.75 – 0.88 (diesel, jet) | Mild steel, coated/epoxy | Yes | | Chemical Tanker (Type 2) | 0.85 – 1.60 (varies) | Stainless steel 316L | Sometimes | | LNG Carrier | 0.43 (LNG at −162°C) | Membrane / Moss spherical | No | | LPG Carrier | 0.51 – 0.58 | Pressure vessel / refrigerated | No | | Edible Oil Tanker | 0.92 – 0.93 | Stainless steel / coated | Yes | > [!IMPORTANT] > A VLCC loaded with **light crude (0.80 t/m³)** requires *more cubic volume* for the same DWT than with **heavy crude (0.95 t/m³)**. This is why **cargo tank cubic capacity** is always a constraint, not just DWT. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **principal functional zones** of a modern double-hull crude oil tanker, illustrating how machinery, cargo, navigation, and safety systems interact. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Bow Region
Forepeak Tank
Mooring Equipment] --> B[Forward Cargo Block
Multiple Sloped Cargo Tanks
Longitudinal Bulkheads] B --> C[Mid-Ship Region
Main Cargo Tanks
Maximum Beam and Depth] C --> D[Engine Room and Pump Room
Main Engine, Boilers
Cargo Pumps and Manifold] D --> E[Stern Cargo Block
Aft Cargo Tanks
Steering Gear Access] E --> F[Stern Region
Rudder, Propeller, Stern Frame] C -.Ballast Pipelines.-> G[Double Bottom Tanks
Segregated Ballast] C -.Wing Spaces.-> H[Double Side Tanks
Wing Ballast] D --> I[Bridge and Accommodation
Navigation, Communication
Crew Quarters] I --> J[Inert Gas System
Flue Gas Scrubbing
Tank Pressurization] J --> C D --> K[Marine Loading Manifold
Deck Piping, Valves
Shore Connection] K --> B K --> C K --> E ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The cargo tanks form the **central longitudinal spine** of the vessel. - The **double hull** (DB + DS) wraps around the cargo block and provides ballast capacity + pollution protection. - The **engine room and pump room** are typically located **aft** for direct propeller-shaft alignment and to keep hazardous pump spaces away from accommodation. - The **inert gas system (IGS)** feeds scrubbed flue gas into the ullage space of cargo tanks to prevent explosive atmospheres. - The **manifold** is the only external cargo interface — it is the single point of transfer between ship and shore. ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Functional Mapping (Transverse View) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Outer Shell
Bottom Plate] --> P2[Double Bottom Tank
Height B over 15 or 2 m] P2 --> P3[Inner Bottom Plate] P3 --> P4[Cargo Tank
Sloped Bottom for Drainage] P4 --> P5[Inner Hull Side
Longitudinal Bulkhead] P5 --> P6[Double Side Wing Tank] P6 --> P7[Side Shell Plate] P7 --> P8[Weather Deck
Stringer Plate and Manholes] P4 -.Swash Bulkhead.- P4 P4 -.Transverse Bulkhead.- P4 ``` **Key takeaway for KTU:** The cargo tank is *sandwiched* between the inner bottom (top of double bottom) and the inner hull side (inner face of double side). This is the **MARPOL double-hull envelope**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** > **Q:** Define a *tanker*. List any **three** classifications of tankers based on cargo type. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A tanker is a merchant ship specifically designed with a flush deck, segregated cargo tanks, and pumping/ piping systems for the bulk transport of liquid cargoes such as crude oil, refined products, chemicals, or liquefied gases. **Three classifications by cargo type (2 Marks — 1 mark each for any two, or ½ mark each for all three):** 1. **Crude Oil Tanker** — transports unrefined crude from production terminals to refineries (e.g., VLCC, Suezmax). 2. **Product Tanker** — carries refined petroleum products (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel) in coated tanks. 3. **Chemical Tanker** — carries industrial liquid chemicals in stainless-steel or specialty-coated tanks, classified under **IMO Type 1, 2, or 3** based on hazard level. *(Acceptable alternates: LNG carrier, LPG carrier, edible oil tanker.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]** > **Q:** What is the **block coefficient** $C_B$ of a tanker? State its typical range for a fully loaded **VLCC**. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** The block coefficient is the dimensionless ratio of the displaced volume of the ship to the product of its length between perpendiculars, moulded breadth, and draft. It is given by: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Typical VLCC range (1 Mark):** For a fully loaded VLCC, $C_B$ typically lies in the range **0.80 to 0.85**, reflecting the fatter hull form that maximizes cargo cubic capacity for long-haul, slow-speed crude transport. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 1]** > **Q:** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, describe the **double-hull construction** of a modern crude oil tanker. Explain how this design satisfies the requirements of **MARPOL Annex I Regulation 19**. Discuss the role of **segregated ballast tanks (SBT)** in pollution prevention. #### Part (a) — Double-Hull Construction (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **1. Definition of Double Hull (2 Marks):** A double hull is a structural arrangement in which the cargo tanks are completely enclosed by a second skin of plating, creating void spaces between the cargo tank boundary and the outer shell of the ship. **2. Components (3 Marks):** - **Double Bottom (DB):** The space between the outer bottom shell plating and the inner bottom plating. The vertical height of the double bottom shall be at least: $$ h_{DB} = \max\left(\frac{B}{15},\ 2\text{ m}\right) $$ where $B$ is the moulded breadth. - **Double Side (DS):** The space between the outer side shell and the inner hull (longitudinal bulkhead). The width of the double side shall be at least: $$ w_{DS} = \max\left(\frac{B}{10},\ 2\text{ m}\right) $$ - **Wing Tanks & Double Bottom Tanks:** These void spaces are typically used as **ballast tanks**. **3. Structural Materials (2 Marks):** Mild steel with sacrificial anodes for cathodic protection; epoxy coatings on internal surfaces of cargo tanks; stainless steel trim at the manifold and pump room. #### Part (b) — MARPOL Compliance and SBT (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **1. MARPOL Annex I Reg. 19 (3 Marks):** Requires that crude oil tankers of 20,000 DWT and above, and product tankers of 30,000 DWT and above, delivered after **31 May 1996**, shall be fitted with a **double hull** throughout the cargo length. The regulation phases out single-hull tankers progressively (single-hull tankers were prohibited from operating after **2010** for most configurations). **2. Pollution Prevention Mechanism (2 Marks):** In the event of a low-energy grounding or side contact, the outer hull is breached first, but the **inner hull** (longitudinal bulkhead) remains intact, retaining the cargo. This redundancy drastically reduces the probability of an oil spill. Statistical studies after the **Erika (1999)** and **Prestige (2002)** casualties confirmed double-hull effectiveness, leading to accelerated phase-out of single-hull vessels. **3. Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT) (2 Marks):** SBTs are dedicated ballast spaces located within the double hull, completely separate from the cargo oil system. They allow the ship to take on seawater ballast during a ballast voyage **without contaminating cargo tanks**. This eliminates the practice of using cargo tanks as ballast tanks (a major historical source of operational oil discharge) and ensures compliance with **MARPOL Reg. 18**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students frequently *omit the dimension formula* $\left(\frac{B}{15},\ \frac{B}{10}\right)$ and lose 1 mark. > - Do **NOT** confuse MARPOL Annex I (oil pollution) with Annex II (chemicals) or Annex V (garbage). > - Always state **the delivery date (31 May 1996)** when explaining double-hull phase-in — examiners mark this strictly. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern, Module 1]** > **Q:** A Suezmax tanker has the following principal particulars: $L_{BP} = 280$ m, $B = 48$ m, $T = 20$ m, block coefficient $C_B = 0.83$, and lightship displacement $LWT = 35{,}000$ tonnes. Seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³. > > **(a)** Calculate the **displacement** $\Delta$ in tonnes. > **(b)** Calculate the **deadweight tonnage (DWT)** and the **cargo carrying capacity** (in m³) if the cargo density is 0.90 t/m³ and the lightweight is as given. #### Part (a) — Displacement Calculation (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the governing formula: $$ \nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T $$ **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Substitute values: $$ \nabla = 0.83 \times 280 \times 48 \times 20 $$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute intermediate: $$ \nabla = 0.83 \times 268{,}800 = 223{,}104 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply Archimedes' principle: $$ \Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 223{,}104 $$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Final answer: $$ \boxed{\Delta = 228{,}681.6 \text{ tonnes} \approx 228{,}682 \text{ t}} $$ **Step 6 (1 Mark):** Statement of units and physical interpretation: This is the total mass of seawater displaced at the loaded draft, equivalent to the total loaded mass of the vessel. #### Part (b) — DWT and Cargo Capacity (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Apply the DWT definition: $$ DWT = \Delta - LWT $$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Substitute: $$ DWT = 228{,}682 - 35{,}000 = 193{,}682 \text{ tonnes} $$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** State the formula for cargo mass. Assuming the entire deadweight is devoted to cargo (simplified — bunker/ballast neglected as per question intent): $$ M_{cargo} \approx 193{,}682 \text{ t} $$ **Step 4 (2 Marks):** Convert cargo mass to volume using cargo density: $$ V_{cargo} = \frac{M_{cargo}}{\rho_{cargo}} = \frac{193{,}682}{0.90} $$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Final answer: $$ \boxed{V_{cargo} \approx 215{,}202.2 \text{ m}^3} $$ **Step 6 (1 Mark):** Sanity check: A Suezmax typically has 2 million barrels capacity (≈ 318,000 m³). Our value of 215,000 m³ is **lower than maximum** because the question specifies a single density of 0.90 t/m³ and assumes all DWT is cargo — this is consistent with the problem constraints. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Unit consistency:** Always keep t and m³ separate. Do NOT write "tonnes/m³". > - **Density interpretation:** $\rho_{cargo} = 0.90$ t/m³ means **1 m³ weighs 0.90 tonnes** — to find volume from mass, **divide** by density. Many students mistakenly multiply. > - **Show ALL substitution steps** — KTU 2024 scheme awards partial credit only when intermediate values are visible. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tankers (Module 1)** - ✅ **Definition:** A tanker is a flush-deck ship with segregated cargo tanks for liquid bulk cargo. - ✅ **Classification axes:** Cargo type, Size (DWT bands), Hull form (single vs. double). - ✅ **Size bands to remember:** Handysize → Aframax → Suezmax → VLCC → ULCC. - ✅ **Block coefficient $C_B$:** 0.80 – 0.85 for tankers; this is **fatter** than container ships (~0.65). - ✅ **Core formula:** $\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ and $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$. - ✅ **MARPOL Annex I Reg. 19:** Mandates double hull for tankers delivered after 31 May 1996. - ✅ **Double bottom dimension:** $\max(B/15,\ 2\text{ m})$. **Double side dimension:** $\max(B/10,\ 2\text{ m})$. - ✅ **Segregated Ballast Tanks (SBT):** Located within the double hull; prevent oil-contaminated ballast discharge. - ✅ **Engine room + pump room:** Located **aft** to keep hazardous spaces away from accommodation. - ✅ **Inert Gas System (IGS):** Supplies scrubbed flue gas to cargo tank ullage to prevent explosive mixtures. - ✅ **Density pitfall:** Cargo volume = mass ÷ density (not mass × density). - ✅ **Historical context:** Erika (1999) and Prestige (2002) casualties accelerated double-hull phase-out. - ✅ **KTU 2024 marking focus:** Always state units, always state governing regulations, always show step-by-step substitutions.

topics.title: Fishing Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fishing Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fishing Vessel** is a boat or ship used to catch fish or other aquatic organisms in open water bodies — oceans, seas, rivers, and lakes. According to the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, as referenced under the **Torremolinos International Convention for the Safety of Fishing Vessels (1977)** and its **1993 Protocol (SFV)**, a fishing vessel is defined as *"a vessel used commercially for catching fish, whales, seals, walrus or other living resources of the sea."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For naval architecture purposes, a fishing vessel is a *working vessel* (as opposed to a transport vessel). Its design is **mission-driven** — every line, space, and weight is optimized around the **fishing method, target species, fishing grounds, and catch-handling cycle**.

In **KTU marine design terminology**, fishing vessels are classified primarily by their **fishing gear/method** and secondarily by:
- Hull material (steel, wood, FRP/composite, aluminum)
- Operating zone (inshore, mid-water, deep-sea)
- Length Overall (LOA)
- Power installation (inboard, outboard, sail-assisted)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Fishing Vessel as a "Mobile Factory on Water":**
> Imagine a fishing vessel not as a *ship* but as a **floating, self-propelled factory + warehouse + hotel combined into one steel box that goes to where the raw material (fish) is found.**
> - The **hull** is the *truck chassis*.
> - The **engine room** is the *engine* that takes the factory to the fish.
> - The **deck** is the *working floor* where the "raw material" is hauled in and processed.
> - The **fish hold** is the *cold storage warehouse* that preserves the catch.
> - The **accommodation** is the *hotel* for the crew working long shifts.
>
> Unlike a cargo ship that *carries pre-made goods between two fixed places*, a fishing vessel must **hunt, capture, process, and preserve** its cargo at sea. This dual identity (hunter + processor + transporter) makes its design uniquely demanding.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (KTU Reference)

The following metrics are universally used in KTU board examinations and design textbooks:

| Metric | Standard Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Length Overall | **LOA** | 6 m – 100+ m |
| Length Between Perpendiculars | **LBP** | 0.95 × LOA |
| Breadth (Moulded) | **B** | 0.25 – 0.35 × LOA |
| Depth (Moulded) | **D** | 0.10 – 0.20 × LOA |
| Design Draft | **T** | 0.50 – 0.80 × D |
| Displacement | **Δ** | 5 t – 5000+ t |
| Service Speed | **V_s** | 8 – 14 knots |
| Gross Tonnage | **GT** | < 10 to > 3000 |

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Fishing Vessels (KTU Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always classify a fishing vessel by **its gear type first**, then by **operating zone**. Examiners expect this hierarchy.

**1. Passive Gear Vessels (Stationary fishing)**
- **Gillnetters** — use vertical nets that entangle fish by their gills.
- **Longliners** — deploy longlines with baited hooks (tuna, cod).
- **Purse Seiners** — encircle schools of pelagic fish (sardine, mackerel, tuna).
- **Trappers/Pot Vessels** — use baited pots for crab, lobster, shrimp.

**2. Active Gear Vessels (Moving fishing)**
- **Trawlers** (Side, Stern, Outrigger, Pair) — drag a conical net through water.
- **Dredgers** (for shellfish, scallops).
- **Pole and Line / Tuna Pole Vessels** (live-bait fishing, e.g., skipjack).
- **Purse Seiners with Fish Pumps** (modern tuna vessels).

**3. Multipurpose / Combination Vessels**
- **Wet-fish Trawlers**, **Factory Trawlers**, **Whalers**, **Sealers**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy of Fishing Vessels — A Stepwise Logic

Unlike cargo ships, a fishing vessel's design is constrained by **five non-negotiable operational demands**. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can identify which design parameter is *driven* by which operational demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Five-Pillar" Design Framework (KTU High-Yield Concept):**

1. **Pillar 1 — The Catch (Fishing Gear Compatibility)**
   - The hull form, deck layout, and stability must accommodate a **specific fishing gear** (trawl winch, net drum, longline hauler, brailing platform, etc.).
   - The size of the gear dictates **bollard pull**, **transom clearances**, and **stern ramp dimensions** (for stern trawlers).

2. **Pillar 2 — The Hold (Catch Preservation)**
   - **Refrigeration / Icing capacity** governs the **fish-hold volume** = $V_{hold}$.
   - Typical: $V_{hold} \approx 0.15 \text{ to } 0.30 \times \text{Enclosed Volume below deck}$.

3. **Pillar 3 — The Crew (Habitability)**
   - **ILO 188 / MLC 2006** mandates minimum accommodation standards.
   - Crew size is roughly $N_{crew} \approx 0.10 \times LOA \text{ (in m)} + 2$ (rough rule for small craft).

4. **Pillar 4 — The Environment (Seakeeping)**
   - Fishing vessels operate in **unpredictable, often severe weather** (small inshore boats, North Sea trawlers).
   - **Roll, pitch, and slamming** govern hull-form selection (e.g., fine entry, soft bilge).

5. **Pillar 5 — The Economics (Profitability)**
   - **Capital cost** vs. **fish-catching capacity** vs. **fuel consumption** is optimized using:
$$
C_{operational} = C_{fuel} + C_{crew} + C_{maintenance} + C_{gear-replacement}
$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-favorite** topics. Master every one of them — they appear in both Module 1 and Module 2 (Resistance & Propulsion) integration problems.

### A. Hull Form Ratios (Dimensionless Design Numbers)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_B &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} \quad &\text{(Block Coefficient)} \\
C_P &= \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot A_M} \quad &\text{(Prismatic Coefficient)} \\
C_M &= \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} \quad &\text{(Midship Coefficient)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\nabla$ = Volumetric displacement (m³)
- $A_M$ = Midship section area (m²)

**Typical fishing-vessel values:** $C_B \approx 0.55$–$0.68$, $C_P \approx 0.60$–$0.72$, $C_M \approx 0.85$–$0.95$.

### B. Displacement & Weight

$$
\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla \quad \text{(in tonnes, with } \rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m³ for seawater)}
$$

$$
W_{total} = W_{hull} + W_{machinery} + W_{outfit} + W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{crew+lub}
$$

**Deadweight Ratio (DWR) for fishing vessels:**
$$
DWR = \frac{W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{stores}}{W_{total}} \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.40
$$

### C. Power Requirements (links Module 1 + Module 2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{(Effective Horse Power, metric)} \\
BHP &= \frac{EHP}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_H} \quad \text{(Brake Horse Power)} \\
MCR &\approx (1.15 \text{ to } 1.25) \times BHP \quad \text{(Max Continuous Rating)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = Total resistance (kgf or N)
- $V_s$ = Service speed (m/s or knots — adjust with constant)
- $\eta_D$ = Propulsive efficiency ($0.55$–$0.65$)
- $\eta_H$ = Hull efficiency ($0.95$–$1.05$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for fishing vessels:** Unlike cargo ships, fishing vessels alternate between **sailing (transit)** and **towing/drifting (fishing)**. The **main engine must be sized for BOTH modes**, often making the MCR much higher than the transit BHP.

### D. Stability — The GZ Curve (Critical for KTU Board)

For a fishing vessel, the righting lever $GZ$ at a heel angle $\phi$ is:
$$
GZ(\phi) = GM \cdot \sin(\phi) - \overline{GG_1} \cdot \tan(\phi)
$$

Where $\overline{GG_1}$ is the rise of the center of gravity due to free-surface effects in fish holds with loose ice/water (the **free-surface effect (FSE)** is a notorious exam topic for fishing vessels).

**IMO SFV / KTU Stability Criteria:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{initial} &\geq 0.35 \text{ m} \\
A_{\phi=0 \to 30°} &\geq 0.055 \text{ m·rad} \\
A_{\phi=0 \to 40°} &\geq 0.090 \text{ m·rad} \\
A_{\phi=30° \to 40°} &\geq 0.030 \text{ m·rad} \\
GZ_{max} &\geq 0.20 \text{ m at } \phi \geq 30° \\
\theta_{range} &\geq 60° \text{ (weather criterion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### E. Fish-Hold Capacity (Catch-Weight Estimation)

$$
W_{catch} = V_{hold} \cdot \rho_{fish} \cdot \text{(stowage factor)}
$$

Typical bulk densities:
- Whole iced fish: $\rho \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ t/m³
- Frozen block: $\rho \approx 0.5$ t/m³
- Fish in ice: $\rho \approx 0.7$ t/m³

### F. Freeboard (Load Line) — Simplified

$$
F_{min} = f \cdot \frac{\nabla}{S} \cdot \frac{L}{D}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = freeboard coefficient (from tables)
- $S$ = total length of superstructure
- Used with **ILLC 1966** (KTU Module 1 reference).

---

## 2.3 Summary Table — KTU High-Yield Equations

| Equation | Physical Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Hull fullness | Resistance & capacity estimate |
| $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Mass from volume | Weight estimation |
| $EHP = R_T \cdot V / 75$ | Power to overcome resistance | Engine sizing |
| $BHP = EHP / (\eta_D \eta_H)$ | Shaft power after losses | Engine selection |
| $GZ = GM\sin\phi - \overline{GG_1}\tan\phi$ | Righting lever at heel | Stability check |
| $W_{catch} = V_{hold} \cdot \rho_{fish}$ | Hold capacity to fish weight | Hold sizing |
| $DWR = (\text{DW})/\Delta$ | Payload efficiency | Economic design |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL: In the table above, all vertical bars for absolute values or divisibility have been replaced with ` / ` or `\vert` in the LaTeX source to preserve the markdown table integrity.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two derivations below are **verbatim-style** KTU board solutions. Every algebraic transition is shown. Practice writing them on **A4 unruled paper** as a board-exam dry run.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating the Required Fish-Hold Volume and Total Displacement

### Problem Statement
A 25 m LOA stern trawler is to be designed for the Indian west coast demersal fishery. The vessel must carry 35 tonnes of iced fish per trip. The skipper has specified a service speed of 10 knots. Using typical hull-form coefficients and standard seawater density, **estimate (a) the fish-hold volume, (b) the volumetric displacement, and (c) the Light Ship Weight and Deadweight components.**

### Given Data
- LOA = 25 m, LBP = 24 m, B = 7.0 m, T = 3.2 m, D = 4.0 m
- $C_B$ = 0.60 (typical for trawler)
- $W_{catch}$ = 35 t
- $\rho_{fish,iced}$ = 0.75 t/m³ (assume loose iced catch)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = 1.025 t/m³

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute the fish-hold volume.**

$$
V_{hold} = \frac{W_{catch}}{\rho_{fish,iced}} = \frac{35 \text{ t}}{0.75 \text{ t/m}^3}
$$

$$
V_{hold} = 46.67 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** We invert the density formula because the *required weight* is the design driver, and the hold is sized to *just accommodate* that weight. The KTU board awards full marks **only if** the units are carried throughout.

**Step 2 — Compute the volumetric displacement.**

Using the block-coefficient definition:
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

$$
\nabla = 0.60 \times 24 \times 7.0 \times 3.2
$$

$$
\nabla = 322.56 \text{ m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** The block coefficient $C_B = 0.60$ is **typical for a mid-sized fishing trawler** — fuller than a cargo ship (0.65–0.80) but slimmer than a tug (0.50–0.55). The 0.60 figure balances seakeeping and hold volume.

**Step 3 — Compute the displacement in tonnes (mass).**

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla
$$

$$
\Delta = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3 \times 322.56 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\Delta = 330.62 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4 — Partition the displacement into Light Ship (LS) and Deadweight (DW).**

Assume DW fraction for a fishing vessel: $DW/\Delta \approx 0.35$.
$$
DW = 0.35 \times 330.62 = 115.72 \text{ t}
$$
$$
LS = \Delta - DW = 330.62 - 115.72 = 214.90 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 5 — Break down Deadweight components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{catch} &= 35.0 \text{ t} \quad \text{(specified)} \\
W_{fuel+oil} &\approx 0.20 \times DW = 23.14 \text{ t} \\
W_{stores+crew} &\approx 0.05 \times DW = 5.79 \text{ t} \\
W_{ice+water} &\approx 0.10 \times DW = 11.57 \text{ t} \\
\hline
\text{Total DW} &= 35.0 + 23.14 + 5.79 + 11.57 = 75.50 \text{ t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation Note for Examiner:** The remaining $115.72 - 75.50 = 40.22$ t is normally allocated to **deck machinery weight, nets, gear, and spare parts** — a common KTU examiner expectation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Engine Power Sizing for a Multi-Purpose Trawler

### Problem Statement
A 28 m side trawler has a total resistance of 18,000 kgf at the design speed of 11 knots. Assuming a propulsive efficiency of 0.60 and a hull efficiency of 1.0, and a service margin of 20% over the **maximum** of (transit BHP) and (towing BHP), determine the **recommended Main Engine MCR**. The vessel must also generate 90 kW of auxiliary electrical power for the refrigeration plant.

### Given Data
- $R_T$ (transit) = 18,000 kgf at $V_s$ = 11 knots
- Towing force required at trawl warps: $F_{tow}$ = 4,500 kgf at tow speed $V_{tow}$ = 4 knots
- $\eta_D$ = 0.60, $\eta_H$ = 1.0
- Service margin: 20% (used in marine design as a safety factor)
- 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Convert speeds to SI units.**

$$
V_s = 11 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 5.658 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_{tow} = 4 \text{ knots} \times 0.5144 = 2.058 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EHP (transit) in Watts and then metric HP.**

In SI, $P = F \cdot V$ with force in Newtons.
$$
R_T = 18{,}000 \text{ kgf} \times 9.81 = 176{,}580 \text{ N}
$$

$$
EHP_{transit} = R_T \cdot V_s = 176{,}580 \times 5.658 = 999{,}090 \text{ W} \approx 999.1 \text{ kW}
$$

Convert to metric HP (1 HP = 0.7355 kW):
$$
EHP_{transit} = \frac{999.1}{0.7355} = 1358.4 \text{ HP (metric)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute BHP (transit).**

$$
BHP_{transit} = \frac{EHP_{transit}}{\eta_D \cdot \eta_H} = \frac{1358.4}{0.60 \times 1.0} = 2264.0 \text{ HP}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute EHP and BHP (towing mode).**

$$
F_{tow} = 4{,}500 \text{ kgf} \times 9.81 = 44{,}145 \text{ N}
$$

$$
EHP_{tow} = 44{,}145 \times 2.058 = 90{,}850 \text{ W} = 90.85 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
BHP_{tow} = \frac{90.85 \text{ kW}}{0.60 \times 1.0 \times 0.7355 \text{ kW/HP}} = 205.8 \text{ HP (metric)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and select the controlling mode.**

$$
BHP_{transit} = 2264.0 \text{ HP} \quad \gg \quad BHP_{tow} = 205.8 \text{ HP}
$$

> **Logic:** The **transit mode dominates** the engine sizing for this vessel. The trawl mode is far less demanding. This is **typical of trawlers operating short distances to fishing grounds** (e.g., Indian coastal trawlers).

**Step 6 — Apply the 20% service margin to get MCR.**

$$
MCR = 1.20 \times BHP_{transit} = 1.20 \times 2264.0 = 2716.8 \text{ HP}
$$

In kW: $MCR \approx 1997 \text{ kW}$ (round to **2000 kW** for a commercial engine selection).

**Step 7 — Auxiliary engine sizing.**

$$
P_{aux} = 90 \text{ kW (specified)}
$$

A standard auxiliary generator of **100–120 kVA** (≈ 90 kW at 0.8 power factor) would be selected.

### Final Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $BHP_{transit}$ | 2264 HP |
| $BHP_{tow}$ | 205.8 HP |
| **Selected MCR** | **≈ 2717 HP (≈ 2000 kW)** |
| Auxiliary Generator | ≈ 100 kVA |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard** rules: all node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix. Labels contain no markdown bold/italics and no HTML tables — only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Fishing Vessel Classification by Gear

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[FISHING VESSEL] --> nodeB[GEAR TYPE]
    nodeB --> nodeC[ACTIVE GEAR]
    nodeB --> nodeD[PASSIVE GEAR]
    nodeB --> nodeE[MULTIPURPOSE]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[STERN TRAWLER]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[SIDE TRAWLER]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[OUTRIGGER TRAWLER]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[DREDGER]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[PURSE SEINER]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[LONGLINER]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[GILLNETTER]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[POT TRAPPER]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[FACTORY TRAWLER]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[COMBINATION VESSEL]

    nodeC1 --> nodeF[STERN RAMP\nNET DRUM\nAFT DECK STRENGTH]
    nodeC2 --> nodeG[SIDE GALLows\nBOOM TRAVEL]
    nodeD1 --> nodeH[SKIFF\nPOWER BLOCK\nBRAILING STATIONS]
    nodeD2 --> nodeI[HAULER\nBAIT STORAGE\nLINE POOL]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Block-Level Design Workflow for a Trawler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[MISSION PROFILE] --> node2[FISHING GEAR\nSELECTION]
    node2 --> node3[OPERATING\nZONE]
    node3 --> node4[PRINCIPAL\nDIMENSIONS]
    node4 --> node5[HULL FORM\nCOEFFICIENTS]
    node5 --> node6[STABILITY\nGM GZ]
    node6 --> node7[POWER\nSIZING]
    node7 --> node8[ARRANGEMENT\nPLAN]
    node8 --> node9[CLASS AND\nFLAG APPROVAL]

    node1 --> nodeN1[CATCH WEIGHT\nCREW SIZE\nTRIP DURATION]
    node4 --> nodeN2[LOA LBP B T D]
    node5 --> nodeN3[CB CP CM]
    node7 --> nodeN4[EHP BHP MCR]
    node8 --> nodeN5[ENGINE ROOM\nHOLD DECK\nACCOMMODATION]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Onboard Systems of a Modern Fishing Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS[VESSEL SYSTEM] --> nodeS1[HULL\nSTRUCTURE]
    nodeS --> nodeS2[PROPULSION\nAND STEERING]
    nodeS --> nodeS3[FISHING\nDECK GEAR]
    nodeS --> nodeS4[CATCH\nHANDLING]
    nodeS --> nodeS5[AUXILIARY\nSYSTEMS]

    nodeS3 --> nodeS3A[TRAWL WINCH]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3B[NET DRUM]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3C[CRANES AND\nBOOMS]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS3D[AUXILIARY\nWINCHES]

    nodeS4 --> nodeS4A[FISH HOLD]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4B[REFRIGERATION\nPLANT]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4C[PROCESSING\nAREA]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS4D[PACKING\nAND STOWAGE]

    nodeS5 --> nodeS5A[ELECTRICAL]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS5B[BALLAST]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS5C[FRESH WATER]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS5D[FIRE FIGHTING]
```

## 4.4 Stability — Free-Surface Effect in Fish Hold (Schematic Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeFS[FISH HOLD WITH LOOSE ICE\nAND LIQUID SLOSH] --> nodeFS1[PARTIAL\nFILLING]
    nodeFS1 --> nodeFS2[VIRTUAL RISE OF G\nDUE TO FSE]
    nodeFS2 --> nodeFS3[GM REDUCED\nEFFECTIVELY]
    nodeFS3 --> nodeFS4[STABILITY\nCOMPROMISED]
    nodeFS4 --> nodeFS5[MITIGATION\nFULL HOLD OR\nBULKHEADS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) papers**. Marks are split exactly as per the official KTU pattern: Part A (3 marks each), Part B (14 marks each with internal sub-choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OENST721 Module 1]`**
*"With a neat sketch, classify fishing vessels based on the type of fishing gear employed. State two distinguishing features of each."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Fishing vessels are broadly classified into **Active-Gear** and **Passive-Gear** types.

1. **Active-Gear Vessels** (vessel moves to catch fish):
   - *Trawlers* — drag a conical net; prominent stern ramp or side gantries.
   - *Dredgers* — drag a metal-framed net for shellfish.

2. **Passive-Gear Vessels** (vessel is stationary or drifts):
   - *Purse Seiners* — encircle pelagic schools; equipped with a *skiff* and *power block*.
   - *Longliners* — set longlines with hooks; equipped with an automatic *line hauler*.

*(One sketch showing a stern trawler with net drum is expected. → +1 mark for sketch.)*

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OENST721 Module 1]`**
*"Explain the significance of the Free-Surface Effect (FSE) on the intact stability of a fishing vessel."* **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Free-Surface Effect (FSE)** arises when a partially filled tank (such as a fish hold with loose ice, bilge water, or loose fish) contains liquid that can **slosh freely** as the vessel heels.

- The liquid's center of gravity shifts **outboard**, creating a **virtual rise** in the vessel's effective center of gravity $G$.
- The effective metacentric height reduces:
$$
GM_{eff} = GM_{intact} - \overline{GG_1}
$$
- For **fishing vessels**, FSE is critical because fish holds are often **only partially full** during the early and late stages of a trip.

**Mitigation:** (a) Fill holds completely with fish/ice, (b) Install **transverse wash bulkheads** to subdivide the free-surface length, (c) Avoid slack tanks at sea.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. We provide **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Design of a Stern Trawler

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OENST721, Module 1, Q7(b)]`**

A 30 m LOA stern trawler is to be designed for the southwest coast of India. The principal particulars proposed are: LBP = 28.5 m, B = 8.0 m, D = 4.5 m, T = 3.5 m, $C_B$ = 0.62. Service speed = 11 knots. The vessel must carry 50 t of iced fish per trip, with 20 tonnes of fuel oil and 8 tonnes of freshwater/ice/provisions.

**(a)** Calculate the **volumetric displacement**, the **mass displacement**, and verify whether the **DWR is in the recommended range for a fishing vessel of this size.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a suitable **freeboard estimate** of 0.6 m and a block coefficient of 0.62, **comment on the suitability of the given T/D ratio and provide one design suggestion** to improve initial stability. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Volumetric Displacement:**
$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$
$$
\nabla = 0.62 \times 28.5 \times 8.0 \times 3.5 = 494.76 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Mass Displacement:**
$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 494.76 = 507.13 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3 — Deadweight:**
$$
DW = W_{catch} + W_{fuel} + W_{stores} = 50 + 20 + 8 = 78 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 4 — DWR:**
$$
DWR = \frac{DW}{\Delta} = \frac{78}{507.13} = 0.154 \text{ (i.e., 15.4\%)}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**
The recommended DWR for a **30 m fishing trawler** is **0.20 to 0.30**. The computed value of **0.154 is below the recommended range**, indicating:
- Either the vessel is **under-loaded** (suggesting the owner intends to add more catch capacity, ice, or fuel).
- Or the principal dimensions are **over-sized** for the specified mission.

**Conclusion:** The DWR should be increased by either **reducing LBP** to ~24 m or **increasing the design catch** to ~80 t.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**T/D Ratio Check:**
$$
\frac{T}{D} = \frac{3.5}{4.5} = 0.778
$$

The **typical T/D for fishing trawlers** is **0.65 to 0.80**. The computed ratio of **0.778 lies within the recommended range** — therefore the **draft is reasonable**, but the **freeboard is on the lower side** (F/D = 0.6/4.5 = 0.133, slightly below the 0.15–0.20 typical range).

**Design Suggestion for Improved Initial Stability:**

1. **Reduce the vertical center of gravity (KG):** Place the **fish hold low in the hull** and put the **wheelhouse, fuel tanks, and heavy machinery low in the superstructure**.
2. **Increase the beam (B):** A wider beam increases the **transverse metacentric radius BM**, since $BM = I_T / \nabla$, where $I_T$ is the second moment of the waterplane.
3. **Add bilge keels** to damp roll, although this affects motions more than initial GM.

**Recommended action:** Increase the beam by ~0.5 m to improve BM, or shift the wheelhouse forward and lower the fuel-tank tops.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Power Sizing and Stability of a Multi-Purpose Vessel

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OENST721, Module 1, Q8(a)]`**

A 22 m multi-purpose fishing vessel operates both as a **gillnetter (passive mode)** and as a **small trawler (active mode)**.

Given:
- $R_T$ (transit, 9 knots) = 9,500 kgf
- Towing speed (active) = 3.5 knots; Towing force = 3,200 kgf
- $\eta_D$ = 0.58, $\eta_H$ = 1.05
- Service margin: 18%
- $V_{hold}$ = 38 m³, average iced-fish density = 0.78 t/m³

**(a)** Determine the **main engine MCR in kW** for the controlling mode of operation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Estimate the **maximum catch weight** the vessel can carry in a single trip and the **effective fish-hold utilization** if the trip is designed to maximize catch. State one design consequence of carrying the hold to 100% capacity. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert speeds:**
$$
V_{transit} = 9 \times 0.5144 = 4.630 \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
V_{tow} = 3.5 \times 0.5144 = 1.800 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert forces to Newtons:**
$$
F_{transit} = 9500 \times 9.81 = 93{,}195 \text{ N}
$$
$$
F_{tow} = 3200 \times 9.81 = 31{,}392 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — EHP (transit):**
$$
EHP_{transit} = 93{,}195 \times 4.630 = 431{,}492 \text{ W} = 431.49 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — BHP (transit):**
$$
BHP_{transit} = \frac{431.49}{0.58 \times 1.05} = \frac{431.49}{0.609} = 708.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — EHP (towing):**
$$
EHP_{tow} = 31{,}392 \times 1.800 = 56{,}505.6 \text{ W} = 56.51 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 — BHP (tow):**
$$
BHP_{tow} = \frac{56.51}{0.609} = 92.8 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Controlling mode:**
$$
BHP_{transit} = 708.5 \text{ kW} \gg BHP_{tow} = 92.8 \text{ kW}
$$

The **transit mode governs**.

**Step 8 — Apply 18% service margin:**
$$
MCR = 1.18 \times 708.5 = 836.0 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final MCR ≈ 836 kW** (round to commercial size of **~900 kW** for off-the-shelf engine selection).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Maximum catch weight:**
$$
W_{catch,max} = V_{hold} \cdot \rho_{fish} = 38 \times 0.78 = 29.64 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 2 — Fish-hold utilization:**
At full capacity, utilization is **100%** — but practical fill (with ice channels, drainage, and stowage gaps) is **85–90%**. So:
$$
W_{catch,practical} = 0.88 \times 29.64 \approx 26.08 \text{ t}
$$

**Step 3 — Design consequence of 100% fill:**
A completely full fish hold **eliminates Free-Surface Effect** (because there is no sloshing liquid), which is **beneficial for stability**. However, it:
- Eliminates the ability to **redistribute weight** during the trip.
- Causes **overloading of deck plating and refrigeration coils** with thermal mass.
- Forces the vessel to **return to port immediately** to offload, reducing fishing time.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes That Cost Marks in OENST721 Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the conversion constant** $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$ in power problems — KTU board examiners will deduct **1 full mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing EHP with BHP** — EHP is the *useful* power at the propeller; BHP is at the *engine* output. Mixing them = lost marks.
> 3. **Using $g = 9.81$ incorrectly** — When force is given in **kgf**, you **must convert to Newtons** by multiplying by 9.81 before using $P = F \cdot V$ in SI.
> 4. **Ignoring the FSE in stability problems** — Whenever a fishing-vessel stability question mentions a *partially filled* fish hold, FSE is *implicit*. Not mentioning it = at least 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — A bare number in the answer sheet gets **0 marks**, even if the calculation is correct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Fishing Vessels" (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** A fishing vessel is a *working vessel* that hunts, captures, and processes fish — classified primarily by **gear type** and secondarily by **operating zone**.
- **Five Design Pillars:** Catch, Hold, Crew, Environment, Economics.
- **Key Hull Ratios:** $L/B \approx 3$–$5$, $B/T \approx 2.0$–$2.5$, $T/D \approx 0.65$–$0.80$, $C_B \approx 0.55$–$0.68$.
- **DWR for fishing vessels:** Typically **0.20–0.40**.
- **Power Sizing:** Engine must satisfy **max(transit BHP, towing BHP)** plus **service margin (15–25%)**.
- **Speed Conversion:** Always remember $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$.
- **Stability Criteria (IMO SFV):** $GM \geq 0.35$ m, $A_{30°} \geq 0.055$ m·rad, $A_{40°} \geq 0.090$ m·rad, $GZ_{max} \geq 0.20$ m.
- **FSE** is the single most important stability hazard for fishing vessels due to **partially filled fish holds**.
- **Class Regulations:** Indian fishing vessels $>24$ m LOA must be classed by **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping)**; smaller vessels follow the **Mercantile Marine Department (MMD)** and state fisheries rules.
- **International Convention:** **IMO Torremolinos Convention 1977 / 1993 Protocol (SFV)** — the primary regulatory backbone.
- **Freeboard:** Governed by the **International Load Line Convention (ILLC) 1966**.
- **Refrigeration:** Modern fishing vessels use either **RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water)** or **CSW (Chilled Sea Water)** systems — the hold volume required is *lower* than for traditional icing.

---

**End of Notes — Module 1: Fishing Vessels**
*Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 / NEP 2020 Aligned / 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factory Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factory Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factory Ship (Definition):** A *factory ship* is a large, ocean-going vessel that functions as a **floating industrial processing plant**. It is fitted with specialized machinery for receiving, processing, preserving, and storing marine catches (fish, krill, whales, or shellfish) harvested either by its own fishing gear or by a fleet of accompanying catcher boats. The vessel is also referred to as a *mother ship*, *processor vessel*, or *floating factory* in KTU-recommended marine design literature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Classification Note:** Under the OENST721 syllabus, factory ships belong to the broader category of **Special-Purpose Commercial Vessels**, distinct from carriers, tankers, or container ships. The course outcome CO1 requires students to identify the functional design drivers specific to such vessels.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Canteen-Kitchen" Idea:**
> Imagine a giant ship that works exactly like a **five-star food processing factory** — but instead of sitting on land, it floats in the middle of the ocean. While traditional fishing boats catch fish, freeze them, and rush back to the shore-based factory within days, a *factory ship* **brings the entire factory to the fishing ground itself**. Smaller catcher boats unload the fresh catch at the mother ship's stern or side slip, and within hours the fish is gutted, filleted, frozen, canned, or converted into fish meal — all while the ship remains offshore for **months at a time**.

Think of it this way:
- **Land-based fish processing plant** + **Reefer ship** + **Trawler** = **Factory Ship**
- A trawler *catches* fish; a factory ship *catches AND manufactures* products.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard metrics define a factory ship's scale and are commonly referenced in KTU numerical problems:

| Metric | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Length Overall (LOA)** | **70 m to 200+ m** |
| **Daily Processing Throughput** | **100 to 800 tonnes/day** |
| **Endurance (Range)** | **60 to 90 days at sea** |
| **Crew Complement** | **80 to 350 persons** |
| **Raw Fish to Fish Meal Ratio** | **5 : 1 to 6 : 1** (mass) |
| **Refrigeration Temperature (Frozen Hold)** | **−25 °C to −30 °C** |
| **Refrigeration Temperature (Chilled Hold)** | **0 °C to +2 °C** |

### 1.4 Historical & Operational Context

> [!NOTE]
> The first true factory ships were **whale factory ships** of the early 20th century (e.g., Norwegian *Lancing* and Soviet *Slava*-class). Modern factory ships are dominated by **fish factory trawlers** and **krill processors** (e.g., Norwegian *Atlantic* and *Saga Sea*-class). The design driver has evolved from raw throughput to **hygiene, automation, and fuel-efficient processing**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Factory Ships

Factory ships are classified by **target species**, **processing technique**, and **catch method**. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following categories:

#### A. By Target Species
1. **Whale Factory Ships** — historically used to render whale oil and baleen; now obsolete due to the 1986 IWC moratorium.
2. **Fish Factory Ships** — process demersal/pelagic fish into fillets, fish meal, and fish oil.
3. **Krill Factory Ships** — convert Antarctic krill into meal, paste, and frozen tails.
4. **Tuna Factory Ships** — produce canned or frozen tuna, with brine refrigeration.
5. **Crab/Shrimp Factory Ships** — equipped with cooking, peeling, and freezing lines.

#### B. By Catch Method
1. **Factory Trawlers** — *catch + process* in a single hull (stern trawlers with factory decks).
2. **Mother Ships with Catcher Boats** — *receives* catch from a fleet of smaller vessels.

### 2.2 Functional Subsystems (Step-by-Step Logic)

A factory ship is engineered around **six interconnected subsystems**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Core Subsystems of a Factory Ship:**
> 1. **Catch Reception System** — stern ramps, side slips, mawsers; receives raw catch from nets or catcher boats.
> 2. **Pre-Processing System** — gutting, washing, grading, and sorting conveyors.
> 3. **Processing System** — filleting, cooking, pressing, and rendering machinery.
> 4. **Preservation System** — plate freezers, blast freezers, brine tanks, canning autoclaves.
> 5. **Storage System** — refrigerated cargo holds (RSW — Refrigerated Sea Water) and dry cargo holds.
> 6. **Utilities System** — power generation, steam boilers, refrigeration compressors, water makers, and waste handling.

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Subsystem

- **Why reception at stern/side?** — Direct vertical lift of fish from net or platform minimizes bruising and accelerates throughput (typical **catch-to-processing window ≤ 3 hours**).
- **Why dedicated pre-processing deck?** — Gutting within **45 minutes** of catch preserves freshness; delay produces histamine and bacterial spoilage.
- **Why plate freezers?** — They are the most **energy-efficient** freezing method for whole fish, achieving core temperature **−25 °C** in **2 to 3 hours**.
- **Why RSW (Refrigerated Sea Water) holds?** — Fish stored at **0 °C** retain freshness for **7–10 days** versus **3 days** in ice.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mandatory formulae** for any KTU numerical or theoretical question on factory ships. Note: Pipes in expressions use `\vert` notation to preserve table syntax integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigeration Heat Load | $Q_{R} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | kJ/day | Sizes compressor plants |
| 2 | Freezing Latent Heat | $Q_{L} = m \cdot L_{f}$ | kJ | Latent load on freezers |
| 3 | Fish Meal Yield | $Y_{meal} = \dfrac{m_{raw}}{\alpha}$ | tonnes | Where $\alpha = 5$ to $6$ |
| 4 | Fish Oil Yield | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{raw}}{\beta}$ | tonnes | Where $\beta = 20$ to $25$ |
| 5 | Daily Throughput | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{M_{hold}}{T_{trip}}$ | tonnes/day | Sizes processing line |
| 6 | Storage Volume | $V_{hold} = \dfrac{m_{catch}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | m³ | Sizes refrigerated hold |
| 7 | Steam Boiler Capacity | $Q_{steam} = \dot{m}_{cook} \cdot (h_{steam} - h_{feed})$ | kW | Sizes boiler plant |
| 8 | Power Plant Sizing | $P_{gen} = P_{proc} + P_{refrig} + P_{aux}$ | kW | Main generator selection |
| 9 | Free Surface Correction | $\Delta GM = -\dfrac{\rho \cdot i_{x}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla}$ | m | Stability check for liquid cargo |
| 10 | Endurance Fuel | $M_{fuel} = P_{aux} \cdot T_{end} \cdot s_{foc}$ | tonnes | Bunker capacity |

Where:
- $m$ = mass processed (kg or tonnes/day)
- $c_{p}$ = specific heat of fish $\approx 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K)
- $L_{f}$ = latent heat of fusion of fish $\approx 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk density of frozen fish $\approx 0.55 \text{ t/m}^3$
- $i_{x}$ = transverse moment of inertia of free surface (m⁴)
- $s_{foc}$ = specific fuel oil consumption $\approx 0.22 \text{ kg/kWh}$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** Factory ship design is the canonical example used in KTU to illustrate the conflict between **carrying capacity (deadweight)** and **plant capacity (volumetric machinery space)**. The vessel's **block coefficient $C_B$** is typically **0.55 to 0.65** — lower than a bulk carrier — because the hull must balance **stability** (heavily penalized by free-surface liquid cargo and high-mounted processing gear) with **operational seakeeping** in adverse weather.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sample Problem Framework (KTU Model)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern):**
> *"A fish factory trawler is designed for a 75-day fishing campaign. The daily raw catch is 400 tonnes/day. The fish is processed into three streams: 60 % frozen whole fish, 25 % fish meal, and 15 % fish oil. If the vessel must store the entire trip's processed product and return, calculate:*
> *(a) The daily mass of fish meal and fish oil produced.*
> *(b) The total refrigerated cargo hold volume required.*
> *(c) The average daily refrigeration load, assuming catch is cooled from $+15\text{ °C}$ to $-25\text{ °C}$."*

---

### 3.2 Part (a) — Daily Mass of Meal and Oil (Step-by-Step)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{raw} = 400 \text{ tonnes/day}$
- Meal yield ratio $\alpha = 5$ (i.e., 5 tonnes raw fish → 1 tonne meal)
- Oil yield ratio $\beta = 20$ (i.e., 20 tonnes raw fish → 1 tonne oil)

**Step 1 — Daily raw fish mass going to meal stream (25 % of catch):**

$$
\dot{m}_{meal,raw} = 0.25 \times 400 = 100 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily fish meal mass produced:**

$$
\dot{m}_{meal} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{meal,raw}}{\alpha} = \dfrac{100}{5} = 20 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily raw fish mass going to oil stream (15 % of catch):**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil,raw} = 0.15 \times 400 = 60 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily fish oil mass produced:**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{oil,raw}}{\beta} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Final Answers (a):** $\dot{m}_{meal} = 20 \text{ t/day}$, $\dot{m}_{oil} = 3 \text{ t/day}$.

---

### 3.3 Part (b) — Total Refrigerated Hold Volume

**Step 1 — Total campaign duration in days:**

$$
T_{trip} = 75 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Total frozen whole fish produced (60 % of catch):**

$$
M_{frozen} = 0.60 \times 400 \times 75 = 18{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3 — Total fish meal stored (bulk density $\rho_{meal} = 0.6 \text{ t/m}^3$):**

$$
M_{meal} = 20 \times 75 = 1{,}500 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
V_{meal} = \dfrac{1{,}500}{0.6} = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Total fish oil stored (density $\rho_{oil} = 0.92 \text{ t/m}^3$, oil tanks):**

$$
M_{oil} = 3 \times 75 = 225 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
V_{oil} = \dfrac{225}{0.92} \approx 244.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Total frozen whole fish volume ($\rho_{bulk,fish} = 0.55 \text{ t/m}^3$):**

$$
V_{frozen} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{0.55} \approx 32{,}727 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Total refrigerated + storage volume:**

$$
V_{total} = V_{frozen} + V_{meal} + V_{oil} = 32{,}727 + 2{,}500 + 244.6 \approx 35{,}471.6 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final Answer (b):** $V_{total} \approx 35{,}472 \text{ m}^3$.

---

### 3.4 Part (c) — Average Daily Refrigeration Load

**Given:**
- $\Delta T = (+15) - (-25) = 40 \text{ K}$
- $c_{p,fish} = 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ (above freezing)
- $c_{p,ice} = 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$ (below freezing, sensible)
- $L_{f} = 235 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (latent)
- Freezing point of fish $\approx -1 \text{ °C}$ → $\Delta T_{above} = 16 \text{ K}$, $\Delta T_{below} = 24 \text{ K}$

**Step 1 — Daily frozen fish mass requiring refrigeration:**

$$
\dot{m}_{frozen} = 0.60 \times 400 = 240 \text{ tonnes/day} = 240{,}000 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat above freezing (cooling from +15 °C to -1 °C):**

$$
Q_{1} = 240{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 16 = 13{,}440{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat of freezing:**

$$
Q_{2} = 240{,}000 \times 235 = 56{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensible heat below freezing (cooling from -1 °C to -25 °C):**

$$
Q_{3} = 240{,}000 \times 2.1 \times 24 = 12{,}096{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Total daily refrigeration load:**

$$
Q_{R,total} = Q_{1} + Q_{2} + Q_{3} = 13{,}440{,}000 + 56{,}400{,}000 + 12{,}096{,}000 = 81{,}936{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kW (1 day = 86,400 s):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{R} = \dfrac{81{,}936{,}000}{86{,}400} \approx 948.3 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final Answer (c):** Average refrigeration load $\approx 948.3 \text{ kW}$ (≈ 270 TR, since 1 TR = 3.517 kW).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Subsystem vs. KTU Design Driver

| Subsystem | KTU Design Driver | Critical Numerical Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Reception Ramp | Throughput & Fish Quality | Catch-to-process time $\le 3 \text{ h}$ |
| Pre-Processing | Hygiene & Yield | Gutter capacity = $1.5 \times$ peak hourly intake |
| Plate Freezer | Energy Efficiency | Freezing time $t_{f} \approx \dfrac{\rho L_{f} d}{T_{f} - T_{c}}$ |
| RSW Hold | Freshness Retention | Brine temperature $\approx -1.5 \text{ °C}$ |
| Steam Boiler | Process Heat | $Q_{steam} \approx 0.4 \text{ kW per kg/h of catch}$ |
| Generator | Power Availability | Total installed $P_{gen} \approx 1.2 \times P_{proc}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **process flow** of a typical fish factory trawler, from catch reception to packaged product storage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Catcher Boats or Trawl Net] --> B[Catch Reception Stern Ramp]
    B --> C[Pre-Processing Deck - Gutting and Washing]
    C --> D{Sorting Grader}
    D --> E[Filleting Line]
    D --> F[Cooking and Pressing Line]
    D --> G[Whole Fish Conveyor]
    E --> H[Plate Freezer -25 degC]
    F --> I[Meal Silo Storage]
    F --> J[Oil Separator Tank]
    G --> K[Blast Freezer]
    H --> L[Refrigerated Cargo Hold RSW]
    K --> L
    I --> M[Dry Cargo Hold]
    J --> N[Oil Storage Tank]
    L --> O[Outbound Discharge Port]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    P[Steam Boiler] --> F
    P --> H
    Q[Main Generator Set] --> P
    Q --> H
    Q --> K
    R[Refrigeration Compressor] --> H
    R --> K
```

### 4.2 General Arrangement Logic (Schematic Block Diagram)

The following block diagram illustrates the **vertical zonation** of a factory ship, mapping each deck to its functional role.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A0[Navigation Bridge Level]
        A01[Wheelhouse]
        A02[Communications and Control Room]
    end
    subgraph A1[Accommodation Level]
        A11[Crew Cabins]
        A12[Mess and Galley]
    end
    subgraph A2[Upper Working Deck]
        A21[Pre-Processing]
        A22[Sorting Station]
    end
    subgraph A3[Main Processing Deck]
        A31[Filleting Machines]
        A32[Cooking Boilers]
        A33[Plate Freezers]
    end
    subgraph A4[Engine Room Level]
        A41[Main Engine]
        A42[Aux Generators]
        A43[Refrigeration Plant]
    end
    subgraph A5[Lower Hold Level]
        A51[Refrigerated Cargo Hold]
        A52[Oil Tanks]
        A53[Fish Meal Silos]
    end
    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5
```

### 4.3 Subsystem Interaction Matrix (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    proc[Processing Plant Subsystem] -->|consumes steam| boil[Steam Boiler]
    proc -->|consumes power| gen[Generator Set]
    proc -->|requires cold| refrig[Refrigeration Plant]
    refrig -->|driven by| gen
    boil -->|driven by| gen
    refrig -->|supplies cold air to| hold[Refrigerated Hold]
    hold -->|stores output of| proc
    proc -->|uses feed water from| fresh[Fresh Water Generator]
    gen -->|consumes fuel from| bunker[Bunker Tank]
    fresh -->|driven by| gen
    boil -->|consumes feed water from| fresh
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:**
> **Define a factory ship. List the four major processing streams typically found on a fish factory trawler.**

**Model Answer:**

A factory ship is a sea-going vessel equipped with **integrated processing machinery** that converts raw marine catch into **finished, storable products** without returning to port for shore-based processing.

The four major processing streams on a fish factory trawler are:
1. **Whole frozen fish** (block-frozen in plate freezers)
2. **Filleted fish** (H&G — Headed and Gutted; or skinless fillets)
3. **Fish meal** (cooked, pressed, and dried from offal and small fish)
4. **Fish oil** (extracted from press liquor by centrifugal separation)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if all four streams are correctly identified with their **function**, not just their name.

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:**
> **Why are factory ships fitted with plate freezers instead of blast freezers for whole fish?**

**Model Answer:**

Plate freezers are preferred for whole fish because:
1. **Higher energy efficiency** — direct contact conduction freezing eliminates the energy loss of cold-air circulation.
2. **Faster core freezing** — block of fish reaches **−25 °C** in **2 to 3 hours** versus **6 to 8 hours** in a blast freezer.
3. **Uniform product shape** — fish freeze into regular rectangular blocks, optimizing **stowage density** (≈ 0.55 t/m³) in cargo holds.
4. **Reduced dehydration loss** — less surface exposure means lower weight loss (typically **< 1.5 %** versus **3 to 5 %** in blast freezers).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]:**
> *(a) Explain, with a block diagram, the major subsystems of a modern fish factory trawler. (7 marks)*
> *(b) A fish factory trawler processes **350 tonnes of raw catch per day**. The catch is partitioned into **55 % whole frozen fish, 30 % fish meal, and 15 % fish oil**. If the trip lasts **80 days**, calculate:*
> *(i) Total mass of fish meal and fish oil produced.*
> *(ii) Total refrigerated cargo volume (use $\rho_{frozen} = 0.55 \text{ t/m}^3$, $\rho_{meal} = 0.6 \text{ t/m}^3$, $\rho_{oil} = 0.92 \text{ t/m}^3$). (7 marks)*

---

##### **Solution A(a) — Major Subsystems (7 marks):**

A modern fish factory trawler is organized into **six major subsystems**:

1. **Catch Handling System** — stern ramp, mawsers, cod-end emptying, conveyor belts to the processing deck. *Function: transfer catch from net to gutting station in < 45 minutes.*
2. **Pre-Processing System** — gutting machines, wash tanks, graders, weighing conveyors. *Function: clean and classify the catch.*
3. **Processing System** — filleting lines, H&G (headed & gutted) machines, cooking vats, presses, separators. *Function: convert raw fish into product streams.*
4. **Preservation System** — plate freezers, blast tunnels, brine tanks, RSW chilling. *Function: extend shelf life to 6–12 months.*
5. **Storage System** — refrigerated holds at **−25 °C**, dry holds for meal, integral tanks for oil. *Function: stow processed product for the trip.*
6. **Auxiliary System** — boilers, generators, refrigeration compressors, freshwater generators, sewage plant. *Function: provide utilities to all other subsystems.*

**Block Diagram (as drawn in SECTION 4 above) carries 2 marks.**

##### **Solution A(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{raw} = 350 \text{ t/day}$, $T = 80 \text{ days}$.

**Step (i) — Fish meal and oil mass:**

$$
M_{meal} = \dfrac{0.30 \times 350 \times 80}{5} = \dfrac{8{,}400}{5} = 1{,}680 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
M_{oil} = \dfrac{0.15 \times 350 \times 80}{20} = \dfrac{4{,}200}{20} = 210 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step (ii) — Total refrigerated volume:**

$$
M_{frozen} = 0.55 \times 350 \times 80 = 15{,}400 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
V_{frozen} = \dfrac{15{,}400}{0.55} = 28{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_{meal} = \dfrac{1{,}680}{0.6} = 2{,}800 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_{oil} = \dfrac{210}{0.92} \approx 228.3 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
V_{total} = 28{,}000 + 2{,}800 + 228.3 = 31{,}028.3 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Final Answers:** Meal = **1,680 t**; Oil = **210 t**; $V_{total} \approx \mathbf{31{,}028 \text{ m}^3}$.

---

#### **Question B [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]:**
