# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Hydrofoil Lift System Architecture

The diagram below maps the operational architecture of a typical fully-submerged hydrofoil support system (KTU-style functional schematic, avoiding physical-drawing complexity).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Propulsive Power] --> B[Transmission and Gearbox]
    B --> C[Propeller Waterjet or Submerged Propulsor]
    C --> D[Forward Velocity V over Foils]
    D --> E[Front Hydrofoil Port]
    D --> F[Front Hydrofoil Starboard]
    D --> G[Rear Hydrofoil Port]
    D --> H[Rear Hydrofoil Starboard]
    E --> I[Hydrodynamic Lift Generation]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Lift equals Weight}
    J -->|Yes| K[Hull Rises Out of Water]
    J -->|No| L[Continue Acceleration]
    K --> M[Foils Bear Hull Load Struts and Submerged Foils]
    M --> N[Reduced Wave Drag and Friction Drag]
    N --> O[High Speed Cruising 40 to 60 knots]
    L --> D
    E --> P[Sensor Monitoring Sensors]
    F --> P
    G --> P
    H --> P
    P --> Q[Active Control Surface Incidence or Flap Angle]
    Q --> E
    Q --> F
    Q --> G
    Q --> H
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Types of Marine Hydrofoils

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Marine Hydrofoil Classification] --> B[By Submersion Geometry]
    B --> C[Surface Piercing Foils]
    B --> D[Fully Submerged Foils]
    B --> E[Lifting Strut Foils]
    C --> F[Self Stabilising at Speed]
    C --> G[Experiences Spray Drag]
    D --> H[Requires Active Control System]
    D --> I[No Spray Drag]
    E --> J[V Shaped Front Rear]
    E --> K[Inverted V Variant]
    A --> L[By Application]
    L --> M[Passenger Ferries Jetfoil]
    L --> N[Naval Patrol Boats]
    L --> O[Sailing Yachts Foiling Catamarans]
    L --> P[Hydrofoil Assisted Cargo Ships]
```

## 4.3 Force-Vector Decomposition on a Single Hydrofoil

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flow Velocity V inf] --> B[Relative Wind on Foil]
    B --> C[Angle of Attack alpha]
    C --> D[Lift L perpendicular to V]
    C --> E[Drag D parallel to V]
    D --> F[Resultant Hydrodynamic Force R]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Component Vertical Supports Weight]
    F --> H[Component Horizontal Opposed by Thrust]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** In **Diagram 4.1**, notice the feedback loop from sensor array `P` to active incidence control `Q`. This is the hallmark of modern fully-submerged hydrofoils — they have no natural self-stabilising moment (unlike surface-piercing foils), so they must use flaps or incidence control just like an aircraft.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Define a hydrofoil. Mention any two advantages of a hydrofoil-supported craft over a conventional displacement hull.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A hydrofoil is a streamlined lifting surface operating in water which, at sufficient forward speed, develops hydrodynamic lift equal to the vessel's weight, raising the hull clear of the water surface.  
**Advantages:**  
(i) Significant reduction in wave-making resistance and frictional resistance, enabling speeds of 40–60 knots compared with the displacement-hull limit of ~$1.34\sqrt{L_{WL}}$ knots.  
(ii) Improved seakeeping and comfort because the hull is decoupled from wave action; only the foil-borne portion interacts with the sea.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Explain the phenomenon of cavitation as applied to hydrofoils. State the cavitation number formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cavitation is the formation of vapour cavities on the suction (low-pressure) surface of a hydrofoil when local static pressure falls to or below the water vapour pressure $p_{v}$. It causes surface pitting, vibration, noise and loss of lift.

$$
\sigma = \frac{p_{\infty} - p_{v}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}}
$$

For safe operation, $\sigma > \sigma_{\text{critical}}$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the Kutta–Joukowski theorem for the lift on a hydrofoil. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hydrofoil craft has a total displacement of $250$ tonnes and operates at $V = 22$ m/s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). It uses a fully-submerged front foil with planform area $25$ m² that carries $40\%$ of the weight and two rear foils carrying $30\%$ each. Calculate:  
(i) Lift coefficient of the front foil. (4 marks)  
(ii) Angle of attack required, assuming $C_{L} = 2\pi\alpha$. (3 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Kutta–Joukowski Derivation (7 marks):**  
Consider a stationary hydrofoil in uniform flow $V_{\infty}$. By Kelvin's circulation theorem in an inviscid, incompressible, steady flow, the circulation $\Gamma$ is constant on any streamline enclosing the foil. Applying the momentum theorem to a control surface at radius $R$ far from the foil:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L' &= \oint p \, dx \\
\text{Using Bernoulli} \; p + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} &= \text{const} \\
\therefore\; L' &= \rho V_{\infty} \Gamma \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; L = \rho V_{\infty}\Gamma b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:** inviscid flow, incompressible fluid, steady state, sharp trailing edge (Kutta condition satisfied).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 250 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 2{,}452{,}500 \;\text{N} \\
L_{\text{front}} &= 0.40 \times 2{,}452{,}500 = 981{,}000 \;\text{N} \\[4pt]
C_{L,\text{front}} &= \frac{L_{\text{front}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S} \\
&= \frac{981{,}000}{\tfrac{1}{2}\times 1025 \times (22)^{2} \times 25} \\
&= \frac{981{,}000}{6{,}203{,}750} \\
C_{L,\text{front}} &= 0.158 \\[4pt]
\alpha &= C_{L}/(2\pi) = 0.158 / 6.283 = 0.0251 \;\text{rad} = 1.44^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, classify hydrofoils based on their submergence geometry. Discuss the relative merits of each type. (7 marks)

**(b)** A submerged hydrofoil section with chord $c = 0.6$ m, span $b = 4$ m, operates at $V = 15$ m/s in seawater. Measured drag is $240$ N and lift is $7200$ N. Calculate:  
(i) Lift and drag coefficients. (3 marks)  
(ii) The lift-to-drag ratio $L/D$. (2 marks)  
(iii) Comment on whether this $L/D$ is acceptable for a marine hydrofoil. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**  

| Type | Sketch Description | Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface-Piercing | Foil tip breaks water surface; two-foil V-shape | Self-stabilising (heel-induced lift changes) | Spray drag, wet-deck slam |
| Fully Submerged | Foil entirely below waterline | No spray drag, smoother ride | Requires active control |
| Lifting Strut | Foils integrated as cambered struts | Compact, light | Limited aspect ratio |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= b \times c = 4 \times 0.6 = 2.4 \;\text{m}^{2} \\
q &= \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 225 = 115{,}312.5 \;\text{Pa} \\[4pt]
C_{L} &= \frac{7200}{115{,}312.5 \times 2.4} = 0.0260 \\
C_{D} &= \frac{240}{115{,}312.5 \times 2.4} = 0.000867 \\[4pt]
L/D &= 7200/240 = 30
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Discussion:** $L/D = 30$ is **acceptable** for a marine hydrofoil at this low $C_{L}$. Racing hydrofoils achieve $L/D \approx 40$–$60$ at higher $C_{L}$ but suffer cavitation risk. Commercial ferry hydrofoils typically design for $L/D \approx 20$–$30$ to balance cavitation margin and efficiency.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Hydrofoil Module):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_{L}$ — students frequently write $L = \rho V^{2} S C_{L}$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up cavitation pressure** — using atmospheric pressure $p_{atm}$ instead of local static pressure $p_{\infty}$ (which equals $p_{atm} + \rho g h$ at depth $h$). Subtract $p_{v}$, not $p_{atm}$.
> 3. **Wrong span in aspect ratio** — use $AR = b^{2}/S$, **not** $c^{2}/S$.
> 4. **Sign of $C_{p,\text{min}}$** — students forget cavitation initiates when $C_{p}$ is most negative; hence $\sigma_{\text{crit}} = -C_{p,\text{min}}$.
> 5. **Units of $C_{L}$** — dimensionless but students write "per radian" by copying from aircraft text. Hydrofoil $C_{L}$ is **not** normalised per radian.
> 6. **Hull speed confusion** — $1.34\sqrt{L_{WL}}$ is in knots with $L_{WL}$ in feet; conversion errors lose 1 mark in numericals.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hydrofoil** = underwater lifting surface generating lift via Bernoulli + Kutta–Joukowski.
- **Lift:** $L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_{L}$ — note factor of ½.
- **Drag:** $D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} S C_{D}$.
- **Aspect Ratio:** $AR = b^{2}/S$; high $AR$ → low induced drag.
- **Thin-foil theory:** $C_{L} = 2\pi\alpha$ (flat plate), $C_{L} = 2\pi(\alpha + \alpha_{0})$ (cambered).
- **Kutta–Joukowski:** $L = \rho V \Gamma b$.
- **Cavitation Number:** $\sigma = (p_{\infty} - p_{v})/(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^{2})$.
- **Three types:** Surface-piercing (self-stabilising), Fully submerged (active control needed), Lifting strut.
- **Hull speed limit:** $V \approx 1.34\sqrt{L_{WL}}$ knots (bypassed by hydrofoils).
- **Seawater density:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³; vapour pressure $p_{v} \approx 2.34$ kPa.
- **Typical $L/D$** for marine hydrofoils: 20–40.
- **Typical $C_{L}$** at design: 0.15–0.40 (low to avoid cavitation).
- **Order of evaluation for a design problem:** (1) compute required lift, (2) distribute among foils, (3) solve for $C_{L}$, (4) check $\alpha$ and $\sigma$.
- **Common foils:** NACA 66-series, Göttingen 398, supercavitating sections (for high speed).
- **Sub-cavitating vs super-cavitating:** low-speed boats use sub-cavitating (high $C_{L}$); racing boats use super-cavitating wedge sections.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Cushion Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Cushion Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV)**, also known as a **Hovercraft** or **Ground-Effect Machine (GEM)**, is a marine/terrestrial hybrid craft that supports itself and its payload entirely on a continuous cushion of pressurized air ejected downward against the operating surface (water, land, ice, or marsh), thereby eliminating hydrodynamic hull drag at low-to-medium speeds and enabling amphibious operation across water-land boundaries.

The cushion is generated by one or more **lift fans** that draw air from above and force it into a plenum chamber bounded by flexible **skirts** (in skirted ACVs) or rigid sidewalls (in SES). The air escapes only at the periphery, creating a steady-state overpressure that supports the craft weight.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Balloon on a Table" Analogy**
> Imagine you place a fully inflated balloon flat on a table and gently press down. The balloon's rubber deforms, and the air inside pressurizes against the table. Now imagine thousands of these balloons underneath a boat — except the "balloons" are continuously refilled by giant fans. The boat essentially *floats on its own breath of air*, a millimeter or two above the water. Because the rubber (skirt) is flexible, it can ride over waves as high as itself without the hull ever touching the water. That is, in essence, how an ACV works.

A second intuitive analogy: think of a **leaf held against a hairdryer**. The leaf does not touch the table because the upward airflow generates a pressure differential across it. An ACV does the same — except the leaf is a 100-tonne ferry, and the hairdryer is a 4 MW gas turbine.

### 1.3 Classification Taxonomy (KTU High-Yield)

ACVs are broadly classified into **two families** based on how the cushion is contained:

| Family | Sub-Type | Cushion Containment | Typical Speed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Skirted ACV (Hovercraft)** | Open plenum / Bag skirt / Finger skirt / Segment skirt | Flexible skirt on all four sides | 30 – 70 knots |
| **Surface Effect Ship (SES)** | Captured-air-bubble (CAB) craft | Rigid catamaran sidewalls + fore/aft seals | 35 – 50 knots |
| **Ram Wing** | Ekranoplan / WIG (Wing-In-Ground) | Dynamic air pressure + forward speed | 200 – 400 knots |

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics

The following design benchmarks are used universally in KTU board questions and in IMO High-Speed Craft Code (HSC 2000 / 2024):

- **Cushion pressure:** typically **p_c = 1.0 kPa to 3.5 kPa** (≈ 100 – 350 kg/m² equivalent static load).
- **Cushion gap height:** **h_c = 1.5 m to 3.0 m** for skirted ACVs.
- **Lift-to-Drag ratio (L/D):** **4 to 8** at design speed.
- **Air mass flow rate:** **Q̇ = (W · C_DL) / (ρ_air · √(2·p_c/ρ_air))** per unit area.
- **Useful load fraction:** **20 % to 35 %** of total displacement (significantly lower than conventional ships).
- **Specific power:** **30 – 60 kW/tonne** (very high compared to displacement ships at 3–6 kW/tonne).
- **Froude number at cushion clearance:** **Fn_V = V/√(g·L) ≈ 1.5 to 3.5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "P-S-F" Principle**
> Every ACV operates on three simultaneous physical principles: **P**ressure cushion (static lift), **S**kirt containment (boundary seal), and **F**an propulsion (forward thrust). Memorise these three letters — every KTU question traces back to one of them.

---

### 1.5 Historical Context (Brief)

The concept was patented by **Sir Christopher Cockerell (UK)** in **1955** after he observed that narrow water jets gave better thrust than wide ones (the "slot effect"). The first practical civilian hovercraft — the **SR.N1** — crossed the English Channel on **25 May 1959**. The largest military ACVs ever built are the **LCAC-class** operated by the US Navy at 160 tonnes payload.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Three-Subsystem Model

An ACV is fundamentally a **coupled three-subsystem engineering problem**:

**Subsystem A — Cushion Aerodynamics (Vertical)**
The cushion is a pressurised air reservoir. The lift fans push air in at rate `Q̇_in`; the air escapes through the annular clearance between skirt and surface at rate `Q̇_out`. At steady state:

$$
Q\_{in} = Q\_{out} = \text{cushion leakage flow}
$$

The leakage area is **A_L = P_m × h_c** (where P_m is the wetted mean perimeter of the skirt seal and h_c is the average cushion gap). The leakage velocity equals the air-jet velocity under the skirt edge:

$$
V\_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}
$$

Therefore the required volumetric flow rate from the lift fans is:

$$
\dot{Q} = C\_{DL} \, A\_L \, V\_c = C\_{DL} \, P\_m \, h\_c \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}
$$

**Subsystem B — Cushion Pressure Equilibrium (Static Lift)**

For steady hover (zero forward speed), Newton's third law on the cushion gives:

$$
p\_c \cdot A\_c = W + F\_{inertia}
$$

In static conditions:

$$
p\_c = \frac{W}{A\_c} \quad \text{(N/m}^2\text{ or Pa)}
$$

where `W` is the gross weight (N) and `A_c` is the effective cushion area (m²).

**Subsystem C — Forward Propulsion**

Forward thrust is provided by **separate air propellers** (or marine propellers when floating). The thrust must overcome:
1. Aerodynamic drag of the upper structure
2. Skirt aerodynamic drag
3. Wave-making drag (residual at hump speed)
4. Aerodynamic wave drag at high speed

### 2.2 Total Resistance Breakdown

The total resistance R_T of an ACV operating over water is:

$$
R\_T = R\_{aero} + R\_{skirt} + R\_{wave} + R\_{visc} + R\_{hump}
$$

| Component | Physical Origin | Typical Magnitude (% of R_T) |
|---|---|---|
| **R_aero** | Air drag on hull + superstructure | 35 – 55 % |
| **R_skirt** | Drag of flexible skirt in air stream | 15 – 25 % |
| **R_wave** | Wave generation by cushion air jets | 10 – 20 % |
| **R_visc** | Viscous shear on cushion boundary layer | 5 – 10 % |
| **R_hump** | Residual hump near Fn ≈ 0.5 – 0.7 | 5 – 15 % |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU board problem reduces to one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Key | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p\_c = \dfrac{W}{A\_c}$ | cushion pressure, weight, cushion area | Pa, N, m² | Static hover lift |
| 2 | $V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}$ | leakage velocity | m/s | Skirt jet speed |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = C\_{DL} \, A\_L \, V\_c$ | leakage flow rate | m³/s | Lift fan sizing |
| 4 | $P\_{lift} = p\_c \, \dot{Q}$ | lift power | W | Cushion power budget |
| 5 | $F\_{prop} = R\_T$ | propulsion thrust = total drag | N | Thrust balance |
| 6 | $P\_{total} = P\_{lift} + P\_{prop}$ | total installed power | W | Power plant sizing |
| 7 | $P\_{prop} = R\_T \cdot V$ | propulsion power | W | Forward motion |
| 8 | $Fn\_V = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \, L}}$ | volumetric Froude number | — | Wave regime check |
| 9 | $L/D = \dfrac{W}{R\_T}$ | lift-to-drag ratio | — | Efficiency metric |
| 10 | $\eta\_{prop} = \dfrac{T \cdot V}{P\_{shaft}}$ | propulsive efficiency | — | Propeller design |

where:
- `ρ_air` = **1.225 kg/m³** (ISA sea-level)
- `g` = **9.81 m/s²**
- `C_DL` (cushion discharge coefficient) ≈ **0.6 – 0.8** for skirted ACVs

### 2.4 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

- **Military Amphibious Assault:** LCACs deliver tanks from ship-to-shore without ports, achieving 40+ knots over wave heights of 1.5 m.
- **Civilian Passenger Ferries:** SES catamarans (e.g., Boeing 929 Jetfoil derivatives) provide 50-knot ferry services where displacement hulls are uneconomical.
- **Arctic Logistics:** Hovercraft operate over pack ice, swamp, tundra — surfaces inaccessible to any displacement vessel.
- **Search & Rescue (SAR):** ACVs cross surf zones and debris fields where no conventional craft can reach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering trade-off:** Doubling the cushion pressure `p_c` quadruples the lift power (`P_lift ∝ p_c^(3/2)`), but only doubles the load capacity. Hence ACVs are **inherently power-hungry** — this is why useful payload rarely exceeds 35 % of total mass.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Cushion Pressure and Air Leakage Velocity

**Statement to prove:** The average air velocity leaking out from beneath a skirt of an ACV is given by $V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}$.

**Starting principle:** Bernoulli's equation applied to a streamline from inside the cushion (point 1, stagnation) to outside the skirt (point 2, atmospheric).

**Step 1 — Identify the two stations.**
- Point 1: inside cushion, air velocity ≈ 0, pressure = `p_c` (gauge above ambient).
- Point 2: just outside the skirt exit, pressure = atmospheric (gauge = 0), velocity = `V_c`.

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli along a streamline.**

$$
p\_c + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho\_{air} (0)^2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho\_{air} V\_c^{\,2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
p\_c = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho\_{air} V\_c^{\,2}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for V_c.**

$$
V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute standard values to obtain numerical sanity-check.**

For `p_c = 2000 Pa`, `ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³`:

$$
V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2000}{1.225}} = \sqrt{3265.3} \approx 57.1 \; \text{m/s}
$$

This is subsonic but very high — which is why ACVs are acoustically loud and skirt wear is severe.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Lift Fan Power Requirement

**Statement to prove:** The shaft power required by the lift fan equals $P\_{lift} = p\_c \cdot \dot{Q}$.

**Step 1 — Recall work-energy per unit mass.**
For a fan that raises fluid pressure from ambient to `p_c` with flow rate `Q̇` and density `ρ_air`, the ideal specific work is `w = p_c / ρ_air` (J/kg).

**Step 2 — Multiply by mass flow rate.**
Mass flow rate `ṁ = ρ_air · Q̇`. Therefore ideal hydraulic power:

$$
P\_{hydraulic} = \dot{m} \cdot w = \rho\_{air} \, \dot{Q} \cdot \dfrac{p\_c}{\rho\_{air}} = p\_c \, \dot{Q}
$$

**Step 3 — Add fan mechanical efficiency `η_fan` (typically 0.55 – 0.70 for centrifugal fans).**

$$
P\_{shaft} = \dfrac{p\_c \, \dot{Q}}{\eta\_{fan}}
$$

For quick KTU problems assume `η_fan = 0.65`.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Total Cushion Air Flow Rate

**Statement to prove:** $\dot{Q} = C\_{DL} \, A\_L \, V\_c$.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation at steady state.**
Air entering cushion per second = air leaking per second:

$$
\dot{Q}\_{in} = \dot{Q}\_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — The leakage occurs through an annular area.**
For a rectangular cushion of footprint L × B, the wetted mean perimeter (excluding the open mouth) is `P_m = 2(L + B)`. The leakage gap is the average height `h_c`. So:

$$
A\_L = P\_m \cdot h\_c
$$

**Step 3 — The leakage is a turbulent jet.**
A discharge coefficient `C_DL` accounts for vena contracta effects (typically 0.6 – 0.8):

$$
\dot{Q}\_{out} = C\_{DL} \cdot A\_L \cdot V\_c
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with V_c from Derivation I.**

$$
\dot{Q} = C\_{DL} \cdot P\_m \cdot h\_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, p\_c}{\rho\_{air}}}
$$

This is the canonical "lift fan sizing equation" used in every KTU 14-mark design problem.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An ACV weighs 250 tonnes. Its cushion footprint is 25 m × 12 m. The skirt mean gap height is 1.8 m, and `C_DL = 0.65`. Determine (a) cushion pressure, (b) leakage velocity, (c) volume flow rate, and (d) lift fan shaft power assuming `η_fan = 0.65`. Use `ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³`.

**Solution:**

**(a) Cushion pressure:**
`W = 250 000 × 9.81 = 2 452 500 N`
`A_c = 25 × 12 = 300 m²`

$$
p\_c = \dfrac{W}{A\_c} = \dfrac{2\,452\,500}{300} = 8175 \; \text{Pa} \;(\approx 8.18 \; \text{kPa})
$$

**(b) Leakage velocity:**

$$
V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8175}{1.225}} = \sqrt{13\,347} \approx 115.5 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**(c) Volume flow rate:**
Perimeter `P_m = 2(25 + 12) = 74 m`. Leakage area `A_L = 74 × 1.8 = 133.2 m²`.

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.65 \times 133.2 \times 115.5 \approx 10\,000 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(d) Lift fan shaft power:**

$$
P\_{hydraulic} = p\_c \cdot \dot{Q} = 8175 \times 10\,000 = 81.75 \; \text{MW (ideal)}
$$

$$
P\_{shaft} = \dfrac{81.75}{0.65} \approx 125.8 \; \text{MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity-check / Why this is realistic:** A 250 t ACV is in the "large" class (similar to a Boeing 929). Typical installed lift power on such a craft is **15 – 25 MW**, so our 125 MW result is **physically excessive** because the perimeter calculation assumed all four sides leak — in a real **sidewall ACV**, only two sides leak, cutting leakage by ~50 %. KTU will accept the full-perimeter model but expect you to comment on the conservatism.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ACV vs SES vs Displacement Hull

| Parameter | Skirted ACV | SES (Captured Air Bubble) | Displacement Hull |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cushion pressure | 1 – 3.5 kPa | 1 – 3 kPa | N/A (buoyancy) |
| Speed (typical) | 30 – 70 knots | 35 – 50 knots | 12 – 25 knots |
| Payload fraction | 20 – 30 % | 25 – 35 % | 60 – 80 % |
| Specific power | 40 – 60 kW/t | 25 – 40 kW/t | 3 – 6 kW/t |
| Amphibious? | Yes | No (water only) | No |
| Wave tolerance | Up to 1.5× cushion gap | Up to 1× cushion gap | Limited by hull |
| Skirt maintenance | High (every 500 – 2000 hrs) | Moderate | N/A |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural & Functional Diagrams (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, the diagrams below use only alphanumeric node IDs and raw uppercase text labels (no markdown formatting inside labels) to guarantee successful Mermaid rendering.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram of an ACV Power Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[PRIME MOVER GAS TURBINE OR DIESEL] --> B[POWER TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
    B --> C[LIFT FAN SUBSYSTEM]
    B --> D[PROPULSION FAN SUBSYSTEM]
    C --> C1[AMBIENT AIR INTAKE DUCT]
    C1 --> C2[CENTRIFUGAL OR AXIAL LIFT FAN]
    C2 --> C3[PLENUM CHAMBER BENEATH HULL]
    C3 --> C4[FLEXIBLE SKIRT SEAL PERIMETER]
    C4 --> C5[AIR JET LEAKAGE TO ATMOSPHERE]
    C5 --> C6[PRESSURE REACTION LIFT FORCE ON HULL]
    D --> D1[DUCTED PROPELLER OR WATERJET]
    D1 --> D2[THRUST FORCE AGAINST AIR OR WATER]
    D2 --> D3[FORWARD MOTION OF CRAFT]
    D3 --> D4[AERODYNAMIC DRAG SKIRT DRAG WAVE DRAG]
    D4 --> D5[RESISTANCE BALANCE WITH THRUST]
    C6 --> E[STATIC WEIGHT OF CRAFT]
    E --> D5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Arrows show **energy and force flow**. The prime mover drives both lift and propulsion fans via a gearbox. The lift fan pressurises the plenum; air leaks through the skirt and the **reaction pressure** supports the hull. Simultaneously, the propulsion fan generates thrust that fights aerodynamic + skirt + wave drag.

---

### 4.2 ACV Sub-System Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS[ACV TOTAL SYSTEM] --> L1[STRUCTURAL HULL]
    SYS --> L2[CUSHION SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> L3[PROPULSION SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> L4[CONTROL AND NAVIGATION SUBSYSTEM]
    SYS --> L5[AUXILIARY SUBSYSTEM]
    L2 --> L2A[LIFT FAN]
    L2 --> L2B[PLENUM]
    L2 --> L2C[SKIRT ASSEMBLY]
    L2C --> L2C1[BAG SKIRT]
    L2C --> L2C2[FINGER SKIRT]
    L2C --> L2C3[SEGMENT SKIRT]
    L3 --> L3A[AIR PROPELLER]
    L3 --> L3B[WATERJET WHEN OFF CUSHION]
    L3 --> L3C[RUDDER AND STEERING]
    L4 --> L4A[ALTITUDE SENSOR]
    L4 --> L4B[SKIRT PRESSURE TRANSDUCER]
    L4 --> L4C[GPS AND RADAR]
```

---

### 4.3 Cushion Air Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[AMBIENT AIR] --> P2[LIFT FAN SUCTION]
    P2 --> P3[PRESSURISED PLENUM PC ABOVE ATMOSPHERIC]
    P3 --> P4[RADIAL OUTFLOW BENEATH SKIRT]
    P4 --> P5[LEAKAGE JET VC AT SKIRT EDGE]
    P5 --> P6[RETURN TO AMBIENT]
    P3 -.REACTION LIFT.-> P7[FORCE ON HULL EQUALS WEIGHT]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

> Define an Air Cushion Vehicle. With a neat sketch explain the principle of operation of a hovercraft. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Concise — exactly 3-mark depth):**

An **Air Cushion Vehicle (ACV)** is a craft that rides on a cushion of pressurised air ejected downward by lift fans and retained beneath a flexible skirt, eliminating contact with the surface.

**Principle:** Air drawn from atmosphere is forced into a plenum chamber by a lift fan, raising the static pressure inside to `p_c`. This pressure acts on the hull underside over area `A_c`, producing an upward force `F_L = p_c · A_c`. When `F_L = W` (weight of craft), the hull lifts clear of the surface. Forward motion is generated by separate air propellers.

**Diagram (text-only schematic):**

```
       | PROPELLER (THRUST →)
       v
   ┌──────────┐
   │   HULL   │ ← superstructure
   └────┬─────┘
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░  ← plenum (p_c)
   ╲___________╱   ← flexible skirt
   ~~~~ water ~~~~
```

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**

> List any THREE advantages and TWO disadvantages of Air Cushion Vehicles compared to conventional displacement ships. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **High speed capability** (30 – 70 knots) without hull contact drag.
2. **Amphibious operation** — can traverse water, land, ice, marsh seamlessly.
3. **Wave insensitivity** at design speed; rides over waves smaller than cushion gap.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Very high specific fuel consumption** due to enormous lift-fan power.
2. **Low payload fraction** (only 20 – 30 % of total mass is useful cargo).

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Rule: Each Part B question offers **OR** choice between two sub-problems (Part-A and Part-B). Both options are given below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the aid of a functional block diagram, explain the major sub-systems of an Air Cushion Vehicle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ACV has a cushion area of 350 m² and weighs 300 tonnes. If the average skirt gap is 2.0 m and the discharge coefficient is 0.70, calculate (i) cushion pressure, (ii) leakage velocity, (iii) total volume flow rate, and (iv) ideal hydraulic lift power. Assume `ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Cushion pressure:**
`W = 300 000 × 9.81 = 2 943 000 N`

$$
p\_c = \dfrac{W}{A\_c} = \dfrac{2\,943\,000}{350} = 8408.6 \; \text{Pa} \approx 8.41 \; \text{kPa}
$$

**(ii) Leakage velocity:**

$$
V\_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8408.6}{1.225}} = \sqrt{13\,728.2} \approx 117.2 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**(iii) Volume flow rate (assume rectangular plan 25 × 14, so P_m = 78 m):**

$$
A\_L = 78 \times 2.0 = 156 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = 0.70 \times 156 \times 117.2 = 12\,798 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 12\,800 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(iv) Hydraulic lift power:**

$$
P\_{lift} = p\_c \cdot \dot{Q} = 8408.6 \times 12\,800 = 1.076 \times 10^8 \; \text{W} \approx 107.6 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Examiner's valuation comment:** State units at every step (Pa, m³/s, MW) — students commonly lose 0.5 mark by omitting `Pa` or writing `m^3/sec` instead of `m³/s`.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Compare Air Cushion Vehicles (ACV) with Surface Effect Ships (SES) under the headings: cushion containment, propulsion, amphibious capability, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **lift-to-drag ratio (L/D)** for an ACV. An ACV with gross weight 200 tonnes cruises at 40 knots. If its total resistance is 50 kN, calculate the L/D ratio and the propulsion shaft power required, assuming `η_prop = 0.70`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Definition:** L/D is the ratio of useful lift (weight W) to total resistance R_T:

$$
L/D = \dfrac{W}{R\_T}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
`W = 200 000 × 9.81 = 1 962 000 N`
`V = 40 knots = 40 × 0.5144 = 20.58 m/s`

**Step 2 — Compute L/D:**

$$
L/D = \dfrac{1\,962\,000}{50\,000} = 39.24
$$

**Step 3 — Useful thrust power:**

$$
P\_{thrust} = R\_T \cdot V = 50\,000 \times 20.58 = 1.029 \times 10^6 \; \text{W} \approx 1.03 \; \text{MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft power with propulsive efficiency:**

$$
P\_{shaft} = \dfrac{P\_{thrust}}{\eta\_{prop}} = \dfrac{1.03}{0.70} \approx 1.47 \; \text{MW}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfalls in ACV questions (compiled from KTU Dec 2022, May 2023, Dec 2023 papers):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert tonnes-force → Newtons.** Always multiply mass (tonnes) by 1000 × 9.81 = 9810 N/tonne.
> 2. **Using water density (1000 kg/m³) instead of air density (1.225 kg/m³).** ACV formulas universally use `ρ_air`.
> 3. **Omitting the discharge coefficient `C_DL`.** Without it, your flow rate is overstated by ~30 %.
> 4. **Confusing "hydraulic power" (ideal) with "shaft power" (actual).** Always divide by `η_fan` to get shaft power.
> 5. **Mixing up volumetric Froude number `Fn_V` with conventional Froude number `Fn_L`.** ACV uses `V/√(g·L)` where L is **cushion length**, not waterline length.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Print this page before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Definition:** ACV = craft supported entirely by a pressurised air cushion, eliminating surface contact.
- ✅ **Three Physical Principles:** Pressure cushion (lift) + Skirt containment (seal) + Fan propulsion (thrust).
- ✅ **Cushion Pressure Equation:** `p_c = W / A_c`.
- ✅ **Leakage Velocity:** `V_c = √(2 p_c / ρ_air)` — derived from Bernoulli.
- ✅ **Volume Flow Rate:** `Q̇ = C_DL · P_m · h_c · V_c`.
- ✅ **Lift Power:** `P_lift = p_c · Q̇` (hydraulic); divide by `η_fan` for shaft power.
- ✅ **Typical cushion pressure:** 1 – 3.5 kPa; **cushion gap:** 1.5 – 3.0 m.
- ✅ **Useful payload fraction:** only 20 – 30 % — a critical limitation.
- ✅ **Two ACV families:** Skirted ACV (amphibious) and SES (water only).
- ✅ **Skirt types:** bag, finger, segment — segment is most modern and durable.
- ✅ **Specific power:** 30 – 60 kW/tonne — order of magnitude higher than displacement ships.
- ✅ **Volumetric Froude number** uses cushion length: `Fn_V = V / √(g · L_cushion)`.
- ✅ **Inventor:** Sir Christopher Cockerell (UK, 1955).
- ✅ **First operational craft:** SR.N1 (1959, English Channel crossing).
- ✅ **Always use `ρ_air = 1.225 kg/m³`** (sea-level ISA) unless stated otherwise.
- ✅ **State units explicitly** in every line of the solution — 0.5 mark per missing unit.
- ✅ **For numerical problems**, show all four steps: pressure → velocity → flow rate → power.

> **End of Module 1 — Air Cushion Vehicles Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Pleasure Crafts (Yachts)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Small Pleasure Crafts (Yachts)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Small Pleasure Craft (SPC)**, popularly known as a **Yacht**, is defined by the **Recreational Craft Directive (RCD 2013/53/EU)** and **ISO 12217** as any watercraft of **hull length between 2.5 m and 24 m**, used primarily for **non-commercial leisure, sport, or recreational purposes**. Within the KTU 2024 marine design curriculum, yachts are treated as a distinct class of vessels where **aesthetic styling, hydrodynamic efficiency, seakeeping, and structural lightness** are optimized simultaneously — unlike merchant or naval vessels, where cargo capacity or mission profile dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Threshold (RCD 2013/53/EU):** Hull length **2.5 m ≤ L_H ≤ 24 m**. Below 2.5 m, no directive applies; above 24 m, the vessel falls under commercial ship codes (SOLAS-equivalent).

### 1.2 Sub-Classification of Yachts (KTU Board Classification)

| Category | Defining Feature | Typical L_OA |
|----------|------------------|--------------|
| **Day Sailer** | No cabin, day-use only | 5 – 8 m |
| **Weekender / Pocket Cruiser** | Minimal cabin, 2-berth | 7 – 10 m |
| **Cruiser-Racer** | Dual-purpose, IRC/ORC rated | 9 – 15 m |
| **Cruising Yacht** | Long-range, full amenities | 10 – 24 m |
| **Motor Yacht** | Inboard/outboard propulsion | 6 – 24 m |
| **Planing Powerboat** | High-speed, semi-displacement | 5 – 12 m |
| **Multihull (Catamaran / Trimaran)** | Twin/triple parallel hulls | 8 – 24 m |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **Sailing Yacht vs Motor Yacht** distinction. The fundamental design divergence is **energy source**: sailing yachts harvest wind via the sail plan (and require ballast keels for righting moment), whereas motor yachts rely entirely on mechanical propulsion and therefore have fundamentally different resistance, stability, and layout trade-offs.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine two vehicles that must both travel efficiently across a rough surface (water):

- A **sailing yacht** is like a **downhill skateboard with a sail and a weighted pendulum underneath** — gravity (via the ballast keel) holds it upright against the wind pushing the sail over. The water provides forward resistance, and the sailor trims the sail angle like steering a kite.
- A **motor yacht** is like a **buoyant sports car on water** — its hull must be slim and light to slice efficiently, with engines providing the energy directly.

The **"small"** in Small Pleasure Craft is not just a size label — it is a regulatory and design philosophy that prioritizes **owner-operator simplicity, trailer-ability, CE certification ease, and aesthetic minimalism** over the heavy commercial-scale considerations (tonnage, cargo, crew endurance) of a merchant ship.

> [!TIP]
> A useful "rule of thumb" for KTU viva: **"If you can tow it behind a family car, it's a small pleasure craft. If it needs a shipyard, it's a yacht; if it needs a harbour, it's a ship."**

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Design Metrics

- Water density (sea): **ρ = 1025 kg/m³**
- Water density (fresh): **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Conversion: **1 knot = 0.5144 m/s**
- **CE Design Categories (RCD Annex I):** A (Ocean), B (Offshore), C (Coastal), D (Sheltered)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Dimensions of a Yacht

The hull form of a small pleasure craft is defined by **six principal dimensions** that govern every subsequent hydrodynamic, structural, and stability calculation.

1. **Length Overall (L_OA)** — Extreme end-to-end length, including bowsprit and stern platform.
2. **Length Waterline (L_WL)** — Length of the hull at the design waterline. *This is the dimension used in all Froude-number and resistance calculations.*
3. **Beam (B)** — Maximum transverse width, typically at or near midships.
4. **Depth (D)** — Vertical distance from the keel baseline to the main deck at midships.
5. **Draft (T)** — Vertical distance from the design waterline to the lowest point of the keel.
6. **Displacement (Δ)** — Total weight of water displaced by the hull, given by **Δ = ρ · ∇**, where ∇ is the displaced volume (m³).

## 2.2 Form Coefficients — The "Shape DNA" of a Yacht

Form (or shape) coefficients are dimensionless ratios that quantify hull fullness. For a yacht, these are the **single most exam-tested topic** in Module 1.

- **Block Coefficient (C_B):** Ratio of displaced volume to a rectangular box of L × B × T.

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

- **Prismatic Coefficient (C_P):** Ratio of displaced volume to the volume of the mid-ship section extended along L_WL. Indicates hull "fineness" lengthwise.

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}}$$

- **Midship Coefficient (C_M):** Ratio of midship area to a rectangle of B × T.

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

- **Fundamental relationship (KTU favorite identity):**

$$C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU values:** Sailing yacht C_B ≈ 0.35 – 0.50; Cruising motor yacht C_B ≈ 0.40 – 0.55; Planing powerboat C_B ≈ 0.30 – 0.45. Lower C_B means a finer, faster, but more wave-making hull.

## 2.3 Speed-Length Ratio & The "Hull Speed" Phenomenon

A **displacement hull** sailing yacht reaches a practical speed ceiling known as the **hull speed**, beyond which adding power produces disproportionately large wave-making resistance. This arises from the phase velocity of a deep-water gravity wave.

For deep water, the wave phase speed is:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot \lambda}{2\pi}}$$

When the wave wavelength (λ) generated by the hull equals the waterline length, the boat sits on the crest of its own wave system — the **"bow and stern wave"** resonance. Substituting λ = L_WL:

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot L_{WL}}{2\pi}}$$

The corresponding **Froude number** at hull speed is:

$$F_n = \frac{V_{hull}}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.40$$

> [!NOTE]
> **F_n = 0.40** is the canonical "hull-speed barrier" for displacement yachts. Planing and semi-displacement craft exceed it by dynamically lifting part of the hull out of the water.

## 2.4 Froude Number & Reynolds Number Regime

Small pleasure crafts operate in a hydrodynamic regime dominated by **Froude scaling (gravity waves)**, not Reynolds scaling (viscous flow). At model testing, both must be matched where possible.

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L_{WL}}{\nu}, \quad \nu_{seawater} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Yacht Value |
|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| Displacement | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | kg or tonnes | 1 – 50 t |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \nabla \div (L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | — | 0.35 – 0.55 |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \nabla \div (A_M \cdot L_{WL})$ | — | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M = A_M \div (B \cdot T)$ | — | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Froude Number | $F_n = V \div \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}$ | — | 0.20 – 0.45 |
| Hull Speed (SI) | $V_{hull} = \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL} \div (2\pi)}$ | m/s | — |
| Hull Speed (knots, ft) | $V_{hull} \approx 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL,ft}}$ | knots | — |
| Reynolds Number | $R_n = V \cdot L_{WL} \div \nu$ | — | 10⁷ – 10⁹ |
| Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_{WL} \div B$ | — | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| Wetted Surface (approx) | $S \approx 2.6 \cdot \sqrt{\nabla \cdot L_{WL}}$ | m² | — |
| Righting Moment | $GZ = \overline{GZ} \cdot \Delta \cdot g$ | N·m | per heel angle |
| Sail Area–Displacement | $SAR = SA \div \nabla^{2/3}$ | — | 14 – 20 |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:** Form coefficients act as "design dials" — adjusting C_P changes the wave pattern (bow wave height), adjusting C_M changes the sectional shape (full vs V-bottom), and adjusting B/L changes the slenderness. A naval architect uses these three numbers to design a hull that is *simultaneously* fast, stable, and manufacturable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Hull Speed from Deep-Water Wave Theory

We begin with the **linear (Airy) wave theory** result for a deep-water gravity wave of wavelength λ. The dispersion relation gives the phase velocity c of a sinusoidal gravity wave in an inviscid, incompressible fluid of infinite depth.

### Step 1 — Statement of the dispersion relation

For an irrotational, deep-water gravity wave, the angular frequency ω and the wavenumber k = 2π / λ are linked by:

$$\omega^2 = g \cdot k$$

### Step 2 — Convert to phase velocity

The phase velocity of a wave is c = ω / k. Therefore:

$$c^2 = \frac{\omega^2}{k^2} = \frac{g \cdot k}{k^2} = \frac{g}{k}$$

Substituting k = 2π / λ:

$$c^2 = \frac{g \cdot \lambda}{2\pi}$$

### Step 3 — Resonant condition for a displacement hull

A displacement hull moving at velocity V generates a wave system. The dominant wave created by the hull has a wavelength approximately equal to the waterline length, λ ≈ L_WL. The "hull speed" is the vessel speed at which the wave generated by the bow and the wave generated by the stern reinforce each other, i.e., the boat travels at the same speed as a wave of its own waterline length.

$$V_{hull} = c \Big|_{\lambda = L_{WL}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot L_{WL}}{2\pi}}$$

With g = 9.81 m/s², the numerical coefficient becomes:

$$\frac{g}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81}{6.2832} = 1.5615$$

So:

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{1.5615 \cdot L_{WL}} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

### Step 5 — Convert to knots (KTU exam-friendly form)

Since 1 m/s = 1.9438 knots:

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{1.5615 \cdot L_{WL}} \cdot 1.9438 \quad [\text{knots}]$$

$$V_{hull} \approx 2.430 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL, \text{m}}} \quad [\text{knots}]$$

For L_WL in feet (1 m = 3.281 ft), note that 2.430 / √3.281 = 1.342, giving the **classical empirical form**:

$$V_{hull} \approx 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL, \text{ft}}} \quad [\text{knots}]$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

For a sailing yacht with L_WL = 10.0 m:

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{1.5615 \cdot 10.0} = \sqrt{15.615} = 3.952 \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to knots:

$$V_{hull} = 3.952 \times 1.9438 = 7.68 \text{ knots}$$

Cross-check with the foot-form: 10 m = 32.81 ft, so:

$$V_{hull} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{32.81} = 1.34 \cdot 5.728 = 7.68 \text{ knots} \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between C_B, C_P, and C_M

### Step 1 — Start with definitions

By definition:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} \quad , \quad C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}} \quad , \quad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply C_P and C_M

$$C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}} \cdot \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

### Step 3 — Cancel A_M

The A_M terms cancel:

$$C_P \cdot C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the right-hand side

By definition, the right-hand side equals C_B. Therefore:

$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \cdot C_M}$$

This is the **canonical identity** tested in KTU Module 1 problems.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

A yacht has L_WL = 12 m, B = 3.6 m, T = 1.8 m, and ∇ = 28 m³. Compute C_B.

$$C_B = \frac{28}{12 \cdot 3.6 \cdot 1.8} = \frac{28}{77.76} = 0.360$$

If the midship area A_M = 5.2 m², then:

$$C_P = \frac{28}{5.2 \cdot 12} = 0.449 \quad , \quad C_M = \frac{5.2}{3.6 \cdot 1.8} = 0.802$$

Check: 0.449 × 0.802 = 0.360 ✓

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Slenderness Ratio & Resistance Trend

The **slenderness ratio** λ = L_WL / B directly affects wave-making resistance. Empirically, the **Holtrop-type trend** for displacement hulls at low Froude numbers shows that wave resistance scales approximately with:

$$R_W \propto \nabla \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot F_n^4 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{\lambda}\right)$$

Thus, **higher slenderness (longer, narrower hulls) reduces wave resistance**. A "racing yacht" typically has λ ≈ 4.0, while a "trawler-style motor yacht" has λ ≈ 2.6. This single number captures the *qualitative* trade-off between speed and stability: a slenderness-4 hull is fast but tender (less initial stability); a slenderness-2.6 hull is sluggish but stiff.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Form Coefficients Drive Hull Behaviour

| Parameter | Increase C_P (more bow volume) | Increase C_M (fuller midship) | Increase λ (slimmer) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Wave resistance | Increases (early bow wave) | Mild increase | Decreases |
| Frictional resistance | Mild increase | Increases (more wetted area) | Decreases |
| Initial stability (GM) | Mild increase | Strong increase | Decreases |
| Cargo / tankage | Increases | Increases | Decreases |
| Top speed (displacement) | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| Manufacturability (moulded panels) | Harder (compound curvature) | Easier (flatter panels) | Harder |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Yacht Design Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of the small pleasure craft design process**, showing how the principal dimensions, form coefficients, and regulatory categories interact to produce a complete design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requirement] --> B[Principal Dimensions]
    B --> C[LOA]
    B --> D[LWL]
    B --> E[Beam]
    B --> F[Draft]
    B --> G[Displacement]
    C --> H[Regulatory Check RCD]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I{CE Category}
    I --> J[Category A Ocean]
    I --> K[Category B Offshore]
    I --> L[Category C Coastal]
    I --> M[Category D Sheltered]
    G --> N[Form Coefficients]
    A --> N
    N --> O[Block Coefficient CB]
    N --> P[Prismatic Coefficient CP]
    N --> Q[Midship Coefficient CM]
    O --> R[Hydrostatic Analysis]
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Stability GZ Curve]
    R --> T[Resistance Estimate]
    T --> U[Fn Calculation]
    U --> V{Hull Regime}
    V --> W[Displacement Fn less than 0.4]
    V --> X[Semi-Displacement Fn 0.4 to 0.6]
    V --> Y[Planing Fn greater than 0.6]
    S --> Z[Final Design Layout]
    W --> Z
    X --> Z
    Y --> Z
    Z --> AA[Structural Scantlings ISO 12215]
    Z --> AB[Weight Estimate]
    AB --> AC[Iterate Delta vs Target]
    AC --> N
```

## 4.2 Hull Form Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Pleasure Craft] --> B[Monohull]
    A --> C[Multihull]
    B --> D[Displacement]
    B --> E[Semi-Displacement]
    B --> F[Planing]
    C --> G[Catamaran]
    C --> H[Trimaran]
    D --> I[Sail Yacht]
    D --> J[Displacement Motor Yacht]
    E --> K[Express Cruiser]
    F --> L[Sports Boat]
    G --> M[Performance Cat]
    H --> N[Ocean Tri]
```

## 4.3 Stability & Resistance Coupling (Subsystem Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Form] --> B[Form Coefficients]
    A --> C[Slenderness]
    B --> D[Initial Stability GM]
    C --> D
    D --> E[GZ Curve]
    E --> F[AvsC Stability Criterion]
    A --> G[Wetted Surface S]
    G --> H[Frictional Resistance Rf]
    B --> I[Wave Resistance Rw]
    C --> I
    H --> J[Total Resistance RT]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Effective Power PE]
    K --> L[Installed Power Selection]
    L --> M[Final Hull Optimization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block diagrams emphasize that **yacht design is iterative, not sequential**. A change in form coefficient (e.g., reducing C_P for a faster bow) affects resistance *and* stability *and* structural scantlings simultaneously. KTU problems sometimes test this by asking the student to *predict a second-order effect* of a single parameter change.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
Define a *Small Pleasure Craft* as per the **Recreational Craft Directive 2013/53/EU**. List any **two** distinguishing design priorities of a yacht compared to a merchant ship.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** A Small Pleasure Craft is any watercraft of hull length **2.5 m ≤ L_H ≤ 24 m** intended for **non-commercial recreational use**, falling under the regulatory framework of the **RCD 2013/53/EU** and **ISO 12217** for stability/buoyancy.
- **Distinguishing priorities (1 mark — any two of the following):**
  1. **Aesthetic styling** dominates over utilitarian layout.
  2. **Lightweight construction** (GRP, carbon composites) preferred over heavy steel.
  3. **Owner-operator ergonomics** (helmsman visibility, single-handed sailing).
  4. **High speed-to-length efficiency** (form coefficients tuned for low wave resistance).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
What is **hull speed**? Derive the Froude number corresponding to hull speed for a displacement yacht.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Hull speed (1.5 marks):** Hull speed is the maximum practical speed of a **displacement-type** vessel, beyond which the boat is in resonance with its own wave system and resistance rises sharply. It is given by:
  
  $$V_{hull} = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot L_{WL}}{2\pi}}$$

- **Froude number at hull speed (1.5 marks):** Substituting V_hull into the Froude number definition:
  
  $$F_n = \frac{V_{hull}}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.40$$

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing L_OA with L_WL** in Froude number calculations → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up C_B, C_P, C_M** definitions or stating the identity C_B = C_P × C_M incorrectly → loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units conversion** (knots ↔ m/s ↔ ft) in hull-speed problems → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not stating the hull regime** (displacement / semi-displacement / planing) when commenting on a Froude number → loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption** "deep water, inviscid flow" in derivations → loses 1 mark.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** A sailing yacht has the following principal particulars:
- Length overall, L_OA = 13.5 m
- Length waterline, L_WL = 11.8 m
- Beam, B = 3.8 m
- Draft, T = 2.0 m
- Displaced volume, ∇ = 24.0 m³
- Midship area, A_M = 5.6 m²

Calculate the **block coefficient (C_B)**, **prismatic coefficient (C_P)**, and **midship coefficient (C_M)**. Verify the identity $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$. Comment on the hull form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same yacht, determine the **hull speed** in m/s and knots. If the yacht is operating at a service speed of **7.0 knots**, calculate the **Froude number** and identify the **hull regime**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Block Coefficient C_B (2 marks)**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{24.0}{11.8 \times 3.8 \times 2.0}$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$11.8 \times 3.8 = 44.84 \quad ; \quad 44.84 \times 2.0 = 89.68$$

$$C_B = \frac{24.0}{89.68} = 0.2676 \approx 0.268$$

**Step 2 — Prismatic Coefficient C_P (2 marks)**

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{WL}} = \frac{24.0}{5.6 \times 11.8} = \frac{24.0}{66.08} = 0.3632 \approx 0.363$$

**Step 3 — Midship Coefficient C_M (1 mark)**

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{5.6}{3.8 \times 2.0} = \frac{5.6}{7.6} = 0.7368 \approx 0.737$$

**Step 4 — Verification of the identity (1 mark)**

$$C_P \times C_M = 0.363 \times 0.737 = 0.2675 \approx C_B = 0.268 \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Comment on hull form (1 mark)**

A C_B of **0.27** and C_P of **0.36** indicate a **finely-shaped, performance-oriented sailing hull** — typical of a modern cruiser-racer. The midship C_M of 0.74 reflects a moderately V-shaped section (good for windward sailing without excessive wetted area).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Hull speed in m/s (2 marks)**

$$V_{hull} = \sqrt{\frac{g \cdot L_{WL}}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 11.8}{6.2832}} = \sqrt{\frac{115.76}{6.2832}} = \sqrt{18.42} = 4.292 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert hull speed to knots (1 mark)**

$$V_{hull} = 4.292 \times 1.9438 = 8.34 \text{ knots}$$

**Step 3 — Convert service speed to m/s (1 mark)**

$$V_{service} = 7.0 \text{ knots} = 7.0 \times 0.5144 = 3.601 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Froude number (2 marks)**

$$F_n = \frac{V_{service}}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{3.601}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 11.8}} = \frac{3.601}{\sqrt{115.76}} = \frac{3.601}{10.760} = 0.3347 \approx 0.335$$

**Step 5 — Identify hull regime (1 mark)**

Since $F_n = 0.335 < 0.40$, the yacht is operating **below hull speed** in the **displacement regime** — wave resistance is the dominant component. The vessel is **not** planing or semi-displacing; this is consistent with a sailing yacht propelled by wind.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** A motor yacht operates at a service speed of **V = 18 knots** with a waterline length of **L_WL = 14 m**. Calculate the **Froude number** and determine the **hull regime**. If the displacement ∇ = 38 m³ and B = 4.2 m, T = 1.4 m, compute the **block coefficient** and comment on whether the hull is suited for the operating speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Holtrop-type empirical trend**, explain qualitatively how the **slenderness ratio** and **prismatic coefficient** affect the **wave-making resistance** of a small pleasure craft. Why does a sailing yacht typically have a lower C_P than a motor yacht of similar displacement? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert service speed to m/s (1 mark)**

$$V = 18 \text{ knots} = 18 \times 0.5144 = 9.259 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Froude number (2 marks)**

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}} = \frac{9.259}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 14}} = \frac{9.259}{\sqrt{137.34}} = \frac{9.259}{11.719} = 0.790$$

**Step 3 — Identify hull regime (1 mark)**

Since $F_n = 0.79 > 0.60$, the vessel is in the **planing regime** — the hull is dynamically lifting and partially disengaging from the water surface.

**Step 4 — Block coefficient (2 marks)**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{WL} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{38}{14 \times 4.2 \times 1.4} = \frac{38}{82.32} = 0.4616 \approx 0.462$$

**Step 5 — Comment on hull suitability (1 mark)**

A C_B of 0.46 with F_n = 0.79 indicates a **planing-type hull** with a deep-V forward section — appropriate for the operating speed. A displacement hull (C_B ≈ 0.55, no V) would be overwhelmed by wave resistance at F_n = 0.79 and would not be suited.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define slenderness (1 mark)**

The slenderness ratio $\lambda = L_{WL} / B$. For typical sailing yachts, $\lambda \approx 3.0$ – $4.0$; for motor yachts, $\lambda \approx 2.5$ – $3.5$.

**Step 2 — Slenderness vs wave resistance (2 marks)**

Wave-making resistance scales approximately as $R_W \propto \nabla \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot F_n^4 \cdot (1 + 1/\lambda)$. Therefore:
- A **higher λ** (slimmer hull) **reduces** wave resistance at a given F_n.
- A **lower λ** (fuller hull) **increases** wave resistance.

**Step 3 — C_P vs wave resistance (2 marks)**

A high C_P means more volume concentrated at the ends (especially the bow), creating a larger bow wave. Sailing yachts adopt lower C_P (~0.55 – 0.62) to keep the entry sharp and reduce wave resistance at moderate speeds.

**Step 4 — Why sailing yachts have lower C_P than motor yachts (2 marks)**

Sailing yachts rely on **wind energy** transmitted through a long, narrow, hydrodynamic envelope that minimizes drag at sailing speeds (F_n ≈ 0.25 – 0.35). Motor yachts, propelled mechanically, can accept higher C_P because they have continuous power to push through the resulting wave field, and their higher C_P supports **greater internal volume** (cabins, tanks) per unit length. Hence, motor yachts typically exhibit **higher C_P (~0.65 – 0.75)** than sailing yachts of similar displacement.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **SPC definition (RCD):** 2.5 m ≤ L_H ≤ 24 m, non-commercial recreation.
- [ ] **Six principal dimensions:** L_OA, L_WL, B, D, T, Δ — know which is used in F_n (L_WL).
- [ ] **Form coefficients canonical identity:** $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ — must be derivable from scratch.
- [ ] **Typical C_B ranges:** Sail yacht 0.35 – 0.50; Motor yacht 0.40 – 0.55; Planing craft 0.30 – 0.45.
- [ ] **Hull speed formula (SI):** $V_{hull} = \sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL} / (2\pi)}$; (empirical, ft): $V_{hull} = 1.34 \cdot \sqrt{L_{WL, ft}}$.
- [ ] **Hull-speed barrier Froude number:** $F_n \approx 0.40$.
- [ ] **Hull regimes:** Displacement ($F_n < 0.4$), Semi-displacement ($0.4 \leq F_n \leq 0.6$), Planing ($F_n > 0.6$).
- [ ] **CE design categories** (A Ocean, B Offshore, C Coastal, D Sheltered) link wave height to permissible operations.
- [ ] **Slenderness ratio** $\lambda = L_{WL}/B$: racing yachts ~4.0, trawler yachts ~2.6.
- [ ] **Conversion factor:** 1 m/s = 1.9438 knots = 3.281 ft/s.
- [ ] **Reynolds regime for yachts:** $R_n \approx 10^7$ to $10^9$ — fully turbulent, friction dominant at low F_n.
- [ ] **Sailing vs Motor Yacht distinction:** Energy source → fundamentally different stability (ballast keel vs beam) and form (slim vs beamy).
- [ ] **Iterative design principle:** Form coefficients ↔ Resistance ↔ Stability ↔ Structure — all linked; no single parameter changes in isolation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ketches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ketches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Ketches

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Ketch** is a two-masted fore-and-aft rigged sailing vessel in which the **forward mast (mainmast)** is taller than the **aft mast (mizzenmast)**. The defining feature of a ketch is that the **mizzenmast is stepped forward of the rudder post (or rudder axis)**, typically at a longitudinal position lying between **45% and 65% of the waterline length (LWL) abaft the forward perpendicular**.

The ketch rig belongs to the broader family of **multi-masted fore-and-aft rigs**, which includes ketches, yawls, and schooners. It is widely used in cruising yachts, fishing vessels, and traditional sailing craft because it offers a favourable balance between **sail area**, **handling simplicity**, and **short-handed operation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A ketch is identified by *two* masts where:
> 1. The **mainmast** is the taller, forward mast.
> 2. The **mizzenmast** (aft mast) is stepped **forward of the rudder post**.
> 3. The mizzen sail is a permanent, working sail (not a mere trysail).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:** If the mizzen is *forward* of the rudder post → **Ketch**. If it is *aft* of the rudder post → **Yawl**. This single positional difference is the most commonly asked distinguishing question in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small truck pulling a smaller cart behind it. The big truck (mainmast) does the heavy lifting and pulls most of the weight, while the smaller cart (mizzenmast) acts as a balancing "tail" — it helps the truck steer, stabilise, and trim loads without being a primary propulsion source. The mizzen in a ketch functions exactly like this: it does **not** drive the boat forward significantly; instead, it **balances sail plan forces**, **reduces weather helm**, and **allows easier handling** of the main sail by acting as a stabilising lever arm.

In practical crew terms, a ketch is like a **two-person rowing boat where one person rows and the other steers and trims** — both work together, but the roles are clearly differentiated. This is why ketches are the preferred rig for **couples and small families** undertaking long voyages (blue-water cruising).

## 1.3 Historical and Engineering Context

The ketch rig emerged in the **17th century** among Mediterranean and North Sea fishing fleets, where the smaller mizzen allowed fishermen to **work the main sail with reduced crew** while the mizzen held the vessel steady into the wind. Modern ketches typically exhibit the following standard metrics (used in preliminary KTU design problems):

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| **Length Overall (LOA)** | **9 m – 18 m** (cruising ketches) |
| **Mizzen position from bow** | **45% – 65% of LWL** |
| **Mizzen height / Mainmast height** | **0.35 – 0.55** (ratio) |
| **Mizzen sail area / Main sail area** | **0.20 – 0.35** (ratio) |
| **Number of headsails** | **1 – 3** (jib, staysail, genoa) |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numerical problems, students should always quote the **mizzen position as a percentage of LWL** when describing a ketch, as this is the metric that examiners expect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rigging Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

A ketch rig consists of two principal vertical spars and a set of standing and running rigging. The breakdown is as follows:

- **Step 1 — Mainmast Identification:** The mainmast is the principal vertical spar located **forward**, typically stepped on deck (or through the deck to the keel) at **5% – 8% of LWL from the bow**. It carries the **mainsail** and is the principal driver of the vessel.
- **Step 2 — Mizzenmast Identification:** The mizzenmast is the **secondary** vertical spar, located **aft** of the mainmast but **forward of the rudder post**. It carries the **mizzen sail** and a **mizzen staysail** (optional). The mizzen height is **35% – 55%** of the mainmast height.
- **Step 3 — Headsail Configuration:** A ketch typically carries a **jib** (100% foretriangle) or **genoa** (>100% foretriangle) on the forestay. The forestay attaches to the mainmast at the **hounds**.
- **Step 4 — Boomkin and Bowsprit (Optional):** Some ketches have a **boomkin** (a short spar projecting aft) for attaching the mizzen sheet, or a **bowsprit** for an extended headsail base.
- **Step 5 — Backstay Arrangement:** A ketch's backstay configuration depends on whether the mizzen stays are run to the main or the mizzen. The **double-headsail rig** uses a single tall main, while a **cutter-ketch** may use multiple staysails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the mizzen stepped *forward* of the rudder post?**
> Engineering logic: When the mizzen's centre of effort (CE) lies **forward of the rudder post**, the rudder remains in undisturbed water flow, ensuring **predictable steering response**. If the mizzen CE were aft of the rudder, the rudder would operate in the mizzen's disturbed wake, causing **sluggish or unpredictable helm** — this configuration is called a yawl.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all high-yield formulas, ratios, and design coefficients for ketch design that are commonly tested in KTU OENST721 examinations.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mizzen position from bow | $X_{mz} = (0.45 \text{ to } 0.65) \cdot L_{WL}$ | m; longitudinal coordinate |
| 2 | Mizzen height ratio | $H_{mz} / H_{main} = 0.35 \text{ to } 0.55$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Mizzen sail area ratio | $A_{mz} / A_{main} = 0.20 \text{ to } 0.35$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Total sail area (ketch) | $A_{S,total} = A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{headsail}$ | m² |
| 5 | Aspect ratio of main | $AR_{main} = E^{2} / A_{main}$ | dimensionless; $E$ = foot length |
| 6 | Sail-area-to-displacement ratio | $SA/D = A_{S,total} \cdot 1013 / \text{Disp}^{\,2/3}$ | SA/D is a KTU standard |
| 7 | Centre of effort (longitudinal) | $\bar{X}_{CE} = \frac{\sum A_i \cdot X_i}{\sum A_i}$ | m; measured from FP |
| 8 | Balance point (≈ 50% LWL) | $\bar{X}_{CE} \approx 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$ | for neutral helm |
| 9 | Displacement-length ratio | $D/L = \text{Disp} / (0.01 \cdot L_{WL})^{3}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Capsize screening formula | $CSF = \text{Beam} / \text{Disp}^{\,1/3}$ | ratio (used with D/L) |

> [!CAUTION]
> In markdown tables, **never use the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside numerical expressions**. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ to avoid breaking table syntax.

## 2.3 Why the Ketch Rig is Engineering-Relevant

The ketch rig is not just a historical curiosity — it remains highly relevant in modern marine engineering for the following reasons:

- **Short-Handed Operation:** The split sail plan means the **mainsail can be reefed or furled independently of the mizzen**, allowing one or two crew members to manage the vessel in heavy weather.
- **Safety in Heavy Weather:** The mizzen can be used to "heave-to" — a storm technique where the vessel lies at **~60° off the wind** with the mizzen backed, providing a stable, self-steering platform.
- **Versatile Sail Plan:** Different sail combinations (main + mizzen, main alone, mizzen alone) allow the vessel to be tuned for various wind conditions without changing rig.
- **Redundancy:** If the mainsail is damaged, the mizzen and headsail can bring the vessel home — a critical safety feature in offshore voyaging.
- **Cargo and Fishing Use:** Traditional fishing ketches (e.g., English Thames ketches, Dutch *tjalken*) used the mizzen for propulsion while nets were hauled, allowing the boat to maintain steerage way.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The *Bounty* (replica), used in the 1962 film, was a full-rigged ship, but many working vessels of similar tonnage were ketches because of crew economy. Modern example: The **Hallberg-Rassy 40** and **Island Packet 420** are production cruising ketches renowned for blue-water capability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centre of Effort (CE) for a Ketch

The **Centre of Effort (CE)** is the aerodynamic equivalent of the centre of gravity of the sail plan. It is the point through which the total aerodynamic force on the sails can be considered to act. For a ketch with main, mizzen, and headsail, the CE is obtained by the **first moment of sail area** about the forward perpendicular (FP).

### Step 1 — Define the Sail Areas and Their Longitudinal Positions

Let:

- $A_{main}$ = area of mainsail, located at longitudinal position $X_{main}$ from FP.
- $A_{mz}$ = area of mizzen sail, located at longitudinal position $X_{mz}$ from FP.
- $A_{jib}$ = area of jib / headsail, located at longitudinal position $X_{jib}$ from FP.

### Step 2 — Write the First-Moment Equation

The total first moment of sail area about the FP is:

$$
M_{FP} = A_{main} \cdot X_{main} + A_{mz} \cdot X_{mz} + A_{jib} \cdot X_{jib}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total Sail Area

The total effective sail area is:

$$
A_{S,total} = A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{jib}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Centre of Effort

The longitudinal coordinate of the centre of effort is:

$$
\bar{X}_{CE} = \frac{M_{FP}}{A_{S,total}} = \frac{A_{main} \cdot X_{main} + A_{mz} \cdot X_{mz} + A_{jib} \cdot X_{jib}}{A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{jib}}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Neutral-Helm Condition

For **neutral helm** (the boat sails straight with no weather or lee helm), the CE should lie at approximately the **midpoint of the waterline length**:

$$
\bar{X}_{CE} \approx 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}
$$

If $\bar{X}_{CE} > 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$, the CE is too far aft and the boat will exhibit **weather helm** (tendency to round up into the wind). If $\bar{X}_{CE} < 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$, the boat will exhibit **lee helm** (tendency to fall off the wind). Ketch designers use the mizzen to fine-tune this balance.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A cruising ketch has the following data:
- $L_{WL} = 12.0$ m
- $A_{main} = 35$ m² at $X_{main} = 4.5$ m from FP
- $A_{mz} = 10$ m² at $X_{mz} = 8.0$ m from FP
- $A_{jib} = 20$ m² at $X_{jib} = 2.0$ m from FP

Determine the longitudinal position of the centre of effort and check whether the helm is neutral, weather, or lee.

**Solution:**

Compute the first moment:

$$
M_{FP} = (35 \times 4.5) + (10 \times 8.0) + (20 \times 2.0)
$$

$$
M_{FP} = 157.5 + 80.0 + 40.0 = 277.5 \; \text{m}^3
$$

Compute the total area:

$$
A_{S,total} = 35 + 10 + 20 = 65 \; \text{m}^2
$$

Compute the centre of effort:

$$
\bar{X}_{CE} = \frac{277.5}{65} = 4.269 \; \text{m from FP}
$$

Check against the neutral-helm reference:

$$
0.50 \cdot L_{WL} = 0.50 \times 12.0 = 6.0 \; \text{m from FP}
$$

Since $\bar{X}_{CE} = 4.269$ m $<$ $6.0$ m, the CE is too far **forward**. The vessel will exhibit **lee helm**. The designer should either **increase jib area**, **shift mizzen aft** (within the ketch constraint), or **move the mast forward** to bring the CE aft.

> [!TIP]
> This type of calculation appears almost every year in KTU module examinations. Master the first-moment technique — it is the most commonly tested numerical in this topic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mizzen Sail Area for Specified Total Area

Given a desired total sail area $A_{S,target}$ and a typical ketch ratio $k_{mz} = A_{mz} / A_{main}$, derive the mizzen area.

### Step 1 — Express the Total Area in Terms of $A_{main}$

$$
A_{S,total} = A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{jib} = A_{main} \cdot (1 + k_{mz}) + A_{jib}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $A_{main}$

$$
A_{main} = \frac{A_{S,total} - A_{jib}}{1 + k_{mz}}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $A_{mz}$

$$
A_{mz} = k_{mz} \cdot A_{main} = \frac{k_{mz} \cdot (A_{S,total} - A_{jib})}{1 + k_{mz}}
$$

For $k_{mz} = 0.25$ and $A_{S,total} = 65$ m², $A_{jib} = 20$ m²:

$$
A_{main} = \frac{65 - 20}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{45}{1.25} = 36 \; \text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{mz} = 0.25 \times 36 = 9 \; \text{m}^2
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Ketch vs. Yawl vs. Schooner

This comparative matrix is the **single most important table for KTU 2-mark comparison questions**.

| Feature | Ketch | Yawl | Schooner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of masts | 2 | 2 | 2 (or more) |
| Taller mast | Mainmast (forward) | Mainmast (forward) | Aft mast (main is *shorter*) |
| Mizzen position | **Forward of rudder post** | **Aft of rudder post** | N/A — it's a different rig |
| Mizzen sail purpose | Working sail, balance | Auxiliary / trysail substitute | Main driver of vessel |
| Typical mizzen size | 20–35% of main | < 20% of main | > 80% of main |
| Crew handling | 1–2 persons (easy) | 1 person (very easy) | 4–6 persons (complex) |
| Heaving-to ability | Excellent (mizzen backed) | Moderate | Difficult |
| Modern use | Cruising, offshore voyaging | Solo sailing, small craft | Tall ships, training vessels |
| KTU identification cue | $X_{mz}$ in [0.45, 0.65] $L_{WL}$ | $X_{mz} > 0.70 \, L_{WL}$ | Aft mast is dominant |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ketch Rig — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a ketch rig, mapping the principal components and their interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef mastStyle fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef sailStyle fill:#c8102e,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef rigStyle fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hullStyle fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    MainMast[Mainmast - Forward - 100 percent height]:::mastStyle
    MzMast[Mizzenmast - Aft - 40 percent height]:::mastStyle
    MainSail[Mainsail - Primary driver]:::sailStyle
    MzSail[Mizzen sail - Balance and trim]:::sailStyle
    JibSail[Jib - Foretriangle headsail]:::sailStyle
    Forestay[Forestay - supports Mainmast]:::rigStyle
    Backstay[Backstay - opposes Forestay]:::rigStyle
    MzStay[Mizzen stay - supports MzMast]:::rigStyle
    RudderPost[Rudder post - aft pivot]:::hullStyle
    Hull[Hull and waterline length LWL]:::hullStyle
    CE[Centre of Effort - aerodynamic resultant]:::sailStyle

    MainMast --> MainSail
    MzMast --> MzSail
    MainMast --> Forestay
    Forestay --> JibSail
    MainMast --> Backstay
    MzMast --> MzStay
    MzStay --> MzSail
    Hull --> RudderPost
    MainSail --> CE
    MzSail --> CE
    JibSail --> CE
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Identifying Two-Masted Rigs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef defStyle fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef branchStyle fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef leafStyle fill:#f4a261,stroke:#333,color:#000

    Start[Two-masted sailing vessel]:::defStyle
    Q1{Is the aft mast shorter than the forward mast?}:::branchStyle
    Q2{Is the aft mast stepped forward of the rudder post?}:::branchStyle
    Q3{Is the aft mast greater than 80 percent of forward mast height?}:::branchStyle
    KetchOut[Identification: KETCH]:::leafStyle
    YawlOut[Identification: YAWL]:::leafStyle
    SchoonerOut[Identification: SCHOONER]:::leafStyle

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2
    Q1 -- No --> Q3
    Q2 -- Yes --> KetchOut
    Q2 -- No --> YawlOut
    Q3 -- Yes --> SchoonerOut
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Sail Force Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef forceStyle fill:#d62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef ptStyle fill:#003049,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef dimStyle fill:#fcbf49,stroke:#333,color:#000

    FP[Forward Perpendicular FP]:::ptStyle
    Xmain[Main CE at Xmain]:::ptStyle
    Xmz[Mizzen CE at Xmz]:::ptStyle
    Xjib[Jib CE at Xjib]:::ptStyle
    Xce[Resultant Xce - 0.50 LWL for neutral helm]:::ptStyle
    LWL[Waterline length LWL]:::dimStyle
    Fmain[Force on Main - F1]:::forceStyle
    Fmz[Force on Mizzen - F2]:::forceStyle
    Fjib[Force on Jib - F3]:::forceStyle
    Fr[Resultant aerodynamic force Fr]:::forceStyle

    FP --> Xjib
    Xjib --> Xmain
    Xmain --> Xmz
    Xmz --> Xce
    Xce --> LWL
    Xjib --> Fjib
    Xmain --> Fmain
    Xmz --> Fmz
    Fmain --> Fr
    Fmz --> Fr
    Fjib --> Fr
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are **functional block architectures**, not literal side-elevation drawings. They are engineered to render reliably in Mermaid while preserving all critical relationships (mast hierarchy, sail force summation, rudder-post position) that KTU examiners expect students to internalise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a ketch. How is it distinguished from a yawl?*

**Model Answer:**
A ketch is a two-masted fore-and-aft rigged sailing vessel in which the **mainmast (forward mast)** is taller than the **mizzenmast (aft mast)**, and the mizzenmast is stepped **forward of the rudder post** (typically at 45%–65% of the waterline length from the bow). The mizzen carries a permanent working sail used for balance and trimming, not merely as a trysail.

A **yawl** is also a two-masted vessel, but in a yawl, the mizzenmast is stepped **aft of the rudder post**, and the mizzen sail is small (typically less than 20% of the mainsail area), used mainly for balance and steering rather than as a driving sail.

| Parameter | Ketch | Yawl |
|---|---|---|
| Mizzen position | Forward of rudder post | Aft of rudder post |
| Mizzen sail size | 20% – 35% of main | < 20% of main |
| Use of mizzen | Working sail | Trim / balance only |

**Key distinguishing feature:** The longitudinal position of the mizzenmast relative to the rudder post.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any three advantages of the ketch rig over the sloop rig for offshore cruising.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Short-handed operation:** The split sail plan allows a smaller crew to reef one sail (main or mizzen) while the other holds the vessel steady, unlike a sloop where the single large mainsail requires more effort to manage in heavy weather.
2. **Redundancy and safety:** If the mainsail is damaged or lost, the mizzen and headsail can be used to bring the vessel safely to harbour, providing a critical safety margin in offshore passages.
3. **Superior heavy-weather performance:** The ketch can heave-to effectively by backing the mizzen sail, creating a self-steering "storm trysail" arrangement with the bow at ~60° off the wind, reducing crew fatigue.
4. *(Bonus)* **Versatile sail combinations:** The vessel can be tuned with main + mizzen, main alone, or mizzen alone, allowing the crew to optimise performance across a wider wind range without changing rig.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a suitable sketch, describe the rigging arrangement of a typical cruising ketch and label the principal components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a cruising ketch with the following particulars, calculate the longitudinal position of the centre of effort of the sail plan and comment on the helm condition: **(7 Marks)**
- Length on waterline, $L_{WL} = 14.0$ m
- Mainsail area, $A_{main} = 42$ m² at $X_{main} = 5.2$ m from FP
- Mizzen sail area, $A_{mz} = 12$ m² at $X_{mz} = 9.5$ m from FP
- Genoa (headsail) area, $A_{genoa} = 28$ m² at $X_{genoa} = 2.4$ m from FP

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Components of a ketch rig (labelled description):**

A typical cruising ketch has **two masts** and the following principal components:

1. **Mainmast** — The taller, forward mast stepped at ~5–8% of $L_{WL}$ from the bow. It carries the **mainsail**, **main boom**, and supports the **forestay** and **backstay**.
2. **Mizzenmast** — The shorter, aft mast stepped at 45%–65% of $L_{WL}$ from the bow (i.e., forward of the rudder post). It carries the **mizzen sail** and is supported by a **mizzen stay** running forward to the mainmast.
