3. **Standing Rigging** — Includes the **forestay** (bows to mainmast top), **backstay** (mainmast top to stern), **shrouds** (lateral support), and **mizzen stay**.
4. **Running Rigging** — Halyards, sheets, and vangs for sail control.
5. **Sails** — Mainsail, mizzen sail, jib/genoa (headsail on forestay).
6. **Bowsprit / Boomkin** (optional) — For attaching headsail tack or mizzen sheet.

**Sketch** (described in words, as KTU allows textual schematic): Side elevation showing the hull, two masts with the mizzen clearly **forward of the rudder post**, mainsail between mainmast and boom, mizzen sail between mizzenmast and its boom, jib forward of the forestay, all sails drawn as triangular panels.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 7 marks only if all six components above are mentioned, labelled correctly, and a sketch is included.)*

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate the first moment of sail area about the FP:**

$$
M_{FP} = A_{main} \cdot X_{main} + A_{mz} \cdot X_{mz} + A_{genoa} \cdot X_{genoa}
$$

$$
M_{FP} = (42 \times 5.2) + (12 \times 9.5) + (28 \times 2.4)
$$

$$
M_{FP} = 218.4 + 114.0 + 67.2 = 399.6 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the total sail area:**

$$
A_{S,total} = 42 + 12 + 28 = 82 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the centre of effort:**

$$
\bar{X}_{CE} = \frac{M_{FP}}{A_{S,total}} = \frac{399.6}{82} = 4.873 \; \text{m from FP}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the neutral-helm reference position:**

$$
X_{neutral} = 0.50 \cdot L_{WL} = 0.50 \times 14.0 = 7.0 \; \text{m from FP}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and comment on the helm condition:**

$$
\bar{X}_{CE} = 4.873 \; \text{m} \; < \; 7.0 \; \text{m} = X_{neutral}
$$

Since the centre of effort lies **forward of the midpoint of $L_{WL}$**, the vessel will exhibit **lee helm** (a tendency to fall off the wind). To correct this, the designer could:
- Increase headsail area or move the jib lead aft.
- Move the mainmast slightly aft (within structural limits).
- Reduce headsail area and shift weight in rig plan.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Many students forget to keep $L_{WL}$ and $X$ coordinates in the same unit (metres). Mixing m and cm causes a 100× error — instant loss of 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Helm direction sign convention:** Always state clearly whether $\bar{X}_{CE} > X_{neutral}$ (weather helm) or $\bar{X}_{CE} < X_{neutral}$ (lee helm). Vague statements like *"the helm is imbalanced"* earn no marks.
> 3. **Mizzen position check:** Confirm that the given $X_{mz} = 9.5$ m is forward of the rudder post (assume rudder post at ~11.5 m for a 14 m LWL). If $X_{mz} > X_{rudder}$, the vessel is a **yawl**, not a ketch. Always classify the rig *before* solving numerical problems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare the **ketch, yawl, and schooner** rigs in a tabular form, covering at least six parameters, and explain why the ketch rig is preferred for blue-water cruising. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A designer wishes to fit a ketch rig on a 10 m LWL vessel. The desired total sail area is 55 m², the headsail is 18 m², and the desired mizzen-to-main ratio is 0.25. Determine: **(i)** the mainsail area, **(ii)** the mizzen sail area, and **(iii)** the mizzen height if the mainmast height is 13 m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Ketch | Yawl | Schooner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of masts | 2 | 2 | 2 or more |
| Forward mast size | Main (tallest) | Main (tallest) | Fore (shorter) |
| Aft mast position | Forward of rudder post | Aft of rudder post | Aft, dominant |
| Mizzen/Driver sail area | 20–35% of main | < 20% of main | > 80% of main |
| Crew needed | 1–2 | 1 (solo-friendly) | 4–6 |
| Best use | Blue-water cruising | Solo sailing | Tall ships, training |

**Why ketch for blue-water cruising (explanation):**

- **Crew economy:** Long passages require the vessel to be manageable by a small, possibly fatigued crew. The ketch's split sail plan allows reefing and sail changes without leaving the cockpit.
- **Storm capability:** The mizzen can be backed to heave-to, holding the bow off the wind while the crew rests — a tactic not as effective in yawls or sloops.
- **Sail balance:** The mizzen provides a "lever arm" aft of the main CE, which designers can use to fine-tune helm balance, reducing weather helm in strong winds.
- **Cargo and storage:** The mizzen boom and rigging can support additional gear (dinghy stowage, antennas, solar panels) without overloading the main boom.

*(Full 7 marks require a complete table with at least 6 parameters + a coherent 4-point explanation.)*

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $L_{WL} = 10$ m
- $A_{S,total} = 55$ m²
- $A_{jib} = 18$ m²
- $A_{mz} / A_{main} = k_{mz} = 0.25$
- $H_{main} = 13$ m

**(i) Mainsail area:**

From the derivation in Section 3.2:

$$
A_{main} = \frac{A_{S,total} - A_{jib}}{1 + k_{mz}} = \frac{55 - 18}{1 + 0.25} = \frac{37}{1.25}
$$

$$
A_{main} = 29.6 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**(ii) Mizzen sail area:**

$$
A_{mz} = k_{mz} \cdot A_{main} = 0.25 \times 29.6 = 7.4 \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Verification:** $A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{jib} = 29.6 + 7.4 + 18.0 = 55.0$ m² ✓

**(iii) Mizzen height:**

Using the standard ketch ratio $H_{mz} / H_{main} = 0.40$ (mid-range value):

$$
H_{mz} = 0.40 \times 13.0 = 5.2 \; \text{m}
$$

(If the student uses 0.35 or 0.45, accept any value in the range $4.55$ m to $5.85$ m, but state the assumed ratio explicitly.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Always verify the total:** Students often compute $A_{main}$ and $A_{mz}$ but forget to check that $A_{main} + A_{mz} + A_{jib} = A_{S,total}$. Skipping this verification loses 1 mark.
> 2. **State the ratio assumption explicitly:** When asked to find $H_{mz}$, students must declare the assumed $H_{mz} / H_{main}$ ratio (e.g., 0.40) before substituting. Silently using a ratio earns partial credit only.
> 3. **Sanity check the mizzen position:** Compute $X_{mz} = 0.55 \times 10 = 5.5$ m from bow, and verify it is forward of the rudder post (typically at ~7.5–8.0 m for a 10 m LWL). If not, the rig is misidentified.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Ketches**

- ✅ A **ketch** has **two masts**: mainmast (forward, taller) and mizzenmast (aft, shorter).
- ✅ The **mizzenmast is stepped forward of the rudder post** — this is the single most important distinguishing feature.
- ✅ The **mizzen position** is typically at **45% – 65% of $L_{WL}$** from the bow.
- ✅ The **mizzen sail area** is **20% – 35%** of the mainsail area.
- ✅ The **mizzen height** is **35% – 55%** of the mainmast height.
- ✅ The **Centre of Effort (CE)** is found using the first-moment formula: $\bar{X}_{CE} = \sum A_i X_i / \sum A_i$.
- ✅ **Neutral helm** condition: $\bar{X}_{CE} \approx 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$.
- ✅ **Weather helm** when $\bar{X}_{CE} > 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$ (CE too far aft).
- ✅ **Lee helm** when $\bar{X}_{CE} < 0.50 \cdot L_{WL}$ (CE too far forward).
- ✅ **Ketch vs Yawl:** mizzen position (forward vs aft of rudder post) is the deciding factor.
- ✅ **Ketch vs Schooner:** in a ketch, the *forward* mast is taller; in a schooner, the *aft* mast is taller.
- ✅ **Advantages of ketch rig:** short-handed sailing, sail redundancy, heaving-to ability, balanced helm.
- ✅ **Typical KTU problem type:** Compute CE from given sail areas and positions, then classify the helm condition.
- ✅ **Always verify rig classification** (ketch vs yawl) by checking mizzen position before solving any numerical.
- ✅ **Unit consistency is critical:** all dimensions in metres, all areas in m², all coordinates measured from the same reference (FP).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** When given a numerical problem, always draw a quick sketch showing the FP, mast positions, sail areas, and the rudder post. This single habit prevents 80% of common errors (misidentifying rig type, sign errors in helm direction, unit mismatches). Examiners award **1–2 grace marks** for clear, labelled sketches even if the numerical answer has minor arithmetic slips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Etc.). Phases of Ship Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Etc.). Phases of Ship Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Phases of Ship Design — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In naval architecture and shipbuilding engineering, **ship design is not a single event but a sequential, iterative process** that progresses through several well-defined phases. Each phase refines the vessel's form, structure, systems, and cost envelope until a buildable, class-approved, commercially viable vessel emerges.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OENST721, Module 1), the principal phases of ship design are:

1. **Concept Design** (also called *Preliminary Design* or *Feasibility Design*)
2. **Contract Design** (also called *Functional Design* or *Basic Design*)
3. **Detailed Design** (also called *Production Design* or *Working Design*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examiner-Ready):**
> *"The phases of ship design refer to the systematic, progressive stages — Concept Design, Contract Design, and Detailed Design — through which a vessel's mission requirements are translated into a fully dimensioned, class-approved, and buildable engineering product, with iterative feedback between cost, performance, safety, and regulatory compliance at every stage."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The House-Building Metaphor

Imagine you want to build a house for a client who says, *"I want a comfortable home for a family of four in Kerala's coastal climate."*

| House-Building Stage | Equivalent Ship Design Phase |
|---|---|
| Sketching floor plans & getting client approval | **Concept Design** |
| Finalizing architectural drawings, electrical & plumbing layouts for the contract | **Contract Design** |
| Producing shop drawings for the carpenter, electrician, and mason to actually build | **Detailed Design** |

Just as you cannot ask a mason to "build a house" without going through plans, you cannot ask a shipyard to "build a ship" without progressing through the structured phases.

## 1.3 Why Phases? — The Need for a Structured Workflow

A modern vessel may involve:
- **3–5 million** individual parts
- **40+** engineering disciplines (structural, mechanical, electrical, HVAC, navigation)
- A build cost of **\$50 million to \$500+ million**
- Regulatory oversight from **Classification Societies** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV) and the **Flag State** (IMO conventions)

> [!NOTE]
> Phasing allows **cost escalation to be controlled**. Studies show roughly **80% of a ship's lifecycle cost is locked in by the end of Contract Design**. Errors caught at the Concept phase cost almost nothing to fix; the same errors caught during construction can cost millions.

**Key Stakeholders in Each Phase:**
- **Owner / Operator** — defines mission & pays
- **Naval Architect (Design Office)** — produces the design
- **Classification Society** — verifies safety & structural integrity
- **Shipyard** — builds the vessel
- **Flag Administration / IMO** — enforces international regulations
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Anatomy of Each Phase

## 2.1 Phase 1 — Concept Design (Preliminary / Feasibility Design)

This is the **"broad-strokes"** phase where the owner's vague needs become a *feasible ship*.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Mission Specification (Owner Requirements):**
  The owner declares: cargo type, deadweight (DWT) or passenger capacity, route, service speed $V_s$, range, year-round operational area, and budget envelope.

- **Step 2 — Selection of Principal Dimensions:**
  Using statistical data from similar ships (parent form / **mother ship design**), the designer estimates:
  - Length between perpendiculars $L_{PP}$
  - Breadth $B$
  - Depth $D$
  - Design draft $T$
  - Block coefficient $C_B$

- **Step 3 — Powering Estimation:**
  Using series such as **Taylor's Standard Series** or **Holtrop-Mennen**, required brake power $P_B$ is estimated from effective power $P_E$ via:

$$\text{EHP} = \frac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75} \quad \text{(metric HP)}$$

  where $R_T$ is total resistance (in kgf) and $V_s$ is service speed (in m/s).

- **Step 4 — General Arrangement (GA) Sketch:**
  A simple top-view sketch is drawn showing hull form, machinery spaces, cargo zones, accommodation.

- **Step 5 — Feasibility Decision:**
  If estimated building cost exceeds owner's budget by more than a threshold (typically **±10–15%**), the loop is iterated: dimensions are modified, speed is reduced, or simpler systems chosen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Concept design output is a **"Feasibility Report" + General Arrangement + approximate specifications** — NOT construction drawings.

## 2.2 Phase 2 — Contract Design (Functional / Basic Design)

This phase produces the **technical specification signed between Owner and Shipyard** — the legal heart of the vessel.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Hull Form Optimization:**
  Model testing in a towing tank or CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) refines the lines plan. Resistance, propulsion, and seakeeping are finalized.

- **Step 2 — Structural Design:**
  Midship section, scantlings (plate thicknesses, frame spacing) are computed per **Classification Society Rules** (e.g., IRS Rules for Bulk Carriers). The midship section modulus $Z$ must satisfy:

$$Z_{\text{required}} \geq \frac{M_{\text{sagging}}}{ \sigma_{\text{permissible}} }$$

  where $M$ is the still-water bending moment and $\sigma$ is allowable steel stress (~**175 N/mm²** for HTS steel).

- **Step 3 — Machinery & System Selection:**
  Main engine type (2-stroke diesel typically), generator capacity, steering gear, pumps, HVAC, and electrical systems are specified to maker and model level.

- **Step 4 — Detailed GA & Capacity Plan:**
  Every compartment is dimensioned; tank capacities (fuel, ballast, fresh water, lube oil) sum-checked to the required DWT.

- **Step 5 — Cost Estimate (Class 2 Estimate):**
  Accuracy of ±**5–10%**. Used in contract negotiation.

- **Step 6 — Specification & Sign-off:**
  The **"Specification of a Ship"** document, including lines plan, midship section, GA, capacity plan, and machinery list, is frozen. Both parties sign. Changes after this are **"variations"** costing money.

## 2.3 Phase 3 — Detailed Design (Production / Working Design)

This is the **shop-floor-ready** phase where every nut, bolt, and weld is detailed.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 — Production Drawing Generation:**
  Hull lines are expanded into **shell plate development** drawings; every plate is numbered, dimensioned, and its steel grade marked.

- **Step 2 — Structural Detail Drawings:**
  Every transverse frame, longitudinal girder, bracket, and stiffener is detailed with welding symbols per **AWS / ISO standards**.

- **Step 3 — Piping & Cable Routing:**
  3D CAD (e.g., **AVEVA Marine / Tribon / FORAN**) produces isometric drawings of every pipe, valve, and cable tray.

- **Step 4 — Workshop Documentation:**
  Cutting plans, nesting drawings for plates, and pre-fabrication lists are generated for the steel stockyard.

- **Step 5 — As-Built Update:**
  After construction, drawings are corrected to **"as-built"** status and form the basis of the vessel's *Trim & Stability Booklet* and *Operating Manuals*.

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **principal formulae and metrics** that reappear across all three phases. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of the pipe symbol to preserve table integrity.

| Parameter | Formula / Symbol | Typical Range (Merchant Ships) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | $10^3$ – $10^6$ | tonnes |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | 0.55 – 0.90 | — |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{PP}}$ | 0.55 – 0.85 | — |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | 0.90 – 0.99 | — |
| Length-to-Beam Ratio | $L_{PP}/\vert B \vert$ | 5.0 – 7.5 (tankers), 4.0 – 6.0 (bulk) | — |
| Beam-to-Draft Ratio | $B/\vert T \vert$ | 2.0 – 3.5 | — |
| Froude Number | $F_n = \dfrac{V_s}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{PP}}}$ | 0.10 – 0.35 | — |
| EHP (metric) | $\text{EHP} = \dfrac{R_T \cdot V_s}{75}$ | $10^3$ – $3 \times 10^4$ | HP |
| Shaft Power | $P_S = \dfrac{\text{EHP}}{\eta_H \cdot \eta_R}$ | — | kW / HP |
| Section Modulus Rule (CSR) | $Z \geq \dfrac{M_{sw}}{k \cdot \sigma_y}$ | varies by class | m³ |
| Deadweight Ratio | $\text{DWR} = \dfrac{\text{DWT}}{\Delta}$ | 0.55 – 0.85 | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyards** (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Hyundai Heavy Industries) use phased design to organize teams into *Design Office* (Phases 1–2) and *Production Drawing Office* (Phase 3).
- **Classification Societies** issue an **"Approval in Principle" (AIP)** during Concept Design, **"Design Approval"** after Contract Design, and **"Workshop Approval"** during Detailed Design.
- **Maritime Finance**: Banks lending for new ships require **Class 2 cost estimate** (from Contract Design) before releasing loan tranches.

> [!TIP]
> **Iteration is constant:** a feedback loop runs between phases. If Phase 3 reveals a structural clash, the design may revert to Phase 2 for hull-form change. Real shipbuilding programs have **3–5 major iterations**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown of Design Phase Outputs

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — From Mission Requirements to Principal Dimensions (Concept Phase)

### Given Mission Requirements (KTU-style typical problem):

> *"Design a bulk carrier to carry **55,000 DWT** at a **service speed of 14 knots** on a worldwide tramp trade route. Estimate the principal dimensions."*

### Step 1 — Choose a Parent (Mother) Ship & Block Coefficient

From statistical data of similar deadweight bulk carriers:
- $C_B \approx 0.78$ to $0.82$ (full-form bulk carrier).

Select:
$$C_B = 0.80$$

### Step 2 — Relate DWT to Displacement

The Deadweight Ratio (DWR) for a typical bulk carrier is:
$$\text{DWR} = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\Delta} \approx 0.72$$

Therefore, the required displacement is:
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\text{DWR}} = \frac{55{,}000}{0.72}$$

$$\Delta \approx 76{,}389 \text{ tonnes}$$

### Step 3 — Relate Displacement to Volume of Displacement

Using seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$:
$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{76{,}389}{1.025}$$

$$\nabla \approx 74{,}526 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Apply Block Coefficient Relation

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Rearrange for the **volume product**:
$$L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{\nabla}{C_B} = \frac{74{,}526}{0.80}$$

$$L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \approx 93{,}158 \text{ m}^3$$

### Step 5 — Apply Standard Ratios for Bulk Carriers

Standard ratios for a 55,000 DWT bulk carrier:
- Length-to-Beam ratio: $L_{PP} / B \approx 6.0$
- Beam-to-Draft ratio: $B / T \approx 2.6$

Let $B$ be the unknown. Then:
$$L_{PP} = 6.0 \cdot B$$
$$T = \frac{B}{2.6}$$

Substitute:
$$(6.0 B) \cdot B \cdot \left(\frac{B}{2.6}\right) = 93{,}158$$

$$\frac{6.0}{2.6} \cdot B^3 = 93{,}158$$

$$2.3077 \cdot B^3 = 93{,}158$$

$$B^3 = \frac{93{,}158}{2.3077} \approx 40{,}372$$

$$B \approx 34.3 \text{ m}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Remaining Dimensions

$$L_{PP} = 6.0 \times 34.3 \approx 205.8 \text{ m}$$

$$T = \frac{34.3}{2.6} \approx 13.2 \text{ m}$$

For depth (typical $D/T$ for bulkers $\approx 1.20$):
$$D = 1.20 \times 13.2 \approx 15.8 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7 — Verification

Re-compute displacement:
$$\nabla = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 205.8 \times 34.3 \times 13.2 \times 0.80$$

$$\nabla \approx 74{,}540 \text{ m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the required 74,526 m³ (rounding error < 0.02%). **Dimensions are feasible.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Outputs vs. Refinement Level per Phase

| Design Deliverable | Concept Design | Contract Design | Detailed Design |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lines Plan | Approximate sketch | Officially faired, class-approved | Expanded for plate development |
| General Arrangement | Single-line plan | Dimensioned GA & Capacity Plan | Construction-ready GA |
| Midship Section | Concept sketch | Scantlings calculated per Rules | Every plate, stiffener detailed |
| Powering Estimate | Statistical estimate (Taylor series) | Model test / CFD verified | Final brake power fixed |
| Cost Estimate | ±25% (Class 4/5) | ±5–10% (Class 2) | Production cost per unit operation |
| Machinery Selection | Maker not chosen | Maker & model fixed | Wiring & piping detailed |
| Class Approval | Approval in Principle (AIP) | Design Approval | Plan Approval / Workshop Approval |
| Documentation Volume | Few hundred pages | Several thousand pages | Tens of thousands of drawings |

## 3.3 Iterative Feedback Loop — Why It Matters

```
Concept  →  Contract  →  Detailed  →  Construction  →  Service
   ↑           ↑           ↑             ↑
   └───────────┴───────────┴─────────────┘
        Feedback loops during build & trials
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per industry data, **70% of all design changes during construction originate from incomplete Concept Design**. KTU examiners often test whether students understand that early-phase decisions dominate lifecycle cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sequential Phases of Ship Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Owner's Mission Requirements] --> B[PHASE 1: CONCEPT DESIGN]
    B --> B1[Select Parent Ship Data]
    B1 --> B2[Estimate Principal Dimensions L B T D]
    B2 --> B3[Preliminary Powering Estimate]
    B3 --> B4[Sketch General Arrangement]
    B4 --> B5[Feasibility Report and Cost Estimate Class 4]
    B5 --> B6{Design Feasible within Budget?}
    B6 -->|No| B1
    B6 -->|Yes| C[PHASE 2: CONTRACT DESIGN]
    C --> C1[Hull Form Optimization and Tank Testing]
    C1 --> C2[Structural Design per Class Rules]
    C2 --> C3[Machinery and Systems Finalized]
    C3 --> C4[Detailed General Arrangement]
    C4 --> C5[Class 2 Cost Estimate]
    C5 --> C6[Ship Specification Signed by Owner and Yard]
    C6 --> D[PHASE 3: DETAILED DESIGN]
    D --> D1[Shell Plate Development Drawings]
    D1 --> D2[Structural Detail Drawings]
    D2 --> D3[Piping Isometrics and Cable Routing]
    D3 --> D4[Workshop Nesting and Cutting Plans]
    D4 --> E[CONSTRUCTION AT SHIPYARD]
    E --> F[SEA TRIALS AND DELIVERY]
    F --> G[IN SERVICE OPERATION]
    G --> H[Feedback to Design Office for Future Ships]
    H -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stakeholder Interaction per Phase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Owner and Operator] -->|Mission and Budget| CON[Concept Phase]
    NA[Naval Architect] -->|Feasibility Study| O
    CON -->|AIP Request| CS[Classification Society]
    CS -->|Approval in Principle| CON
    CON --> CT[Contract Phase]
    NA -->|Lines Plan and GA| CT
    CT -->|Design Approval Request| CS
    CS -->|Design Approval Certificate| CT
    O -->|Signs Specification| CT
    SY[Shipyard] -->|Builds per Contract| CT
    CT --> DD[Detailed Phase]
    NA -->|Production Drawings| SY
    DD -->|Workshop Approval| CS
    CS -->|Plan Approval| SY
    SY -->|Constructs Vessel| SEA[Sea Trials]
    SEA -->|Final Approval| CS
    CS -->|Class Certificate Issued| O
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Information Maturity Across Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Concept] -->|Low fidelity broad-strokes data| P2[Phase 2: Contract]
    P2 -->|Refined and frozen data| P3[Phase 3: Detailed]
    P3 -->|Construction-ready data| P4[Build]
    P1 -.->|Vague specs| Note1[Cost commitment ~ 5 percent]
    P2 -.->|Frozen specs| Note2[Cost commitment ~ 80 percent]
    P3 -.->|Locked specs| Note3[Cost commitment ~ 99 percent]
    P4 -.->|Actual spend| Note4[Cost commitment 100 percent]
    Note1 --- P1
    Note2 --- P2
    Note3 --- P3
    Note4 --- P4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Visual Cue:** Always include at least one schematic in your answer for a 14-mark question on Phases of Ship Design. A clean block diagram showing **inputs → phase → outputs → cost commitment** scores maximum marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any three phases of ship design and state the principal output of each phase.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Concept Design:** Output is a *Feasibility Report* containing preliminary principal dimensions ($L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$, $D$), general arrangement sketch, approximate power, and a Class 4/5 cost estimate (±25%). (1 mark)
2. **Contract Design:** Output is the *Ship Specification* document — lines plan, structural midship section, capacity plan, machinery list, and a Class 2 cost estimate (±5–10%), signed by Owner and Shipyard. (1 mark)
3. **Detailed Design:** Output is *Production Drawings* — shell plate development, structural details, piping isometrics, and workshop nesting plans — sufficient for the shipyard to fabricate every component. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Why is the Concept Design phase considered the most economically critical phase of ship design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Approximately **80% of the vessel's lifecycle cost is committed** by the end of Concept Design, because principal dimensions, hull form coefficient $C_B$, and service speed $V_s$ are locked in. (1 mark)
- Changes made after the Contract phase (post-specification sign-off) are treated as **"variations"** and incur heavy cost penalties and contractual disputes. (1 mark)
- Early-stage design errors propagate — a 1% error in displacement estimate at Concept stage can translate to multi-million dollar variations in steel weight and fuel consumption over the vessel's 25-year life. (1 mark)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule:** Answer **ONE** of the following (either Question A **OR** Question B). Each question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Concept Design phase** of a ship. With a neat sketch, describe its inputs, key activities, and deliverables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier of **35,000 DWT** is to operate at a service speed of **13 knots**. Using a parent ship with $C_B = 0.78$ and DWR = 0.70, estimate the principal dimensions $L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$, and $D$. Assume $L_{PP}/B = 5.8$ and $B/T = 2.6$ for this vessel type. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Inputs (1 mark):**
- Owner's mission specification: DWT, $V_s$, route, cargo type, range, budget envelope, regulatory zone (e.g., IMO Tier III ECA compliance).

**Key Activities (4 marks):**
1. **Selection of parent ship data** — use statistical databases of sister vessels to choose $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, and form coefficients.
2. **Estimation of principal dimensions** — from DWT, choose DWR → displacement $\Delta$ → volumetric displacement $\nabla$ → $L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T$ via $C_B$.
3. **Preliminary powering** — EHP from Taylor's Standard Series or Holtrop-Mennen; convert to brake power using propulsive coefficients $\eta_D$, $\eta_R$, $\eta_H$.
4. **General Arrangement sketch** — single-line plan showing hull, machinery aft, cargo holds, accommodation block, tanks.
5. **Feasibility check** — cost estimated via parametric methods (e.g., \$/DWT); compare with owner's budget.

**Deliverables (2 marks):**
- Feasibility Report, Lines Plan (sketch), GA (sketch), preliminary specifications, Class 4 cost estimate.
- Approval in Principle (AIP) from Classification Society.

**Sketch:** A simple block diagram showing flow from **Owner Requirements → Principal Dimension Estimation → Powering → GA → Feasibility Report** should be drawn.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute displacement (1 mark):**
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\text{DWR}} = \frac{35{,}000}{0.70} = 50{,}000 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric displacement (1 mark):**
$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.025} \approx 48{,}780 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Volume product (1 mark):**
$$L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T = \frac{\nabla}{C_B} = \frac{48{,}780}{0.78} \approx 62{,}538 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Solve using standard ratios (2 marks):**

Let $B = x$. Then $L_{PP} = 5.8x$ and $T = x/2.6$.

$$(5.8x) \cdot x \cdot \left(\frac{x}{2.6}\right) = 62{,}538$$

$$\frac{5.8}{2.6} \cdot x^3 = 62{,}538$$

$$2.2308 \cdot x^3 = 62{,}538$$

$$x^3 = 28{,}038 \implies x = B \approx 30.35 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Final dimensions (2 marks):**

$$L_{PP} = 5.8 \times 30.35 \approx 176.0 \text{ m}$$

$$T = \frac{30.35}{2.6} \approx 11.67 \text{ m}$$

$$D = 1.20 \times T \approx 14.0 \text{ m} \quad \text{(assuming } D/T = 1.20\text{)}$$

**Verification:** $L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 176.0 \times 30.35 \times 11.67 \times 0.78 \approx 48{,}750$ m³ ✓ (within rounding tolerance of 48,780 m³).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls (Loses Marks If Ignored):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** displacement from tonnes to m³ using seawater density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$. This is the **#1 source of lost marks** in principal-dimension problems.
> 2. **Mixing up DWT and displacement** $\Delta$ — they are not equal. DWT is the *payload + consumables*; $\Delta$ is the *total weight of the ship including steel*.
> 3. **Not specifying units** for $L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$, $D$ (must be metres).
> 4. **Skipping the verification step** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for cross-checking the final dimensions.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Contract Design** and **Detailed Design** phases. List at least five deliverables unique to each phase and justify why Contract Design is considered the "legal heart" of shipbuilding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a flowchart, explain the **iterative feedback loops** that occur between the three phases of ship design. Why is iteration essential, and how does it impact the **build cost** of a vessel? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Contract Design | Detailed Design |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Freeze technical specification | Produce workshop drawings |
| Hull Form | Officially faired, model-tested | Expanded to plates |
| Cost Estimate | Class 2 (±5–10%) | Production-cost per operation |
| Approval | Design Approval by Class | Workshop / Plan Approval by Class |
| Documentation | Spec. document, Lines plan, GA, Capacity plan, Midship section | Production drawings, Pipe isometrics, Cable schedules, Nesting plans |
| Audience | Owner & Shipyard (legal) | Shipyard workshop (fabrication) |
| Iteration Frequency | Moderate | High (within fixed spec.) |

**Five unique deliverables of Contract Design (1.5 marks):**
1. Signed *Specification of the Ship*
2. Officially faired *Lines Plan*
3. *Midship Section* with scantlings per Class Rules
4. *Capacity Plan* with tank-volume reconciliation
5. *Machinery List* with maker/model numbers

**Five unique deliverables of Detailed Design (1.5 marks):**
1. *Shell plate development drawings* with plate numbers
2. *Structural detail drawings* with welding symbols
3. *Piping isometrics* and valve lists
4. *Electrical cable schedules* and routing diagrams
5. *Nesting / cutting plans* for steel stockyard

**Why Contract Design is the "Legal Heart" (this phrasing earns the +bonus mark):**
The Specification signed at the end of Contract Design is a **legally binding contract document** between Owner and Shipyard. Any deviation thereafter is a "variation order" with cost and time implications, often leading to **arbitration disputes** under maritime law.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Flowchart (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept Design] -->|Outputs Principal Dimensions| B[Contract Design]
    B -->|Outputs Specification| C[Detailed Design]
    C -->|Outputs Production Drawings| D[Construction]
    D -->|Construction Issues Found| C
    C -->|Hull Form Conflict Found| B
    B -->|Cost Overrun Found| A
    A -->|Market Change Owner Modifies Requirement| B
```

**Why Iteration is Essential (2 marks):**
- Ship design is a **coupled multi-variable optimization**. Changing $L_{PP}$ (a Concept-stage variable) alters resistance, stability, structural weight, and cost simultaneously.
- Real-world shipbuilding is subject to **regulatory changes** (e.g., new IMO EEXI/CII rules), **supplier delays**, and **owner preference changes** — iteration accommodates these.
- Without iteration, errors propagate to construction, where steel is already cut; corrections cost **10–100× more** than corrections at the drawing board.

**Impact on Build Cost (2 marks):**
- The **"rule of ten"** in ship design: an error that costs **\$1 to fix at Concept Design costs \$10 to fix at Contract Design, \$100 to fix at Detailed Design, and \$1000 to fix after construction has started.**
- Approximately **70% of construction-stage design changes** originate from incomplete Concept Design, and **15–20%** of total build cost on typical commercial vessels is spent on rework due to design iterations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Drawing the flowchart but not labeling the loops** — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if feedback arrows are unlabelled.
> 2. **Confusing "iteration" with "rework"** — clarify that iteration is *planned and structured*; rework is *unplanned and costly*.
> 3. **Forgetting the cost-multiplication factor** — students often state the "rule of ten" qualitatively without quantifying it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⏱ **Three Principal Phases:** Concept → Contract → Detailed (each progressively refines fidelity).
- 📊 **Cost Commitment Curve:** ~5% locked at Concept, ~80% locked at Contract, ~99% locked at Detailed.
- 📐 **Concept Phase Output:** Principal dimensions ($L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$, $D$), $C_B$, EHP, Class 4 cost estimate, GA sketch.
- 📋 **Contract Phase Output:** Frozen Specification, faired Lines Plan, Midship Section, Capacity Plan, Class 2 cost.
- 🔩 **Detailed Phase Output:** Production drawings, shell plate development, pipe isometrics, nesting plans.
- 🔁 **Iteration is Mandatory:** Real ship design involves 3–5 major iterations between phases.
- ⚖️ **"Rule of Ten":** Errors multiply in cost by 10× each phase they advance.
- 🏛 **Class Society Approvals:** AIP → Design Approval → Plan/Workshop Approval.
- 📝 **DWT ≠ Displacement:** Always divide DWT by DWR to get $\Delta$.
- 💧 **Density Conversion:** $\nabla = \Delta / 1.025$ m³ (seawater).
- 🧮 **Verification Step:** Always re-compute $\nabla$ from final $L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ to confirm.
- 🌍 **Real Vessels:** Kochi-Metro Shipyard-built vessels and Vizhinjam-class boats follow this exact phasing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Spiral. Ship Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Spiral. Ship Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Design Spiral — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Spiral (KTU Syllabus Definition):** The *Design Spiral* is a classical naval-architectural methodology that models the iterative, non-linear, and interdependent process of ship design. It graphically represents how the ship's principal characteristics (displacement, length, beam, draft, power, stability, etc.) influence one another in a cyclic sequence, requiring repeated refinement until a feasible and optimal solution is reached.

The Design Spiral was formally introduced by **D. G. M. Evans (1959)** and later refined by **Benford, Hoekendorf, Taggart, and many others** as the foundational concept in marine vessel preliminary design. Each "loop" of the spiral represents a complete design iteration, and the engineer progresses inward as the design converges toward a finalized solution.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe of a Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are baking a cake. You don't just throw ingredients together once and serve it. You taste it, adjust sugar, adjust flour, and bake again. Each tasting-and-adjusting cycle makes the cake better. The **Design Spiral** is exactly this — but for a ship. You start with a guess (mission requirements), calculate size → weight → power → stability, and then check if the ship's *speed* is achievable, if it floats properly, if the propeller is big enough, etc. If not, you adjust and loop back. With every loop, the design gets sharper, more accurate, and more detailed.

### 1.3 Why is it called a "Spiral" and not a "Circle"?

Because as we move through iterations, the *level of detail and accuracy* increases. The radius of each loop represents the *degree of uncertainty*. The innermost loops are highly refined (final design), while the outermost loops are rough estimates (concept design). This progressive tightening is what gives the visual representation its **spiral** shape rather than a closed circle.

### 1.4 Standard Naval Architectural Reference Metrics

- **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** $L_{BP}$ — typically in **meters (m)**.
- **Length Overall (LOA):** $L_{OA}$ — includes bulbous bow and stern overhangs.
- **Beam (B):** Maximum molded breadth, in **meters (m)**.
- **Draft (T):** Maximum loaded draft, in **meters (m)**.
- **Depth (D):** Moulded depth, keel to main deck, in **meters (m)**.
- **Displacement (Δ):** Total weight of water displaced by the ship, in **tonnes (t)** or **kiloNewtons (kN)**.
- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** A dimensionless form factor, typically **0.40 to 0.90**.
- **Speed-Length Ratio (V/√L):** A Froude-scaled dimensionless speed parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Design Spiral is universally tested as a *short answer* (3 marks) question in Part A of ESE papers, where students are expected to *sketch and label* the spiral with all stages and briefly explain the iterative logic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-to-Seven Stages of the Design Spiral

The classical **Evans Design Spiral** (and its modern KTU-aligned version) consists of the following iterative stages. Each stage depends on the outputs of the previous one, and a check at the end of each loop often forces the designer to return to an earlier stage.

1. **Mission Requirement / Owner Specification:** Define the vessel's purpose, route, cargo, speed, and regulatory framework (SOLAS, MARPOL, Class rules).
2. **Main Dimensions Determination:** Compute L, B, T, D using empirical formulas, statistical databases, or regression lines.
3. **Form / Hull Coefficient Selection:** Choose $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_WP$ based on ship type and speed.
4. **Powering & Resistance Estimation:** Apply **Holtrop–Mennen**, **ITTC-78**, or **Savitsky** to estimate effective power ($P_E$) and required brake power ($P_B$).
5. **Stability & Trim Assessment:** Verify that GM, GZ-curve, trim, and freeboard meet IMO criteria.
6. **Weight & Cost Estimation:** Confirm that the lightweight + deadweight equals displacement, and estimate building cost.
7. **Economic & Feasibility Loop:** Verify freight rates, payback period, and return on investment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Iteration Logic:** If *any* check at the inner stages (e.g., powering) fails, the designer must *back-track* along the spiral to an earlier stage (e.g., increase length) and re-calculate. The spiral collapses into a single solution only when **all** constraints are simultaneously satisfied.

### 2.2 Ship Parameters — Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Ship Parameters (or Principal Particulars)** are the master dimensions and form coefficients that uniquely define a vessel's geometry and hydrodynamic character. They are the *quantitative inputs and outputs* of the Design Spiral.

#### A. **Geometric / Dimensional Parameters**

| Symbol | Parameter | Description | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:----------|:------------|:------------:|
| $L_{OA}$ | Length Overall | Total length including appendages | **m** |
| $L_{BP}$ | Length Between Perpendiculars | Length from FP to AP | **m** |
| $L_{WL}$ | Length at Waterline | Length measured at design draft | **m** |
| $B$ | Beam (Moulded Breadth) | Maximum breadth amidships | **m** |
| $T$ | Draft | Vertical distance WL to keel | **m** |
| $D$ | Moulded Depth | Keel to main deck | **m** |

#### B. **Form Coefficients (Dimensionless)**

| Symbol | Coefficient | Definition | Range |
|:------:|:------------|:-----------|:------|
| $C_B$ | Block Coefficient | $\nabla \div (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | **0.40 – 0.90** |
| $C_P$ | Prismatic Coefficient | $\nabla \div (A_M \cdot L_{BP})$ | **0.55 – 0.85** |
| $C_M$ | Midship Section Coefficient | $A_M \div (B \cdot T)$ | **0.90 – 0.99** |
| $C_WP$ | Waterplane Area Coefficient | $A_{WP} \div (L_{WL} \cdot B)$ | **0.70 – 0.95** |
| $C_V$ | Vertical Prismatic Coefficient | $\nabla \div (A_{WP} \cdot T)$ | derived |
| $C_{f}$ | Frictional Coefficient | ITTC-57 correlation | dimensionless |

#### C. **Performance Parameters**

| Symbol | Parameter | Formula / Source | Unit |
|:------:|:----------|:-----------------|:----:|
| $\Delta$ | Displacement | $\rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | **t** or **kN** |
| $V$ | Service Speed | Owner-specified | **knots (kt)** |
| $V / \sqrt{L}$ | Speed-Length Ratio | Froude scaling | dimensionless |
| $Fn$ | Froude Number | $V \div \sqrt{g \cdot L}$ | dimensionless |
| $P_E$ | Effective Power | $R_T \cdot V$ | **kW** |
| $P_B$ | Brake Power | $P_E \div \eta_D \cdot \eta_S$ | **kW** |
| $DWT$ | Deadweight | Cargo + Bunker + Stores | **t** |

### 2.3 Key Inter-Relationships (The "Why" Behind the Math)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ship parameters are *not independent*. They are linked by the laws of naval architecture, hydrostatics, and hydrodynamics. For example:
> - **$C_B$ depends on Froude Number** — high $Fn$ (fast ships) → low $C_B$ (slender hulls).
> - **$L$ depends on $V$ and $Fn$** — for a given speed, doubling $Fn$ requires $L$ to drop by a factor of 4.
> - **$P_E$ scales with $V^3$** — small increases in speed lead to *cubic* jumps in power demand.
> - **$C_P = C_B \div C_M$** — an algebraic identity that the KTU examiner *frequently* tests.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concept / Bid Design:** Ship parameters are used in the *earliest* stage of a newbuilding project to produce competitive offers to shipowners.
- **Class & Statutory Approval:** The principal particulars are the inputs to every stability, structural, and tonnage calculation submitted to IACS classification societies.
- **Conversion & Modification Studies:** Existing ship parameters serve as the baseline when assessing lengthening, beamening, or re-engining.
- **Port & Route Planning:** Beam and draft dictate which canals, locks, and terminals the vessel can use (e.g., *Panamax*, *Suezmax*, *Malaccamax*).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Block Coefficient $C_B$

The Block Coefficient is the most fundamental form coefficient. Its definition flows directly from hydrostatics.

**Step 1 — Start with the volume of displacement.**
By Archimedes' principle, the volume of water displaced by a floating ship equals the underwater hull volume $\nabla$:

$$\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{BP}} A(x) \, dx$$

where $A(x)$ is the cross-sectional area of the hull at station $x$ along the length.

**Step 2 — Define the bounding rectangular prism.**
Imagine an imaginary rectangular box that encloses the entire underwater hull, with dimensions $L_{BP} \times B \times T$:

$$V_{box} = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Define the Block Coefficient as the volumetric "fullness" ratio.**
$C_B$ is defined as the ratio of the actual underwater volume to the bounding rectangular volume:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
- If $C_B = 1.0$, the hull fully fills the box (a "brick").
- If $C_B = 0.5$, the hull occupies only half the box (a "slim" or fine hull).
- **Typical $C_B$ values:** Tanker ≈ **0.80–0.85**, Bulk Carrier ≈ **0.75–0.82**, Container Ship ≈ **0.55–0.70**, Naval Frigate ≈ **0.40–0.50**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$

**Step 1 — Define the midship area.**
The midship section (at amidships, typically at $x = 0.5 \cdot L_{BP}$) has the maximum cross-sectional area:

$$A_M = B \cdot T \cdot C_M$$

**Step 2 — Define the Prismatic Coefficient.**
$C_P$ is the ratio of the underwater volume to a hypothetical volume of constant cross-section equal to $A_M$ stretched along the entire length:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

**Step 3 — Algebraic identity linking $C_P$, $C_B$, and $C_M$.**
Substitute the expression for $A_M$ into the $C_P$ formula:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{B \cdot T \cdot C_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

Recognize that $\nabla \div (B \cdot T \cdot L_{BP}) = C_B$, therefore:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

This is the *most-tested* identity in KTU naval architecture papers.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Displacement Formula

**Step 1 — Mass-based displacement.**
The displacement $\Delta$ in tonnes (mass) is the volume of the underwater hull multiplied by the density of seawater (typically $\rho = 1.025 \, t/m^3$ for salt water):

$$\Delta_{mass} = \rho \cdot \nabla \quad \text{[tonnes]}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\nabla$ using $C_B$.**
Substitute $\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$:

$$\Delta_{mass} = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Weight-based displacement.**
In SI units, the weight displacement is the mass displacement multiplied by gravitational acceleration ($g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$):

$$\Delta_{weight} = \rho \cdot g \cdot C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$

Or, expressed in **kiloNewtons (kN)** for naval architectural use:

$$\Delta = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}{1000} \quad \text{[kN]}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Speed-Length Ratio and Froude Number

**Step 1 — Froude's Scaling Logic.**
William Froude showed that geometrically similar hulls produce *similar* wave patterns only when their **Froude Number** is equal. This ensures dynamic similarity in model testing.

$$Fn = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the speed-length ratio.**
Rearranging and noting that $\sqrt{g} \approx \sqrt{9.81} \approx 3.132$, the speed-length ratio is:

$$\frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{WL}}} = Fn \cdot \sqrt{g} \approx 3.132 \cdot Fn$$

**Step 3 — Typical Speed-Length Ratios by Ship Type:**

| Ship Type | $V / \sqrt{L}$ (knots/√m) | Typical $Fn$ |
|:----------|:-------------------------:|:------------:|
| Tanker (slow) | 0.50 – 0.65 | 0.16 – 0.21 |
| Bulk Carrier | 0.65 – 0.80 | 0.21 – 0.26 |
| Container Ship | 0.85 – 1.10 | 0.27 – 0.35 |
| Naval Combatant | 1.20 – 1.80 | 0.38 – 0.58 |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A bulk carrier has $L_{BP} = 150 \, m$, $B = 25 \, m$, $T = 9.5 \, m$, and $C_B = 0.78$. Find the displacement in tonnes and the prismatic coefficient if $C_M = 0.95$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the underwater volume.**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \cdot 150 \cdot 25 \cdot 9.5$$

$$\nabla = 0.78 \cdot 35625 = 27,787.5 \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate displacement in tonnes (salt water, $\rho = 1.025 \, t/m^3$).**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 27,787.5 = 28,482.2 \, t$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the prismatic coefficient.**

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.78}{0.95} = 0.821$$

**Step 4 — Verification (consistency check).**
Re-derive $\nabla$ from $C_P$:

$$\nabla = C_P \cdot C_M \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.821 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 35625 = 27,786.6 \, m^3$$

The minor difference is due to rounding of $C_P$. Both routes yield ~27,790 $m^3$, confirming **internal consistency**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Classical Design Spiral (Mermaid Block Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below uses **only alphanumeric node IDs** and **plain uppercase text labels** (no bold, italic, or HTML tags inside the labels), strictly complying with the KTU-Premier-Engine V10 rendering safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STAGE 1 MISSION AND OWNER REQUIREMENTS] --> A2[STAGE 2 MAIN DIMENSIONS L B T D]
    A2 --> A3[STAGE 3 FORM COEFFICIENTS CB CP CM CWP]
    A3 --> A4[STAGE 4 POWERING AND RESISTANCE ESTIMATION]
    A4 --> A5[STAGE 5 STABILITY AND TRIM CHECK]
    A5 --> A6[STAGE 6 WEIGHT AND COST ESTIMATION]
    A6 --> A7[STAGE 7 ECONOMIC FEASIBILITY]
    A7 --> A8{ALL CONSTRAINTS SATISFIED}
    A8 -- NO RETURN TO STAGE 2 --> A2
    A8 -- YES FINAL DESIGN APPROVED --> A9[INNERMOST LOOP DETAILED PRODUCTION DRAWINGS]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Interdependence of Ship Parameters

This schematic maps how each *ship parameter* feeds information into the other, justifying the iterative nature of the Design Spiral.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[PRINCIPAL DIMENSIONS L B T D] --> P2[BLOCK COEFFICIENT CB]
    P2 --> P3[DISPLACEMENT DELTA]
    P2 --> P4[PRISMATIC COEFFICIENT CP]
    P4 --> P5[MIDSHIP COEFFICIENT CM]
    P1 --> P6[SPEED LENGTH RATIO V OVER SQRT L]
    P6 --> P7[FROUDE NUMBER FN]
    P3 --> P8[EFFECTIVE POWER PE]
    P6 --> P8
    P1 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[BRAKE POWER PB]
    P3 --> P10[STABILITY GM GZ CURVE]
    P1 --> P10
    P3 --> P11[DEADWEIGHT DWT]
    P9 --> P12[ECONOMIC FEASIBILITY]
    P11 --> P12
    P10 --> P12
    P12 --> P13{ECONOMICALLY VIABLE}
    P13 -- NO ITERATE --> P1
    P13 -- YES APPROVED --> P14[FINAL SHIP PARTICULARS]
```

### 4.3 Visual Mapping of the Form Coefficients on a Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[HULL UNDERWATER VOLUME NABLA] --> Cb[CB NABLA DIVIDED BY L B T]
    H1 --> Cp[CP NABLA DIVIDED BY AM x LBP]
    H1 --> Cv[CV NABLA DIVIDED BY AWP x T]
    Am[MIDSHIP AREA AM] --> Cm[CM AM DIVIDED BY B x T]
    Awp[WATERPLANE AREA AWP] --> Cwp[CWP AWP DIVIDED BY LWL x B]
    Cb --> Cmp[CB DIVIDED BY CM EQUALS CP]
    Cm --> Cmp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the Design Spiral. Why is it represented as a spiral and not as a circle?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Design Spiral** is a graphical representation of the iterative process of ship design, where each cycle of calculation and verification progressively refines the vessel's principal characteristics (length, beam, draft, power, stability, displacement, etc.) until an optimal solution is reached. It is depicted as a **spiral** rather than a circle because each successive loop brings the design *closer* to the final solution, with **progressively reducing uncertainty and increasing detail**. The outer loops represent rough concept design, while the inner loops represent fully detailed production design. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Block Coefficient ($C_B$) and the Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$). State the algebraic relationship between $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Block Coefficient $C_B$** is the ratio of the underwater volume of the hull to the volume of a bounding rectangular box of dimensions $L_{BP} \times B \times T$:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

The **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** is the ratio of the underwater volume to the volume of a prism of constant midship area stretched along the entire length:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$$

The algebraic relationship is:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Sketch and explain the Design Spiral as applied to the preliminary design of a merchant ship. Label all stages clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Panamax bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- $L_{BP} = 220 \, m$, $B = 32.2 \, m$, $T = 12.5 \, m$
- $C_M = 0.96$, $C_WP = 0.84$
- Service speed $V = 14.5$ knots
- Seawater density $\rho = 1.025 \, t/m^3$

Calculate: **(i)** the Block Coefficient, **(ii)** the Prismatic Coefficient, **(iii)** the Displacement in tonnes, **(iv)** the Froude Number, and **(v)** the Speed-Length Ratio. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Spiral Sketch and Explanation (7 marks):**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1. The student should draw a spiral with **7 stages**:

1. **Mission and Owner Requirements** — define the *why* of the ship (cargo, route, speed).
2. **Main Dimensions** — choose L, B, T, D.
3. **Form Coefficients** — choose $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_WP$.
4. **Powering / Resistance** — compute effective and brake power.
5. **Stability / Trim** — check GM, GZ, freeboard, trim.
6. **Weight / Cost** — verify $\Delta = LWT + DWT$ and estimate building cost.
7. **Economic Feasibility** — ROI, freight rates, payback.

The spiral *iterates* — if any check fails, the designer returns to Stage 2 and adjusts the main dimensions. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for explanation)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Block Coefficient:**
The student must first determine $C_B$ using the *typical* Panamax bulk carrier assumption. The KTU board typically expects a *given or assumed* $C_B$. Using standard Panamax data, $C_B \approx 0.78$.

$$C_B = 0.78$$

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient:**

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.78}{0.96} = 0.8125 \approx 0.813$$

**(iii) Displacement in tonnes:**

$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.78 \cdot 220 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 12.5$$

$$\nabla = 0.78 \cdot 88550 = 69,069 \, m^3$$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 69069 = 70,795.7 \, t \approx 70,796 \, t$$

**(iv) Froude Number:**

Convert $V = 14.5 \, kt$ to m/s: $V = 14.5 \cdot 0.5144 = 7.459 \, m/s$

$$Fn = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}} = \frac{7.459}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 220}} = \frac{7.459}{\sqrt{2158.2}} = \frac{7.459}{46.456}$$

$$Fn = 0.1606 \approx 0.161$$

**(v) Speed-Length Ratio:**

$$\frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{BP}}} = \frac{7.459}{\sqrt{220}} = \frac{7.459}{14.832} = 0.503 \, (m/s)/\sqrt{m}$$

Or in the *common naval-architect unit* (knots / √m):

$$\frac{V}{\sqrt{L_{BP}}} = \frac{14.5}{\sqrt{220}} = \frac{14.5}{14.832} = 0.978 \, \text{kt}/\sqrt{m}$$

**(7 marks: 1 + 1 + 2 + 1.5 + 1.5)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the principal dimensions and form coefficients used in ship design. Discuss how Block Coefficient varies with ship type and Froude Number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A naval frigate has the following particulars:
- $L_{BP} = 120 \, m$, $B = 14 \, m$, $T = 4.5 \, m$
- Displacement $\Delta = 4,800 \, t$ (salt water)
- Service speed $V = 28$ knots

Calculate: **(i)** the Block Coefficient, **(ii)** the underwater volume $\nabla$, **(iii)** the prismatic coefficient if $C_M = 0.78$, and **(iv)** the Froude Number. Comment on whether the $C_B$ is consistent with a high-speed combatant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

*Principal Dimensions:* Discuss $L_{BP}$, $L_{OA}$, $B$, $T$, $D$ with their respective roles (hydrodynamic, statutory, structural). **(2 marks)**

*Form Coefficients:* Define $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_WP$ with formulas. **(2 marks)**

*Variation with Ship Type and Froude Number:* Use a table or descriptive explanation. **(3 marks)**

| Ship Type | $Fn$ | $C_B$ |
|:----------|:----:|:-----:|
| Tanker (slow) | 0.15 – 0.20 | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Bulk Carrier | 0.20 – 0.25 | 0.75 – 0.82 |
| Container Ship | 0.25 – 0.32 | 0.60 – 0.70 |
| Frigate / Corvette | 0.40 – 0.55 | 0.40 – 0.55 |

**Key rule:** As $Fn$ increases, $C_B$ decreases — i.e., *fast ships have slender hulls* to reduce wave-making resistance.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Block Coefficient:**

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{4800}{1.025} = 4682.93 \, m^3$$

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{4682.93}{120 \cdot 14 \cdot 4.5} = \frac{4682.93}{7560}$$

$$C_B = 0.619 \approx 0.62$$

**(ii) Underwater volume:**

$$\nabla = 4682.93 \, m^3 \approx 4683 \, m^3$$

**(iii) Prismatic Coefficient:**

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.62}{0.78} = 0.795 \approx 0.80$$

**(iv) Froude Number:**

$V = 28 \, kt = 28 \cdot 0.5144 = 14.403 \, m/s$

$$Fn = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}} = \frac{14.403}{\sqrt{9.81 \cdot 120}} = \frac{14.403}{\sqrt{1177.2}} = \frac{14.403}{34.31}$$

$$Fn = 0.4198 \approx 0.42$$

**Comment:** A $C_B$ of 0.62 is *higher* than the typical 0.40–0.55 expected for a frigate at $Fn \approx 0.42$. This suggests the hull is *somewhat full* for a combatant, which may indicate a multi-role design (e.g., a frigate with helicopter hangar and sonar dome causing additional volume). However, it is *marginally acceptable* and within tolerance for a modern frigate. **(7 marks: 1.5 + 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert knots → m/s** when computing $Fn$. A student who writes $Fn = 14.5 / \sqrt{9.81 \cdot 220}$ without conversion loses **1.5 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Using freshwater density ($\rho = 1.000$)** instead of saltwater ($1.025$) for seagoing vessels — this is a **classic trap** in the KTU board.
> 3. **Confusing $C_P$ with $C_V$** — the vertical prismatic coefficient uses $A_{WP}$, not $A_M$. Easy 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Drawing the Design Spiral as a closed circle** — the spiral's *open-ended, inward-progressing* nature is the *whole point* of the question. Loss of 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the "iteration" arrows** on the spiral diagram — the board explicitly tests whether the student understands that the spiral is *cyclic*, not linear. Loss of 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up $L_{BP}$ with $L_{OA}$** in $Fn$ calculation — the Froude number *must* use the *wetted* length, conventionally $L_{BP}$ or $L_{WL}$. Loss of 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Design Spiral & Ship Parameters):**

- The **Design Spiral** is an *iterative* naval-architectural methodology that progressively refines a ship's design through interconnected stages of dimensioning, form selection, powering, stability, and economic verification.
- The spiral is **NOT a circle** — each loop tightens around the final solution with reducing uncertainty.
- The **7 classical stages** are: Mission → Dimensions → Form → Powering → Stability → Weight/Cost → Economics.
- **Ship Parameters** are the *quantitative DNA* of a vessel, comprising *principal dimensions* (L, B, T, D), *form coefficients* ($C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_WP$, $C_V$), and *performance metrics* ($\Delta$, $V$, $Fn$, $P_E$, $P_B$, $DWT$).
- **Block Coefficient $C_B$** = ratio of actual underwater volume to bounding box volume; range **0.40–0.90**.
- **Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$** = ratio of volume to stretched midship prism; always **$\leq 1$**.
- **Midship Coefficient $C_M$** = ratio of actual midship area to bounding rectangle; range **0.90–0.99**.
- **Key identity:** $C_P = C_B / C_M$ — *memorize this, it appears in nearly every KTU paper*.
- **Key identity 2:** $Fn = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L}$ — *always convert knots to m/s first*.
- **Key identity 3:** $\Delta_{mass} = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$ — *use $\rho = 1.025 \, t/m^3$ for saltwater*.
- **Design rule of thumb:** High $Fn$ (fast ships) → Low $C_B$ (slender hulls). Low $Fn$ (slow tankers) → High $C_B$ (full hulls).
- **Speed-Length Ratio** $V/\sqrt{L}$ is a convenient dimensionless speed parameter; typical range **0.5–1.8** for commercial and naval vessels.
- The spiral is *non-linear* and *backward-feeding* — failures at inner stages force redesign at outer stages.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadweight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadweight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In naval architecture and marine vessel design, **Deadweight (DWT or DW)** is defined as the total weight that a vessel is designed to safely carry when it is fully loaded, including cargo, fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, ballast water, provisions, crew, passengers, and their baggage.

Mathematically, it is the difference between the **Load Waterline Displacement** (total weight of the vessel including everything aboard) and the **Lightship Weight** (weight of the empty vessel itself).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (High-Yield):**
> *Deadweight (DWT) = Loaded Displacement (Δ_L) − Lightship Weight (W_LS)*
> It is expressed in **metric tonnes (t)** and represents the *earning capacity* of a commercial vessel.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Think of a cargo truck:
- The **truck itself** (chassis, engine, body, tires) is like the *Lightship Weight*.
- The **maximum load the truck can legally and safely carry** (goods, fuel in the tank, driver, luggage) is the *Deadweight*.

If you weigh the fully loaded truck and subtract the empty truck's weight, you get exactly what the truck is "carrying for a living" — its **payload**. For a ship, this same idea is what makes commercial sense: a ship earns revenue based on how much Deadweight it can transport. A higher Deadweight for the same ship size = more profit potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Deadweight Matters in Marine Design:**
> - Determines the **earning capacity** of a merchant ship.
> - Directly governs **cargo intake**, **fuel endurance**, and **voyage planning**.
> - It is the **primary metric** in ship chartering (e.g., Capesize, Panamax vessels are classified by DWT).

### Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| Deadweight | **DWT** | Total carrying capacity of a loaded ship |
| Lightship Weight | **W_LS** | Weight of empty ship (hull + machinery + outfit) |
| Loaded Displacement | **Δ_L** | Total weight of ship + everything aboard |
| Light Displacement | **Δ_LL** | Weight of ship when empty (numerically = W_LS) |
| Deadweight Coefficient | **C_D** | Form coefficient comparing DWT to block volume |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Breaking Down Deadweight — Structured Logic

Deadweight is **NOT a single quantity**. It is a *composite* of two clearly separable groups:

**A. Cargo Deadweight (Payload)** — The "revenue-generating" weight:
1. **Cargo** — actual goods being transported.
2. **Passengers and baggage** — for passenger vessels.
3. **Container weight** — for container ships (tare + contents).

**B. Ship's Outfittings / Consumables** — The "operational" weight:
1. **Fuel oil (FO)** and **Diesel oil (DO)** for propulsion.
2. **Lubricating oil** for machinery.
3. **Fresh water (FW)** for crew and boilers.
4. **Ballast water** — taken on for stability when unladen.
5. **Provisions and stores** — food, spares, safety equipment.
6. **Crew and effects** — crew weight and personal luggage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Deadweight Classification:**
> Separating DWT into these categories allows the ship operator to compute:
> - **Net Deadweight (NDWT)** = Cargo-only carrying capacity.
> - **Useful Deadweight** = Cargo + Passengers.
> - **Total Deadweight** = Sum of *everything consumable and carryable* (used in charter parties).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}$ | Deadweight definition | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $\Delta_L = W_{LS} + DWT$ | Loaded displacement | tonnes (t) |
| 3 | $W_{LS} = W_{Steel} + W_{Outfit} + W_{Machinery}$ | Lightship breakdown | tonnes (t) |
| 4 | $C_D = \dfrac{DWT}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D}$ | Deadweight coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement = density × volume displaced | tonnes |
| 6 | $DWT_{Cargo} = DWT - (W_{Fuel} + W_{FW} + W_{Stores})$ | Net cargo deadweight | tonnes (t) |

Where:
- $L_{BP}$ = Length Between Perpendiculars
- $B$ = Molded Breadth
- $D$ = Molded Depth
- $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement (m³)
- $\rho$ = Density of seawater = **1.025 t/m³** (standard), **1.000 t/m³** for freshwater

### Engineering Utility — Real-World Application

The Deadweight Coefficient $C_D$ is a powerful tool used during the **preliminary design phase**. Naval architects use it as a benchmark to:
1. **Compare similar ship types** — e.g., a well-designed bulk carrier has $C_D \approx 0.55$ to $0.70$.
2. **Estimate DWT** early in design when only principal dimensions are known.
3. **Validate design feasibility** — unusually high or low $C_D$ signals design inefficiency.

In shipping economics, vessels are routinely classified by DWT:
- **Handysize**: ~15,000 – 35,000 DWT
- **Panamax**: ~60,000 – 80,000 DWT
- **Capesize**: >150,000 DWT

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Deadweight from Displacement Principle

**Starting Concept:**
A floating ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the total weight of the ship (Archimedes' Principle).

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Build-up:**

The total weight of the loaded ship equals the weight of the displaced water:

$$
W_{Loaded} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_L
$$

where $\nabla_L$ is the displaced volume at the **Load Waterline (LWL)**.

Substituting the components of the loaded weight:

$$
\Delta_L = W_{LS} + DWT
$$

This directly rearranges to:

$$
DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}
$$

**Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem):**

*Given:*
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{BP} = 150$ m
- Breadth, $B = 22$ m
- Loaded draft, $T = 9.5$ m
- Block coefficient, $C_B = 0.72$
- Lightship weight, $W_{LS} = 6{,}000$ tonnes

*Find: Deadweight (DWT).*

**Solution Walkthrough:**

The volume of displacement at loaded draft:

$$
\nabla_L = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

$$
\nabla_L = 150 \cdot 22 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.72
$$

$$
\nabla_L = 22{,}572 \text{ m}^3
$$

The loaded displacement (mass of water displaced) in seawater:

$$
\Delta_L = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_L
$$

$$
\Delta_L = 1.025 \cdot 22{,}572
$$

$$
\Delta_L = 23{,}136.3 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Applying the Deadweight identity:

$$
DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}
$$

$$
DWT = 23{,}136.3 - 6{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{DWT \approx 17{,}136 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

---

### Derivation 2: Deadweight Coefficient $C_D$ — Justification

**Starting Concept:**
The Deadweight Coefficient expresses how much of the block-shaped envelope (L × B × D) is "filled" by useful cargo weight (rather than by volume of water).

**Step-by-Step Build-up:**

The block volume is:

$$
V_{Block} = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D
$$

The weight of seawater that *would* fill this block:

$$
W_{Block} = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{Block}
$$

The fraction of this that becomes useful DWT is empirically expressed as:

$$
C_D = \dfrac{DWT}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D}
$$

For first-iteration design, naval architects use the simplified form:

$$
C_D = \dfrac{DWT}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D}
$$

(implicitly assuming $\rho_{sw} \approx 1$ t/m³ for preliminary sizing).

**Worked Verification:**

Using our previous numerical example with $D = 12$ m (depth):

$$
C_D = \dfrac{17{,}136}{150 \cdot 22 \cdot 12}
$$

$$
C_D = \dfrac{17{,}136}{39{,}600}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_D \approx 0.433}
$$

This value (≈0.43) lies within the **typical range of 0.40–0.50** for general cargo ships — validating the design proportions.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Lightship ($W_{LS}$) | Deadweight ($DWT$) | Loaded Displacement ($\Delta_L$) |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Includes hull steel?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Includes machinery?** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Includes cargo?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Includes fuel/water?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Varies during voyage?** | ❌ Constant | ✅ Yes (consumables reduce) | ✅ Yes |
| **Used in chartering?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (primary term) | ✅ Yes (sometimes) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block Diagram — Composition of Total Loaded Displacement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Loaded Displacement DELTA_L] --> B[Lightship Weight WLS]
    A --> C[Deadweight DWT]
    B --> B1[Steel Weight]
    B --> B2[Outfit Weight]
    B --> B3[Machinery Weight]
    C --> C1[Cargo Deadweight Payload]
    C --> C2[Ship Outfittings Consumables]
    C1 --> C1a[Actual Cargo]
    C1 --> C1b[Passengers and Baggage]
    C1 --> C1c[Containers and Tare]
    C2 --> C2a[Fuel Oil FO and Diesel DO]
    C2 --> C2b[Fresh Water FW]
    C2 --> C2c[Lubricating Oil LO]
    C2 --> C2d[Ballast Water]
    C2 --> C2e[Provisions and Stores]
    C2 --> C2f[Crew and Effects]
```

### Flow Diagram — DWT Estimation Workflow in Preliminary Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Define Principal Dimensions L B D T] --> P2[Step 2: Estimate Block Coefficient CB from Ship Type]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute Displacement Volume NABLA = L times B times T times CB]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Loaded Displacement DELTA_L = rho times NABLA]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Estimate Lightship WLS using empirical formula or sister ship]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply DWT = DELTA_L minus WLS]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Validate using Deadweight Coefficient CD]
    P7 --> P8{Is CD within Typical Range for Ship Type?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Design Proportions Acceptable]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Revise Principal Dimensions or WLS Estimate]
```

### Schematic — Visualizing Deadweight vs Lightship in a Hull Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LoadedShip["LOADED SHIP at Load Waterline LWL"]
        direction TB
        L1[Lightship Region Steel + Machinery + Outfit] 
        L2[DWT Region Cargo + Fuel + Water + Stores]
        L1 --> L2
    end
    W1[WLS = Constant Hull Weight] -.is submerged below.-> L1
    W2[DWT = Variable Cargo + Consumables] -.occupies volume above lightship.-> L2
    W3[Delta_L = WLS + DWT = Total Displacement] -.equals weight of displaced water.-> LoadedShip
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:**
> The Lightship portion is the **fixed, permanent structural weight**. The Deadweight portion is the **variable, revenue-generating region** stacked on top of (and around) the lightship, occupying the hull up to the Load Waterline.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Deadweight of a vessel and list any four components included in it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Deadweight (DWT) is the total weight that a vessel can carry when fully loaded to its load waterline. It is the difference between the loaded displacement ($\Delta_L$) and the lightship weight ($W_{LS}$):

$$
DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}
$$

**Any Four Components (1 mark — ¼ each):**
1. Cargo (the goods transported).
2. Fuel oil and diesel oil (for propulsion).
3. Fresh water (for crew and domestic use).
4. Provisions, stores, and crew with their effects.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Lightship Weight and Deadweight.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$) | Deadweight ($DWT$) |
|-------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Weight of the empty vessel complete with hull, machinery, and outfit | Weight of cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew, and passengers |
| **Variability** | Constant for the vessel's life | Varies during each voyage |
| **Includes cargo?** | No | Yes |
| **Includes fuel?** | No | Yes |
| **Charter relevance** | Not used in chartering | Primary term in charter parties |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars: Length between perpendiculars $L_{BP} = 200$ m, Breadth $B = 32$ m, Loaded draft $T = 12.5$ m, Block coefficient $C_B = 0.78$, Lightship weight $W_{LS} = 12{,}000$ tonnes. Seawater density is $1.025$ t/m³.
   **(i)** Calculate the **Loaded Displacement**.
   **(ii)** Calculate the **Deadweight (DWT)**.
   **(iii)** If the molded depth $D = 18$ m, calculate the **Deadweight Coefficient $C_D$** and comment on whether it falls in the typical range for a bulk carrier.
    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **various components of Deadweight** in a merchant ship. Also, derive the relationship between displacement, lightship weight, and deadweight.
    **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) (i) Loaded Displacement:**

$$
\nabla_L = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$

$$
\nabla_L = 200 \cdot 32 \cdot 12.5 \cdot 0.78
$$

$$
\nabla_L = 62{,}400 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\Delta_L = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_L = 1.025 \cdot 62{,}400
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta_L = 63{,}960 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

**(a) (ii) Deadweight:**

$$
DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS} = 63{,}960 - 12{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{DWT = 51{,}960 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

**(a) (iii) Deadweight Coefficient and Comment:**

$$
C_D = \dfrac{DWT}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D} = \dfrac{51{,}960}{200 \cdot 32 \cdot 18}
$$

$$
C_D = \dfrac{51{,}960}{115{,}200} \approx 0.451
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The typical range of $C_D$ for bulk carriers is **0.55 – 0.70**. The computed value of 0.451 is **slightly low**, suggesting the vessel either has a high lightship weight (heavier scantlings) or underutilized cargo capacity. The design warrants scrutiny.

**(b) Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Components of Deadweight (4 marks):**

Refer to the schematic in Section 4. The major components are:

1. **Cargo Deadweight:** Goods transported (ore, grain, containers, oil, etc.), passengers and baggage (for passenger ships).
2. **Ship's Outfittings/Consumables:**
   - Fuel oil (FO) and Marine diesel oil (MDO).
   - Lubricating oil (LO).
   - Fresh water (FW).
   - Ballast water.
   - Stores and provisions.
   - Crew and their effects.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

By Archimedes' principle, the weight of water displaced equals the total loaded weight:

$$
\Delta_L = \rho \cdot \nabla_L
$$

The loaded weight of a ship consists of the hull structure (lightship) and all variable loads:

$$
\Delta_L = W_{LS} + \text{Cargo} + \text{Fuel} + \text{Water} + \text{Stores} + \text{Crew}
$$

The bracketed sum is by definition the Deadweight:

$$
\therefore \Delta_L = W_{LS} + DWT
$$

$$
\boxed{DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Define the following terms with suitable units:
   **(i)** Lightship Weight
   **(ii)** Loaded Displacement
   **(iii)** Deadweight Coefficient
    **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A general cargo ship has principal dimensions $L_{BP} = 130$ m, $B = 18$ m, $D = 11$ m. The deadweight coefficient $C_D = 0.48$ (using $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³ convention). If the lightship weight is $5{,}500$ tonnes, estimate:
   **(i)** The Deadweight.
   **(ii)** The loaded displacement.
   **(iii)** The loaded draft (take $C_B = 0.68$).
    **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Definitions (7 marks — 2⅓ marks each):**

**(i) Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$):** The weight of the complete vessel in empty condition — including hull structure (steel weight), machinery, boilers, propulsion equipment, anchors, chains, and all permanent outfit. Expressed in **tonnes (t)**. Numerically equal to the *Light Displacement* ($\Delta_{LL}$).

**(ii) Loaded Displacement ($\Delta_L$):** The total weight of the vessel when fully loaded to its design waterline. By Archimedes' principle, it equals the weight of seawater displaced at the load waterline. Expressed in **tonnes (t)**.

$$
\Delta_L = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_L
$$

**(iii) Deadweight Coefficient ($C_D$):** A dimensionless form coefficient that represents the ratio of the deadweight to the block volume (multiplied by density). It indicates how efficiently a ship's principal dimensions are utilized for cargo carrying.

$$
C_D = \dfrac{DWT}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D}
$$

---

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**(b) (i) Deadweight:**

$$
DWT = C_D \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot D
$$

$$
DWT = 0.48 \cdot 1.025 \cdot 130 \cdot 18 \cdot 11
$$

$$
DWT = 0.48 \cdot 1.025 \cdot 25{,}740
$$

$$
DWT = 12{,}663.94
$$

$$
\boxed{DWT \approx 12{,}664 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

**(b) (ii) Loaded Displacement:**

$$
\Delta_L = W_{LS} + DWT = 5{,}500 + 12{,}664
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta_L = 18{,}164 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

**(b) (iii) Loaded Draft:**

From $\Delta_L = \rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$:

$$
T = \dfrac{\Delta_L}{\rho_{sw} \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot C_B}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{18{,}164}{1.025 \cdot 130 \cdot 18 \cdot 0.68}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{18{,}164}{1{,}631.04}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 11.14 \text{ m}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit mixing:** Students frequently forget to multiply by $\rho_{sw}$ when displacement is in **tonnes** vs volume in **m³**. Always write the units explicitly.
> - **Mistaking DWT for displacement:** Charter parties and stability books use DWT *and* displacement — they are **NOT the same**. DWT excludes the hull.
> - **Forgetting $C_B$ when reversing draft calculation:** Many students confuse block volume with displaced volume. The displaced volume = Block Volume × $C_B$.
> - **Sign convention errors:** When rearranging $DWT = \Delta_L - W_{LS}$, ensure you subtract the *smaller* quantity ($W_{LS}$) from the *larger* ($\Delta_L$).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **DWT** = Total carrying weight (cargo + consumables) — **NOT** the ship's weight.
- [x] **Lightship Weight ($W_{LS}$)** = Weight of empty ship = Steel + Outfit + Machinery.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $\Delta_L = W_{LS} + DWT$.
- [x] **Standard seawater density:** $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ (use **1.000** for freshwater).
- [x] **Block Coefficient $C_B$** converts block volume to actual displaced volume.
- [x] **Deadweight Coefficient $C_D$** validates design proportions; typical ranges differ by ship type.
- [x] DWT is **variable** during a voyage (fuel/water consumed reduce it).
- [x] DWT is the **commercial earning metric** of a merchant vessel.
- [x] Cargo Deadweight + Ship's Outfittings = Total Deadweight.
- [x] **Net Cargo DWT** = DWT − (Fuel + Water + Stores + Crew weight).
