- [x] Always check that $C_D$ falls within the **typical range for the ship type** before approving design.
- [x] The **Plimsoll Line / Load Line** marks the maximum permissible draft for a given DWT and water density.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Displacement (Δ)** in marine vessel design is defined as the *actual mass of water displaced by the fully submerged volume of a ship's hull* at a given draft, expressed in metric tonnes (t). It is mathematically equal to the weight of the floating ship, in accordance with **Archimedes' Principle**, which states that any floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the total weight of the body.

$$ \Delta = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot \nabla \cdot g_{\text{local}} $$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = Displacement (in tonnes or kN, depending on convention)
- $\rho_{\text{water}}$ = Mass density of water (typically **1.025 t/m³** for seawater and **1.000 t/m³** for freshwater)
- $\nabla$ (Nabla) = Submerged volume of the hull (m³)
- $g_{\text{local}}$ = Local gravitational acceleration (≈ 9.81 m/s²)

In naval architecture, the displacement is more commonly expressed using **mass-tonnage convention**:

$$ \Delta \, (\text{in tonnes}) = \nabla \, (\text{in m}^3) \times \rho_{\text{water}} \, (\text{t/m}^3) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Displacement" in Module 1 covers not just the scalar value but also **types of displacement** (light, load, deadweight), **displacement volume (∇)**, **displacement sheets**, and **Moulded Displacement (Δ_m)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Real-World Analogy — The Bathtub Experiment:**
> Imagine pushing a small toy boat gently into a filled bathtub. As the boat sinks deeper, water overflows the edges. The volume of water that overflowed is exactly equal to the *submerged volume* of the toy boat at that depth. If you could weigh that overflowed water, it would equal the **weight of the toy boat itself**. That "weight of displaced water" is the **Displacement** of the boat. A loaded cargo ship pushes away (displaces) *thousands of tonnes* of seawater because its own weight is that massive — it has to push away exactly its own weight in water just to stay afloat!

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sea Water Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1.025 t/m³** |
| Fresh Water Density | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1.000 t/m³** |
| Density Ratio (Salt/Fresh) | $\rho_{sw}/\rho_{fw}$ | **1.025** |
| 1 Salt Water Ton (SWT) | Equivalent | **0.975 Long Tons / 1.008 Short Tons** |
| Standard Gravity | $g$ | **9.80665 m/s²** |

> [!NOTE]
> The difference between **salt water** and **fresh water** displacement is critical during a ship's transit between oceans and rivers (e.g., vessels moving from the sea into the St. Lawrence Seaway). A ship rides **higher in salt water** and **lower in fresh water** for the same weight, because salt water is denser.

---

### 1.4 Types of Displacement (KTU Syllabus Focus)

| Type | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Light Ship Displacement** | $\Delta_{\text{LSD}}$ | Weight of the empty ship — hull, machinery, outfit, but **no cargo, fuel, crew, or stores**. |
| **Load Displacement** | $\Delta_{\text{Load}}$ | Total weight of the fully loaded ship at **Load Draft (T_L)** — includes cargo, fuel, water, crew, supplies. |
| **Deadweight** | $\text{DWT}$ | $\Delta_{\text{Load}} - \Delta_{\text{LSD}}$. The *earning capacity* of the ship. |
| **Moulded Displacement** | $\Delta_m$ | Displacement calculated using **moulded lines** (the theoretical hull surface), *excluding* shell plating and appendages. |
| **Total Displacement** | $\Delta_{\text{Total}}$ | Displacement including shell plating thickness, wetted appendages (rudder, bilge keels), and propellers. |

$$ \Delta_{\text{Total}} = \Delta_m + \Delta_{\text{shell}} + \Delta_{\text{appendages}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **KTU Board Examination purposes**, when not otherwise specified, the question refers to **Moulded Displacement (Δ_m)** computed from hydrostatic curves. Always verify the wording in the question paper before solving.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles Behind Displacement

The concept of displacement rests on three pillars that govern every ship design calculation:

1. **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy** — The upward buoyant force on a floating body equals the weight of fluid displaced.
2. **Equilibrium of Forces** — A floating ship is in static equilibrium: $W_{\text{ship}} = F_{\text{buoyancy}} = \Delta \cdot g$.
3. **Conservation of Mass (Volume)** — The displaced volume is the integral of the ship's underwater cross-sectional areas from keel to waterline.

**Derivation Logic (Why this matters):**

Consider a ship floating at mean draft $T$. The pressure at any depth $h$ below the waterline is $P = \rho g h$. Integrating this pressure over the entire wetted (underwater) hull surface yields the total upward buoyant force:

$$ F_B = \int_{\text{wetted surface}} \rho g h \, dA = \rho g \nabla $$

For static floating equilibrium: $W = F_B$, hence $W = \rho g \nabla$, or in tonnes:

$$ \Delta \, (\text{tonnes}) = \nabla \, (\text{m}^3) \times \rho \, (\text{t/m}^3) $$

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU Board Exam High-Yield** — they are tested every semester in Part A (definitions) and Part B (numerical problems).

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Displacement (mass) | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes (t) | Salt water: $\rho = 1.025$ |
| 2 | Displacement (weight) | $W = \Delta \cdot g$ | kN | $g \approx 9.81$ m/s² |
| 3 | Moulded Volume | $\nabla = C_b \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$ | m³ | $C_b$ = Block Coefficient |
| 4 | Deadweight | $\text{DWT} = \Delta_{\text{Load}} - \Delta_{\text{LSD}}$ | tonnes | Earning capacity |
| 5 | Deadweight Coefficient | $C_{DW} = \text{DWT} / \Delta_{\text{Load}}$ | dimensionless | Typically **0.55–0.75** for tankers |
| 6 | Block Coefficient | $C_b = \nabla / (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | Bulk carriers: **0.78–0.85** |
| 7 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p = \nabla / (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$ | dimensionless | $A_m$ = Midship area |
| 8 | Midship Coefficient | $C_m = A_m / (B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | Typically **0.85–0.98** |
| 9 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_w = A_w / (L_{pp} \cdot B)$ | dimensionless | Affects stability & trim |
| 10 | Longitudinal Prismatic | $C_{lp} = C_b / C_m$ | dimensionless | Indicates fullness of ends |
| 11 | Displacement-Speed Relation | $P \propto \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3$ | kW | Admiralty Coefficient |
| 12 | Fresh-Water Allowance | $\text{FWA} = \nabla / (4 \cdot A_w)$ | m | Draft change FW → SW |
| 13 | Tons per Inch (TPI) | $\text{TPI} = 0.0253 \cdot A_w / 100$ | tonnes/inch | For draft changes |
| 14 | Total Δ | $\Delta_{\text{total}} = \Delta_m (1 + 0.005)$ | tonnes | Empirical ≈ +0.5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - $C_b = 1$ → Rectangular block (unrealistic, but is the theoretical maximum)
> - $C_b \to 0$ → A pure needle-shaped hull
> - $C_m = 1$ → Midship section is a rectangle
> - $C_p = 1$ → Prismatic body (constant cross-section) — typical of submarines

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Displacement governs nearly every aspect of ship operation:

- **Stability**: A higher displacement for the same dimensions means a deeper draft, lowering the metacenter relative to the center of gravity.
- **Resistance**: Resistance to motion scales with displacement (EHP depends on $\Delta$ and $V$).
- **Powering**: Admiralty Coefficient $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3 / \text{EHP}$ is used to compare vessels.
- **Loading Limits**: Dredging of canals (e.g., Panama Canal) imposes maximum displacement restrictions.
- **Port Charges**: Many ports levy charges by **Gross Tonnage (GT)**, which is a *function of* displacement and form.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Displacement Volume using Simpson's 1st Rule

**Background:** A ship's underwater volume is found by integrating cross-sectional areas from the aft perpendicular (AP) to the forward perpendicular (FP). The standard naval-architecture method uses **Simpson's 1st Rule** over an even number of equally spaced ordinates (typically 10, 20, or 40 stations).

Let $L_{pp}$ be split into **$n$** equal intervals (where $n$ is even), producing $n+1$ ordinates (stations 0, 1, 2, …, $n$).

Each station has a half-breadth $y_i$ measured from the centerline to the hull. The cross-sectional area at station $i$ is:

$$ A_i = 2 \int_0^{y_i} z(y) \, dy $$

For a typical half-breadth distribution $y(x)$ along the length, the **volume** (Nabla) is:

$$ \nabla = \int_0^{L_{pp}} A(x) \, dx $$

Applying Simpson's 1st Rule with common interval $s = L_{pp} / n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \frac{L_{pp}}{3n} \left[ A_0 + A_n + 4(A_1 + A_3 + \dots + A_{n-1}) + 2(A_2 + A_4 + \dots + A_{n-2}) \right] \\
&= \frac{L_{pp}}{3n} \left[ A_0 + A_n + 4 \sum_{\text{odd}} A_i + 2 \sum_{\text{even}} A_i \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $A_0$ and $A_n$ are end areas (often zero at AP/FP for fine ends)
- Odd-indexed areas get **multiplier 4**
- Even-indexed (interior) areas get **multiplier 2**

**Step-by-Step Solution Logic:**
1. Read half-breadths $y_0, y_1, \dots, y_n$ from the body plan.
2. At each station, compute $A_i = 2 y_i \times (\text{depth at station})$ — for the waterline cut, $A_i = 2 y_i \times T_i$.
3. Apply the Simpson's multipliers to each $A_i$.
4. Sum and multiply by $L_{pp} / (3n)$.
5. Multiply the result by $\rho$ to obtain the **mass displacement** $\Delta$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Simpson's Rule?** Marine hulls are smooth, fair curves — Simpson's Rule integrates polynomials of degree ≤ 3 exactly, which matches the curvature of the hull. The trapezoidal rule would overestimate or underestimate volume.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Form Coefficient Relationships

The four principal form coefficients are interconnected. Here is the algebraic chain:

**Step 1:** Define block coefficient:
$$ C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} $$

**Step 2:** Define prismatic coefficient:
$$ C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} $$

**Step 3:** Define midship coefficient:
$$ C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} $$

**Step 4:** Multiply $C_p$ and $C_m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p \cdot C_m &= \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} \cdot \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
&= C_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result (The Master Identity):**

$$ \boxed{\, C_b = C_p \cdot C_m = C_p \cdot C_m \,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This identity is a **guaranteed 3-mark question** in KTU Part A. Memorize the derivation — it is asked word-for-word from previous year papers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Tons Per Inch (TPI) Immersion

When a ship floats at mean draft $T$, the **waterplane area** is $A_w$. A small change in draft $dT$ causes a small change in displacement volume $d\nabla = A_w \cdot dT$.

The corresponding change in displacement (in tonnes) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d\Delta &= \rho \cdot d\nabla \\
&= \rho \cdot A_w \cdot dT
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to **tonnes per inch**, where $dT = 1 \text{ inch} = 0.0254 \text{ m}$:

$$ \text{TPI} = \rho \cdot A_w \cdot 0.0254 $$

In **salt water** ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³):

$$ \text{TPI} = 0.0254 \times 1.025 \times A_w = 0.02604 \, A_w \quad (\text{tonnes/inch}) $$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $L_{pp} = 120$ m, $B = 18$ m, $T = 6$ m
- Waterplane area $A_w = 0.85 \times L_{pp} \times B = 0.85 \times 120 \times 18 = 1836$ m²
- Sea water

Compute:
$$ \text{TPI} = 0.0254 \times 1.025 \times 1836 = 47.82 \text{ tonnes/inch} $$

**Interpretation:** Loading or unloading **47.82 tonnes** changes the ship's mean draft by exactly **1 inch**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Coefficient Ranges by Vessel Type

| Vessel Type | $C_b$ | $C_p$ | $C_m$ | Typical $\Delta$ (t) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tanker** (VLCC) | 0.83 | 0.84 | 0.99 | 200,000–320,000 |
| **Bulk Carrier** | 0.78–0.85 | 0.80 | 0.97 | 30,000–180,000 |
| **Container Ship** | 0.60–0.70 | 0.65 | 0.95 | 20,000–200,000 |
| **General Cargo** | 0.65–0.75 | 0.70 | 0.95 | 5,000–30,000 |
| **Naval Frigate** | 0.40–0.55 | 0.55 | 0.85 | 2,000–6,000 |
| **Aircraft Carrier** | 0.55–0.65 | 0.60 | 0.92 | 60,000–100,000 |
| **Sailboat / Yacht** | 0.30–0.45 | 0.50 | 0.80 | <500 |
| **Submarine (submerged)** | 0.50–0.60 | ~1.00 | 0.55 | 5,000–25,000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Displacement Computation Pipeline

The following mermaid diagram maps the **entire workflow** by which raw hull form data is converted into a usable displacement value — a process that KTU students must understand holistically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Hull Lines Plan] --> B1[Read Half-Breadths at Each Station]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Cross-Sectional Areas A sub i]
    C1 --> D1{Choose Integration Method}
    D1 -->|Simpson 1st| E1[Apply Multipliers 1, 4, 2]
    D1 -->|Trapezoidal| E2[Apply Uniform Multipliers]
    E1 --> F1[Sum and Multiply by Lpp / 3n]
    E2 --> F2[Sum and Multiply by Lpp / n]
    F1 --> G1[Submerged Volume Nabla in m3]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{Choose Water Density}
    H1 -->|Salt 1.025| I1[Mass Displacement = 1.025 Nabla]
    H1 -->|Fresh 1.000| I2[Mass Displacement = 1.000 Nabla]
    H1 -->|Custom rho| I3[Mass Displacement = rho Nabla]
    I1 --> J1[Add Shell Plate and Appendages]
    I2 --> J1
    I3 --> J1
    J1 --> K1[Final Total Displacement Delta sub Total]
    K1 --> L1[Compute Deadweight DWT]
    K1 --> L2[Compute TPI for Trim Analysis]
    K1 --> L3[Compute Block Coefficient C sub b]
    K1 --> L4[Hydrostatic Curves and Bonjean Data]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map — Displacement and Its Coefficients

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Submerged Volume Nabla] --> M1[Moulded Displacement Delta sub m]
    N1 --> M2[Block Coefficient C sub b]
    N1 --> M3[Prismatic Coefficient C sub p]
    A1[Midship Area A sub m] --> M3
    A1 --> M4[Midship Coefficient C sub m]
    B1[Waterplane Area A sub w] --> M5[Waterplane Coefficient C sub w]
    B1 --> M6[Tons per Inch TPI]
    W1[Ship Weight W] --> M1
    M2 --> REL1[C sub b equals C sub p times C sub m]
    M3 --> REL1
    M4 --> REL1
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Forces Acting on a Floating Ship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W0[Total Ship Weight W acting at Center of Gravity G] --> EQ[Static Equilibrium Condition]
    FB0[Buoyant Force F sub B acting at Center of Buoyancy B] --> EQ
    EQ -->|W equals F sub B| DISP[Mass Displacement Delta equals rho Nabla]
    W0 --> CB1[Center of Gravity G]
    FB0 --> CB2[Center of Buoyancy B]
    CB1 --> GM[Metacenter M derived from Stability]
    CB2 --> GM
    GM --> RESULT[Transverse Metacentric Height GM sub T]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams in an exam:** Mermaid diagrams render directly on KTU's LMS (Moodle-based), but in handwritten answer sheets, students should redraw these as labelled block diagrams. A clean, labelled schematic often earns 1–2 extra marks in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *Define "Displacement" of a ship. Distinguish clearly between "Light Ship Displacement" and "Load Displacement".*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Displacement of a ship is the **mass of water displaced by the submerged volume of the hull**, expressed in tonnes. By Archimedes' Principle, it is numerically equal to the **weight of the floating ship**.

$$ \Delta = \rho_{\text{water}} \cdot \nabla \quad (\text{in tonnes}) $$

**Light Ship Displacement (LSD) (1 mark):** This is the weight of the empty, fully outfitted ship with **no cargo, fuel, fresh water, lubricating oil, ballast, crew, or consumables** on board. It includes the hull structure, main and auxiliary machinery, permanent outfit, and permanent ballast.

**Load Displacement (1 mark):** This is the **total weight of the ship when fully loaded** to her **Load Line Draft (Load Draft / T_L)**. It includes the Light Ship weight plus the full Deadweight — cargo, fuel (Bunkers), fresh water, stores, crew, and effects.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *State and prove the relationship $C_b = C_p \times C_m$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The Block Coefficient of a ship is the **product of the Prismatic Coefficient and the Midship Coefficient**.

**Definitions (1 mark):**

$$ C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}, \quad C_p = \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}}, \quad C_m = \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} $$

**Proof (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p \cdot C_m &= \frac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L_{pp}} \cdot \frac{A_m}{B \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} \\
&= C_b \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Simpson's 1st Rule** for calculating the displacement volume of a ship. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{pp} = 150$ m
- Breadth $B = 22$ m
- Mean draft $T = 8.5$ m
- Block coefficient $C_b = 0.82$
- Water density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³

Calculate the **displacement**, **deadweight** if the lightship weight is 8,500 tonnes, and the **Tons Per Inch (TPI)** assuming waterplane area coefficient $C_w = 0.86$. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Simpson's 1st Rule (7 marks):**

The submerged volume $\nabla$ is calculated by integrating the cross-sectional underwater areas $A(x)$ from AP (Aft Perpendicular) to FP (Forward Perpendicular). The hull is divided into $n$ equally spaced stations (where $n$ must be **even** — e.g., 10, 20, or 40). At each station, the half-breadth $y_i$ is measured from the body plan and the area $A_i = 2 y_i \cdot T_i$ is calculated.

Simpson's 1st Rule formula:

$$ \nabla = \frac{L_{pp}}{3n} \left[ A_0 + A_n + 4(A_1 + A_3 + \dots + A_{n-1}) + 2(A_2 + A_4 + \dots + A_{n-2}) \right] $$

The standard multipliers are therefore: **1, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, …, 4, 2, 4, 1**. The end ordinates ($A_0$ at AP and $A_n$ at FP) are typically zero or very small for fine-ended ships.

**Sketch (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- Draw a longitudinal section showing 11 stations (for 10 intervals).
- Indicate the half-breadths $y_0, y_1, \dots, y_{10}$.
- Annotate the Simpson multipliers beneath each station.

**Why Simpson's Rule:** Because hull sections vary smoothly, Simpson's Rule integrates polynomial curves of degree ≤ 3 with no error. It is the standard ISO/KTU-prescribed method.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume:**

$$ \nabla = C_b \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T = 0.82 \times 150 \times 22 \times 8.5 $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= 0.82 \times 150 \times 22 \times 8.5 \\
&= 0.82 \times 28050 \\
&= 23{,}001 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass Displacement:**

$$ \Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 23{,}001 = 23{,}576.03 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 3 — Deadweight:**

$$ \text{DWT} = \Delta - \Delta_{\text{LSD}} = 23{,}576.03 - 8{,}500 = 15{,}076.03 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 4 — Waterplane Area:**

$$ A_w = C_w \cdot L_{pp} \cdot B = 0.86 \times 150 \times 22 = 2838 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Tons Per Inch (TPI):**

$$ \text{TPI} = \rho \cdot A_w \cdot 0.0254 = 1.025 \times 2838 \times 0.0254 $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TPI} &= 1.025 \times 2838 \times 0.0254 \\
&= 73.91 \text{ tonnes/inch}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\nabla = 23{,}001$ m³
- $\Delta = 23{,}576$ tonnes
- $\text{DWT} = 15{,}076$ tonnes
- $\text{TPI} = 73.91$ tonnes/inch

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Define the four principal **form coefficients** of a ship — Block, Prismatic, Midship, and Waterplane Coefficients. Explain how they influence resistance and propulsion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A naval frigate has $L_{pp} = 110$ m, $B = 14$ m, $T = 4.5$ m. Her waterplane area $A_w = 1180$ m² and her displacement is 3,800 tonnes in sea water ($\rho = 1.025$ t/m³). Calculate:
- (i) The displacement volume $\nabla$
- (ii) The Block Coefficient $C_b$
- (iii) The Tons Per Inch (TPI)
- (iv) The change in draft if the ship enters fresh water (no weight change). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Form Coefficients (7 marks):**

| Coefficient | Definition | Effect on Resistance |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Coefficient $C_b$** | $C_b = \nabla / (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$ | Higher $C_b$ → fatter hull → higher wave-making and viscous resistance. |
| **Prismatic Coefficient $C_p$** | $C_p = \nabla / (A_m \cdot L_{pp})$ | Higher $C_p$ → fuller ends → higher wave resistance. Lower $C_p$ → fine ends, lower resistance. |
| **Midship Coefficient $C_m$** | $C_m = A_m / (B \cdot T)$ | Higher $C_m$ → fuller midsection → more displacement, more viscous drag. |
| **Waterplane Coefficient $C_w$** | $C_w = A_w / (L_{pp} \cdot B)$ | Higher $C_w$ → broader waterplane → better initial stability but greater added resistance in waves. |

**Effect on Propulsion:** A higher $C_b$ requires **more brake power** to achieve a given speed because resistance scales roughly with $C_b^{1.5}$ for a given displacement. Warships, frigates, and container ships therefore have **low $C_b$** (0.40–0.65), while tankers and bulk carriers have **high $C_b$** (0.80–0.85).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume:**

$$ \nabla = \Delta / \rho = 3800 / 1.025 = 3707.32 \text{ m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Block Coefficient:**

$$
C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{3707.32}{110 \times 14 \times 4.5}
$$

$$
C_b = \frac{3707.32}{6930} = 0.535
$$

**Step 3 — Tons Per Inch (TPI):**

$$ \text{TPI} = \rho \cdot A_w \cdot 0.0254 = 1.025 \times 1180 \times 0.0254 = 30.72 \text{ tonnes/inch} $$

**Step 4 — Change in Draft (Salt → Fresh):**

In salt water, $\Delta = 1.025 \cdot \nabla_{sw}$ and in fresh water, $\Delta = 1.000 \cdot \nabla_{fw}$. Since the ship's weight is unchanged:

$$ \nabla_{fw} = 1.025 \times \nabla_{sw} = 1.025 \times 3707.32 = 3800 \text{ m}^3 $$

The extra submerged volume is:

$$ \Delta \nabla = 3800 - 3707.32 = 92.68 \text{ m}^3 $$

The change in draft is:

$$ \Delta T = \Delta \nabla / A_w = 92.68 / 1180 = 0.0785 \text{ m} \approx 7.85 \text{ cm} $$

The ship sinks **deeper in fresh water by approximately 7.85 cm (3.09 inches)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Density Confusion:** Students often mix up the formula for fresh water ($\rho = 1.000$) and salt water ($\rho = 1.025$). Always re-read the problem statement.
> 2. **Unit Errors in TPI:** The constant **0.0254** is for converting *inches to metres*. Using 0.0254 without the $A_w$ term gives incorrect units. The correct formula is `TPI = ρ × A_w × 0.0254` tonnes per inch.
> 3. **Moulded vs Total:** If the problem specifies "Moulded", do not add shell plate thickness. If unspecified in KTU papers, **assume Moulded**.
> 4. **Simpson's Multiplier Error:** End ordinates ($A_0$, $A_n$) get multiplier **1**, not 4. Many students mistakenly apply 4 throughout.
> 5. **Draft Change Direction:** Ship sinks **deeper in fresh water** and rises in salt water — not the reverse. Memorise this physically: denser liquid supports the ship better.
> 6. **Block Coefficient Range:** A $C_b > 1$ is **physically impossible**. If your answer exceeds 1, recheck the computation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: Displacement**

- **Δ = ρ × ∇** — Master formula. Density (t/m³) × Volume (m³) = Displacement (tonnes).
- **Salt water density = 1.025 t/m³**, Fresh water = **1.000 t/m³**.
- **Block Coefficient $C_b = \nabla / (L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T)$** — dimensionless, between 0.3 (sleek warship) and 0.85 (bulky tanker).
- **Prismatic Coefficient $C_p$** — measures fullness of ends; $C_p = 1$ for a uniform prism (submarine).
- **Midship Coefficient $C_m$** — measures fullness of the mid-section; $C_m \leq 1$.
- **Master Identity**: $C_b = C_p \cdot C_m$ — must be derivable in 3 minutes.
- **Deadweight = Load Displacement − Light Ship Displacement**.
- **Tons Per Inch**: $\text{TPI} = 0.0254 \cdot \rho \cdot A_w$ tonnes/inch.
- **Simpson's 1st Rule** with multipliers **1, 4, 2, 4, 2, …, 4, 1** for volume integration.
- **Fresh water → ship sinks deeper**; **Salt water → ship rises**.
- **Moulded Displacement** = theoretical ∇ from lines plan; **Total Δ** = Moulded + shell + appendages (≈ +0.5%).
- **Units consistency**: Length in metres, Breadth in metres, Draft in metres, Density in t/m³ → Δ in tonnes.
- **Displacement governs**: Stability (GM), Resistance (EHP), Powering (Admiralty Coeff.), Tonnage (GT/NT), Draft (port access).
- **Admiralty Coefficient**: $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} \cdot V^3 / \text{EHP}$ — for comparing similar vessels.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadweight Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deadweight Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Deadweight Coefficient (C<sub>D</sub>)** is a fundamental dimensional (or non-dimensional, depending on convention) form coefficient used in naval architecture that quantifies the proportion of the ship's displaced volume occupied by its *carrying capacity* — i.e., the cargo, fuel, freshwater, stores, crew, and passengers that the vessel is designed to transport.

In its most rigorous KTU-board accepted form:

> **Deadweight Coefficient (C<sub>D</sub>) = Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) / Volume of Displacement (∇)**

where the volume of displacement is measured in cubic metres (m³) and DWT is measured in tonnes (t).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note:** C<sub>D</sub> is essentially the *weight-based analogue* of the **Block Coefficient (C<sub>B</sub>)**. While C<sub>B</sub> tells you how *full* the underwater hull shape is geometrically, C<sub>D</sub> tells you how *heavy* the carrying capacity is relative to that underwater volume.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **large rectangular water tank** of fixed volume. Now fill it with two things:

1. **The ship's own steel skeleton, machinery, and outfit** (lightweight).
2. **The actual cargo the ship earns money from** (deadweight — oil, containers, grain, cars).

The **Deadweight Coefficient** simply asks: *"Out of every cubic metre of water this ship pushes aside, how many tonnes are actually paying cargo / variable load?"*

- A **high C<sub>D</sub>** (e.g., 0.75) → the ship is a *heavy hauler* — like a giant oil tanker. Most of its underwater volume is "earning" its keep.
- A **low C<sub>D</sub>** (e.g., 0.30) → the ship has a *fat empty hull* relative to its cargo — typical of fast naval frigates or research vessels where volume goes into buoyancy, speed, and stability, not cargo tonnage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant:** Density of seawater **ρ<sub>sw</sub> = 1.025 t/m³** ; density of freshwater **ρ<sub>fw</sub> = 1.000 t/m³**. These directly govern the relationship between weight and volume.

---

### 1.3 Relationship to Other Principal Coefficients

| Coefficient | Symbol | Formula | What it measures |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Coefficient | C<sub>B</sub> | ∇ / (L × B × T) | Hull fullness (geometric) |
| Weight (Displacement) Coefficient | C<sub>W</sub> | Δ / ∇ | Mass per unit displaced volume (= ρ) |
| **Deadweight Coefficient** | **C<sub>D</sub>** | **DWT / ∇** | **Cargo/variable load per unit volume** |
| Lightweight Coefficient | C<sub>L</sub> | LWT / ∇ | Steel structure per unit volume |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of C<sub>D</sub>

**Step 1 — Start with Archimedes' Principle.**
A floating ship displaces a volume of water whose weight equals the ship's total weight (Δ, the displacement):

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

In marine engineering tonnes (where 1 t ≈ 1000 kgf):

$$\Delta_{\text{(tonnes)}} = \rho_{\text{(t/m}^3\text{)}} \cdot \nabla_{\text{(m}^3\text{)}}$$

**Step 2 — Partition the displacement into two halves.**
Every ship can be split, by weight, into:

$$\Delta = \text{LWT} + \text{DWT}$$

where:
- **LWT (Lightweight)** = hull steel, machinery, outfit, permanent ballast — everything that *does not change* between voyages.
- **DWT (Deadweight)** = cargo, fuel (Bunker), freshwater, lube oil, stores, crew, passengers — everything that *changes* voyage to voyage.

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by ∇ (displaced volume).**

$$\frac{\Delta}{\nabla} = \frac{\text{LWT}}{\nabla} + \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$$

$$\boxed{C_W = C_L + C_D}$$

This is the **master relationship** every KTU student must memorise.

**Step 4 — Recognise that C<sub>W</sub> = ρ.**
Because Δ = ρ·∇, dividing both sides by ∇ gives C<sub>W</sub> = ρ. Therefore:

$$\rho = C_L + C_D$$

In seawater:

$$1.025 = C_L + C_D$$

This single equation lets you find any one coefficient if the other two are known — a favourite **3-mark KTU question**.

**Step 5 — Express C<sub>D</sub> in terms of Block Coefficient.**

Since ∇ = L · B · T · C<sub>B</sub>:

$$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}$$

**Step 6 — Practical interpretation.**
Higher C<sub>B</sub> with the same DWT → lower C<sub>D</sub> (more volume for the same cargo).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every equation you need for Module 1 KTU board questions on Deadweight Coefficient.**

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_D = \dfrac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$ | Definition (t / m³) |
| 2 | $C_W = C_L + C_D$ | Master partitioning identity |
| 3 | $C_W = \rho$ | Weight coefficient equals water density (t/m³) |
| 4 | $\rho = C_L + C_D$ | Seawater → 1.025 = C<sub>L</sub> + C<sub>D</sub> |
| 5 | $C_D = \dfrac{\text{DWT}}{L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}$ | In terms of principal dimensions |
| 6 | $\Delta = \text{LWT} + \text{DWT}$ | Displacement partitioning (tonnes) |
| 7 | $\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho}$ | Volume from displacement |
| 8 | $C_D = C_W - C_L$ | Useful rearrangement |

### 2.3 Typical Range of C<sub>D</sub> for Real Ships (KTU Data Bank)

| Vessel Type | Typical C<sub>D</sub> | Typical C<sub>B</sub> |
|---|---|---|
| VLCC / Suezmax tanker | **0.70 – 0.85** | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| Panamax bulk carrier | **0.65 – 0.78** | 0.75 – 0.82 |
| Handysize bulker | **0.60 – 0.72** | 0.72 – 0.80 |
| Container ship (feeder) | **0.45 – 0.60** | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| Container ship (ULCV) | **0.55 – 0.70** | 0.65 – 0.72 |
| General cargo ship | **0.45 – 0.60** | 0.55 – 0.68 |
| Naval frigate / Corvette | **0.20 – 0.35** | 0.45 – 0.55 |
| Research / Survey vessel | **0.15 – 0.30** | 0.40 – 0.55 |
| Pure car carrier (PCC) | **0.30 – 0.45** | 0.55 – 0.70 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Preliminary Design Selection:** Naval architects pick C<sub>D</sub> first when sketching a vessel, then reverse-engineer dimensions.
2. **Deadweight–Draft Curves:** C<sub>D</sub> governs how the draft changes as cargo is loaded.
3. **Fleet Optimisation:** Shipping companies benchmark vessels by C<sub>D</sub> to assess earning efficiency per cubic metre of steel.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** IMO EEDI/EEXI calculations use C<sub>D</sub> indirectly through C<sub>B</sub> and DWT.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Finding C<sub>D</sub> Directly

**Given:**
- A bulk carrier has displacement Δ = 35,000 tonnes.
- Lightweight LWT = 7,500 tonnes.
- The vessel floats in seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³).

**Find:** The Deadweight Coefficient C<sub>D</sub>.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find DWT.**
The deadweight is the *variable load* portion of displacement:

$$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{DWT} = 35{,}000 - 7{,}500 = 27{,}500 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2 — Find the displaced volume ∇.**
From Archimedes' principle (in marine tonnes):

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho}$$

$$\nabla = \frac{35{,}000}{1.025}$$

$$\nabla = 34{,}146.34 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the C<sub>D</sub> definition.**

$$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$$

$$C_D = \frac{27{,}500}{34{,}146.34}$$

$$\boxed{C_D = 0.8054}$$

> **Interpretation:** For every cubic metre of seawater this bulk carrier displaces, **0.8054 tonnes** is paying cargo. This is well within the typical Panamax bulker range (0.65 – 0.78, here slightly higher — consistent with a fully loaded condition).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Using the Master Identity

**Given:**
- A product tanker has lightweight coefficient C<sub>L</sub> = 0.250.
- The vessel is in seawater.

**Find:** C<sub>D</sub> and the lightweight-to-deadweight ratio.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the master identity.**

$$C_W = C_L + C_D$$

In seawater, C<sub>W</sub> = ρ = 1.025:

$$1.025 = 0.250 + C_D$$

**Step 2 — Solve.**

$$C_D = 1.025 - 0.250$$

$$\boxed{C_D = 0.775}$$

**Step 3 — Find the LWT : DWT ratio.**

Since both LWT and DWT share the same denominator ∇, the ratio equals:

$$\frac{\text{LWT}}{\text{DWT}} = \frac{C_L}{C_D} = \frac{0.250}{0.775} = 0.3226$$

So **LWT ≈ 32.3 % of DWT** — typical for a mid-sized product tanker.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — C<sub>D</sub> from Block Coefficient

**Given:**
- Container ship with L = 200 m, B = 32 m, T = 12 m, C<sub>B</sub> = 0.65.
- DWT = 45,000 tonnes, ρ = 1.025 t/m³.

**Find:** Verify the data consistency by computing C<sub>D</sub> two ways.

**Solution:**

**Method A — Direct from displacement.**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12 \times 0.65$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 49{,}920 = 51{,}168 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\text{LWT} = \Delta - \text{DWT} = 51{,}168 - 45{,}000 = 6{,}168 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\nabla = \frac{\Delta}{\rho} = \frac{51{,}168}{1.025} = 49{,}920 \text{ m}^3$$

$$C_D = \frac{45{,}000}{49{,}920} = 0.9014$$

> **Sanity check:** A C<sub>D</sub> of 0.90 is *very high* for a container ship (typical 0.45–0.70). This indicates the data set is inconsistent — most likely DWT was over-specified relative to the hull volume. KTU board examiners love this type of *consistency check* question.

**Method B — Using the compact form.**

$$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B} = \frac{45{,}000}{49{,}920} = 0.9014$$

Both methods yield identical results — confirming the algebraic identity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Formula | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Displacement volume ∇ | ∇ = Δ / ρ | Δ, ρ | m³ |
| Deadweight DWT | DWT = Δ − LWT | Δ, LWT | tonnes |
| Block coefficient C<sub>B</sub> | C<sub>B</sub> = ∇ / (LBT) | L, B, T, ∇ | — |
| Weight coefficient C<sub>W</sub> | C<sub>W</sub> = Δ / ∇ | Δ, ∇ | = ρ (t/m³) |
| Deadweight coefficient C<sub>D</sub> | C<sub>D</sub> = DWT / ∇ | DWT, ∇ | t/m³ |
| Lightweight coefficient C<sub>L</sub> | C<sub>L</sub> = LWT / ∇ | LWT, ∇ | t/m³ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Displacement Partitioning — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the total displacement (Δ) of a vessel is partitioned, and how the three coefficients — C<sub>W</sub>, C<sub>L</sub>, and C<sub>D</sub> — relate to each other through the common denominator ∇.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOTAL DISPLACEMENT DELTA in tonnes] --> B[Divided by displaced volume NABLA in cubic metres]
    B --> C{Weight Coefficient C sub W equals NABLA into DELTA}
    C --> D[Lightweight LWT]
    C --> E[Deadweight DWT]
    D --> F[Lightweight Coefficient C sub L equals NABLA into LWT]
    E --> G[Deadweight Coefficient C sub D equals NABLA into DWT]
    F --> H[Master Identity C sub W equals C sub L plus C sub D]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Numerical Anchor In seawater one point zero two five equals C sub L plus C sub D]
    I --> J[Typical Split Cargo Tanker around zero point two five plus zero point seven seven equals one point zero two five]
    I --> K[Typical Split Container Ship around zero point four five plus zero point five seven equals one point zero two five]
    I --> L[Typical Split Naval Frigate around zero point seven five plus zero point two seven equals one point zero two five]
```

> **Reading guide:** Every arrow ending at the Master Identity node confirms that **C<sub>W</sub> = C<sub>L</sub> + C<sub>D</sub>** regardless of vessel type; only the *split* between C<sub>L</sub> and C<sub>D</sub> changes by ship category.

### 4.2 Coefficient Dependency Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Principal Dimensions L B T] --> V1[Displaced Volume NABLA]
    BC1[Block Coefficient C sub B] --> V1
    V1 --> CD1[Deadweight Coefficient C sub D]
    DWT1[Deadweight Tonnage] --> CD1
    RHO1[Water density rho] --> CW1[Weight Coefficient C sub W]
    V1 --> CW1
    DEL1[Displacement DELTA] --> CW1
    CW1 --> ML1[Master Law C sub W equals C sub L plus C sub D]
    CL1[Lightweight Coefficient C sub L] --> ML1
    CD1 --> ML1
```

### 4.3 Vessel-Type vs C<sub>D</sub> Spectrum (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Very Low C sub D below zero point three] --> S1L[Naval Frigates, Research Vessels]
    S2[Low C sub D zero point three to zero point five] --> S2L[Pure Car Carriers, Small Fast Ferries]
    S3[Moderate C sub D zero point five to zero point six five] --> S3L[Container Ships, General Cargo]
    S4[High C sub D zero point six five to zero point eight] --> S4L[Bulk Carriers, Product Tankers]
    S5[Very High C sub D above zero point eight] --> S5L[VLCC Crude Oil Tankers, Ore Carriers]
```

> **Take-away:** As you slide along this spectrum, the ship transitions from **speed/versatility-optimised** (left) to **cargo-mass-optimised** (right).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the Deadweight Coefficient. State its typical range for a crude oil tanker.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Deadweight Coefficient (C<sub>D</sub>) is defined as the ratio of the Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) of a vessel to its displaced volume ∇:
>
> $$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$$
>
> For a VLCC / crude oil tanker, the typical range of C<sub>D</sub> is **0.70 to 0.85** (in seawater, with ρ = 1.025 t/m³). This high value reflects the design intent of these vessels: maximising cargo-carrying mass per cubic metre of underwater hull volume.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for definition with formula; 1 mark for unit/displacement-volume explanation; 1 mark for the typical numerical range with ship-type justification.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State and explain the master identity relating weight, lightweight, and deadweight coefficients.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The master identity is:
>
> $$C_W = C_L + C_D$$
>
> **Explanation:**
> - The displacement Δ of a ship can be split into Lightweight (LWT) and Deadweight (DWT): Δ = LWT + DWT.
> - Dividing the entire equation by the displaced volume ∇ yields three coefficients whose sum equals C<sub>W</sub> = Δ / ∇ = ρ.
> - In seawater, ρ = 1.025 t/m³, so **C<sub>L</sub> + C<sub>D</sub> = 1.025**.
>
> This identity is fundamental because it allows the naval architect to determine any one of the three coefficients if the other two are known, using only Archimedes' principle and a known water density.

*(Mark split: 1 mark for the formula; 1 mark for derivation sketch; 1 mark for the seawater numerical anchor and physical interpretation.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** (i.e., answer **either** Question A **or** Question B). Sub-parts typically split (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the master identity $C_W = C_L + C_D$ starting from Archimedes' principle. State clearly the physical meaning of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A Panamax bulk carrier of length 225 m, breadth 32.2 m, and design draft 14 m has a block coefficient C<sub>B</sub> = 0.78. Its deadweight carrying capacity is 75,000 tonnes in seawater (ρ = 1.025 t/m³).

   (i) Calculate the displacement volume ∇.
   (ii) Calculate the displacement Δ and lightweight LWT.
   (iii) Determine C<sub>D</sub>, C<sub>L</sub>, and verify the master identity. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Archimedes' principle states that for a floating body, weight equals weight of displaced fluid:

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 2:** Partition Δ into two physical components:

$$\Delta = \text{LWT} + \text{DWT}$$

where LWT = hull + machinery + permanent outfit (constant), and DWT = cargo + fuel + stores + crew + passengers (variable).

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by ∇:

$$\frac{\Delta}{\nabla} = \frac{\text{LWT}}{\nabla} + \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$$

**Step 4:** Recognise the three coefficients:

$$C_W = C_L + C_D$$

where:
- **C<sub>W</sub>** (Weight Coefficient) = Δ / ∇ — total mass per unit displaced volume, numerically equal to ρ (in t/m³).
- **C<sub>L</sub>** (Lightweight Coefficient) = LWT / ∇ — mass of structure per unit volume.
- **C<sub>D</sub>** (Deadweight Coefficient) = DWT / ∇ — mass of earning cargo per unit volume.

**Step 5 (numerical anchor):** In seawater, ρ = 1.025, therefore:

$$C_L + C_D = 1.025$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**(i) Displacement volume ∇:**

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$

$$\nabla = 225 \times 32.2 \times 14 \times 0.78$$

$$\nabla = 79{,}235.4 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Displacement Δ and Lightweight LWT:**

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 79{,}235.4$$

$$\Delta = 81{,}216.3 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\text{LWT} = \Delta - \text{DWT} = 81{,}216.3 - 75{,}000 = 6{,}216.3 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) The three coefficients:**

$$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla} = \frac{75{,}000}{79{,}235.4} = 0.9466$$

$$C_L = \frac{\text{LWT}}{\nabla} = \frac{6{,}216.3}{79{,}235.4} = 0.0785$$

**Verification of master identity:**

$$C_L + C_D = 0.0785 + 0.9466 = 1.0251 \approx 1.025 \; (= \rho_{\text{sw}}) \quad \checkmark$$

> **Interpretation:** The C<sub>D</sub> of 0.9466 is exceptionally high — even above the upper bound of typical VLCC tankers. This indicates the Panamax bulker's principal dimensions and block coefficient have been specified at fully loaded (design draft) condition where DWT dominates the displacement. For preliminary design, KTU expects students to recognise when computed coefficients lie *outside* the typical range and to comment on the physical reason.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the aid of a sketch, explain the components of displacement (Δ) of a ship and show how the Deadweight Coefficient C<sub>D</sub> is obtained from them. Discuss how C<sub>D</sub> varies between an oil tanker, a container ship, and a naval frigate, giving typical numerical ranges. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A general cargo vessel has the following particulars:
   - Length between perpendiculars L<sub>pp</sub> = 140 m
   - Breadth B = 22 m
   - Mean draft T = 8.5 m
   - Block coefficient C<sub>B</sub> = 0.65
   - Lightweight = 4,200 tonnes

   Compute:
   (i) Displacement volume ∇ and displacement Δ in seawater.
   (ii) Deadweight DWT and C<sub>D</sub>.
   (iii) C<sub>L</sub> and verify the identity C<sub>L</sub> + C<sub>D</sub> = ρ. **[7 marks]**

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation [7 marks]:**

**Sketch description (textual fallback):**

```
       <-------------------- DELTA (Total Displacement) -------------------->
       |                                                                      |
       |  <----- LWT ----->  <--------------------- DWT --------------------->|
       |  Hull steel        |  Cargo       | Fuel  | FW | Stores | Crew/Pass |
       |  Machinery         |              | Bunker|     |        |           |
       |  Outfit            |              |       |     |        |           |
       |  Perm. Ballast     |              |       |     |        |           |
       |____________________|______________|_______|_____|________|___________|
       |                    |                                                      
       |  C_L               |  C_D                                                  
       |  ________________  |  ________________                                      
       |  LWT / NABLA       |  DWT / NABLA                                          
```

(For the answer script, the student should draw a clear rectangular block divided into LWT and DWT sub-blocks, label each, and write the coefficient formula below each region.)

**Variation of C<sub>D</sub> across vessel types:**

| Vessel Type | Typical C<sub>D</sub> | Design Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Crude oil tanker (VLCC) | 0.70 – 0.85 | Maximise cargo tonnage per voyage |
| Container ship | 0.45 – 0.65 | Maximise TEU capacity + speed |
| Naval frigate | 0.20 – 0.35 | Maximise speed, stealth, stability |

**Physical reasoning:** The Deadweight Coefficient C<sub>D</sub> reflects the *economic purpose* of the vessel. Tankers carry dense bulk liquids → high C<sub>D</sub>. Containers carry relatively low-density cargo occupying much volume → lower C<sub>D</sub>. Warships carry little cargo but lots of weapons, electronics, and fuel for high-speed operation → very low C<sub>D</sub>.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**(i) Displacement volume and displacement:**

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 140 \times 22 \times 8.5 \times 0.65$$

$$\nabla = 17{,}017 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 17{,}017 = 17{,}442.4 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(ii) Deadweight and Deadweight Coefficient:**

$$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT} = 17{,}442.4 - 4{,}200 = 13{,}242.4 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$C_D = \frac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla} = \frac{13{,}242.4}{17{,}017} = 0.7781$$

**(iii) Lightweight Coefficient and identity check:**

$$C_L = \frac{\text{LWT}}{\nabla} = \frac{4{,}200}{17{,}017} = 0.2468$$

**Verification:**

$$C_L + C_D = 0.2468 + 0.7781 = 1.0249 \approx 1.025 \; (= \rho_{\text{sw}}) \quad \checkmark$$

> **Interpretation:** C<sub>D</sub> ≈ 0.78 — at the upper end of typical general-cargo range (0.50–0.65) and into bulker territory. This suggests the vessel may be operating at near-maximum load, or alternatively the principal dimensions given describe a hull that is geometrically relatively small for its cargo mass.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state C<sub>D</sub> is dimensionless when expressed in t/m³ (since ρ is t/m³ and Δ = ρ·∇ in marine tonnes). Failing to clarify units = −½ mark.
> 2. **Mixing up Δ with DWT:** Δ is *total* displacement; DWT is the *variable* part. Conflating them is the #1 mistake. Always show the partitioning step.
> 3. **Using freshwater density by default:** If the problem does not specify seawater, **assume ρ = 1.025 t/m³** unless told otherwise. Using 1.000 leads to ~2.5% error and full-mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** KTU examiners award a free mark for explicitly verifying $C_L + C_D = \rho$ at the end of numerical answers — never omit it.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the typical range:** A numerical answer without a brief comment on whether the result is realistic = −1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $C_D = \dfrac{\text{DWT}}{\nabla}$ — ratio of deadweight tonnage to displaced volume.
- **Master identity (memorise verbatim):** $C_W = C_L + C_D$ where $C_W = \rho$.
- **Numerical anchor in seawater:** $C_L + C_D = 1.025$ t/m³.
- **Partitioning:** $\Delta = \text{LWT} + \text{DWT}$ — always partition before computing.
- **Alternative form:** $C_D = \dfrac{\text{DWT}}{L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}$.
- **Typical C<sub>D</sub> ranges:**
  - Tankers → 0.70 – 0.85 (highest)
  - Bulk carriers → 0.65 – 0.78
  - Container ships → 0.45 – 0.65
  - General cargo → 0.50 – 0.65
  - Naval frigates → 0.20 – 0.35 (lowest)
- **Verification step:** Always finish numerical problems by checking that $C_L + C_D \approx \rho$.
- **Sanity check:** If your computed C<sub>D</sub> lies outside the typical range for the stated ship type, **comment on it** — KTU rewards critical engineering judgement.
- **Block Coefficient Link:** Higher $C_B$ with the same DWT ⇒ lower $C_D$ (the hull "absorbs" more volume without adding cargo).
- **Practical application:** C<sub>D</sub> governs deadweight–draft curves, fleet earning efficiency, and is an input to IMO EEDI/EEXI compliance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hold Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hold Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In naval architecture, **Hold Capacity** refers to the volumetric and gravimetric cargo-carrying capability of a ship's enclosed cargo spaces, bounded by the inner-shell plating, the underside of hatch covers, and the top of the inner-bottom tank-top plating. It is the **primary design constraint** that governs the commercial earning potential of a merchant vessel.

The discipline distinguishes between three principal measures:

| Term | Rigorous Definition | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Grain Capacity (Vg)** | Total enclosed volume of the cargo hold, measured to the **inside of the shell plating**, **inside of hatch coamings**, and the **top of hatch covers**. Includes all structural voids taken up by frames, brackets, and stringers. | Free-flowing bulk cargoes (grain, ore, coal) |
| **Bale Capacity (Vb)** | Volume available for the stowage of **baled, packaged, or palletised** cargoes. Obtained by **deducting** the volume of frames, brackets, stiffeners, and stringers from Vg. | Bagged grain, cartons, machinery, steel coils |
| **Deadweight Cargo Capacity (DWc)** | The **gravimetric** cargo the ship can carry at the design draft. Equal to total deadweight minus fuel, lubricating oil, fresh water, stores, and crew effects. | All cargoes (final commercial limit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Sea Water Density** used universally in KTU problems and naval architecture practice is **ρ = 1.025 t/m³** (also written as **64.0 lb/ft³**). Fresh water is **ρ = 1.000 t/m³**.

> [!NOTE]
> The ICLL (International Convention on Load Lines) and most classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) require that the **grain capacity** be certified when the ship is to carry grain in bulk, because free-flowing grain can shift into voids and create dangerous stability losses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Building Blocks" View)

Imagine a high-rise apartment building with **identical rectangular rooms**, each fitted with internal pillars and beams (which take up some floor area). The **Grain Capacity** is the total *cuboidal volume* of every room measured wall-to-wall and floor-to-ceiling — it counts even the small spaces behind the pillars. The **Bale Capacity** is the *useful floor area* on each level that a tenant could actually furnish — the pillars' footprint is excluded. The **Deadweight Cargo** is the total weight the elevator and structural columns can support *after* reserving some load for the building's own services (water tanks, AC, fire systems, and people).

In a ship, the rooms are the **cargo holds**, the pillars are the **frames and brackets**, the elevator capacity is the **displacement minus lightweight**, and the building services are the **fuel, water, and stores**.

## 1.3 Why Hold Capacity is the Design Driver

- It directly determines the **freight-earning tonnage** and hence the **return-on-investment** for the shipowner.
- It couples **three independent design axes**: principal dimensions (L, B, T), hull form (block coefficient Cb), and structural scantlings.
- It is the **yardstick** against which the designer's choice of length, beam, and depth is validated during preliminary design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Coupled Capacities — Step-by-Step Logic

A ship is, in commercial terms, a **floating warehouse**. Its capacity is therefore constrained by *two* ceilings that must be evaluated together:

1. **Volume ceiling (Hull geometry)** — how many cubic metres can be packed inside the hold?
2. **Weight ceiling (Buoyancy)** — how many tonnes can the ship carry before it sinks below its Plimsoll line?

The **actual cargo carried** is the *smaller* of these two ceilings. This is the central dogma of cargo-capacity calculations and the most heavily tested concept in KTU Module 1.

### Step 1 — Geometric (Volume) Pathway

The hold is essentially a rectangular box integrated over the ship's length. The total enclosed volume of all cargo holds is:

$$\nabla_{holds} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_{h,i} \cdot B_{h,i} \cdot H_{h,i}$$

Where:
- $L_{h,i}, B_{h,i}, H_{h,i}$ are the length, breadth, and height (measured to inside of shell plating) of the *i-th* hold.
- $n$ is the number of holds.
- This gives the **Grain Capacity** ($V_g$).

### Step 2 — Structural Deduction (Bale Capacity)

The **obstruction factor** ($f_{obst}$) is the volumetric fraction of the hold consumed by frames, brackets, stringers, and girders. Empirically:

$$f_{obst} \in \left[0.05, 0.15\right]$$

Hence:

$$V_b = V_g \cdot \left(1 - f_{obst}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Buoyancy (Weight) Pathway

Starting from Archimedes' principle:

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

Then subtracting the empty ship's own weight (lightweight, LW):

$$DW = \Delta - LW$$

Then subtracting the *constants* (fuel, lube oil, fresh water, stores, crew):

$$DW_c = DW - \text{Constants}$$

Where **Constants** = $F + LO + FW + Stores + Crew$.

### Step 4 — The Stowage Factor Bridge

The **Stowage Factor (SF)** is the bridge that links weight to volume for any specific cargo:

$$SF = \frac{V_{cargo}}{W_{cargo}} \quad \left[ \frac{m^3}{t} \right]$$

Two reciprocal quantities are also useful:

$$\text{Cargo Density } \rho_c = \frac{1}{SF} \quad \left[ \frac{t}{m^3} \right]$$

### Step 5 — The "Limiting Factor" Decision

| Cargo Character | Stowage Factor | Limiting Ceiling |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heavy (iron ore, steel, sand) | $SF < 0.6 \, m^3/t$ | **Weight ceiling** governs |
| Medium (grain, bagged cement) | $0.6 \le SF \le 1.5 \, m^3/t$ | Either may govern — must check |
| Light (wool, cotton, hay, machinery on cradles) | $SF > 1.5 \, m^3/t$ | **Volume ceiling** governs |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
| :- | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_b$ | $C_b = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | — | 0.55 – 0.85 |
| 2 | Volumetric Displacement | $\nabla$ | $\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b$ | $m^3$ | — |
| 3 | Mass Displacement | $\Delta$ | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | tonnes | 1,000 – 400,000 |
| 4 | Deadweight | $DW$ | $DW = \Delta - LW$ | tonnes | — |
| 5 | Cargo Deadweight | $DW_c$ | $DW_c = DW - (F + LO + FW + S + C)$ | tonnes | — |
| 6 | Stowage Factor | $SF$ | $SF = V_{cargo} \,/\, W_{cargo}$ | $m^3/t$ | 0.3 – 4.0 |
| 7 | Cargo Density | $\rho_c$ | $\rho_c = 1 \,/\, SF$ | $t/m^3$ | 0.25 – 3.3 |
| 8 | Grain Capacity | $V_g$ | $V_g = \sum L_h B_h H_g$ | $m^3$ | — |
| 9 | Bale Capacity | $V_b$ | $V_b = V_g (1 - f_{obst})$ | $m^3$ | — |
| 10 | Volume Required | $V_{req}$ | $V_{req} = W_{cargo} \cdot SF$ | $m^3$ | — |
| 11 | Holds Required | $n_{req}$ | $n_{req} = V_{req} \,/\, V_{b,h}$ | — | — |
| 12 | Max Cargo (Volume) | $W_{V}$ | $W_{V} = V_b \,/\, SF$ | tonnes | — |
| 13 | Max Cargo (Weight) | $W_{\Delta}$ | $W_{\Delta} = DW_c$ | tonnes | — |
| 14 | Actual Cargo | $W_{act}$ | $W_{act} = \min(W_V, W_{\Delta})$ | tonnes | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorise Equation 14.** It is the single most-tested formula in KTU Module 1. Every cargo-capacity problem ends with this "minimum of the two ceilings" rule.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

The hold-capacity concept drives every phase of a ship's commercial life:

- **Conceptual design** — used to size principal dimensions from a target deadweight.
- **Contract specification** — owners and shipyards negotiate on grain and bale capacity before signing the building contract.
- **Voyage planning** — the charterer selects cargoes whose SF is compatible with the **TPC vs Hold** trade-off.
- **Stability software** — programs like *NAPA* and *Maxsurf* compute Vg and Vb directly from the GA model.
- **Port-State inspection** — overloaded grain shipments are checked against the **grain stability booklet**, which is itself derived from Vg.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Cargo Carrying Capacity from Principal Dimensions

This is the canonical derivation that appears in nearly every KTU Part B paper. We derive the maximum cargo weight for a vessel carrying a cargo of known stowage factor.

**Given** a ship of length $L$, breadth $B$, design draft $T$, block coefficient $C_b$, lightweight $LW$, and constants $K = F + LO + FW + S + C$. The ship is to carry a cargo of stowage factor $SF$ in $n$ holds, each of bale capacity $V_{b,h}$.

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume (from hull geometry)**

The underwater volume of the hull, by definition of the block coefficient, is the rectangular parallelepiped $L \cdot B \cdot T$ scaled by the fullness ratio $C_b$:

$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b$$

**Step 2 — Total Displacement (Archimedes' Principle)**

The displaced water weighs:

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

Substituting $\nabla$:

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b$$

Substitute $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$:

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b \quad [\text{tonnes}]$$

**Step 3 — Total Deadweight**

Deadweight is everything *above* the lightweight that the ship can carry at the design draft:

$$DW = \Delta - LW$$

**Step 4 — Cargo Deadweight (Weight Ceiling)**

After reserving $K$ for consumables and crew, the remaining tonnage is available for paying cargo:

$$DW_c = DW - K = \Delta - LW - K$$

**Step 5 — Total Bale Capacity (Volume Ceiling)**

With $n$ holds of equal bale capacity:

$$V_b^{total} = n \cdot V_{b,h}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Cargo by Volume**

For a cargo of stowage factor $SF$, every tonne occupies $SF \, m^3$ of hold:

$$W_V = \frac{V_b^{total}}{SF}$$

**Step 7 — The Governing Equation**

The vessel can carry at most the **smaller** of the two ceilings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{act} &= \min \left( W_V, \, DW_c \right) \\
&= \min \left( \frac{n \cdot V_{b,h}}{SF}, \; \Delta - LW - K \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

If $W_V < DW_c$, the vessel is **volume-limited** (light cargo in heavy hull). If $W_V > DW_c$, the vessel is **weight-limited** (heavy cargo in voluminous holds).

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Number of Holds Required for a Given Cargo

**Given** a required cargo weight $W_{req}$ of stowage factor $SF$, and each hold has grain capacity $V_{g,h}$ with obstruction factor $f_{obst}$.

**Step 1 — Bale Capacity of One Hold**

$$V_{b,h} = V_{g,h} \cdot (1 - f_{obst})$$

**Step 2 — Total Volume Required**

$$V_{req} = W_{req} \cdot SF$$

**Step 3 — Number of Holds**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{req} &= \frac{V_{req}}{V_{b,h}} \\
&= \frac{W_{req} \cdot SF}{V_{g,h} \cdot (1 - f_{obst})}
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is always **rounded up** to the next whole integer, because a partial hold cannot be built.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Master-Style)

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
$L = 150 \, m$, $B = 25 \, m$, $T = 9.5 \, m$, $C_b = 0.78$, $LW = 5{,}200 \, t$, $K = 1{,}350 \, t$, $n = 5$ holds, $V_{g,h} = 4{,}300 \, m^3$, $f_{obst} = 0.08$, cargo $SF = 1.25 \, m^3/t$.

**Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Displacement Volume:**

$$\nabla = 150 \cdot 25 \cdot 9.5 \cdot 0.78 = 27{,}787.5 \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Total Displacement:**

$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot 27{,}787.5 = 28{,}482.19 \, t$$

**Step 3 — Cargo Deadweight (Weight Ceiling):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DW_c &= \Delta - LW - K \\
&= 28{,}482.19 - 5{,}200 - 1{,}350 \\
&= 21{,}932.19 \, t \quad \approx 21{,}932 \, t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Bale Capacity (Volume Ceiling):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_b^{total} &= n \cdot V_{g,h} \cdot (1 - f_{obst}) \\
&= 5 \cdot 4{,}300 \cdot (1 - 0.08) \\
&= 5 \cdot 4{,}300 \cdot 0.92 \\
&= 19{,}780 \, m^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Max Cargo by Volume:**

$$W_V = \frac{19{,}780}{1.25} = 15{,}824 \, t$$

**Step 6 — Governing Comparison:**

$$W_V = 15{,}824 \, t \quad < \quad DW_c = 21{,}932 \, t$$

Hence the vessel is **volume-limited**, and the actual cargo carried is:

$$\boxed{W_{act} = 15{,}824 \, t}$$

The cargo weight ceiling is **6,108 tonnes higher** than the volume ceiling, so the vessel sails with substantial reserve buoyancy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity Table

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $DW_c$ | Effect on $W_V$ | Net Effect on $W_{act}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Length $L$ | Increases | Increases (more holds fit) | Increases (until volume-rich cargo) |
| Beam $B$ | Increases | Increases | Increases |
| Draft $T$ | Increases | No direct change | Increases until cargo is light |
| Block coeff. $C_b$ | Increases | No direct change | Increases |
| Lightweight $LW$ | **Decreases** | No change | Decreases |
| Stowage factor $SF$ | No change | **Decreases** | Decreases for volume-limited ships |
| Obstruction factor $f_{obst}$ | No change | **Decreases** | Decreases for volume-limited ships |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hold Capacity Computation Flow

The diagram below maps the **dual-pathway** calculation structure (weight path on the left, volume path on the right) and shows where they converge at the **feasibility decision node**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dimA[Ship Principal Dimensions L B T] --> geomB[Hull Geometry]
    geomB --> coefC[Block Coefficient Cb]
    coefC --> volD[Volumetric Displacement]
    volD --> dispE[Total Displacement Delta]
    dispE --> dwF[Deadweight DW]
    dwF --> dwcG[Cargo Deadweight DWc]

    gaH[General Arrangement GA] --> numI[Number of Holds n]
    gaH --> dimJ[Hold Dimensions Lh Bh Hg]
    dimJ --> grainK[Grain Capacity per Hold]
    numI --> grainK
    grainK --> grainL[Total Grain Capacity]
    dimJ --> obstM[Volume of Obstructions]
    grainK --> baleN[Bale Capacity per Hold]
    obstM --> baleN
    numI --> baleN
    baleN --> baleO[Total Bale Capacity]

    cargoP[Cargo Stowage Factor SF] --> volR[Required Volume per Tonne]
    baleO --> shipS[Weight Allowed by Volume WV]
    volR --> shipS
    dwcG --> shipT[Weight Allowed by Buoyancy DWc]
    shipS --> checkU[Feasibility Decision Node]
    shipT --> checkU
    checkU --> outV[Actual Cargo Wact minimum of WV DWc]
    checkU --> flagW[Governing Constraint Identified]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Capacity Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    capA[Hold Capacity Master Concept] --> pathB[Volumetric Path]
    capA --> pathC[Gravimetric Path]
    pathB --> grainD[Grain Capacity Vg]
    pathB --> baleE[Bale Capacity Vb]
    grainD --> baleE
    pathC --> dispF[Total Displacement Delta]
    dispF --> dwG[Deadweight DW]
    dwG --> dwcH[Cargo Deadweight DWc]
```

## 4.3 Physical Cross-Section of a Cargo Hold (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hatchA[Hatch Cover Top Reference] --> coamB[Hatch Coaming]
    coamB --> shellC[Inner Shell Plating]
    shellC --> frameD[Frames and Brackets consume Vg minus Vb]
    frameD --> tankE[Inner Bottom Tank Top]
    tankE --> innerF[Dedicated Volume for Cargo]
    innerF --> utilG[Net Usable Bale Volume]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** the outer rectangle (between hatch cover, shell, and tank top) defines the **Grain Capacity**. The internal obstructions (frames and brackets) carve out the inner shaded region that distinguishes **Bale Capacity** from Grain Capacity. This is exactly how naval architects visualise the difference between the two on a lines plan or GA drawing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define the terms **grain capacity** and **bale capacity** of a ship's cargo hold. State the relationship between them with a typical numerical factor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Grain Capacity (Vg):** The total enclosed volume of the cargo hold measured to the **inside of the shell plating**, **inside of the hatch coamings**, and the **top of hatch covers**. It includes the volume occupied by frames, brackets, stringers, and other internal structural members. *(1 mark)*
- **Bale Capacity (Vb):** The volume of the cargo space available for stowage of baled, packaged, or palletised cargo, obtained by **deducting** the volume of frames, brackets, stiffeners, and stringers from the grain capacity. *(1 mark)*
- **Relationship:** $V_b = V_g \cdot (1 - f_{obst})$, where $f_{obst}$ is the obstruction factor, typically between **0.05 and 0.15**. Hence bale capacity is usually **85–95 %** of grain capacity. *(1 mark)*

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

What is **stowage factor**? Explain how it is used in determining the cargo-carrying capacity of a vessel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Stowage factor (SF) is the volume of cargo space occupied by **one unit weight** of a particular commodity, expressed in $m^3/t$ (or $ft^3/long \, ton$). Mathematically, $SF = V/W$. *(1 mark)*
- **Use in cargo calculation:** For any given cargo, the **hold volume required** is $V_{req} = W_{cargo} \cdot SF$. *(1 mark)*
- **Capacity decision:** The vessel's maximum cargo is the smaller of (i) **$DW_c$** (the buoyancy ceiling) and (ii) **$V_b / SF$** (the volume ceiling). This comparison determines whether the vessel is **weight-limited** or **volume-limited** for that cargo. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question B1A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

A bulk carrier has the following particulars:

- Length between perpendiculars, $L = 150 \, m$
- Breadth, $B = 25 \, m$
- Design draft, $T = 10 \, m$
- Block coefficient, $C_b = 0.78$
- Lightweight, $LW = 4{,}500 \, t$
- Fuel + Lube oil + Fresh water + Stores + Crew, $K = 1{,}300 \, t$
- Number of holds, $n = 5$
- Grain capacity per hold, $V_{g,h} = 4{,}200 \, m^3$
- Obstruction factor per hold, $f_{obst} = 0.07$

The vessel is to carry **coal** with a stowage factor of $SF = 1.20 \, m^3/t$.

**(a)** Calculate the **deadweight cargo capacity** of the vessel at the design draft. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **maximum weight of coal** the vessel can carry and state whether it is **weight-limited or volume-limited**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B1A

**(a) — Cargo Deadweight (Weight Ceiling):**

**Step 1: Volumetric displacement** *(1 mark)*
$$\nabla = L \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_b = 150 \cdot 25 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.78 = 29{,}250 \, m^3$$

**Step 2: Total displacement** *(1 mark)*
$$\Delta = 1.025 \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \cdot 29{,}250 = 29{,}981.25 \, t$$

**Step 3: Total deadweight** *(1 mark)*
$$DW = \Delta - LW = 29{,}981.25 - 4{,}500 = 25{,}481.25 \, t$$

**Step 4: Cargo deadweight** *(1 mark)*
$$DW_c = DW - K = 25{,}481.25 - 1{,}300 = 24{,}181.25 \, t$$

**Step 5: Rounding** *(1 mark)*
$$\boxed{DW_c \approx 24{,}181 \, t}$$

*(Remaining 2 marks: correct unit handling and step-wise presentation.)*

**(b) — Maximum Coal Carried:**

**Step 1: Bale capacity per hold** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{b,h} = V_{g,h} \cdot (1 - f_{obst}) = 4{,}200 \cdot (1 - 0.07) = 4{,}200 \cdot 0.93 = 3{,}906 \, m^3$$

**Step 2: Total bale capacity of all holds** *(1 mark)*
$$V_b^{total} = n \cdot V_{b,h} = 5 \cdot 3{,}906 = 19{,}530 \, m^3$$

**Step 3: Max cargo by volume ceiling** *(1 mark)*
$$W_V = \frac{V_b^{total}}{SF} = \frac{19{,}530}{1.20} = 16{,}275 \, t$$

**Step 4: Compare with weight ceiling** *(1 mark)*
$$W_V = 16{,}275 \, t \quad < \quad DW_c = 24{,}181 \, t$$

**Step 5: Conclusion** *(1 mark)*

Since $W_V < DW_c$, the vessel is **volume-limited**.

$$\boxed{W_{act} = 16{,}275 \, t \text{ of coal}}$$

*(Remaining 2 marks for correct interpretation of governing constraint and final statement.)*

---

### Question B1B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks)

A general cargo vessel has **4 holds** with the following particulars:

- Length per hold, $L_h = 22 \, m$
- Breadth per hold, $B_h = 18 \, m$
- Grain height per hold, $H_g = 13 \, m$
- Obstruction factor, $f_{obst} = 0.08$
- Total displacement at design draft, $\Delta = 32{,}000 \, t$
- Lightweight + constants, $LW + K = 6{,}200 \, t$
- Cargo to be carried: **bagged sugar** with $SF = 1.55 \, m^3/t$

**(a)** Calculate the **total grain capacity** and **total bale capacity** of the vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine whether the vessel is **weight-limited** or **volume-limited** for this cargo, and state the **maximum cargo the vessel can carry**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B1B

**(a) — Grain and Bale Capacities:**

**Step 1: Grain capacity per hold** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{g,h} = L_h \cdot B_h \cdot H_g = 22 \cdot 18 \cdot 13 = 5{,}148 \, m^3$$

**Step 2: Total grain capacity** *(1 mark)*
$$V_g^{total} = 4 \cdot 5{,}148 = 20{,}592 \, m^3 \quad \boxed{}$$

**Step 3: Bale capacity per hold** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{b,h} = V_{g,h} \cdot (1 - f_{obst}) = 5{,}148 \cdot (1 - 0.08) = 5{,}148 \cdot 0.92 = 4{,}737.36 \, m^3$$

**Step 4: Total bale capacity** *(1 mark)*
$$V_b^{total} = 4 \cdot 4{,}737.36 = 18{,}949.44 \, m^3 \quad \boxed{\approx 18{,}949 \, m^3}$$

*(Remaining 3 marks for correct sequencing, units, and clear presentation.)*

**(b) — Limiting Factor Decision:**

**Step 1: Cargo deadweight (weight ceiling)** *(1 mark)*
$$DW_c = \Delta - (LW + K) = 32{,}000 - 6{,}200 = 25{,}800 \, t$$

**Step 2: Max cargo by volume** *(1 mark)*
$$W_V = \frac{V_b^{total}}{SF} = \frac{18{,}949.44}{1.55} = 12{,}225.45 \, t$$

**Step 3: Compare ceilings** *(1 mark)*
$$W_V = 12{,}225 \, t \quad \ll \quad DW_c = 25{,}800 \, t$$

**Step 4: Conclusion on constraint** *(1 mark)*

Since $W_V < DW_c$, the vessel is **volume-limited** for bagged sugar. The volume ceiling is binding.

**Step 5: Actual cargo carried** *(1 mark)*
$$\boxed{W_{act} = 12{,}225 \, t \text{ of bagged sugar}}$$

*(Remaining 2 marks for correct reasoning, units and final statement of governing constraint.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the two ceilings.** Marks are heavily docked when students write the final answer as $DW_c$ without ever computing the volume ceiling. *Always compute both and take the minimum.*
> 2. **Wrong density.** Using **1.000 t/m³** (fresh water) instead of **1.025 t/m³** (sea water) costs full marks on displacement.
> 3. **Forgetting the obstruction deduction.** Many students compute $V_g$ but then use it as if it were $V_b$ for bagged cargo. This inflates the volume ceiling and gives a wrongly large cargo weight.
> 4. **Rounding prematurely.** Round only the *final* answer. Intermediate steps should retain at least 2–3 decimal places, or errors propagate.
> 5. **Skipping the comparison statement.** Even if the arithmetic is right, you lose marks if you do not explicitly state *"the vessel is volume-limited"* or *"weight-limited"*. This is a mandatory closing step.
> 6. **Treating $n_{req}$ as non-integer.** In Derivation B, the number of holds must be rounded *up* to the next whole number — a partial hold cannot be constructed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hold Capacity** has two interpretations: **volumetric** ($V_g, V_b$) and **gravimetric** ($DW_c$). Both must be checked.
- **Standard seawater density** is **1.025 t/m³** — used in all displacement calculations unless told otherwise.
- **Grain Capacity** includes frames and brackets; **Bale Capacity** excludes them: $V_b = V_g (1 - f_{obst})$.
- **Obstruction factor** $f_{obst}$ typically ranges from **0.05 to 0.15** depending on ship type and structural design.
- **Stowage Factor (SF)** is the bridge between weight and volume: $V_{req} = W_{cargo} \cdot SF$.
- **Master formula** for actual cargo: $W_{act} = \min(W_V, \, DW_c)$ — *the smaller of the two ceilings always wins*.
- **Heavy cargo** (low SF) makes the ship **weight-limited**; **light cargo** (high SF) makes it **volume-limited**.
- **Block coefficient** $C_b$ governs displacement; it does **not** directly appear in hold-capacity equations (holds are computed from GA dimensions, not from $C_b$).
- **Number of holds** required is always rounded *up* — you cannot build a fractional hold.
- **Grain Stability Booklet** is mandatory for grain carriers; it relies directly on $V_g$.
- **Cross-checks:** $W_V < DW_c \Rightarrow$ volume-limited; $W_V > DW_c \Rightarrow$ weight-limited; $W_V = DW_c \Rightarrow$ optimal design (both ceilings reached simultaneously).
- **Units consistency:** Keep all lengths in metres ($m$), areas in $m^2$, volumes in $m^3$, weights in **tonnes** ($t$). Never mix $ft$ and $m$ in the same problem.
- **KTU-favourite values:** $C_b = 0.78$ (bulk carriers), $f_{obst} = 0.07$ (general cargo), $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \, t/m^3$ (sea water).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stowage Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stowage Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stowage Factor (SF):** The volume occupied by a unit mass (one **metric ton** or one **long ton**) of cargo when packed and stowed in a ship's hold. It is formally defined as the ratio of the **stowed volume** of a cargo to its **mass**.

Mathematically:

$$SF = \frac{V}{M}$$

where:
* $V$ = Total stowed volume of cargo (m³ or ft³)
* $M$ = Mass of cargo (MT or long ton)

**Standard Units used in Marine Practice:**
* **Imperial (Traditional):** cubic feet per long ton (**ft³/ton**)
* **Metric (Modern):** cubic meters per metric ton (**m³/MT**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In modern ship chartering and the Shipping Gazette, the **m³/MT** unit is the global standard. However, traditional British / Lloyd's publications still use **ft³/ton**. Students must master conversion in both directions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are packing two boxes for a shipment:
1. **Box A** is filled with **iron rods** — extremely heavy, but compact. A single ton of iron takes up very little space.
2. **Box B** is filled with **wool bales** — feather-light, but bulky. A single ton of wool requires a huge box.

Even though both boxes carry **one ton** of cargo, the *space* they consume is vastly different. This "space-per-ton" number is exactly the **Stowage Factor**. It tells the ship's officer, *before loading*, whether the ship will run out of **weight** first or **space** first.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** *Heavy cargo = Small SF. Light cargo = Large SF.* Iron (heavy) has an SF of ~14 ft³/ton. Wool (light) has an SF of ~125 ft³/ton.

### 1.3 The 40 ft³/ton Threshold — The Critical Divide

The magic number that separates the two worlds of shipping is **40 ft³ per long ton** (≈ **1.13 m³/MT**).

| Cargo Type | Stowage Factor Range | Limiting Factor |
|------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Deadweight Cargo** | SF < 40 ft³/ton | Ship's Deadweight Capacity |
| **Measurement Cargo** | SF > 40 ft³/ton | Ship's Volume Capacity |
| **Borderline** | SF ≈ 40 ft³/ton | Both limits reached simultaneously |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind the Numbers

The Stowage Factor is the central parameter that links the **two competing limitations** of every merchant ship:

1. **Weight Limit (Deadweight Tonnage, DWT):** The structural strength of the hull limits how many tons the ship can carry. The ship sinks too deep if exceeded.
2. **Volume Limit (Cubic Capacity):** The internal volume of the holds is physically fixed. The holds "fill up" before the ship sinks.

A ship operator MUST calculate which limit will be hit **first** for a given cargo — and the Stowage Factor is the tool that gives the answer.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Classification Logic

**Step 1 —** Calculate SF of the proposed cargo.
**Step 2 —** Compare SF with the threshold of **40 ft³/ton** (or **1.13 m³/MT**).
**Step 3 —** Apply the rule:
* **If SF < 40 ft³/ton (Heavy Cargo):** A full hold weighs more than the DWT limit. The ship is **sunk to its Plimsoll line** before the holds are full. Loading stops at the DWT mark.
* **If SF > 40 ft³/ton (Light Cargo):** A full hold has unused weight capacity. The ship still has **buoyancy reserve** when the holds are brim-full. Loading stops at the hatch covers.
**Step 4 —** Compute the **Cargo Intake** — the actual tonnage that can be loaded.

$$Cargo \, Intake = Min \left( DWT, \frac{Cubic \, Capacity}{SF} \right)$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $SF = \dfrac{V}{M}$ | Fundamental definition of Stowage Factor | m³/MT or ft³/ton |
| 2 | $SF = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | SF is the reciprocal of bulk density | m³/MT when $\rho$ is in MT/m³ |
| 3 | $V = M \times SF$ | Volume required to stow mass $M$ | m³ or ft³ |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{V}{SF}$ | Mass that can fill a hold of volume $V$ | MT or ton |
| 5 | $Intake = Min\left(DWT, \dfrac{Vol_{hold}}{SF}\right)$ | Actual cargo loaded on board | MT |
| 6 | $SF_{broken} = SF_{solid} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{BS\%}{100}\right)$ | Stowage factor with broken stowage | ft³/ton |
| 7 | $1 \, \dfrac{m^3}{MT} \approx 35.315 \, \dfrac{ft^3}{ton}$ | Metric to Imperial conversion | — |
| 8 | $1 \, \dfrac{ft^3}{ton} \approx 0.0283 \, \dfrac{m^3}{MT}$ | Imperial to Metric conversion | — |
