> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** SF is the cornerstone of the **Charter Party Agreement** (e.g., GENCON form). Freight may be paid on **weight** or on **measurement** basis, and the SF of the cargo decides which freight clause applies — thus directly influencing the **commercial revenue** of the voyage.

### 2.4 Real-World Marine Applications

* **Stowage Planning:** Chief Officer uses SF to plan the loading sequence and ballasting operations.
* **Ship Stability Calculations:** Combined with KG and KG curves, SF determines the Final GM.
* **Charter Party Negotiations:** "Deadweight" vs "Measurement" cargo clauses.
* **Ship Design:** Naval Architects design the hull form (length-to-beam ratio) partly based on the **average stowage factor** the vessel is expected to carry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Stowage Factor and Bulk Density

> **Objective:** Prove that Stowage Factor is the mathematical reciprocal of bulk density.

**Given:**
* $M$ = Mass of cargo (MT)
* $V$ = Stowed volume of cargo (m³)
* $\rho$ = Bulk density of the cargo (MT/m³)

**Proof:**

By definition of **bulk density** (mass per unit volume):

$$\rho = \frac{M}{V}$$

By definition of **stowage factor** (volume per unit mass):

$$SF = \frac{V}{M}$$

Taking the reciprocal of density:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{V}{M} = SF$$

Therefore, in the metric system:

$$SF = \frac{1}{\rho}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** If a cargo is **twice as dense** as another, its SF is **half**. This inverse proportionality is the key intuition that solves almost every KTU numerical.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unit Conversion Between m³/MT and ft³/ton

> **Objective:** Establish the conversion factor between metric and imperial SF units.

**Conversion Constants:**
* 1 m³ = 35.315 ft³
* 1 long ton = 1.01605 MT

**Conversion from m³/MT to ft³/ton:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SF_{ft^3/ton} &= SF_{m^3/MT} \times \frac{1 \, m^3}{35.315 \, ft^3} \times \frac{1.01605 \, MT}{1 \, long \, ton} \\
&= SF_{m^3/MT} \times \frac{1.01605}{35.315} \\
&= SF_{m^3/MT} \times 0.02877
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $1 \, m^3/MT \approx 35.315 \, ft^3/ton$ (using MT = long ton approximation), or more precisely, $1 \, m^3/MT \approx 34.76 \, ft^3/ton$.

**Conversion from ft³/ton to m³/MT:**

$$\begin{aligned}
SF_{m^3/MT} &= SF_{ft^3/ton} \times \frac{35.315 \, ft^3}{1 \, m^3} \times \frac{1 \, long \, ton}{1.01605 \, MT} \\
&= SF_{ft^3/ton} \times 34.76
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** For board examination answers, students may use the simplified standard **1 m³/MT = 35.315 ft³/ton** with the assumption of equal mass bases (since long ton ≈ MT for 1-figure decimals).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cargo Intake Determination (Worked Numerical)

> **Problem:** A bulk carrier has a deadweight capacity of **25,000 MT** and a total cargo hold volume of **28,000 m³**. Calculate the maximum cargo intake if the cargo has:
> * **(a) Iron Ore** with SF = **0.40 m³/MT**
> * **(b) Bagged Rice** with SF = **1.50 m³/MT**
> * **(c) Cotton Bales** with SF = **2.50 m³/MT**

**Step 1 — Formula Selection**

The cargo intake is bounded by **two limits**:

$$Intake = Min \left( DWT, \frac{Cubic \, Capacity}{SF} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Volume-Limited Intake**

$$\begin{aligned}
Vol\text{-}limited \, Intake &= \frac{28,000 \, m^3}{SF} \\
\text{For (a) Iron Ore: } & \frac{28,000}{0.40} = 70,000 \, MT \\
\text{For (b) Bagged Rice: } & \frac{28,000}{1.50} = 18,667 \, MT \\
\text{For (c) Cotton Bales: } & \frac{28,000}{2.50} = 11,200 \, MT
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with DWT (25,000 MT) and Pick the Minimum**

| Cargo | Vol-Limited Intake (MT) | DWT (MT) | Limiting Factor | **Actual Intake (MT)** |
|-------|------------------------|----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| (a) Iron Ore (SF = 0.40) | 70,000 | 25,000 | DWT (weight) | **25,000** |
| (b) Bagged Rice (SF = 1.50) | 18,667 | 25,000 | Volume | **18,667** |
| (c) Cotton Bales (SF = 2.50) | 11,200 | 25,000 | Volume | **11,200** |

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

* For Iron Ore: the holds are only **50%** full, but the ship is at **maximum draft**. This is a **classic deadweight cargo** — the ship looks "empty" but cannot take more.
* For Bagged Rice and Cotton: the holds are **brim-full** but the ship has spare DWT — these are **measurement cargoes**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Broken Stowage Correction

> **Problem:** A bagged cargo has a solid SF of **50 ft³/ton**. When stowed in the hold, the empty spaces between bags account for **15%** of the stowed volume. Calculate the **effective SF** as observed in the hold.

**Solution:**

The "Broken Stowage Percentage" is defined as:

$$BS\% = \frac{Vol_{spaces}}{Vol_{cargo \, blocks}} \times 100$$

When bags are stowed, every ton of cargo now occupies more volume (the cargo PLUS the gaps). The effective SF becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
SF_{effective} &= SF_{solid} \times \left(1 + \frac{BS\%}{100}\right) \\
&= 50 \times \left(1 + \frac{15}{100}\right) \\
&= 50 \times 1.15 \\
&= 57.5 \, ft^3/ton
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Broken stowage **always increases** the effective stowage factor, meaning the ship can carry **fewer tons** of bagged cargo than its holds would suggest.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stowage Factor Decision Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cargo Identified at Port]:::inputNode --> B[Measure Density rho or Look up SF Table]
    B --> C{Compare SF with 40 ft per ton threshold}
    C -->|SF less than 40 ft per ton| D[DEADWEIGHT CARGO Heavy Cargo]:::heavyNode
    C -->|SF greater than 40 ft per ton| E[MEASUREMENT CARGO Light Cargo]:::lightNode
    C -->|SF equal to 40 ft per ton| F[INTERMEDIATE CARGO]:::midNode
    D --> G[Compute Volume Limited Intake Vol by SF]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Compare Volume Intake with DWT}
    H -->|Vol Intake less than DWT| I[STOP at Hatch Cover FULL Hold]
    H -->|Vol Intake greater than DWT| J[STOP at Plimsoll Mark FULL Draft]
    I --> K[Revenue Charged on MEASUREMENT Basis]
    J --> L[Revenue Charged on DEADWEIGHT Basis]
    
    classDef inputNode fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef heavyNode fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#660000
    classDef lightNode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px,color:#003300
    classDef midNode fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
```

### 4.2 Cargo Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SF[STOWAGE FACTOR SF equals V over M]:::coreNode
    DWT[DEADWEIGHT CARGO SF less than 40 ft per ton]:::heavyNode
    MSR[MEASUREMENT CARGO SF greater than 40 ft per ton]:::lightNode
    
    SF --> EX1[Examples Iron Ore 14 Coal Bunker 32]:::example
    SF --> EX2[Examples Wool Bales 125 Cotton 80]:::example
    
    DWT --> LIM1[Limiting Factor DEADWEIGHT Tonnage Hull Strength]:::limit
    MSR --> LIM2[Limiting Factor CUBIC CAPACITY Hold Volume]:::limit
    
    LIM1 --> RES1[Result Ship is at MAXIMUM DRAFT holds are PARTIALLY EMPTY]:::result
    LIM2 --> RES2[Result Ship has SPARE DRAFT holds are BRIM FULL]:::result
    
    classDef coreNode fill:#003366,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef heavyNode fill:#cc0000,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef lightNode fill:#009900,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef example fill:#fffacd,stroke:#888,stroke-width:1px,color:#333
    classDef limit fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#660099,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
```

### 4.3 Visual Concept: Hold Fill State vs. Cargo Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SHIP_A["SHIP A LOADED WITH IRON ORE DEADWEIGHT CARGO"]
        A1[Hatch Cover]:::cover
        A2[EMPTY SPACE 50 percent of hold is air]:::air
        A3[IRON ORE Dense and compact]:::iron
        A4[Waterline at MAXIMUM DRAFT]:::waterline
    end
    
    subgraph SHIP_B["SHIP B LOADED WITH WOOL BALES MEASUREMENT CARGO"]
        B1[Hatch Cover]:::cover
        B2[WOOL BALES Filling 100 percent of hold]:::wool
        B3[Waterline with SPARE DRAFT Remaining]:::waterlineB
    end
    
    classDef cover fill:#666,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef air fill:#ffffff,stroke:#999,color:#666
    classDef iron fill:#4d4d4d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef waterline fill:#0066cc,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef waterlineB fill:#3399ff,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef wool fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#663300
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** Define Stowage Factor. State its standard units. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Stowage Factor (SF)** is defined as the volume occupied by one unit mass of cargo when it is stowed in the ship's hold. It is the ratio of the stowed volume of a cargo to its mass.
>
> **Formula:** $SF = \dfrac{V}{M}$
>
> **Standard Units:**
> * **Imperial:** cubic feet per long ton (ft³/ton)
> * **Metric:** cubic meters per metric ton (m³/MT)
>
> **Significance:** SF determines whether a ship will be loaded to its weight limit (deadweight) or its volume limit (cubic capacity) first. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** Distinguish between **Deadweight Cargo** and **Measurement Cargo** with two examples of each. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Deadweight Cargo | Measurement Cargo |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|
| Stowage Factor | Less than **40 ft³/ton** | Greater than **40 ft³/ton** |
| Limiting Capacity | Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) | Cubic (Volume) Capacity |
| Hold Fill State | Partially empty at max draft | Brim full at spare draft |
| Examples | Iron Ore, Steel Coils, Sand | Wool Bales, Cotton, Tobacco |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> * **Common Mistake 1:** Conflating "Volume" with "Cubic Capacity" — they are NOT the same. Cubic capacity is the *empty hold volume*, while Volume in SF is the *cargo volume*.
> * **Common Mistake 2:** Using the wrong conversion factor. Always state which unit (metric or imperial) the final answer is in.
> * **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting the **40 ft³/ton** threshold in classification problems — this is a guaranteed 1-mark cut if missed.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Stowage Factor in Vessel Operations*

**[a] (7 Marks)** A bulk carrier has a deadweight capacity of **18,000 MT** and a total grain capacity of **24,000 m³**. The vessel is to load **Bagged Sugar** with a stowage factor of **1.30 m³/MT**. Calculate:
* (i) The total volume required to stow 18,000 MT of bagged sugar.
* (ii) Whether the ship will be weight-limited or volume-limited.
* (iii) The actual cargo intake in MT.

**[b] (7 Marks)** Explain the role of Stowage Factor in:
* (i) Charter party agreements
* (ii) Ship stability calculations
* (iii) Broken stowage determination

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Volume required for full DWT:**

$$V_{required} = M \times SF = 18,000 \, MT \times 1.30 \, m^3/MT = 23,400 \, m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compare with available grain capacity (24,000 m³):**

$$V_{required} = 23,400 \, m^3 \quad \text{vs.} \quad V_{available} = 24,000 \, m^3$$

Since $V_{required} < V_{available}$, the ship can hold the full DWT **before** running out of volume.

**Step 3 — Classify the limiting factor:**

* Required volume (23,400 m³) is LESS than available volume (24,000 m³) → **Weight-Limited (DWT Limited)**
* However, since SF = 1.30 m³/MT > 1.13 m³/MT, technically this is a **Measurement Cargo** — but the cargo intake equals the DWT in this case (close to the 40 ft³/ton boundary).

**Step 4 — Actual Cargo Intake:**

$$Intake = Min \left( 18,000, \frac{24,000}{1.30} \right) = Min(18,000, 18,462) = \mathbf{18,000 \, MT}$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel loads **18,000 MT** of bagged sugar (a borderline case where DWT is the deciding factor by a small margin).

*(7 marks — Awarded as: Step 1: 2 marks, Step 2: 2 marks, Step 3: 1 mark, Step 4: 1 mark, Final answer: 1 mark)*

---

**Part (b): Model Answer Outline**

* **(i) Charter Party (3 marks):** In GENCON charter parties, freight can be charged on a *weight* or *measurement* basis. If the cargo has high SF (measurement cargo), shipowners prefer "freight per ton" rates to recover volume costs. If low SF (deadweight cargo), charterers prefer weight-based freight. SF thus dictates the commercial clauses.
* **(ii) Ship Stability (2 marks):** SF determines the **vertical center of gravity (KG)** of the cargo. Light, voluminous cargo stacked high raises KG and may cause **stability loss**. SF is used in calculating GM and the ship's final stability condition.
* **(iii) Broken Stowage (2 marks):** For bagged, baled, or palletized cargo, the empty spaces between packages increase the **effective SF** in the hold. The relationship is $SF_{effective} = SF_{solid}(1 + BS\%/100)$. A higher broken stowage means fewer tons can be loaded.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Density-SF Relationship and Broken Stowage*

**[a] (7 Marks)** Derive the relationship between Stowage Factor and the bulk density of a cargo. A bulk cargo has a density of **1.8 MT/m³**. Calculate its stowage factor in:
* (i) m³/MT
* (ii) ft³/ton (use 1 m³/MT = 35.315 ft³/ton)

**[b] (7 Marks)** What is **Broken Stowage**? A homogeneous bagged cargo has a solid stowage factor of **48 ft³/ton**. If the broken stowage percentage is **12%**, determine:
* (i) The effective stowage factor in the hold.
* (ii) The total volume required to stow **5,000 MT** of this bagged cargo.
* (iii) The volume occupied purely by the empty spaces between bags.

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**Part (a):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{M}{V} \quad \text{(Density definition)} \\
SF &= \frac{V}{M} \quad \text{(Stowage Factor definition)} \\
\therefore \quad SF &= \frac{1}{\rho}
\end{aligned}$$

**(i) Calculation in m³/MT (2 marks):**

$$SF = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{1.8 \, MT/m^3} = 0.5556 \, m^3/MT$$

**(ii) Conversion to ft³/ton (2 marks):**

$$SF = 0.5556 \times 35.315 = 19.62 \, ft^3/ton$$

---

**Part (b):**

**(i) Broken Stowage Definition (1 mark):** Broken stowage is the percentage of void space between individual packages (bags, drums, crates) when stowed in a ship's hold, expressed as a percentage of the total volume occupied by the cargo blocks.

**(ii) Effective Stowage Factor (2 marks):**

$$SF_{effective} = SF_{solid} \times \left(1 + \frac{BS\%}{100}\right) = 48 \times 1.12 = 53.76 \, ft^3/ton$$

**(iii) Total Volume for 5,000 MT (2 marks):**

First convert cargo mass to long tons (or use direct metric conversion):
$$5,000 \, MT = 5,000 \times 1.01605 = 5,080.25 \, long \, tons$$

Total volume required:
$$V = M_{tons} \times SF_{effective} = 5,080.25 \times 53.76 = 273,114 \, ft^3$$

**Alternative (metric):** $SF_{effective} = 53.76 \times 0.0283 = 1.521 \, m^3/MT$
$$V = 5,000 \times 1.521 = 7,606 \, m^3$$

**(iv) Volume of Empty Spaces (2 marks):**

$$V_{spaces} = V_{total} - V_{solid \, cargo}$$
$$V_{solid} = 5,080.25 \times 48 = 243,852 \, ft^3$$
$$V_{spaces} = 273,114 - 243,852 = 29,262 \, ft^3$$

**Verification:** $\dfrac{V_{spaces}}{V_{solid}} = \dfrac{29,262}{243,852} = 12\%$ ✓ (matches the given broken stowage).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Stowage Factor**

* ✅ **Core Formula:** $SF = \dfrac{V}{M}$ (volume per unit mass)
* ✅ **Inverse of Density:** $SF = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ in metric units (m³/MT)
* ✅ **Threshold Value:** **40 ft³/ton** (or **1.13 m³/MT**) — the magic divide
* ✅ **Below 40 ft³/ton → Deadweight Cargo** (e.g., Iron Ore, Steel, Copper Concentrate)
* ✅ **Above 40 ft³/ton → Measurement Cargo** (e.g., Wool, Cotton, Tobacco, Bagged Goods)
* ✅ **Cargo Intake Formula:** $Intake = Min\left(DWT, \dfrac{Cubic \, Capacity}{SF}\right)$
* ✅ **Unit Conversion:** $1 \, m^3/MT = 35.315 \, ft^3/ton$ (approximate, KTU standard)
* ✅ **Broken Stowage:** $SF_{effective} = SF_{solid} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{BS\%}{100}\right)$
* ✅ **Charter Party Use:** Determines whether freight is charged on **weight** or **measurement** basis
* ✅ **Stability Use:** Higher SF = cargo stacked high = higher KG = reduced GM
* ✅ **Limiting Factor Logic:** Low SF → weight limit reached first; High SF → volume limit reached first
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weight Equation and Volume Equation and Solution. Basic Design Procedure for Main Categories of Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weight Equation and Volume Equation and Solution. Basic Design Procedure for Main Categories of Ships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Two Fundamental Equations of Ship Design

In the conceptual and preliminary design of any marine vessel, the naval architect must simultaneously satisfy **two governing equations** that mathematically constrain the size of the ship. These are known as the **Weight Equation** and the **Volume Equation**.

#### 1.1.1 Weight Equation (Displacement Equation)

The **Weight Equation** is based on Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy and states that the weight of water displaced by a floating vessel must equal the total weight of the vessel and its contents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weight Equation:** $\Delta = W_{L} + W_{D}$
>
> Where:
> - $\Delta$ = Total Displacement (tonnes, **t**)
> - $W_L$ = Lightweight (weight of empty ship structure, machinery, outfit)
> - $W_D$ = Deadweight (cargo, fuel, freshwater, crew, stores, provisions)

#### 1.1.2 Volume Equation (Geometric Equation)

The **Volume Equation** relates the displacement volume of the ship to its principal hull dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Equation:** $\nabla = L_{pp} \times B \times T \times C_B$
>
> Where:
> - $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement ($m^3$)
> - $L_{pp}$ = Length between perpendiculars (**m**)
> - $B$ = Moulded breadth (**m**)
> - $T$ = Moulded draught (**m**)
> - $C_B$ = Block coefficient (dimensionless, typically **0.55 to 0.85**)

#### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Legged Stool"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a ship as a **two-legged stool** standing on the seabed of the ocean.
>
> - **One leg is the Weight Equation** — the ship must weigh *enough* to ride at its design waterline. Too light, it floats too high; too heavy, it sinks below safe limits.
> - **The other leg is the Volume Equation** — the hull must enclose *enough underwater volume* to physically push aside that weight of water. Too small a hull, even a "light" ship will sink.
>
> For the stool (ship) to stand, **both legs must be perfectly equal in length**. The naval architect's job during preliminary design is to "shave" each leg until the two equations are simultaneously satisfied. This iterative balancing act is famously known as the **Design Spiral**.

#### 1.3 Connecting Weight to Volume — The Density Link

The two equations are not independent; they are linked by the density of seawater:

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \times \nabla$$

Where $\rho_{sw}$ = density of seawater = **1.025 t/m³** (standard) and can vary from **1.020 to 1.030 t/m³** depending on salinity and temperature.

### 1.4 Definition of Key Weights

| Symbol | Full Name | Description |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| $\Delta$ | Displacement | Total weight of ship + everything aboard |
| $W_L$ | Lightweight (LWT) | Empty ship: hull steel, machinery, outfit |
| $W_D$ | Deadweight (DWT) | Variable load: cargo, fuel, water, crew |
| $\Delta_L$ | Light Displacement | Ship with no DWT (ballast only) |
| $\Delta_S$ | Full Load Displacement | $\Delta_L + $ full DWT |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic Behind the Weight Equation

The Weight Equation partitions the total displacement into **two mutually exclusive categories**:

- **Lightweight ($W_L$):** The constant, structural component that exists whether the ship is sailing empty or fully loaded. Includes:
  - Hull steel (plating, stiffeners, frames)
  - Superstructure
  - Main propulsion machinery
  - Auxiliary systems (electrical, piping, HVAC)
  - Outfit (paint, insulation, joinery, navigation equipment)
  - Ballast systems (permanent)

- **Deadweight ($W_D$):** The variable, commercial/mission component. Includes:
  - Cargo (pay-load)
  - Bunker fuel (HFO, DO, LO)
  - Freshwater (drinking + boiler feed)
  - Crew + effects + stores + provisions
  - Luggage, mail (passenger ships)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight Coefficient:** $C_D = \dfrac{W_D}{\Delta}$
> This ratio is a primary indicator of ship type. Tankers have $C_D \approx 0.85$, while naval combatants have $C_D \approx 0.30$.

### 2.2 The Logic Behind the Volume Equation

The block coefficient $C_B$ is essentially an **"efficiency factor"** telling us how much of the rectangular envelope $L_{pp} \times B \times T$ is filled by the actual underwater hull form.

- A **slender, fast hull** (e.g., naval frigate) has a low $C_B \approx 0.45 - 0.55$.
- A **full, slow hull** (e.g., oil tanker) has a high $C_B \approx 0.80 - 0.85$.

The **longitudinal prismatic coefficient** $C_P$ and **midship section coefficient** $C_M$ are further refinements:

$$C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \times L_{pp}} \quad \text{and} \quad C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \times T}$$

With the fundamental relationship:
$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

### 2.3 Auxiliary Coefficients Used in Preliminary Design

To estimate displacement before detailed weight breakdowns exist, naval architects use empirical coefficients derived from similar existing vessels (called **"parent designs"** or **"reference ships"**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Admiralty Coefficient ($C_A$):**
> $$C_A = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \times V_s^3}{P}$$
> Used to compare propulsive efficiency across ships.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bantam Coefficient / Deadweight Coefficient:**
> $$\eta = \dfrac{W_D}{\nabla} = \dfrac{W_D \times \rho_{sw}}{\Delta}$$
> Indicates cargo-carrying efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tonnage to Displacement Conversion:**
> - 1 **GT** (Gross Tonnage) ≈ **1 tonne** displacement (approximation only for early design)
> - 1 **tonne** = **1000 kg** = **1 metric ton**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta = W_L + W_D$ | Weight Equation | t |
| 2 | $\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Volume Equation | $m^3$ |
| 3 | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement–Volume Link | t |
| 4 | $C_B = \nabla / (L_{pp} B T)$ | Block Coefficient | – |
| 5 | $C_P = \nabla / (A_M L_{pp})$ | Prismatic Coefficient | – |
| 6 | $C_M = A_M / (B T)$ | Midship Coefficient | – |
| 7 | $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ | Coefficient Relationship | – |
| 8 | $C_D = W_D / \Delta$ | Deadweight Coefficient | – |
| 9 | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$ | Seawater Density (standard) | $t/m^3$ |
| 10 | $C_A = \Delta^{2/3} V_s^3 / P$ | Admiralty Coefficient | – |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Notation:** Always use $\Delta$ (capital Greek delta) for displacement and $\nabla$ (nabla) for volume. Do not interchange. In KTU valuation scripts, missing the correct Greek symbol often results in a **0.5 mark deduction**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Weight and Volume equations are **the cornerstone of every shipbuilding contract worldwide**. Before any steel is cut, the shipyard and owner must agree on:
- The **design displacement** (sets hull thickness, scantlings per classification society rules like **IRS, DNV, Lloyd's Register**).
- The **design speed** at that displacement (sets engine power).
- The **principal dimensions** $L, B, T$ (set docking constraints, canal transit fees, port accessibility).

Failure to balance these equations before detailed design causes costly redesigns, sometimes running into **millions of dollars** in rework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weight Equation from Archimedes' Principle

Archimedes' Principle states that the buoyant force on a floating body equals the weight of the fluid displaced.

**Step 1:** Buoyant Force
$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 2:** For a floating ship in static equilibrium, buoyant force equals total weight (mass × gravity):
$$F_B = W_{total}$$

**Step 3:** Equating and cancelling $g$ on both sides:
$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla = W_{total} \cdot g$$

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = W_{total}$$

**Step 4:** Expressing the total weight as the sum of structural (lightweight) and variable (deadweight) loads:
$$W_{total} = W_L + W_D$$

**Step 5:** Substituting and expressing in mass units (1 t = 1000 kg, with $g$ implicit in tonnes-force):
$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = W_L + W_D}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Volume Equation

**Step 1:** The volume of the underwater hull can be visualized as an irregular shape. Its exact computation requires integration of the sectional area curve:
$$\nabla = \int_{0}^{L_{pp}} A(x) \, dx$$

**Step 2:** If we approximate the hull as a rectangular prism of length $L_{pp}$, breadth $B$, and draught $T$, its volume would be $L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$. The actual hull occupies only a fraction of this rectangular envelope.

**Step 3:** Define $C_B$ as the ratio of actual underwater volume to the bounding rectangular volume:
$$C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:
$$\boxed{\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}$$

### 3.3 Solution of Simultaneous Equations — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following preliminary data:
> - $L_{pp} = 200 \ m$, $B = 32 \ m$, $T = 14 \ m$, $C_B = 0.78$
> - $W_D = 50{,}000 \ t$
> - $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$
>
> **Required:** Calculate (i) Volume of displacement, (ii) Total displacement, (iii) Lightweight.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute volume of displacement using Volume Equation:**
$$\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$$

$$\nabla = 200 \times 32 \times 14 \times 0.78$$

$$\nabla = 89{,}600 \times 0.78$$

$$\nabla = 69{,}888 \ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute total displacement using the density link:**
$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$$

$$\Delta = 1.025 \times 69{,}888$$

$$\Delta = 71{,}635.2 \ t$$

**Step 3 — Compute Lightweight by rearranging the Weight Equation:**
$$\Delta = W_L + W_D \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_L = \Delta - W_D$$

$$W_L = 71{,}635.2 - 50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{W_L = 21{,}635.2 \ t}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Deadweight Coefficient for verification:**
$$C_D = \dfrac{W_D}{\Delta} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{71{,}635.2} = 0.698 \approx 0.70$$

This is a typical value for a bulk carrier, confirming our calculation is realistic.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Values by Ship Category

| Ship Category | $C_B$ Range | $C_D$ Range | $\Delta$ Range (t) | $L_{pp}/B$ |
|---------------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|------------|
| Oil Tanker (VLCC) | 0.82 – 0.85 | 0.83 – 0.87 | 200,000 – 320,000 | 6.0 – 6.5 |
| Bulk Carrier (Capesize) | 0.78 – 0.82 | 0.75 – 0.80 | 100,000 – 180,000 | 5.5 – 6.0 |
| Container Ship (Post-Panamax) | 0.60 – 0.68 | 0.55 – 0.65 | 50,000 – 200,000 | 6.5 – 7.5 |
| General Cargo | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 5,000 – 25,000 | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| Naval Frigate | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 3,000 – 6,000 | 8.0 – 10.0 |
| LNG Carrier | 0.72 – 0.78 | 0.65 – 0.72 | 70,000 – 100,000 | 6.0 – 6.5 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Faster = Slimmer":** As service speed increases, $C_B$ decreases. Tankers sail at 15–16 knots → high $C_B$. Frigates do 28+ knots → low $C_B$.

### 3.5 Iterative Solution Procedure (Design Spiral Step)

When solving the Weight and Volume equations during preliminary design, the following iterative algorithm is used:

```
1. Start with parent ship data (Lpp, B, T, Cb, WL, WD).
2. Compute Δ = WL + WD  (Weight Equation).
3. Compute ∇_required = Δ / ρ_sw.
4. Compute Lpp × B × T = ∇_required / Cb  (Volume Equation).
5. Check if Lpp × B × T matches parent design.
6. If mismatch > tolerance (±2%), adjust one dimension
   (typically B or T) and repeat.
7. Converge when both equations are satisfied within tolerance.
```

This iterative procedure is the mathematical core of the **Design Spiral**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Design Spiral (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Mission Requirements] --> B[Estimate Principal Dimensions Lpp, B, T]
    B --> C[Weight Equation: Δ = WL + WD]
    C --> D[Volume Equation: ∇ = Lpp·B·T·Cb]
    D --> E{Satisfies Both Equations?}
    E -- No --> F[Adjust Dimensions or Coeff]
    F --> B
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute Powering Requirement]
    G --> H[Check Stability GM and Freeboard]
    H --> I{Meets Stability Criteria?}
    I -- No --> J[Modify Hull Form or CoB]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Preliminary Design Complete]
    K --> L[Proceed to Contract Design]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Weight and Volume Equation Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL[WL Lightweight] --> SUM
    WD[WD Deadweight] --> SUM
    SUM((Weight Equation)) --> DELTA[Δ Total Displacement]
    DELTA --> RHO[ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³]
    RHO --> NABLA[∇ Volume of Displacement]
    LPP[Lpp] --> MULT
    B[B] --> MULT
    T[T] --> MULT
    CB[Cb Block Coeff] --> MULT
    MULT((Volume Equation)) --> NABLA
    NABLA --> CHECK{Δ_calc = Δ_iter?}
    CHECK -- Yes --> DONE[Converged Solution]
    CHECK -- No --> ADJUST[Modify Lpp/B/T/Cb]
    ADJUST --> MULT
    ADJUST --> SUM
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Partitioning of Ship Displacement

```mermaid
graph TD
    DISP[Δ TOTAL DISPLACEMENT] --> LW[WL LIGHTWEIGHT]
    DISP --> DW[WD DEADWEIGHT]
    LW --> LH[Hull Steel]
    LW --> LM[Machinery]
    LW --> LS[Superstructure]
    LW --> LO[Outfit]
    LW --> LB[Permanent Ballast]
    DW --> DC[Cargo Payload]
    DW --> DF[Bunker Fuel]
    DW --> DW2[Fresh Water]
    DW --> DC2[Crew + Stores]
    DW --> DP[Provisions + Spares]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Basic Design Procedure for Main Ship Categories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Ship Category Selected] --> CAT{Tanker or Bulk or Container or Naval?}
    CAT -- Tanker --> T1[High Cb 0.82, large DWT, slow speed]
    CAT -- Bulk --> B1[Moderate Cb 0.78, geared/engine room aft]
    CAT -- Container --> C1[Low Cb 0.65, high speed, long Lpp]
    CAT -- Naval --> N1[Very low Cb 0.50, sleek, high speed]
    T1 --> STEP2[Set Mission Profile]
    B1 --> STEP2
    C1 --> STEP2
    N1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3[Select Parent Ship as Reference]
    STEP3 --> STEP4[Apply Weight and Volume Equations]
    STEP4 --> STEP5[Iterate Design Spiral until Convergence]
    STEP5 --> STEP6[Verify Stability, Powering, Seakeeping]
    STEP6 --> END[Preliminary Design Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of the design procedure," present the **Design Spiral flowchart** (4.1) or the **Category-Specific Decision Tree** (4.4). Either fetches full marks in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Weight Equation of a ship and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**
The Weight Equation of a ship expresses the equality between total displacement and the sum of structural and variable weights. It is written as:

$$\Delta = W_L + W_D$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = Total displacement (tonnes), the weight of seawater displaced by the ship at its loaded waterline.
- $W_L$ = Lightweight (tonnes), the permanent weight of the empty ship including hull steel, machinery, superstructure, outfit, and permanent ballast.
- $W_D$ = Deadweight (tonnes), the variable load including cargo, fuel oil, diesel, lubricating oil, freshwater, crew, stores, and provisions.

This equation is derived from Archimedes' principle of buoyancy applied to a floating vessel in static equilibrium. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Block Coefficient? How does it vary between a tanker and a naval frigate?**

**Model Answer:**
Block coefficient $C_B$ is the ratio of the underwater volume of the hull to the product of its length, breadth, and draught:

$$C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

It is dimensionless and indicates how "full" or "slender" the underwater hull form is.

- For an **oil tanker**, $C_B \approx 0.82 - 0.85$ (high) because the ship sails slowly (15 knots) and benefits from a full hull for maximum cargo capacity.
- For a **naval frigate**, $C_B \approx 0.45 - 0.55$ (low) because the ship must achieve high speeds (28+ knots), requiring a streamlined, slender hull form.

In general, **higher speeds correlate with lower block coefficients**. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the Weight Equation and the Volume Equation of a ship from first principles. State clearly all assumptions and define every symbol used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Weight Equation — Derivation:**

Starting from Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force $F_B$ on a floating body equals the weight of fluid displaced:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$$

For a ship in static floating equilibrium, this buoyant force equals the total weight of the ship:

$$F_B = W_{total} \cdot g$$

Equating both expressions and cancelling $g$:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = W_{total}$$

The total weight of the ship is the sum of its lightweight (constant structural mass) and its deadweight (variable mission load):

$$W_{total} = W_L + W_D$$

Combining, and noting that in marine engineering $\Delta$ represents displacement in mass units (tonnes), with $g$ absorbed:

$$\boxed{\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = W_L + W_D}$$

**Assumptions:** Ship is floating in calm seawater at equilibrium; $g$ cancels on both sides; $\rho_{sw}$ taken as **1.025 t/m³**; weights are in metric tonnes.

**Symbols:**
- $\Delta$ = displacement (t), $W_L$ = lightweight (t), $W_D$ = deadweight (t)
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume ($m^3$), $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density (t/m³)

**Volume Equation — Derivation:**

The underwater volume of a ship is obtained by integrating the cross-sectional area along the length:

$$\nabla = \int_0^{L_{pp}} A(x) \, dx$$

If the hull were a perfect rectangular prism with the same $L_{pp}$, $B$, and $T$, its volume would be $L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T$. The actual hull fills only a fraction of this envelope, defined as the block coefficient:

$$C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B}$$

**Assumptions:** Hull is treated as a single block; trim is zero (even keel); $C_B$ is the average value along the length. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A Handysize bulk carrier is to be designed with the following preliminary specifications: $L_{pp} = 130 \ m$, $B = 22 \ m$, $T = 9.5 \ m$, $C_B = 0.80$, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$. The estimated cargo carrying capacity is 35,000 t. Determine:
1. The total displacement $\Delta$.
2. The lightweight $W_L$.
3. The deadweight coefficient $C_D$.
4. Comment on whether the design is realistic for a Handysize bulk carrier. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute volume of displacement:**
$$\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B = 130 \times 22 \times 9.5 \times 0.80$$

$$\nabla = 27{,}170 \times 0.80 = 21{,}736 \ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute total displacement:**
$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1.025 \times 21{,}736 = 22{,}279.4 \ t$$

**Step 3 — Compute lightweight:**
Given cargo capacity (assume $W_D \approx$ cargo) = 35,000 t. However, total $W_D$ also includes fuel, water, crew etc. For this problem we assume $W_D = 35{,}000 \ t$ as given total.

$$W_L = \Delta - W_D = 22{,}279.4 - 35{,}000 = -12{,}720.6 \ t$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NEGATIVE LIGHTWEIGHT IS PHYSICALLY IMPOSSIBLE.** This means the assumed $W_D = 35{,}000 \ t$ exceeds the available displacement. The design is **NOT feasible** with the current $L_{pp}, B, T$ combination. Either dimensions must increase, or $W_D$ must be reduced.

**Step 4 — Compute deadweight coefficient (using given cargo):**
$$C_D = \dfrac{W_D}{\Delta} = \dfrac{35{,}000}{22{,}279.4} = 1.571$$

This $C_D > 1$ confirms the infeasibility — cargo alone exceeds total displacement, leaving no room for lightweight structure.

**Step 5 — Realistic Comment:**
For a Handysize bulk carrier (typically 25,000–40,000 DWT), the principal dimensions must yield $\Delta$ at least 130%–140% of DWT. Current dimensions produce only $\Delta \approx 22{,}000 \ t$, far too small. **Recommended action:** Increase $T$ to ~12 m or $L_{pp}$ to ~160 m, then re-iterate the Design Spiral. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **Basic Design Procedure** for the main categories of ships. Compare the procedure for an **oil tanker** and a **container ship**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram — Basic Design Procedure (Common to all ship categories):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Mission Requirements] --> B[Step 2: Deadweight & Speed]
    B --> C[Step 3: Select Parent Ship]
    C --> D[Step 4: Initial Lpp, B, T from Regression]
    D --> E[Step 5: Solve Weight Equation]
    E --> F[Step 6: Solve Volume Equation]
    F --> G[Step 7: Iterate Design Spiral]
    G --> H[Step 8: Powering Estimation]
    H --> I[Step 9: Stability Check]
    I --> J{Meets Criteria?}
    J -- No --> D
    J -- Yes --> K[Step 10: Preliminary Design Output]
```

**Comparison Table — Oil Tanker vs Container Ship:**

| Design Aspect | Oil Tanker (VLCC) | Container Ship (Post-Panamax) |
|---------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
| Mission | Carry crude oil in bulk | Carry TEU containers |
| Design Speed | 15–16 knots | 22–25 knots |
| $C_B$ | 0.82 – 0.85 (high) | 0.60 – 0.68 (low) |
| $C_D$ | 0.85 – 0.88 (very high) | 0.55 – 0.65 |
| $L_{pp}/B$ | 6.0 – 6.5 | 7.0 – 8.0 |
| Hull Form | Full, single skeg | Fine, bulbous bow, stern flaps |
| Machinery | Single slow-speed diesel | Single slow-speed + bow thruster |
| Key Constraint | Cargo capacity, draft limits | TEU capacity, port crane outreach |

**Procedure for Oil Tanker:**
1. Mission: transport ~300,000 t crude oil.
2. From parent ship regression, estimate $L_{pp} \approx 320 \ m$, $B \approx 58 \ m$, $T \approx 20 \ m$.
3. Apply Weight Equation: $\Delta = W_L + W_D$, with $W_D \approx 300{,}000 \ t$, $W_L \approx 45{,}000 \ t$, so $\Delta \approx 345{,}000 \ t$.
4. Apply Volume Equation: $\nabla = \Delta / \rho_{sw} = 336{,}585 \ m^3$.
5. With high $C_B = 0.83$, dimensions match — no iteration needed.
6. Verify powering for 15.5 knots; install single 25 MW MAN B&W engine.

**Procedure for Container Ship:**
1. Mission: carry 8,000 TEU at 24 knots.
2. From parent (e.g., Emma Maersk class), estimate $L_{pp} \approx 360 \ m$, $B \approx 49 \ m$, $T \approx 15 \ m$.
3. $W_D \approx 110{,}000 \ t$ (containers), $W_L \approx 65{,}000 \ t$, so $\Delta \approx 175{,}000 \ t$.
4. $\nabla = 170{,}732 \ m^3$, with $C_B \approx 0.65$, check dimensions.
5. Powering for 24 knots requires ~80 MW — multiple diesels or single ultra-long-stroke.
6. Stability check critical due to high GM from tall container stacks.

**(7 Marks)**

---

**(b)** A naval frigate is being designed with the following: $\Delta = 4{,}500 \ t$, $L_{pp} = 130 \ m$, $B = 14 \ m$, $C_B = 0.50$, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$. Calculate:
1. The required mean draught $T$.
2. The deadweight coefficient if the lightweight is 2,800 t.
3. Comment on the $C_B$ value compared to a commercial vessel. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute displaced volume:**
$$\nabla = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \dfrac{4{,}500}{1.025} = 4{,}390.24 \ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Solve Volume Equation for $T$:**
$$T = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot C_B} = \dfrac{4{,}390.24}{130 \times 14 \times 0.50}$$

$$T = \dfrac{4{,}390.24}{910} = 4.82 \ m$$

**Step 3 — Compute deadweight and $C_D$:**
$$W_D = \Delta - W_L = 4{,}500 - 2{,}800 = 1{,}700 \ t$$

$$C_D = \dfrac{W_D}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1{,}700}{4{,}500} = 0.378$$

**Step 4 — Comment on $C_B$:**
The block coefficient of **0.50** is significantly lower than that of a typical commercial vessel (tanker $C_B \approx 0.83$, bulk carrier $C_B \approx 0.78$). This reflects the frigate's high-speed (28+ knots), slender, streamlined hull form required to minimize wave-making resistance. The low $C_B$ allows higher Froude numbers and superior seakeeping performance at the cost of reduced cargo/variable-load capacity. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $W_L$ and $W_D$:** Lightweight is the EMPTY ship; Deadweight is the LOAD. Marks deducted if swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting density:** When going from $\Delta$ (tonnes) to $\nabla$ ($m^3$), ALWAYS divide by $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$, not by 1.0.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** Dimensions must be in **metres**, weight in **tonnes**, volume in $m^3$.
> 4. **Block diagram without labels:** Flowchart nodes must contain text; arrows must show flow direction. Empty boxes = 0 marks.
> 5. **Numerical errors in long multiplication:** Always show intermediate steps — partial credit is awarded even if final answer is wrong.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Weight Equation:** $\Delta = W_L + W_D$ — derived from Archimedes' principle.
- ⭐ **Volume Equation:** $\nabla = L_{pp} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ — describes hull geometry.
- ⭐ **Link Equation:** $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$, with $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \ t/m^3$.
- ⭐ **Block Coefficient $C_B$:** indicates hull fullness. High for slow ships (tankers), low for fast ships (frigates).
- ⭐ **Coefficient Relation:** $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ (always remember this identity).
- ⭐ **Deadweight Coefficient $C_D = W_D / \Delta$:** identifies ship type — tankers ~0.85, frigates ~0.30.
- ⭐ **Design Spiral:** iterative procedure alternating between Weight and Volume equations until convergence (±2% tolerance).
- ⭐ **Basic Design Procedure:** Mission → Parent ship → Regression → Weight & Volume Equations → Iterate → Powering → Stability → Output.
- ⭐ **Ship Categories:** Tankers (high $C_B$), Bulk Carriers (high $C_B$, aft engine), Container Ships (low $C_B$, high speed), General Cargo (medium $C_B$), Naval Vessels (very low $C_B$, high speed).
- ⭐ **Memory Rule:** "Faster = Slimmer = Lower $C_B$"; "Higher Cargo % = Higher $C_D$".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deadweight Carriers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Deadweight Carriers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Deadweight Carrier (DWC)** is a specialized category of merchant ship specifically engineered and dimensioned to transport exceptionally heavy, dense, and often over-dimensional cargoes that exceed the volumetric and structural capacity limits of conventional dry-bulk carriers, general cargo ships, or tankers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"A Deadweight Carrier is a vessel whose primary design criterion is the maximization of Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) relative to Lightweight (LWT), achieved by sacrificing speed, hull form efficiency, and sophisticated hydrodynamics in favor of structural strength, large deck areas, and heavy-lift cargo gear."* The characteristic design metric used to classify such vessels is the **Deadweight-to-Lightweight Ratio (DWT/LWT)**, which for a standard bulk carrier lies between **1.5 and 2.5**, while for a Deadweight Carrier it typically exceeds **3.0** and can reach values as high as **6.0 to 8.0** for semi-submersible heavy-lift vessels. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine the difference between a **regular delivery truck** and a **specialized heavy-haulage low-bed trailer** used to transport a 100-tonne generator or a locomotive engine. | Transport Type | Marine Equivalent | Primary Design Goal | |---|---|---| | Delivery Truck (Box Van) | General Cargo Ship | Volume + Variety of goods | | Tanker Truck | Oil/Chemical Tanker | Fluid volume containment | | Heavy-Haulage Low-Bed Trailer | **Deadweight Carrier** | **Concentrated Mass per square meter (t/m²)** | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Just as a low-bed trailer has reinforced axles, hydraulic suspensions, and a low deck to keep the center of gravity down while carrying a transformer, a Deadweight Carrier features a **reinforced tank top**, **heavy-duty double-bottom plating**, **massive hatch covers**, and often **onboard gantry cranes** capable of lifting hundreds of tonnes — all to safely accept loads that would buckle a conventional ship's deck. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following standard design metrics govern Deadweight Carrier geometry: - **Block Coefficient (Cₑᵥ or C_B):** Typically **0.75 – 0.85** (high fullness for cargo volume) - **Length-to-Beam Ratio (L/B):** Usually **5.0 – 6.5** (shorter, wider hulls than fast vessels) - **Beam-to-Draft Ratio (B/T):** Approximately **2.5 – 3.5** (wide shallow hulls) - **Service Speed (Vₛ):** **12 – 16 knots** (low, since speed penalty is acceptable) - **Deck Load Capacity:** **10 – 25 tonnes/m²** (versus 1.5 – 3.0 t/m² for normal bulkers) - **Cargo Gear SWL:** Up to **2 × 1,500 tonnes** tandem lift capacity in modern semi-submersibles - **DWT/LWT Ratio:** **3.0 – 8.0** (defining parameter) > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The single most important exam-friendly takeaway for KTU Module 2 is the relationship **"High DWT/LWT ratio ⇒ Sacrificed Hydrodynamics ⇒ Reinforced Structure ⇒ Reduced Service Speed."** This cause-effect chain is a frequent 3-mark question. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Classification of Deadweight Carriers Deadweight Carriers are classified by KTU/IMO conventions into **three functional sub-categories**: ### 2.1.1 Type A — Conventional Heavy-Lift Ships - Equipped with **pedestal cranes** or **deck cranes** (SWL: 80–600 t per crane). - Typically twin cranes for tandem lifts. - Single-deck or tweendeck arrangement with reinforced hatch covers. - **Example:** *Happy*-class heavy-lift vessels. ### 2.1.2 Type B — Semi-Submersible Heavy-Lift Ships - Feature **ballastable buoyancy chambers** at the bow and stern. - The vessel can **submerge its deck below water level** to float cargo on/off. - Used for transporting **drilling rigs, FPSOs, port cranes, and damaged warships**. - **Example:** *Dockwise*-type vessels (Blue Marlin, Black Marlin). ### 2.1.3 Type C — Dock Ships (Float-On/Float-Off — FOFLO) - Have a **bow door that opens** to allow cargo to be floated in. - Internal dock area can be ballasted down. - **Example:** *Combi Lift* and *COSCO* specialized FOFLO vessels. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic — The "Why" and "How" The design logic for a Deadweight Carrier follows this strict hierarchy: 1. **Step 1 — Cargo Profile Definition:** Identify the maximum single-lift mass (M_lift), footprint area (A_fp), and center-of-gravity height (KG_cargo). 2. **Step 2 — Deck Strength Sizing:** Calculate required plating thickness and stiffener spacing using **Class Rules (DNV, LR, ABS)** for the local panel load. 3. **Step 3 — Hull Girder Section Modulus:** Increase the midship section modulus (Z) by **25–60%** over a standard bulk carrier to handle high still-water bending moments (SWBM). 4. **Step 4 — Stability Optimization:** Ensure intact stability (GM > 0.15 m) and damage stability (SOLAS Reg. II-1) criteria are met despite the high vertical CG. 5. **Step 5 — Powering Reduction:** Since speed is sacrificed, installed power (P_B) is reduced, lowering fuel consumption and emissions per tonne-mile. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - LWT$ | tonnes (t) | Total variable load | | 2 | Lightweight | $LWT = \Delta_{lightship}$ | tonnes (t) | Constant structural mass | | 3 | DWT/LWT Ratio | $R_{DL} = \dfrac{DWT}{LWT}$ | dimensionless | **> 3.0** for DWC | | 4 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³) | | 5 | Midship Section Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | dimensionless | Higher than conventional ships | | 6 | Waterplane Area Coefficient | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | dimensionless | Affects $TPC$ and $BM_T$ | | 7 | Tonnage per cm immersion | $TPC = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{100} \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | t/cm | $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³ | | 8 | Transverse BM | $BM_T = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{B^2}{12 \cdot C_M}$ | m | For rectangular approximation | | 9 | Longitudinal BM | $BM_L = \dfrac{L^2}{12 \cdot C_B}$ | m | Prismatic hull assumption | | 10 | Service Power (approx.) | $P_B = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{C_{adm}}$ | kW | $C_{adm}$ = Admiralty coefficient | | 11 | Admiralty Coefficient | $C_{adm} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2/3} \cdot V_s^3}{P_B}$ | dimensionless | Typically **350 – 500** for DWC | | 12 | Still-Water Bending Moment | $SWBM = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{eff}^2}{g}$ | kN·m | $W$ = weight per unit length | | 13 | Section Modulus (req.) | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}}$ | cm³·m | $\sigma_{perm}$ = allowable stress | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Deadweight Carriers are the **unsung workhorses of global energy infrastructure**. They transport: - **Offshore drilling rigs** (jack-ups, semi-submersibles) — often requiring float-on operations. - **Wind turbine components** — modern blades exceed 100 m length. - **Port and shipyard cranes** — STS cranes weighing 1,800+ tonnes. - **Power generation equipment** — turbines, generators, reactor vessels. - **Damaged naval vessels** — for salvage or repair. - **FPSOs and FSOs** — full offshore production units. > [!NOTE] > Without Deadweight Carriers, the modern offshore wind, oil & gas, and shipbuilding industries would be unable to relocate their largest assets across oceans. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The DWT/LWT Ratio as a Design Classifier We derive why the **DWT/LWT ratio** is the defining parameter for a Deadweight Carrier. **Step 1 — Define total displacement at load draft:** The displacement at load draft ($\Delta_{loaded}$) equals the sum of Lightweight ($LWT$) and Deadweight ($DWT$): $$\Delta_{loaded} = LWT + DWT$$ **Step 2 — Solve for Deadweight:** $$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - LWT$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio:** $$R_{DL} = \dfrac{DWT}{LWT}$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of structural efficiency:** Let $\eta_s$ denote the **structural efficiency factor**, defined as the fraction of total displacement available for cargo: $$\eta_s = \dfrac{DWT}{\Delta_{loaded}} = \dfrac{R_{DL}}{1 + R_{DL}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the ratio in terms of $\eta_s$:** $$R_{DL} = \dfrac{\eta_s}{1 - \eta_s}$$ **Step 6 — Tabulate representative values:** | Vessel Class | $R_{DL}$ | $\eta_s = R_{DL}/(1+R_{DL})$ | |---|---|---| | Container Ship (feeder) | 1.6 | 0.615 | | Panamax Bulk Carrier | 2.2 | 0.688 | | Capesize Bulk Carrier | 2.6 | 0.722 | | **Heavy-Lift Ship (Type A)** | **3.5** | **0.778** | | **Semi-Submersible DWC (Type B)** | **6.0** | **0.857** | | **Pure DWC (Theoretical)** | **8.0** | **0.889** | > **Engineering Interpretation:** A higher $R_{DL}$ implies that **more than 75% of the loaded displacement is paying cargo**, which is the explicit design goal of any DWC. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Required Section Modulus for High Deck Loads When heavy cargo is concentrated on deck, the local still-water bending moment increases dramatically. **Step 1 — Effective weight per unit length for concentrated cargo:** Let the concentrated deck load be $P_{deck}$ (in tonnes) distributed over an effective longitudinal length $L_{eff}$: $$W = \dfrac{P_{deck} \cdot g}{L_{eff}} \quad \text{[kN/m]}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum bending moment for simply-supported beam (sagging condition):** $$M_{max} = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{eff}^2}{8}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute W:** $$M_{max} = \dfrac{P_{deck} \cdot g \cdot L_{eff}}{8}$$ **Step 4 — Apply bending stress equation:** $$\sigma_{perm} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z_{req}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for required section modulus:** $$Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \dfrac{P_{deck} \cdot g \cdot L_{eff}}{8 \cdot \sigma_{perm}}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical Example:** Assume $P_{deck} = 1500$ t, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $L_{eff} = 40$ m, $\sigma_{perm} = 175 \cdot 10^3$ kN/m² (mild steel H.A.). $$Z_{req} = \dfrac{1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 40}{8 \cdot 175 \cdot 10^3}$$ $$Z_{req} = \dfrac{588{,}600}{1{,}400{,}000}$$ $$Z_{req} = 0.4204 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 7 — Compare with standard bulk carrier:** A Panamax bulk carrier typically requires $Z_{req} \approx 0.18$ m³ — confirming DWC need a **section modulus roughly 2.3× larger**, achieved by **deeper webs, thicker flanges, and shorter span between bulkheads**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Design Parameters | Design Parameter | Conventional Bulker | Heavy-Lift (Type A) | Semi-Submersible (Type B) | |---|---|---|---| | $DWT/LWT$ Ratio | 1.8 – 2.6 | 3.0 – 4.0 | 5.0 – 8.0 | | Block Coefficient $C_B$ | 0.78 – 0.82 | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.75 – 0.82 | | Length/Beam ($L/B$) | 6.0 – 7.5 | 5.0 – 6.0 | 4.0 – 5.5 | | Service Speed (knots) | 14 – 16 | 13 – 16 | 12 – 15 | | Deck Load (t/m²) | 1.5 – 3.0 | 8 – 15 | 15 – 25 | | Crane SWL (t) | None (gearless) | 80 – 600 | 2 × 600 – 2 × 1500 | | Cargo Gear Type | Shore-based | Pedestal/Deck cranes | Gantry + ballast system | | Submersibility | No | No | Yes (3 – 10 m below WL) | | Hull Form | Fine forward | Full midship, fine ends | Box-like, blunt ends | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a Deadweight Carrier The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **inter-locked functional systems** of a semi-submersible Deadweight Carrier, mapping how cargo flow, ballast control, stability management, and structural loading interact. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cargo Specification
Mass, Footprint, CG Height] A --> B[Hull Structural Sizing
Plating, Stiffeners, Girders] A --> C[Deck Reinforcement
High t per sq m Capacity] A --> D[Cargo Handling System
Pedestal or Gantry Cranes] B --> E[Midship Section Modulus Z req
25 to 60 percent Increase] C --> F[Ballast System
Submersible Operation] D --> G[Load Distribution Network
to Double Bottom] E --> H[Stability Analysis
Intact and Damage SOLAS] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Final Design Verification
DWT to LWT Ratio] I --> J{Speed Priority} J -->|Low Speed Acceptable| K[Reduced Installed Power
Lower Fuel Consumption] J -->|High Speed Required| L[Refined Hull Form
Higher Admiralty Coeff] K --> M[Type B Semi Submersible DWC] L --> N[Type A Heavy Lift Ship] M --> O[Vessel Delivery] N --> O ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The architecture flow shows that **cargo specification (Node A)** is the root driver — every downstream subsystem (hull, deck, cranes, ballast, stability) is dimensioned by the **maximum single-lift mass and footprint**, not by speed or range. This contrasts sharply with a tanker or container ship, where cargo volume density is the driver. ## 4.2 Schematic Comparison: Cargo Loading Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TYPE_A[Type A Heavy Lift] A1[Vessel at Quay] --> A2[Pedestal Cranes Lift Cargo] A2 --> A3[Cargo Placed on Reinforced Deck] end subgraph TYPE_B[Type B Semi Submersible] B1[Vessel Ballasts Down] --> B2[Deck Submerges Below Water] B2 --> B3[Cargo Floats Above Deck] B3 --> B4[Vessel Deballasts Up] B4 --> B5[Cargo Now Seated on Deck] end subgraph TYPE_C[Type C FOFLO Dock Ship] C1[Bow Door Opens] --> C2[Internal Dock Floods] C2 --> C3[Cargo Floats Into Internal Dock] C3 --> C4[Internal Dock Dewatered] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** The **ballast system** in a Type B vessel is the single most critical auxiliary system — it must handle **30–40% of DWT in ballast water** within a controlled time window (typically 6–10 hours) while maintaining transverse stability during submergence. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a Deadweight Carrier and state its primary design objective.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Deadweight Carrier (DWC) is a specialized merchant vessel designed to transport heavy, dense, and over-dimensional cargoes that cannot be carried by conventional ships. **(1 mark)** Its primary design objective is the **maximization of Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) relative to Lightweight (LWT)**, expressed as a DWT/LWT ratio exceeding **3.0**, achieved by reinforcing hull structure, enlarging deck areas, and sacrificing service speed. **(2 marks)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is the significance of the DWT/LWT ratio in classifying a Deadweight Carrier?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The DWT/LWT ratio is a dimensionless index that quantifies the **cargo-carrying efficiency** of a vessel. **(1 mark)** For a standard bulk carrier, this ratio lies between **1.5 and 2.6**; for a conventional heavy-lift ship it is **3.0 – 4.0**; and for a semi-submersible Deadweight Carrier it can reach **6.0 – 8.0**. **(1 mark)** A higher ratio indicates that a larger fraction of the loaded displacement consists of paying cargo, which is the explicit design goal of any DWC. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse DWC with general Heavy-Lift ships.** A DWC must have a DWT/LWT ratio > 3.0; below this, it is just a "heavy-lift multipurpose vessel." > 2. **Always quote numerical values** (C_B, L/B, speed range) in design-comparison answers — vague qualitative answers fetch only 1–2 marks per sub-part. > 3. **For derivations, explicitly write the substitution step** — students lose marks by jumping from equation to equation without intermediate algebra. > 4. **Mention SOLAS damage stability** when discussing Type B submersible operations — examiners specifically test regulatory awareness. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain with sketches the **three main types** of Deadweight Carriers, listing **two typical cargoes** carried by each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A semi-submersible Deadweight Carrier has a loaded displacement of **80,000 tonnes** and a lightweight of **12,000 tonnes**. Calculate: 1. The DWT 2. The DWT/LWT ratio 3. Comment on whether the vessel qualifies as a Deadweight Carrier. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Type A — Conventional Heavy-Lift Ship:** Features pedestal cranes (SWL 80–600 t) and a reinforced deck. Typical cargoes: locomotive engines and power plant turbines. **(2 marks)** **Type B — Semi-Submersible Heavy-Lift Ship:** Has submersible buoyancy chambers at bow/stern allowing the deck to submerge 3–10 m below water. Typical cargoes: offshore drilling rigs and FPSOs. **(2.5 marks)** **Type C — FOFLO Dock Ship:** Has a bow door opening to an internal dock that can be flooded for float-on/float-off operations. Typical cargoes: barges and small naval vessels. **(2.5 marks)** *(Use simple sketch description in lieu of figure for KTU paper.)* **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Displacement decomposition:** $$\Delta_{loaded} = LWT + DWT$$ **Step 2 — Solve for DWT:** $$DWT = \Delta_{loaded} - LWT = 80{,}000 - 12{,}000 = 68{,}000 \text{ tonnes} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$ **Step 3 — Compute DWT/LWT ratio:** $$R_{DL} = \dfrac{DWT}{LWT} = \dfrac{68{,}000}{12{,}000} = 5.667 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 4 — Comment:** Since $R_{DL} = 5.667 > 3.0$, the vessel **qualifies as a Deadweight Carrier** (specifically Type B semi-submersible range). **(2 marks)** --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a)** List and briefly explain the **five key design characteristics** that distinguish a Deadweight Carrier from a conventional bulk carrier. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DWC has a deck load of **20 t/m²** distributed over a deck area of **40 m × 20 m**. The effective span is **40 m**. Assuming simply-supported beam behavior and an allowable bending stress of **175 MPa**, calculate: 1. The maximum bending moment 2. The required section modulus. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** 1. **High DWT/LWT ratio (> 3.0)** — maximizes cargo fraction. **(1.4 marks)** 2. **Reinforced deck plating** — supports 10–25 t/m² versus 1.5–3 t/m². **(1.4 marks)** 3. **Heavy-lift cranes onboard** — eliminates reliance on shore cranes. **(1.4 marks)** 4. **Reduced service speed** — 12–16 knots, lowering fuel cost. **(1.4 marks)** 5. **Increased midship section modulus** — 25–60% above standard to handle concentrated deck loads. **(1.4 marks)** **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Total concentrated deck load:** $$P_{deck} = 20 \cdot 40 \cdot 20 = 16{,}000 \text{ t} = 16{,}000 \cdot 10^3 \text{ kg}$$ Convert to force: $$F = 16{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 156{,}960 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent UDL over effective span:** $$W = \dfrac{F}{L_{eff}} = \dfrac{156{,}960}{40} = 3{,}924 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum bending moment (simply-supported):** $$M_{max} = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{eff}^2}{8} = \dfrac{3{,}924 \cdot 40^2}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = \dfrac{3{,}924 \cdot 1{,}600}{8} = \dfrac{6{,}278{,}400}{8}$$ $$M_{max} = 784{,}800 \text{ kN·m} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$ **Step 4 — Required section modulus:** $$Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \dfrac{784{,}800}{175 \cdot 10^3}$$ $$Z_{req} = 4.4846 \text{ m}^3 \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering comment:** This required section modulus is **about 25× larger** than a standard Panamax bulk carrier, confirming the need for substantially reinforced hull girder construction. **(2 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Deadweight Carriers** - [x] **Definition:** Specialized vessel for heavy/dense/oversized cargo with **DWT/LWT > 3.0**. - [x] **Three types:** Type A (heavy-lift cranes), Type B (semi-submersible), Type C (FOFLO dock ship). - [x] **Primary design metric:** DWT/LWT ratio (3.0 – 8.0). - [x] **Block Coefficient:** 0.75 – 0.85 (high fullness). - [x] **Service Speed:** 12 – 16 knots (sacrificed). - [x] **Deck Load Capacity:** 10 – 25 t/m². - [x] **Hull Girder Section Modulus:** Increased 25–60% over standard bulker. - [x] **Admiralty Coefficient:** 350 – 500 (low, due to slow speeds). - [x] **Cargo Handling:** Pedestal cranes, gantry cranes, or submersible deck operations. - [x] **Stability Requirement:** Must satisfy SOLAS intact AND damage stability even during submergence (Type B). - [x] **Typical cargoes:** Drilling rigs, FPSOs, port cranes, wind turbine blades, power plant equipment. - [x] **Key engineering trade-off:** Structural strength + cargo capacity ↑↑ vs. Speed + hydrodynamic efficiency ↓↓. - [x] **Exam-favorite formula:** $R_{DL} = DWT/LWT$ and $Z_{req} = M_{max}/\sigma_{perm}$. - [x] **Always quote units** (tonnes, kN, m, m³) in numerical answers — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

topics.title: Capacity Carriers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Capacity Carriers

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacity Carriers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Capacity Carrier** (also called a **dry bulk carrier** in its pure form) is a merchant ship specifically designed and optimized for the **maximum transportation of dry bulk cargo** such as iron ore, coal, grain, and bauxite, where the principal design driver is the **deadweight carrying capacity (DWT)** per unit of vessel size, rather than service speed or multi-trade flexibility.

According to the **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) for Bulk Carriers** (2006, revised 2015), capacity carriers are classified by their structural design philosophy — emphasizing long, clear cargo holds, large hatches, and heavy-duty double bottoms for fast loading/discharging by shore-based gantry cranes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Capacity Carriers represent the *pure bulk carrier* family. They are distinct from *combination carriers* (OBO/OOC), which compromise capacity for flexibility, and from *specialized carriers* (e.g., ore carriers with engine room aft).

### Key Sub-Categories of Capacity Carriers

| Sub-Class | DWT Range (t) | Defining Constraint |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Handysize** | 15,000 – 35,000 | None (smallest unrestricted) |
| **Handymax / Supramax** | 35,000 – 60,000 | Gearless or geared |
| **Panamax** | 60,000 – 80,000 | Old Panama Canal limits |
| **New Panamax / Neopanamax** | 100,000 – 120,000 | Expanded Canal limits |
| **Capesize** | 100,000 – 400,000 | Too large for canals |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:**
> Think of a capacity carrier as a **giant floating railway wagon** — a long, steel box whose only job is to swallow as much ore or coal as possible, hold it securely across an ocean, and spit it out at the destination as quickly as a shore crane can lift it. Unlike a passenger ship (which is shaped for comfort and speed) or a tanker (which is shaped to slosh fluid safely), the capacity carrier is shaped purely for **volume × density = profit**.

The hull form is a **full-bodied block coefficient** ($C_B \approx 0.78 - 0.85$), giving a fat, blunt ship — exactly the opposite of a sleek container ship. Every cubic meter of hull is "earning" cargo revenue.

### Governing Classification & Regulatory Standard

The design of capacity carriers is governed by:

- **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)** — especially **UR S21** (corrosion protection) and **UR S23** (hatch cover design).
- **IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR) for Bulk Carriers** (entered force 1 April 2006; mandatory for all new-builds > 90 m LOA contracted from that date).
- **SOLAS Chapter XII** — additional safety measures for bulk carriers (2002/2004 amendments after the *Derbyshire* and *Stellar Daisy* losses).
- **MARPOL Annex VI** — for EEDI/EEXI compliance of new capacity carriers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in bold:**
> - Block coefficient $C_B$ range: **0.78 – 0.85**
> - Length-to-Breadth ratio $L/B$ range: **5.5 – 7.0**
> - Breadth-to-Depth ratio $B/D$ range: **1.8 – 2.4**
> - Service speed: **13 – 15 knots** (lower than container ships → higher capacity per ton)
> - Cargo density handled: **0.5 t/m³ (grain) to 3.0 t/m³ (iron ore)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Design Philosophy of Capacity Carriers

The defining design logic of a capacity carrier revolves around **maximizing the cargo deadweight ratio** while satisfying structural strength, stability, and SOLAS regulations. The design proceeds in this order:

1. **Determine required DWT** from the trade route and cargo density.
2. **Select a block coefficient $C_B$** based on speed and length:
   - Slender high-speed ships → lower $C_B$ (≈ 0.70)
   - Slow, full-bodied capacity carriers → higher $C_B$ (≈ 0.85)
3. **Compute principal dimensions** ($L, B, T, D$) from displacement, $C_B$, and draft limits (e.g., port depth, canal draft).
4. **Layout cargo holds and ballast tanks** — the heart of capacity-carrier design.
5. **Apply CSR scantlings** to plating, stiffeners, and double bottom.
6. **Verify longitudinal strength** — hogging and sagging in still water and waves.

### Structural Hallmarks of a Capacity Carrier

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Structural Hallmarks** (board-favorite topic):

1. **Single deck** with a **long forecastle** and sometimes a **poop deck** aft.
2. **Large cargo holds** (typically **5 – 9 holds**) separated by transverse bulkheads.
3. **Double bottom tanks** (DB) — mandatory under SOLAS XII for ships > 500 DWT carrying high-density cargoes.
4. **Topside tanks (TS)** and **hopper tanks** in cargo region for ballast.
5. **Transverse bulkhead stools** for hatch-end support.
6. **Hatch covers** sized to accept shore-crane grabs (typically **0.5 × B** or more opening width).

### Hold Configuration – "Double Side Skin" vs "Single Skin"

Two principal side-shell philosophies exist:

| Feature | **Single Skin (SS)** | **Double Side Skin (DSS)** |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Side shell | Single plate with stiffeners | Inner + outer skin with corridor |
| Corrosion | Internal compartments | Better protected corridor |
| Inspection | Limited access | Walkable corridor |
| CSR requirement | Allowed for smaller ships | Mandatory for new ≥ 90 m (post-2006) |
| Strength | Weaker at high density cargo | Stronger against lateral pressure |
| Cost | Lower | +5 – 8 % steel cost |

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | – | $\nabla$ = displaced volume |
| 2 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | – | $A_M$ = submerged mid area |
| 3 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{C_B}{C_M}$ | – | Always $C_P \ge C_B$ |
| 4 | Displacement | $\Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla = \rho \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$ | t | $\rho \approx 1.025$ t/m³ (seawater) |
| 5 | Deadweight Ratio | $DWR = \dfrac{DWT}{\Delta}$ | – | Capacity carriers: 0.75 – 0.85 |
| 6 | Lightweight | $LWT = \Delta - DWT$ | t | Steel + outfit + machinery |
| 7 | Still Water Bending Moment (approx) | $M_{sw} = \dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{10}$ to $\dfrac{w \cdot L^2}{8}$ | kN·m | $w$ = weight per unit length |
| 8 | Wave Bending Moment (rule) | $M_w = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | kN·m | $k$ from CSR table |
| 9 | Shear stress (hogging) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | N/mm² | $Z$ = section modulus |
| 10 | Hold/Hatch Width Ratio | $\ell/\ell_H \approx 0.5$ to $0.6$ | – | Hatch narrower than hold |
| 11 | Number of holds (typical) | $n_{hold} = 5$ to $9$ | – | More holds = more pumping |
| 12 | Double bottom height | $h_{DB} = B/15$ to $B/20$ | m | CSR minimum |
| 13 | Hold volume per hold | $V_h = L_{hold} \cdot B \cdot H_{hold} \cdot \eta$ | m³ | $\eta$ = volumetric efficiency 0.85 – 0.92 |
| 14 | Cargo deadweight | $DWT = \sum V_{hold} \cdot \rho_{cargo}$ | t | Iron ore 1.8 – 3.0 t/m³ |
| 15 | EEDI reference (capacity) | $EEDI_{req} = (1 - X) \cdot EEDI_{base}$ | g·CO₂/t·nm | $X$ = reduction factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas drive the *initial design spiral* of any bulk carrier. A naval architect begins with a tender specification, computes $C_B$ from a speed–power estimate, sizes $L \cdot B \cdot T$ from DWT and draft limits, and then lays out the hold-and-ballast arrangement. CSR scantlings come last, because the geometry must converge *before* scantling checks can be made.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Block Coefficient for a Given DWT

Let us derive the principal dimensions of a typical **Capesize capacity carrier** step-by-step. This is a classic KTU-type 14-mark derivation.

### Given
- Required Deadweight: $DWT = 180{,}000$ t
- Cargo density (iron ore): $\rho_c = 2.4$ t/m³
- Service speed: $V_s = 14.5$ knots
- Trial draft: $T = 18.0$ m
- Length-to-Breadth ratio: $L/B = 6.5$
- Breadth-to-Depth ratio: $B/D = 2.2$
- Estimated lightweight: $LWT = 22{,}000$ t
- Seawater density: $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³
- Volumetric hold efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$

### Step 1 – Total Displacement
The total displacement at trial draft is the sum of DWT and LWT:

$$
\Delta_{trial} = DWT + LWT
$$

$$
\Delta_{trial} = 180{,}000 + 22{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta_{trial} = 202{,}000 \text{ t}}
$$

### Step 2 – Displaced Volume
Dividing by seawater density gives the displaced volume:

$$
\nabla = \frac{\Delta_{trial}}{\rho_{sw}}
$$

$$
\nabla = \frac{202{,}000}{1.025}
$$

$$
\boxed{\nabla = 197{,}073.17 \text{ m}^3}
$$

### Step 3 – Assumed Block Coefficient
For a Capesize bulk carrier at 14.5 knots, typical $C_B$ from Holtrop–Mennen and standard lines:

$$
\boxed{C_B = 0.82}
$$

### Step 4 – Express L·B·T Product
By definition:

$$
\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T
$$

Substitute known values:

$$
197{,}073.17 = 0.82 \cdot L \cdot B \cdot 18.0
$$

$$
197{,}073.17 = 14.76 \cdot L \cdot B
$$

$$
L \cdot B = \frac{197{,}073.17}{14.76}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \cdot B = 13{,}351.30 \text{ m}^2}
$$

### Step 5 – Use the L/B Ratio
We have $L/B = 6.5$, so $L = 6.5 \cdot B$:

$$
L \cdot B = 6.5 \cdot B^2 = 13{,}351.30
$$

$$
B^2 = \frac{13{,}351.30}{6.5} = 2{,}053.97
$$

$$
\boxed{B = 45.32 \text{ m}}
$$

### Step 6 – Length
$$
L = 6.5 \cdot B = 6.5 \cdot 45.32
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 294.58 \text{ m}}
$$

### Step 7 – Depth
From $B/D = 2.2$:

$$
D = \frac{B}{2.2} = \frac{45.32}{2.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 20.60 \text{ m}}
$$

### Step 8 – Verify the L/D Ratio
Stern-to-bow strength check:

$$
\frac{L}{D} = \frac{294.58}{20.60} = 14.30
$$

This lies within the typical 12 – 16 range for Capesizes — **acceptable**.

### Step 9 – Total Cargo Hold Volume
Required hold volume from the cargo mass and density:

$$
V_{cargo} = \frac{DWT}{\rho_c} = \frac{180{,}000}{2.4}
$$

$$
V_{cargo} = 75{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

Including hold efficiency $\eta = 0.90$, geometric hold volume must be:

$$
V_{hold,total} = \frac{V_{cargo}}{\eta} = \frac{75{,}000}{0.90}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{hold,total} = 83{,}333.33 \text{ m}^3}
$$

### Step 10 – Distribute Across 7 Holds
A typical Capesize has 7 holds. Volume per hold:

$$
V_h = \frac{83{,}333.33}{7} = 11{,}904.76 \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 11 – Compute Hold Length
Approximating the hold cross-section as $B \cdot (D - T) \cdot \eta$ (above-double-bottom, inside the ship):

$$
A_{hold,xs} = B \cdot (D - T) \cdot \eta = 45.32 \cdot (20.60 - 18.00) \cdot 0.90
$$

$$
A_{hold,xs} = 45.32 \cdot 2.60 \cdot 0.90 = 106.05 \text{ m}^2
$$

Hold length per hold:

$$
L_h = \frac{V_h}{A_{hold,xs}} = \frac{11{,}904.76}{106.05}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_h = 112.26 \text{ m per hold}}
$$

### Step 12 – Total Cargo Hold Length
$$
\sum L_h = 7 \cdot 112.26 = 785.82 \text{ m}
$$

But the ship is only 294.58 m long — this **exceeds the available length**! This is because the assumed 7 holds is wrong; we have over-estimated hold volume by ignoring double bottom, hopper, and tank space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Real capacity carriers use a *short ratio* of hold length to ship length, plus **stiffener heights** consume volume. The final layout is iterative. The derivation above shows the **first-pass sizing logic**; final $L_h$ is shortened, and additional holds (9 instead of 7) may be introduced.

### Step 3.13 – Re-Layout with 9 Holds
If we use 9 holds, volume per hold halves approximately:

$$
V_h^{(9)} = \frac{83{,}333.33}{9} \approx 9{,}259.26 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
L_h^{(9)} = \frac{9{,}259.26}{106.05} \approx 87.32 \text{ m per hold}
$$

$$
\sum L_h^{(9)} = 9 \cdot 87.32 = 785.88 \text{ m}
$$

Even with 9 holds, total hold length is still much greater than ship length — confirming the volumetric excess is from overestimating $A_{hold,xs}$. In practice, $D - T$ (freeboard) is used differently, and double bottom height + hopper side slope reduce the actual cross-section.

### Step 14 – Corrected Hold Cross-Section
Reasonable double bottom height $h_{DB} = 2.0$ m, hopper angle $45°$, gives an effective freeboard minus DB:

$$
h_{eff} = (D - T) - h_{DB} = 2.60 - 2.0 = 0.60 \text{ m} ??? 
$$

This is too small. Re-examining: the *upper hold height* should be from main deck down to top of hopper, not from waterline. The proper hold depth is:

$$
H_{hold} = D - h_{DB} - h_{bilge}
$$

Using $h_{bilge} = 1.0$ m:

$$
H_{hold} = 20.60 - 2.0 - 1.0 = 17.60 \text{ m} ??? 
$$

That is too large. The **ship's depth is measured from keel to main deck at side**, so:

$$
A_{hold,xs}^{correct} = B \cdot H_{hold} \cdot \eta = 45.32 \cdot 17.60 \cdot 0.90
$$

$$
A_{hold,xs}^{correct} = 717.87 \text{ m}^2
$$

Hold length per hold (9 holds):

$$
L_h^{(9)} = \frac{9{,}259.26}{717.87} = 12.90 \text{ m per hold}
$$

$$
\sum L_h^{(9)} = 9 \cdot 12.90 = 116.10 \text{ m}
$$

This fits within 294.58 m comfortably. The remaining length (178 m) is occupied by machinery spaces, forepeak, aft peak, and tank divisions.

$$
\boxed{\text{Hold length per hold} \approx 12.9 \text{ m}; \text{ total cargo length} = 116.1 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic in Step 14:** We corrected the mistake in **Step 11** where we used freeboard (D − T) instead of the actual hold depth (D − DB height − bilge clearance). This is the typical KTU valuation pitfall — examiners love this very correction.

---

## 3.2 Longitudinal Strength — Hogging/Sagging Verification

The **still-water bending moment** in a uniformly loaded capacity carrier (a good first approximation) is:

$$
M_{sw} = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{10}
$$

where $w$ is the weight per unit length (DWT + LWT) / L.

For our Capesize:

$$
w = \frac{202{,}000}{294.58} = 685.74 \text{ t/m}
$$

Convert to kN/m ($1$ t $\approx 9.81$ kN):

$$
w = 685.74 \cdot 9.81 = 6{,}727.11 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
M_{sw} = \frac{6{,}727.11 \cdot 294.58^2}{10} = \frac{6{,}727.11 \cdot 86{,}777.38}{10}
$$

$$
M_{sw} = \frac{583{,}805{,}920}{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{sw} = 58{,}380{,}592 \text{ kN·m} \approx 58.4 \text{ GN·m}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** This is the *static* moment. The *dynamic* wave-induced moment is typically **1.5 to 2.0 times larger** for a Capesize in head seas, so the total design bending moment reaches ~ 100 GN·m. The midship section modulus must satisfy:

$$
Z_{req} \ge \frac{M_{total}}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

with $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm² for high-tensile steel (HTS).

$$
Z_{req} \ge \frac{100 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm}}{175 \text{ N/mm}^2}
$$

$$
Z_{req} \ge 5.71 \times 10^8 \text{ cm}^3
$$

A Capesize's actual $Z$ is typically 7 – 9 × 10⁸ cm³, leaving margin for corrosion and fatigue.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – CSR Scantling Comparison

A summary table comparing the **minimum CSR scantling requirements** for a Capesize vs a Panamax capacity carrier:

| Scantling Item | Capesize (180 k DWT) | Panamax (75 k DWT) | Ratio |
|----------------|----------------------|--------------------|-------|
| Side shell plate thickness | 28 – 32 mm | 22 – 25 mm | 1.30 |
| Inner skin (DSS) thickness | 18 – 22 mm | 16 – 20 mm | 1.12 |
| Bottom plate thickness | 30 – 36 mm | 24 – 28 mm | 1.25 |
| Double bottom plate | 20 – 24 mm | 16 – 20 mm | 1.20 |
