| Hatch cover plate | 25 – 30 mm | 20 – 24 mm | 1.25 |
| Transv. bulkhead plate | 18 – 22 mm | 14 – 18 mm | 1.25 |
| Section modulus $Z$ (m³) | 7.5 × 10⁻³ | 4.0 × 10⁻³ | 1.88 |
| Double bottom height (m) | 2.0 – 2.5 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.20 |
| Hopper tank width (m) | 4.5 – 5.0 | 3.5 – 4.0 | 1.25 |
| Hatch opening ratio $b_H/B$ | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.50 – 0.60 | – |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Midship Section of a Modern DSS Capacity Carrier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UpperRegion[UPPER REGION - HATCH and DECK]
        HATCH[Hatch Opening - 0.55 B wide]
        DECK[Strength Deck - sheer strake, stringer plate]
        COVER[Steel Hatch Covers - hydraulic or pontoon]
    end

    subgraph HoldRegion[CARGO HOLD REGION]
        TOPSIDE[Topside Tank - TSS or TS]
        CORRIDOR[Double Side Skin Corridor - 1.8 m wide]
        HOPPER[Hopper Tank - 45 deg sloped]
        HOLD[Cargo Hold - 7 to 9 holds]
    end

    subgraph BottomRegion[DOUBLE BOTTOM REGION]
        DBPLATE[Inner Bottom Plate]
        DBTANK[Double Bottom Tank - 2.0 m high]
        KEEL[Keel Plate - centerline girders]
    end

    DECK --> HATCH
    DECK --> COVER
    COVER --> HOLD
    DECK --> TOPSIDE
    TOPSIDE --> CORRIDOR
    CORRIDOR --> HOPPER
    HOPPER --> HOLD
    HOPPER --> DBTANK
    DBTANK --> DBPLATE
    DBPLATE --> KEEL

    classDef steel fill:#c0c0c0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef void fill:#fff5e0,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fluid fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class DECK,COVER,DBPLATE,KEEL,HOPPER steel
    class HOLD void
    class TOPSIDE,CORRIDOR,DBTANK fluid
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Capacity Carrier Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tender Specification] --> B[Trade Route Analysis]
    B --> C[Select DWT and Cargo Density]
    C --> D[Compute Block Coefficient CB]
    D --> E[Solve LB and BT product]
    E --> F[Apply L/B and B/D Ratios]
    F --> G[Principal Dimensions L B T D]
    G --> H[Hold and Tank Layout]
    H --> I[CSR Scantling Check]
    I --> J{Converged?}
    J -->|No| K[Adjust Layout or Ratios]
    K --> H
    J -->|Yes| L[Longitudinal Strength Check]
    L --> M[Stability and SE Verification]
    M --> N[Final General Arrangement]
    N --> O[Production Drawings]

    classDef input fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1e90ff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#228b22,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#dc143c,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,C input
    class D,E,F,G,H,I,L,M process
    class N,O output
    class J decision
```

## 4.3 Hold-Loading Sequence (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arrive at Loading Port] --> B[Ballast Tanks Empty - Light Ship]
    B --> C[Ballast Water Discharged to Shore]
    C --> D[Ship Rises to Loadline]
    D --> E[Shore Cranes Load Hold 1]
    E --> F[Load Hold 2 then 3 sequentially]
    F --> G[Trim Adjustment by Transferring Fuel or Ballast]
    G --> H[Load Remaining Holds]
    H --> I[Draft Survey and Stability Check]
    I --> J{Within Loadline?}
    J -->|No| K[Stop Loading - Adjust Trim]
    K --> H
    J -->|Yes| L[Close Hatch Covers and Depart]

    classDef port fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    classDef act fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endnode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B,C,D,I,L endnode
    class E,F,G,H,K act
    class J decision
```

## 4.4 IACS CSR – Bulk Carrier Scantling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Input: L, B, T, D, Cb] --> B[Compute Rule Length Lr]
    B --> C[Compute Rule Breadth Br]
    C --> D[Compute Rule Depth Dr]
    D --> E[Compute Design Wave Pressure]
    E --> F[Compute Shear and Bending]
    F --> G[Determine Net Scantlings]
    G --> H[Apply Corrosion Addition tcor]
    H --> I[Apply Minimum Scantlings]
    I --> J[Gross Scantling = Net + Corrosion]
    J --> K[Verify with FEM Analysis]
    K --> L{All OK?}
    L -->|No| M[Increase Plate or Stiffener]
    M --> G
    L -->|Yes| N[Issue Approval Drawings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU exams:** The CSR scantling flow is the heart of the *Common Structural Rules*. The order Net → Corrosion → Minimum → Gross is the canonical sequence; examiners award marks only when candidates follow this exact pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define a "capacity carrier" and state the two design drivers that distinguish it from other dry cargo ships.**

**Model Answer:**
A capacity carrier is a dry bulk merchant ship whose hull form, structural arrangement, and machinery outfit are optimized for maximum deadweight carrying capacity of homogeneous dry bulk cargo (iron ore, coal, grain).

The two distinguishing design drivers are:
1. **High deadweight-to-displacement ratio (DWR = 0.75 – 0.85)**, achieved through a full block coefficient $C_B$ in the range 0.78 – 0.85.
2. **Large unobstructed cargo hold volume** with hatch openings ≥ 0.5 × B and double bottom height ≥ B/15, enabling fast loading and discharging by shore gantry cranes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Definition (1.5) + two design drivers (0.75 + 0.75).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**List the six structural hallmarks of a capacity carrier as specified by IACS CSR.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Single deck** with long forecastle.
2. **Large cargo holds** (5 – 9 in number).
3. **Double bottom tanks (DB)** — mandatory under SOLAS XII.
4. **Topside tanks and hopper tanks** for ballast capacity.
5. **Transverse bulkhead stools** at hatch ends.
6. **Steel hatch covers** sized for grab loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** 0.5 per correct hallmark.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches, describe the **single-skin** and **double-side-skin (DSS)** structural arrangements used in capacity carriers. Compare their merits with respect to strength, corrosion protection, and CSR compliance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Capesize capacity carrier is to carry 175,000 t of iron ore ($\rho_c$ = 2.5 t/m³). Service speed 14 knots, trial draft 17.5 m, L/B = 6.4, B/D = 2.1, C_B = 0.83, lightweight = 21,000 t, ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³, hold volumetric efficiency η = 0.88. Compute:
(i) Principal dimensions L, B, T, D  
(ii) Total geometric hold volume and volume per hold (assuming 9 holds)  
(iii) The corrected hold length per hold, using double bottom height = 2.2 m and bilge clearance = 0.8 m

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Single Skin vs DSS:**

**Single Skin (SS):**
- One side shell plate stiffened by longitudinals and transverses.
- Holds: typically 5, no walkable corridor at the side.
- Lower steel weight; older design.
- Corrosion of internal frames: difficult to inspect.
- Permitted by CSR for ships < 90 m LOA, or for non-CSR-classed ships.

**Double Side Skin (DSS):**
- Outer hull + inner skin + walkable corridor (typically 1.6 – 1.8 m wide).
- Holds: typically 7 – 9; corridor allows inspection of both skins.
- Higher strength: corridor acts as ring web.
- Mandatory for new bulk carriers ≥ 90 m LOA under IACS CSR (post-April 2006).
- Slightly higher steel cost (~5 – 8 %), justified by improved safety.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Single Skin | DSS |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| CSR compliance | Only < 90 m | Mandatory ≥ 90 m |
| Corrosion inspection | Difficult | Easy via corridor |
| Strength at high density cargo | Adequate | Superior |
| Steel weight | Lower | +5 – 8 % |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

*(Sketch description: an SS section shows a single side shell with a T-section frame; a DSS section shows outer hull, corridor, inner skin, and a continuous longitudinal stiffener on the corridor side.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) Principal dimensions:**

Step 1: Displacement at trial draft
$$
\Delta = 175{,}000 + 21{,}000 = 196{,}000 \text{ t}
$$

Step 2: Displaced volume
$$
\nabla = \frac{196{,}000}{1.025} = 191{,}219.51 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 3: $L \cdot B \cdot T$ from $C_B$
$$
L \cdot B = \frac{\nabla}{C_B \cdot T} = \frac{191{,}219.51}{0.83 \cdot 17.5} = \frac{191{,}219.51}{14.525}
$$

$$
L \cdot B = 13{,}165.82 \text{ m}^2
$$

Step 4: From $L/B = 6.4$:
$$
B^2 = \frac{13{,}165.82}{6.4} = 2{,}057.16
$$

$$
B = 45.35 \text{ m}; \quad L = 6.4 \cdot 45.35 = 290.27 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5: From $B/D = 2.1$:
$$
D = \frac{45.35}{2.1} = 21.60 \text{ m}
$$

**Final:** $L = 290.27$ m, $B = 45.35$ m, $T = 17.5$ m, $D = 21.60$ m.

**(ii) Total hold volume:**

Cargo volume:
$$
V_{cargo} = \frac{175{,}000}{2.5} = 70{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

Geometric hold volume:
$$
V_{hold,total} = \frac{70{,}000}{0.88} = 79{,}545.45 \text{ m}^3
$$

Per hold (9 holds):
$$
V_h = \frac{79{,}545.45}{9} = 8{,}838.38 \text{ m}^3
$$

**(iii) Corrected hold length per hold:**

Effective hold depth (between main deck and top of double bottom):
$$
H_{hold} = D - h_{DB} - h_{bilge} = 21.60 - 2.2 - 0.8 = 18.60 \text{ m}
$$

Cross-sectional hold area (using $B$ and $\eta$):
$$
A_{hold,xs} = B \cdot H_{hold} \cdot \eta = 45.35 \cdot 18.60 \cdot 0.88
$$

$$
A_{hold,xs} = 742.29 \text{ m}^2
$$

Hold length per hold:
$$
L_h = \frac{V_h}{A_{hold,xs}} = \frac{8{,}838.38}{742.29} = 11.91 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answer:** $L_h \approx 11.91$ m per hold; total cargo length = $9 \times 11.91 = 107.2$ m, which is 36.9 % of ship length — well within layout limits.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **SOLAS Chapter XII** requirements for bulk carriers. Explain the rationale behind the "double-side-skin" and "double-bottom-height" rules in the light of casualty history (e.g., the *Derbyshire*, 1980, and *Stellar Daisy*, 2015).

**(b) [7 marks]** A Panamax capacity carrier has the following particulars:
$L = 225$ m, $B = 32.2$ m, $T = 12.2$ m, $D = 19.0$ m, $C_B = 0.82$, lightweight = 11,500 t, DWT = 73,000 t, 7 holds, double bottom height 1.6 m.

Calculate:
(i) The midship section modulus required, assuming wave bending moment $M_w = 2.0 \times 10^6 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ N·mm and allowable stress 175 N/mm².
(ii) The lightship weight per metre $w_{LWT}$ and the deadweight per metre $w_{DWT}$.
(iii) Comment on whether the L/B and B/D ratios are suitable for a Panamax vessel.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — SOLAS Chapter XII:**

SOLAS Chapter XII was introduced in 1997, with major amendments in 2002 and 2004, after bulk-carrier casualties (e.g., *Derbyshire* 1980 — 44 dead; *Stellar Daisy* 2015 — 22 dead, 1 ship lost with full ore cargo).

Key requirements:

1. **Double bottom throughout cargo length**, of height:
   - $h_{DB} \ge B/15$ for ships with $L > 150$ m (or
   - $h_{DB} \ge 2.0$ m minimum for Capesizes).
2. **Double side skin** for ships with $L \ge 150$ m.
3. **Strength assessment of holds** by finite element analysis.
4. **Hatch cover strength** as per IACS UR S21.
5. **Water ingress detection** in cargo holds.
6. **Pumping and drainage** arrangements.

**Rationale:** Casualty analyses showed that single-skin bulkers can fail catastrophically by progressive flooding after a side-shell breach. DSS + DB + hopper tanks ensure that even if one skin is breached, flooding is contained, and the ship can survive the voyage. The *Derbyshire* was lost by side-shell collapse in a typhoon; the *Stellar Daisy* by hull-girder failure — both modes are mitigated by SOLAS XII.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**(i) Section modulus required:**

Wave bending moment:
$$
M_w = 2.0 \times 10^6 \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B
$$

$$
M_w = 2.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 225^2 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 0.82
$$

$$
M_w = 2.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 50{,}625 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 0.82
$$

$$
M_w = 2.0 \times 10^6 \cdot 1{,}337{,}128.50
$$

$$
M_w = 2.674 \times 10^{12} \text{ N·mm}
$$

Required section modulus:
$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_w}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{2.674 \times 10^{12}}{175}
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{req} = 1.528 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^3 = 1.528 \times 10^4 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical check:** $Z_{req} \approx 15{,}280$ cm³. This is *too low* — the formula $2.0 \times 10^6$ typically yields a moment in kN·m, and the result should be in the range $1 - 3 \times 10^5$ cm³ for a Panamax. The exam's coefficient is intentionally extreme to test student alertness to units. **Re-scale the answer by 10⁻⁶ for kN·m → N·mm** if the question's coefficient is in kN·m:

$$
M_w \text{ (kN·m)} = 2.0 \cdot 225^2 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 0.82 / 10^3 \text{ ...}
$$

Use the formula as given; trust the problem statement.

**(ii) Weight per metre:**

Total weight at full load: $W = 73{,}000 + 11{,}500 = 84{,}500$ t

$$
w_{total} = \frac{84{,}500}{225} = 375.56 \text{ t/m}
$$

Convert to kN/m: $375.56 \cdot 9.81 = 3{,}683.74$ kN/m

$$
w_{LWT} = \frac{11{,}500}{225} = 51.11 \text{ t/m}
$$

$$
w_{DWT} = \frac{73{,}000}{225} = 324.44 \text{ t/m}
$$

**(iii) L/B and B/D suitability:**

$$
\frac{L}{B} = \frac{225}{32.2} = 6.99
$$

This is at the **upper end** of Panamax range (5.5 – 7.0). It is acceptable for a long, slender Panamax. Higher L/B improves speed but reduces $C_B$.

$$
\frac{B}{D} = \frac{32.2}{19.0} = 1.69
$$

This is **below the typical 1.8 – 2.4 range** for capacity carriers, meaning the ship is relatively *deep* — good for stability but increases windage area. It is borderline; a B/D of 1.8 would be more typical.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Marks Lost by Most Students):**
> 1. **Using freeboard $(D - T)$ as hold depth.** Freeboard is the *air gap above the waterline*; hold depth is from main deck to top of double bottom. Always use $H_{hold} = D - h_{DB} - h_{bilge}$. *(Lose 1 – 2 marks in part-b derivations.)*
> 2. **Forgetting seawater density in displacement conversion.** Many students equate $\Delta$ directly with $\nabla$. Always: $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$.
> 3. **Confusing $C_B$ with $C_P$.** $C_P$ (prismatic) = $C_B / C_M$. For capacity carriers, $C_P \approx 0.85$ and $C_M \approx 0.96$. Mixing them produces wrong dimensions.
> 4. **Treating cargo volume = DWT.** Volume requires dividing by cargo density $\rho_c$. Iron ore ≈ 1.8 – 3.0 t/m³; grain ≈ 0.5 – 0.8 t/m³; coal ≈ 0.8 – 1.0 t/m³.
> 5. **Ignoring volumetric hold efficiency $\eta$.** Always multiply by $1/\eta$ when computing geometric hold volume from cargo volume.
> 6. **Unit mix-up in section modulus.** Section modulus is usually reported in **cm³** (or m³); bending moment may be given in kN·m or N·mm. Convert consistently.
> 7. **Skipping the "Net → Corrosion → Minimum → Gross" CSR pipeline.** Many students write a single equation for plate thickness; CSR requires the multi-step sequence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacity Carriers (Module 2):**

- **Definition:** Dry bulk ship optimized for maximum DWT of homogeneous dry cargo.
- **Sub-classes:** Handysize → Handymax → Panamax → Capesize; size dictated by canal/port limits.
- **Block coefficient $C_B$:** 0.78 – 0.85 (full-bodied).
- **Six structural hallmarks:** Single deck, long forecastle, large holds, double bottom, topside/hopper tanks, hatch covers.
- **SS vs DSS:** DSS mandatory for new ships ≥ 90 m LOA under IACS CSR.
- **SOLAS Chapter XII:** Double bottom height ≥ B/15 (or 2.0 m for large Capesizes), DSS for $L \ge 150$ m, FEM strength assessment.
- **IACS CSR scantling pipeline:** Net → Corrosion addition → Minimum scantling → Gross scantling.
- **Displacement formula:** $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T$.
- **DWR:** 0.75 – 0.85 for capacity carriers.
- **Cargo density matters:** Iron ore 2.4 t/m³, coal 0.9 t/m³, grain 0.7 t/m³.
- **Hold depth:** $H_{hold} = D - h_{DB} - h_{bilge}$, **not** $D - T$.
- **Hatch opening ratio:** $b_H/B \ge 0.5$ (modern Capesizes reach 0.65).
- **Wave bending moment:** $M_w = k \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ — a memory formula every KTU student must recall.
- **Section modulus $Z$:** $Z_{req} = M_{total} / \sigma_{allow}$; $\sigma_{allow} = 175$ N/mm² for HTS.
- **Number of holds:** 5 (older Panamax) to 9 (modern Capesize).
- **L/B:** 5.5 – 7.0; **B/D:** 1.8 – 2.4.
- **EEDI/EEXI:** All new capacity carriers from 2013 onwards must comply with MARPOL Annex VI energy-efficiency rules.
- **Capesize identity:** Cannot transit Suez or Panama; rounds Cape of Good Hope or Cape Horn.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear Dimension Ships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Linear Dimension Ships # ⚓ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Linear Dimensions of Ships ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Naval Architecture** and **Marine Vessel Design**, the term **Linear Dimensions of Ships** refers to the principal geometric measurements that completely define the size, shape envelope, and volumetric capacity of a ship's hull. These are the foundational parameters upon which every subsequent calculation—stability, resistance, powering, structural strength, and tonnage—is built. The four cardinal linear dimensions of a ship are: 1. **Length (L)** — measured along the longitudinal axis. 2. **Beam (B)** — measured along the transverse (athwartships) axis. 3. **Depth (D)** — measured along the vertical axis. 4. **Draft (T)** — measured along the vertical axis from the waterline downward. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, every linear dimension must be understood with respect to its **reference datum** (molded line, baseline, designed waterline) — a frequent 2–3 mark exam question. ## 1.2 The Five Primary Lengths of a Ship A ship is *not* characterized by a single "length." Naval architects distinguish between multiple length measurements, each serving a unique purpose: | Abbreviation | Full Form | Definition | |:---:|:---|:---| | **LOA** | Length Overall | Maximum length of the ship from the foremost point of the bow to the aftermost point of the stern (including bulbous bow, rudder, and any projections). | | **LPP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Length measured from the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** to the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**. This is the **design length** used in all hydrostatic and resistance calculations. | | **LWL** | Length at Waterline | Length of the ship measured at the **designed waterline (DWL)**. | | **LBP** | Length Between Perpendiculars | Synonym for LPP in KTU textbooks; measures 96% of LWL for typical merchant ships. | | **LCB-related** | Length of CB to CB | Length measured from the forward face of the stem (cant frame) to the aft face of the stern frame. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shipping Container of a Truck" 🏗️ Imagine designing a **cargo truck** to haul the maximum amount of boxes down a highway: - **Length (L)** = How long the trailer is. A longer trailer carries more boxes *but* struggles to turn sharp corners and burns more fuel. - **Beam (B)** = How wide the trailer is. A wider trailer holds more boxes *but* may be restricted by road width and wind resistance. - **Depth (D)** = How tall the trailer is. Taller gives more volume *but* becomes top-heavy and unstable on curves. - **Draft (T)** = How deep the truck's wheels sink into the mud. For a ship, it is how deep the hull sits in water — deeper means more cargo *but* limits which harbors and canals it can use. > [!NOTE] > The **ratios** between these dimensions (L/B, B/T, L/D, D/T) are what *truly* define a ship's **personality** — whether it is a swift naval frigate, a bulky oil tanker, or a sleek container ship. A change of just 0.5 in the L/B ratio can transform the same hull from a passenger liner into a cargo carrier. ## 1.4 Reference Datum Lines (Foundation Concepts) Every linear dimension is measured *between specific reference lines*: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Molded Line:** A theoretical line offset **half the plate thickness** inside the plating. All design dimensions are referenced to this line. It is a *designer's* dimension, not a real physical one. - **Baseline (BL):** Horizontal reference at the lowest point of the hull (keel). - **Molded Base Line:** A line parallel to and above the keel by an amount equal to the **keel plate thickness**. - **Designed Waterline (DWL):** The waterline at the **design draft** (T), under full load condition. - **Load Waterline (LWL):** The waterline at maximum allowable draft. - **Forward Perpendicular (FP):** A vertical line through the point where the **design waterline** meets the **stem** (or in some definitions, the forward side of the stem at the DWL). - **Aft Perpendicular (AP):** A vertical line through the **after side of the rudder post**, or at 96.5% of LWL from FP, depending on the design standard. --- # ⚓ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Linear Dimensions — The "What, Where, Why" Breakdown ### 🔹 2.1.1 Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP / LPP) **What it is:** Distance from FP to AP measured parallel to the **designed waterline**. **Why it is the most important length:** - All **Admiralty coefficients**, **resistance formulas (Holtrop-Mannen, ITTC)**, and **form factor calculations** use LBP. - The **Froude number** $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}}$ uses LBP. - Hydrostatic curves and **Bonjean curves** are tabulated against LBP. **Typical range:** For a Panamax bulk carrier, LBP ≈ 220 m; for a VLCC, LBP ≈ 320 m. > [!NOTE] > **Designer's Rule of Thumb (Statutory):** $L_{BP} \approx 0.96 \times L_{WL}$ for normal cruiser-stern ships. For vessels with a **transom stern**, $L_{BP} \approx L_{WL}$ exactly (since the AP is at the transom). --- ### 🔹 2.1.2 Length Overall (LOA) **What it is:** The extreme end-to-end length including the **bulbous bow, anchors, rudder, and any projections**. **Why it matters:** - Determines which **dry docks, locks, and harbors** the ship can enter. - **Suez Canal** has no LOA limit but Panama Canal's new locks accommodate LOA ≤ 366 m. - **Berthing** and mooring arrangements depend on LOA. --- ### 🔹 2.1.3 Length at Waterline (LWL) **What it is:** The actual length of the hull where it intersects the waterline. This depends on the **loading condition**. **Significance:** - Used in **slenderness ratio** calculations and **wave-making resistance** estimates. - LWL is **always ≤ LOA** and **always ≥ LBP** (for normal ships). --- ### 🔹 2.1.4 Beam (B) — The Molded Beam **What it is:** The **maximum horizontal width** of the hull measured at or near the **midship section** at the design waterline, taken to the molded lines. **Why the beam is critical:** - Determines the **ship's transverse stability** (initial GM depends on B²). - Affects **docking** — the widest dock the ship can fit into. - Beam-to-draft ratio (B/T) governs **course stability and resistance**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Molded Beam vs. Extreme Beam:** The **Extreme Beam** includes the **shell plate thickness** and any projections (sponsons, fenders). Extreme Beam = Molded Beam + 2 × (shell plate thickness). Designers always quote the **Molded Beam** in specifications. --- ### 🔹 2.1.5 Depth (D) — The Molded Depth **What it is:** The vertical distance measured at the **midship section** from the **molded base line** to the **molded deck line** (the top of the strength deck plating, on the centerline). **Key points:** - The **depth** determines the **freeboard** indirectly: $F = D - T$. - D is a **statutory dimension** governed by the **International Load Line Convention (ILLC)** of 1966. - It also defines the **double bottom tank height** and **cargo hold depth** in bulk carriers. --- ### 🔹 2.1.6 Draft (T) — The Molded Draft **What it is:** The vertical distance from the **molded base line** to the **design waterline** at the **midship section**. **Three key drafts to remember:** - $T_F$ — Draft at FP (forward). - $T_A$ — Draft at AP (aft). - $T_M$ — Draft at midship (mean draft). The **trim** is defined as: $\text{Trim} = T_A - T_F$ (positive = trimmed by stern). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Molded"?** Because real ships have a **keel plate** and **shell plating** of finite thickness. The molded line is offset inside the plating so that the **shipyard's cutting templates** are based on clean theoretical dimensions. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 | # | Formula | Description | Typical Units | |:--:|:---|:---|:---:| | 1 | $L_{BP} \approx 0.96 \cdot L_{WL}$ | Length Between Perpendiculars to Waterline Length | m | | 2 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}}$ | Froude Number (where $V$ is ship speed) | dimensionless | | 3 | $C_V = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{L_{BP}}}$ | Speed-Length Ratio (Admiralty coefficient component) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\text{Freeboard } (F) = D - T$ | Freeboard = Depth minus Draft | m | | 5 | $\text{Slenderness Ratio } (L/B) = \dfrac{L_{BP}}{B}$ | Hull slenderness — higher = sleeker | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{Beam-Draft Ratio } (B/T) = \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Hull fullness — higher = fuller | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{Depth-Length Ratio } (D/L) = \dfrac{D}{L_{BP}}$ | Hull depth proportion | dimensionless | | 8 | $\text{Depth-Draft Ratio } (D/T) = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Reserve buoyancy indicator | dimensionless | | 9 | $\text{Longitudinal Center of Buoyancy (LCB)} = \dfrac{\int_0^{L} x \cdot A(x)\, dx}{\nabla}$ | Longitudinal position of CB from AP or midship | m | | 10 | $\nabla = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$ | Displacement Volume (where $C_B$ is block coefficient) | m³ | | 11 | $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement (Weight) | tonnes or kN | | 12 | $\text{Block Coefficient } C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Hull fullness coefficient | dimensionless | | 13 | $\text{Prismatic Coefficient } C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}}$ | Longitudinal prismatic fullness | dimensionless | | 14 | $\text{Wetted Surface Area} \approx 1.7 \cdot L_{BP} \cdot T + \dfrac{\nabla}{T}$ | Approximate (Tudor) wetted surface | m² | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** use `|` for absolute value in tables — KTU valuation scripts flag it. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for clean typesetting. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Linear Dimension Used | Why | |:---|:---:|:---| | **Holtrop-Mannen Resistance** | $L_{BP}$, $B$, $T$ | Standard resistance prediction method | | **Froude Number Scaling** | $L_{BP}$ | Model-to-prototype extrapolation | | **Initial Stability (GM)** | $B$ | $BM = \dfrac{B^2}{12 \cdot T}$ for rectangular approximation | | **Load Line Markings** | $D$ | Statutory minimum freeboard | | **Tonnage (IMO 1969)** | $L$, $B$, $D$ | Tonnage measurement regulations | | **Port & Canal Clearance** | $LOA$, $B$, $T$ | Lock dimensions and depth restrictions | | **Structural Scantlings** | $L$, $B$, $D$ | CSR (Common Structural Rules) | --- # ⚓ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Approximate Relation $L_{BP} \approx 0.96 \cdot L_{WL}$ ### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometric Picture A typical merchant ship with a **cruiser stern** has a waterline that extends slightly forward of the FP and slightly aft of the AP, because the bow and stern are raked (sloped). The waterline length is therefore **longer** than the perpendicular length. ### Step 2 — Define the Ratios Let $\alpha$ = the average slope angle of the stem from vertical. Let $\beta$ = the average slope angle of the sternframe from vertical. The horizontal extension of the waterline beyond FP = $T_F \cdot \tan(\alpha)$. The horizontal extension beyond AP = $T_A \cdot \tan(\beta)$. ### Step 3 — Express $L_{WL}$ in Terms of $L_{BP}$ $$ L_{WL} = L_{BP} + T_F \cdot \tan(\alpha) + T_A \cdot \tan(\beta) $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute Standard Design Values For a typical merchant ship: - $T_F \approx T_A \approx T$ (small trim). - $\tan(\alpha) \approx \tan(15°) \approx 0.27$ for a typical raked stem. - $\tan(\beta) \approx \tan(10°) \approx 0.18$ for a typical cruiser stern. $$ L_{WL} = L_{BP} + T \cdot (0.27 + 0.18) = L_{BP} + 0.45 \cdot T $$ ### Step 5 — Express $L_{BP}$ as a Fraction of $L_{WL}$ For a typical ship with $L_{BP}/T \approx 18$ (a common slenderness ratio for cargo ships): $$ \frac{L_{BP}}{L_{WL}} = \frac{L_{BP}}{L_{BP} + 0.45 \cdot T} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.45 \cdot T}{L_{BP}}} $$ Substituting $L_{BP}/T = 18$: $$ \frac{L_{BP}}{L_{WL}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{0.45}{18}} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.025} = \frac{1}{1.025} $$ ### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation $$ \frac{L_{BP}}{L_{WL}} = 0.9756 \approx 0.96 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{L_{BP} \approx 0.96 \cdot L_{WL}} $$ > [!NOTE] > The 0.96 factor is an **empirical rule of thumb**; exact values range from 0.93 (tankers with strongly raked stems) to 1.00 (transom-stern vessels). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Block Coefficient $C_B$ and its Bounds ### Step 1 — Definition The block coefficient is the ratio of the **submerged volume** $\nabla$ to the volume of a **rectangular box** enclosing the underwater hull: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ ### Step 2 — Lower Bound Analysis For an **ultra-slim needle-shaped hull** (theoretical minimum): $C_B \rightarrow 0$. ### Step 3 — Upper Bound Analysis For a **fully rectangular barge** (box-shaped hull): The underwater hull *is* the box, so $C_B = 1.0$ exactly. ### Step 4 — Practical Ranges for Different Ship Types | Ship Type | Typical $C_B$ | |:---|:---:| | Sailing yacht / racing dinghy | 0.30 – 0.40 | | Naval frigate / destroyer | 0.40 – 0.50 | | Container ship | 0.55 – 0.65 | | General cargo ship | 0.60 – 0.70 | | Bulk carrier | 0.72 – 0.82 | | Tanker (VLCC) | 0.80 – 0.88 | | Tugboat / river barge | 0.85 – 0.95 | ### Step 5 — Why These Differences Exist A higher $C_B$ means the ship has **more displacement per unit length-beam-draft**. This translates to: - Greater cargo capacity (good for tankers, bulkers). - Higher frictional and wave resistance. - Lower required propulsion power per ton-mile. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: The "Personality" Ratios of a Ship Let us define the principal dimensionless ratios and tabulate their effect: | Ratio | Name | Low Value Means | High Value Means | KTU 2024 Exam Hook | |:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---| | $L_{BP}/B$ | Slenderness Ratio | Full, blunt hull — good for slow bulk carriers | Sleek, slender hull — good for fast ships | "Why is L/B high for frigates?" | | $B/T$ | Beam-to-Draft Ratio | Deep-drafted (tankers) — good transverse stability | Shallow-drafted (river boats) — good for restricted waters | "Why do barges have high B/T?" | | $D/T$ | Depth-to-Draft Ratio | Low freeboard — risk of deck immersion | High freeboard — large reserve buoyancy | "Why do warships have high D/T?" | | $L_{BP}/D$ | Length-to-Depth Ratio | Stiff, strong hull (good for heavy seas) | Flexible hull (risk of hull girder failure) | Common 7-mark derivations | | $L_{BP}/T$ | Length-to-Draft Ratio | Slender, fast hull | Blocky, slow hull | Used in powering estimates | ### Example Problem (Modeled for KTU) > **Problem:** A bulk carrier has $L_{BP} = 200$ m, $B = 32$ m, $T = 12.5$ m, $D = 18$ m. Calculate: (i) Slenderness ratio, (ii) B/T ratio, (iii) Freeboard, (iv) D/T ratio. Comment on the ship's expected performance. **Solution:** $$ \text{(i) Slenderness Ratio} = \frac{L_{BP}}{B} = \frac{200}{32} = 6.25 $$ $$ \text{(ii) B/T Ratio} = \frac{32}{12.5} = 2.56 $$ $$ \text{(iii) Freeboard } (F) = D - T = 18 - 12.5 = 5.5 \text{ m} $$ $$ \text{(iv) D/T Ratio} = \frac{18}{12.5} = 1.44 $$ **Comments:** A slenderness ratio of 6.25 is moderate, typical of bulk carriers. The B/T ratio of 2.56 indicates good transverse stability. The freeboard of 5.5 m is consistent with bulk carrier statutory requirements. The D/T ratio of 1.44 provides adequate reserve buoyancy. --- ## 3.4 Displacement & Tonnage Calculation — Worked Derivation Given: A container ship with $L_{BP} = 300$ m, $B = 45$ m, $T = 14$ m, $C_B = 0.65$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. ### Step 1 — Compute the Underwater Volume $$ \nabla = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B $$ $$ \nabla = 300 \cdot 45 \cdot 14 \cdot 0.65 $$ Intermediate calculation: $$ 300 \cdot 45 = 13{,}500 $$ $$ 13{,}500 \cdot 14 = 189{,}000 $$ $$ 189{,}000 \cdot 0.65 = 122{,}850 \text{ m}^3 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Displacement Mass $$ \Delta_{mass} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla = 1025 \cdot 122{,}850 $$ $$ \Delta_{mass} = 125{,}921{,}250 \text{ kg} \approx 125{,}921 \text{ tonnes} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Displacement Weight $$ \Delta_{weight} = \Delta_{mass} \cdot g = 125{,}921{,}250 \cdot 9.81 $$ $$ \Delta_{weight} \approx 1.235 \times 10^9 \text{ N} = 1{,}235{,}285 \text{ kN} $$ ### Step 4 — Final Summary $$ \boxed{\nabla = 122{,}850 \text{ m}^3, \quad \Delta \approx 125{,}921 \text{ t}, \quad W \approx 1{,}235{,}285 \text{ kN}} $$ --- # ⚓ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Principal Linear Dimensions of a Ship ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeS1["SHIP HULL - TOP VIEW"] nodeS2["FP - Forward Perpendicular
Vertical line at design waterline meeting stem"] nodeS3["AP - Aft Perpendicular
Vertical line at after side of rudder post"] nodeS4["LBP = Distance from FP to AP along DWL"] nodeS5["LOA = Maximum extreme length including bulbous bow and rudder"] nodeS6["LWL = Length of hull at the waterline"] nodeS7["Bulbous Bow Projection - extends LOA forward of FP"] nodeS8["Rudder Projection - extends LOA aft of AP"] nodeS1 --> nodeS2 nodeS1 --> nodeS3 nodeS2 --> nodeS4 nodeS3 --> nodeS4 nodeS1 --> nodeS5 nodeS1 --> nodeS6 nodeS5 --> nodeS7 nodeS5 --> nodeS8 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Vertical Section Showing Draft, Depth, and Freeboard ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeV1["VERTICAL LONGITUDINAL SECTION OF SHIP"] nodeV2["MOLDED DECK LINE - Top reference for Depth"] nodeV3["MOLDED BASE LINE - Bottom reference parallel to keel"] nodeV4["DESIGN WATERLINE DWL - Waterline at design draft"] nodeV5["DEPTH D = Vertical distance from base line to deck line"] nodeV6["DRAFT T = Vertical distance from base line to DWL"] nodeV7["FREEBOARD F = D minus T - Exposed hull above water"] nodeV8["KEEL PLATE - Below molded base line by half plate thickness"] nodeV9["RESERVE BUOYANCY = Volume of hull between DWL and deck line"] nodeV1 --> nodeV2 nodeV1 --> nodeV3 nodeV1 --> nodeV4 nodeV3 --> nodeV5 nodeV2 --> nodeV5 nodeV3 --> nodeV6 nodeV4 --> nodeV6 nodeV4 --> nodeV7 nodeV2 --> nodeV7 nodeV3 --> nodeV8 nodeV4 --> nodeV9 nodeV2 --> nodeV9 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Linear Dimension Relations ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA1["INPUT: LBP, B, T, D"] nodeA2["FORM COEFFICIENTS: CB, CP, CM"] nodeA3["SLENDERNESS RATIOS: L over B, L over T, L over D"] nodeA4["DISPLACEMENT CALC: Del = rho times g times LBP times B times T times CB"] nodeA5["STABILITY CALC: BM = B squared over 12 times T"] nodeA6["RESISTANCE: Froude Number Fn = V over sqrt g times LBP"] nodeA7["FREEBOARD: F = D minus T"] nodeA8["TONNAGE: GT and NT per IMO 1969"] nodeA9["STRUCTURAL: CSR scantlings"] nodeA10["OUTPUT: Ship Design Specification"] nodeA1 --> nodeA2 nodeA1 --> nodeA3 nodeA2 --> nodeA4 nodeA3 --> nodeA4 nodeA2 --> nodeA5 nodeA1 --> nodeA7 nodeA1 --> nodeA6 nodeA1 --> nodeA8 nodeA1 --> nodeA9 nodeA4 --> nodeA10 nodeA5 --> nodeA10 nodeA6 --> nodeA10 nodeA7 --> nodeA10 nodeA8 --> nodeA10 nodeA9 --> nodeA10 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Reference Datum Lines Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeR1["REFERENCE LINES IN SHIP DESIGN"] nodeR2["KEEL - Lowest physical point of the hull"] nodeR3["MOLDED BASE LINE - Parallel to keel, offset up by half keel plate thickness"] nodeR4["BASELINE - Reference for draft measurement in shipyard"] nodeR5["DESIGNED WATERLINE DWL - Waterline at design draft"] nodeR6["LOAD WATERLINE LWL - Waterline at maximum permissible draft"] nodeR7["MOLDED DECK LINE - Reference for depth measurement"] nodeR8["FORWARD PERPENDICULAR FP - Vertical through stem at DWL"] nodeR9["AFT PERPENDICULAR AP - Vertical through rudder post or 96 percent LWL"] nodeR10["LENGTH BETWEEN PERPENDICULARS LBP - FP to AP at DWL"] nodeR1 --> nodeR2 nodeR1 --> nodeR3 nodeR1 --> nodeR4 nodeR1 --> nodeR5 nodeR1 --> nodeR6 nodeR1 --> nodeR7 nodeR1 --> nodeR8 nodeR1 --> nodeR9 nodeR8 --> nodeR10 nodeR9 --> nodeR10 ``` --- # ⚓ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define the following terms with neat sketches: (a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP), (b) Length Overall (LOA), (c) Molded Depth (D).** **Model Answer:** **(a) Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP):** It is the distance measured along the **designed waterline** from the **Forward Perpendicular (FP)** — a vertical line passing through the point where the DWL meets the stem — to the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)** — a vertical line passing through the aft side of the rudder post. LBP is the **design length** used in all resistance, powering, and stability calculations. **(b) Length Overall (LOA):** It is the **maximum end-to-end length** of the vessel measured from the foremost point of the bow (including any bulbous bow projection) to the aftermost point of the stern (including the rudder and any other projections). LOA determines which dry docks, locks, and harbors the ship can use. **(c) Molded Depth (D):** It is the **vertical distance** measured at the **midship section** from the **molded base line** (parallel to the keel, offset up by half the keel plate thickness) to the **molded deck line** (the top of the strength deck plating, on the centerline). Depth is a **statutory dimension** governed by the International Load Line Convention (ILLC). > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Students often confuse "molded depth" with "depth to the sheer line" or "depth to the stringer plate." Always quote D to the **molded deck line** at the centerline. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the significance of the L/B ratio and B/T ratio in ship design. Mention typical values for (i) a naval frigate, (ii) a bulk carrier.** **Model Answer:** **L/B Ratio (Slenderness Ratio):** It is the ratio of the length between perpendiculars to the molded beam. A high L/B indicates a **slender, sleek hull** suitable for high speeds (low wave-making resistance). A low L/B indicates a **full, blunt hull** with high cargo-carrying capacity at the cost of speed. **B/T Ratio:** It is the ratio of molded beam to molded draft. A high B/T indicates a **shallow, stable hull** suitable for river/coastal operations. A low B/T indicates a **deep-drafted hull** with good seakeeping in heavy seas. **Typical Values:** | Ship Type | L/B | B/T | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Naval Frigate | 7.5 – 9.0 | 2.7 – 3.2 | | Bulk Carrier | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.4 – 2.8 | The frigate's higher L/B gives it speed; the bulk carrier's lower L/B gives it cargo volume. --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **A.** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the various **linear dimensions of a ship**. Define clearly: LOA, LBP, LWL, B, D, and T. State **which dimension is used in (i) Froude number calculation, (ii) Freeboard calculation, (iii) Transverse stability (BM) calculation.** **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A general cargo ship has the following particulars: $L_{BP} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 13$ m, $T = 9$ m, $C_B = 0.68$. Calculate: 1. The slenderness ratio $L_{BP}/B$. 2. The block coefficient (verify it matches the given value). 3. The underwater displacement volume $\nabla$. 4. The displacement mass (assume $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). 5. The freeboard $F$. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution (a):** The principal linear dimensions of a ship are shown in the sketch below (refer to the diagrams in Section 4). Definitions: - **LOA (Length Overall):** The maximum end-to-end length including all projections. - **LBP (Length Between Perpendiculars):** The horizontal distance from FP to AP measured at the DWL. - **LWL (Length at Waterline):** The actual length of the hull at the waterline in any given loading condition. - **B (Molded Beam):** The maximum molded width of the hull at the midship section. - **D (Molded Depth):** The vertical distance from the molded base line to the molded deck line at midship. - **T (Molded Draft):** The vertical distance from the molded base line to the designed waterline at midship. **Usage:** - (i) Froude Number: $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}}$ — uses **LBP**. - (ii) Freeboard: $F = D - T$ — uses **D and T**. - (iii) Transverse Stability: $BM = \dfrac{B^2}{12 \cdot T}$ — uses **B and T**. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** $L_{BP} = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 13$ m, $T = 9$ m, $C_B = 0.68$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. **1. Slenderness Ratio:** $$ \frac{L_{BP}}{B} = \frac{150}{22} = 6.82 $$ **2. Verification of Block Coefficient:** The formula for $C_B$ is: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ We will verify this once we compute $\nabla$ in the next step. Note: For a general cargo ship, $C_B = 0.68$ is realistic. **3. Underwater Displacement Volume:** $$ \nabla = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B $$ $$ \nabla = 150 \cdot 22 \cdot 9 \cdot 0.68 $$ Step-by-step multiplication: $$ 150 \cdot 22 = 3{,}300 $$ $$ 3{,}300 \cdot 9 = 29{,}700 $$ $$ 29{,}700 \cdot 0.68 = 20{,}196 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{\nabla = 20{,}196 \text{ m}^3} $$ **4. Displacement Mass:** $$ \Delta = \rho \cdot \nabla $$ $$ \Delta = 1025 \cdot 20{,}196 = 20{,}700{,}900 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta \approx 20{,}701 \text{ tonnes}} $$ **5. Freeboard:** $$ F = D - T = 13 - 9 = \boxed{4 \text{ m}} $$ **6. Verification of $C_B$:** $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{20{,}196}{150 \cdot 22 \cdot 9} = \frac{20{,}196}{29{,}700} = 0.68 \checkmark $$ The block coefficient is verified. --- ### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **B.** **(a)** Define the following terms and explain their significance in ship design: **(i)** Block Coefficient, **(ii)** Prismatic Coefficient, **(iii)** Midship Section Coefficient. State the **range of typical values** for each, with one example ship type. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A VLCC tanker has $L_{BP} = 320$ m, $B = 58$ m, $T = 20$ m, $C_B = 0.83$. Calculate: 1. The underwater volume $\nabla$ in m³. 2. The displacement weight in **kN** (use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). 3. The **prismatic coefficient** $C_P$, given that the midship section area $A_M = 2680$ m². 4. The **midship section coefficient** $C_M$, given that the molded beam $B = 58$ m and molded draft $T = 20$ m. 5. Comment on whether this is a slender or full-bodied vessel. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution (a):** **(i) Block Coefficient ($C_B$):** Defined as the ratio of the **submerged volume** of the hull to the volume of a **rectangular box** of length $L_{BP}$, beam $B$, and draft $T$: $$ C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T} $$ **Significance:** $C_B$ measures the **overall fullness** of the underwater hull. A high $C_B$ means the ship carries a lot of cargo per unit of principal dimensions (efficient for tankers and bulkers). A low $C_B$ means a slender hull (efficient for fast ships). **Typical Range:** VLCC tanker: **0.80 – 0.88**. **(ii) Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$):** Defined as the ratio of the **submerged volume** to the volume obtained by extruding the **midship section area** $A_M$ through the length $L_{BP}$: $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} $$ **Significance:** $C_P$ measures the **longitudinal fullness** of the hull. A $C_P$ close to $C_B$ indicates a **parallel middle body** (typical of tankers). A $C_P$ much larger than $C_B$ indicates fine ends. **Typical Range:** Container ship: **0.60 – 0.70**. **(iii) Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$):** Defined as the ratio of the **midship section area** $A_M$ to the area of a rectangle of beam $B$ and draft $T$: $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} $$ **Significance:** $C_M$ measures the **transverse fullness** of the hull. A high $C_M$ (close to 1) means a **full midship** (typical of tankers). A low $C_M$ means a **fine midship** with good seakeeping. **Typical Range:** Naval frigate: **0.70 – 0.80**. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given:** $L_{BP} = 320$ m, $B = 58$ m, $T = 20$ m, $C_B = 0.83$, $A_M = 2680$ m², $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **1. Underwater Volume:** $$ \nabla = L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B $$ $$ \nabla = 320 \cdot 58 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.83 $$ Step-by-step: $$ 320 \cdot 58 = 18{,}560 $$ $$ 18{,}560 \cdot 20 = 371{,}200 $$ $$ 371{,}200 \cdot 0.83 = 308{,}096 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ \boxed{\nabla = 308{,}096 \text{ m}^3} $$ **2. Displacement Weight in kN:** Mass first: $$ \Delta_{mass} = \rho \cdot \nabla = 1025 \cdot 308{,}096 $$ $$ \Delta_{mass} = 315{,}798{,}400 \text{ kg} $$ Weight: $$ W = \Delta_{mass} \cdot g = 315{,}798{,}400 \cdot 9.81 $$ $$ W = 3{,}097{,}981{,}504 \text{ N} \approx 3.098 \times 10^9 \text{ N} $$ $$ \boxed{W \approx 3{,}097{,}982 \text{ kN} \approx 3.10 \times 10^6 \text{ kN}} $$ **3. Prismatic Coefficient:** $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{308{,}096}{2680 \cdot 320} $$ $$ 2680 \cdot 320 = 857{,}600 $$ $$ C_P = \frac{308{,}096}{857{,}600} = 0.359 $$ $$ \boxed{C_P = 0.359} $$ **4. Midship Section Coefficient:** $$ C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{2680}{58 \cdot 20} = \frac{2680}{1160} = 2.31 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical Error Alert:** A $C_M$ cannot exceed 1.0! This indicates the given $A_M = 2680$ m² is **inconsistent** with the $B$ and $T$ values. The correct maximum possible $A_M = B \cdot T = 1160$ m². A valid $A_M$ for a tanker with $C_M \approx 0.98$ would be $A_M = 1137$ m². **Recomputation with a consistent $A_M$:** Assuming $C_M = 0.98$: $$ A_M = 0.98 \cdot B \cdot T = 0.98 \cdot 1160 = 1136.8 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L_{BP}} = \frac{308{,}096}{1136.8 \cdot 320} = \frac{308{,}096}{363{,}776} = 0.847 $$ $$ \boxed{C_P \approx 0.847 \text{ (consistent with a tanker)}} $$ **5. Commentary:** The VLCC has a high block coefficient ($C_B = 0.83$) and a high prismatic coefficient ($C_P \approx 0.85$). This indicates a **full-bodied vessel** with a long parallel middle body — ideal for carrying large volumes of crude oil at moderate speeds. The slenderness ratio $L_{BP}/B = 320/58 = 5.52$ is low, confirming the full form. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones (2-5 marks per mistake):** > > 1. **Forgetting to use LBP in Froude number:** Students often mistakenly use LOA. This gives a wrong $F_n$ and propagates errors into resistance and powering calculations. > 2. **Mixing molded and extreme dimensions:** Always quote and use **molded dimensions** in design calculations. Mixing them leads to wrong tonnage and stability results. > 3. **Sign error in freeboard:** Freeboard $F = D - T$, not $T - D$. Freeboard is always positive. > 4. **Inconsistent units:** When using $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s², the result is in **N**. To get **kN**, divide by 1000. To get **tonnes-force**, divide the mass (in kg) by 1000. > 5. **Forgetting to convert $\nabla$ when computing $\Delta$:** Volume is in m³, density is in kg/m³, mass comes out in kg. > 6. **Confusing $C_B$, $C_P$, and $C_M$:** Each has a distinct geometric meaning. KTU often tests the student's ability to derive one from the others: $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Dimensions of Ships** - **Four Cardinal Dimensions:** Length (L), Beam (B), Depth (D), Draft (T). Always state whether they are **molded** or **extreme**. - **Length Variants:** LOA > LWL ≥ LBP. Use **LBP for design**, **LOA for port clearance**, **LWL for resistance at actual trim**. - **Reference Lines:** Molded base line, designed waterline (DWL), forward perpendicular (FP), aft perpendicular (AP). All dimensions reference these. - **Freeboard:** $F = D - T$. Governed by the **International Load Line Convention (ILLC)**. - **Slenderness Ratio ($L_{BP}/B$):** Sleek → high (7–9); Full → low (5–6). - **Beam-to-Draft ($B/T$):** Shallow → high (3+); Deep → low (2.3). - **Depth-to-Draft ($D/T$):** High freeboard → high (1.5+); Low freeboard → low. - **Block Coefficient $C_B = \nabla / (L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T)$:** Range 0.40 (warships) to 0.90 (tankers). - **Displacement:** $\Delta = \rho \cdot g \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B$. Units: tonnes or kN. - **Froude Number:** $F_n = V / \sqrt{g \cdot L_{BP}}$ — fundamental to model testing and resistance scaling. - **Initial Transverse Stability:** $BM \approx B^2 / (12 \cdot T)$ for a rectangular approximation. $GM = KB + BM - KG$. - **Form Coefficients Relationship:** $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$. - **Statutory vs. Design:** Depth is **statutory** (Load Line), Length is **design** (resistance, powering). - **Tonnage (IMO 1969):** Uses molded L, B, D — independent of form. - **Rule-of-Thumb:** $L_{BP} \approx 0.96 \cdot L_{WL}$ for cruiser-stern merchant ships. - **Examiner's Mantra:** Always sketch the hull, label FP/AP/DWL, then write equations — partial credit is awarded for clear diagrams.

topics.title: Special Purpose Ships. Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions and Form Coefficients. Estimation of Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Special Purpose Ships. Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions and Form Coefficients. Estimation of Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Special Purpose Ships — Formal Definition

In KTU Naval Architecture terminology, **Special Purpose Ships (SPS)** are vessels whose hull form, machinery arrangement, and structural configuration are specifically optimized — rather than generalized — to perform a single dominant commercial, industrial, or military mission. The mission dictates the geometry; the geometry dictates the resistance; the resistance dictates the power. This is the inverse of a "general cargo" ship, which is built as a geometric compromise.

Under the **IMO (International Maritime Organization)** and **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** framework, special purpose vessels are often codified into specialized notations such as:
- **Oil Tanker** (CSR — Common Structural Rules)
- **Bulk Carrier** (CSR-BC)
- **Container Ship** (CSR)
- **LNG/LPG Carrier** (IGC Code)
- **Ro-Ro / Ro-Pax**
- **Chemical Tanker** (IBC Code)
- **Reefer Ship**
- **Drillship, FPSO, DSV** (offshore)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Special purpose ships are introduced not as standalone definitions but as the *context* for selecting **Main Dimensions** and **Form Coefficients**. The mission profile (cargo, route, speed) of an SPS drives every numerical choice in the preliminary design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a ship like designing a **vehicle for a specific sport**:
- A **Bulk Carrier** is like a **mining dump truck** — wide open hold, low power-to-weight, doesn't need to be fast.
- A **Container Ship** is like a **double-decker freight train** — long, boxy, optimized for repeatable stacking.
- A **LNG Carrier** is like a **cryogenic milk tanker** — spherical/independent tanks dominate midship section, motion constraints are severe.
- A **Ro-Ro** is like a **floating garage** — internal ramp, large freeboard, often short and wide.

> [!NOTE]
> The "specialness" of a purpose ship is geometric: it forces the naval architect to choose **non-prismatic, mission-coupled block coefficients ($C_B$)**, slenderness ratios ($L/B$), and beam-to-draft ratios ($B/T$) that a general cargo ship would never use.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Length Between Perpendiculars | $L_{PP}$ | 100 – 400 | m |
| Breadth (Moulded) | $B$ | 15 – 60 | m |
| Design Draft | $T$ | 6 – 22 | m |
| Depth (Moulded) | $D$ | 8 – 30 | m |
| Service Speed | $V_s$ | 12 – 25 | knots |
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | 0.55 – 0.90 | dimensionless |
| Service Deadweight | $DWT$ | 5,000 – 400,000 | tonnes |
| Lightweight | $LWT$ | 10–25% of $DWT$ | tonnes |
| Sea Water Density | $\rho_{sw}$ | **1.025** | t/m³ |
| Fresh Water Density | $\rho_{fw}$ | **1.000** | t/m³ |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Special Purpose Ships (by Mission Geometry)

The mission of a ship is best understood by its **dominant volume occupation** and **operational constraint profile**:

1. **Liquid Bulk:** Oil Tanker, Chemical Tanker, LNG/LPG Carrier
   - Cargo: 100% liquid, free-surface effect critical, sloshing concerns.
   - Typical $C_B$: 0.78 – 0.85
2. **Dry Bulk:** Bulk Carrier (Capesize, Panamax, Handymax, Supramax)
   - Cargo: Homogeneous granular, no tankage but multiple holds.
   - Typical $C_B$: 0.78 – 0.85
3. **Container Ships:** Feeder, Panamax, Post-Panamax, ULCV (Ultra Large Container Vessel)
   - Cargo: Uniform TEU slots, fixed slot dimensions drive beam.
   - Typical $C_B$: 0.55 – 0.68 (slim, fast)
4. **Ro-Ro & Ro-Pax:**
   - Cargo: Wheeled, internal ramps, large clear deck areas.
   - Typical $C_B$: 0.55 – 0.70
5. **Reefer Ships:**
   - Cargo: Temperature-controlled, insulated holds, smaller than break-bulk.
6. **Specialized / Offshore:** FPSO, Drillship, Cable Layer, DSV.
   - Mission: Station-keeping, dynamic positioning; hull form is non-conventional.

### 2.2 Procedure for Selection of Main Dimensions — The Closed-Loop Logic

The selection of **Length ($L_{PP}$)**, **Breadth ($B$)**, and **Draft ($T$)** is NOT sequential in a strict sense; it is a **closed iterative loop** driven by constraints. The logical hierarchy is:

**Step 1 — Define the Mission Requirements:**
- Deadweight required ($DWT_{req}$)
- Service speed ($V_s$)
- Route constraints (Panamax locks: $L \le 294.13$ m, $B \le 32.31$ m; Suezmax: $B \le 50.0$ m, $T \le 20.1$ m)
- Cargo density (oil ~0.86 t/m³, bulk iron ore ~2.5 t/m³ in stowage)

**Step 2 — Initial Dimension Ratios from Mission Statistics:**
Use empirical databases (e.g., **Holtrop & Mennen**, **Schneekluth**, **Taylor's Standard Series**) to seed the ratios:

$$
\tfrac{L_{PP}}{B}, \quad \tfrac{B}{T}, \quad \tfrac{D}{T}, \quad C_B
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Displacement Equation:**
$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot C_B
$$
where $\Delta$ is the displacement (tonnes). This is the **fundamental volumetric identity** of ship design.

**Step 4 — Apply Deadweight Equation:**
$$
DWT = \Delta - LWT
$$
where $LWT$ (Lightweight) is the structural + outfitting + machinery weight, typically estimated as a percentage of $DWT$ or by parametric formulas.

**Step 5 — Apply Resistance & Power Constraint (EHP/BHP):**
Estimate Effective Horsepower using $C_B$, Froude number $F_n = V_s / \sqrt{gL}$, and the appropriate resistance series. Iterate until $EHP \le$ allowable installed $BHP$.

**Step 6 — Stability & Freeboard Constraint:**
Check **GM** (metacentric height), **KB**, and **IMO Freeboard** requirements. Adjust $B$ upward or $D$ upward to satisfy stability without violating draft.

**Step 7 — Iterate Until Converged:**
Re-enter Step 3 with adjusted ratios until all constraints are simultaneously satisfied within engineering tolerance ($\pm 2\%$).

> [!NOTE]
> The classical KTU exam treatment of this procedure emphasizes **Step 3, 4, and 5** as the three "must-solve" equations. Be prepared to write the displacement equation and the deadweight equation as coupled algebraic identities.

### 2.3 Form Coefficients — The Geometric Vocabulary

Form coefficients are dimensionless numbers that completely describe the **fineness** of a ship's hull. They are derived from ratios of areas and volumes of the actual hull to those of a rectangular prism of dimensions $L_{PP} \times B \times T$.

| Coefficient | Symbol | Definition | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Coefficient | $C_B$ | $\nabla / (L_{PP} B T)$ | Overall fullness of the underwater volume |
| Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P$ | $\nabla / (A_M \cdot L_{PP})$ | fullness of the ends relative to midship |
| Midship Coefficient | $C_M$ | $A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | fullness of the cross-section amidships |
| Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{WP}$ | $A_{WP} / (L_{PP} \cdot B)$ | fullness of the waterplane area |
| Longitudinal Prismatic | $C_P$ | as above | — |

**Fundamental Identity:**
$$
C_B = C_P \times C_M
$$

**Typical $C_B$ values by ship type (High-Yield KTU Data):**

| Ship Type | $C_B$ Range | $C_P$ Range | $C_M$ Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tanker (full-form) | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.80 – 0.85 | 0.98 – 0.99 |
| Bulk Carrier | 0.75 – 0.83 | 0.78 – 0.84 | 0.97 – 0.99 |
| Container Ship (ULCV) | 0.55 – 0.65 | 0.62 – 0.72 | 0.90 – 0.94 |
| General Cargo | 0.62 – 0.72 | 0.68 – 0.78 | 0.92 – 0.97 |
| Naval Frigate | 0.45 – 0.55 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.75 – 0.85 |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The selection procedure is the **preliminary design backbone** of every shipyard in the world. At HHI (Hyundai Heavy Industries), Daewoo, and Maersk's in-house design offices, this iterative procedure is encoded into **CAD-integrated design kernels** (e.g., NAPA, FORAN, AVEVA). The form coefficients directly feed into:
- Resistance prediction (Holtrop-Mannen, ITTC 1978)
- Propulsion selection (B-Series or Wageningen B-Wageningen propellers)
- Trim and stability analysis
- Seakeeping (RAO — Response Amplitude Operators)

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Displacement Equation to Main Dimensions

We start with the **Archimedes' Principle** for a floating body. The mass of water displaced equals the ship's displacement (in tonnes, when $\rho$ is in t/m³):

$$
\Delta_{tonnes} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla_{m^3}
$$

For a ship approximated as a rectangular block of length $L_{PP}$, breadth $B$, draft $T$:

$$
\nabla_{block} = L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

The actual underwater volume is a fraction of this block — the fraction is the **block coefficient** $C_B$:

$$
\nabla_{actual} = C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single equation is the **starting identity** of every preliminary ship design problem. KTU exams invariably provide 3 of the 4 unknowns ($L, B, T, \Delta$) and test the fourth.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Coupled Displacement–Deadweight System

By definition:

$$
DWT = \Delta - LWT
$$

Combining with the displacement identity:

$$
DWT = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L_{PP} \cdot B \cdot T - LWT
$$

For a fully-laden design draft, $LWT$ is typically estimated using a **parametric formula** (Watson & Gilfillan being classical):

$$
LWT = K \cdot \Delta^{0.7} \quad \text{(approximate, for early-stage design)}
$$

or, more commonly in KTU 2024 problems, $LWT$ is given directly as a percentage:

$$
LWT = (0.15 \text{ to } 0.25) \cdot DWT
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Analytical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Skeleton):**
> Given: $DWT = 50{,}000$ t, $LWT = 12{,}500$ t, $C_B = 0.80$, $L/B = 6.0$, $B/T = 2.5$, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³
> Find: $L_{PP}$, $B$, $T$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Step 1: Compute $\Delta$:
$$
\Delta = DWT + LWT = 50{,}000 + 12{,}500 = 62{,}500 \text{ t}
$$

Step 2: Express $L$, $B$, $T$ in terms of one variable. Let $T = x$. Then:
$$
B = 2.5x, \quad L = 6.0 \cdot B = 15x
$$

Step 3: Substitute into the displacement equation:
$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T
$$
$$
62{,}500 = 1.025 \cdot 0.80 \cdot (15x) \cdot (2.5x) \cdot (x)
$$
$$
62{,}500 = 0.82 \cdot 37.5 \cdot x^3
$$
$$
62{,}500 = 30.75 \cdot x^3
$$

Step 4: Solve for $x^3$:
$$
x^3 = \frac{62{,}500}{30.75} = 2032.52 \text{ m}^3
$$

Step 5: Solve for $x$ (i.e., $T$):
$$
T = x = (2032.52)^{1/3} = 12.67 \text{ m}
$$

Step 6: Back-substitute to find $B$ and $L$:
$$
B = 2.5 \times 12.67 = 31.68 \text{ m}
$$
$$
L = 15 \times 12.67 = 190.10 \text{ m}
$$

Step 7: Sanity check using the displacement equation:
$$
\nabla_{check} = 190.10 \times 31.68 \times 12.67 = 76{,}293 \text{ m}^3
$$
$$
\Delta_{check} = 1.025 \times 0.80 \times 76{,}293 = 62{,}560 \text{ t} \quad (\text{within } 0.1\% \text{ of target})
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
T = 12.67 \text{ m}, \quad B = 31.68 \text{ m}, \quad L_{PP} = 190.1 \text{ m}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Special Purpose Ship Parameters

| Ship Type | Typical $DWT$ (t) | $L_{PP}/B$ | $B/T$ | $C_B$ | $C_P$ | $C_M$ | $LWT/DWT$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **VLCC Tanker** | 200,000 – 320,000 | 5.5 – 6.0 | 2.4 – 2.6 | 0.82 – 0.85 | 0.83 – 0.86 | 0.985 | 0.10 – 0.13 |
| **Capesize Bulker** | 150,000 – 400,000 | 6.0 – 6.8 | 2.5 – 2.8 | 0.80 – 0.84 | 0.82 – 0.86 | 0.97 | 0.11 – 0.14 |
| **Panamax Bulker** | 60,000 – 80,000 | 6.5 – 7.0 | 2.4 – 2.6 | 0.78 – 0.82 | 0.80 – 0.84 | 0.97 | 0.13 – 0.16 |
| **ULCV Container** | 100,000 – 240,000 | 7.0 – 8.5 | 2.3 – 2.6 | 0.58 – 0.65 | 0.65 – 0.72 | 0.90 | 0.18 – 0.22 |
| **Feeder Container** | 5,000 – 25,000 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.2 – 2.5 | 0.60 – 0.68 | 0.68 – 0.75 | 0.92 | 0.20 – 0.25 |
| **LNG Carrier** | 70,000 – 270,000 m³ | 5.0 – 6.0 | 2.0 – 2.3 | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.72 – 0.80 | 0.92 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| **Chemical Tanker** | 5,000 – 50,000 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.2 – 2.5 | 0.72 – 0.80 | 0.75 – 0.82 | 0.96 | 0.20 – 0.25 |
| **Ro-Ro / Ro-Pax** | 3,000 – 25,000 | 5.0 – 6.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | 0.55 – 0.70 | 0.65 – 0.75 | 0.88 | 0.30 – 0.40 |

### 3.4 Estimation of Weight — Three-Tier Hierarchy

The weight estimation in KTU 2024 Module 2 is structured into three nested categories:

**Tier 1: Lightweight (LWT)**
$$
LWT = W_{steel} + W_{outfit} + W_{machinery}
$$

**Tier 2: Deadweight (DWT)**
$$
DWT = W_{cargo} + W_{stores} + W_{crew} + W_{fuel} + W_{fresh\ water} + W_{ballast}
$$

**Tier 3: Displacement ($\Delta$)**
$$
\Delta = LWT + DWT
$$

**Parametric Estimators (often used in early design):**

For steel weight:
$$
W_{steel} = C_1 \cdot L_{PP}^{1.5} \cdot B \cdot D \quad \text{(Munro Smith approach)}
$$

where $C_1$ is a coefficient depending on ship type:
- Tanker: $C_1 \approx 0.04$ – $0.05$
- Bulk Carrier: $C_1 \approx 0.045$ – $0.055$
- Container Ship: $C_1 \approx 0.05$ – $0.06$

For outfit weight:
$$
W_{outfit} = C_2 \cdot L_{PP}^2 \quad \text{(schematic)}
$$
where $C_2 \approx 0.10$ – $0.20$ depending on outfit complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem says "estimate the lightweight," you are NOT required to derive the parametric formulas from scratch. You are expected to **use** a given formula or a given $LWT/DWT$ ratio and then apply the displacement equation. The marks go to **correct algebraic setup and substitution**, not to memorizing empirical constants.
