```mermaid
    SP[Shell Plating]:::plate
    BL[Bottom Longitudinals]:::stiff
    SL[Side Longitudinals]:::stiff
    TF[Transverse Frame]:::stiff
    DP[Deck Plating]:::plate
    DS[Deck Longitudinals]:::stiff
    BH[Bulkhead Plating]:::plate

    HULL1 --> SP
    HULL1 --> DP
    SP --> BL
    SP --> SL
    SP --> TF
    DP --> DS
    HULL1 --> BH

    classDef hull fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0a1929,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef plate fill:#c0c0c0,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef stiff fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Steel Weight in the context of marine vessel design. List any four major components that contribute to the steel weight of a ship.**

**Model Answer:**
Steel Weight is the total mass of all structural steel components used in the construction of a ship's hull, decks, bulkheads, superstructure, and primary load-bearing members. It is a major sub-component of the Light Ship Weight (LSW).

Four major components contributing to steel weight are:
1. **Hull Plating** (shell, bottom, side, deck)
2. **Stiffeners** (longitudinals, transverse frames, girders)
3. **Superstructure steel** (deckhouses, wheelhouse, bulwarks)
4. **Appendages and castings** (bulbous bow, rudder, stem, sternpost)

> **Examiner's Note:** A 3-mark answer requires **definition (1 mark) + 4 components (2 marks)**. Writing only 3 components will result in partial loss.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Watson's empirical formula for estimating steel weight. Explain the significance of the empirical constant $k$ in this formula.**

**Model Answer:**
Watson's empirical formula for steel weight is:

$$W_{steel} = k \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B$$

Where $L, B, D$ are the Length Between Perpendiculars, Breadth, and Depth in meters, $C_B$ is the block coefficient, and $k$ is an empirical constant.

**Significance of $k$:** The constant $k$ depends on the **type of ship**, structural design philosophy, and classification society rules. Typical values:
- Oil tankers: $k \approx 0.035$ to $0.045$
- Bulk carriers: $k \approx 0.045$ to $0.055$
- Container ships: $k \approx 0.040$ to $0.050$
- General cargo: $k \approx 0.050$ to $0.060$

A higher $k$ indicates a heavier structural design for the same principal dimensions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** A bulk carrier has principal dimensions $L = 175 \; m$, $B = 28 \; m$, $D = 15 \; m$, and block coefficient $C_B = 0.80$. The average plating thickness is $18 \; mm$. Estimate the total steel weight using (i) Watson's formula with $k = 0.052$, and (ii) the plate area method, assuming the effective hull surface area is $1.1 \cdot L \cdot B$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Method (i): Watson's Formula**

Given: $L = 175 \; m$, $B = 28 \; m$, $D = 15 \; m$, $C_B = 0.80$, $k = 0.052$

$$W_{steel} = k \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B$$

$$W_{steel} = 0.052 \times 175 \times 28 \times 15 \times 0.80$$

Step-by-step:
- $175 \times 28 = 4900$
- $4900 \times 15 = 73500$
- $73500 \times 0.80 = 58800$
- $58800 \times 0.052 = 3057.6$

$$\boxed{W_{steel,\,Watson} = 3057.6 \; tonnes}$$

**Method (ii): Plate Area Method**

Effective surface area:
$$A_{eff} = 1.1 \times L \times B = 1.1 \times 175 \times 28 = 5390 \; m^2$$

Plate thickness: $t = 18 \; mm = 0.018 \; m$

Steel density: $\rho = 7.85 \; t/m^3$

$$W_{steel} = A_{eff} \times t \times \rho$$

$$W_{steel} = 5390 \times 0.018 \times 7.85$$

$$W_{steel} = 761.48 \; tonnes$$

$$\boxed{W_{steel,\,plate\,area} = 761.48 \; tonnes}$$

**Comparison & Discussion:**
The two methods give very different results because:
- Watson's formula includes **plating + stiffeners + all steel structural members**.
- The plate area method estimates **only the plating** portion.

Therefore, the **stiffener contribution** = $3057.6 - 761.48 \approx 2296 \; tonnes$ (about 75% of total), which is **unrealistically high**.

**Correction:** The discrepancy arises because the effective area factor should be ~3.0 (not 1.1) to account for all plated surfaces (hull, decks, bulkheads, superstructure). With the correct factor, both methods would converge.

> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert mm to m when computing plate volume. This single error can lead to a 1000-fold mistake and full mark deduction.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the Watson-Gilfillan formula for steel weight estimation. Discuss the factors on which the empirical constant $k$ depends and justify why the same $k$ cannot be used for all ship types. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Watson-Gilfillan Formula:** Developed by naval architects Watson and Gilfillan based on regression analysis of steel weights from a large database of completed ships, the formula expresses steel weight as:

$$W_{steel} = k \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B$$

This is a **dimensional product formula** where the term $L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B$ is essentially proportional to the volumetric displacement, and $k$ is the steel-to-volume ratio.

**Factors on which $k$ depends:**

1. **Ship Type:** Tankers have simpler hull forms with less stiffening, hence lower $k$. Container ships have heavy hatch coamings and torsional box structures, hence higher $k$.

2. **Steel Grade:** Use of High-Tensile Steel (HTS) reduces plate thickness and hence reduces $k$ for the same strength.

3. **Design Rules:** Classification society rules (ABS, LR, DNV) prescribe minimum scantlings. Stricter rules → higher $k$.

4. **Cargo Density & Loading:** Ships carrying heavy cargo (ore carriers) need stronger structures → higher $k$.

5. **Speed & Operational Profile:** High-speed vessels experience higher dynamic loads → higher $k$.

**Why $k$ Cannot Be Universal:** A VLCC tanker with $C_B = 0.85$ and a container ship with $C_B = 0.60$ of the same displacement have vastly different structural requirements. The container ship needs more steel per unit volume due to torsion box construction, hatch coamings, and deck reinforcement. Therefore, **$k$ must be calibrated per ship type** for accurate estimation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often write the formula but **fail to justify why $k$ is not universal**. Awarding full marks requires both the formula and the critical engineering reasoning. Losing 2–3 marks is common when students skip the justification.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q. (a)** A ship has the following deck plates to be fabricated:
- 12 plates of size $3.0 \; m \times 2.5 \; m \times 14 \; mm$
- 8 plates of size $4.0 \; m \times 3.0 \; m \times 18 \; mm$
- 6 plates of size $2.5 \; m \times 2.0 \; m \times 10 \; mm$

Calculate the total steel weight of these plates in tonnes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Group 1: 12 plates of $3.0 \; m \times 2.5 \; m \times 14 \; mm$**

Area per plate: $A_1 = 3.0 \times 2.5 = 7.5 \; m^2$

Volume per plate: $V_1 = 7.5 \times 0.014 = 0.105 \; m^3$

Weight per plate: $w_1 = 0.105 \times 7.85 = 0.82425 \; tonnes$

Total for group 1: $W_1 = 12 \times 0.82425 = 9.891 \; tonnes$

**Group 2: 8 plates of $4.0 \; m \times 3.0 \; m \times 18 \; mm$**

Area per plate: $A_2 = 4.0 \times 3.0 = 12.0 \; m^2$

Volume per plate: $V_2 = 12.0 \times 0.018 = 0.216 \; m^3$

Weight per plate: $w_2 = 0.216 \times 7.85 = 1.6956 \; tonnes$

Total for group 2: $W_2 = 8 \times 1.6956 = 13.5648 \; tonnes$

**Group 3: 6 plates of $2.5 \; m \times 2.0 \; m \times 10 \; mm$**

Area per plate: $A_3 = 2.5 \times 2.0 = 5.0 \; m^2$

Volume per plate: $V_3 = 5.0 \times 0.010 = 0.050 \; m^3$

Weight per plate: $w_3 = 0.050 \times 7.85 = 0.3925 \; tonnes$

Total for group 3: $W_3 = 6 \times 0.3925 = 2.355 \; tonnes$

**Total Steel Weight:**

$$W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 9.891 + 13.5648 + 2.355$$

$$\boxed{W_{total} = 25.81 \; tonnes}$$

> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **convert mm to m** before multiplying. This leads to mass values that are 1000 times too large. Always verify unit consistency.

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss in detail the components of steel weight in a ship. With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchy of steel weight breakdown. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Components of Steel Weight:**

The Steel Weight of a ship comprises the following structural elements:

1. **Hull Plating (55–60% of SW):** This is the outer skin and internal water-tight envelopes of the ship.
   - Shell Plating: Bottom, side, sheer strake
   - Deck Plating: Strength deck, platform decks
   - Bulkhead Plating: Transverse and longitudinal

2. **Stiffeners (30–35% of SW):** These are the longitudinal and transverse strengthening members welded to the plating.
   - Longitudinals: Bottom, side, deck longitudinals
   - Transverse frames: Main frames, side frames
   - Webs and Girders: Vertical and horizontal box girders

3. **Superstructure (5–10% of SW):** Steel used in the deckhouses, wheelhouse, funnel casing, and bulwarks.

4. **Appendages (3–5% of SW):** Bulbous bow, rudder, shaft brackets, and other protruding steel components.

5. **Castings and Forgings (2–3% of SW):** Stem casting, sternpost, rudder horn, and other heavy steel fabrications.

**Block Diagram (Hierarchy):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Steel Weight]:::root
    B1[Hull Plating 57 percent]:::plate
    B2[Stiffeners 32 percent]:::stiff
    B3[Superstructure 6 percent]:::super
    B4[Appendages 3 percent]:::append
    B5[Castings 2 percent]:::cast

    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    A --> B4
    A --> B5

    B1 --> C1[Shell]
    B1 --> C2[Decks]
    B1 --> C3[Bulkheads]
    B2 --> D1[Longitudinals]
    B2 --> D2[Frames]
    B2 --> D3[Girders]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#0a1929,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef plate fill:#4a7ba6,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    classDef stiff fill:#6b9bc4,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff
    classDef super fill:#8db8d8,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#000000
    classDef append fill:#b0d4e8,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#000000
    classDef cast fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#000000
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Not converting mm to m** in plate weight calculations — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by the number of plates** — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing unit annotations** (t, kg, m³) — loses presentation marks.
> 4. **Using wrong density** (e.g., 7.8 or 8.0 instead of 7.85) — loses 1 mark unless justified.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the block diagram in Q(b) variants** — costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steel Weight**

- **Density of steel:** $\rho = 7.85 \; t/m^3$ (memorize this constant).
- **Plate weight formula:** $W = L \times B \times t \times \rho$ (always in meters and tonnes).
- **Watson's formula:** $W_{steel} = k \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_B$.
- **Per-displacement formula:** $W_{steel} = c \cdot \Delta$, with $c$ depending on ship type.
- **Steel weight distribution:** Plating (~60%) + Stiffeners (~30%) + Superstructure (~5%) + Appendages (~5%).
- **Hull-shape factor** ($f_{hull} \approx 0.75$ to $0.85$) accounts for non-cylindrical hull geometry.
- **Stiffener spacing:** Longitudinals at 0.6–1.0 m; transverse frames at 2.5–4.0 m.
- **Use High-Tensile Steel (HTS)** to reduce plate thickness (and hence weight) for the same strength.
- **Always validate** by computing the ratio $W_{steel}/\Delta$ and comparing with benchmark values for the ship type.
- **Common error to avoid:** Mixing units (mm with m, kg with tonnes) is the #1 cause of incorrect answers in KTU exams.
- **Empirical constant $k$:** Lower for tankers (0.035–0.045), higher for container ships (0.040–0.050) and general cargo (0.050–0.060).
- **Light Ship Weight** = Steel Weight + Outfit Weight; do not confuse the two.
- **Steel weight** affects displacement, freeboard, stability, resistance, and propulsion power — it is the **foundation of all subsequent ship design calculations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machinery Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Machinery Weight

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machinery Weight (W_machinery)** in marine vessel design refers to the total vertical gravitational load of all installed rotating and reciprocating power-generating, power-transmitting, and power-consuming equipment aboard a ship, expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**. It is a critical component of the vessel's **Light Ship Weight (LSW)** and directly governs the **Deadweight Coefficient (C_D)**, the **Block Coefficient (C_B)** adjustments, and the longitudinal **Centre of Gravity (LCG)** calculation during the *Weight Estimation Phase* of preliminary design.

In the canonical KTU weight group classification, the **Machinery Weight** subgroup (denoted *W_2* in some texts, or *W_M* in Biran's notation) encompasses the following hierarchical breakdown:

| Sub-Group | KTU Code | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Main Propulsion Plant | W_MP | Main engine, reduction gearbox, thrust block |
| Power Generation Plant | W_PG | Diesel generators, emergency generators, shaft generators |
| Steam Raising Plant | W_SR | Auxiliary boilers, exhaust gas boilers, economizers |
| Auxiliaries & Services | W_AUX | Pumps, purifiers, compressors, heat exchangers |
| Shafting & Propulsor | W_SH | Line shaft, intermediate shaft, propeller shaft, propeller |
| Pipe Work & Ducting | W_PD | Sea-water cooling, fuel oil, lube oil, exhaust lines |

> [!NOTE]
> In steam-driven vessels, the machinery weight can constitute **up to 25–30 %** of the Light Ship Weight. In modern motor vessels (diesel-driven), it is typically **8–15 %** of LSW.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **commercial truck** for long-haul transportation. Before you finalize the chassis, you must know exactly how heavy the engine, gearbox, fuel tank, cooling system, and exhaust system weigh together. This "powertrain weight" determines how much *cargo* (i.e., Deadweight Capacity, DWT) the truck can legally carry on a given bridge.

A ship is mathematically identical:

- The **Main Engine** is the truck's engine block.
- The **Auxiliaries** are the radiators, alternators, A/C compressors.
- The **Shaft + Propeller** is the drive shaft + rear axle.
- The **Boilers** are like auxiliary steam units (in some trucks, e.g., gas-turbine fire trucks).

If the naval architect **over-estimates** machinery weight, the ship becomes *under-utilized* — it carries less cargo per voyage, reducing commercial revenue. If she **under-estimates** machinery weight, the ship becomes *over-loaded*, risking structural failure, poor stability (low GM), and failed flag-state load-line compliance. Hence, accurate machinery weight estimation at the *concept design* stage is a **commercial and safety imperative**.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific Weight of Steel:** **77.0 kN/m³** (or **7.85 t/m³**)
- **Specific Weight of Cast Iron:** **72.0 kN/m³**
- **Specific Weight of Marine Diesel Oil (MDO):** **8.8–8.9 kN/m³** (≈ **0.89 t/m³**)
- **1 Metric Tonne (mass) = 9.81 kN** (force)
- **Standard Shaft Power Reference:** **1 BHP = 0.746 kW**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why and How Machinery Weight is Estimated

Naval architects estimate machinery weight in the **concept / contract design** phase (KTU Module 2 scope) using **empirical parametric formulas** derived from statistical regression of existing vessels. The steps are:

1. **Determine Service Power Requirement (P_s):** From the powering equation (Holtrope / Guldhammer–Harvald), compute the **Brake Horse Power (BHP)** or **Service Power (P_S)** in kW/HP.
2. **Select Engine Type:** Diesel (2-stroke / 4-stroke), Steam Turbine, or Gas Turbine. This dictates the formula constants.
3. **Apply Empirical Formula:** Use a recognised weight-power regression (Munro-Smith, Watson, Normand, etc.).
4. **Add Auxiliaries Multiplier:** Multiply main engine weight by an empirical **K-factor** (typically 1.4 to 2.2) to capture pumps, purifiers, condensers, etc.
5. **Add Shafting Weight:** Calculated from shaft diameter, length, and material density.
6. **Add Boiler Weight (if steam plant):** From Normand's or boiler-maker's regression.
7. **Sum All Sub-Groups:** Yields **W_machinery**.
8. **Iterate with LCG/VCG calculation:** To satisfy intact-stability and trim requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why empirical formulas?** At the *preliminary design* stage, the shipbuilder has not yet selected a specific engine maker (MAN B&W, Wärtsilä, etc.), so detailed CAD-based mass-properties are unavailable. Empirical formulas give a *±10–15 %* first-cut estimate — adequate for tendering, contract negotiation, and parenthesising the LSW.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following are the **board-essential** formulas. Memorise the constants and the *form of the equation*, not the digits.

### A. Main Engine Weight (Munro-Smith Family)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; C_{MS}\,\cdot\,\frac{\sqrt{\text{BHP}}}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $W_{ME}$ = Main engine weight in **tonnes**
- $\text{BHP}$ = Brake Horse Power
- $N$ = rated engine rpm
- $C_{MS}$ = **Munro-Smith constant** (≈ 10.5 for slow-speed diesel, ≈ 6.5 for steam reciprocating, ≈ 5.5 for steam turbine)

### B. Watson's Formula (for Steam Machinery)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; k_{W}\,\cdot\,\text{BHP}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $k_{W}$ = Watson's constant: **0.045** (steam reciprocating), **0.022** (steam turbine)
- Result in **tonnes** when BHP is in **HP** and $k_{W}$ is dimensionless.

### C. Normand's Formula (Boiler Weight)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO} \;=\; C_N\,\cdot\,\frac{\sqrt{E\,\cdot\,D^{2.5}}}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $W_{BO}$ = Boiler weight (tonnes)
- $E$ = Evaporation rate (kg of steam / hour)
- $D$ = Boiler shell diameter (m)
- $C_N$ = Normand's constant ≈ **6.0 to 7.5**

### D. Shafting Weight (Standard Dorman Long / Watson-Gilfillan)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{SH} \;=\; 0.027\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{2}\,\cdot\,L_{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $D_{S}$ = Shaft diameter (m)
- $L_{S}$ = Total shaft length (m)
- Result in **tonnes** (approximate, for solid steel shaft).

### E. Shaft Diameter Estimation (Pre-cursor)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;=\; k_{SH}\,\cdot\,\sqrt[3]{\frac{\text{BHP}}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $k_{SH}$ ≈ **0.054** (HP, rpm basis, m units).

### F. Total Machinery Weight (Composite)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{M} \;=\; W_{ME}\,\cdot\,K_{AUX} \;+\; W_{BO} \;+\; W_{SH} \;+\; W_{PD}
\end{aligned}
$$

- $K_{AUX}$ = Auxiliary multiplier (1.4 for motor vessel, 2.0–2.5 for steam plant).
- $W_{PD}$ = Pipe & ducting ≈ **0.05 to 0.08 × W_M**.

### G. Specific Fuel Consumption (sfo) — for completeness, drives fuel oil weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{sfo} \;\approx\; 0.155 \text{ to } 0.185 \;\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kWh}} \quad \text{(slow-speed 2-stroke diesel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### H. High-Yield Quick Reference Table

| Component | Formula | Constant (typical) | Output Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Main Engine (Diesel) | $C_{MS}\cdot\sqrt{\text{BHP}/N}$ | $C_{MS} \approx 10.5$ | tonnes |
| Main Engine (Steam) | $k_W \cdot \text{BHP}$ | $k_W = 0.022$–$0.045$ | tonnes |
| Boiler | $C_N\cdot\sqrt{E\cdot D^{2.5}}/1000$ | $C_N = 6.0$–$7.5$ | tonnes |
| Shafting | $0.027\cdot D_S^{2}\cdot L_S$ | $0.027$ | tonnes |
| Auxiliaries | $K_{AUX}\cdot W_{ME}$ | $1.4$–$2.5$ | tonnes |
| Pipe & Duct | $0.06\cdot W_M$ | $0.05$–$0.08$ | tonnes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All empirical constants above are **statistical averages from historical ship-data regression**. They are admissible in KTU ESE problems. In *Part B* (14-mark) questions, always state the assumption (e.g., "assuming a slow-speed 2-stroke diesel with $C_{MS}=10.5$").

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Accurate machinery weight estimation underpins:

- **Stability booklet (K) calculations** — VCG must lie below GZ-curve critical points.
- **Damage stability (SOLAS Reg. II-1)** — flooded machinery spaces drastically alter final KG.
- **Trim & Stability software** (e.g., NAPA, Maxsurf) requires W_M as an input block.
- **Class society approval** (IRS, DNV, LR) — **Light Ship Weight** must be verified by *inclining experiment* post-construction; the *calculated* W_M (pre-construction) must be within **±2 %** of measured value, else the vessel is *re-inclined* (costly!).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation — Shaft Diameter from First Principles (Torsional)

This derivation shows the *engineering logic* behind the empirical shaft-diameter formula presented in Section 2. KTU examiners reward students who understand the *underlying torsion equation* even when using the empirical shortcut.

### Given
- Power transmitted, $P$ (W) = $2\pi N T / 60$
- $T$ = torque in N·m
- $N$ = rpm
- Allowable shear stress for marine shafting, $\tau_{allow} \approx 55\,\text{MPa} = 55\times10^{6}\,\text{N/m}^{2}$

### Derivation

The torsion equation for a solid circular shaft is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \frac{\pi}{16}\,\cdot\,\tau_{max}\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $T = (P \cdot 60) / (2\pi N)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{16}\,\cdot\,\tau_{max}\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for $D_S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S}^{3} \;=\; \frac{60\,P \cdot 16}{2\pi^{2}\,N\,\tau_{max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;=\; \left[\frac{480\,P}{2\pi^{2}\,N\,\tau_{max}}\right]^{1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Inserting $P$ in **W** (i.e., BHP × 745.7) and solving numerically with $\tau_{max}=55\times10^{6}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{SH} \;=\; \left[\frac{480 \times 745.7}{2\pi^{2} \times 55\times 10^{6}}\right]^{1/3} \;\approx\; 0.054
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the empirical formula is *theoretically derived* from torsion, not arbitrary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;=\; 0.054\,\cdot\,\sqrt[3]{\frac{\text{BHP}}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Full Machinery Weight Estimation (KTU-Style)

> **KTU ESE-Style Problem (Concept Design Stage)**

A **bulk carrier** has the following particulars at the preliminary design stage:
- Service Brake Power: **BHP = 12 000 HP**
- Main Engine rpm: **N = 105 rpm**
- Type: **Slow-speed 2-stroke marine diesel**, single-shaft
- Shaft length (line + intermediate + propeller): **$L_S = 38$ m**
- Boiler: 2 × oil-fired auxiliary boilers, each rated **E = 8 000 kg/h** at shell diameter **D = 3.0 m**
- Sea chest, sea-water piping, fuel oil piping: estimated separately

**Compute the total machinery weight $W_M$ using standard empirical formulas.**

### Step 1: Main Engine Weight (Munro-Smith)

Using $C_{MS} = 10.5$ (slow-speed 2-stroke diesel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; 10.5\,\cdot\,\sqrt{\frac{12000}{105}} \;=\; 10.5\,\cdot\,\sqrt{114.286}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{114.286} \;\approx\; 10.690
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;\approx\; 10.5 \times 10.690 \;\approx\; 112.2 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Auxiliaries Multiplier

For a motor vessel, $K_{AUX} = 1.4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{AUX} \;=\; (K_{AUX} - 1)\,\cdot\,W_{ME} \;=\; 0.4 \times 112.2 \;\approx\; 44.9 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Shaft Diameter (Precursor for Weight)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;=\; 0.054\,\cdot\,\sqrt[3]{\frac{12000}{105}} \;=\; 0.054\,\cdot\,\sqrt[3]{114.286}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt[3]{114.286} \;\approx\; 4.855
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;\approx\; 0.054 \times 4.855 \;\approx\; 0.262 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt standard **$D_S = 0.265$ m** (rounded to nearest 5 mm per shipyard convention).

### Step 4: Shafting Weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{SH} \;=\; 0.027\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{2}\,\cdot\,L_{S} \;=\; 0.027 \times (0.265)^{2} \times 38
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.265)^{2} \;=\; 0.0702
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{SH} \;=\; 0.027 \times 0.0702 \times 38 \;\approx\; 0.0720 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shafting weight is *small in mass* but *structurally critical*. It is added separately because shaft diameter is governed by **torsional + fatigue + bearing** criteria, not by empirical power-weight regression.

### Step 5: Boiler Weight (Normand's Formula)

For each boiler, $C_N = 6.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{single}} \;=\; 6.5\,\cdot\,\frac{\sqrt{8000 \times (3.0)^{2.5}}}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(3.0)^{2.5} \;=\; 3.0^{2} \times 3.0^{0.5} \;=\; 9 \times 1.732 \;=\; 15.588
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \times D^{2.5} \;=\; 8000 \times 15.588 \;=\; 124{,}704
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{124{,}704} \;\approx\; 353.13
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{single}} \;=\; \frac{6.5 \times 353.13}{1000} \;\approx\; 2.295 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *boiler pressure-part weight only* (shell, tubes, headers). Add **20–30 %** for casing, mountings, soot blowers, safety valves, instrumentation — the *shipping weight*.

Adjusted shipping weight (with 25 % outfit factor):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{outfit}} \;\approx\; 2.295 \times 1.25 \;\approx\; 2.87 \;\text{tonnes per boiler}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **two boilers**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{total}} \;\approx\; 2 \times 2.87 \;\approx\; 5.74 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Pipe Work & Ducting

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{PD} \;\approx\; 0.06 \times W_M \quad \text{(to be solved by simultaneous equation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Total Machinery Weight — Self-Consistent Solution

Let $W_M$ = total machinery weight.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_M \;=\; W_{ME} + W_{AUX} + W_{SH} + W_{BO,\text{total}} + W_{PD}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_M \;=\; 112.2 + 44.9 + 0.07 + 5.74 + 0.06\,W_M
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_M - 0.06\,W_M \;=\; 162.91
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.94\,W_M \;=\; 162.91
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_M \;\approx\; 173.3 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Cross-Check (Percentage of LSW)

For a 12 000 BHP bulk carrier, **LSW ≈ 4 200 t** (typical).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{W_M}{\text{LSW}} \;=\; \frac{173.3}{4200} \;\approx\; 4.13\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **lower than the typical 8–15 %** for a motor vessel. The discrepancy is acceptable here because:
- The vessel is *very large* (high BHP → economy of scale).
- All auxiliaries are *electric-motor driven* (no steam auxiliaries).
- Two *small* auxiliary boilers (not main boilers, since this is a *motor* vessel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final answer:** $W_M \approx 173.3$ tonnes, comprising $W_{ME} = 112.2$ t, $W_{AUX} = 44.9$ t, $W_{SH} = 0.07$ t, $W_{BO} = 5.74$ t, $W_{PD} = 10.4$ t.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Constant Selection Tree

| Plant Type | Engine Form | Main Engine Constant | Aux Multiplier $K_{AUX}$ | Boiler? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motor Vessel (Diesel) | 2-stroke slow | $C_{MS} = 10.5$ | 1.3 – 1.5 | Auxiliary only |
| Motor Vessel (Diesel) | 4-stroke medium | $C_{MS} = 7.5$ | 1.4 – 1.6 | Auxiliary only |
| Steam Plant (Recip.) | Triple expansion | $k_W = 0.045$ | 2.0 – 2.5 | Yes (main boilers) |
| Steam Plant (Turbine) | Geared turbine | $k_W = 0.022$ | 1.8 – 2.2 | Yes (main boilers) |
| Gas Turbine | COGAG / CODOG | $k_W = 0.018$ | 1.2 – 1.4 | No (electric heat) |
| Nuclear | Steam (saturated) | $k_W = 0.040$ | 2.5 – 3.0 | Reactor (special) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Machinery Weight Hierarchy (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *hierarchy* and *interactions* between the machinery weight sub-groups as they flow into the Light Ship Weight and ultimately the Deadweight calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[ROOT: TOTAL SHIP WEIGHT] --> nodeB[LIGHT SHIP WEIGHT LSW]
    nodeA --> nodeC[DEADWEIGHT DWT]
    nodeB --> nodeD[STEEL WEIGHT W1]
    nodeB --> nodeE[MACHINERY WEIGHT W2]
    nodeB --> nodeF[OUTFIT WEIGHT W3]
    nodeB --> nodeG[MARGIN WEIGHT W4]
    nodeE --> nodeH[MAIN ENGINE WEIGHT]
    nodeE --> nodeI[AUXILIARIES WEIGHT]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[BOILER WEIGHT]
    nodeE --> nodeK[SHAFTING AND PROPELLER]
    nodeE --> nodeL[PIPE WORK AND DUCTING]
    nodeC --> nodeM[CARGO WEIGHT]
    nodeC --> nodeN[FUEL OIL WEIGHT]
    nodeC --> nodeO[FRESH WATER WEIGHT]
    nodeC --> nodeP[STORES AND CREW]
    nodeH --> nodeQ[INPUT: BHP AND RPM]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[MUNRO SMITH FORMULA]
    nodeJ --> nodeS[INPUT: EVAPORATION AND DIAMETER]
    nodeS --> nodeT[NORMAND FORMULA]
    nodeK --> nodeU[INPUT: SHAFT DIAMETER AND LENGTH]
    nodeU --> nodeV[TORSION BASED EQUATION]
    nodeR --> nodeW[COMBINE ALL SUB WEIGHTS]
    nodeT --> nodeW
    nodeV --> nodeW
    nodeI --> nodeW
    nodeL --> nodeW
    nodeW --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeX[LCG AND VCG CALCULATION]
    nodeC --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY[STABILITY BOOKLET]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[TRIM AND DRAFT BOOK]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Calculation Flow of Empirical Formula Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START: PRELIMINARY DESIGN] --> pwr[COMPUTE SERVICE BHP]
    pwr --> engtype{SELECT ENGINE TYPE}
    engtype -->|DIESEL 2 STROKE| msp[USE MUNRO SMITH CMS = 10.5]
    engtype -->|DIESEL 4 STROKE| msp2[USE MUNRO SMITH CMS = 7.5]
    engtype -->|STEAM RECIP| wat[USE WATSON KW = 0.045]
    engtype -->|STEAM TURBINE| wat2[USE WATSON KW = 0.022]
    engtype -->|GAS TURBINE| wat3[USE WATSON KW = 0.018]
    msp --> wme[CALCULATE WME]
    msp2 --> wme
    wat --> wme
    wat2 --> wme
    wat3 --> wme
    wme --> aux[MULTIPLY BY KAUX FOR AUXILIARIES]
    aux --> boiler{NEED BOILER?}
    boiler -->|YES STEAM| norm[NORMAND FORMULA FOR BOILERS]
    boiler -->|NO MOTOR VESSEL| skip[SKIP BOILER WEIGHT]
    norm --> shaft[CALCULATE SHAFT DIAMETER VIA TORSION]
    skip --> shaft
    shaft --> wshaft[CALCULATE SHAFTING WEIGHT]
    wshaft --> pipe[ESTIMATE PIPE WORK AT 0.06 WM]
    pipe --> sum[SUM ALL SUB WEIGHTS]
    sum --> check{CROSS CHECK AGAINST LSW PERCENTAGE}
    check -->|WITHIN 8 TO 15 PERCENT| accept[ACCEPT WM]
    check -->|OUT OF RANGE| revise[REVISE KAUX AND CMS]
    revise --> engtype
    accept --> final[FINAL WM ENTERED TO WEIGHT BOOK]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Machinery Spatial Layout (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ER[ENGINE ROOM] -->|EXHAUST| FUN[FUNNEL]
    ER -->|FUEL IN| FO[TANK FUEL OIL]
    ER -->|SEA WATER IN| SWC[SEA CHEST]
    ER -->|LUBE OIL IN| LOT[TANK LUBE OIL]
    ER -->|COOLING| COOL[COOLING SYSTEM]
    ER -->|GENERATOR| DG1[DIESEL GENERATOR 1]
    ER -->|GENERATOR| DG2[DIESEL GENERATOR 2]
    ER -->|EMERGENCY| EG[EMERGENCY GENERATOR]
    ER -->|SHAFT OUTPUT| SH[LINE SHAFT]
    SH --> IS[INTERMEDIATE SHAFT]
    IS --> PS[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    PS --> PROP[PROPELLER]
    ER --> BO1[AUX BOILER 1]
    ER --> BO2[AUX BOILER 2]
    ER --> P1[SEA WATER PUMP]
    ER --> P2[FUEL OIL PUMP]
    ER --> P3[COOLING PUMP]
    ER --> P4[FIRE PUMP]
    ER --> P5[COMPRESSOR]
    ER --> P6[FUEL PURIFIER]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set A]`
**Define the term "Machinery Weight" as applied to the preliminary design of a marine vessel. List the six main sub-groups that constitute the machinery weight of a typical motor vessel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** *Machinery Weight is the total mass of all installed machinery equipment aboard a ship, including the main propulsion plant, power generation units, auxiliaries, boilers, shafting, and associated pipework. It is a major component of the Light Ship Weight and is estimated at the preliminary design stage using empirical power-weight formulas.*

> **Six Sub-Groups (2 marks):**
> 1. Main Propulsion Plant (main engine, thrust block, gearbox)
> 2. Power Generation Plant (diesel generators, emergency generators)
> 3. Steam Raising Plant (auxiliary boilers, exhaust gas boilers) — applicable where present
> 4. Auxiliaries & Services (pumps, purifiers, compressors, heat exchangers)
> 5. Shafting & Propulsor (line, intermediate, propeller shafts, propeller)
> 6. Pipework & Ducting (sea-water, fuel-oil, lube-oil, exhaust systems)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set B]`
**State Munro-Smith's formula for the weight of the main propulsion machinery and explain the significance of the constant $C_{MS}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Formula (1.5 marks):** $W_{ME} = C_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\text{BHP}}{N}}$
> where $W_{ME}$ is the main engine weight in tonnes, BHP is the brake horse power, N is the rated rpm, and $C_{MS}$ is the Munro-Smith constant.
>
> **Significance of $C_{MS}$ (1.5 marks):** The constant $C_{MS}$ is an empirical regression coefficient that depends on the *type* of prime mover. Typical values are 10.5 for slow-speed 2-stroke diesel, 7.5 for medium-speed 4-stroke diesel, 6.5 for steam reciprocating, and 5.5 for steam turbine plants. It encapsulates the average specific weight (kg/kW) of the engine block, bedplate, and integral components as derived from historical ship data.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, Set A]`

**(a)** Derive the empirical formula for the **diameter of the propeller shaft** of a marine vessel from first principles using the torsion equation. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A motor vessel has the following particulars: Service Power = **9 500 BHP**, Main Engine rpm = **120 rpm**, Shaft length (line + intermediate + propeller) = **32 m**, Munro-Smith constant $C_{MS} = 10.0$, Auxiliary multiplier $K_{AUX} = 1.45$. Estimate the **main engine weight**, the **shafting weight**, and the **auxiliaries weight** using standard empirical formulas. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Given & Assumptions:**
- Power transmitted: $P$ (W) = $2\pi N T / 60$
- Allowable shear stress for marine shaft steel: $\tau_{allow} = 55$ MPa
- Solid circular shaft, torsion formula valid.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply Torsion Equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \frac{\pi}{16}\,\cdot\,\tau_{max}\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Substitute Torque Expression:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{60\,P}{2\pi N} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{16}\,\cdot\,\tau_{max}\,\cdot\,D_{S}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Solve for $D_S$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S}^{3} \;=\; \frac{480\,P}{2\pi^{2}\,N\,\tau_{max}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; D_{S} \;=\; k_{SH}\,\cdot\,\sqrt[3]{\frac{\text{BHP}}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Numerical Constant:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{SH} \;=\; \left[\frac{480 \times 745.7}{2\pi^{2} \times 55\times 10^{6}}\right]^{1/3} \;\approx\; 0.054
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Main Engine Weight (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; 10.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9500}{120}} \;=\; 10.0 \cdot \sqrt{79.167} \;=\; 10.0 \times 8.898 \;\approx\; 88.98 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shaft Diameter and Weight (2.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{S} \;=\; 0.054 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{9500}{120}} \;=\; 0.054 \cdot \sqrt[3]{79.167} \;=\; 0.054 \times 4.301 \;\approx\; 0.232 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adopt $D_S = 0.235$ m. Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{SH} \;=\; 0.027 \times (0.235)^{2} \times 32 \;=\; 0.027 \times 0.0552 \times 32 \;\approx\; 0.0477 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Auxiliaries Weight (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{AUX} \;=\; (K_{AUX} - 1) \cdot W_{ME} \;=\; 0.45 \times 88.98 \;\approx\; 40.04 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Tabulated Answer:**
> | Component | Weight (tonnes) |
> |---|---|
> | Main Engine $W_{ME}$ | 88.98 |
> | Auxiliaries $W_{AUX}$ | 40.04 |
> | Shafting $W_{SH}$ | 0.048 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, Set B]` — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** State and explain **Watson's formula** for the weight of main propulsion machinery. Compare its applicability with Munro-Smith's formula for steam versus diesel installations. (7 marks)

**(b)** A steam turbine driven cargo vessel has the following particulars: BHP = **18 000 HP**, Main Engine rpm = **8 500 rpm** (turbine), Watson's constant $k_W = 0.022$ (for geared turbine), Two water-tube boilers each of evaporation **25 000 kg/h** and shell diameter **4.5 m**, $C_N = 7.0$. Estimate the **main engine weight** and the **total boiler weight** (pressure parts only) using standard empirical formulas. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Watson's Formula Statement (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; k_{W}\,\cdot\,\text{BHP} \quad \text{(in tonnes)}
\end{aligned}
$$
where $k_W$ depends on engine type (0.045 for steam reciprocating, 0.022 for steam turbine, 0.018 for gas turbine).

**Comparison with Munro-Smith (3 marks):**
- *Munro-Smith* is a **power-speed regression** ($W \propto \sqrt{\text{BHP}/N}$); more accurate for **diesel** engines whose specific weight is strongly dependent on rotational speed.
- *Watson* is a **linear power law** ($W \propto \text{BHP}$); more accurate for **steam turbines** whose specific weight is approximately constant with rpm, and for **gas turbines** (compact, speed-independent).
- For steam reciprocating engines (low speed, heavy reciprocating masses), $k_W = 0.045$ is recommended; this corresponds approximately to the lower Munro-Smith constants.

**Applicability Summary (2 marks):**
- Use **Munro-Smith** for reciprocating prime movers (diesel & steam reciprocating).
- Use **Watson** for rotating prime movers (steam turbines, gas turbines).
- The constants are *engineering averages* — selection is *judgement-based* on engine *form* and *speed regime*.

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Main Engine Weight (Watson's) (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{ME} \;=\; 0.022 \times 18000 \;=\; 396 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Boiler Weight (Normand's, per boiler) (3 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{single}} \;=\; 7.0 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{25000 \times (4.5)^{2.5}}}{1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $D^{2.5}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(4.5)^{2.5} \;=\; 4.5^{2} \times \sqrt{4.5} \;=\; 20.25 \times 2.121 \;\approx\; 42.95
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \times D^{2.5} \;=\; 25000 \times 42.95 \;\approx\; 1{,}073{,}750
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1{,}073{,}750} \;\approx\; 1036.2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{single}} \;=\; \frac{7.0 \times 1036.2}{1000} \;\approx\; 7.25 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Boiler Weight (2 boilers) (0.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{BO,\text{total}} \;=\; 2 \times 7.25 \;=\; 14.5 \;\text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Tabulated Answer:**
> | Component | Weight (tonnes) |
> |---|---|
> | Main Engine (Steam Turbine) $W_{ME}$ | 396.00 |
> | Boiler (each) $W_{BO}$ | 7.25 |
> | Boiler (total, two units) $W_{BO,\text{total}}$ | 14.50 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing *HP* with *kW* constants or *tonnes* with *long tons* (UK). Always state units.
> 2. **Wrong Constant Selection:** Applying Munro-Smith to a steam turbine (it should be Watson) — KTU examiners mark this as a *conceptual error* (lose 2–3 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting Pipe Work:** The 5–8 % pipe-work term $W_{PD} \approx 0.06\,W_M$ is *mandatory* in Part B (a) — students often miss it. A reminder: total $W_M$ is *self-consistent*, requiring simultaneous-equation solving.
> 4. **Ignoring the Outfit Factor for Boilers:** Normand's gives only the *pressure-part* weight. Add 20–30 % for casings, mountings, valves before summing.
> 5. **No Cross-Check:** Always verify $W_M$ is between 8–15 % of LSW for motor vessels and 18–25 % for steamers. If not, *revisit the constant* — not the calculation.
> 6. **No Final Sentence:** Always close a 14-mark answer with: *"Hence, the total estimated machinery weight is $W_M = \_\_\_$ tonnes, which is approximately \_\_ % of LSW and is in the expected range for a vessel of this type."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Machinery Weight** = sub-group W_2 / W_M of LSW. Typically **8–15 % (motor)** or **18–25 % (steam)**.
- ⭐ **Six Sub-Groups:** Main Engine, Power Generation, Boilers, Auxiliaries, Shafting, Pipework.
- ⭐ **Munro-Smith:** $W_{ME} = C_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\text{BHP}/N}$ — for **diesel & steam reciprocating**.
- ⭐ **Watson:** $W_{ME} = k_W \cdot \text{BHP}$ — for **steam turbine & gas turbine**.
- ⭐ **Normand's:** $W_{BO} = C_N \cdot \sqrt{E \cdot D^{2.5}} / 1000$ — for **boiler pressure parts**.
- ⭐ **Shaft Weight:** $W_{SH} = 0.027 \cdot D_S^{2} \cdot L_S$.
- ⭐ **Shaft Diameter (empirical from torsion):** $D_S = 0.054 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\text{BHP}/N}$.
- ⭐ **Auxiliaries:** $W_{AUX} = (K_{AUX} - 1) \cdot W_{ME}$; $K_{AUX} \in [1.4, 2.5]$.
- ⭐ **Pipe Work:** $W_{PD} \approx 0.06 \cdot W_M$ (self-consistent).
- ⭐ **Specific Weight of Steel = 77.0 kN/m³**; **MDO = 8.8 kN/m³**; **1 tonne mass = 9.81 kN**.
- ⭐ **Standard Conversions:** 1 BHP = 0.746 kW; 1 nautical mile = 1.852 km; 1 m³ = 35.31 ft³.
- ⭐ **Cross-Check Heuristic:** Always validate $W_M / \text{LSW}$ against the 8–25 % band; flag *outliers*.
- ⭐ **Margin for Outfit & Lagging:** Boiler *shipping weight* = pressure-part weight × 1.25.
- ⭐ **Torsion Derivation** is a *favourite 7-mark question* — memorise the torsion equation $T = (\pi/16)\cdot\tau_{max}\cdot D_S^{3}$ and the resulting $D_S = 0.054 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\text{BHP}/N}$ formula.
- ⭐ **Light Ship Weight Definition:** Mass of the *complete* ship in *lightship condition* — no cargo, no fuel, no fresh water, no crew, no consumables. *Includes* all machinery.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outfit Weight
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Outfit Weight # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Outfit Weight (W_O)** is defined as the complete weight of all items of outfit, equipment, fittings, and accessories required to make a seaworthy and operational vessel, but **excluding** the weight of the hull structure (Lightweight) and the weight of the propulsion machinery (Machinery Weight or Machinery Deadweight). In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification, the **Total Displacement (Δ)** of a ship is the fundamental weight relationship: $$\Delta = W_{L} + W_{M} + W_{O} + W_{D}$$ Where: - $\Delta$ = Total Displacement (tonnes) - $W_{L}$ = Lightweight or Lightship Weight (tonnes) - $W_{M}$ = Machinery Deadweight (tonnes) - $W_{O}$ = Outfit Weight (tonnes) - $W_{D}$ = Deadweight (Cargo + Fuel + Stores + Crew) (tonnes) > [!NOTE] > **Outfit Weight constitutes the "third pillar" of a vessel's lightweight-plus-machinery mass.** It represents everything needed to make the bare hull function as a habitable, navigable, and cargo-handling ship. ## 1.2 Components Included in Outfit Weight The Outfit Weight encompasses a wide variety of non-structural, non-propulsive items: | Category | Examples | |----------|----------| | **Crew Accommodation** | Cabins, mess rooms, galley, pantries, sanitary fittings | | **Deck Machinery** | Cranes, winches, capstans, windlasses, hatch covers | | **Navigation & Communication** | Radars, GPS, compasses, radio sets, gyro systems | | **Electrical Installations** | Generators, switchboards, cabling, lighting | | **Piping Systems** | Bilge, ballast, fire-fighting, fuel oil, lubricating oil, freshwater | | **Safety & Life-Saving** | Lifeboats, life rafts, fire extinguishers, CO₂ systems | | **Hull Fittings** | Doors, windows, portlights, hatches, ventilators | | **Joinery Work** | Furniture, paneling, ceiling, flooring | | **Insulation** | Thermal & acoustic insulation | | **Paint & Coating** | Anticorrosive, antifouling, decorative paint | | **Outfit Auxiliaries** | Pumps, compressors, purifiers, heat exchangers (non-propulsion) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy: "Building a House Analogy"** > > Imagine you construct the bare concrete shell of a house (this is the **Lightweight** of a ship — the hull steel). You then install the elevator system and central air conditioning (this is the **Machinery Weight**). However, the house is still uninhabitable! To make it a home, you must add: > - Doors, windows, flooring, paint, furniture > - Electrical wiring, plumbing, sanitary fittings > - Kitchen equipment, wardrobes, lighting > - Fire alarms, safety equipment > > **All these finishing items together represent the Outfit Weight of a ship.** Similarly, outfit weight transforms a bare steel hull with engines into a fully functional, habitable, and operable vessel. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Unit:** Metric Tonnes (t) or Long Tonnes (LT) — KTU convention uses **Metric Tonnes**. > > **Standard Principal Dimensions Used in Outfit Weight Formulas:** > - $L$ = Length Between Perpendiculars (m) > - $B$ = Moulded Breadth (m) > - $D$ = Moulded Depth (m) > - $C_B$ = Block Coefficient (dimensionless) > - $V$ = Displacement Volume (m³) > > **Typical Outfit Weight Range:** **15% to 35%** of the Lightweight, depending on vessel type and complexity. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework Outfit Weight estimation is essential during the **preliminary design stage** because the actual detailed outfit list is not available. Naval architects therefore rely on **empirical and statistical formulas** derived from data of similar existing ships. The estimation process follows a logical hierarchy: ### Step 1: Identify Vessel Type Different vessel types have radically different outfit weight distributions: - **Tanker:** Lower outfit (no cargo-handling cranes, minimal crew) - **Container Ship:** Moderate (no cargo gear, automated systems) - **General Cargo Ship:** Higher (cranes, derricks, hatch covers) - **Naval Vessel:** Highest (combat systems, complex electronics) - **Passenger Ship:** Very high (luxury accommodation, complex systems) ### Step 2: Apply Empirical Formula The outfit weight is calculated using a **coefficient-based formula** where the coefficient $C_O$ absorbs vessel-type characteristics. ### Step 3: Validate Against Statistical Range Cross-check the estimated value with typical ranges for the vessel type. ### Step 4: Breakdown into Sub-Categories Allocate the total outfit weight to the various sub-categories based on percentages. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains all **KTU-exam-ready formulas** for Outfit Weight estimation. **Memorize these formulas** before attempting Part B questions. | **Sl. No.** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Coefficient / Typical Value** | |:-----------:|:-----------:|:----------------|:------------------------------:| | 1 | $W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$ | Basic LBD-based Outfit Weight Formula | $C_{O} = 0.06$ to $0.15$ | | 2 | $W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)^{1/2}$ | Square-root form (Watson-type) | $C_{O} = 2.5$ to $6.0$ | | 3 | $W_{O} = k \cdot L^{1.5}$ | Length-based empirical formula | $k = 0.4$ to $0.8$ | | 4 | $W_{O} = (0.15 \text{ to } 0.30) \cdot W_{L}$ | Outfit as percentage of Lightweight | Varies with ship type | | 5 | $W_{O} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.10) \cdot \Delta$ | Outfit as percentage of Displacement | Varies with ship type | | 6 | $W_{O} = c \cdot L \cdot B \cdot D / 100$ | Schneekluth-type (in tonnes) | $c = 8$ to $15$ | | 7 | $\% W_{O, \text{ accomm}} \approx 35\%$ | Crew accommodation share | Approximate | | 8 | $\% W_{O, \text{ deck}} \approx 20\%$ | Deck machinery share | Approximate | | 9 | $\% W_{O, \text{ elect}} \approx 12\%$ | Electrical share | Approximate | | 10 | $\% W_{O, \text{ piping}} \approx 13\%$ | Piping share | Approximate | | 11 | $\% W_{O, \text{ safety}} \approx 8\%$ | Safety equipment share | Approximate | | 12 | $\% W_{O, \text{ misc}} \approx 12\%$ | Insulation, paint, joinery share | Approximate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** The most commonly tested formula in KTU board exams is **$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$** with appropriate coefficient selection. Always state the assumed coefficient value and justify the choice based on vessel type. ## 2.3 Breakdown of Outfit Weight — Sub-Category Analysis > [!NOTE] > The detailed sub-division of outfit weight is a frequent KTU exam topic. The standard KTU breakdown follows the **"Big-7" classification**: ``` OUTFIT WEIGHT (100%) ├── 1. Crew Accommodation → 30–40% ├── 2. Deck Machinery → 15–20% ├── 3. Electrical → 10–15% ├── 4. Piping → 10–15% ├── 5. Safety Equipment → 5–10% ├── 6. Insulation & Joinery → 5–8% └── 7. Paint & Miscellaneous → 3–5% ``` ### 2.3.1 Crew Accommodation (30–40%) This includes all items needed for crew comfort and operation: - **Furniture:** Beds, wardrobes, desks, chairs - **Galley equipment:** Ovens, refrigerators, freezers - **Sanitary fittings:** Toilets, showers, washbasins - **HVAC ducting** within accommodation spaces - **Interior panelling** and decorative finishes > **Why this is largest:** Crew complement ranges from 20 (small cargo) to 1500+ (cruise ship), driving significant outfit weight. ### 2.3.2 Deck Machinery (15–20%) - **Cargo handling:** Cranes, derricks, winches - **Mooring equipment:** Windlasses, capstans, bollards - **Hatch covers** (for cargo ships) - **Steering gear** (sometimes classified separately) ### 2.3.3 Electrical Installations (10–15%) - **Main switchboards, emergency switchboards** - **Cabling** throughout the vessel - **Lighting fixtures** (internal + external) - **Generators** (auxiliary diesel generators) - **Batteries, transformers** ### 2.3.4 Piping Systems (10–15%) - **Bilge and ballast systems** - **Fuel oil transfer and filling** - **Lubricating oil system** - **Fresh water and sanitary systems** - **Fire-fighting (main and emergency)** - **Compressed air system** - **Steam system** (if applicable) ### 2.3.5 Safety & Life-Saving (5–10%) - **Lifeboats, life rafts, life jackets** - **Fire extinguishers, fire hoses, hydrants** - **CO₂ flooding systems, foam systems** - **Emergency escape breathing devices (EEBD)** ### 2.3.6 Insulation & Joinery (5–8%) - **Thermal insulation** (refrigerated compartments, engine room) - **Acoustic insulation** - **Joinery bulkheads, doors, ceiling panels** ### 2.3.7 Paint & Miscellaneous (3–5%) - **Anticorrosive paint, antifouling paint** - **Miscellaneous fittings, name plates** ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why does Outfit Weight matter in ship design?** > > 1. **Stability & Freeboard:** Outfit weight affects the vertical center of gravity (KG) and therefore the metacentric height (GM). A miscalculation can compromise vessel stability. > > 2. **Cost Estimation:** Outfit represents **20–30% of total building cost** of a ship. Accurate estimation is vital for tender preparation. > > 3. **Cargo Capacity:** Every tonne of outfit weight reduces the deadweight (cargo) capacity — directly affecting commercial revenue. > > 4. **Construction Scheduling:** Outfit is installed **parallel to hull construction** (pre-outfitting) or post-launch; accurate weight helps plan crane capacities and block-lift limits. > > 5. **Regulatory Compliance:** SOLAS, MARPOL, and Classification Society rules mandate minimum outfit equipment. ## 2.5 Common Empirical Coefficients by Vessel Type | **Vessel Type** | **C_O in W_O = C_O × LBD** | **Outfit as % of W_L** | |:----------------|:---------------------------:|:----------------------:| | Oil Tanker | 0.06 – 0.08 | 15 – 20% | | Bulk Carrier | 0.07 – 0.10 | 18 – 23% | | General Cargo | 0.09 – 0.12 | 22 – 28% | | Container Ship | 0.10 – 0.13 | 25 – 32% | | Reefer Ship | 0.10 – 0.14 | 26 – 32% | | Naval Frigate | 0.12 – 0.18 | 30 – 40% | | Cruise Ship | 0.15 – 0.25 | 35 – 50% | | Fishing Vessel | 0.07 – 0.10 | 18 – 25% | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the LBD-Based Outfit Weight Formula The empirical formula $W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$ is derived from the principle of **dimensional analysis and statistical regression** on existing ship data. ### 3.1.1 Dimensional Reasoning > Outfit weight scales with the **internal volume** of the ship (since most outfit items fill internal spaces) but is **inversely proportional** to ship size efficiency (larger ships are more outfit-efficient). We assume outfit weight depends on the principal dimensions as: $$W_{O} = f(L^{a}, B^{b}, D^{c})$$ Applying dimensional homogeneity and the empirical observation that outfit weight is **roughly proportional to the square root of displacement volume**: $$W_{O} \propto (L \cdot B \cdot D)^{n}$$ Statistical regression on a database of 200+ ships yields: $$n \approx 1.0 \text{ (linear form)} \quad \text{or} \quad n \approx 0.5 \text{ (square-root form)}$$ The **linear form** $W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot LBD$ is the most commonly used in KTU-style problems. ### 3.1.2 Complete Step-by-Step Derivation Starting from the basic relationship: $$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$$ **Step 1:** Substitute dimensions in metres. $$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L_{m} \cdot B_{m} \cdot D_{m})$$ **Step 2:** Determine coefficient $C_{O}$ from regression database. $$C_{O} = \frac{W_{O, \text{actual}}}{L \cdot B \cdot D}$$ **Step 3:** For a General Cargo ship, typical $C_{O} \approx 0.10$. $$W_{O} = 0.10 \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D) \quad [\text{tonnes}]$$ **Step 4:** Verification — for a ship with $L = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 14$ m: $$L \cdot B \cdot D = 150 \times 22 \times 14 = 46{,}200 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_{O} = 0.10 \times 46{,}200 = 4{,}620 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 5:** Express as a percentage of Lightweight (if $W_{L} = 18{,}000$ t): $$\% W_{O} = \frac{4{,}620}{18{,}000} \times 100 = 25.67\%$$ This falls within the **acceptable range (22–28%)** for General Cargo ships ✓ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Outfit Weight Estimation (Full Solution) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example for KTU Exam Practice** **Given:** A Bulk Carrier has the following principal particulars: - Length Between Perpendiculars: $L_{BP} = 200$ m - Moulded Breadth: $B = 32$ m - Moulded Depth: $D = 18$ m - Block Coefficient: $C_{B} = 0.78$ - Designed Draft: $T = 12.5$ m - Lightweight: $W_{L} = 15{,}500$ tonnes - Machinery Weight: $W_{M} = 2{,}800$ tonnes - Deadweight: $W_{D} = 55{,}000$ tonnes **Find:** 1. Total Displacement $\Delta$ 2. Outfit Weight $W_{O}$ using LBD formula with $C_{O} = 0.085$ 3. Sub-category breakdown of Outfit Weight ### Solution **Step 1: Calculate Total Displacement** $$\Delta = W_{L} + W_{M} + W_{O} + W_{D}$$ But we need $W_{O}$ first to find $\Delta$ — this is **iterative** in preliminary design. We will first estimate $W_{O}$ directly. **Step 2: Calculate Outfit Weight using LBD Formula** $$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$$ $$L \cdot B \cdot D = 200 \times 32 \times 18$$ Let us compute step by step: $$200 \times 32 = 6{,}400$$ $$6{,}400 \times 18 = 115{,}200 \text{ m}^3$$ Therefore: $$W_{O} = 0.085 \times 115{,}200 = 9{,}792 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 3: Verify Against Typical Range for Bulk Carriers** $$\% W_{O} = \frac{9{,}792}{15{,}500} \times 100 = 63.17\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **This value (63%) is unrealistically HIGH!** For a Bulk Carrier, typical outfit is only **18–23% of Lightweight.** The chosen coefficient $C_{O} = 0.085$ is too high. **Step 4: Re-estimate with Corrected Coefficient** For a Bulk Carrier, the correct coefficient is $C_{O} \approx 0.045$ to $0.055$. $$W_{O} = 0.05 \times 115{,}200 = 5{,}760 \text{ tonnes}$$ $$\% W_{O} = \frac{5{,}760}{15{,}500} \times 100 = 37.16\%$$ > [!WARNING] > Still slightly high. Bulk carriers use lower outfit coefficients due to large cargo holds with minimal internal outfit. **Step 5: Acceptable Final Estimate** Using $C_{O} = 0.045$: $$W_{O} = 0.045 \times 115{,}200 = 5{,}184 \text{ tonnes}$$ $$\% W_{O} = \frac{5{,}184}{15{,}500} \times 100 = 33.4\%$$ This is within acceptable engineering tolerance for preliminary design. **Step 6: Calculate Total Displacement** $$\Delta = 15{,}500 + 2{,}800 + 5{,}184 + 55{,}000 = 78{,}484 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 7: Sub-Category Breakdown of $W_{O}$** | Sub-Category | % of $W_O$ | Weight (tonnes) | |:-------------|:----------:|:---------------:| | Crew Accommodation | 30% | $0.30 \times 5{,}184 = 1{,}555.2$ | | Deck Machinery | 20% | $0.20 \times 5{,}184 = 1{,}036.8$ | | Electrical | 12% | $0.12 \times 5{,}184 = 622.1$ | | Piping | 13% | $0.13 \times 5{,}184 = 673.9$ | | Safety Equipment | 8% | $0.08 \times 5{,}184 = 414.7$ | | Insulation & Joinery | 7% | $0.07 \times 5{,}184 = 362.9$ | | Paint & Misc. | 5% | $0.05 \times 5{,}184 = 259.2$ | | **Aux. Machinery** | 5% | $0.05 \times 5{,}184 = 259.2$ | | **TOTAL** | **100%** | **5,184.0 t** | **Step 8: Sanity Check using Percentage of Displacement** $$\% W_{O} = \frac{5{,}184}{78{,}484} \times 100 = 6.60\%$$ This is within the typical range of **5–10% of Displacement** ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Coefficient Sensitivity Analysis > [!NOTE] > The table below demonstrates how the Outfit Weight coefficient $C_O$ varies with vessel type. KTU exam problems often provide the coefficient; if not, **state your assumed value clearly**. | **Vessel Type** | **$C_O$ (LBD form)** | **$C_O$ (√LBD form)** | **$W_O$ as % of $W_L$** | **Comments** | |:----------------|:--------------------:|:---------------------:|:-----------------------:|:------------| | Oil Tanker (VLCC) | 0.05 | 3.5 | 15–18% | Minimal cargo handling gear | | Bulk Carrier (Capesize) | 0.045 | 3.0 | 16–20% | Large holds, low crew | | General Cargo | 0.10 | 5.0 | 22–28% | Cranes, derricks, hatch covers | | Container (Feeder) | 0.11 | 5.5 | 25–30% | Reefer plugs, lashing gear | | Container (ULCV) | 0.09 | 4.8 | 22–27% | High automation reduces outfit | | LNG Carrier | 0.10 | 5.0 | 25–30% | Complex cargo containment aux. | | Reefer Ship | 0.12 | 6.0 | 28–33% | Heavy refrigeration plant | | Naval Frigate | 0.15 | 7.5 | 35–42% | Combat systems, complex electronics | | Cruise Ship | 0.20 | 9.0 | 40–55% | Maximum outfit for passenger comfort | | Fishing Trawler | 0.08 | 4.0 | 20–25% | Fish processing equipment | | Yacht (Large) | 0.18 | 8.5 | 40–50% | Luxury fittings | | Drill Ship | 0.13 | 6.5 | 30–38% | Drilling equipment, dynamic positioning | ## 3.4 Conversion Formulas — Useful in KTU Problems > [!NOTE] > Sometimes outfit weight is given in **Long Tonnes (LT)** or **Displacement Tonnes (DT)**. Use these conversions: $$\boxed{1 \text{ Long Tonne (LT)} = 1.016 \text{ Metric Tonnes (t)}}$$ $$\boxed{1 \text{ Displacement Tonne (DT)} = 1 \text{ Metric Tonne (t)} = 1 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of seawater}}$$ Conversion equation: $$W_{O, \text{metric}} = W_{O, \text{LT}} \times 1.016$$ Or equivalently: $$W_{O, \text{LT}} = \frac{W_{O, \text{metric}}}{1.016}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Outfit Weight Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical breakdown of Outfit Weight, its relationship to the overall weight equation, and the typical percentage distribution across sub-categories. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Total Displacement Δ] nodeB[Lightweight W_L
Hull Steel Weight] nodeC[Machinery Weight W_M
Main + Aux. Engines] nodeD[Outfit Weight W_O
Non-structural items] nodeE[Deadweight W_D
Cargo + Fuel + Stores] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF1[Accommodation 30%] nodeD --> nodeF2[Deck Machinery 20%] nodeD --> nodeF3[Electrical 12%] nodeD --> nodeF4[Piping 13%] nodeD --> nodeF5[Safety Equipment 8%] nodeD --> nodeF6[Insulation Joinery 7%] nodeD --> nodeF7[Paint Miscellaneous 5%] nodeD --> nodeF8[Aux Machinery 5%] nodeF1 --> nodeG1[Cabins Bunks Furniture] nodeF1 --> nodeG2[Galley Pantry Equipment] nodeF1 --> nodeG3[Sanitary Fittings] nodeF2 --> nodeH1[Cranes Winches] nodeF2 --> nodeH2[Windlasses Capstans] nodeF2 --> nodeH3[Hatch Covers] nodeF3 --> nodeI1[Generators Switchboards] nodeF3 --> nodeI2[Cabling Lighting] nodeF4 --> nodeJ1[Bilge Ballast Piping] nodeF4 --> nodeJ2[Fuel Oil Piping] nodeF4 --> nodeJ3[Fire Fighting System] nodeF5 --> nodeK1[Lifeboats Life Rafts] nodeF5 --> nodeK2[Fire Extinguishers] nodeF5 --> nodeK3[CO2 Systems] nodeF6 --> nodeL1[Thermal Insulation] nodeF6 --> nodeL2[Acoustic Insulation] nodeF6 --> nodeL3[Joinery Bulkheads] nodeF7 --> nodeM1[Anticorrosive Paint] nodeF7 --> nodeM2[Antifouling Paint] nodeF8 --> nodeN1[Pumps Compressors] nodeF8 --> nodeN2[Purifiers Heat Exchangers] style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeD fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeB fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeC fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeF1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF6 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF7 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style nodeF8 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart — Outfit Weight Estimation Procedure > [!NOTE] > This diagram maps the systematic procedure to estimate Outfit Weight during preliminary ship design, as expected in KTU 14-mark questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Start: Vessel Type Identified] stepB[Extract Principal Dimensions L, B, D] stepC{Vessel Type?} stepD1[Tanker: C_O approx 0.06] stepD2[Cargo Ship: C_O approx 0.10] stepD3[Naval: C_O approx 0.15] stepD4[Cruise: C_O approx 0.20] stepE[Calculate W_O = C_O x LBD] stepF[Verify: W_O in 15 to 35 percent of W_L] stepG{Within Range?} stepH[Yes: Accept W_O] stepI[No: Re-iterate C_O or check data] stepJ[Apply percentage breakdown 30, 20, 12, 13, 8, 7, 5, 5] stepK[Compute total displacement Δ] stepL[End: Final W_O documented] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD1 stepC --> stepD2 stepC --> stepD3 stepC --> stepD4 stepD1 --> stepE stepD2 --> stepE stepD3 --> stepE stepD4 --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepG --> stepI stepI --> stepE stepH --> stepJ stepJ --> stepK stepK --> stepL style stepA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style stepE fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style stepH fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style stepI fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style stepL fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Concept Map — Relationship Between Ship Weight Components ```mermaid flowchart LR compA[Total Displacement Δ] compB[Lightweight W_L] compC[Deadweight DWT] compA --> compD[Sum of W_L + W_M + W_O + W_D] compB --> compE[Hull Steel + Superstructure Steel + Castings] compC --> compF[Cargo + Bunker + Stores + Crew + Fresh Water] compD --> compG[Outfit Weight W_O] compE --> compG compF --> compG compG --> compH[Volumetric Method C_O x LBD] compG --> compI[Statistical Method percent of W_L] compG --> compJ[Component Build-up Method] style compA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style compG fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff style compH fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style compI fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 style compJ fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Outfit Weight. List any four major items included in the outfit weight of a merchant ship.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): > **Definition (2 marks):** Outfit Weight ($W_O$) is the total weight of all equipment, fittings, and accessories on a vessel required for its operation, habitability, and safety, **excluding** the hull structural weight (Lightweight) and the propulsion machinery weight. > **Four Major Items (1 mark — list any 4):** > 1. Crew accommodation furniture and fittings > 2. Deck machinery (cranes, winches, windlasses) > 3. Electrical installations (generators, switchboards, cabling) > 4. Piping systems (bilge, ballast, fire-fighting, fuel) > 5. Safety and life-saving equipment > 6. Insulation and joinery work > 7. Paint and hull fittings --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State the empirical formula commonly used for estimating Outfit Weight in preliminary ship design. What is the typical range of outfit weight as a percentage of Lightweight for a General Cargo ship?** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): > **Formula (2 marks):** > $$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$$ > where $C_O$ is the outfit weight coefficient, typically $0.06$ to $0.15$ depending on vessel type, and $L$, $B$, $D$ are the principal dimensions in metres. > **Typical Range (1 mark):** For a General Cargo ship, outfit weight is typically **22% to 28%** of the Lightweight ($W_L$). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two questions (A or B). Each carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various **components of Outfit Weight** of a merchant cargo vessel. State the approximate percentage contribution of each component. **(b)** For a **General Cargo Ship** with the following particulars, estimate the Outfit Weight using the LBD formula and compute the Total Displacement: - Length Between Perpendiculars: $L = 160$ m - Moulded Breadth: $B = 24$ m - Moulded Depth: $D = 14$ m - Lightweight: $W_L = 8{,}000$ t - Machinery Deadweight: $W_M = 1{,}200$ t - Deadweight: $W_D = 18{,}000$ t - Use outfit coefficient: $C_O = 0.10$ #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 Marks** The Outfit Weight of a merchant cargo vessel comprises the following major components: | **Component** | **% of W_O** | **Description** | |:--------------|:------------:|:----------------| | Crew Accommodation | 30% | Cabins, furniture, galley, sanitary fittings | | Deck Machinery | 20% | Cranes, winches, windlasses, hatch covers | | Electrical Installations | 12% | Generators, switchboards, cabling, lighting | | Piping Systems | 13% | Bilge, ballast, fuel, fire-fighting, sanitary | | Safety Equipment | 8% | Lifeboats, life rafts, fire extinguishers, CO₂ | | Insulation & Joinery | 7% | Thermal, acoustic insulation, joinery panels | | Paint & Miscellaneous | 5% | Anticorrosive, antifouling paint | | Auxiliary Machinery | 5% | Pumps, compressors, purifiers | | **TOTAL** | **100%** | | **Key Explanation Points (2 marks):** - Accommodation forms the largest share due to the need for crew comfort on long voyages - Deck machinery is heavy for cargo ships because of cargo-handling equipment - Outfit does NOT include hull steel or main propulsion machinery **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Apply the LBD Formula** $$W_{O} = C_{O} \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$$ $$L \cdot B \cdot D = 160 \times 24 \times 14$$ Step-by-step multiplication: $$160 \times 24 = 3{,}840$$ $$3{,}840 \times 14 = 53{,}760 \text{ m}^3$$ Therefore: $$W_{O} = 0.10 \times 53{,}760 = 5{,}376 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 2: Verify with Typical Range** $$\% W_{O} = \frac{5{,}376}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 67.2\%$$ This is high, but since $C_O = 0.10$ was specified in the problem, we proceed with this value. **Step 3: Calculate Total Displacement** $$\Delta = W_{L} + W_{M} + W_{O} + W_{D}$$ $$\Delta = 8{,}000 + 1{,}200 + 5{,}376 + 18{,}000$$ Adding step by step: $$8{,}000 + 1{,}200 = 9{,}200$$ $$9{,}200 + 5{,}376 = 14{,}576$$ $$14{,}576 + 18{,}000 = 32{,}576 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{W_{O} = 5{,}376 \text{ t} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta = 32{,}576 \text{ t}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a)** Describe the **significance of Outfit Weight estimation** in the preliminary design of a ship. Why is it important to estimate it accurately? **(b)** Two ships are being compared: - **Ship X (Tanker):** $L = 280$ m, $B = 44$ m, $D = 24$ m - **Ship Y (General Cargo):** $L = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m, $D = 16$ m Calculate the Outfit Weight of both ships using the LBD formula with appropriate coefficients. Comment on the difference in outfit weight between the two vessel types. **Use:** $C_O$ (Tanker) $= 0.065$ and $C_O$ (General Cargo) $= 0.105$ #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Significance of Outfit Weight Estimation (7 marks):** 1. **Affects Total Displacement (2 marks):** Outfit weight is a direct component of the displacement equation: $$\Delta = W_{L} + W_{M} + W_{O} + W_{D}$$ Any error in $W_O$ propagates to displacement, affecting hull form design and powering. 2. **Affects Stability (1 mark):** Outfit weight's vertical center of gravity (VCG) influences the metacentric height (GM). Misplaced or underestimated outfit weight can lower GM, causing dangerous stability loss. 3. **Affects Deadweight & Commercial Revenue (1 mark):** Every tonne of overweight outfit reduces cargo capacity, directly affecting the ship owner's earning potential. 4. **Cost Estimation (1 mark):** Outfit constitutes 20–30% of total shipbuilding cost. Accurate estimation is essential for tender pricing and contract negotiations. 5. **Construction Planning (1 mark):** Outfit is installed pre-launch (pre-outfitting) or post-launch. Accurate weight estimates help plan block-lift crane capacities and dry-dock schedules. 6. **Regulatory Compliance (1 mark):** SOLAS and Classification Society rules mandate minimum outfit standards. Estimation ensures these are met without overshooting. **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Ship X (Tanker):** $$W_{O,X} = C_{O,X} \cdot (L_X \cdot B_X \cdot D_X)$$ $$L_X \cdot B_X \cdot D_X = 280 \times 44 \times 24$$ Step-by-step: $$280 \times 44 = 12{,}320$$ $$12{,}320 \times 24 = 295{,}680 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_{O,X} = 0.065 \times 295{,}680 = 19{,}219.2 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Ship Y (General Cargo):** $$W_{O,Y} = C_{O,Y} \cdot (L_Y \cdot B_Y \cdot D_Y)$$ $$L_Y \cdot B_Y \cdot D_Y = 180 \times 28 \times 16$$ Step-by-step: $$180 \times 28 = 5{,}040$$ $$5{,}040 \times 16 = 80{,}640 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_{O,Y} = 0.105 \times 80{,}640 = 8{,}467.2 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Ship X (Tanker) | Ship Y (General Cargo) | |:----------|:---------------:|:----------------------:| | Dimensions (m) | 280 × 44 × 24 | 180 × 28 × 16 | | $C_O$ | 0.065 | 0.105 | | $W_O$ (tonnes) | 19,219.2 | 8,467.2 | | $W_O / LBD$ (kg/m³) | 65 | 105 | **Comments (2 marks):** - The **General Cargo ship has a higher outfit coefficient (0.105 vs 0.065)** because it carries more crew accommodation, cargo-handling cranes, derricks, winches, and hatch covers. - The **Tanker has a lower outfit coefficient** because it has minimal cargo-handling gear (pipelines on deck only), smaller crew, and more automated systems. - The ratio $W_{O,Y} / W_{O,X} = 8{,}467.2 / 19{,}219.2 = 0.44$ shows that despite being larger, the tanker's outfit is still lower per unit volume due to its simpler outfit requirements. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always express $L$, $B$, $D$ in **metres** and $W_O$ in **tonnes**. Mixing units is a common error costing 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Wrong Coefficient Selection:** Using $C_O = 0.10$ for a Tanker (should be 0.05–0.08) results in overestimation. Always justify the coefficient based on vessel type. > > 3. **Forgetting to Convert:** If a problem provides dimensions in feet, you MUST convert to metres first: $1 \text{ ft} = 0.3048 \text{ m}$. > > 4. **Skipping Verification Step:** Examiners award marks for cross-checking the result. Always state the percentage and verify against the typical range. > > 5. **Confusing Outfit with Lightweight:** Outfit is a **sub-component** of Lightweight in some classifications, but in KTU's weight equation, Outfit is a **separate term**. Check the question carefully. > > 6. **Arithmetic Errors:** The multiplication $L \times B \times D$ involves large numbers. Show intermediate steps to avoid mistakes and gain method marks. > > 7. **Not Stating Formula:** Always write the formula explicitly before substituting values — this earns easy 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Outfit Weight** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Outfit weight = All non-structural, non-propulsive items needed to make the ship operational - ☐ Displacement equation: $\Delta = W_L + W_M + W_O + W_D$ - ☐ Outfit is the **third-largest** weight component in most ships - ☐ Typical range: **15% to 35%** of Lightweight, depending on vessel type ### Essential Formulas - ☐ **LBD Formula:** $W_O = C_O \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)$ — $C_O$ varies with vessel type - ☐ **Percentage Method:** $W_O = (15\text{–}35\%) \times W_L$ - ☐ **Square-root form:** $W_O = C_O \cdot (LBD)^{1/2}$ for very large vessels - ☐ **Length-based:** $W_O = k \cdot L^{1.5}$ ### Coefficient Values to Memorize - ☐ Tanker: $C_O = 0.05$ to $0.08$ - ☐ Bulk Carrier: $C_O = 0.04$ to $0.06$ - ☐ General Cargo: $C_O = 0.09$ to $0.12$ - ☐ Container Ship: $C_O = 0.09$ to $0.13$ - ☐ Naval Frigate: $C_O = 0.12$ to $0.18$ - ☐ Cruise Ship: $C_O = 0.15$ to $0.25$ ### Sub-Category Percentage Breakdown - ☐ Accommodation: 30% - ☐ Deck Machinery: 20% - ☐ Electrical: 12% - ☐ Piping: 13% - ☐ Safety: 8% - ☐ Insulation & Joinery: 7% - ☐ Paint & Misc: 5% - ☐ Aux Machinery: 5% ### Key Engineering Points - ☐ Outfit weight affects **stability** (VCG and GM) - ☐ Outfit is **20–30% of total building cost** - ☐ Overestimating outfit reduces **cargo capacity (DWT)** - ☐ SOLAS regulations mandate minimum safety outfit - ☐ Unit conversion: $1 \text{ LT} = 1.016 \text{ t}$ (metric) ### Common KTU Exam Traps - ☐ Always state assumed $C_O$ value - ☐ Always show verification step (% of $W_L$) - ☐ Never confuse "Outfit" with "Lightweight" — they are distinct - ☐ Watch for unit conversion (feet ↔ metres) - ☐ Show all intermediate arithmetic for full marks

topics.title: Deadweight. General Arrangement (GA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Deadweight. General Arrangement (GA)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Deadweight (DWT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadweight (DWT)** is the total weight that a ship must carry at the design load condition, including cargo, fuel (bunkers), fresh water, lubricating oil, ballast, provisions, stores, crew, passengers, and their effects. It is mathematically defined as the difference between the **Displacement (Δ)** of the vessel at the load waterline and the **Lightweight (LWT)** of the vessel.

In simple KTU terminology:

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{Loaded}} - \Delta_{\text{Light}}$$

Where:
- **DWT** = Deadweight Tonnage (in tonnes or long tons)
- **Δ_Loaded** = Total displacement at the design load draft (weight of water displaced)
- **Δ_Light** = Light Ship Displacement (Lightweight)

### 1.2 Components of Deadweight (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires students to distinguish between **Deadweight components** and **Displacement components**. Memorize this classification as a 3-mark short-answer favorite.

| Sl. No. | Component | Typical % of DWT | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cargo Deadweight** | 60–70% | The actual paying cargo carried |
| 2 | **Fuel (Bunkers)** | 10–15% | HFO, DO, MDO for propulsion and auxiliaries |
| 3 | **Fresh Water** | 2–4% | Drinking, washing, boiler feed |
| 4 | **Lubricating Oil** | 1–2% | LO for main engine, aux engines, deck machinery |
| 5 | **Stores & Provisions** | 1–2% | Food, spares, consumables |
| 6 | **Crew & Effects** | <1% | Crew, baggage, personal items |
| 7 | **Ballast** | Variable | Water in ballast tanks for stability |

### 1.3 General Arrangement (GA) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Arrangement (GA) Drawing** is the master engineering drawing of a ship that defines the internal subdivision, spatial allocation, and functional zoning of all compartments, machinery spaces, cargo holds, tanks, and accommodation areas. It is the "**blueprint of the ship's identity**" — every naval architect's first deliverable.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Deadweight Analogy — The "Loaded Truck" Concept:**
Imagine a truck with an empty body (the lightweight structure). The truck has a maximum carrying capacity stamped on its chassis plate. The maximum weight it can legally carry — including goods, fuel in its tank, driver, and any luggage — is its **Deadweight**. The truck itself is the "Lightweight," and the total weight of the truck plus its load is the "Displacement."

**GA Analogy — The "Building Floor Plan" Concept:**
Think of a multi-storey hotel. The architect first produces a **floor plan** showing where rooms, corridors, elevators, kitchens, staircases, and utility shafts are placed. Similarly, a ship GA is the **3D unrolled floor plan** of a floating vessel — showing where the engine room sits, where cargo holds are, where the bridge is positioned, and how watertight compartments are distributed fore, mid, and aft.

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sea Water Density (ρ_sw):** **1.025 t/m³** (standard KTU assumption for displacement calculations)
> - **Fresh Water Density (ρ_fw):** **1.000 t/m³**
> - **DWT Unit:** **Metric Tonnes (t)** or **Long Tons (LT)** where 1 LT = 1.016 t
> - **Displacement Volume → Weight Conversion:** 1 m³ sea water ≈ **1.025 tonnes**
