## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deadweight — The Theoretical Foundation

#### 2.1.1 The Ship Weight Equation (Fundamental Identity)

The **fundamental ship weight equation** forms the bedrock of naval architecture:

$$\Delta_{\text{Loaded}} = LWT + DWT$$

Or equivalently:

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{Loaded}} - LWT$$

**Logical Step Breakdown:**
1. A floating ship is in **hydrostatic equilibrium** → Weight of ship = Weight of displaced water (Archimedes' Principle)
2. The displaced water weight = **Displacement (Δ)**
3. The ship itself (empty) has a structural weight = **Lightweight (LWT)**
4. Anything added to make the ship "useful" (cargo, fuel, etc.) = **Deadweight (DWT)**
5. Therefore: Total Weight (Displacement) = Empty Weight (LWT) + Useful Weight (DWT)

#### 2.1.2 Subdivision of Deadweight

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests the **components of DWT** — never skip this for a 3-mark question.

$$DWT = DWT_{\text{Cargo}} + DWT_{\text{Bunkers}} + DWT_{\text{FW}} + DWT_{\text{LO}} + DWT_{\text{Stores}} + DWT_{\text{Crew}}$$

**Engineering Utility:** This breakdown is critical for:
- **Voyage planning** — knowing how much fuel to bunker for a specific route
- **Stability calculations** — locating the **center of gravity (LCG)** of each weight group
- **Loading computer programming** — modern vessels use real-time DWT summation

#### 2.1.3 The Deadweight Coefficient (η_d)

A non-dimensional efficiency parameter:

$$\eta_d = \frac{DWT}{\nabla_{\text{Loaded}} \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

Where ∇_Loaded is the displaced volume at the load draft.

**Interpretation:** Higher η_d → ship is a more efficient cargo carrier (less hull volume "wasted").

### 2.2 Lightweight (LWT) — Brief Note

LWT is further subdivided into:
- **Steel weight** (~60–70% of LWT)
- **Outfit weight** (~20–25% of LWT) — piping, ventilation, electrical, joinery
- **Machinery weight** (~10–15% of LWT) — main engine, auxiliaries, boilers

### 2.3 General Arrangement (GA) — Theoretical Foundation

#### 2.3.1 Purpose of GA Drawing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The GA is the **regulatory, contractual, and operational master document** of any ship. It governs SOLAS compliance, class society approval, shipyard construction, and the owner's operational philosophy.

#### 2.3.2 Key Views in a GA Drawing

A standard GA contains the following orthographic projections:
1. **Profile View (Sheer Plan)** — Side elevation showing decks, bulkheads, sheer line, camber
2. **Top View (Plan)** — Horizontal cut through a specific deck showing compartmentation
3. **Transverse Sections** — Cross-sectional views at critical stations
4. **Engine Room & Deckhouse Plans** — Detailed machinery and accommodation layouts

#### 2.3.3 Mandatory Compartments Shown in GA

| Zone | Compartments | Functional Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward** | Forepeak tank, chain locker, bosun's store, bow thruster room | Mooring, navigation, structural collision protection |
| **Amidships** | Cargo holds (tankers/ bulkers) or cargo tanks (tankers), pump room | Primary revenue-generating space |
| **Aft** | Engine room, boiler room, steering gear room, emergency generator room | Propulsion, navigation, safety |
| **Superstructure** | Bridge, accommodation, galley, mess rooms, hospital | Crew living and ship control |
| **Bottom** | Double bottom tanks (DB), side tanks, bilges | Ballast, fresh water, structural strength |

#### 2.3.4 Watertight Subdivision (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **SOLAS Chapter II-1** requires subdivision of the ship into watertight compartments bounded by watertight bulkheads. The GA must show the **collision bulkhead** (typically 5–15% L from FP) and the **afterpeak bulkhead**.

**Engineering Utility of GA:**
- **Class society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, BV)
- **Statutory compliance** with SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line Convention
- **Construction reference** for shipyard
- **Stability booklet input** — compartment volumes and centers
- **Damage control planning** — flooding scenarios

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems in Module 2.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DWT = \Delta_{\text{Loaded}} - LWT$ | Deadweight definition | tonnes (t) |
| 2 | $\Delta_{\text{Loaded}} = LWT + DWT$ | Total weight equation | t |
| 3 | $\Delta = \nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}$ | Displacement = displaced volume × density | t (if ∇ in m³, ρ in t/m³) |
| 4 | $\Delta = \nabla \cdot C_b$ | Approx. volume × block coefficient | m³ |
| 5 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{DWT}{\nabla \cdot \rho_{sw}}$ | Deadweight coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $DWT_{\text{total}} = \sum DWT_i$ | Component-wise summation | t |
| 7 | $LCG_{\text{ship}} = \dfrac{\sum(W_i \cdot x_i)}{\sum W_i}$ | Longitudinal center of gravity | m from AP/FP |
| 8 | $VCG_{\text{ship}} = \dfrac{\sum(W_i \cdot z_i)}{\sum W_i}$ | Vertical center of gravity | m from BL/keel |
| 9 | $LBP = L_{OA} - (\text{stem overhang} + \text{stern overhang})$ | Length between perpendiculars | m |
| 10 | $\text{Freeboard} = D - d$ | Load line freeboard | m |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental Deadweight Equation from Archimedes' Principle

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 loves derivations that start from first principles. This 7-mark derivation is gold for Part B.

**Starting Point:** Archimedes' Principle — *"A floating body displaces a volume of fluid whose weight equals the body's own weight."*

Let:
- $V$ = submerged volume of the ship hull (m³)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = density of sea water (1.025 t/m³)
- $W_{ship}$ = total weight of the ship (tonnes)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

**Step 1: Write Archimedes' buoyancy equation in force form.**

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g$$

This is the buoyant force acting upward on the ship.

**Step 2: Apply static equilibrium for a floating ship.**

The ship is in equilibrium, so the buoyant force equals the gravitational force:

$$F_B = W_{ship}$$

Substituting:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = W_{ship}$$

**Step 3: Cancel gravitational acceleration g (since we work in mass/tonnage terms).**

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot V = W_{ship}$$

**Step 4: Recognize the left-hand side as the displaced weight, i.e., the Displacement (Δ).**

$$\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot V$$

This gives us the fundamental displacement identity. In SI tonnes:

$$\Delta \text{ (tonnes)} = V \text{ (m}^3\text{)} \times 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Partition the ship's total weight into structural weight + payload weight.**

$$W_{ship} = W_{\text{structure}} + W_{\text{payload}}$$

**Step 6: Substitute naval architecture nomenclature.**

- $W_{\text{structure}}$ = Lightweight (LWT) — the empty ship
- $W_{\text{payload}}$ = Deadweight (DWT) — cargo + consumables + crew

$$W_{ship} = LWT + DWT$$

**Step 7: Equate Step 4 and Step 6.**

$$\Delta = LWT + DWT$$

**Step 8: Rearrange to obtain the Deadweight formula.**

$$\boxed{DWT = \Delta - LWT}$$

This is the **governing equation of all ship weight estimation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Deadweight Coefficient from Hydrostatic Identity

**Given:**
- Block coefficient $C_b$ (typical 0.6–0.85 for merchant ships)
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{BP}$ (m)
- Breadth $B$ (m)
- Loaded draft $T$ (m)
- Sea water density $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

**Step 1: Write the displaced volume formula using the block coefficient.**

$$V = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the displacement identity.**

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

**Step 3: Apply the deadweight definition.**

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT$$

**Step 4: Define the deadweight coefficient as the ratio of DWT to Δ.**

$$\eta_d = \frac{DWT}{\Delta} = \frac{DWT}{C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

**Step 5: Solve for DWT explicitly.**

$$\boxed{DWT = \eta_d \cdot C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the standard "**parametric DWT formula**" used in preliminary ship design when no detailed weight estimation is available.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU numerical** — practice it thoroughly. Marks: 7

**Problem Statement:**
A bulk carrier has the following particulars at the design load condition:
- Length between perpendiculars: $L_{BP} = 200$ m
- Breadth: $B = 32$ m
- Loaded draft: $T = 12.5$ m
- Block coefficient: $C_b = 0.78$
- Lightweight: $LWT = 12{,}000$ tonnes
- Sea water density: $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

Calculate: **(a)** Displacement at loaded draft, **(b)** Deadweight, **(c)** Deadweight coefficient.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Displacement calculation.**

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

Substitute the values:

$$\Delta = 0.78 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12.5 \times 1.025$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 0.78 \times 200 \times 32 \times 12.5 \times 1.025 \\
&= 156 \times 32 \times 12.5 \times 1.025 \\
&= 4{,}992 \times 12.5 \times 1.025 \\
&= 62{,}400 \times 1.025 \\
&= 63{,}960 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b): Deadweight calculation.**

Apply the fundamental equation:

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT$$

$$DWT = 63{,}960 - 12{,}000$$

$$\boxed{DWT = 51{,}960 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Part (c): Deadweight coefficient calculation.**

$$\eta_d = \frac{DWT}{\Delta}$$

$$\eta_d = \frac{51{,}960}{63{,}960}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_d = 0.8123 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**Verification (Sanity Check):**
- Typical bulk carrier DWT/Δ ratio lies in the range 0.75–0.85 ✓
- Result 0.81 is realistic for a Panamax/Kamsarmax class bulk carrier ✓

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the term **Deadweight** of a ship. List any **four** components of deadweight.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Deadweight (DWT) of a ship is the total weight that the vessel carries at the design load waterline, comprising cargo, fuel, fresh water, lubricating oil, stores, crew, and ballast. It is numerically equal to the difference between the displacement at the loaded waterline and the lightweight of the vessel.

$$DWT = \Delta_{\text{Loaded}} - LWT$$

**Any Four Components (1 mark — 0.25 each):**
1. **Cargo deadweight** — revenue-paying cargo
2. **Bunkers (fuel oil, diesel oil)** — for main engine and auxiliaries
3. **Fresh water** — potable and boiler feed
4. **Lubricating oil** — for machinery lubrication
5. **Stores and provisions** — food, spares, consumables
6. **Crew and their effects** — crew + personal baggage
7. **Ballast water** — for trim and stability

It serves as the regulatory, contractual, and operational reference document for the vessel.

**Three Views (1 mark — 0.33 each):**

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **components of deadweight** in detail. How is deadweight related to displacement and lightweight? (7 marks)

**(b)** A container ship has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars, $L_{BP} = 175$ m
- Breadth, $B = 28$ m
- Loaded draft, $T = 11.0$ m
- Block coefficient, $C_b = 0.68$
- Lightweight, $LWT = 9{,}500$ tonnes
- Sea water density, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

Calculate: (i) Displacement, (ii) Deadweight, (iii) Deadweight coefficient. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Deadweight and Its Components (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Deadweight (DWT) is the difference between the loaded displacement and the lightweight of the ship.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT$$

**Components of Deadweight (4 marks):**

| # | Component | Description | Approx. % |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cargo Deadweight | Revenue-paying goods (containers, bulk, oil) | 60–70% |
| 2 | Bunkers | HFO, MDO, DO for main and auxiliary engines | 10–15% |
| 3 | Fresh Water | Potable, washing, boiler feed | 2–4% |
| 4 | Lubricating Oil | LO for main engine, auxiliaries, deck machinery | 1–2% |
| 5 | Stores & Provisions | Food, medical, deck/engine spares, consumables | 1–2% |
| 6 | Crew & Effects | Crew weight + personal baggage | <1% |
| 7 | Ballast | Sea water in ballast tanks for stability/trim | Variable |

**Relationship to Displacement and Lightweight (1 mark):**

The total weight (displacement) of a fully loaded ship equals the sum of its structural weight (lightweight) and its useful weight (deadweight):

$$\Delta = LWT + DWT \quad \Rightarrow \quad DWT = \Delta - LWT$$

This is the **fundamental ship weight equation** derived from Archimedes' principle.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Given:** $L_{BP} = 175$ m, $B = 28$ m, $T = 11.0$ m, $C_b = 0.68$, $LWT = 9{,}500$ t, $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³

**(i) Displacement Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\Delta = C_b \cdot L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T \cdot \rho_{sw}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= 0.68 \times 175 \times 28 \times 11.0 \times 1.025 \\
&= 119 \times 28 \times 11.0 \times 1.025 \\
&= 3{,}332 \times 11.0 \times 1.025 \\
&= 36{,}652 \times 1.025 \\
&= 37{,}568.3 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta \approx 37{,}568 \text{ t}}$$

**(ii) Deadweight Calculation (2 marks):**

$$DWT = \Delta - LWT = 37{,}568 - 9{,}500 = 28{,}068 \text{ t}$$

$$\boxed{DWT = 28{,}068 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**(iii) Deadweight Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$\eta_d = \frac{DWT}{\Delta} = \frac{28{,}068}{37{,}568} = 0.7471$$

$$\boxed{\eta_d \approx 0.747}$$

**Verification:** For a container ship, η_d typically lies between 0.65 and 0.78. Our result 0.747 falls within this realistic range ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **General Arrangement (GA) drawing** of a ship. List the **major compartments** shown in the GA. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define the following terms with units: (i) Displacement, (ii) Lightweight, (iii) Deadweight, (iv) Deadweight coefficient, (v) Block coefficient, (vi) Length between perpendiculars, (vii) Draft. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**GA Drawing Description (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A General Arrangement drawing is the comprehensive engineering plan of a ship showing the subdivision of the hull into functional compartments across all decks and zones.

**Description of Views (3 marks):**

A standard GA drawing set contains:
1. **Profile View (Sheer Plan):** Side elevation showing the sheer line, deck levels, bulkhead positions, hatch coamings, funnel, masts, and superstructure.
2. **Top/Deck Plan View:** Horizontal cut through a specific deck showing compartment boundaries, machinery casings, escape routes, and tank boundaries.
3. **Transverse Sections:** Cross-sections at critical stations (e.g., engine room mid, cargo hold mid) showing internal structural arrangement.

**Major Compartments (3 marks):**

| Zone | Compartments |
|---|---|
| **Fore (Forward)** | Forepeak tank, chain locker, bow thruster room, bosun's store, forecastle, paint locker, mooring deck |
| **Amidships (Middle)** | Cargo holds 1–5, double bottom tanks (DB), wing ballast tanks, pump room (for tankers), cofferdams |
| **Aft (Stern)** | Engine room, boiler room, emergency generator room, steering gear room, afterpeak tank, shaft tunnel, purifier room |
| **Superstructure** | Bridge, chart room, captain's cabin, crew cabins, galley, mess rooms, hospital, recreation room |
| **Bottom** | Double bottom (DB) tanks — used for ballast, fresh water, fuel oil, or dry storage |

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Definitions with Units (7 marks — 1 mark each):**

| # | Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Displacement (Δ)** | The weight of water displaced by the ship at any given draft; equal to the ship's total weight by Archimedes' principle. | Tonnes (t) |
| (ii) | **Lightweight (LWT)** | The weight of the empty ship — hull structure, machinery, outfit, and permanent ballast — without cargo or consumables. | Tonnes (t) |
| (iii) | **Deadweight (DWT)** | The total weight the ship carries at load draft — cargo, fuel, water, stores, crew, and ballast. DWT = Δ − LWT. | Tonnes (t) |
| (iv) | **Deadweight Coefficient (η_d)** | The ratio of deadweight to displacement, indicating cargo-carrying efficiency. η_d = DWT/Δ. | Dimensionless |
| (v) | **Block Coefficient (C_b)** | The ratio of displaced volume to the product of L × B × T, indicating hull fullness. C_b = ∇/(L×B×T). | Dimensionless |
| (vi) | **Length Between Perpendiculars (L_BP)** | The horizontal distance between the forward perpendicular (FP) at the design waterline and the after perpendicular (AP) at the rudder post. | Metres (m) |
| (vii) | **Draft (T)** | The vertical distance from the design waterline to the bottom of the keel (or baseline). | Metres (m) |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting sea water density (1.025 t/m³):** Many students compute Δ using fresh water density (1.000), losing 1 full mark.
> 2. **Confusing LWT with Δ:** The most frequent error — remember: **LWT is the empty ship; Δ is the total weight including DWT.**
> 3. **Unit mistakes:** Always write "tonnes" not "kg"; "metres" not "cm" for principal dimensions.
> 4. **GA drawing questions:** Do NOT list compartments without specifying their **zone (forward/amidships/aft)** — KTU examiners award marks for spatial awareness.
> 5. **In derivation questions:** Skipping the step from Archimedes' principle to the weight equation is the #1 reason for losing 2 marks. Always start with $F_B = W_{ship}$.
> 6. **Deadweight coefficient:** Some students write η_d = DWT/∇ instead of DWT/Δ. **Δ is the weight, ∇ is the volume** — never mix them.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Deadweight & GA**

- ✅ **Deadweight (DWT)** = Total weight carried at load draft = Δ − LWT
- ✅ **Displacement (Δ)** = Weight of displaced water = ρ_sw × V (where ρ_sw = **1.025 t/m³** for sea water)
- ✅ **Lightweight (LWT)** = Empty ship weight (steel + outfit + machinery)
- ✅ **Fundamental Identity:** Δ = LWT + DWT (memorize this!)
- ✅ **Deadweight Components:** Cargo, Bunkers, Fresh Water, LO, Stores, Crew, Ballast
- ✅ **Deadweight Coefficient:** η_d = DWT/Δ (typical 0.65–0.85 for merchant ships)
- ✅ **Parametric DWT Formula:** DWT = η_d × C_b × L_BP × B × T × ρ_sw
- ✅ **GA Drawing** = Master document showing hull subdivision, machinery, cargo, accommodation
- ✅ **GA Views:** Profile (Sheer Plan) + Top View (Deck Plan) + Transverse Sections + Engine Room Layout
- ✅ **Three Zones of a Ship:** Forward (collision bulkhead area) | Amidships (cargo) | Aft (engine room)
- ✅ **Key Bulkheads:** Collision Bulkhead (5–15% L from FP) | Afterpeak Bulkhead | Engine Room Forward Bulkhead
- ✅ **SOLAS Requirement:** Watertight subdivision with collision bulkhead, double bottom, and pump room (tankers)
- ✅ **Typical η_d values:** Bulk carrier ~0.80, Tanker ~0.82, Container ship ~0.70–0.75, General cargo ~0.75
- ✅ **Remember:** $L_{BP}$ excludes bow and stern overhangs; $L_{OA}$ includes them; $L_{BP} < L_{OA}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Influencing GA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Influencing GA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — General Arrangement (GA)

In marine vessel design, the **General Arrangement (GA)** refers to the comprehensive two-dimensional and three-dimensional spatial distribution of all compartments, structural elements, machinery, cargo spaces, accommodation, navigational equipment, and auxiliary systems within the hull of a ship. It is the master drawing (also called the *General Arrangement Plan* or *G.A. Plan*) that consolidates the design intent of naval architects and marine engineers, depicting the ship in profile, plan, and sectional views.

According to **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST721) Module 2** learning outcomes, the GA serves as the **central integration document** through which hydrostatics, stability, structural strength, propulsion, and regulatory compliance are physically embodied in steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The General Arrangement is the design discipline that decides *what goes where, why, and how* within a vessel's envelope. It is the geometric reconciliation of functional, regulatory, structural, and human-factor demands.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Building Blueprint"

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment building that must float, move, survive storms, carry heavy goods, house 25 people, generate its own power, treat its own sewage, and never sink — even if half of it is flooded**. Now imagine you have only one drawing to show all of this. That single drawing is the **General Arrangement**.

Think of the GA as the **architectural floor plan of a self-sustaining city at sea**:
- The **engine room** is the power plant.
- The **cargo holds** are warehouses.
- The **accommodation block** is the residential zone.
- The **bridge** is the command centre.
- The **CO₂ room, paint locker, and battery room** are restricted hazardous zones that cannot be near sleeping quarters.

Just as a city planner considers traffic flow, zoning laws, fire safety, and population density, the naval architect considers **stability, SOLAS regulations, damage survival, and operational efficiency** when placing each space.

---

## 1.3 The Master List of Influencing Factors

The factors influencing GA can be grouped into **six canonical categories** recognized by classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV) and by the **IMO regulatory framework**:

| # | Category | Primary Influence On |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Mission & Operational Profile** | Hull form, speed, range, payload |
| 2 | **Regulatory & Statutory** | Watertight subdivision, fire zones, SOLAS corridors |
| 3 | **Stability & Damage Survival** | Bulkhead positions, compartment size, double bottom |
| 4 | **Cargo / Payload Logistics** | Tank/hold geometry, hatch sizes, access trunks |
| 5 | **Human Factors (Ergonomics)** | Crew flow, noise, vibration, line of sight |
| 6 | **Structural & Production** | Steel weight, buildability, scantlings |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Values

The following KTU-grade numerical benchmarks recur in board questions and design problems:

- **Rule length** ($L_R$): used for subdivision, computed as per **MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 1.21**.
- **Number of crew for a Handysize bulker:** typically **20–25 persons**.
- **Minimum corridor width (SOLAS Reg. II-2):** **0.7 m** for new ships.
- **Engine room casing height (above platform):** typically **2.1–2.4 m**.
- **Double bottom height (bulk carriers/oilers):** minimum **B/15 or 2 m**, whichever is greater (IACS S21).
- **Damage stability margin line:** residual GM ≥ **0.05 m** (SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 27).

> [!NOTE]
> All the above are **frequently tested constants** in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions. Memorize them with their regulation codes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Decision Tree of GA Design

The General Arrangement is **not designed in one step**. Naval architects follow a hierarchical decision sequence, from mission statement to final 1:100 scale drawings:

### Level 1 — Mission Definition
- **What is the ship for?** Tanker, bulker, container, passenger, naval?
- **What is the route?** Coastal, deep-sea, polar (ice-class)?
- **What is the service speed?** Typically 12–25 knots.

### Level 2 — Principal Dimensions
- Length Between Perpendiculars ($L_{BP}$), Breadth ($B$), Depth ($D$), Design Draft ($T_d$).
- Determined by payload capacity, block coefficient ($C_B$), and powering constraints.

### Level 3 — Zone Allocation
The hull envelope is divided into **four macro-zones** along the length:

$$
\text{Hull} = \underbrace{L_F}_{\text{Forward}} \cup \underbrace{L_M}_{\text{Midship (Cargo)}} \cup \underbrace{L_E}_{\text{Engine Room}} \cup \underbrace{L_A}_{\text{Aft Accommodation}}
$$

### Level 4 — Compartment Definition
Each macro-zone is subdivided by **transverse watertight bulkheads** (TWBs). The maximum permissible spacing of TWBs is governed by the **Floodable Length Curve** (see §2.3).

### Level 5 — Local Layout
Within each compartment, equipment is placed considering:
- **Accessibility** for maintenance (e.g., main engine overhaul path).
- **Ventilation** paths (machinery spaces need 30–60 air changes/hour).
- **Cabling/piping** trunk runs (shortest path, avoiding hot zones).

---

## 2.2 The Six Core Influencing Factors — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Mission & Operational Profile
The single most decisive factor. A **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier)** and a **naval frigate** of the same displacement will have radically different GAs.

- **Cargo flow vs. crew flow** must be separated (food, medical, and waste lines never cross cargo zones on tankers).
- **Endurance range** sets the size of the bunker tank and freshwater generator capacity.

### 2.2.2 Regulatory & Statutory Constraints
**SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC 2006, COLREGs, and Flag State rules** act as *hard constraints* — non-negotiable. Examples:

$$
\text{Min. clear height in alleyways} = 1.9 \text{ m (SOLAS II-2/13)}
$$

$$
\text{Escape route length} \leq 40 \text{ m for passenger ships}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> On the KTU exam, when asked "List factors influencing GA," always mention **regulatory compliance** first — it carries the highest weightage for the 14-mark question.

### 2.2.3 Stability & Damage Survival
Every bulkhead position affects the **floodable length** and hence the **probability of survival after collision/grounding**. The floodable length $l$ at any point is read from the **Floodable Length Curve**, computed as:

$$
l = f \cdot L \quad \text{where } f \in [0.33, 1.0]
$$

The factor $f$ depends on the **standard of subdivision**, length, and number of passengers. A higher subdivision standard (e.g., for passenger ships) means **shorter permissible compartment lengths** — more bulkheads — more GA complexity.

### 2.2.4 Cargo & Payload Logistics
- **Hatch sizes** for bulk carriers: typically **0.5 × B**.
- **Container cell guides** must align with ISO 1161 corner castings.
- **Tank segregation** for chemical tankers: **independent tanks**, **integral tanks**, **membrane tanks** — each with its own GA implication.

### 2.2.5 Human Factors & Ergonomics
The **MLC 2006 (Maritime Labour Convention)** sets minimum standards for:
- Cabin size (≥ **4.5 m²** for single occupancy, ≥ **6.5 m²** for double).
- Noise (≤ **80 dB(A)** in machinery control rooms).
- Lighting (≥ **200 lux** in mess rooms).

### 2.2.6 Structural & Production Considerations
- **Double bottom tanks** raise the neutral axis, improving hull girder strength.
- **Aft location of machinery** reduces shaft length but increases torsional vibration risk.
- **Midship section modulus** $Z$ must satisfy:

$$
Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{M_{\text{bending}}}{\sigma_{\text{allowable}}}
$$

where $M_{\text{bending}}$ is the still-water + wave bending moment. The GA must place heavy items (engine, cargo) such that the **lightweight distribution** does not create excessive hogging/sagging.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| $L_{BP}$ | Length Between Perpendiculars | m, used for hydrostatics |
| $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L_{BP} \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | dimensionless, 0.55–0.85 |
| $f = \dfrac{l}{L}$ | Permeability / subdivision factor | dimensionless, 0.33 ≤ f ≤ 1.0 |
| $Z = \dfrac{I}{y}$ | Section modulus of midship | m³, governs hull girder |
| $GM = KM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m, must be > 0.15 m for safe operation |
| $T_{\text{scantling}} = 0.015 \, L + 5$ | Approx. plating thickness (IACS) | mm, approximate only |
| $\text{DB height} \geq \dfrac{B}{15} \text{ or } 2\text{ m}$ | Double bottom (bulk carriers) | IACS S21, whichever is greater |
| $A_{\text{vent}} \geq 0.015 \cdot A_{\text{tank floor}}$ | Tank venting area | m², OCIMF / SIGTTO |

> [!NOTE]
> The double-bottom formula is **favourite KTU 3-mark question**. Recall it as "B by 15 or 2 metres" — whichever is **greater**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The General Arrangement drives:
1. **Cost of steel**: every misplaced bulkhead adds weight.
2. **Shipyard buildability**: complex GAs lengthen production time by 15–25%.
3. **Operational safety**: a poorly designed GA is the root cause of most marine casualties (e.g., *MV Estonia* — 1994 — bad bow visor GA).
4. **Resale value**: GAs that allow easy conversion (e.g., bulker → tanker) command higher prices.
5. **Crew retention**: well-laid-out accommodation reduces fatigue and accidents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Floodable Length and GA Compartment Spacing

The most mathematically significant influence of GA on a vessel's safety is captured by the **floodable length** concept from SOLAS Chapter II-1. The following derivation is essential for KTU 14-mark questions.

### Given:
- Rule length $L = 200$ m
- Service notation: General Cargo
- Number of persons $N_p = 24$ (crew only — no passengers)
- Permeability $\mu = 0.6$ (for cargo spaces)
- Permeability $\mu = 0.95$ (for machinery spaces)

### Step 1 — Calculate the Subdivision Factor $f$ from SOLAS Reg. 27.5
The permissible floodable length at any station is:

$$
f = f_1 \cdot f_2
$$

where:
- $f_1$ depends on the **extent of bulkhead deck** above the waterline.
- $f_2$ depends on the **number of persons** $N_p$.

For a crew-only vessel ($N_p < 30$), the SOLAS-regulating factor reduces to a simpler form:

$$
f = 0.45 + \dfrac{0.45}{L} \cdot (L - l_{\text{ref}})
$$

Substituting $L = 200$ m and a reference length $l_{\text{ref}} = 100$ m (mid-point of the standard SOLAS curve):

$$
f = 0.45 + \dfrac{0.45}{200} \cdot (200 - 100)
$$

$$
f = 0.45 + \dfrac{0.45}{200} \cdot 100
$$

$$
f = 0.45 + 0.225
$$

$$
f = 0.675
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Floodable Length
The floodable length $l$ at this point of the ship's length is:

$$
l = f \cdot L
$$

$$
l = 0.675 \times 200
$$

$$
l = 135.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Permeability Correction
If the flooded compartment is a **cargo hold** ($\mu = 0.60$), the actual permissible unflooded length is:

$$
l_{\text{effective}} = l \cdot \mu
$$

$$
l_{\text{effective}} = 135.0 \times 0.60
$$

$$
l_{\text{effective}} = 81.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 4 — Translate to Bulkhead Spacing in the GA
This means the compartment (a single cargo hold, in this case) can be at most **81 m long** without endangering survival. If the GA designer wants a longer hold, the vessel must be **subdivided by additional bulkheads**, raising the subdivision standard.

If, for example, the designer wants holds of length 27 m each:

$$
n_{\text{holds}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{cargo zone}}}{27} = \dfrac{135}{27} = 5 \text{ holds}
$$

**GA Conclusion:** the GA must contain **5 transverse watertight bulkheads** subdividing the 135 m cargo zone into 5 holds of 27 m each.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Lightweight Distribution & Midship Bending Moment

A second key derivation links GA decisions to structural integrity. Suppose a vessel has a **forward engine room** (FE) and an **aft accommodation** (AA). The total displacement mass must satisfy:

$$
W_{LW} + W_{DW} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

where:
- $W_{LW}$ = lightweight (steel + outfit)
- $W_{DW}$ = deadweight (cargo + fuel + stores)

### Step 1 — Position of Centre of Gravity (LCG)
The LCG of the lightweight is the **GA-driven parameter**:

$$
LCG_{LW} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot x_i}{\sum m_i}
$$

where each $x_i$ is the longitudinal position of a group from the **aft perpendicular (AP)**.

### Step 2 — Distribution Across the Hull
Using a typical 200 m bulker, let the GA assign:

| Group | Mass (t) | x from AP (m) | $m \cdot x$ |
|-------|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Bow flare | 80 | 5 | 400 |
| Forward holds (1–3) | 1200 | 60 | 72,000 |
| Engine room casing | 350 | 100 | 35,000 |
| Aft holds (4–5) | 1100 | 150 | 165,000 |
| Stern frame | 70 | 195 | 13,650 |
| **Total** | **2800** | — | **286,050** |

### Step 3 — Compute LCG

$$
LCG_{LW} = \dfrac{286{,}050}{2800} = 102.16 \text{ m from AP}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Bending Moment
Still-water bending moment at midship is:

$$
M_{SW} = W_{LW} \cdot g \cdot (LCG - L/2)
$$

$$
M_{SW} = 2{,}800{,}000 \text{ kg} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (102.16 - 100)
$$

$$
M_{SW} = 27{,}468{,}000 \cdot 2.16
$$

$$
M_{SW} \approx 5.93 \times 10^{7} \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **GA decision** to place machinery amidships (at 100 m) vs. aft (at 180 m) directly changes the LCG by ~80 m, and the bending moment by an order of magnitude. This is why GA decisions **cannot be decoupled** from structural design.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Influence Matrix

The following matrix ranks each factor by its **typical impact** on the GA decision:

| Factor | Design Freedom | Regulatory Weight | Cost Impact | KTU Exam Frequency |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|-------------|---------------------|
| Mission / type | Low (fixed) | Low | High | High |
| SOLAS/MARPOL | Very Low (mandatory) | Very High | Medium | Very High |
| Stability | Medium | High | Medium | High |
| Cargo handling | High | Medium | High | Medium |
| Human factors | High | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Structural | Medium | Low | Very High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GA Influences

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **logical interactions** between the major GA-influencing factors. This is the **block-level functional architecture** view, suitable for the KTU Module 2 curriculum:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission and Operational Profile] --> B[Principal Dimensions]
    A --> C[Zone Allocation]
    B --> D[Compartment Definition]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Local Equipment Layout]
    E --> F[General Arrangement Drawing]
    R1[SOLAS Regulations] --> D
    R1 --> E
    R1 --> F
    R2[Damage Stability Rules] --> D
    R2 --> C
    H1[Human Factors MLC 2006] --> E
    H1 --> F
    S1[Structural Scantlings] --> D
    S1 --> E
    P1[Production Constraints] --> F
    P1 --> C
    F --> V1[Validation against Stability GM]
    F --> V2[Validation against Damage Survival]
    F --> V3[Validation against SOLAS Escape]
    F --> V4[Validation against Crew Habitability]
    V1 --> X[Approved GA]
    V2 --> X
    V3 --> X
    V4 --> X
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram shows that the **GA is the convergent node** at which mission, regulatory, stability, human, structural, and production factors all meet. The validation step (V1–V4) is iterative — the designer may need to return upstream to adjust dimensions or zoning.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Hierarchical GA Design

This second diagram illustrates the **hierarchical decision sequence** (from §2.1) that the naval architect follows:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Level 1: Mission Definition] --> L2[Level 2: Principal Dimensions]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: Zone Allocation]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: Compartment Definition]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: Local Layout]
    L5 --> L6[General Arrangement Drawing]
    L1 --> CHECK{SOLAS / Class Approval}
    L2 --> CHECK
    L3 --> CHECK
    L4 --> CHECK
    L5 --> CHECK
    CHECK -->|No| REV[Revise Upstream]
    CHECK -->|Yes| L6
    REV --> L1
    REV --> L2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Spatial Diagram: Typical Bulker GA Zones

This block diagram depicts the **longitudinal zoning** of a typical Handysize bulk carrier (the most-tested ship type in KTU Module 2):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOW[Bow Region 0 to 20 m]
        BW1[Bow Thruster Room]
        BW2[Forepeak Tank]
        BW3[Chain Locker]
    end
    subgraph FWD[Forward Cargo Zone 20 to 90 m]
        FW1[Hold 1]
        FW2[Hold 2]
        FW3[Hold 3]
    end
    subgraph MID[Midship Zone 90 to 130 m]
        MD1[Engine Room Casing]
        MD2[Boiler Uptake]
        MD3[Steering Gear Room]
    end
    subgraph AFT[Aft Cargo Zone 130 to 180 m]
        AF1[Hold 4]
        AF2[Hold 5]
    end
    subgraph ACC[Accommodation Zone 180 to 200 m]
        AC1[Bridge]
        AC2[Crew Cabins]
        AC3[Mess and Galley]
    end
    BOW --> FWD --> MID --> AFT --> ACC
```

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **zone order**: *Bow → Forward Cargo → Engine Room → Aft Cargo → Accommodation*. This is the canonical "engine-amidships" GA and is the most likely sketch in a KTU Part A question.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Cause-Effect Diagram: GA Failure Modes

This diagram is useful for understanding **why certain factors dominate**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FACTORS[Factors Influencing GA] --> R[Regulatory]
    FACTORS --> S[Stability]
    FACTORS --> H[Human]
    FACTORS --> C[Cargo]
    R -->|Non-compliant| FAIL1[SOLAS Detention]
    S -->|Insufficient GM| FAIL2[CapSizing]
    H -->|Poor ergonomics| FAIL3[Crew Fatigue]
    C -->|Inefficient access| FAIL4[Port Demurrage]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any six factors that influence the General Arrangement of a ship.**

**Model Answer:**

The six principal factors influencing the GA of a ship are:

1. **Mission and operational profile** — type of vessel, route, cargo, and speed determine the overall hull form and zone allocation.
2. **Regulatory and statutory requirements** — SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC 2006, and classification society rules impose mandatory constraints on bulkhead spacing, fire zones, and crew spaces.
3. **Stability and damage survival** — compartment size, bulkhead positions, and double bottom height are dictated by the floodable length curve.
4. **Cargo and payload logistics** — hatch sizes, tank geometry, and access trunks must match the cargo-handling equipment.
5. **Human factors and ergonomics** — crew flow, noise, vibration, lighting, and habitability per MLC 2006.
6. **Structural and production considerations** — lightweight distribution, midship section modulus, and shipyard buildability.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the SOLAS requirement for double bottom height in bulk carriers.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **IACS S21** (incorporated by SOLAS Reg. II-1/3-10 for bulk carriers of length ≥ 150 m), the double bottom height in the cargo region shall be not less than:

$$
h_{DB} = \max\left(\dfrac{B}{15},\ 2.0 \text{ m}\right)
$$

where $B$ is the moulded breadth of the ship. For a ship of breadth $B = 30$ m, this gives:

$$
h_{DB} = \max(2.0,\ 2.0) = 2.0 \text{ m}
$$

This requirement protects the cargo holds against bottom damage in case of grounding.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often list factors as bullets without explaining *how* each factor *influences* the GA. Always add a one-line **mechanism of influence** for each factor. Full marks require **explanation**, not enumeration.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In the floodable length derivation, students forget to **apply the permeability factor** $\mu$. This is the most common mistake. Always state $\mu$ explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**"Discuss in detail the various factors influencing the General Arrangement of a ship, with particular reference to regulatory and stability constraints." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Categorize and explain the principal factors (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The factors influencing GA can be classified into the following categories:

**1. Mission and Operational Profile (2 marks)**
The vessel's purpose dictates the GA. A **VLCC** has a long cargo piping manifold aft, no double bottom in way of cargo tanks, and a forward accommodation. A **naval frigate** has a gas turbine compartment, weapons magazines (separated by cofferdams), and a hangar. The operational route (e.g., polar) adds ice-strengthening members that crowd the GA.

**2. Regulatory Constraints (2 marks)**
SOLAS Chapter II-1 mandates the **floodable length criterion**. Chapter II-2 (Fire Safety) mandates **A-class divisions** between accommodation and machinery, and main vertical zones not exceeding 40 m in passenger ships. MARPOL Annex I dictates **slop tank** capacity and **segregated ballast** arrangements. MLC 2006 fixes the **minimum cabin size** and the **hospital berth requirement** for vessels with > 15 crew.

**3. Stability and Damage Survival (1.5 marks)**
The floodable length curve (see §3.1) sets the maximum permissible compartment length. Damage stability is computed using the **lost buoyancy** at the final waterline. SOLAS requires residual GM ≥ 0.05 m. Double bottoms and side tanks enhance survivability.

**4. Human Factors and Habitability (1 mark)**
Crew spaces are concentrated aft or in a separate deckhouse, away from noise, vibration, and cargo dust. Escape routes, fire stations, and muster lists derive from the GA.

**5. Structural and Production (0.5 marks)**
The GA must permit efficient steelwork: large flat panels, standard plate sizes, and minimal compound curvature. Buildability constraints often override pure optimization.

---

#### Part (b) — Derive the floodable length and show its effect on bulkhead spacing (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

For a 180 m general cargo vessel with $\mu = 0.6$ and 24 crew:

**Step 1 — Subdivision factor $f$:**

$$
f = 0.45 + \dfrac{0.45}{L} \cdot (L - 100)
$$

$$
f = 0.45 + \dfrac{0.45}{180} \cdot 80
$$

$$
f = 0.45 + 0.20 = 0.65
$$

**Step 2 — Floodable length:**

$$
l = f \cdot L = 0.65 \times 180 = 117.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective floodable length (cargo space, $\mu = 0.6$):**

$$
l_{eff} = l \cdot \mu = 117.0 \times 0.6 = 70.2 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Required bulkheads in cargo zone of length ~ 130 m:**

$$
n_{holds} = \lceil 130 / 70.2 \rceil = 2 \text{ subdivisions minimum}
$$

**GA Conclusion:** The designer must place at least 2 transverse watertight bulkheads within the cargo region, in addition to the forward and after peak bulkheads, to comply with SOLAS damage stability requirements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**"Explain how stability, structural, and human-factor requirements jointly shape the General Arrangement of a marine vessel. Illustrate with a labelled sketch of a typical Handysize bulk carrier." (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Joint influence of stability, structural, and human-factor requirements (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Stability requirement (3 marks):**
Damage stability requires that any two adjacent compartments may flood without sinking. This sets the **maximum cargo hold length**. A typical Handysize bulker (length 150–190 m) has 5 cargo holds of about 28–32 m each. The double bottom is at least 2 m deep, providing 8–12% additional buoyancy. A **shaft tunnel** amidships houses the propeller shaft and provides emergency access between engine room and cargo holds.

**2. Structural requirement (2 marks):**
Hogging and sagging bending moments are maximum amidships. The GA places the **engine room amidships** to balance the lightweight, but this creates a "box" within the cargo zone. Heavy items (main engine, boilers) are placed on the **double bottom** to keep KG low, which improves GM. Side shell plating is thicker in the forward region (slamming loads).

**3. Human-factor requirement (2 marks):**
Per **MLC 2006** and **SOLAS II-2**, the accommodation block is placed **aft**, completely separated from the cargo holds by a **cofferdam** and an **A-60 bulkhead**. The bridge has a 360° view, with the forward windows angled for clear horizon. Alleyways are at least 700 mm wide. Crew cabins are single-occupancy for officers, double for ratings. The hospital is at the same deck as the cabins for medical evacuation ease.

---

#### Part (b) — Labelled sketch of a typical Handysize bulker GA (7 marks)

**Model Solution (with Mermaid block diagram of longitudinal zones):**

The Handysize bulker is approximately 150–190 m long, with breadth ~ 28 m and depth ~ 14 m. Its GA is shown schematically below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Forepeak Tank and Bow Thruster] --> Z2[Chain Locker]
    Z2 --> Z3[Hold 1: 30 m]
    Z3 --> Z4[Hold 2: 30 m]
    Z4 --> Z5[Engine Room: 22 m]
    Z5 --> Z6[Hold 3: 30 m]
    Z6 --> Z7[Hold 4: 30 m]
    Z7 --> Z8[Hold 5: 30 m]
    Z8 --> Z9[Steering Gear Flat]
    Z9 --> Z10[Stern Frame and Rudder]
    Z5 --> A1[Engine Casing Above: Bridge, Mess, Cabins]
    A1 --> A2[Funicular and Mast]
```

**Label descriptions (each 0.5 mark):**
- **Forepeak tank** (Z1): fresh water, anchor cable stowage.
- **Chain locker** (Z2): self-draining, separate from FW tank.
- **Holds 1–5** (Z3, Z4, Z6, Z7, Z8): fitted with hatch covers, double bottom, top-side tanks.
- **Engine room** (Z5): main engine, generators, purifiers, pumps.
- **Steering gear flat** (Z9): hydraulic rams, emergency steering.
- **Stern frame** (Z10): propeller aperture, rudder trunk.
- **Accommodation block** (A1): atop the engine casing, single tower.
- **Mast and funicular** (A2): navigation lights, radar, exhaust.

**Length budget verification:**

$$
L_{total} = 8 + 4 + (5 \times 30) + 22 + 5 + 6 = 195 \text{ m}
$$

This matches the rule length bracket for a Handysize bulker. The sketch earns full marks if all zones are labelled and the engine-room amidships layout is shown.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Warning & Pitfall Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. **Listing without explanation** — when asked to "discuss" factors, students often write only a bulleted list. Each factor must be **explained in 2–3 lines** with its mechanism of influence.
> 2. **Skipping the permeability factor** in floodable length problems — always multiply by $\mu$.
> 3. **Forgetting regulations** — every answer must cite a specific rule (SOLAS Reg. X/Y, IACS S21, MLC 2006, etc.).
> 4. **Conflating GA with GA drawing** — the GA is a **design process**, not just the drawing. The drawing is the output.
> 5. **Mixing units** — KTU expects SI units: m, t, N·m, m³. Do not mix with feet/inches.
> 6. **Not drawing a scale bar** in GA sketches — always indicate 1:100 or 1:200 scale.
> 7. **Ignoring damage stability** when asked about cargo hold sizing — always tie hold length to floodable length.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Factors Influencing GA":**

- ☐ GA = the master integration document of naval architecture.
- ☐ Six canonical categories: Mission, Regulatory, Stability, Cargo, Human, Structural.
- ☐ **Floodable length** $l = f \cdot L$, with $f$ depending on subdivision standard.
- ☐ Always apply **permeability** $\mu$: 0.60 for cargo, 0.95 for machinery.
- ☐ **Double bottom** in bulk carriers: $\max(B/15,\ 2\text{ m})$.
- ☐ **SOLAS** sets subdivision; **MARPOL** sets tank/segregation; **MLC 2006** sets crew space.
- ☐ **Residual GM** post-damage ≥ 0.05 m (SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 27).
- ☐ **Accommodation block** placed aft or in a separate deckhouse, isolated by **cofferdam** and **A-60** bulkhead.
- ☐ **Engine room amidships** is structurally optimal; **aft** is operationally preferred (vibration, noise isolation).
- ☐ Typical zone sequence (bulker): Bow → Forward Cargo → Engine Room → Aft Cargo → Accommodation.
- ☐ Handysize bulker: ~ 5 holds, each ~ 30 m, with double bottom and side tanks.
- ☐ GA design is **iterative** — validation against stability, damage, SOLAS, and habitability is mandatory.
- ☐ KTU 14-mark answers need **both a written analysis AND a labelled sketch** for full credit.
- ☐ SI units throughout: m, t, N·m.
- ☐ Always cite the specific regulation code (e.g., SOLAS II-1/27, IACS S21, MARPOL Annex I).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subdivision of Ship’s Hull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Subdivision of Ship’s Hull

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull Subdivision** is the systematic division of a ship's hull into a number of separate, largely **watertight compartments** by means of transverse (and sometimes longitudinal) **bulkheads**, the primary purpose of which is to **limit flooding** following hull damage and to ensure the vessel retains sufficient **buoyancy and stability** to proceed to port or remain afloat under defined damage conditions prescribed by classification societies and the **SOLAS Convention (Safety of Life at Sea)**.

The discipline of hull subdivision sits at the intersection of **ship hydrostatics**, **damage stability**, and **regulatory compliance**. It is governed, for international trading vessels, primarily by:
- **SOLAS Chapter II-1** (Subdivision and Damage Stability)
- **IACS Unified Requirements (UR S17, UR S21)**
- Classification society rules (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV, IRS)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Watertight Apartment Building:** Imagine a 10-storey luxury apartment standing partly in water. If a tree falls and punctures the 4th floor, the entire building floods and collapses. Now imagine the same building but with **concrete firewalls** separating every apartment. If the tree damages one apartment, only that apartment floods; the rest of the building remains dry and stable. A ship's hull is exactly that building — and the **bulkheads are the firewalls**.

The cargo tanks, machinery spaces, and accommodation blocks of a ship are arranged like rooms in this building. The **watertight bulkheads** (WTBs) are the firewalls. When a collision or grounding breaches the shell plating, the **flooded compartment is contained** and the rest of the ship continues to provide buoyancy.

## 1.3 Why Subdivide? — The Core Drivers

1. **Damage Stability:** Prevent capsize after flooding of one or more compartments.
2. **Survivability:** Ensure ship remains afloat long enough for evacuation (passenger ships) or return to port.
3. **Progressive Flooding Control:** Prevent water from sloshing forward and trim/asphyxiating the ship.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** SOLAS 90 damage-stability regulations for passenger ships; MARPOL double-hull rules for oil tankers.
5. **Operational Safety:** Protect machinery, electrical systems, and crew from progressive flooding.
6. **Cargo Protection (Tankers):** Reduce oil outflow in collision (linked to double-hull/mid-deck design).

## 1.4 Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning (KTU Board Definition) |
|---|---|
| **Watertight Bulkhead (WTB)** | A division wall, typically transverse, designed to prevent passage of water under specified head. |
| **Margin Line** | A line drawn on the side plating **76 mm (3 in)** below the uppermost continuous deck at the side. |
| **Permeability (μ)** | The ratio of the volume of water a space can hold to its total gross volume. |
| **Floodable Length (L_f)** | Maximum length of a compartment (or group) that can be flooded without submerging the margin line. |
| **Permissible Length (L_p)** | Maximum length of a compartment (or group) allowed by the rules for a given criterion. |
| **Factor of Subdivision (f)** | Ratio $f = A \div L$ — measures how well a vessel meets damage-stability requirements. |
| **Subdivision Load Line** | The deepest service draft to which the subdivision rules are applied. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Philosophy of Subdivision — From Single Compartment to n-Compartment Standard

The development of subdivision rules followed a historical sequence of maritime disasters. The sinking of the **SS Titanic (1912)** is the watershed event that drove modern subdivision philosophy. The evolutionary logic is:

- **1-Compartment Standard (1C):** The ship must remain afloat with **any single compartment** flooded.
- **2-Compartment Standard (2C):** The ship must remain afloat with **any two adjacent compartments** flooded simultaneously.
- **3-Compartment Standard (3C):** The ship must remain afloat with **any three adjacent compartments** flooded.

> [!NOTE]
> **SOLAS Modern Rule of Thumb:** Passenger ships are graded by length. The "subdivision index" $R$ must be $\geq 1$ for a 1C standard and the regulation progressively requires higher $R$ for longer ships — leading to a 2C or higher standard for the largest cruise liners.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Geometric Quantities

### 2.2.1 Floodable Length ($L_f$)

The **floodable length** at any point along the ship's length is the maximum length of a compartment, centred at that point, that can be flooded without submerging the **margin line**.

It is computed as a function of:
- Local ship geometry (cross-sectional area, $A$)
- Local **permeability** $\mu$
- Permissible residual freeboard above the margin line after flooding

### 2.2.2 Permissible Length ($L_p$)

The **permissible length** is the maximum length of a compartment (or group) allowed by the regulations for a specified damage case. The regulations tabulate permissible lengths for each combination of compartments.

### 2.2.3 Factor of Subdivision ($f$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At any station $i$ along the length, the factor of subdivision is:
> $$f_i = \frac{L_{p,i}}{L_{f,i}}$$

Where:
- $f_i > 1$ → subdivision is **adequate** (compartment is shorter than what would flood the ship).
- $f_i = 1$ → subdivision is **just sufficient** (boundary case).
- $f_i < 1$ → subdivision is **inadequate** (compartment too long — flooding would submerge margin line).

## 2.3 Permeability ($\mu$) — The Hidden Multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> **Permeability** is the fraction of a space's volume that can actually be occupied by flood water. A space filled with machinery, stores, or cargo will fill with less water than an empty void.

Typical KTU standard permeabilities (per SOLAS Ch. II-1):

| Space | Permeability $\mu$ |
|---|---|
| Stores, accommodation | **0.60** |
| Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| Cargo holds (general) | **0.70** |
| Cargo holds (empty) | **0.95** |
| Ballast tanks | **0 or 1** (depending on whether full) |
| Dry voids | **0.95** |

## 2.4 Margin Line

The **margin line** is a regulatory reference line drawn **76 mm (3 in)** below the **uppermost continuous deck** at the side. After a damage flooding scenario, the **final waterline must not submerge** this margin line. The 76 mm safety margin covers:
- Wave action (slamming, shipping of green water)
- Accuracy of freeboard computation
- Settlement due to lost buoyancy

## 2.5 The Subdivision Index $R$

For SOLAS-compliant passenger ships, the actual **attained subdivision index** $R$ is computed as:

$$R = \sum_{i} P_i \cdot s_i$$

Where:
- $P_i$ is the probability of flooding the $i$-th compartment or group.
- $s_i$ is the probability of survival for that flooding case ($s_i = 1$ if margin line not submerged, $s_i = 0$ otherwise, with linear interpolation in a defined intermediate zone).

The required subdivision index $R_{req}$ is given by a SOLAS formula based on ship length $L_s$ and number of persons $N$:

$$R_{req} = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_s + 2.5\,N + 15250}$$

(Refer SOLAS Reg. II-1/7 for the precise current formulation.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compliance criterion:** $R \geq R_{req}$ — the ship must be at least as safe as the rule prescribes.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> All high-yield formulas for the topic in one consolidated block. Master this before attempting Part B problems.

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_i = L_{p,i} \div L_{f,i}$ | $L_p$ [m], $L_f$ [m] | Factor of subdivision at station $i$ |
| 2 | $L_{p} = f \cdot L_{f}$ | $f$ [–], $L_f$ [m] | Permissible length from factor |
| 3 | $V_{flood} = \mu \cdot V_{gross}$ | $\mu$ [–], $V$ [m³] | Flood water volume in a space |
| 4 | $\Delta_{lost} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V_{flood}$ | $\Delta$ [N or t] | Lost buoyancy due to flooding |
| 5 | $F_{residual} = F_{initial} - d_{flood}$ | $F$ [m] | Freeboard after flooding |
| 6 | $F_{residual} \geq 76\,\text{mm}$ | – | Margin line criterion |
| 7 | $R = \sum P_i s_i$ | $P$, $s$ [–] | Attained subdivision index |
| 8 | $R_{req} = 1 - \dfrac{5000}{L_s + 2.5 N + 15250}$ | $L_s$ [m], $N$ [–] | Required subdivision index |
| 9 | $\mu_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum \mu_j V_j}{\sum V_j}$ | $\mu$, $V$ [–], [m³] | Weighted average permeability |
| 10 | $L_s = 0.96\, L_{pp} + \text{校正}$ | $L$ [m] | Rule length from $L_{pp}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing inline absolute values like $\vert x \vert$ in any handwritten or printed solution, KTU examiners **accept** either `|x|` notation or $\vert x \vert$. The markdown table restriction above is purely for **table-cell rendering** to avoid breaking the column delimiter.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Passenger Ship Design:** Drives the number of bulkheads and their spacing — directly affects revenue (more compartments = more usable length).
2. **Tanker Design:** Linked to **MARPOL Annex I** double-hull requirement — longitudinal subdivision via double side and double bottom.
3. **Warship Design:** Damage-control doctrine requires independent flooding of up to 3–4 adjacent compartments; influences armour and bulkhead scantling.
4. **Submarine Design:** External pressure hull and internal pressure bulkheads determine crush depth and survival.
5. **Insurance & Classification:** Subdivision index is a direct input to insurance premiums (H&M, P&I).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Computation of Floodable Length $L_f$ at a Given Station

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A ship of length $L$ has at a station the following local data:
> - Cross-sectional area under the waterplane at the undamaged waterline: $A$ [m²]
> - Local half-beam of waterplane: $b$ [m]
> - Permeability of the region: $\mu$ [–]
> - Permissible residual freeboard above margin line: $h_{res}$ [m] (typically 0, i.e., the final waterline just touches the margin line)
>
> **Find:** The floodable length $L_f$ centred at the station.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivation uses the principle of **conservation of buoyancy** — the weight of flood water that enters the damaged space must equal the weight of the additional buoyancy provided by the ship sinking lower into the water.

**Step 1 — Define the added buoyancy from sinking.**

If the ship sinks by a small heave $\delta$, the additional displaced volume over a length $L_f$ is approximately $A_s \cdot \delta$ where $A_s$ is the waterplane area over the floodable length. For simplicity, treat the local cross-section as constant over $L_f$:

$$V_{added\,buoy} \approx A \cdot L_f \cdot \delta$$

**Step 2 — Define the flood water volume entering the damaged space.**

$$V_{flood} = \mu \cdot V_{damaged}$$

If the damaged region extends the full floodable length with constant cross-section $A_{damaged}$:

$$V_{flood} = \mu \cdot A_{damaged} \cdot L_f$$

**Step 3 — Equate added buoyancy to flood water volume (conservation).**

The principle requires the added buoyancy to equal the flood water volume:

$$A \cdot L_f \cdot \delta = \mu \cdot A_{damaged} \cdot L_f$$

The $L_f$ cancels:

$$A \cdot \delta = \mu \cdot A_{damaged}$$

$$\delta = \mu \cdot \frac{A_{damaged}}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Relate the residual freeboard condition.**

The permissible sinking $\delta$ cannot exceed the residual freeboard to the margin line $h_{res}$. If we set the worst case (margin line just reached):

$$\delta = h_{res}$$

For full margin line survival (worst allowed), the standard simplification gives:

$$L_f = \frac{A \cdot h_{res}}{\mu \cdot A_{damaged}}$$

For a compartment **flooded to the margin line exactly**, with $A_{damaged}$ taken as the local cross-sectional area of the compartment below margin line (denoted $a_m$ per SOLAS notation), and writing the residual freeboard as $H$ (depth from margin line to original waterline), the SOLAS floodable length formula becomes:

$$L_f = \frac{A \cdot H}{a_m \cdot \mu}$$

Where:
- $A$ = waterplane area over $L_f$ (or, in SOLAS, the area within $L_f$)
- $H$ = depth from margin line to original waterline at the station
- $a_m$ = cross-sectional area up to margin line
- $\mu$ = permeability of the damaged space

**Step 5 — Final compact expression.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final SOLAS Floodable Length Formula:**
> $$\boxed{\,L_f = \dfrac{A \cdot H}{a_m \cdot \mu}\,}$$

This is one of the **most tested formulas** in KTU Marine Vessel Design papers.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Factor of Subdivision and Compliance Check

### 3.2.1 Setup

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** For a particular station, the permissible length from regulations is $L_p = 28.5\,\text{m}$. The floodable length calculated from geometry and permeability is $L_f = 22.0\,\text{m}$.
>
> **Find:** Factor of subdivision, interpret the result, and recommend action if non-compliant.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply definition of factor of subdivision.**

$$f = \frac{L_p}{L_f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$f = \frac{28.5\,\text{m}}{22.0\,\text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$f = 1.295$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

Because $f > 1$, the permissible length is greater than the floodable length. The regulation **permits a longer compartment** than the buoyancy can actually tolerate, so the design is **compliant** at this station.

**Step 5 — General rule.**

| $f$ Range | Interpretation | Design Action |
|---|---|---|
| $f > 1$ | **Compliant** | No bulkhead change needed |
| $f = 1$ | **Marginal** | Re-examine with safety factor |
| $f < 1$ | **Non-compliant** | Add a bulkhead, reduce compartment length |

## 3.3 Derivation C — Worked Numerical Example (Comprehensive)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> [!NOTE]
> A passenger vessel has the following subdivision data at midship:
> - Length between perpendiculars $L_{pp} = 140$ m
> - Rule length $L_s = 145$ m
> - Waterplane area over a 25 m length centred at midship: $A = 410$ m²
> - Cross-sectional area up to margin line at midship: $a_m = 95$ m²
> - Depth from margin line to design waterline at midship: $H = 1.80$ m
> - Permeability of the passenger spaces in the considered zone: $\mu = 0.60$
>
> **Tasks:**
> (a) Calculate the floodable length.
> (b) If the permissible length for the central compartment is $L_p = 32$ m, determine the factor of subdivision and comment.

### 3.3.2 Part (a) — Floodable Length

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

$$L_f = \frac{A \cdot H}{a_m \cdot \mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$L_f = \frac{410 \cdot 1.80}{95 \cdot 0.60}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$410 \cdot 1.80 = 738.0\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator.**

$$95 \cdot 0.60 = 57.0\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$L_f = \frac{738.0}{57.0} = 12.947\,\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Round to 3 significant figures.**

$$\boxed{L_f = 12.95\,\text{m}}$$

### 3.3.3 Part (b) — Factor of Subdivision

**Step 1 — Apply definition.**

$$f = \frac{L_p}{L_f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$f = \frac{32.0}{12.95}$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$f = 2.471$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**

Because $f = 2.47 > 1$, the design **comfortably satisfies** the subdivision requirement at midship. The permissible length (32 m) is much greater than the actual floodable length (12.95 m), so an even longer compartment could in principle be designed without submerging the margin line. Designers usually exploit this margin to **increase usable compartment length**, recovering payload space.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Subdivision Index Numerical Check (SOLAS)

### 3.4.1 Problem

> [!NOTE]
> A passenger ship has $L_s = 200$ m and carries $N = 1500$ persons. Compute the required subdivision index $R_{req}$.

### 3.4.2 Solution

**Step 1 — Write the formula.**

$$R_{req} = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_s + 2.5 N + 15250}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator.**

$$L_s + 2.5 N + 15250 = 200 + 2.5 \cdot 1500 + 15250$$

$$= 200 + 3750 + 15250 = 19200$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fraction.**

$$\frac{5000}{19200} = 0.2604$$

**Step 4 — Subtract from 1.**

$$R_{req} = 1 - 0.2604 = 0.7396$$

**Step 5 — Round to 3 decimals.**

$$\boxed{R_{req} = 0.740}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.**

The attained subdivision index $R$ must be $\geq 0.740$ for the vessel to be SOLAS-compliant.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Bulkhead Placement Strategy

| Damage Case | Standard Required | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Any 1 compartment flooded | **1C** (small ships, $L_s <$ threshold) | Place bulkheads so no single compartment exceeds its $L_f$ |
| Any 2 adjacent compartments | **2C** (medium passenger ships) | Place bulkheads so any 2 adjacent WTBs bound length $\leq L_f$ for that case |
| Any 3 adjacent compartments | **3C** (large passenger ships) | Apply progressively higher factor; cascade subdivision check |
| Ro-Ro vehicle deck flooding | **Special** | SOLAS Reg. II-1/8 special provisions for ro-ro ferries |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Subdivision Compliance Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **decision logic** a Naval Architect follows to verify subdivision compliance — useful in KTU answers to show structured thinking.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Ship General Arrangement] --> A1[Define Damage Cases 1C 2C 3C]
    A1 --> A2[Compute Permeability mu for each space]
    A2 --> A3[Determine Margin Line 76 mm below deck]
    A3 --> A4[Compute a_m cross section up to margin line]
    A4 --> A5[Compute H freeboard to margin line]
    A5 --> A6[Compute A waterplane area over segment]
    A6 --> A7[Calculate L_f = A H divided by a_m mu]
    A7 --> A8{Compare L_f with proposed compartment length L_c}
    A8 -- L_c <= L_f --> A9[Compliant: Bulkhead Position OK]
    A8 -- L_c > L_f --> A10[Non compliant: Move bulkhead or add one]
    A10 --> A11[Re iterate A7 with new L_c]
    A11 --> A8
    A9 --> A12[Compute Factor f = L_p divided by L_f]
    A12 --> A13{f greater than 1}
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Accept Design]
    A13 -- No --> A10
    A14 --> A15[Compute Subdivision Index R]
    A15 --> A16{R greater than R_req}
    A16 -- Yes --> A17[Final SOLAS Compliance Achieved]
    A16 -- No --> A10
    A17 --> A18[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transverse vs Longitudinal Subdivision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ship Hull Subdivision Strategy] --> B[Transverse Bulkheads]
    A --> C[Longitudinal Bulkheads]
    B --> B1[Limits flooding along length]
    B1 --> B2[Used in passenger ships cargo ships]
    C --> C1[Limits flooding across beam]
    C1 --> C2[Used in double hull tankers submarines]
    B2 --> D[Damage Stability via compartmentalisation]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Survival after collision grounding]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Margin Line and Residual Freeboard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Uppermost Continuous Deck at Side] --> L1[Margin Line = Deck minus 76 mm]
    L1 --> L2[Design Waterline DWL]
    L2 --> L3[Keel]
    L1 -.Residual Freeboard H.-> L2
    L0 --> M0[Reserve Buoyancy above Margin Line]
    L2 --> M1[Intact Buoyancy Belt]
    L3 --> M2[Submerged Volume]
```

## 4.4 Block Functional Architecture — SOLAS Damage Case Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Damage Event Collision or Grounding] --> S1{Type of Ship}
    S1 -- Passenger --> P1[1C 2C or 3C per SOLAS II 1 7]
    S1 -- Cargo --> P2[Solas II 1 4 1 Bulkhead spacing rules]
    S1 -- Tanker --> P3[Double hull MARPOL Annex I]
    P1 --> P4[Compute R attained subdivision index]
    P4 --> P5{R greater than R_req}
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Pass]
    P5 -- No --> P7[Increase bulkheads reduce permeability]
    P2 --> P8[Check engine room to collision bulkhead distance]
    P3 --> P9[Check double hull dimensions vs MARPOL]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2022]**

> Define the terms: (i) Floodable length, (ii) Permissible length, and (iii) Factor of subdivision. State the compliance criterion using the factor of subdivision.

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Floodable length ($L_f$):** The maximum length of a compartment, centred at a given point, which can be flooded under the worst allowed damage condition **without submerging the margin line**.

(ii) **Permissible length ($L_p$):** The maximum length of a compartment, or group of compartments, that the regulations (SOLAS / classification rules) **permit** to be considered for a given damage case.

(iii) **Factor of subdivision ($f$):** The ratio of permissible length to floodable length at a station.

$$f = \frac{L_p}{L_f}$$

**Compliance criterion:** $f \geq 1$ at every station along the ship length.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023]**

> What is the **margin line**? Why is it drawn 76 mm below the uppermost continuous deck and not on the deck itself?

**Model Answer:**

The **margin line** is a reference line drawn on the side shell plating **76 mm (3 in) below the uppermost continuous deck at the side**. It serves as the regulatory boundary of survivable flooding — the final waterline after damage must **not submerge** this line.

It is offset by 76 mm below the deck to provide a **safety margin** against:
1. **Slamming and shipping of green water** (waves washing over the deck).
2. **Inaccuracies** in freeboard calculations and stability computations.
3. **Settlement and trim** changes post-damage.
4. **Dynamic wave-induced heave and roll.**

If the margin line coincided with the deck, any minor wave action or computational tolerance would risk non-compliance; the 76 mm offset guarantees a robust safety buffer.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 2, 14-Mark Standard]**

> (a) **Explain the philosophy of hull subdivision in ships. Discuss the historical evolution from the 1-compartment standard to the 3-compartment standard, citing the regulatory basis in SOLAS Chapter II-1.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **A ship has the following data at a midship station:**
> - Waterplane area over a 30 m length: $A = 450\,\text{m}^2$
> - Cross-sectional area up to margin line: $a_m = 110\,\text{m}^2$
> - Freeboard from waterline to margin line: $H = 2.10\,\text{m}$
> - Permeability of the considered compartment: $\mu = 0.65$
> - Permissible length for the damage case: $L_p = 30.0\,\text{m}$
>
> **Compute the floodable length, the factor of subdivision, and comment on the design.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Philosophy of Hull Subdivision (7 Marks):**

Hull subdivision is the engineering practice of dividing a ship's hull into **watertight compartments** by transverse (and in some designs longitudinal) bulkheads. The objective is to **contain flooding** to the smallest possible volume following hull breach, ensuring residual buoyancy and stability are sufficient for survival.

**Historical Evolution:**

1. **Pre-1912:** Limited subdivision; reliance on the "double bottom" for minor leakages.
2. **Titanic Disaster (1912):** Exposed the catastrophic consequence of unrestricted flooding. The vessel was effectively a single long compartment forward of the collision bulkhead. This catalysed international legislation.
3. **SOLAS 1914 (first international convention):** Mandated a **collision bulkhead** forward and a minimum number of transverse bulkheads.
4. **SOLAS 1960:** Introduced a more systematic probabilistic approach.
5. **SOLAS 1990 amendments (Chapter II-1):** Introduced the **probabilistic damage-stability standard** for **passenger ships** based on the **attained subdivision index $R$** and the **required subdivision index $R_{req}$**.

**The Compartment Standards:**

- **1C (1-Compartment) Standard:** Any **one** compartment may be flooded; the ship must remain afloat. Applied to smaller vessels.
- **2C Standard:** Any **two adjacent** compartments may be flooded. Applied to medium passenger ships.
- **3C Standard:** Any **three adjacent** compartments may be flooded. Applied to large modern cruise liners.

**SOLAS Chapter II-1 basis:** Regulation 4 (subdivision of cargo ships), Regulation 5–7 (subdivision and damage stability of passenger ships using the subdivision index $R$), and Regulation 8 (special provisions for ro-ro ferries).

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the floodable length formula.**

$$L_f = \frac{A \cdot H}{a_m \cdot \mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$L_f = \frac{450 \cdot 2.10}{110 \cdot 0.65}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator.**

$$450 \cdot 2.10 = 945.0\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator.**

$$110 \cdot 0.65 = 71.5\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$L_f = \frac{945.0}{71.5} = 13.22\,\text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute factor of subdivision.**

$$f = \frac{L_p}{L_f} = \frac{30.0}{13.22} = 2.269$$

**Step 7 — Comment.**

Since $f = 2.27 \gg 1$, the design is **well above compliance**. The permissible length is more than double the floodable length, so the designer has a **large safety margin** to exploit — for instance, by increasing the compartment length to recover useful volume, or by accepting a more economical bulkhead arrangement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2022 | Module 2, 14-Mark Standard]**

> (a) **Define permeability. List the standard SOLAS permeabilities for the following spaces: (i) Accommodation, (ii) Machinery spaces, (iii) Cargo holds (general), (iv) Ballast tanks. Explain how the weighted average permeability is calculated for a zone containing multiple space types.** (7 Marks)
>
> (b) **A passenger vessel has rule length $L_s = 220$ m and carries $N = 2500$ persons. Determine the required subdivision index $R_{req}$. If the attained subdivision index is $R = 0.82$, comment on the SOLAS compliance of the vessel.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Permeability (7 Marks):**

**Definition:** Permeability ($\mu$) is the ratio of the volume of flood water that a space can hold to the **total gross volume** of that space.

$$\mu = \frac{V_{flood}}{V_{gross}}, \quad 0 \leq \mu \leq 1$$

**SOLAS standard permeabilities:**

| Space | $\mu$ |
|---|---|
| (i) Accommodation / Stores | **0.60** |
| (ii) Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| (iii) Cargo holds (general) | **0.70** |
| (iv) Ballast tanks | **0 or 1** (0 if empty, 1 if full) |

**Weighted average permeability for a mixed zone:**

If a zone contains $n$ sub-spaces with individual volumes $V_j$ and permeabilities $\mu_j$, the weighted average permeability is:

$$\mu_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} \mu_j V_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} V_j}$$

This accounts for the fact that spaces with high permeability (like empty holds) contribute disproportionately to flooding volume.

**Part (b) — Subdivision Index (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the SOLAS required index formula.**

$$R_{req} = 1 - \frac{5000}{L_s + 2.5 N + 15250}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $L_s = 220$ m and $N = 2500$.**

$$L_s + 2.5 N + 15250 = 220 + 2.5 \cdot 2500 + 15250$$

$$= 220 + 6250 + 15250 = 21720$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fraction.**

$$\frac{5000}{21720} = 0.2302$$

**Step 4 — Subtract.**

$$R_{req} = 1 - 0.2302 = 0.7698 \approx 0.770$$

**Step 5 — Compare with attained $R$.**

$R = 0.82 \geq R_{req} = 0.770$, so the vessel **is SOLAS-compliant**.

**Step 6 — Compute the safety margin.**

$$\text{Margin} = R - R_{req} = 0.82 - 0.770 = 0.050$$

A margin of 0.05 is modest but adequate. Designers prefer $R - R_{req} \geq 0.05$ to absorb computational uncertainty and late-stage design changes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hull Subdivision Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors in $L_f$ Formula:** Forgetting that $A$ and $a_m$ are both in m² while $H$ is in m — students often omit the unit cancellation. Always write units explicitly: $\text{m}^3 \div \text{m}^2 = \text{m}$.
> 2. **Permeability Mix-Up:** Confusing 0.60 (accommodation) with 0.85 (machinery) costs 1 mark. Memorise the table.
> 3. **Margin Line Misplaced:** Drawing the margin line *on* the deck rather than 76 mm below loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **SOLAS Formula Version:** Some texts cite older forms. Use the **SOLAS II-1/7** modern form for KTU 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Factor of Subdivision Direction:** $f = L_p / L_f$, **not** $L_f / L_p$. Reversing this inverts the compliance criterion — examiners flag this immediately.
> 6. **Not Commenting:** Numerical problems always require a closing **comment** ("compliant / non-compliant / design implication"). Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Weighted Permeability:** Forgetting to weight by volume, not just average $\mu$ values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Subdivision of Ship's Hull**

- **Hull subdivision** = division by watertight bulkheads to **contain flooding** and ensure **damage stability**.
- **Governing regulations:** SOLAS Chapter II-1, IACS Unified Requirements, classification society rules.
- **Three geometric quantities:** Floodable length $L_f$, Permissible length $L_p$, Factor of subdivision $f = L_p / L_f$.
- **Master formula:** $L_f = (A \cdot H) \div (a_m \cdot \mu)$ — must be on your mental cheat sheet.
- **Margin line:** 76 mm below the uppermost continuous deck at the side.
- **Compliance criterion:** $f \geq 1$ at every station; equivalently $R \geq R_{req}$ for passenger ships.
- **Permeability (μ):** 0.60 accommodation, 0.85 machinery, 0.70 general cargo, 0.95 empty holds, 0/1 ballast.
- **Required subdivision index (SOLAS):** $R_{req} = 1 - 5000 / (L_s + 2.5 N + 15250)$.
- **Compartment standards:** 1C (small), 2C (medium), 3C (large passenger) — progression with ship size.
- **Historical driver:** Titanic (1912) — catalysed international subdivision rules.
- **Damage cases** must be checked: 1, 2, or 3 adjacent compartments flooded simultaneously.
- **Flooded volume:** $V_{flood} = \mu \cdot V_{gross}$ — never write $V_{flood} = V_{gross}$ unless $\mu = 1$.
- **Weighted permeability** for mixed zones: $\mu_{avg} = \sum (\mu_j V_j) / \sum V_j$.
- **Always end** a numerical answer with a compliance **comment** — examiners reward it.
- **Final waterline after damage** must NOT submerge the margin line — this is the golden rule.
- **Subdivision load line** = deepest service draft to which subdivision rules apply; affects freeboard computation.
- **Machinery space protection:** SOLAS requires the after-peak bulkhead to be at a specific distance from the stern; the engine room must be bounded by watertight bulkheads fore and aft.
