- **Collision bulkhead** — fitted at a specified distance from the forward perpendicular per SOLAS Reg. II-1/11.
- **Double-hull tankers** (MARPOL) — an extreme application of subdivision philosophy, reducing oil outflow in grounding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Superstructure and Deckhouses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Superstructure and Deckhouses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **superstructure** in marine vessel design is defined as a decked structure located above the **freeboard deck**, extending from one side of the ship to the other (athwartships), i.e., from shell to shell. According to the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966/1988)**, a superstructure is a "decked structure on the freeboard deck, extending from side to side of the ship, with proper side openings, and is treated as an extension of the hull for buoyancy and reserve buoyancy calculations."

A **deckhouse** is a decked structure that does **not** extend from side to side of the ship. It is set inboard from the ship's sides and may be located on the freeboard deck, on a superstructure deck, or on any other deck. It typically houses the **bridge, accommodation spaces, machinery control rooms**, and other operational compartments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The key distinction between a *superstructure* and a *deckhouse* is the **lateral extent**:
> - **Superstructure** → Shell-to-shell (full breadth) → Contributes to **effective freeboard** and reserve buoyancy.
> - **Deckhouse** → Set inboard from shell → Generally does **not** contribute to freeboard but may still affect stability and structural design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **multi-storey building constructed on top of a ship's hull**:

- The **hull** is the ground floor (think of a long, sealed box that provides flotation).
- The **superstructure** is like a **full-width extension** sitting on top of the main deck — imagine adding an entire extra room that stretches wall-to-wall across the house. Because it is fully enclosed and extends across the full beam, it acts as a "lid" and contributes to keeping the ship watertight and buoyant if waves wash over the deck.
- The **deckhouse** is like a **smaller room placed in the middle of the roof** — it does not touch the outer walls. It's used for the captain's bridge, the crew's living quarters, the galley, etc. While it doesn't add buoyancy, it provides essential operational and living space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Metric Reminder:** A superstructure must extend at least **96% of the breadth** (per ICLL) to be considered as such, and its length must be at least a certain percentage of the ship's length to qualify for **"effective length"** calculations that reduce required freeboard.

## 1.3 Classification of Superstructures (As Per ICLL)

| Type | Description | Effective Length Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Poop deck** | Raised deck at the **stern** (aft end) | Used for forecastle/poop calculations |
| **Forecastle** | Raised deck at the **bow** (forward end) | Located forward of amidships |
| **Bridge** | Superstructure located **amidships** containing the navigating bridge | Full superstructure |
| **Full superstructure** | Extends from poop to forecastle region without break | Maximum freeboard reduction |
| **Raised quarter deck** | Stepped-up deck forward of the poop | Limited effective length |
| **Longitudinal superstructure** | Discontinuous with breaks but elongated | Partial effective length |

> [!NOTE]
> A superstructure can be positioned anywhere along the ship's length, but its **effective length (E)** for freeboard reduction depends on its location and the unbroken nature of the superstructure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy and Purpose

The placement, type, and size of superstructures and deckhouses are governed by **multiple competing engineering drivers**:

1. **Operational Requirements** – Visibility from the bridge, accessibility of cargo gear, separation of accommodation from machinery.
2. **Safety and Survival** – Reserve buoyancy, damage stability, fire safety zones, escape routes.
3. **Structural Integrity** – Loads from wind, seas, vibration, and ship motion must be transmitted safely to the main hull.
4. **Stability** – High and heavy superstructures raise the **center of gravity (KG)**, reducing **GM** and potentially making the ship "soft."
5. **Hydrodynamics** – Forward-facing superstructures (e.g., forecastle) improve seakeeping by reducing green-water shipping on deck.
6. **Rules and Regulations** – SOLAS, ICLL, MARPOL, and Classification Society rules (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, BV, IRS) dictate minimum requirements.

## 2.2 Structural Arrangement of Superstructure

A typical superstructure consists of:

- **Superstructure deck plating** (top plate, minimum thickness per CSR-B)
- **Side shell plating** (acts as continuous sheer strake when full-width)
- **End bulkheads** (collision bulkheads if forward, weathertight bulkheads elsewhere)
- **Supporting pillars / stanchions** transmitting deck loads to the main hull
- **Cross-section / T-section** stiffeners (longitudinals and transverses)
- **Web frames / transverses** for global strength

The superstructure is structurally integrated with the **tween deck** below it through a **connection bracket** or **continuous sheer strake** at the sheer line. This joint is one of the most highly stressed regions due to **shear lag** and **relative motion** between the main hull and the superstructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Key Concept — Shear Lag Effect:**
> The normal stress in the superstructure deck is **not uniformly distributed** across the ship's breadth. The side plating (close to the hull's sheer strake) carries more load than the central plating. This non-uniformity is known as **shear lag**. For long superstructures, the effective breadth is reduced by a factor η, often taken as:
> $$\eta = \frac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \cdot \ell_{eff} - b}$$
> where $\ell_{eff}$ is the effective length of the superstructure and $b$ is the breadth.

## 2.3 Deckhouse Design Considerations

Deckhouses typically house:

- **Navigation bridge** – Forward, with maximum 360° visibility.
- **Accommodation** – Crew cabins, mess rooms, recreation.
- **Service spaces** – Galley, pantries, laundry, hospital.
- **Control rooms** – Engine control room (ECR), cargo control room.
- **Funnel base** – Uptake space, exhaust trunks.

Design drivers for deckhouses:

- **Visibility** – SOLAS Chapter V requires specific **blind sectors** (typically ≤ 10° per side, max 20° total forward of the beam).
- **Insulation** – Thermal and acoustic insulation, fire-rated divisions (A-class, B-class per SOLAS).
- **Vibration control** – Floating floors, resilient mounts, especially near machinery.
- **Escape routes** – At least two independent escape paths.
- **Weathertight doors** – Limited in number and location.
- **Heeling angle** – Must remain functional under damage conditions (e.g., 20° list).

## 2.4 Loads Acting on Superstructures and Deckhouses

| Load Type | Source | Magnitude / Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (dead) load** | Self-weight of structure, outfit, fittings | Per outfit weight density (kg/m²) |
| **Live load** | Crew, stores, snow, water accumulation | **2.5 kN/m²** for accommodation decks (DNV/CSR-B) |
| **Wind load** | Lateral pressure on side structures | $p_w = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V^2 \cdot C_d$ |
| **Sea pressure** | Green water loading on forward superstructures | Hydrostatic head from boarding seas |
| **Dynamic load** | Ship motion accelerations (roll, pitch) | $a = a_0 \cdot f(\omega t)$ |
| **Thermal load** | Solar heating, hot machinery spaces | Causes expansion stresses |
| **Local loads** | Mooring equipment, lifeboat davits, cranes | Per equipment maker's spec |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The **scantlings** (dimensions of plating and stiffeners) of a superstructure are determined using the **Common Structural Rules for Bulk Carriers and Oil Tankers (CSR)** or **IACS S21** for container ships. The minimum plate thickness is governed by **corrosion addition + design stress criteria**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shear lag factor** | $\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \ell_{eff} - b}$ | — | Used in IACS S21 for long superstructures |
| 2 | **Effective length factor** | $E = \eta \cdot L_{actual}$ | m | $L_{actual}$ is actual superstructure length |
| 3 | **Effective breadth of deck plating** | $b_{eff} = \eta \cdot b$ | m | $b$ is the breadth of the deckhouse/superstructure |
| 4 | **Wind pressure (basic)** | $p_w = 0.5 \rho_a V^2$ | Pa | $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (air density) |
| 5 | **Wind force on side** | $F_w = p_w \cdot C_d \cdot A_{proj}$ | N | $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A_{proj}$ = projected area |
| 6 | **Reserve buoyancy (R)** | $R = \int_0^H A(z) dz - \nabla$ | m³ | Integrated cross-section above WL up to freeboard deck |
| 7 | **Superstructure freeboard reduction** | $f_{ss} = f_b \cdot (E/L)$ | mm | Per ICLL — depends on type & location |
| 8 | **Superstructure height (min)** | $h_{min} = $ standard height for type | m | ICLL: poop 1.8 m, bridge 1.8 m, forecastle depends on $L$ |
| 9 | **Accel. on superstructure** | $a_{ss} = a_0 \cdot (1 + z/R_{roll})$ | m/s² | $z$ = height above WL, $R_{roll}$ = roll radius |
| 10 | **Plating min thickness** | $t_{min} = c \cdot s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{allow}}}$ | mm | $c$ = stiffener coefficient, $s$ = spacing, $p$ = pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> The most frequently examined formulas in KTU exams for Module 2 are: **shear lag factor (η)**, **wind pressure (p_w)**, and **freeboard reduction (f_ss)**. Master these three.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship designers** use these principles to balance **operational visibility** (e.g., a high bridge gives long-range view) with **stability** (low KG is preferred).
- **Naval architects** calculate superstructure scantlings to ensure the structure survives **green seas** during heavy weather — a critical failure mode on container ships.
- **Maritime authorities** use ICLL definitions to compute **minimum freeboard**, ensuring adequate reserve buoyancy for the vessel.
- **Insurance surveyors** use these formulas when assessing **weather damage** to deckhouses (e.g., cruise ship deckhouse failures in heavy seas).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Shear Lag Factor (η) for Superstructure Decks

### Background Theory
When a superstructure deck plate is subjected to **longitudinal hull girder bending** (e.g., sagging or hogging), the stress distribution across the breadth is non-uniform due to **in-plane shear deformation** of the plate. The "effective breadth" concept is introduced to convert this non-uniform stress block into an equivalent uniform stress block over a reduced breadth $b_{eff} = \eta \cdot b$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation (Simplified IACS Approach)

We begin by considering the compatibility of strains between the **superstructure deck plate** and the **hull's main deck plating** at the sheer line. The superstructure plate has shear flexibility, so the strain $\varepsilon(y)$ varies parabolically across the breadth $b$ (measured from the centerline).

Let us define a coordinate $y$ measured from the **centerline of the ship**, with the superstructure extending from $y = -b/2$ to $y = +b/2$. The longitudinal strain in the superstructure plate is assumed to vary as:

$$
\varepsilon(y) = \varepsilon_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{2y}{b}\right)^2\right]
$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the strain at the **sheer line** (i.e., the connection between superstructure and hull). This parabolic distribution is a well-established result from **Timoshenko's plate shear theory**.

> The peak strain occurs at the edges ($y = \pm b/2$) and the minimum at the centerline ($y = 0$).

**Step 1:** Define the **average strain** over the breadth $b$.

$$
\bar{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{b} \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} \varepsilon(y) \, dy
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the parabolic strain distribution.

$$
\bar{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0}{b} \int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} \left[1 - \dfrac{4y^2}{b^2}\right] dy
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral term by term.

$$
\int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} 1 \, dy = b
$$

$$
\int_{-b/2}^{+b/2} \dfrac{4y^2}{b^2} \, dy = \dfrac{4}{b^2} \cdot \dfrac{y^3}{3} \bigg|_{-b/2}^{+b/2} = \dfrac{4}{3b^2} \cdot 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)^3 = \dfrac{4}{3b^2} \cdot \dfrac{b^3}{4} = \dfrac{b}{3}
$$

**Step 4:** Combine the two integrals.

$$
\bar{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0}{b} \left[b - \dfrac{b}{3}\right] = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0}{b} \cdot \dfrac{2b}{3} = \dfrac{2\varepsilon_0}{3}
$$

**Step 5:** The **shear lag factor η** is defined as the ratio of the **average strain to peak strain**:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\bar{\varepsilon}}{\varepsilon_0} = \dfrac{2}{3} \approx 0.667
$$

This is the **theoretical lower bound** for a very long superstructure (where $\ell_{eff} \to \infty$). In practice, for finite-length superstructures, the IACS formula is used:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b} \quad \text{(for } \ell_{eff} > b\text{)}
$$

**Step 6:** Verify the limit. As $\ell_{eff} \to \infty$:

$$
\eta_{\infty} = \lim_{\ell_{eff} \to \infty} \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b} = \dfrac{1}{2.6} \approx 0.385
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the discrepancy between the simplified parabolic solution (η ≈ 0.667) and the IACS empirical formula (η ≈ 0.385). The IACS value accounts for **deck plate flexibility in both longitudinal and transverse directions**, while the simple parabolic solution considers only shear deformation in the transverse direction. KTU board exams often test this distinction.

### Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A superstructure on a container vessel has a length $\ell_{ss} = 40$ m, the ship's breadth $B = 32$ m, and the superstructure breadth $b = 24$ m. Determine the **shear lag factor η** and the **effective breadth** of the superstructure deck.

> **Solution:**

**Step 1:** For a long superstructure, the effective length is taken as the actual length:

$$
\ell_{eff} = \ell_{ss} = 40 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the IACS shear lag formula:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b} = \dfrac{40}{2.6 \times 40 - 24} = \dfrac{40}{104 - 24} = \dfrac{40}{80} = 0.50
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the effective breadth:

$$
b_{eff} = \eta \cdot b = 0.50 \times 24 = 12 \text{ m}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only **12 m** of the **24 m** deck plating effectively contributes to the hull girder section modulus. The remaining 12 m is "ineffective" due to shear lag.

**Step 4:** Now consider the same case but for a shorter superstructure with $\ell_{ss} = 15$ m. Recompute:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{15}{2.6 \times 15 - 24} = \dfrac{15}{39 - 24} = \dfrac{15}{15} = 1.00
$$

> When $\eta = 1$, there is **no shear lag reduction**, and the full breadth is effective. This occurs for short superstructures where the stress can be transferred more uniformly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU Students:** Short superstructures (like a small bridge) contribute **fully** to hull girder strength, while long ones (like a continuous deckhouse on a cruise ship) suffer significant shear lag. This is why cruise ships have **transverse bulkheads** at regular intervals — to break the superstructure into shorter "effective" segments.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Effective Superstructure Length for Freeboard Reduction (ICLL Method)

### Step-by-Step Logic

According to the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966)**, the **effective length (E)** of a superstructure depends on its **type, location, and continuity**.

**Step 1:** Identify the superstructure type and its standard percentage:

- **Full superstructure** (one continuous structure): 100% of its length
- **Poop or forecastle only**: 100% of their length
- **Bridge superstructure** alone: 100% of its length
- **Raised quarterdeck**: 50% of its length
- **Discontinuous superstructure**: Sum of effective lengths of each segment

**Step 2:** Apply the **position factor** based on location along the ship:

> [!NOTE]
> The ICLL Table 4 (or Annex) provides position factors. A superstructure located **forward of amidships** has its effective length multiplied by a factor less than 1.0, while a superstructure extending from the poop region gets a factor based on its actual position.

**Step 3:** Compute the **percentage of length (E/L)**:

$$
\%E = \dfrac{E}{L} \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Use the ICLL Table 6 to find the **percentage of freeboard reduction (F)** for the given type and %E.

**Step 5:** Apply the reduction:

$$
f_{reduction} = f_b \times F / 100
$$

where $f_b$ is the **tabular freeboard** (from ICLL Table 2.2 for the ship's length).

### Numerical Example

**Given:** A bulk carrier of length $L = 150$ m has a **full superstructure** extending from $x = 0.25L$ to $x = 0.85L$ (i.e., 60% of L). Tabular freeboard $f_b = 1900$ mm.

**Step 1:** The actual length of the superstructure is $0.60 \times 150 = 90$ m.

**Step 2:** Position factor (assume centerline) ≈ 1.0. So $E = 90$ m.

**Step 3:** $\%E = (90/150) \times 100 = 60\%$.

**Step 4:** From ICLL Table 6 (Type "B" ship, full superstructure, 60% E): Freeboard reduction $F = 28\%$.

**Step 5:** Actual reduction:

$$
f_{reduction} = 1900 \times 0.28 = 532 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Reduced freeboard:

$$
f_{actual} = 1900 - 532 = 1368 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The superstructure reduces the required freeboard by **532 mm**, which allows the ship to carry more cargo (higher DWT) at the same draft.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchy and Functional Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Hull Structure] --> B[Above Main Deck]
    A --> C[Below Main Deck]
    B --> D[Superstructure]
    B --> E[Deckhouse]
    D --> D1[Poop]
    D --> D2[Forecastle]
    D --> D3[Bridge Superstructure]
    D --> D4[Raised Quarterdeck]
    E --> E1[Navigation Bridge]
    E --> E2[Accommodation Block]
    E --> E3[Engine Control Room]
    E --> E4[Funnel Uptake Space]
    D --> F[Structural Connection]
    F --> F1[Sheer Strake]
    F --> F2[End Bulkheads]
    F --> F3[Pillars and Stanchions]
    A --> G[Design Drivers]
    G --> G1[Stability - KG and GM]
    G --> G2[SOLAS Visibility]
    G --> G3[ICLL Freeboard]
    G --> G4[Classification Rules]
    G --> G5[Operability and Habitability]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Design Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StepA[Step 1 Identify Ship Type and Mission] --> StepB[Step 2 Determine Required Visibility and Bridge Position]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3 Compute Required Freeboard via ICLL Tables]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4 Decide Superstructure vs Deckhouse Configuration]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5 Compute Shear Lag Factor]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6 Calculate Loads - Wind, Sea, Live, Dead]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7 Determine Scantlings per CSR or IACS Rules]
    StepG --> StepH[Step 8 Verify Stability - GM and KG]
    StepH --> StepI[Step 9 Check SOLAS Compliance - Visibility, Escape, Fire]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 10 Final Approval by Classification Society]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison of Superstructure vs Deckhouse

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Comp[Compare Superstructure vs Deckhouse] --> Q1{Extends from Shell to Shell?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| SS[Superstructure]
    Q1 -->|No| DH[Deckhouse]
    SS --> SS1[Contributes to Reserve Buoyancy]
    SS --> SS2[Reduces Required Freeboard]
    SS --> SS3[Forms Weathertight Envelope]
    SS --> SS4[Example: Bridge Block on Tanker]
    DH --> DH1[No Contribution to Reserve Buoyancy]
    DH --> DH2[No Freeboard Reduction]
    DH --> DH3[Provides Operational and Living Space]
    DH --> DH4[Example: Accommodation Block on Bulk Carrier]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Students:** When asked to sketch in exams, you can use these block diagrams as a base, and add technical labels (dimensions, scantlings, load directions) to earn full marks. A diagram with clear **arrows and annotations** is worth at least 2-3 marks on its own.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Superstructure" as per the International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL). How is it different from a "deckhouse"?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Superstructure (ICLL Definition):** A superstructure is a **decked structure on the freeboard deck** that extends from **side to side of the ship** (i.e., from shell to shell), with proper side openings and end bulkheads, contributing to the vessel's reserve buoyancy and qualifying for freeboard reduction.
>
> **Deckhouse:** A decked structure that does **not** extend from side to side; it is set inboard from the shell plating and is primarily used for accommodation, navigation, or operational spaces. It does **not** contribute to freeboard reduction.
>
> **Key Difference:** The lateral extent — a superstructure is **shell-to-shell**, while a deckhouse is set **inboard**.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is the "shear lag effect" in superstructure design, and how is it accounted for in IACS rules?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **shear lag effect** is the non-uniform distribution of longitudinal stress in a wide superstructure deck plate due to the in-plane shear flexibility of the plating. Stress is highest at the sheer line (junction with the hull) and lowest at the centerline.
>
> In IACS S21 and CSR rules, this is accounted for by introducing a **shear lag factor (η)** and an **effective breadth (b_eff = η·b)** that is used in section modulus calculations.
>
> $$\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b}$$
>
> The effective breadth replaces the full breadth when calculating the contribution of the superstructure deck to the hull girder moment of inertia.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks

**Q: A bulk carrier of length L = 200 m, breadth B = 32 m, has a continuous bridge superstructure of length ℓ_ss = 50 m and breadth b = 24 m. The tabular freeboard is f_b = 2200 mm.**

**(a) Compute the shear lag factor (η) for the superstructure deck and hence find the effective breadth.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) If the wind velocity is 30 m/s and the projected side area of the superstructure above the main deck is 1200 m² with a drag coefficient C_d = 1.2, compute the total wind force on the superstructure.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Given Data:**
- Superstructure length, $\ell_{ss} = 50$ m
- Superstructure breadth, $b = 24$ m
- $\ell_{eff} = \ell_{ss} = 50$ m (assuming superstructure is long)

**Step 1:** Apply the IACS shear lag formula:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b} = \dfrac{50}{2.6 \times 50 - 24} = \dfrac{50}{130 - 24} = \dfrac{50}{106} \approx 0.4717
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the effective breadth:

$$
b_{eff} = \eta \cdot b = 0.4717 \times 24 \approx 11.32 \text{ m}
$$

> **Result:** $\eta \approx 0.47$ and $b_{eff} \approx 11.32$ m. The effective breadth is less than half the actual breadth, indicating significant shear lag in this relatively long superstructure.

**Step 3:** Discuss the implication:
- The full breadth (24 m) cannot be used in hull girder strength calculations.
- The superstructure contributes only $\eta \cdot A_{plate}$ to the section modulus.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given Data:**
- Wind velocity, $V = 30$ m/s
- Projected area, $A_{proj} = 1200$ m²
- Drag coefficient, $C_d = 1.2$
- Air density, $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (ISA standard)

**Step 1:** Calculate the **dynamic wind pressure**:

$$
p_w = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 30^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 900 = 551.25 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **total wind force**:

$$
F_w = p_w \cdot C_d \cdot A_{proj} = 551.25 \times 1.2 \times 1200
$$

$$
F_w = 551.25 \times 1440 = 793{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 793.8 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Result:** The total wind force on the superstructure is approximately **793.8 kN** (or about **81 tonnes-force**).

**Step 3:** Discuss the implication:
- This force acts laterally at the centroid of the projected area.
- It contributes to the **overturning moment** and must be considered in **stability analysis** and **mooring design**.
- Designers typically combine wind force with **heeling moments** from cargo, turns, and beam winds to find the worst-case heel angle.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q: A container vessel of length L = 175 m, breadth B = 28 m has a forward forecastle of length 22 m and a poop of length 15 m. The tabular freeboard is 2100 mm. The superstructure is "Type B" with a single tier height of 1.8 m.**

**(a) Determine the effective length (E) of the superstructures and the percentage of length (%E). Find the freeboard reduction according to ICLL Table 6 for a Type B ship.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) If the worst-case roll acceleration at the center of the superstructure is 0.25g, and the superstructure mass is 800 tonnes with KG = 14.5 m above the keel, determine the dynamic load factor (DLF) and the additional vertical load on the supporting structure due to roll acceleration.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Step 1:** Identify the superstructure segments.
- Forecastle: $\ell_F = 22$ m (located forward, position factor ≈ 0.7 for forecastle alone)
- Poop: $\ell_P = 15$ m (located aft, position factor ≈ 0.7 for poop alone)

**Step 2:** Compute the effective length E using position factors from ICLL Annex (Reg. 35):

For this problem, assume **standard position factors**:
- Forecastle: 0.7 → $E_F = 0.7 \times 22 = 15.4$ m
- Poop: 0.7 → $E_P = 0.7 \times 15 = 10.5$ m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a Type B ship, the position factors for forecastle and poop are **0.7** each (per ICLL), but the bridge (if present) is **1.0**. The exact factor depends on whether the structure is continuous or broken.

**Step 3:** Total effective length:

$$
E = E_F + E_P = 15.4 + 10.5 = 25.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the percentage of length:

$$
\%E = \dfrac{E}{L} \times 100 = \dfrac{25.9}{175} \times 100 \approx 14.8\%
$$

**Step 5:** From **ICLL Table 6** (Type B ship, with forecastle + poop, no bridge):

> For %E = 14.8%, freeboard reduction F ≈ **7%** (interpolated from the table).

**Step 6:** Compute the actual reduction:

$$
f_{reduction} = f_b \times F/100 = 2100 \times 0.07 = 147 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7:** Reduced freeboard:

$$
f_{actual} = 2100 - 147 = 1953 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Result:** The superstructures reduce the required freeboard by **147 mm**, giving a final freeboard of **1953 mm**.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Roll acceleration, $a = 0.25g = 0.25 \times 9.81 = 2.4525$ m/s²
- Superstructure mass, $m = 800$ tonnes = 800,000 kg
- KG (above keel) = 14.5 m

**Step 1:** Compute the **vertical dynamic load** due to roll acceleration:

The dynamic force component acting vertically is:

$$
F_{vert} = m \cdot a = 800{,}000 \times 2.4525 = 1{,}962{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 1.962 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **static weight**:

$$
W = m \cdot g = 800{,}000 \times 9.81 = 7{,}848{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 7.848 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)**:

$$
DLF = 1 + \dfrac{a}{g} = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important Note:** The DLF is **not** simply $1 + a/g$ in all rules. For sinusoidal excitation, the peak value may use $DLF = 1 + \sqrt{2} \cdot (a/g)$ or other formulations. For this exam-style problem, we use the simple form.

**Step 4:** Compute the **total vertical load on supporting structure**:

$$
F_{total} = W + F_{vert} = 7.848 + 1.962 = 9.81 \text{ MN} = m \cdot g \cdot DLF
$$

Alternatively:

$$
F_{total} = W \times DLF = 7.848 \times 1.25 = 9.81 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 5:** Discuss the implication:
- The pillars/stanchions supporting the superstructure must be designed for this **amplified load**.
- The transverse distribution depends on the **roll radius** ($R_{roll}$), roll period ($T_{roll}$), and the **lever arm** of the superstructure.
- Designers must also consider the **eccentricity** of the superstructure's center of gravity from the centerline (transverse moment arm).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing superstructure and deckhouse:** Marks lost if a student calls a deckhouse a superstructure (or vice versa) when quoting ICLL rules.
> 2. **Wrong units in wind force:** Many students forget to convert $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ correctly or mix up **Pa** with **kPa**. Always state the final answer in **kN** or **N**.
> 3. **Forgetting to interpolate:** ICLL Table 6 is given in **discrete %E values**. Students often pick the closest value without **interpolating**, losing a mark.
> 4. **Sign errors in DLF:** The dynamic load factor is always **> 1.0** for vertical accelerations **upward**, but the support structure must be checked for the **worst-case direction** (could be downward if the superstructure decelerates).
> 5. **Not stating the formula used:** Always write the **formula**, substitute values, and **box the final answer** with units. KTU examiners follow a strict marking scheme — show working for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Superstructure and Deckhouses (Module 2)**

- [x] **Superstructure** = shell-to-shell; **Deckhouse** = inboard only.
- [x] A superstructure **contributes to reserve buoyancy** and **reduces required freeboard** (per ICLL).
- [x] A deckhouse does **NOT** contribute to freeboard reduction but is critical for **habitability and operation**.
- [x] **ICLL Types of Superstructure**: Poop, Forecastle, Bridge, Full, Raised Quarterdeck.
- [x] **Standard superstructure height** (per ICLL): typically **1.8 m to 2.0 m**.
- [x] **Shear Lag Factor (η)**: $\eta = \dfrac{\ell_{eff}}{2.6 \, \ell_{eff} - b}$; effective breadth $b_{eff} = \eta \cdot b$.
- [x] **Wind pressure** on superstructure: $p_w = 0.5 \cdot \rho_a \cdot V^2$; total force $F_w = p_w \cdot C_d \cdot A_{proj}$.
- [x] **Air density** at sea level: $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Freeboard reduction** per ICLL Table 6: depends on **%E** and **ship type (A or B)**.
- [x] **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)** for superstructure: $DLF = 1 + a/g$ (basic form).
- [x] **SOLAS visibility**: max blind sector forward of beam is **10° per side, 20° total** (some rules allow up to 30° in specific cases).
- [x] **End bulkheads of superstructures** must be **weathertight**; if forward of 0.15L from FP, must be **collision bulkhead** grade.
- [x] **Sheer strake** is the structural connection between hull and superstructure — a **high-stress region**.
- [x] **Rules to remember**: SOLAS (safety), ICLL (load lines), MARPOL (pollution), CSR-B / IACS S21 (scantlings).
- [x] **Cruise ship deckhouse failures** are a famous real-world case — large unbroken superstructures + heavy seas = structural collapse (e.g., *Explorer* class issues).
- [x] **Common exam keywords**: "Shell-to-shell", "Effective length", "Shear lag", "Reserve buoyancy", "Freeboard reduction", "Weathertight integrity".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip (2024 Scheme):** When answering Module 2 questions on superstructures, always start with the **ICLL definition**, then relate it to the **ship type** (A or B), and finally compute the required parameter. This structured approach earns you full marks on definition, explanation, and calculation parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of Cargo Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arrangement of Cargo Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arrangement of Cargo Spaces** refers to the systematic and engineered configuration of internal volume within a ship's hull dedicated to the carriage, stowage, and preservation of cargo. It is the discipline within naval architecture that defines the **number, size, shape, vertical stacking, and longitudinal division** of holds, tanks, and associated auxiliary spaces, while satisfying statutory, structural, operational, and commercial requirements.

The cargo space arrangement is a direct output of the **ship design spiral**, where the designer's selection of ship type (general cargo, bulk carrier, tanker, container vessel, reefer, ro-ro, etc.) dictates the geometry of the cargo block. The arrangement must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Statutory constraints** — SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), MARPOL, ICLL (International Convention on Load Lines), and Classification Society Rules (DNV, LR, ABS, BV).
2. **Structural integrity** — Adequate double bottom depth, web frame spacing, and bulkhead placement.
3. **Operational efficiency** — Rapid loading/discharge, hatch cover operability, crane outreach.
4. **Cargo integrity** — Protection from sweat, contamination, crushing, and shifting.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the ship's cargo space as a multi-storey warehouse built inside a steel tube that floats.**

Imagine you have a long, narrow, floating warehouse. You must decide:
- How many floors (decks) does the warehouse have?
- Where are the pillars (bulkheads) that divide it into rooms (holds)?
- Where is the elevator shaft (hatchway) that drops cargo from the roof (main deck) to the basement (tank top)?
- Is there a basement (double bottom) for pipes, fuel, or to absorb a grounding impact?
- How are the rooms shaped — square for pallets, long and thin for pipes, or cylindrical for liquids?

Just as a land-based warehouse must be tailored to the goods it stores (a grain silo looks nothing like a car showroom), a ship's cargo arrangement is **tailored to the cargo profile**. A bulk carrier resembles a series of giant open-topped bath tubs. A tanker is a network of interconnecting cylindrical pipes. A container ship looks like a multi-storey parking garage with no ramps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric Box:**
> - **Stowage Factor (SF):** Cargo volume per unit mass, typically **m³/tonne** (range 0.6 for steel to 4.0+ for light goods).
> - **Deadweight Cargo (DWC):** Total cargo mass carried, expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **Metric Tonnes (MT)**.
> - **Cargo Hold Volume:** Measured in **m³**, must exceed **DWC × SF**.
> - **Block Coefficient (Cₐ):** Typically **0.55 – 0.85** for cargo ships.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Cargo Space Logic

The arrangement of cargo spaces is governed by a top-down decision hierarchy. Every decision cascades into the next.

**Step 1 — Cargo Profile Definition**
- What commodity? (dry bulk, liquid, containerized, break-bulk, refrigerated, vehicles)
- What is the **stowage factor (SF)**?
- What is the **cargo density (ρc)** = **1 / SF** (t/m³)?
- What is the required **deadweight cargo (DWC)** in tonnes?

**Step 2 — Selection of Ship Type & Hull Form**
- Hull form coefficient (Cₐ) is set by speed vs. capacity trade-off.
- Higher Cₐ → fatter hull → more volume per tonne → better for low SF cargo.
- Lower Cₐ → finer hull → faster → better for high-value, time-sensitive cargo.

**Step 3 — Number and Length of Holds**
- Determined by the **rule of longitudinal subdivision** (e.g., SOLAS Chapter II-1 for passenger ships, and damage stability for cargo).
- For bulk carriers, the **IACS Bulk Carrier Common Structural Rules (CSR BC)** mandate hold number based on L (length) and D (moulded depth).
- Typical: **5 to 9 holds** for Panamax / Capesize bulkers.

**Step 4 — Vertical Subdivision — Decks and Double Bottoms**
- **Single deck** — most common for bulk carriers and tankers.
- **Tween decks (intermediate decks)** — used in general cargo and multi-purpose vessels.
- **Double bottom height (h_DB)** — typically **1.0 m to 2.5 m** in cargo region, providing:
  - Ballast capacity
  - Protection against bottom damage (SOLAS Reg. II-1)
  - Structural stiffness

**Step 5 — Transverse and Longitudinal Bulkheads**
- **Transverse bulkheads** divide the ship into holds and provide transverse strength.
- **Longitudinal bulkheads** (in tankers and some bulkers) prevent free-surface effect.
- A **collision bulkhead** is mandatory forward (typically at **0.05 L** to **0.08 L** from FP).
- An **aftpeak bulkhead** separates machinery/cargo.

**Step 6 — Hatchways and Access**
- **Hatch opening** must be sized for the largest single unit of cargo (or container).
- For cellular container ships, hatch corners must align with cell guides.
- **Hatch coamings** must be at least **600 mm** above the freeboard deck (Load Lines Convention).

**Step 7 — Auxiliary Spaces and Cofferdams**
- **Cofferdams** (typically **760 mm** minimum width) isolate cargo from oil-fuel tanks, boilers, or heated spaces.
- **Pump rooms, engine room, accommodation** must be isolated from cargo to prevent fire, vapour ingress, and contamination.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Arrangement Matters |
|-------------|------------------------|
| **Port turnaround** | Larger, fewer hatches = faster discharge, lower demurrage |
| **Cargo damage claims** | Proper ventilation, lashing, segregation reduces claims |
| **Fuel efficiency** | Optimal block coefficient minimises propulsion power |
| **Safety** | Damage stability, fire safety, crew egress all depend on bulkhead layout |
| **Regulatory compliance** | Class approval hinges on correct subdivision |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula required for KTU ESE numerical problems on cargo space arrangement. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` to avoid breaking table syntax.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Required Cargo Volume** | $V_{cargo} = DWC \times SF$ | m³ | Holds must satisfy $V_{hold} \geq V_{cargo}$ |
| 2 | **Cargo Density** | $\rho_c = \dfrac{1}{SF} = \dfrac{m_{cargo}}{V_{cargo}}$ | t/m³ | Inverse of stowage factor |
| 3 | **Cargo Block Volume (in hull)** | $V_{block} = L_{bp} \times B \times D \times C_{block}$ | m³ | Geometric hull volume (cargo block only) |
| 4 | **Block Coefficient (cargo block)** | $C_{block} = \dfrac{V_{block}}{L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D}$ | dimensionless | Typical 0.55 – 0.85 |
| 5 | **Deadweight Utilization** | $DWC = \Delta_{loaded} - \Delta_{lightship}$ | t | Mass balance identity |
| 6 | **Hold Length (for n equal holds)** | $L_{hold} = \dfrac{L_{bp} - L_{eng} - L_{fwd} - L_{aft}}{n}$ | m | Subtracts fore/aft extensions |
| 7 | **Prismatic Coefficient** | $C_p = \dfrac{C_{block}}{C_m}$ | dimensionless | $C_m$ = midship section coefficient |
| 8 | **Number of Containers (TEU)** | $N_{TEU} = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{TEU_{volume}}$ (for cellular) | TEU | $TEU_{volume} \approx 33.2$ m³ standard |
| 9 | **Double Bottom Volume** | $V_{DB} = L_{DB} \times B \times h_{DB}$ | m³ | Used for ballast or fuel oil |
| 10 | **Free Surface Effect** | $\Delta GGM = \dfrac{\rho \cdot i}{m_{ship}}$ | m | $i$ = transverse moment of inertia of free surface |
| 11 | **Stability Criterion (Intact)** | $GM \geq 0.15$ m (passenger / large cargo) | m | Damage stability more complex |
| 12 | **Hatch Coaming Height (min)** | $h_{coaming} \geq 600$ mm (load line reg.) | mm | Above freeboard deck |
| 13 | **Minimum Double Bottom Height** | $h_{DB} \geq \dfrac{B}{20}$ or as per Class | m | Typical range 1.0 – 2.5 m |
| 14 | **Collision Bulkhead Position** | $x_{coll} \geq 0.05 L_{bp}$ from FP | m | SOLAS Reg. II-1/11 |
| 15 | **Lightweight Reduction** | $LWT = \Delta_{loaded} - DWT$ | t | Lightweight = hull + machinery + outfit |

### 2.4 Engineering Insight: The "Design Spiral" Cascade

A change in **stowage factor** (e.g., switching from iron ore, SF ≈ 0.4, to bagged rice, SF ≈ 1.8) cascades through the design:

$$
SF \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{cargo} \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{block} \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D \uparrow \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{block} \uparrow
$$

The naval architect must therefore select the **principal particulars** ($L_{bp}$, $B$, $D$, $T$, $C_b$) such that the cargo block volume equals or exceeds the required cargo volume, with a margin of **15 – 20%** for "broken stowage" (volume wasted by non-perfect packing).

$$
V_{block} \geq 1.15 \times DWC \times SF
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Hull Volume from a Given Deadweight

**Given:**
- Deadweight Cargo, $DWC = 50{,}000$ tonnes
- Stowage Factor, $SF = 1.2$ m³/t (typical for bagged sugar)
- Stowage efficiency factor (broken stowage allowance), $\eta = 0.85$ (i.e., 15% wasted)
- Block coefficient, $C_b = 0.72$

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical cargo volume**

$$
V_{cargo} = DWC \times SF
$$

$$
V_{cargo} = 50{,}000 \times 1.2 = 60{,}000 \; \text{m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** Each tonne of cargo occupies 1.2 m³; therefore 50,000 t occupies 60,000 m³. This is the *minimum* geometric space required if we could pack the cargo with zero waste.

**Step 2 — Apply broken stowage correction**

In reality, the cargo cannot fill 100% of the geometric hold volume because of:
- Frame recess (gutter strakes) reducing effective width
- Inner bottom crown (tank top camber) reducing effective height
- Hopper tanks and bilges consuming volume
- Non-uniform shapes of cargo items

$$
V_{hold, required} = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{\eta}
$$

$$
V_{hold, required} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{0.85} = 70{,}588.2 \; \text{m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** Because only 85% of the hold's geometric volume is packable, we must provide a geometric hold volume of 70,588 m³ to actually carry 60,000 m³ of cargo.

**Step 3 — Express the cargo block in terms of L, B, D**

$$
V_{block} = L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_b
$$

Setting $V_{block} = V_{hold, required}$:

$$
L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D = \dfrac{V_{hold, required}}{C_b}
$$

$$
L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D = \dfrac{70{,}588.2}{0.72} = 98{,}039 \; \text{m}^3
$$

> **Logic:** The product $L \times B \times D$ is the volume of the rectangular block that circumscribes the ship. Because the ship's hull is "fatness ratio" $C_b = 0.72$ of this box, the box itself must be 1 / 0.72 = 1.389 times the required cargo volume.

**Step 4 — Choose principal particulars**

If we choose a Panamax constraint $B = 32.2$ m and design $D = 18$ m:

$$
L_{bp} = \dfrac{98{,}039}{32.2 \times 18} = \dfrac{98{,}039}{579.6} = 169.2 \; \text{m}
$$

> **Logic:** Given beam and depth, the length is forced to be 169.2 m. This satisfies the volumetric requirement exactly. In real design, this would be iterated with stability, resistance, and structural criteria.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hold Subdivision of a Bulk Carrier

**Given:**
- $L_{bp} = 280$ m (Capesize bulk carrier)
- $B = 45$ m, $D = 25$ m
- Engine room length, $L_{eng} = 28$ m (aft)
- Forward collision bulkhead at $0.05 L_{bp} = 14$ m from FP
- Aft peak bulkhead at $0.04 L_{bp} = 11.2$ m from AP
- Required number of holds, $n = 9$ (per IACS CSR BC for L > 250 m)

**Step 1 — Compute effective cargo length**

$$
L_{cargo} = L_{bp} - L_{fwd} - L_{aft} - L_{eng}
$$

$$
L_{cargo} = 280 - 14 - 11.2 - 28 = 226.8 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Distribute among 9 holds**

$$
L_{hold, avg} = \dfrac{L_{cargo}}{n} = \dfrac{226.8}{9} = 25.2 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply non-uniformity (hogging/sagging bending moment mitigation)**

Holds at midships are typically **shorter**, while end holds are **longer** to optimize structural weight and reduce shear forces:

| Hold No. | Length (m) | Position |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| 1 (fwd) | 28.0 | Forward |
| 2 | 26.0 | |
| 3 | 25.0 | |
| 4 | 24.0 | |
| 5 (midship) | 23.6 | Amidships |
| 6 | 24.0 | |
| 7 | 25.0 | |
| 8 | 26.0 | |
| 9 (aft) | 27.2 | Aft |
| **Total** | **228.8** | (≈ Lcargo with margin) |

> **Logic:** The midship holds are shorter because shear forces are smaller amidships but bending moments are largest; shorter holds reduce the lever arm of cargo weight, which lowers still-water bending moment.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cargo Type vs. Arrangement Strategy

| Cargo Type | Ship Type | Hold Shape | Decks | Special Features | Stowage Factor (m³/t) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------|------------------|------------------------|
| Iron Ore | Capesize Bulker | Rectangular, large | Single | Sloped hoppers, double skin | 0.35 – 0.45 |
| Coal | Panamax Bulker | Rectangular | Single | Ventilation, CO monitoring | 0.85 – 1.10 |
| Grain | General Cargo / Bulker | Box-shaped | Single + tweens | Hatch covers, trimming | 1.20 – 1.45 |
| Containers | Cellular Container | Cell-guided slots | Multi-stack | Corner castings, twist-locks | 1.40 – 1.90 |
| Liquid (Oil) | Tanker | Cylindrical tanks | Single | C.O.W., SBT, IGS | 1.10 – 1.30 |
| Vehicles | PCTC / RoRo | Open decks | Multiple liftable | Ramps, lashing decks | 1.60 – 2.50 |
| Refrigerated | Reefer | Insulated holds | Multiple | Air circulation, defrost | 1.80 – 2.30 |
| LNG | LNG Carrier | Moss / Membrane spheres | Single | Cryogenic insulation | 1.30 – 1.50 |
| Chemicals | Chemical Tanker | Stainless tanks | Single | Segregation, coating | 1.10 – 1.80 |

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Container Slot Calculation

**Given:**
- Cellular container ship with $B = 32.2$ m (Panamax)
- 6 rows across the ship
- Bay pitch (transverse) = 2.44 m
- Each TEU occupies 2.44 m length × 2.44 m width × 2.59 m height

**Step 1 — Transverse slots per bay**

$$
\text{rows} = 6 \quad \text{(e.g., 3 + 3 on each side of centerline, or 4 + 2)}
$$

**Step 2 — TEU per stack (vertical)**

$$
\text{TEU}_{stack} = \dfrac{D - h_{hatch} - h_{DB}}{\text{TEU height}}
$$

For $D = 13.5$ m, $h_{hatch} = 2.0$ m, $h_{DB} = 1.5$ m:

$$
\text{TEU}_{stack} = \dfrac{13.5 - 2.0 - 1.5}{2.59} = \dfrac{10.0}{2.59} \approx 3.86
$$

Since we can stack at most **3 TEUs** below deck (and more on deck), assume below-deck stack = 3 TEU.

**Step 3 — Total slot capacity**

$$
\text{Slots}_{below} = 6 \times N_{bay} \times 3
$$

For 30 bays:

$$
\text{Slots}_{below} = 6 \times 30 \times 3 = 540 \; \text{TEU}
$$

On deck, additional capacity depends on lashing bridge height (often 4–5 tiers, = 4–5 × 6 × 30 = 720 – 900 TEU).

> **Logic:** The vertical pitch is the binding constraint. Even with 30 bays of length, we are height-limited below deck to 3 TEU, while on deck we can stack higher because of lashing bridges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cargo Space Subsystems

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *interactions* of all subsystems that constitute a cargo space arrangement, in lieu of a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    subgraph vertSubdiv["VERTICAL SUBDIVISION"]

        TD["TWEEN DECKS - Optional"]
        TK["TANK TOP - Cargo floor"]
    end

    subgraph transSubdiv["TRANSVERSE / LONGITUDINAL"]
        CB["COLLISION BULKHEAD - 0.05 Lbp"]
        WB1["WATERTIGHT BULKHEAD 1"]
        WB2["WATERTIGHT BULKHEAD 2"]

    subgraph accessSub["ACCESS / HATCHWAY"]

    subgraph auxSub["AUXILIARY SPACES"]

    end

    H1 --- DB
    H2 --- DB

    H2 --- TK

    H3 --- TD

    H2 --- WB2
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CARGO PROFILE INPUT\n(Commodity, DWC, SF)"] --> B["SHIP TYPE SELECTION\n(Bulker, Tanker, Container)"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define "broken stowage" in the context of cargo space design. Why must it be considered when sizing cargo holds?*

**Model Answer:**
Broken stowage refers to the **fraction of the geometric cargo hold volume that cannot be physically occupied by cargo** due to:
- Frame recess / gutter strakes
- Bilge and hopper tank intrusions
- Tank top crown / camber
- Non-uniform shape of cargo items (e.g., pipes, vehicles)
- Lashing and securing equipment

It is considered because the *theoretical* volume $V_{cargo} = DWC \times SF$ is never fully usable. Designers inflate the required geometric volume by a factor of $1/η$, where $η \approx 0.85$. Failure to account for it leads to **cargo over-stowage, damage to cargo and structure, and potential instability**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the SOLAS requirement for the position of the collision bulkhead and the minimum cofferdam width between cargo tanks and machinery spaces.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Collision bulkhead:** Must be located at a distance from the forward perpendicular of **not less than 0.05 $L_{bp}$** and **not more than 0.08 $L_{bp}$** (typical). It must be watertight up to the freeboard deck.
- **Cofferdam width:** Not specifically prescribed by SOLAS in absolute terms, but **Classification Society Rules (e.g., DNV, LR)** mandate a **minimum width of 760 mm** between cargo spaces and oil-fuel tanks, machinery, or any source of heat.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **stowage factor (SF)** with **density** — they are reciprocals. Writing $SF = 1/\rho$ is a common error.
> - Forgetting the **broken stowage factor** (1.15–1.20) loses 2 marks on a 7-mark sub-part.
> - The collision bulkhead position is **from the forward perpendicular**, not from midship.
> - Double bottom height is given in **moulded depth terms**, not in absolute metric units; specify correctly.
> - Always state the **SOLAS / ICLL / Class** rule when justifying a dimension — this shows valuation awareness.

---

#### **Part B – Question A (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** *A general cargo ship is to carry 35,000 tonnes of bagged cement with a stowage factor of 1.5 m³/t. The block coefficient of the vessel is 0.70. Allowing 18% for broken stowage, determine the minimum required cargo block volume and the principal particulars (Lbp, B, D) satisfying this, given B = 30 m and D = 16 m.*

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required cargo volume (theoretical):**

$$
V_{cargo} = DWC \times SF = 35{,}000 \times 1.5 = 52{,}500 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply broken stowage correction (18% wasted, 82% usable, so $\eta = 0.82$):**

$$
V_{hold, required} = \dfrac{V_{cargo}}{\eta} = \dfrac{52{,}500}{0.82} = 64{,}024 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Required cargo block volume:**

$$
V_{block} = L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_b
$$

Setting equal to $V_{hold, required}$:

$$
L_{bp} = \dfrac{V_{hold, required}}{B \cdot D \cdot C_b} = \dfrac{64{,}024}{30 \times 16 \times 0.70}
$$

$$
L_{bp} = \dfrac{64{,}024}{336} = 190.55 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Final principal particulars:**

- $L_{bp} = 190.55$ m (≈ 191 m)
- $B = 30$ m
- $D = 16$ m
- $C_b = 0.70$

**Result:** The minimum length between perpendiculars is **190.55 m** to carry the required deadweight. **(7 Marks: Volume = 2; Broken stowage = 2; Principal particulars = 2; Unit & conclusion = 1.)**

---

**(b)** *With reference to SOLAS and Classification Society Rules, explain the principles governing the arrangement of transverse bulkheads, double bottom, and cofferdams in a general cargo ship. Sketch a labelled midship section showing these features.*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**1. Transverse Bulkheads (3 marks):**
- Divide the hull into watertight compartments.
- Must include a **collision bulkhead** (SOLAS Reg. II-1/11) at $0.05 L_{bp}$ to $0.08 L_{bp}$ from FP.
- Other transverse bulkheads separate cargo holds and prevent progressive flooding.
- Spacing affects damage stability: smaller holds = better subdivision, but more structural weight.

**2. Double Bottom (2 marks):**
- Mandatory in cargo ships (SOLAS Reg. II-1/Part B Reg. 9).
- **Minimum height** typically $B/20$ to $B/15$ (or per Class rules), e.g., 1.5 m to 2.5 m.
- Used for **ballast water**, fuel oil, fresh water.
- Provides **bottom protection** in case of grounding or contact damage.

**3. Cofferdams (2 marks):**
- **Minimum width 760 mm** (typical Class rule).
- Serve to **isolate cargo from oil-fuel tanks, boilers, or heated spaces**.
- Prevent vapour ingress, fire spread, and thermal damage to cargo.

**Labelled Midship Section (text representation):**

```
    ┌─────────────────────────┐  ← Main Deck
    │      Hatch Coaming      │
    │ ┌─────────────────────┐ │
    │ │                     │ │
    │ │   CARGO HOLD        │ │
    │ │                     │ │
    │ ├──── Hopper Tank ────┤ │  ← Sloped hopper (void)
    │ │                     │ │
    │ ├──── Double Bottom ──┤ │  ← Ballast / FO
    │ │     Tank Top        │ │
    │ └─────────────────────┘ │
    └─────────────────────────┘
              KEEL
```

**(7 Marks: Bulkheads = 3; Double bottom = 2; Cofferdams = 2; Total = 7.)**

---

#### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Compare the cargo space arrangement of a Capesize bulk carrier and a Panamax cellular container ship under the following heads: hold geometry, hatchway size, double bottom, subdivision, and stowage factor range.*

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Feature | Capesize Bulk Carrier | Panamax Container Ship |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Hold geometry** | 5–9 large rectangular holds, open-top, no tween decks | Cell-guided slots, vertical stacking, no internal bulkheads between bays |
| **Hatchway size** | Large hatches (>0.6×B) for grab/loader access | Hatch width < internal cell span; container corner castings line up with cells |
| **Double bottom** | ~2.0–2.5 m, used for ballast + FO | ~1.0–1.5 m, used for ballast only |
| **Subdivision** | Transverse bulkheads between holds; IACS CSR BC mandate | Transverse wash bulkheads every ~2–3 bays for strength |
| **Stowage factor (typical)** | 0.35–1.10 m³/t (ore, coal, grain) | 1.40–1.90 m³/t (containers, light) |
| **Cargo density** | High (ore ≈ 3 t/m³) | Low–medium (boxes ≈ 0.5–0.7 t/m³) |
| **Volume utilization** | η ≈ 0.85–0.90 (ore) | η ≈ 0.95 (cells are perfect fit) |

**Marks distribution (7):** Hold geometry = 1.5; Hatchway = 1.5; Double bottom = 1.5; Subdivision = 1.5; Stowage factor = 1.0.

---

**(b)** *A bulk carrier has $L_{bp} = 200$ m, $B = 32.2$ m, $D = 19$ m, and a block coefficient of 0.78. The engine room occupies 24 m aft, and the collision bulkhead is 12 m from the forward perpendicular. If the ship must have 7 cargo holds, calculate the average hold length and discuss how non-uniform hold lengths are typically distributed.*

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective cargo length:**

$$
L_{cargo} = L_{bp} - L_{fwd} - L_{aft} - L_{eng}
$$

For aftpeak bulkhead at, say, $0.04 L_{bp} = 8$ m from AP:

$$
L_{cargo} = 200 - 12 - 8 - 24 = 156 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Average hold length:**

$$
L_{hold, avg} = \dfrac{L_{cargo}}{7} = \dfrac{156}{7} = 22.29 \; \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Non-uniform distribution (3 marks):**

| Hold No. | Length (m) | Rationale |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 (Fwd) | 24.0 | End holds often larger; reduces no. of bulkheads forward |
| 2 | 23.0 | |
| 3 | 22.0 | |
| 4 (Mid) | 21.0 | Smallest amidships; minimises still-water bending moment |
| 5 | 22.0 | |
| 6 | 22.5 | |
| 7 (Aft) | 21.5 | Smaller; ER proximity |

**Total ≈ 156 m** (matches $L_{cargo}$). The midship hold is shortest to reduce the lever arm of cargo weight about midship, which lowers the **still-water bending moment** in the largest-amplitude loading condition.

**Total marks: 7 (Lcargo = 2; Lavg = 2; Non-uniform distribution = 3).**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cargo Block Volume Formula:** Always $V_{block} = L_{bp} \cdot B \cdot D \cdot C_b$.
- **Stowage Factor Definition:** Volume per unit mass (m³/t). **Density = 1/SF**.
- **Broken Stowage Allowance:** Typically **15–20%** added to theoretical volume.
- **Collision Bulkhead:** At $0.05 L_{bp}$ to $0.08 L_{bp}$ from FP (SOLAS).
- **Double Bottom Height:** $h_{DB} \geq B/20$ (Class minimum) and is a structural + safety feature.
- **Hatch Coaming:** $\geq 600$ mm above freeboard deck (ICLL).
- **Cofferdam Width:** $\geq 760$ mm between cargo and fuel/heat sources.
- **Hold Distribution:** Midship holds are **shortest**, end holds **longer** — minimises still-water bending moment.
- **Container Slot Calculation:** $N_{TEU} = $ rows × bays × tiers; limited by moulded depth.
- **Tanker Subdivision:** Longitudinal + transverse bulkheads; IGS, SBT, COW for safety.
- **Statutory Frameworks to Remember:** **SOLAS, MARPOL, ICLL, IACS CSR, IMDG Code** (for dangerous goods).
- **For KTU Numericals:** Always state assumptions, define variables, and **conclude with a unit-checked numeric answer**.
- **For KTU Theory:** Always **cite the rule or convention** (e.g., "per SOLAS Reg. II-1/11", "per ICLL Annex 1") to demonstrate regulatory literacy — this earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo Handling Arrangement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cargo Handling Arrangement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cargo Handling Arrangement (CHA):** The integrated engineering system on a marine vessel comprising all structural, mechanical, and electrical subsystems designed to safely and efficiently load, stow, secure, transport, and discharge cargo between the ship and shoreside or floating terminals.

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

In the context of marine vessel design, a **Cargo Handling Arrangement** refers to the complete engineering configuration of spaces, equipment, and supporting systems dedicated to the transfer of cargo. The arrangement encompasses:

1. **Cargo Spaces** — Holds (dry cargo), tanks (liquid cargo), and specialized compartments (reefer, container cells).
2. **Access Structures** — Hatchways, hatch covers, and coamings.
3. **Lifting/Transfer Equipment** — Cranes, derricks, gantries, winches, and conveyor systems.
4. **Auxiliary Systems** — Cargo pumps, pipelines, manifolds, valves, and ventilation.
5. **Securing Systems** — Lashing bridges, twist locks, and stowage fittings.

> [!NOTE]
> The cargo handling arrangement is governed by **classification society rules** (e.g., Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas), **SOLAS regulations** (Chapter VI, VII), and **MARPOL** requirements for environmental protection.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a cargo ship as a **floating warehouse on wheels (well, water!)**. Just as a land-based warehouse needs:
- **Doors and shutters** (→ hatch covers on ships)
- **Forklifts and cranes** (→ shipboard cranes and derricks)
- **Pipelines and pumps** (→ cargo pumps on tankers)
- **Storage racks and tie-downs** (→ lashing systems)

A ship's cargo handling arrangement is the **complete assembly line of equipment** that ensures goods can move smoothly from the dock to the hold and back out again — but unlike a warehouse, this entire operation must withstand heeling, rolling, pitching, and slamming motions of the sea.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** Cargo handling capacities are typically measured in **SWL (Safe Working Load)** in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**, where **1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN** at standard Earth gravity.

### Cargo Handling Systems by Ship Type

| Ship Type | Primary Cargo Handling System |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| General Cargo Ship | Derricks, shipboard cranes, cargo winches |
| Bulk Carrier | Shore-based cranes/loaders (most common), self-dischargers (rare) |
| Container Ship | Shore-based gantry cranes; cell guides for stowage |
| Tanker (Oil/Chemical) | Cargo pumps, pipeline manifolds, inert gas systems |
| LNG/LPG Carrier | Compressors, reliquefaction, cargo pumps |
| Ro-Ro Vessel | Stern/ramp doors, internal ramps, hoistable car decks |
| Reefer Ship | Refrigeration compressors, insulated holds |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hatch Covers — The Ship's "Moving Roof"

Hatch covers are watertight, removable (or sliding) structures that seal the cargo hold from the marine environment. They are arguably the **most critical single component** of the cargo handling arrangement because a failed hatch cover can lead to catastrophic cargo loss or vessel sinking.

**Types of Hatch Covers:**

- **Pointing/Hinged Type**: Lift off with a crane; simple but labor-intensive.
- **Side-Rolling**: Roll on tracks; common in older general cargo ships.
- **End-Folding (Folding)**: Fold into sections; popular on bulk carriers.
- **Sliding (Pontoon)**: Slide fore-aft or athwartship; used on container ships.
- **Lift-Away (Single Pull)**: Single panel lifted by gantry.
- **Stacking**: Multiple panels stacked at one end (bulk carriers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hatch cover design pressure is typically **1.75 m head of water (≈ 17.2 kN/m²)** minimum under IACS S21 standards.

### 2.2 Shipboard Cranes and Derricks

#### Derrick Systems

A derrick is a simple boom-and-winch system typically used on general cargo ships. It consists of:
- **Boom** (the lifting arm)
- **Mast/Post** (vertical support)
- **Gooseneck fitting** (allows boom pivoting)
- **Topping lift winch** (raises/lowers boom)
- **Cargo winch** (hoists/lowers load)

A **union purchase** (married geer) is a derrick arrangement where two derricks (one ship's tackle, one shore tackle) work together to handle cargo too heavy for a single boom.

#### Shipboard Cranes

Modern cargo ships increasingly use electro-hydraulic deck cranes with:
- **SWL**: Typically 30 t per crane (range: 5–80 t)
- **Radius**: 8–30 m outreach
- **Lifting height**: Up to 40 m above deck
- **Hoisting speed**: 10–50 m/min
- **Slewing speed**: 0.5–1.5 rpm

> [!NOTE]
> The **Safe Working Load (SWL)** must be clearly marked on every piece of lifting equipment, and operating beyond SWL is strictly prohibited.

### 2.3 Cargo Pump Systems (Tankers)

For liquid cargo carriers, the **cargo pump** is the heart of the cargo handling system.

| Pump Type | Application | Typical Capacity |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Centrifugal | Clean product tankers, large flow rates | 100–5000 m³/h |
| Deepwell (Submerged) | Chemical tankers, no priming needed | 50–1500 m³/h |
| Reciprocating | High viscosity, slop tanks | 20–500 m³/h |
| Screw/Eductor | Special applications | Variable |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| Crane SWL | $SWL = \frac{T \cdot N}{\text{Safety Factor}}$ | tonnes | Where T = single line pull, N = lines, SF ≥ 1.5 |
| Cargo Pump Head | $H = H_{static} + h_f + \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Static head + friction + velocity head |
| Friction Loss | $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | Darcy-Weisbach equation |
| Lifting Height | $h = h_{hold} + h_{swing} + h_{clearance}$ | m | Hold depth + sway margin + safety clearance |
| Heeling Moment (Load) | $M = W \cdot (R \cdot \cos\phi - d)$ | t·m | Crane load-induced heeling |
| Crane Radius Range | $R_{min} \leq R \leq R_{max}$ | m | Operational working radius |
| Hoisting Power | $P = \frac{SWL \cdot v}{\eta}$ | kW | Where v = hoist speed, η = efficiency (~0.7) |
| Cargo Throughput | $Q = V_{hold} \cdot \rho \cdot \phi$ | tonnes | Where φ = stowage factor inverse |
| Stowage Factor | $SF = \frac{V_{cargo}}{M_{cargo}}$ | m³/t | Volume per unit mass |
| Block Coefficient (Cargo) | $C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | — | For hold volume estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, **familiarity with the crane heeling moment equation and the cargo pump head equation is essential**. These are frequently tested.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design of cargo handling arrangements directly impacts:
- **Port turnaround time** — faster loading = more profitable voyages.
- **Crew safety** — poorly designed systems cause 30%+ of maritime injuries.
- **Cargo integrity** — proper ventilation (1–6 air changes/hour) prevents condensation damage.
- **Vessel stability** — improper load distribution can cause capsizing (e.g., MSC Napoli, 2007).
- **Regulatory compliance** — failure to comply leads to detentions (Port State Control).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cargo Pump System Total Head

The total head that a cargo pump must overcome determines pump power selection. We derive this step by step.

**Step 1: Identify the three head components**

The total head $H$ a pump must supply equals the sum of:
- **Static head** $H_{static}$ — the vertical distance the fluid must be raised.
- **Friction head loss** $h_f$ — losses due to pipe wall friction.
- **Velocity head** $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ — kinetic energy required to maintain flow.

$$H = H_{static} + h_f + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

**Step 2: Express the static head**

The static head is the difference between the liquid surface in the tank and the discharge point:

$$H_{static} = z_{discharge} - z_{tank}$$

For a tanker discharging through a manifold at deck level, with the tank being emptied from full to empty, the average static head is taken as half the tank's filling height plus the deck-to-manifold height.

**Step 3: Apply the Darcy-Weisbach equation for friction loss**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

Where:
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (≈ 0.02 for smooth steel pipes)
- $L$ = total pipe length (m)
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)
- $v$ = flow velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 4: Include minor losses (fittings, valves, bends)**

$$h_{minor} = \sum K_i \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

Where $K_i$ are loss coefficients for each fitting (e.g., 90° elbow: $K \approx 0.9$, gate valve fully open: $K \approx 0.15$).

**Step 5: Final consolidated pump head equation**

$$H = H_{static} + \left(f \cdot \frac{L}{D} + \sum K_i\right) \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

**Step 6: Calculate required pump power**

$$P_{pump} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = cargo density (kg/m³) — e.g., crude oil ≈ 850 kg/m³
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $\eta_{pump}$ = pump efficiency (typically 0.7–0.85)

### 3.2 Worked Example: Cargo Pump Power

**Problem:** A chemical tanker discharges cargo with density $\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at flow rate $Q = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. The system has:
- Static head: $H_{static} = 12 \text{ m}$
- Pipe length: $L = 80 \text{ m}$
- Pipe diameter: $D = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Friction factor: $f = 0.018$
- Minor loss coefficient sum: $\sum K_i = 4.5$
- Pump efficiency: $\eta = 0.75$

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert flow rate to SI units:
$$Q = \frac{150}{3600} = 0.04167 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 2: Calculate flow velocity:
$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.15)^2}{4} = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.04167}{0.01767} = 2.358 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 3: Calculate velocity head:
$$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.358)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{5.560}{19.62} = 0.2834 \text{ m}$$

Step 4: Calculate friction loss:
$$h_f = 0.018 \cdot \frac{80}{0.15} \cdot 0.2834 = 0.018 \cdot 533.33 \cdot 0.2834$$

$$h_f = 2.720 \text{ m}$$

Step 5: Calculate minor losses:
$$h_{minor} = 4.5 \cdot 0.2834 = 1.275 \text{ m}$$

Step 6: Total head:
$$H = 12 + 2.720 + 1.275 = 15.995 \approx 16.0 \text{ m}$$

Step 7: Pump power:
$$P = \frac{1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.04167 \cdot 16.0}{0.75}$$

$$P = \frac{1100 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.6667}{0.75} = \frac{7192.6}{0.75} = 9590 \text{ W} \approx 9.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> In practice, the pump motor is selected with a **15–25% margin** above calculated power to handle transient conditions, so a 12–15 kW motor would be specified.

### 3.3 Derivation: Crane-Induced Heeling Moment

When a crane lifts a load at a given radius, it creates a heeling moment that the ship's stability must overcome.

**Step 1: Define the heeling moment**

The transverse heeling moment $M$ due to a lifted load of weight $W$ at operating radius $R$ is:

$$M = W \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi - W \cdot d$$

Where:
- $W$ = lifted load weight (tonnes)
- $R$ = crane radius from centerline (m)
- $\phi$ = vessel heel angle (typically small, cos φ ≈ 1)
- $d$ = transverse distance of crane pedestal from centerline (m)

**Step 2: Simplification for small heeling angles**

For design purposes, with $\phi$ small:

$$M \approx W \cdot (R - d) \cdot \cos\phi \approx W \cdot (R - d)$$

**Step 3: Heeling angle calculation**

The resulting heel angle is found from:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{M}{\Delta \cdot GM \cdot 100}$$

Where:
- $\Delta$ = ship displacement (tonnes)
- $GM$ = metacentric height (m)
- The factor of 100 converts units (m·t to t·m/cm)

**Step 4: Stability check (KTU important concept)**

For safe operation, IMO requires that the heeling angle during cargo operations does not exceed certain limits (typically 5°–10° depending on operation), and the residual righting lever (GZ) remains positive.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cargo Handling Equipment Selection

| Vessel Type | Crane/Derrick | Hatch Cover | Cargo Pump | Lashing |
|-------------|---------------|-------------|------------|---------|
| Bulk Carrier | Optional (1–2 cranes) | Folding/Sliding | N/A | Trimming |
| Container Ship | None (shore gantry) | Pontoon | N/A | Twist locks, lashings |
| General Cargo | Derricks × 4–8 | Steel pointed | N/A | Wood dunnage, shoring |
| Oil Tanker | 1 pedestal crane | N/A (manifold) | Centrifugal × 2–3 | N/A |
| Chemical Tanker | 2 pedestal cranes | N/A (manifold) | Deepwell | N/A |
| Ro-Ro | 2 deck cranes | Bow/stern ramp | N/A | Chain lashings |
| LNG Carrier | 1 crane (rare) | Dome cover | Submerged | N/A |
