## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define Safe Working Load (SWL) of a shipboard crane. What is its significance in cargo handling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **SWL Definition (1.5 marks):** Safe Working Load is the maximum load that a lifting appliance (crane, derrick, winch) is certified to handle under specific service conditions. It is determined by the manufacturer and verified by classification society tests.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** SWL ensures the lifting equipment operates within safe stress limits, preventing structural failure, accidents, and cargo loss. Exceeding SWL is prohibited under SOLAS and ISM Code regulations. SWL is typically marked on the equipment and documented in the ship's cargo gear register.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**List any three types of hatch covers used in cargo ships and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Sliding/Pontoon Type (1 mark):** Advantage — Single operator can open/close; minimal manual labor; weather-tight sealing.
2. **Folding/End-Folding Type (1 mark):** Advantage — Compact stowage; covers stow vertically at hatch end; saves deck space.
3. **Side-Rolling Type (1 mark):** Advantage — Simple mechanism; reliable in harsh weather; suitable for general cargo ships.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **union purchase derrick rig** used for cargo handling on general cargo ships. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipboard crane is required to lift a load of **25 tonnes** at a maximum radius of **18 m** from the ship's centerline. The crane pedestal is located **2.5 m** from the centerline. If the hoisting speed is **15 m/min** and the overall efficiency is **0.70**, calculate:
   (i) The **heeling moment** produced.
   (ii) The **hoisting power** required.
   (iii) The **minimum Safe Working Load (SWL)** of the crane, assuming a safety factor of 1.5. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) — Union Purchase Derrick (7 marks):**

> **Construction (3.5 marks):**
> - **Two derricks** are rigged together — one from the ship's mast (or another derrick), the other from shore.
> - The **ship's derrick** uses the vessel's own cargo winch and topping lift.
> - The **shore derrick** (or shore crane) provides the lifting tackle.
> - The **gooseneck** at the derrick head allows the boom to pivot.
> - **Wire ropes**: topping lift (supports boom), cargo runner (lifts load), guy ropes (stabilize mast).
> - **Mast/king post**: vertical support for the derrick.

> **Working (3.5 marks):**
> 1. The two derricks are positioned so their heads meet above the load.
> 2. The cargo runner is connected to the load hook.
> 3. Both derricks are **topped up** (raised) simultaneously to lift the load clear of the hold.
> 4. The load is then **swung** inboard or outboard by paying out one derrick while topping the other.
> 5. This "married geer" system allows handling of heavy lifts exceeding single derrick capacity.
> 6. Used extensively in ports lacking shore cranes, or for self-sustaining cargo operations.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) — Crane Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Load $W = 25 \text{ t}$
- Radius $R = 18 \text{ m}$
- Pedestal offset $d = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- Hoist speed $v = 15 \text{ m/min}$
- Efficiency $\eta = 0.70$
- Safety factor $SF = 1.5$

**(i) Heeling Moment (2 marks):**

$$M = W \cdot (R - d)$$

$$M = 25 \cdot (18 - 2.5) = 25 \cdot 15.5$$

$$M = 387.5 \text{ t·m}$$

**(ii) Hoisting Power (2.5 marks):**

Convert hoist speed to m/s:

$$v = \frac{15}{60} = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P = \frac{W \cdot g \cdot v}{\eta}$$

$$P = \frac{25{,}000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.25}{0.70}$$

$$P = \frac{61{,}312.5}{0.70}$$

$$P = 87{,}589 \text{ W} \approx 87.6 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Minimum SWL (2.5 marks):**

$$SWL_{min} = W \cdot SF = 25 \cdot 1.5 = 37.5 \text{ t}$$

The crane must therefore have an SWL rating of at least **37.5 tonnes** to safely lift the 25-tonne load with the required safety margin.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the various **types of cargo pumps** used on oil tankers. Compare **centrifugal pumps** and **deepwell pumps** in terms of construction, priming, and application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cargo pump system on a chemical tanker has the following specifications:
   - Cargo density: $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
   - Flow rate: $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$
   - Pipe internal diameter: $D = 0.20 \text{ m}$
   - Total pipe length (including equivalent length for fittings): $L_{eq} = 120 \text{ m}$
   - Darcy friction factor: $f = 0.020$
   - Static discharge head: $H_{static} = 14 \text{ m}$
   - Pump efficiency: $\eta = 0.78$

Calculate:
   (i) The **flow velocity** in the pipeline.
   (ii) The **friction head loss**.
   (iii) The **total pump head**.
   (iv) The **pump power** required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q(a) — Cargo Pumps (7 marks):**

| Feature | Centrifugal Pump | Deepwell Pump |
|---------|------------------|---------------|
| **Location** | Mounted in pump room | Submerged in tank |
| **Priming** | Requires priming | Self-priming |
| **Suction Head** | Limited (NPSH issues) | High (no suction issues) |
| **Maintenance** | Easier access | Requires tank entry |
| **Application** | Clean products, large flow | Chemicals, high viscosity |
| **Sealing** | Mechanical seal | Submerged (no seal needed) |

> **Other types (1.5 marks):**
> - **Reciprocating (positive displacement):** For high-viscosity cargo; constant flow regardless of head.
> - **Screw pumps:** For viscous, shear-sensitive cargoes (e.g., polymers).
> - **Eductor/Jet pumps:** For stripping residual cargo from tanks.

> **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Modern chemical tankers prefer **deepwell pumps** for safety (no pump room, no leak path) and **centrifugal pumps** remain dominant in clean-product tankers for high throughput.

---

**Model Solution for Q(b) — Pump System Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$
- $D = 0.20 \text{ m}$
- $L_{eq} = 120 \text{ m}$
- $f = 0.020$
- $H_{static} = 14 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.78$

**(i) Flow Velocity (1.5 marks):**

Convert flow rate:
$$Q = \frac{200}{3600} = 0.0556 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.20)^2}{4} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

Flow velocity:
$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.0556}{0.0314} = 1.771 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Friction Head Loss (2 marks):**

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L_{eq}}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

$$h_f = 0.020 \cdot \frac{120}{0.20} \cdot \frac{(1.771)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81}$$

$$h_f = 0.020 \cdot 600 \cdot \frac{3.136}{19.62}$$

$$h_f = 12 \cdot 0.1598 = 1.918 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Total Pump Head (1.5 marks):**

$$H = H_{static} + h_f = 14 + 1.918 = 15.92 \text{ m}$$

**(iv) Pump Power (2 marks):**

$$P = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta}$$

$$P = \frac{950 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0556 \cdot 15.92}{0.78}$$

$$P = \frac{8246.5}{0.78} = 10{,}572 \text{ W} \approx 10.6 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students lose marks by forgetting to convert m³/h to m³/s for flow rate, and m/min to m/s for hoisting speed. **Always use SI base units** in formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting Efficiency:** The heeling moment is independent of efficiency, but power calculations MUST include η in the denominator.
> 3. **Confusing SWL and Load:** SWL is the **safe limit**, not the actual load. The actual load must always be **less than** SWL.
> 4. **Ignoring Velocity Head:** For short pipelines, velocity head is negligible, but for long discharge lines, include $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ in total head.
> 5. **Sign Convention in Heeling:** The heeling moment is **transverse**; ensure the radius is measured perpendicular to the ship's centerline, not along the boom.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Cargo Handling Arrangement**

- ⭐ **SWL** of a crane must be ≥ (Actual Load × Safety Factor, typically 1.5).
- ⭐ **Hatch covers** must withstand a minimum design head of **1.75 m water column** (IACS S21).
- ⭐ **Union purchase** = two derricks working in tandem for heavy lifts beyond single-boom capacity.
- ⭐ **Centrifugal pumps** need priming; **deepwell pumps** are self-priming and submerged.
- ⭐ **Total pump head** = Static head + Friction loss + Velocity head + Minor losses.
- ⭐ **Heeling moment** from crane operation: $M = W \cdot (R - d) \cdot \cos\phi$.
- ⭐ **Hoisting power**: $P = \frac{W \cdot g \cdot v}{\eta}$, always include efficiency.
- ⭐ **Cargo handling equipment** is governed by SOLAS Ch. VI/VII, IACS standards, and the ship's Cargo Gear Register (Blue Book).
- ⭐ **Hatch cover types** to remember: Side-rolling, Folding, Sliding/pontoon, Stacking, Lift-away.
- ⭐ **Friction loss** uses Darcy-Weisbach: $h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$.
- ⭐ **Stowage factor** (m³/t) determines hold volume requirements.
- ⭐ **Container ship lashing** uses twist locks, turnbuckles, and lashing bridges — never rely on container weight alone for stack strength.
- ⭐ **Inert gas systems** (IGS) on tankers prevent cargo tank explosions — mandatory under SOLAS for oil tankers > 20,000 DWT.
- ⭐ **Ventilation rates** for cargo holds: typically 1–6 air changes/hour to prevent condensation.
- ⭐ **Always convert units to SI base units** before substituting into formulas.
- ⭐ **IMO crane heeling limits:** Heel angle during single-crane operation should not exceed 5°; residual GZ must remain positive.
- ⭐ **Pump room vs. Deepwell:** Modern chemical tankers eliminate pump rooms for safety; deepwell pumps dominate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrangement of Machinery Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Arrangement of Machinery Spaces ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Machinery Spaces (KTU 2024 Definition):** Machinery spaces in a marine vessel are purpose-built, structurally reinforced compartments housing the prime mover, propulsion machinery, auxiliary power units, and all associated support systems required for the generation, transmission, and distribution of mechanical and electrical energy onboard. According to **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) Chapter II-2**, machinery spaces are categorically classified as: - **Category A Machinery Spaces:** Compartments containing internal combustion machinery (diesel engines, gas turbines) using liquid fuel having a flashpoint ≤ **60 °C**, or fuel oil heating installations. - **Other Machinery Spaces:** Compartments containing electric motors, generators, pumps, purifiers, steering gear, and refrigeration units not falling under Category A. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The arrangement problem is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization** between *propulsion efficiency*, *cargo capacity*, *damage survivability*, and *maintainability*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Human Body" Analogy) Think of a ship as a **giant living organism**: - The **hull** is the skeleton and skin. - The **cargo holds** are the stomach (where resources are stored). - The **bridge and navigation systems** are the brain. - The **machinery space** is the **heart and lungs** — pumping energy (electricity) and propelling blood (coolant, fuel) throughout the body, while expelling exhaust gases like metabolic waste. Just as the human heart sits in the chest cavity (centrally protected by the rib cage), the **engine room** is usually placed in a **protected, accessible, and structurally robust** part of the ship. The **shaft tunnel** acts like a spine carrying nerves (drive shaft) from the heart to the limbs (propeller). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Typical engine room volume per shaft horsepower:** **0.25 m³/kW to 0.40 m³/kW** (merchant vessels). - **Standard design headroom in ER:** **2.0 m minimum** to **3.5 m** above gratings. - **Fire class divisions (SOLAS A-60):** **60 minutes** structural fire resistance for ER boundaries. - **Permissible noise level (ILO/IMO A.468(XIII)):** **90 dB(A)** max for engine room crews. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Philosophy: The "Why" of Arrangement The arrangement of machinery spaces is governed by **six cardinal engineering principles**: 1. **Longitudinal Balance Principle** — The machinery weight must be distributed such that the **Longitudinal Centre of Gravity (LCG)** lies within acceptable trim limits (typically **±0.5% Lpp** from the design LCG). 2. **Protection Principle** — Critical machinery must be protected from **battle damage** (warships) or **collision/grounding** (merchant vessels). 3. **Maintainability Principle** — Overhead cranes, side shell doors, and trunking must permit major engine overhauls without dry-docking. 4. **Ventilation & Heat Rejection Principle** — Adequate supply of combustion air (typically **6 m³/kW·h** for diesel engines) and removal of waste heat. 5. **Vibration & Noise Principle** — Resonance frequencies of hull girder must be decoupled from engine excitation frequencies. 6. **Economic Principle** — Maximize cargo earning capacity; minimize shaft length, weight, and power transmission losses. ### 2.2 Location Options — Comparative Analysis | **Location** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | **Typical Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **Aft** (70% merchant ships) | Shorter shaft → lower transmission loss, larger unobstructed cargo bay, better trim with aft superstructure | Stern tube maintenance complex, vulnerable to flooding, less efficient wake distribution for single-screw | Tankers, bulk carriers, container ships | | **Amidships** (legacy) | Good fore-aft balance for symmetric hulls, protected from bow/stern damage | Splits cargo space into 2–3 holds, longer shafting | Older tramp steamers, RO-RO ships | | **Forward** (warships) | Maximum protection from torpedo hits aft, separates combat zones | Increases draft at bow, requires cruising turbines or COGAG arrangements | Naval combatants, frigates, aircraft carriers | | **Distributed (CODAG/CODOG)** | Redundancy, survivability, optimized trim | Complex controls, higher capital cost | Warships, advanced multi-purpose vessels | ### 2.3 Engine Room (ER) — Internal Arrangement Logic The internal layout follows a **"Hot-Cold-Access" zoning principle**: - **Hot Zone (Forward Lower Platform):** Main engine (slow-speed diesel), exhaust manifold, turbochargers, silencers. - **Cold Zone (Aft Lower Platform):** Generators, switchboards, electrical panels, control consoles. - **Workshop & Store Zone:** A dedicated **2.0 m × 2.5 m** minimum area for spares, machine shop. - **Pump & Purifier Zone:** Centrifugal purifiers, fuel/lub oil purifiers, freshwater generators. - **Ergonomic Access:** Catwalks and ladders with **max vertical rise of 4.5 m** between platforms. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** A common ESE (End Semester Examination) question asks students to sketch the **"single-line diagram"** of a marine engine room. Master the layout of: *Main Engine → Shaft Line → CPP/FPP → Stern Tube → Sea Water Inlet.* ### 2.4 Shaft Tunnel — Engineering Rationale The **shaft tunnel** is a watertight passage running from the engine room to the forward end of the stern tube. It serves **three critical functions**: 1. **Access to Stern Tube Bearing:** Allows inspection and bearing renewal without dry-docking. 2. **Emergency Escape Route:** Per SOLAS Reg. II-2, machinery spaces must have **two independent escape routes**; the shaft tunnel serves as one. 3. **Structural Protection:** Shields the propeller shaft from cargo shifting or collision impact. The tunnel cross-section must accommodate a man standing upright (typically **1.6 m × 1.8 m** clear) and allow a small chain block (≥ **2 tonne SWL**) for shaft removal. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |---|---|---|---| | Specific volume of ER per SHP | $V_{ER} = (0.25 \text{ to } 0.40) \times P_{SHP}$ | m³ | Merchant vessels | | Engine room length (approx) | $L_{ER} \approx 1.2 \times \text{ME length} + 4$ | m | Empirical | | Combustion air requirement | $Q_{air} = 6.0 \times P_{SHP}$ | m³/h | Per kW rating | | Trim correction | $\Delta T = \frac{W_{mach} \times (x_{mach} - LCG_{design})}{MTC \times 100}$ | m | MTC in t·m/cm | | LCG shift due to machinery | $\Delta LCG = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{\Delta W}$ | m | Longitudinal movement | | Stern tube bearing pressure | $p_{b} = \frac{R_{eff}}{L_{b} \times d_{shaft}}$ | N/mm² | $L_b$ = bearing length | | Effective hull weight density | $\rho_{hull} = 0.85 \times \rho_{steel}$ | t/m³ | $7850 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 0.85$ | | Shaft diameter (empirical) | $d_{shaft} = 0.06 \times \sqrt[3]{P_{SHP} \times R_{pm}^{-1}}$ | m | Lloyds rule (approximate) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention Used:** $P_{SHP}$ is **Shaft Horsepower (kW)**, $R_{pm}$ is **gear reduction ratio**, $MTC$ is the **Moment to Change Trim (t·m/cm)**, and $L_{b}$ is the **shaft bearing length (mm)**. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Modern merchant vessels are designed with **integrated electric propulsion (IEP)** where the engine room houses **prime movers and generators**, while propulsion is achieved via **electric motors** driving the propeller. This decouples physical shaft routing from engine location, freeing the General Arrangement (GA) for optimization of cargo volume. LNG carriers, cruise liners, and naval combatants extensively use this philosophy. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Longitudinal Trim Change Due to Machinery Relocation **Statement:** Compute the trim change when machinery of weight $W_{mach}$ is shifted by a distance $\Delta x$ from its design longitudinal position. **Given Data (Typical KTU Problem):** - Vessel displacement $\Delta = 25{,}000$ tonnes - MTC (Moment to Change Trim) = $350$ t·m/cm - Original LCG (forward of midship) = $+0.5$ m (positive = aft of midship) - Design machinery center = $+12.0$ m from midship - Proposed machinery center = $+15.0$ m from midship - Machinery weight $W_{mach} = 1200$ tonnes **Step 1 — Identify the change in LCG.** The shift in machinery causes a parallel shift in the overall LCG by the following amount: $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{\Delta} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** The numerical shift in LCG is: $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{1200 \text{ t} \times (15.0 - 12.0) \text{ m}}{25{,}000 \text{ t}} $$ $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{1200 \times 3.0}{25{,}000} $$ $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{3600}{25{,}000} = 0.144 \text{ m aft of original LCG} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the change in trim.** The trim formula, using the MTC principle, is: $$ \Delta Trim = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{MTC \times 100} $$ **Step 4 — Convert MTC to consistent units and substitute.** Since MTC is in t·m/cm, we divide displacement-derived moment by MTC to obtain cm of trim: $$ \Delta Trim = \frac{1200 \times 3.0}{350 \times 100} $$ $$ \Delta Trim = \frac{3600}{35{,}000} = 0.1028 \text{ m} = 10.28 \text{ cm (by the stern)} $$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A trim change of **+10.28 cm by the stern** (positive sign convention = stern-down). This is acceptable if the design trim envelope allows **±15 cm**. The vessel will now float with a slight stern-down attitude. Corrective action: relocate **forward auxiliary ballast tanks** or **shift bridge wing consoles** forward. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Engine Room Volume Estimation **Multi-step derivation for ER volume from fundamental principles.** **Step 1 — Engine Block Dimensions (2-stroke low-speed diesel).** For a 2-stroke MAN B&W type engine, the cylinder bore $D$ and stroke $S$ relate to SHP as: $$ P_{SHP} = \frac{p_{me} \times L_{me} \times A_{piston} \times n}{60} \times \eta_{mech} $$ where $p_{me}$ = mean effective pressure (typically **0.8 to 1.0 MPa** for modern 2-strokes), $L_{me}$ = number of cylinders, $n$ = rpm. **Step 2 — Engine envelope (length, width, height).** Empirically, for slow-speed 2-stroke diesels: $$ L_{engine} \approx 2.5 \times (S \times n_{cyl}) + 1.5 $$ $$ H_{engine} \approx 2.0 \times D + 1.0 $$ $$ W_{engine} \approx 1.8 \times D $$ **Step 3 — Required ER clearances.** - Front (free end): **1.5 m minimum** (for piston withdrawal) - Aft (coupling end): **1.0 m** minimum - Sides: **0.9 m** walkways - Top: **1.5 m** for cylinder head lifting - Bottom: **1.2 m** for crankcase inspection pit **Step 4 — Total ER Plan Area.** $$ A_{ER} = (L_{engine} + 2.5) \times (W_{engine} + 1.8) $$ **Step 5 — ER Volume.** $$ V_{ER} = A_{ER} \times (H_{engine} + 2.5) $$ This gives the **clear engine room volume**, which is then multiplied by a **1.4 to 1.6 layout factor** to account for auxiliaries, switchboards, funnels, and passageways. > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** For a 20,000 kW container ship engine, expected $V_{ER} \approx 5000$–$8000$ m³. Students should always validate the final number against the empirical range **0.25–0.40 m³/kW**. ### 3.3 Comparative Derivation: Aft vs. Amidships ER (Shaft Weight Trade-off) **Objective:** Compare shaft tunnel weight in two arrangements. For an aft ER with a single shaft of length $L_{aft}$ and an amidships ER with length $L_{mid}$: $$ W_{shaft,aft} = \rho_{steel} \times \frac{\pi \times d^2}{4} \times L_{aft} $$ $$ W_{shaft,mid} = \rho_{steel} \times \frac{\pi \times d^2}{4} \times L_{mid} $$ Since $L_{mid} \approx 1.8 \times L_{aft}$ (typical merchant ship), the **amidships ER adds 80% shaft weight** and corresponding bearing/support costs. This is a **key economic argument** for the modern preference of aft engine rooms. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Machinery Spaces The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between machinery spaces and the propulsion/electrical system of a typical merchant vessel. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Fuel Oil Settling
and Service Tanks] -->|FO supply| nodeB nodeB[Main Engine Room
ER Level 1 and 2] -->|Exhaust gases| nodeC[Uptake and Funnel] nodeB -->|Drive shaft| nodeD[Shaft Tunnel] nodeD -->|Propeller shaft| nodeE[Stern Tube and Propeller] nodeF[Emergency Generator Room] -->|Backup power| nodeG[Main Switchboard Room] nodeB -->|Electrical output| nodeG nodeG -->|Distribution| nodeH[Bridge and Accommodation] nodeG -->|Distribution| nodeI[Cargo Holds and Deck Machinery] nodeJ[Steering Gear Compartment] -->|Hydraulic lines| nodeK[Rudder] nodeL[Sea Water Inlet and Box Cooler] -->|Cooling| nodeB nodeM[Purifier Room] -->|Clean lube oil| nodeB nodeN[Bow Thruster Room] -->|Forward propulsion assist| nodeO[Man overboard zone] ``` ### 4.2 Engine Room Internal Layout — Zonal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ERPLATFORMS nodeP1[Upper Platform: Switchboard
and ECR] --> nodeP2[Middle Platform: Auxiliaries
Pumps and Purifiers] nodeP2 --> nodeP3[Lower Platform: Main Engine
and Turbochargers] end nodeP1 -->|Man access via ladders| nodeP2 nodeP2 -->|Man access via ladders| nodeP3 nodeP3 -->|Stern tunnel access| nodeQ[Shaft Tunnel] nodeP1 -->|Escape trunk| nodeR[Weather Deck] nodeP3 -->|Sea chest| nodeS[Lube Oil Storage] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Decision Tree — ER Location Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart{Vessel Type?} --> nodeWarship[Warship] nodeStart --> nodeMerchant[Merchant Vessel] nodeStart --> nodeSpecial[Special Vessel LNG Cruise] nodeWarship --> nodeW1[Forward ER for protection] nodeW1 --> nodeW2[CODAG or COGAG] nodeMerchant --> nodeM1{Cargo type?} nodeM1 --> nodeM2[Tanker Bulk Carrier] nodeM1 --> nodeM3[Container Ship] nodeM2 --> nodeM4[Aft ER preferred] nodeM3 --> nodeM5[Mid or Aft depending on bay count] nodeSpecial --> nodeSp1[Distributed IEP layout] nodeSp1 --> nodeSp2[Multiple machinery modules] ``` ### 4.4 Machinery Space Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeTop[Machinery Spaces Onboard] --> nodeCatA nodeTop --> nodeCatB nodeCatA[Category A Machinery Spaces] --> nodeA1[Main Engine Room] nodeCatA --> nodeA2[Boiler Room if any] nodeCatB[Other Machinery Spaces] --> nodeB1[Auxiliary Engine Room] nodeCatB --> nodeB2[Steering Gear Compartment] nodeCatB --> nodeB3[Purifier and Pump Room] nodeCatB --> nodeB4[Shaft Tunnel and Tunnel Space] nodeCatB --> nodeB5[Emergency Generator Room] nodeCatB --> nodeB6[Bow and Stern Thruster Rooms] nodeCatB --> nodeB7[Refrigeration Plant Room] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Machinery Space" as per SOLAS classification. Differentiate between Category A and Other Machinery Spaces.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Machinery Space (1 Mark):** A machinery space is any space containing propulsion machinery, boilers, oil fuel units, steam and internal combustion engines, generators, electric motors, and other similar machinery and equipment, including spaces used for steering gear, pumps, purifiers, and refrigeration. > **Category A (1 Mark):** A Category A machinery space is a compartment containing internal combustion engines using liquid fuel having a flash point ≤ **60 °C**, OR fuel oil heating installations. These require the highest level of fire protection (A-60 class divisions). > **Other Machinery Spaces (1 Mark):** Compartments housing electric motors, generators, pumps, purifiers, refrigeration, and steering gear — not falling under Category A. These are subject to A-30 or A-0 fire class division standards. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State any three advantages of locating the main engine room in the aft of a modern merchant vessel.* **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 Mark Each):** 1. **Shorter shaft line:** The shaft length is reduced to typically 0.3 Lpp instead of 0.6 Lpp, reducing transmission losses by 30–40% and saving shaft weight (1 Mark). 2. **Larger uninterrupted cargo space:** Cargo holds are not split by an amidships machinery space, maximizing cargo earning capacity (1 Mark). 3. **Improved trim control:** With superstructure aft and engine aft, the vessel's center of gravity is naturally aft-balanced, reducing corrective ballast needs (1 Mark). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]** **(a)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **internal layout of a main engine room** of a single-screw bulk carrier, highlighting the zonal arrangement of hot, cold, and access zones. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 30,000 DWT bulk carrier has its main engine relocated **5 m aft** of the design machinery centerline. Given: Displacement = **40,000 t**, MTC = **380 t·m/cm**, machinery weight = **1800 t**, original LCG (from midship) = **+2.0 m**. Calculate the **change in trim** caused by this relocation. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** The answer must include a **sketch (3 Marks)** and **descriptive labels (4 Marks)**. Key elements to include: - **Hot Zone (Forward Lower Platform):** Main engine, turbochargers, exhaust uptakes, silencers, fuel injection pumps. - **Cold Zone (Aft Lower Platform):** Auxiliary engines, diesel generators, main switchboard, ECR (Engine Control Room), workshop. - **Access Zone:** Two independent escape routes — one ladder to the main deck forward, one via the shaft tunnel aft. - **Pump & Purifier Zone:** On a separate platform, lube oil purifier, fuel oil purifier, freshwater generator. - **Shaft Tunnel:** Aft exit to stern tube, 1.8 m clear height, with emergency lighting and watertight doors at both ends. > **Mark Distribution:** Sketch (3) + Zone Identification (2) + Escape & Access Routes (1) + Logical Flow Description (1) = 7 Marks **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Identify the change in LCG using the formula (2 Marks):** $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{\Delta} = \frac{1800 \times 5.0}{40{,}000} $$ $$ \Delta LCG = \frac{9000}{40{,}000} = 0.225 \text{ m (aft of original LCG)} $$ **Step 2 — Convert new LCG position (1 Mark):** $$ LCG_{new} = +2.0 + 0.225 = +2.225 \text{ m aft of midship} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the trim change (2 Marks):** $$ \Delta Trim = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{MTC \times 100} $$ $$ \Delta Trim = \frac{1800 \times 5.0}{380 \times 100} = \frac{9000}{38{,}000} = 0.2368 \text{ m} = 23.68 \text{ cm} $$ **Step 4 — Direction of trim (1 Mark):** The trim is by the **stern (positive sign convention)**. **Step 5 — Engineering commentary (1 Mark):** > **Result:** 23.68 cm by the stern. This **exceeds the typical ±15 cm** design envelope. **Recommendation:** Distribute auxiliary ballast forward or relocate 200 t of bridge wing consoles forward to counter the stern-down tendency. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]** **(a)** Compare **aft, amidships, and forward** machinery space arrangements, citing two advantages and disadvantages of each, with one example vessel type for each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Estimate the **engine room volume** required for a 25,000 kW slow-speed 2-stroke diesel installation. Use the empirical range **0.25 to 0.40 m³/kW** and justify which value you select based on modern container ship practice. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** | **Location** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** | **Vessel Example** | |---|---|---|---| | **Aft** | (i) Shorter shaft, (ii) Larger cargo bay | (i) Vulnerable to stern flooding, (ii) Stern tube maintenance complex | Container ships, tankers | | **Amidships** | (i) Symmetric weight distribution, (ii) Good protection | (i) Splits cargo bay, (ii) Long shafting cost | Older ro-ro, ice-class vessels | | **Forward** | (i) Protected from torpedo aft, (ii) Redundancy in combat | (i) Increases bow draft, (ii) Complex power transmission | Warships, frigates, carriers | > **Mark Distribution:** 1 Mark per column × 7 categories (3 locations × 2 advantages + 3 locations × disadvantage) = 7 Marks. **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Apply the lower bound of the empirical range (2 Marks):** $$ V_{ER,min} = 0.25 \times 25{,}000 = 6250 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the upper bound (2 Marks):** $$ V_{ER,max} = 0.40 \times 25{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Choose the appropriate value (2 Marks):** For a **modern container ship** with high integration, compact turbochargers, and modular switchboards, the **lower end of the range** is applicable. Selecting $V_{ER} = 0.28$ m³/kW: $$ V_{ER} = 0.28 \times 25{,}000 = 7000 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Justify the selection (1 Mark):** > **Justification:** Modern 2-stroke diesels (e.g., MAN B&W 11S90ME-C) feature vertically arranged auxiliaries, integrated cooling modules, and skid-mounted purifiers, which **reduce the auxiliary footprint by ~15%** compared to legacy installations. Hence, **0.28 m³/kW** is the optimal design value, well within the empirical envelope. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Machinery Space Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Category A" with "Engine Room"** — A vessel can have a non-Category-A machinery space (e.g., electric motor room). Always state the **flashpoint criterion (≤ 60 °C)** for Category A. > 2. **Unit Mismatch in Trim Calculation** — MTC is in **t·m/cm**, but the displacement is in **tonnes**. Missing the **/100** factor to convert cm to m is the single most common error (loses 1–2 marks). > 3. **Forgetting Escape Routes** — A machinery space layout sketch without **two independent escape routes** (ladder + shaft tunnel) is considered **incomplete** per SOLAS. > 4. **Midship Sign Convention** — Positive LCG = **aft of midship**, negative = **forward of midship**. Reversing this loses 1 mark per question. > 5. **Shaft Tunnel Height** — Less than **1.6 m clear** headroom violates SOLAS Reg. II-2. Examiners look for this compliance check. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Arrangement of Machinery Spaces** > > - **SOLAS Definition:** Category A (flashpoint ≤ 60 °C) vs. Other Machinery Spaces. > - **Standard ER Volume:** 0.25–0.40 m³ per kW of SHP. > - **Location Choice:** Aft (70% modern merchant), Amidships (legacy), Forward (warships). > - **Aft ER advantages:** Short shaft, large cargo bay, easier trim control. > - **Shaft Tunnel Functions:** Stern tube access, SOLAS escape route, shaft protection. > - **Headroom:** 1.6 m minimum in shaft tunnel; 2.0 m in ER. > - **Fire class:** A-60 between ER and accommodation. > - **Combustion air:** ~6 m³/kW·h. > - **Trim formula:** $\Delta T = \frac{W \times \Delta x}{MTC \times 100}$ (cm). > - **LCG shift formula:** $\Delta LCG = \frac{W_{mach} \times \Delta x}{\Delta}$. > - **Two escape routes:** Mandatory from every Category A space. > - **Ventilation:** Independent supply and exhaust; fire dampers on both. > - **Noise limit:** 90 dB(A) per ILO/IMO. > - **Modern trend:** Integrated Electric Propulsion (IEP) decouples engine location from shaft route. > - **Auxiliaries to remember:** Purifiers, FW generator, pumps, switchboards, ECR, workshop. > - **Always show units** — marks awarded for consistency in trim/displacement problems.

topics.title: Accommodation and Associated Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accommodation and Associated Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Accommodation spaces** in a marine vessel refer to the totality of all enclosed volumes, compartments, and zones dedicated to the safe habitability, operation, well-being, and administrative functioning of the crew and (where applicable) passengers. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the regulatory framework codified in **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)**, the **Maritime Labour Convention 2006 (MLC 2006)**, and the **International Labour Organization (ILO) Convention No. 92 & 133**, accommodation encompasses sleeping cabins, mess rooms, recreation rooms, sanitary facilities, the galley, pantries, the navigation bridge, the engine control room, workshops, offices, medical spaces, and storage zones supporting daily shipboard life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulatory Anchor (KTU Board Frequently Tested):**
> Accommodation design is governed by the triad: **SOLAS Ch. II-2 & III**, **MLC 2006 Title 3 (Accommodation and Recreational Facilities)**, and the **Classification Society Rules (IRS, LR, DNV, BV, ABS)**. KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **headroom minimum of 2.03 m**, the **marking of escape routes**, and **fire integrity boundaries** between accommodation and machinery spaces.

### 1.2 Sub-Categorization of Spaces

For systematic study and regulatory compliance, accommodation is classified into four functional families:

| Family | Functional Purpose | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Crew Living Spaces** | Rest, sleep, personal hygiene | Cabins, bathrooms, mess rooms, lounges |
| **Operational Workspaces** | Navigation, control, command | Wheelhouse, ECR, cargo control room |
| **Service & Support Spaces** | Food, health, logistics | Galley, pantry, hospital, laundry, stores |
| **Circulation & Safety Spaces** | Movement and emergency egress | Corridors, stairways, muster stations, embarkation areas |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Township" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a ship not as a vehicle, but as a **floating township** that must remain operationally self-sufficient for voyages lasting between **30 to 180 days** without touching land. A township has houses (cabins), restaurants (galley + mess), hospitals (sick bay), police stations (bridge), power plants (engine room), and roads (corridors). However, this township faces a unique engineering challenge: it must withstand **constant rolling, pitching, high humidity (up to 95% RH), salinity, vibration, and severe space constraints**. Every square metre costs shipping capacity and fuel. Therefore, marine accommodation design is the art of compressing the livability of a small town into the hull of a vessel, while never compromising the **physical safety, psychological welfare, and operational efficiency** of the seafarer.

## 1.4 The Three Foundational Design Pillars

The KTU 2024 scheme framework identifies three non-negotiable pillars governing accommodation design:

1. **Habitability** — ensuring the physical environment supports human physiology (thermal comfort, lighting, noise limits).
2. **Safety** — providing intact escape routes, fire subdivision, and life-saving appliance accessibility.
3. **Functionality** — ensuring workflows (food service, navigation, maintenance) are ergonomically optimized within the available envelope.

The **minimum ceiling height (headroom)** in any space where crew move freely is set at **2.03 m (6 ft 8 in)** as a baseline ILO/Ministerial standard, with the **wheelhouse and hospital requiring 2.30 m or more**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Hierarchy of Marine Spaces

Accommodation spaces are designed using a **top-down hierarchy** rooted in regulatory hierarchy, naval architectural physics, and ergonomics. The logical flow is:

**Step 1 — Crew Complement Determination (CCD)**
The total number of seafarers to be accommodated is fixed by the **Minimum Safe Manning Document (MSMD)** issued by the flag administration. This number determines every downstream space allocation.

**Step 2 — Rank Segregation**
Accommodation is strictly segregated by rank to respect hierarchy, privacy, and operational security:
- **Officer Class** — Captain, Chief Officer, Chief Engineer, etc.
- **Junior Officer / Cadet Class** — Third Officer, Engine Cadets.
- **Rating Class** — Bosun, ABs, Oiler, Cook, etc.

**Step 3 — Function Zoning**
Zones are organized such that:
- Navigation spaces (bridge) are placed **forward and high** (clear sightlines).
- Engineering spaces (ECR) are placed **adjacent to the engine room**.
- Crew living spaces are placed **amidships** (lowest motion zone, maximum stability).
- Hazardous areas (paint locker, CO₂ room) are isolated and **outside the accommodation block**.

**Step 4 — Volume & Area Allocation**
Each space is allocated a minimum area per occupant derived from the governing regulation.

**Step 5 — Environmental Conditioning**
HVAC, lighting, and noise control are overlaid on the spatial envelope.

**Step 6 — Safety Overlay**
Escape routes, fire dampers, and subdivision boundaries are superimposed.

## 2.2 Why This Hierarchy? (The Engineering "Why")

> The placement of crew spaces **amidships** is not arbitrary. In a seaway, the **pitching motion** is most severe at the bow and stern, while **rolling** is moderate amidships. Amidship placement reduces seasickness incidence, improves sleep quality, and protects personnel during heavy weather. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes this **motion sickness mitigation principle**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **high-yield** for the ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize the numerical constants and the units.

| S.No | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Governing Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum Headroom (general crew spaces)** | $H_{min} = 2.03$ | m | ILO 133 / MLC 2006 |
| 2 | **Minimum Headroom (corridors, sanitary)** | $H_{cor} \geq 2.00$ | m | Class Rules |
| 3 | **Minimum Headroom (hospital, wheelhouse)** | $H_{sp} \geq 2.30$ | m | Class Rules |
| 4 | **Floor Area per Officer (single cabin)** | $A_{off} \geq 7.5$ | m² | MLC Reg 3.2 |
| 5 | **Floor Area per Rating (single cabin)** | $A_{rat} \geq 4.5$ | m² | MLC Reg 3.2 |
| 6 | **Floor Area per Rating (shared, max 2)** | $A_{sh} \geq 3.75$ | m²/person | MLC Reg 3.2 |
| 7 | **Mess Room Area per Seat** | $A_{mess} \geq 1.0$ | m²/seat | MLC Reg 3.3 |
| 8 | **Hospital Bed Area (vessels > 500 GT, crew > 15)** | $A_{hosp} \geq 4.0$ | m² | MLC Reg 3.5 |
| 9 | **Air Change Rate (sanitary spaces)** | $N_{ac} = 6$ to $8$ | changes/hr | HVAC design |
| 10 | **Air Change Rate (galley)** | $N_{gal} = 15$ to $20$ | changes/hr | HVAC design |
| 11 | **Air Change Rate (accommodation general)** | $N_{gen} = 4$ to $6$ | changes/hr | HVAC design |
| 12 | **Noise Limit (cabins, mess rooms)** | $L_{n} \leq 55$ | dB(A) | MLC / Class |
| 13 | **Noise Limit (wheelhouse, ECR)** | $L_{n} \leq 65$ | dB(A) | MLC / Class |
| 14 | **Illuminance (cabins)** | $E_{cab} = 100$ to $150$ | lux | IEC 92-101 |
| 15 | **Illuminance (mess, recreation)** | $E_{rec} = 200$ to $300$ | lux | IEC 92-101 |
| 16 | **Illuminance (engine room)** | $E_{eng} = 200$ to $300$ | lux | IEC 92-101 |
| 17 | **Illuminance (corridors, stairs)** | $E_{cor} = 50$ to $100$ | lux | IEC 92-101 |
| 18 | **Ventilation Duct Velocity (main)** | $v_{duct} \leq 10$ | m/s | Smoke control |
| 19 | **Total Ventilation Air (Q_total)** | $Q_{total} = N_{ac} \times V$ | m³/hr | HVAC design |
| 20 | **Fire Integrity Boundary (A-class division)** | $60$ min | — | SOLAS Ch. II-2 |

**Critical Substitution Rule (HVAC):**

$$Q_{total} = N_{ac} \times V_{room} \quad \left[ \text{m}^3 / \text{hr} \right]$$

Where $V_{room}$ is the room volume in cubic metres, and $N_{ac}$ is the air changes per hour.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipyards, these standards are not academic exercises — they form the **statutory survey basis**. A vessel failing MLC 2006 compliance can be **detained at any port worldwide** by Port State Control (Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU). For shipowners, this translates to **direct commercial loss**. For naval architects, these formulas govern the **preliminary General Arrangement (GA) drawing** and directly influence the **hull form** (length-to-beam ratio must accommodate cabin blocks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Total Accommodation Block Area (TABA)

**Problem Context:** A 30,000 DWT bulk carrier has a crew of **25 persons** (5 officers, 20 ratings). Determine the **minimum total cabin area** required, assuming single-occupancy cabins for officers and double-occupancy for ratings.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing per-person areas.**

From the KTU Formula Sheet:
- Officer single cabin: $A_{off} = 7.5 \text{ m}^2$
- Rating shared cabin (max 2): $A_{sh} = 3.75 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}$

**Step 2 — Compute total officer area.**

$$A_{O,total} = N_{off} \times A_{off}$$

Substituting $N_{off} = 5$:

$$A_{O,total} = 5 \times 7.5 = 37.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute total rating area (using double occupancy).**

The number of cabins required for 20 ratings at 2 per cabin is:

$$N_{cab,rat} = \frac{N_{rat}}{2} = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ cabins}$$

Total rating cabin area:

$$A_{R,total} = N_{cab,rat} \times 2 \times A_{sh}$$

$$A_{R,total} = 10 \times 2 \times 3.75 = 75.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Sum to find Total Accommodation Block Area (TABA).**

$$TABA_{cabins} = A_{O,total} + A_{R,total}$$

$$TABA_{cabins} = 37.5 + 75.0 = 112.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Add 25% circulation and sanitary allowance.**

In practice, corridors, vestibules, bathrooms, and duct trunks add **20 to 30 %** to the raw cabin area. Taking the **25%** benchmark:

$$TABA_{gross} = TABA_{cabins} \times 1.25 = 112.5 \times 1.25 = 140.625 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check with empirical shipbuilding rule.**

Empirical rule: A typical **TABA of 5.5 to 6.5 m² per crew member** is expected on a modern bulk carrier.

$$TABA_{rule} = 25 \times 6.0 = 150 \text{ m}^2$$

**Verification:**

$$140.625 \text{ m}^2 \leq 150 \text{ m}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ (within empirical range)}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum cabin and circulation envelope is **140.6 m²**, consistent with industry empirical practice. This block is then placed amidships above the double bottom and below the boat deck.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Ventilation Air Quantity for a Galley

**Problem Context:** A ship's galley has dimensions: **Length $L = 8$ m, Breadth $B = 4$ m, Height $H = 2.3$ m**. Calculate the **total ventilation airflow** required, given the MLC/Class recommended air change rate.

**Step 1 — Compute room volume.**

$$V_{gal} = L \times B \times H$$

$$V_{gal} = 8 \times 4 \times 2.3 = 73.6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Identify the air change rate.**

From the KTU Formula Sheet, the galley range is $N_{gal} = 15$ to $20$ changes/hr. We adopt the **conservative design value of 20**:

$$N_{gal} = 20 \text{ changes/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total airflow.**

$$Q_{total} = N_{gal} \times V_{gal}$$

$$Q_{total} = 20 \times 73.6 = 1472 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard ventilation units.**

$$Q_{total} = \frac{1472}{60} = 24.53 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \approx 0.41 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using duct velocity constraint.**

Assuming a single main supply duct of cross-section $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m} = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$:

$$v_{duct} = \frac{Q_{total}}{A_{duct}} = \frac{0.41}{0.25} = 1.64 \text{ m/s}$$

This is well below the **10 m/s** smoke-control limit. **Two ducts** would halve the velocity, ensuring silent operation.

**Conclusion:** The galley requires **1472 m³/hr of fresh air**, satisfied typically by a dedicated **galley exhaust fan + supply fan pair** with grease filters and a fire damper.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Acoustic Treatment for the Wheelhouse

**Problem Context:** The engine room produces **90 dB(A)**. A single steel bulkhead of **8 mm** separates the ECR from a cabin. The required cabin noise level is **55 dB(A)**. Determine the **insertion loss required** and the **transmission loss** of the bare steel bulkhead.

**Step 1 — Required Insertion Loss (IL).**

$$IL_{req} = L_{source} - L_{target}$$

$$IL_{req} = 90 - 55 = 35 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 2 — Mass Law Transmission Loss for bare steel.**

The **mass law** (simplified) for a single partition is:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m \times f) - 47 \quad [\text{dB}]$$

Where $m$ is the surface density in $\text{kg/m}^2$ and $f$ is the frequency in Hz.

For 8 mm steel: $\rho_{steel} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$m = \rho \times t = 7850 \times 0.008 = 62.8 \text{ kg/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute TL at 500 Hz (dominant speech/audible band).**

$$TL_{bare} = 20 \log_{10}(62.8 \times 500) - 47$$

$$TL_{bare} = 20 \log_{10}(31400) - 47$$

$$TL_{bare} = 20 \times 4.497 - 47 = 89.94 - 47 = 42.94 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with requirement.**

$$TL_{bare} = 42.94 \text{ dB} > IL_{req} = 35 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The bare 8 mm steel bulkhead provides **42.9 dB** of transmission loss, exceeding the **35 dB** required. However, **flanking paths** (doors, cable penetrations, ventilation ducts) typically reduce the field performance by 5 to 10 dB, so **acoustic doors and resilient mountings** are still mandated.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Space-by-Space Compliance Table

| Space | Min. Headroom (m) | Min. Area (m²) | Air Changes (/hr) | Noise (dB(A)) | Illum. (lux) | Key Adjacency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Captain's Cabin | 2.03 | 12.0 | 4-6 | ≤ 55 | 150 | Adjacent to bridge |
| Officer Cabin | 2.03 | 7.5 | 4-6 | ≤ 55 | 150 | Officer alleyway |
| Rating Cabin | 2.03 | 4.5 | 4-6 | ≤ 55 | 100 | Rating alleyway |
| Mess Room | 2.03 | 1.0/seat | 6-8 | ≤ 55 | 200 | Galley, lounge |
| Galley | 2.03 | (functional) | 15-20 | ≤ 70 | 300 | Mess room |
| Hospital | 2.30 | 4.0 | 6-8 | ≤ 55 | 200 | Aft, low traffic |
| Laundry | 2.03 | (functional) | 10-15 | ≤ 70 | 200 | Provisions area |
| Wheelhouse | 2.30 | (functional) | 6-10 | ≤ 65 | 300 | Top forward |
| ECR | 2.30 | (functional) | 8-12 | ≤ 65 | 300 | Adjacent E/R |
| Battery Room | 2.03 | (functional) | 10-15 | — | 100 | Divided from cabins |
| Paint Locker | 2.03 | (functional) | 10 | — | 100 | **Outside accommodation block** |
| Workshop | 2.03 | (functional) | 6-8 | ≤ 75 | 200 | Near E/R |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Accommodation Spatial Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional and regulatory hierarchy of all accommodation spaces on a typical ocean-going vessel. It shows how safety, habitability, and operational workflows interconnect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vessel Hull Envelope] --> B1[Forward Zone]
    A0 --> B2[Amidship Zone]
    A0 --> B3[Aft Zone]

    B1 --> C1A[Navigation Block]
    C1A --> D1A[Wheelhouse]
    C1A --> D1B[Chart Room]
    C1A --> D1C[Radio Room]

    B2 --> C2A[Crew Living Block]
    C2A --> D2A[Officer Cabins]
    C2A --> D2B[Junior Officer Cabins]
    C2A --> D2C[Rating Cabins]
    C2A --> D2D[Officer Mess]
    C2A --> D2E[Rating Mess]
    C2A --> D2F[Recreation Room]
    C2A --> D2G[Sanitary Block]
    C2A --> D2H[Laundry]

    B2 --> C2B[Service Block]
    C2B --> D2I[Galley]
    C2B --> D2J[Pantry]
    C2B --> D2K[Provision Stores]
    C2B --> D2L[Refrigerated Rooms]
    C2B --> D2M[Hospital]

    B3 --> C3A[Engineering Block]
    C3A --> D3A[Engine Control Room]
    C3A --> D3B[Workshop]
    C3A --> D3C[Machinery Spaces]
    C3A --> D3D[Shaft Alley]

    C2A --> E1[HVAC Overlay]
    C2A --> E2[Fire Subdivision A60]
    C2A --> E3[Escape Routes Overlay]
    C2A --> E4[Noise Vibration Damping]
    C2A --> E5[Lighting and Power]
    C2A --> E6[MLC 2006 Compliance]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Crew Space Allocation Logic

The following flowchart captures the **decision sequence** a naval architect follows from initial crew count to final space allocation, exactly as it would be performed during the preliminary General Arrangement (GA) design phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Start: Vessel Type and Trade Route] --> F1[Determine Minimum Safe Manning]
    F1 --> F2[Segregate by Rank: Officer vs Rating]
    F2 --> F3[Apply MLC Minimum Areas per Rank]
    F3 --> F4[Decide Single vs Shared Occupancy]
    F4 --> F5[Compute TABA Cabin Block]
    F5 --> F6[Add 25 percent Circulation Allowance]
    F6 --> F7[Allocate Mess, Galley, Hospital, Recreation]
    F7 --> F8[Place Navigation Block Forward]
    F8 --> F9[Place Engineering Block Aft]
    F9 --> F10[Position Crew Block Amidships]
    F10 --> F11[Overlay HVAC Ducting]
    F11 --> F12[Overlay Fire Subdivision A60]
    F12 --> F13[Verify Headroom 2.03 m Minimum]
    F13 --> F14[Run Noise and Vibration Analysis]
    F14 --> F15[Check Escape Route Distances]
    F15 --> F16[Issue Class Approved GA Drawing]
```

## 4.3 Spatial Adjacency Matrix (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram illustrates the **mandatory and forbidden adjacencies** in accommodation planning, a key KTU 2024 exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Wheelhouse] -->|MANDATORY| G2[Chart Room]
    G1 -->|MANDATORY| G3[Captain Cabin]
    G2 -->|FORBIDDEN| G4[Engine Room]
    G1 -->|FORBIDDEN| G5[Galley]
    G6[Galley] -->|MANDATORY| G7[Mess Room]
    G6 -->|MANDATORY| G8[Provision Room]
    G6 -->|FORBIDDEN| G9[Crew Cabins Direct]
    G10[Crew Cabins] -->|MANDATORY| G11[Sanitary Block]
    G10 -->|MANDATORY| G12[Corridors]
    G13[Hospital] -->|MANDATORY| G14[Outside Noise Zone]
    G13 -->|FORBIDDEN| G15[Engine Room Direct]
    G16[Paint Locker] -->|FORBIDDEN| G17[Accommodation Block]
    G16 -->|MANDATORY| G18[Dedicated Ventilation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A solid arrow with "MANDATORY" means these spaces **must be adjacent** for workflow or safety. A solid arrow with "FORBIDDEN" means these spaces **must be physically separated**, usually by an A-60 fire boundary and sometimes by cofferdams, to prevent fire, smoke, noise, or contamination transfer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define "Accommodation Spaces" as per MLC 2006 and list any four examples. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Accommodation spaces, as defined under **Regulation 3 of Title 3 of the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006**, are those spaces on a ship used for the **crews living, working, and recreational purposes**, including sleeping rooms, mess rooms, sanitary facilities, the galley, hospital, recreation rooms, and offices.

**Four examples:**
1. Single-occupancy officer cabin
2. Crew mess room
3. Hospital / sick bay
4. Galley and pantry complex

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *accommodation spaces* with *accommodation block*. The **accommodation block** is the architectural envelope; the **accommodation spaces** are the individual compartments within. Marks are lost for this distinction.

---

### Question 2: State the minimum headroom requirement for general crew spaces and the minimum illuminance for mess rooms. **[3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

1. The **minimum headroom** in general crew accommodation spaces (cabins, mess, recreation) is **2.03 m (6 ft 8 in)** as per **ILO Convention 133** and MLC 2006.
2. The **minimum illuminance** in mess rooms and recreation areas is **200 lux**, as per **IEC 92-101 / Class Rules**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing "2 meters" without specifying **2.03 m** is a common error costing 0.5 marks. Always state the precise regulatory figure.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**Design the accommodation block of a 25,000 DWT product carrier with a crew of 22 persons (6 officers and 16 ratings). State the governing regulations, compute the total minimum cabin area, and explain the placement philosophy with a neat sketch description.**

#### (a) Governing Regulations and Design Standards [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The design of the accommodation block shall comply with the following regulations, in order of precedence:

1. **SOLAS Consolidated Edition 2020** — Chapter II-2 (Fire Protection) and Chapter III (Life-Saving Appliances).
2. **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006** — Title 3, Regulation 3.2 (Sleeping Rooms), 3.3 (Mess Rooms), 3.4 (Recreation), 3.5 (Sanitary).
3. **ILO Convention No. 133** — Accommodation of Crews (Supplementary Provisions).
4. **Classification Society Rules** — Indian Register of Shipping (IRS) or equivalent, providing structural fire integrity (A-60 divisions), escape route geometry, and HVAC duct specifications.
5. **Flag State Administration Rules** — e.g., DG Shipping India for Indian-flag vessels.

**Key minimum headroom:** **2.03 m** general, **2.30 m** for wheelhouse/hospital.

**Key minimum floor areas:**
- Officer single cabin: **7.5 m²**
- Rating shared cabin (2 persons): **3.75 m²/person**, i.e., **7.5 m² per cabin**

**Fire Integrity:** The accommodation block must be bounded by **A-60 class divisions** where it adjoins machinery spaces, service spaces, or control stations.

**Escape:** Maximum travel distance from any cabin to a muster station or embarkation deck shall not exceed **45 m**, with at least two independent escape routes.

**HVAC:** Galley 15-20 ACH; mess and recreation 6-8 ACH; cabins 4-6 ACH.

**Noise:** Cabins and mess ≤ **55 dB(A)**; wheelhouse and ECR ≤ **65 dB(A)**.

#### (b) Total Cabin Area Calculation and Placement Philosophy [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Officer cabin area (single occupancy).**

$$A_{O} = 6 \times 7.5 = 45.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Rating cabin area (double occupancy).**

Number of cabins required: $N_{cab,R} = 16 / 2 = 8$ cabins

$$A_{R} = 8 \times 2 \times 3.75 = 60.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Net cabin block area.**

$$A_{net} = A_{O} + A_{R} = 45.0 + 60.0 = 105.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Gross accommodation block (with 30% circulation/sanitary allowance).**

$$A_{gross} = 105.0 \times 1.30 = 136.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Placement Philosophy (Sketch Description).**

The accommodation block is positioned on the **deckhouse superstructure amidships**, in the following vertical and longitudinal arrangement:

- **Topmost deck (Bridge Deck):** Wheelhouse, chart room, captain's cabin.
- **Deck below (A Deck / Accommodation Deck 2):** Officer cabins, officer mess, recreation room, hospital.
- **Next deck (B Deck / Accommodation Deck 1):** Rating cabins (2 per cabin), rating mess, galley, pantry, provision rooms.
- **Lowest accommodation deck:** Laundry, sanitary blocks, changing rooms, storerooms.

**Longitudinal:** The block is centered **amidships** to minimize pitch and roll accelerations, with stairway towers at both fore and aft ends providing two independent escape routes to the boat deck. The galley is positioned **adjacent to the mess room** to enable efficient food service, and **vertically above the dry provision room** to enable gravity-fed stock movement. The hospital is placed **portside, low-traffic, with direct access to a dedicated escape route**, but **never directly above or below the engine room** to avoid noise, vibration, and oil contamination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to convert cabin area to **gross accommodation block area** by adding the circulation factor. Marks are specifically allocated for the 25-30% circulation allowance and the **amidships placement justification**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Explain in detail the categories of spaces within a ships accommodation block. For each category, state the governing regulation, the minimum headroom, the recommended illuminance level, and the minimum air change rate. Draw a block diagram of the spatial hierarchy.**

#### (a) Categories of Accommodation Spaces with Standards [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The accommodation block is divided into **four functional categories**, each with specific statutory standards:

**Category 1 — Sleeping Spaces (Cabins)**

- **Governing regulation:** MLC 2006 Reg. 3.2; ILO 133.
- **Minimum headroom:** 2.03 m.
- **Illuminance:** 100–150 lux at desk level.
- **Air change rate:** 4–6 changes/hour.
- **Sub-types:** Single-occupancy officer cabin (≥ 7.5 m²), single-occupancy rating cabin (≥ 4.5 m²), shared rating cabin (max 2 persons, ≥ 3.75 m²/person).

**Category 2 — Messing and Recreational Spaces**

- **Governing regulation:** MLC 2006 Reg. 3.3 (Mess), Reg. 3.4 (Recreation).
- **Minimum headroom:** 2.03 m.
- **Illuminance:** 200–300 lux.
- **Air change rate:** 6–8 changes/hour.
- **Sub-types:** Officer mess room, rating mess room, lounge, smoking room (ventilated separately), TV room, gym.

**Category 3 — Service Spaces**

- **Governing regulation:** MLC 2006 Reg. 3.6 (Galley), Reg. 3.5 (Hospital).
- **Minimum headroom:** 2.03 m (galley), 2.30 m (hospital).
- **Illuminance:** 300 lux (galley, food prep), 200 lux (hospital).
- **Air change rate:** 15–20 changes/hour (galley), 6–8 (hospital), 10–15 (laundry).
- **Sub-types:** Galley, pantry, provision rooms (dry, chilled, frozen), hospital, laundry, iron room.

**Category 4 — Navigation and Control Spaces (Operative Spaces)**

- **Governing regulation:** SOLAS Ch. V (Bridge), Class Rules.
- **Minimum headroom:** 2.30 m.
- **Illuminance:** 300 lux (general), with dimmable red lighting for night operations.
- **Air change rate:** 6–10 changes/hour.
- **Sub-types:** Wheelhouse, chart room, ECR, cargo control room.

#### (b) Block Diagram of Spatial Hierarchy and Ventilation Logic [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A **vertical hierarchy** from top to bottom of a typical product carrier superstructure is presented below in tabular block form (the visual sketch is supplemented by the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1).

| Level | Deck Name | Spaces |
|---|---|---|
| Level 5 | Compass Deck / Bridge Deck | Wheelhouse, chart room, captain's day cabin |
| Level 4 | A Deck (Upper Accommodation) | Officer cabins, officer mess, recreation, hospital |
| Level 3 | B Deck (Main Accommodation) | Rating cabins, rating mess, galley, pantry |
| Level 2 | C Deck (Lower Accommodation) | Laundry, sanitary, changing rooms, storerooms |
| Level 1 | Tank Top | (Reserved for machinery, segregated) |

**Ventilation Logic (Air Flow Architecture):**

The HVAC system supplies **100 % fresh air** to accommodation (no recirculation for hygiene), ducted from intakes on the **forward superstructure** (clean side, forward of the funnel) and exhausted via **aft-facing cowls** to ensure cross-flow and prevent exhaust re-ingestion. The supply ducts run fore-to-aft within the ceiling void; return air grilles are positioned low on the corridor bulkheads to capture heat and odours. The galley, hospital, and sanitary blocks have **independent extract fans** to prevent cross-contamination of air streams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often claim accommodation uses "recirculated air" like in building HVAC — **this is false and loses marks**. Marine accommodation requires **100% fresh air supply** under MLC Reg 3.7, primarily to prevent Legionella and respiratory illness outbreaks during long voyages.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section for the night before the exam.**

- **MLC 2006 Title 3** is the **master regulation** for accommodation. Memorize it.
- **Minimum headroom: 2.03 m** (general), **2.30 m** (wheelhouse, hospital).
- **Officer cabin minimum area: 7.5 m² (single)**.
- **Rating cabin minimum area: 4.5 m² (single)** or **3.75 m²/person (shared, max 2)**.
- **Mess room: 1.0 m²/seat** minimum; **Hospital: 4.0 m²** for vessels > 500 GT with crew > 15.
- **100% fresh air supply** is mandatory — no recirculation in accommodation spaces.
- **Air change rates:** Cabins 4-6, Mess 6-8, Galley 15-20, Laundry 10-15, Hospital 6-8 (all in changes/hr).
- **Noise limits:** ≤ 55 dB(A) in cabins and mess, ≤ 65 dB(A) in wheelhouse/ECR, ≤ 75 dB(A) in workshops.
- **Illuminance:** Cabins 100-150 lux, Mess/Rec 200-300 lux, Engine Room 200-300 lux, Corridors 50-100 lux.
- **Crew block placement: AMIDSHIPS**, lowest motion zone, to reduce seasickness and improve sleep.
- **Galley MUST be adjacent to the mess room**, and vertically above provision stores.
- **Wheelhouse MUST be at the forward-most, highest position** for clear sightlines (SOLAS Ch. V).
- **Hazardous spaces** (paint locker, CO₂ room, battery room) are **OUTSIDE the accommodation block**.
- **A-60 fire integrity** required between accommodation and machinery spaces.
- **At least TWO independent escape routes** from every accommodation level, max travel **45 m** to muster station.
- **Captain's cabin** is typically **adjacent to the bridge** for rapid emergency response.
- **Hospital is NEVER placed directly above or below the engine room** (noise and contamination).
- **Door widths:** minimum **600 mm** for cabins, **800 mm** for mess and recreation, **900 mm** for escape routes.
- **Headroom is measured** to the lowest overhead obstruction (e.g., beams, duct soffits).
- **Bilge keels and double bottom** do NOT house accommodation; accommodation sits in the superstructure or the deckhouse.
- **The painting of the accommodation block** in GA drawings is conventionally in **light tones** (cream, light grey) to enhance the perception of space.
- **Ventilation intakes** are placed **forward of the funnel** to avoid smoke ingestion (TOXIC and a common cause of IMO detentions).
- **Cubic capacity per crew member** is also regulated: typically **7 to 9 m³/person** in addition to area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For a 14-mark question, the answer must contain (1) at least one regulatory citation, (2) a numerical calculation, (3) a justification of placement, and (4) either a diagram or a tabular hierarchy. Marks are distributed roughly as **3 + 4 + 4 + 3 = 14**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tanks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Tanks in Marine Vessels — Core Foundations

## Formal Definition
In marine vessel architecture, **tanks** are integral, fully enclosed structural compartments integrated into the hull and superstructure, engineered to store, transfer, or process fluids essential for the vessel's operation, stability, safety, and commercial function. According to **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** and **MARPOL (Marine Pollution) conventions**, tanks are classified as any enclosed space specifically designed and constructed to contain liquids such as sea water, fresh water, fuel oil, lubricating oil, sludge, bilge water, or cargo liquids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Tanks are studied as a primary subdivision of the hull. Their arrangement, capacity, structural design, sounding, and integration with the vessel's longitudinal strength directly influence **intact stability**, **damage stability**, **trim**, and **operational endurance** of the vessel.

## Intuitive Overview — The "Real World" Analogy
Imagine a large commercial building with several built-in water reservoirs on different floors:
- The **roof tank** stores fresh water for daily use.
- The **underground tank** collects rainwater and sewage.
- The **fire-water tank** holds pressurized water for emergencies.
- Each tank has **pipes going in** (filling), **pipes going out** (discharging), **vents on top** (air exchange), and **gauge openings** (level measurement).

A ship works exactly the same way, but instead of being stacked vertically, the tanks are arranged **lengthwise and breadthwise** along the hull. They are bounded by watertight or oiltight bulkheads, decks, and the hull plating. The "roof" of a ship's tank is the **deck above**, the "walls" are the **bulkheads and side shell plating**, and the "floor" is the **inner bottom plating or tank top**.

> [!NOTE]
> A ship is essentially a **floating assembly of interconnected tanks** — the spaces that are NOT tanks (corridors, engine rooms, cabins) are surrounded by tanks. The engineering design of these tanks determines the vessel's seaworthiness.

## Fundamental Classification of Tanks

| Category | Purpose | Example |
|----------|---------|---------|
| **Ballast Tanks** | Maintain stability, draft, and trim | Forepeak, side, double-bottom |
| **Fuel Tanks** | Store propulsion fuel | Bunker tanks, service tanks |
| **Fresh Water Tanks** | Potable water storage | Domestic water tanks |
| **Cargo Tanks** | Liquid cargo transport | Oil tanker cargo holds |
| **Service Tanks** | Daily operational fluids | Lub oil, feed water |
| **Sludge/Bilge Tanks** | Waste collection | Bilge wells, sludge tanks |
| **Cofferdams** | Safety isolation spaces | Between fuel and fresh water |

## Standard Units and Metrics in Tank Design
- **Volume**: Cubic meters (m³) or metric tons (MT), with 1 m³ of sea water ≈ **1.025 t** and 1 m³ of fresh water ≈ **1.000 t**.
- **Fill Level**: Expressed in percentage (%) or sounding height (mm).
- **Structural Thickness**: Millimeters (mm), with tank boundary plating typically ranging from **6 mm to 25 mm** depending on location.
- **Pressure Head**: Expressed in meters of water column (mWC) or kilopascals (kPa).
- **Density Constants (Mandatory for Calculations)**:
  - Sea water density: $\rho_{sw} = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$
  - Fresh water density: $\rho_{fw} = 1.000 \text{ t/m}^3$
  - Fuel oil density (HFO): $\rho_{fo} \approx 0.94 \text{ t/m}^3$
  - Diesel oil density: $\rho_{do} \approx 0.86 \text{ t/m}^3$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis — Tank Design Principles

## 2.1 The Four Functional Pillars of Tank Engineering

Every tank on a marine vessel must satisfy **four simultaneous engineering requirements**:

### Pillar 1 — Hydrostatic Containment
The tank must resist the **static pressure head** of the liquid it contains without permanent deformation or rupture. For a tank filled to depth $h$, the pressure at the bottom equals:
$$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$
where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. This pressure load governs the **minimum plating thickness** per classification society rules (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS).

### Pillar 2 — Boundary Integrity (Watertightness / Oiltightness)
The tank boundary — comprising **plating, bulkheads, and penetrations** — must prevent leakage. Per **SOLAS Chapter II-1**, any tank forming part of the vessel's subdivision must maintain:
- **Watertight integrity** for sea-water boundary tanks.
- **Oiltight integrity** for oil-fuel boundary tanks (per **MARPOL Annex I, Regulation 14**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why oiltight ≠ watertight**: Oiltight boundaries use gaskets, sealants, and welded joints because oil has lower viscosity and surface tension than water, allowing it to seep through hairline cracks that would hold water.

### Pillar 3 — Sounding and Level Management
Every tank must have a **reliable measurement system** to determine the volume of liquid inside at any moment. This includes:
- **Sounding pipes** (manual dip measurements)
- **Float-level sensors** (automatic, electrical)
- **Capacitive/ultrasonic sensors** (modern, electronic)
- **Pressure transducers** at tank bottom (hydrostatic)

### Pillar 4 — Structural Continuity
The tank's boundaries (bulkheads, tank top, inner bottom) form part of the **ship's primary hull girder**. Cutting openings or reducing thickness in these areas reduces the vessel's **section modulus** and bending strength. Engineers must therefore compensate by **ring stiffening, bracket reinforcement, or girder insertion**.

## 2.2 The Eight Types of Ballast Tanks (Detailed Taxonomy)

Ballast tanks are arguably the most structurally critical tanks because they are integral to **safe navigation, stability, and pollution prevention**.

| # | Tank Type | Location | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Forepeak Tank (FPT)** | Forward of collision bulkhead | Trim adjustment, anchor chain support |
| 2 | **Aft Peak Tank (APT)** | Aft of engine room, forward of rudder | Propeller immersion, stern trim |
| 3 | **Double Bottom Tank (DBT)** | Between inner bottom and shell bottom | Main ballast capacity |
| 4 | **Wing Ballast Tank (WBT)** | Side of cargo hold, port & starboard | Stability during partial load |
| 5 | **Topside Tank** | Outboard of cargo space, above tank top | Stability at high drafts |
| 6 | **Hopper Tank** | Sloped portion above double bottom | Cargo wash drainage, sloshing reduction |
| 7 | **Segregated Ballast Tank (SBT)** | Dedicated ballast, isolated from cargo | Crude oil tanker compliance (MARPOL) |
| 8 | **Cofferdam** | Empty narrow space between two tanks | Thermal/safety isolation |

## 2.3 Sounding Systems — The "Eyes" of the Tank

A sounding system allows the crew to know **how much liquid** is in the tank. There are three principal methods:

### Method 1: Manual Sounding (Sounding Pipe + Dip Tape)
- A vertical pipe of **40–50 mm diameter** runs from the tank top to within **25–50 mm of the bottom**.
- A calibrated **brass dip tape** with a weighted bob is lowered; the wetted length is read against a **calibration table** to determine volume.
- **Advantages**: Zero power dependency, absolute reliability.
- **Disadvantages**: Labour-intensive, no continuous reading.

### Method 2: Float-Operated Mechanical Gauge
- A **float** rides on the liquid surface, connected via a pivot arm to an **external pointer** on the tank-top gauge board.
- Reads **directly in volume (m³) or percentage**.
- **Used in**: Most bunker, fresh water, and lubricating oil tanks.

### Method 3: Electronic Sensors
- **Capacitive probes**, **ultrasonic transducers**, or **pressure transmitters** give continuous analog/digital readings to the **Integrated Automation System (IAS)**.
- **Used in**: Modern chemical tankers, LNG carriers, UMS-class vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why sounding pipes don't reach the bottom completely**: If the pipe touched the bottom, sludge, sediment, or scale would block it. Keeping the pipe 25–50 mm above the bottom ensures **clear reading** and **free drainage**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Symbol Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | $P$: pressure, $\rho$: density, $g$: 9.81 m/s², $h$: liquid depth | Bottom plate stress calculation |
| $V_{tank} = L \times B \times H$ | $L$: length, $B$: breadth, $H$: height of tank | Rectangular tank volume (basic) |
| $M = \rho \cdot V$ | $M$: mass, $\rho$: density, $V$: volume | Mass of liquid for stability |
| $t_{min} = \dfrac{s \cdot \sqrt{p}}{k}$ | $s$: spacing, $p$: pressure, $k$: class factor | Min. plate thickness per class rules |
| $FS_{ullage} = H_{tank} - h_{ullage}$ | $FS$: free surface, $H$: tank depth, $h_{ullage}$ | Free surface effect for stability |
| $I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | $I_T$: transverse moment of inertia, $L$, $B$: tank dimensions | Free surface moment (transverse) |
| $I_L = \dfrac{B \cdot L^3}{12}$ | $I_L$: longitudinal moment of inertia | Free surface moment (longitudinal) |
| $GG' = \dfrac{\rho \cdot I_T}{\Delta}$ | $GG'$: virtual rise of G, $I_T$: FS moment, $\Delta$: displacement | Free surface correction to stability |
| $h_{sounding} = H_{tank} - h_{ullage}$ | $h_{sounding}$: dip reading, $h_{ullage}$: empty space | Sounding tape reading |
| $V_{cal} = V_{total} \times \dfrac{h_{sounding}}{H_{tank}}$ | $V_{cal}$: calibration volume | Linear interpolation for non-prismatic tanks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip**: The free surface moment of inertia $I$ is computed about the **tank's centerline axis parallel to the free surface**, NOT about the vessel's centerline. This is a common student error costing 2–3 marks per KTU paper.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
Tank design sits at the intersection of:
- **Naval Architecture** (stability, trim, structural strength)
- **Marine Engineering** (pumping systems, valves, sensors)
- **Chemical Engineering** (corrosion, material compatibility for chemical tankers)
- **Environmental Engineering** (MARPOL compliance, oil spill prevention)
- **Operational Logistics** (range, endurance, port turnaround time)

A bulk carrier with poorly designed ballast tanks can suffer **sloshing-induced structural failure**; a tanker without cofferdams risks **catastrophic oil contamination** of fresh water; a naval vessel without proper fuel tank segregation cannot achieve its **mission endurance**.
