| 5 | Displacement (numerical integration — Simpson's 1st rule) | $V = \int_{-L/2}^{+L/2} A(x) \, dx \approx \dfrac{h}{3}\bigl[f_0 + f_n + 4\!\sum f_{\text{odd}} + 2\!\sum f_{\text{even}}\bigr]$ | $h$ = common spacing, $A(x)$ = sectional area |
| 6 | Sectional Area (from offsets) | $A(x) = \sum_{j=0}^{m} \left( y_{j+1} - y_{j-1} \right) \cdot \dfrac{z_{j+1}-z_{j-1}}{2}$ | Trapezoidal rule in body plan |
| 7 | Fairing — Slope Continuity | $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\Big\vert_{i-} = \dfrac{dy}{dx}\Big\vert_{i+}$ | C¹ continuity at station $i$ |
| 8 | Fairing — Curvature Continuity | $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}\Big\vert_{i-} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}\Big\vert_{i+}$ | C² continuity (used in modern fairing) |
| 9 | Newton's Forward Interpolation (for half-breadth) | $y(x) = y_0 + p\,\Delta y_0 + \dfrac{p(p-1)}{2!}\Delta^2 y_0 + \cdots$ | $p = \dfrac{x - x_0}{h}$, used in KTU problems |
| 10 | Lagrange Interpolation (general points) | $y(x) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} y_i \prod_{\substack{j=0\\j\neq i}}^{n} \dfrac{x - x_j}{x_i - x_j}$ | Use for **non-uniform spacing** of stations |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Lofting (Full-Scale Layout):** The lines plan was historically used to *"loft"* the full-size hull on the mold loft floor (often a 1:1 plywood or concrete surface) for template-making.
- **Classification Society Approval:** Bodies like **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping), Lloyd's Register, and DNV** still require a **Table of Offsets** and Lines Plan for every new vessel.
- **Hydrostatic & Stability Software:** Software like *Maxsurf*, *RHINO*, and *AVEVA* ultimately outputs offsets in the *conventional table format* as a final deliverable to the yard.
- **Stability Booklet Generation:** All **GZ curves**, **KM**, **KB** values are computed by integrating sectional areas derived from the *same* Table of Offsets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Converting a Station Number to a Physical X-Coordinate

> **Given:** A vessel of **LBP = 120 m** with **21 stations** numbered 0 to 20 equally spaced.
> **Find:** The longitudinal X-coordinate of **Station 7** measured from the **Aft Perpendicular (AP)**.

**Step 1 — Establish the station spacing formula.**
By KTU convention, the spacing between adjacent stations is:

$$
h = \frac{L_{BP}}{n - 1}
$$

where $n$ is the total number of stations. *(Note: Some texts use $n$ for the *number of intervals*; KTU exam questions often say "21 ordinates" meaning 21 stations.)*

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
h = \frac{120 \text{ m}}{21 - 1} = \frac{120}{20} = 6.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the X-coordinate of Station 7.**

$$
x_7 = 7 \cdot h = 7 \times 6.0 \text{ m} = 42.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for the answer.**
Station 7 is located **42 m forward of the AP**, which places it in the **forward half** of the ship (since amidships is at station 10 → 60 m).

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Newton's Forward Interpolation of Half-Breadth

> **Given:** At three consecutive stations of a hull, the **half-breadths at the design waterline (WL 5)** are:
> - Station 4 → $y_0 = 5.20$ m
> - Station 5 → $y_1 = 5.95$ m
> - Station 6 → $y_2 = 6.40$ m
> Station spacing is **6.0 m** (longitudinal).
>
> **Find:** The half-breadth at the *intermediate* position **2.0 m forward of Station 4**.

**Step 1 — Build the forward difference table.**

$$
\Delta y_0 = y_1 - y_0 = 5.95 - 5.20 = 0.75 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta y_1 = y_2 - y_1 = 6.40 - 5.95 = 0.45 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta^2 y_0 = \Delta y_1 - \Delta y_0 = 0.45 - 0.75 = -0.30 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fractional index $p$.**

$$
p = \frac{x - x_0}{h} = \frac{2.0}{6.0} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Forward Interpolation Formula.**

$$
y(x) = y_0 + p \cdot \Delta y_0 + \frac{p(p-1)}{2!}\cdot \Delta^2 y_0
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
y(x) = 5.20 + \left(\tfrac{1}{3}\right)(0.75) + \frac{\tfrac{1}{3}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{3}-1\right)}{2}\cdot(-0.30)
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each term carefully.**

$$
y(x) = 5.20 + 0.25 + \frac{\tfrac{1}{3}\!\left(-\tfrac{2}{3}\right)}{2}\cdot(-0.30)
$$

$$
y(x) = 5.20 + 0.25 + \left(\frac{-2/9}{2}\right)\cdot(-0.30)
$$

$$
y(x) = 5.20 + 0.25 + \left(-\tfrac{1}{9}\right)\cdot(-0.30)
$$

$$
y(x) = 5.20 + 0.25 + 0.03333\ldots
$$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{y(2.0 \text{ m from Stn 4}) = 5.4833 \text{ m} \approx 5.48 \text{ m}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Sectional Area by Trapezoidal Rule (Body Plan Offsets)

> **Given:** At **Station 8** of a hull, the half-breadths (in meters) measured at the following waterlines (heights above baseline) are:
>
> | WL | Height Z (m) | Half-Breadth y (m) |
> | :--- | :---: | :---: |
> | WL 0 (Baseline) | 0.0 | 0.00 |
> | WL 1 | 1.5 | 1.80 |
> | WL 2 | 3.0 | 3.20 |
> | WL 3 (DWL) | 4.5 | 3.80 |
> | WL 4 | 6.0 | 3.60 |
> | WL 5 (Sheer) | 7.5 | 2.40 |
>
> **Find:** The **underwater sectional area** at Station 8 (assume WL 3 is the design waterline; only WL 0 → WL 3 contribute).

**Step 1 — Recall the trapezoidal rule for sectional area (underwater, using half-breadths).**

$$
A = 2 \int_0^{T} y(z)\, dz \;\;\approx\;\; 2 \cdot \left[ \tfrac{h_z}{2}\left(y_0 + 2\sum_{j=1}^{m-1} y_j + y_m\right) \right]
$$

where $h_z$ is the vertical spacing between waterlines and the factor 2 accounts for port + starboard (since the body plan shows only half-breadth).

**Step 2 — Identify spacing and indices.**

For WL 0 → WL 3 (the underwater portion), we use $m = 3$, $h_z = 1.5$ m, and the half-breadths $y_0=0.00$, $y_1=1.80$, $y_2=3.20$, $y_3=3.80$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the trapezoidal formula.**

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ \frac{1.5}{2}\left(0.00 + 2(1.80) + 2(3.20) + 3.80\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify inside the brackets.**

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ 0.75 \times \left(0.00 + 3.60 + 6.40 + 3.80\right) \right]
$$

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ 0.75 \times 13.80 \right]
$$

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ 10.35 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer.**

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{Stn 8}} = 20.70 \text{ m}^2}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Disadvantages of the Conventional Method

| Aspect | Conventional Method of Lines | Modern (CAD/NURBS) Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Storage** | Numerical table (Table of Offsets) | 3D point cloud / surface patches |
| **Accuracy** | Limited to station/WL/BL density | Effectively continuous |
| **Fairing Method** | Manual (splines) or numerical | Algorithmic (C² curvature plots) |
| **Modification Flexibility** | Requires re-lofting the table | Local control points can be edited |
| **Computational Effort** | High for hydrostatic calculation | Built-in parametric evaluation |
| **Pedagogical Value (KTU)** | **High** — emphasizes hull geometry fundamentals | **Low** — hides the geometry behind the GUI |
| **Industry Status** | Still used as **final deliverable format** | Used as **creation/iteration tool** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Conventional Lines Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the interactions of the *three views*, the *Table of Offsets*, and the downstream calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hull Form Concept 3D Mental Model] --> B[Define Principal Dimensions LBP B T]
    B --> C[Select Reference Planes WL BL ST]
    C --> D1[Draw Sheer Plan Side View]
    C --> D2[Draw Half-Breadth Plan Top View]
    C --> D3[Draw Body Plan Cross Section View]
    D1 --> E[Fairing Process Splines or Numerical C2 Check]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Generate Table of Offsets Numerical Data]
    F --> G[Hydrostatic Calculations KB BM TPC MCTC]
    F --> H[Stability Cross Curves GZ KN]
    F --> I[Resistance and Power EHP SHP]
    F --> J[Classification Society Submission IRS LRS DNV]
    G --> K[Ship Particulars Booklet]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> L[Vessel Built at Shipyard]
    K --> L
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — The Three-View Geometric Relationship

The diagram below illustrates how the *same single point* on the hull appears simultaneously in the Sheer Plan, Half-Breadth Plan, and Body Plan. (Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SP[Sheer Plan Side View]
        nodeP1[Point P on Waterline W3]
    end
    subgraph HB[Half-Breadth Plan Top View]
        nodeP2[Point P on Waterline W3]
    end
    subgraph BP[Body Plan Cross View]
        nodeP3[Point P at Station 8]
    end
    nodeP1 --- nodeP2
    nodeP2 --- nodeP3
    nodeP3 --- nodeP1
    nodeT[Table of Offsets Station 8 WL3 Y Z] --- nodeP1
    nodeT --- nodeP2
    nodeT --- nodeP3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In the Sheer Plan you read **Z (height) and X (length)**. In the Half-Breadth Plan you read **Y (half-breadth) and X (length)**. In the Body Plan you read **Y and Z**. Together, the three views give the complete **(X, Y, Z)** coordinate of every line of the hull.

### 4.3 Schematic — Station Numbering Convention (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Station 0 AP] --> A2[Station 2]
    A2 --> A5[Station 5]
    A5 --> A10[Station 10 Amidships CL]
    A10 --> A15[Station 15]
    A15 --> A18[Station 18]
    A18 --> A20[Station 20 FP]
    A0 --> B0[Baseline 0 m]
    B0 --> B1[WL1]
    B1 --> B2[WL2]
    B2 --> B3[WL3 DWL]
    B3 --> B4[WL4]
    B4 --> B5[WL5 Sheer]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention check (commonly asked in KTU vivas):**
> - **Station 0** = Aft Perpendicular (AP).
> - **Station 20** = Forward Perpendicular (FP). [for 20-station hull]
> - **Station 10** = Midship section.
> - **WL 3** = Design Waterline (DWL) in many KTU textbooks.
> - **WL 5** = Sheer Line (top of freeboard deck).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q1. Define the "Conventional Method of Lines Design" of a ship's hull. Mention the three reference planes used in this method.**

**Model Answer:**
The Conventional Method of Lines Design is a traditional naval-architecture technique in which a ship's 3D hull form is defined graphically and numerically by plotting the intersection of the hull with three families of mutually perpendicular reference planes. The three reference planes are:

1. **Waterline Planes (WL):** Horizontal planes parallel to the design waterline — produce waterline curves.
2. **Buttock Planes (BL):** Vertical longitudinal planes parallel to the centerline — produce buttock curves.
3. **Station Planes (ST):** Vertical transverse planes perpendicular to the centerline — produce station (section) curves.

These intersections are plotted in the *Sheer Plan*, *Half-Breadth Plan*, and *Body Plan* to fully describe the hull. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for the three planes + 1 for any one plan mention.)*

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q2. What is a "Table of Offsets"? Why is it the single most important numerical deliverable of the lines design process?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Table of Offsets** is a structured numerical dataset in which, for each combination of a station number and a waterline number, the **half-breadth (Y)** and the **height (Z)** of the hull are tabulated. It is the single most important deliverable because:

- It is the *complete numerical description* of the hull form, suitable for direct use by shipyard, classification society, and stability software.
- All downstream engineering quantities — displacement, KB, BM, TPC, MCTC, and cross-curves of stability — are derived by integrating the sectional areas built from the offsets.
- It is the **industry-standard input** for resistance, powering, and seakeeping software. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for integration usage + 1 for industry-standard role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Paper Module 3]`**

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches, explain the three principal views of a *Lines Plan*. State what each view shows and how the three views are correlated through the Table of Offsets.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A Lines Plan is the traditional 2D drawing of a ship's hull form generated by intersecting the hull surface with three sets of reference planes.

**Sheer Plan / Profile (2 marks):** It is a side elevation of the ship showing the **waterlines** (as smooth horizontal curves) and the **buttocks** (as smooth vertical curves). The baseline, design waterline, and sheer line are clearly visible. It provides the **X (length)** and **Z (height)** coordinates.

**Half-Breadth Plan (2 marks):** It is a top view of the hull showing the **waterlines** (as horizontal curves) and the **stations** (as transverse curves). Only one side of the hull is drawn (port half or starboard half) because the hull is symmetric about the centerline. It provides the **X and Y** (half-breadth) coordinates.

**Body Plan (2 marks):** It is an end view showing the **stations** (as closed U/V-shaped curves) and the **waterlines** (as horizontal lines). Forward stations are drawn on the right of the centerline; aft stations on the left. It provides the **Y and Z** coordinates.

**Correlation via Table of Offsets (1 mark — see sketch in Section 4.2):**
The three views are tied together by the Table of Offsets. A single point P on the hull at Station 8 / WL 3 has:
- $(X, Z)$ in the Sheer Plan,
- $(X, Y)$ in the Half-Breadth Plan,
- $(Y, Z)$ in the Body Plan,
and the cell $\left[\text{Station 8, WL 3}\right]$ in the Table of Offsets contains the **Y and Z** values uniquely.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vessel of LBP = 100 m is offset with 21 equally spaced stations. Using **Newton's Forward Interpolation formula**, determine the **half-breadth at the design waterline** at a point **3.0 m forward of Station 6**, given the following WL 3 (DWL) half-breadths:

- Station 6: $y_0 = 4.40$ m
- Station 7: $y_1 = 5.10$ m
- Station 8: $y_2 = 5.65$ m

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Station spacing (1 mark):**

$$
h = \frac{L_{BP}}{n - 1} = \frac{100}{20} = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the forward difference table (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta y_0 = 5.10 - 4.40 = 0.70 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta y_1 = 5.65 - 5.10 = 0.55 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta^2 y_0 = 0.55 - 0.70 = -0.15 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parameter $p$ (1 mark):**

$$
p = \frac{x - x_0}{h} = \frac{3.0}{5.0} = 0.6
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's forward interpolation (2 marks):**

$$
y(x) = y_0 + p\cdot\Delta y_0 + \frac{p(p-1)}{2!}\cdot \Delta^2 y_0
$$

$$
y = 4.40 + (0.6)(0.70) + \frac{0.6(0.6-1)}{2}\cdot(-0.15)
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical evaluation (1 mark):**

$$
y = 4.40 + 0.42 + \frac{0.6 \times (-0.4)}{2}\cdot(-0.15)
$$

$$
y = 4.40 + 0.42 + \frac{-0.24}{2}\cdot(-0.15)
$$

$$
y = 4.40 + 0.42 + (-0.12)\cdot(-0.15)
$$

$$
\boxed{y = 4.40 + 0.42 + 0.018 = 4.838 \text{ m} \approx 4.84 \text{ m}}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **fairing process** in the conventional method of lines design. What are the **continuity conditions (C¹ and C²)** and why are they important?

**Model Answer:**

**Fairing Definition (1 mark):** Fairing is the iterative process of smoothing the lines of a hull so that the surface is free of bumps, hollows, and discontinuities. A *fair* line has uniform second-derivative (curvature).

**Historical Method (2 marks):** Traditionally, draftsmen used a long, thin flexible strip of wood or plastic called a *spline*, held in place with weighted lead pieces called *"ducks"*. The spline was incrementally adjusted to pass through offset points while remaining tangent-smooth. Modern software (e.g., *Maxsurf*, *Rhino*) replaces this with **C¹ and C² continuity plots**.

**C¹ Continuity — Slope Continuity (2 marks):** The first derivative of the line (slope) must be **equal from the left and from the right** at every offset point:

$$
\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{i^-} = \left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{i^+}
$$

This prevents *kinks* in the curve. A hull with a C¹-only condition is geometrically valid but may have visible slope changes.

**C² Continuity — Curvature Continuity (2 marks):** The second derivative (curvature) must also be continuous:

$$
\left.\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\right|_{i^-} = \left.\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}\right|_{i^+}
$$

This is the **industrial standard** for a fair hull. A C²-continuous hull has a smooth *rate of change of slope*, which improves both aesthetics and resistance performance (less wave-making drag from form irregularities).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** At **Station 12** of a ship's hull, the half-breadths (in m) at the underwater waterlines are: WL 0 → 0.00, WL 1 → 1.20, WL 2 → 2.40, WL 3 → 3.10. The vertical spacing between consecutive waterlines is **1.5 m**. Compute the **underwater sectional area** at Station 12 using the **trapezoidal rule**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify data (1 mark):**
$m = 3$ (intervals), $h_z = 1.5$ m, $y_0=0.00$, $y_1=1.20$, $y_2=2.40$, $y_3=3.10$.

**Step 2 — Trapezoidal formula for sectional area (1 mark):**

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ \tfrac{h_z}{2}\left(y_0 + 2y_1 + 2y_2 + y_3\right) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (2 marks):**

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ \tfrac{1.5}{2}\left(0.00 + 2(1.20) + 2(2.40) + 3.10\right) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify inside brackets (2 marks):**

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ 0.75 \times \left(0.00 + 2.40 + 4.80 + 3.10\right) \right]
$$

$$
A = 2 \cdot \left[ 0.75 \times 10.30 \right] = 2 \cdot 7.725
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{A_{\text{Stn 12}} = 15.45 \text{ m}^{2}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the sectional-area formula:** Many students use *only* the half-breadth values without doubling for the port + starboard halves. **Always multiply by 2** unless explicitly told that offsets are *full-breadths*.
> 2. **Station-numbering confusion:** KTU questions frequently use **21 stations numbered 0–20**. Using $n=21$ in $h = L_{BP}/n$ (instead of $L_{BP}/(n-1)$) is a common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Sign errors in Newton's interpolation $p(p-1)$ term:** The second-difference term is **negative** when $\Delta^2 y < 0$. Read the formula exactly as $\frac{p(p-1)}{2!}$, **not** $\frac{p(p+1)}{2!}$ (that is the backward form).
> 4. **Mixing up the three views:** Students sometimes write "Half-Breadth Plan shows buttocks" — *wrong*. It shows **waterlines and stations**. Memorize: Sheer → WL + BL, Half-Breadth → WL + ST, Body → ST + WL/BL.
> 5. **Unit omission:** KTU mandates SI units throughout. A correct numerical answer **without the unit (m or m²)** loses **0.5 to 1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this before the exam)**

- [ ] The **Conventional Method of Lines** uses three reference plane families: **Waterlines (horizontal), Buttocks (longitudinal vertical), Stations (transverse vertical)**.
- [ ] The three views are **Sheer Plan** (side), **Half-Breadth Plan** (top), and **Body Plan** (cross-section).
- [ ] **Station 0 = Aft Perpendicular (AP)**; **Station 20 (last) = Forward Perpendicular (FP)** in the 21-station KTU convention.
- [ ] **Station spacing** for $n$ stations is $h = L_{BP} / (n-1)$ when stations are indexed 0 to $n-1$.
- [ ] The **Table of Offsets** stores $(X, Y, Z)$ for every (Station, Waterline) intersection — it is the **numerical heart** of the design.
- [ ] **Fairing** requires **C¹** (slope) and ideally **C²** (curvature) continuity at every offset point.
- [ ] **Sectional area** is computed from the *underwater half-breadths* using trapezoidal / Simpson's rule, **multiplied by 2** to account for the missing half.
- [ ] **Newton's Forward Interpolation** uses $p = (x - x_0)/h$ and the forward-difference table $\Delta y_0, \Delta^2 y_0, \ldots$
- [ ] **Lagrange Interpolation** is used when stations are **not uniformly spaced**.
- [ ] **Displacement, KB, BM, TPC, MCTC** and **cross-curves of stability** are *all* derived by integrating offsets.
- [ ] The conventional method is still the **industry's final deliverable format**; CAD/NURBS is a *creation tool*, not a replacement.
- [ ] **Common KTU exam trap:** The factor of **2** in the sectional-area formula and the **denominator $(n-1)$** in the station spacing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lines Design Using Existing Lines Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lines Design Using Existing Lines Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Lines Design Using Existing Lines Plan** is a practical hull form development methodology in naval architecture wherein a new vessel's hull form is derived by selecting a previously built, sea-tested ship (referred to as the **parent ship** or **mother ship**) as a baseline, and then systematically modifying its offset table and lines plan to satisfy the new design's principal particulars, form coefficients, and operational requirements. This approach is the **industry-preferred method** for the preliminary and contract design stages because it leverages empirical hydrodynamic performance data, accumulated shipyard production experience, and proven structural arrangements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic directly addresses Module 3 outcome *"Apply the concept of lines design using existing lines plan to develop a new hull form"* and is recurrently tested as a Part B (14-mark) question in KTU End Semester Examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Genetic Engineering" of Hulls

Imagine you have a beautifully crafted, time-tested family **recipe** (the parent ship's lines plan) that produces a cake everyone loves. Instead of inventing a brand-new cake recipe from scratch (which could fail), you **tweak the existing recipe** — slightly more flour, a touch less sugar, a different frosting — to create a new cake tailored to a customer's preferences. The cake's "DNA" (proportions, fairness, hydrodynamic balance) remains, but specific characteristics are modified.

In the same way, a ship designer does not reinvent a hull from a blank canvas. They inherit the **genetic blueprint** of a successful ship and **amend specific genes** (length, beam, draft, block coefficient, bow shape) to produce a new variant that meets fresh operational demands. This dramatically reduces the **design risk**, **development cost**, and **time-to-delivery**.

## 1.3 Why This Method Dominates Real Shipyards

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Justifications for Using an Existing Lines Plan**
> 1. **Empirical Validation:** The parent ship has proven seakeeping, resistance, and propulsion characteristics at sea.
> 2. **Reduced Hydrodynamic Risk:** Wave-making resistance, squat, and course-keeping behavior are extrapolated with confidence.
> 3. **Production Familiarity:** Shipyard labor and tooling are already adapted to similar hull geometries.
> 4. **Cost & Schedule Economy:** Eliminates expensive model towing-tank iterations at the preliminary stage.
> 5. **Class Society Acceptance:** Classification societies (IRS, ABS, DNV, LR) readily accept derivative designs from approved parent forms.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators in Bold

The fundamental design metrics a naval architect manipulates during lines design are:

- **Principal Dimensions:** **Length Overall (LOA)**, **Length Between Perpendiculars (LBP)**, **Beam (B)**, **Design Draft (T)**, **Depth (D)**.
- **Form Coefficients:** **Block Coefficient (Cb)**, **Prismatic Coefficient (Cp)**, **Midship Coefficient (Cm)**, **Waterplane Area Coefficient (Cw)**.
- **Displacement Volume:** **∇** (cubic meters).
- **Offsets:** Tabulated coordinates (x, y, z) defining the hull surface at stations, waterlines, and buttocks.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** The lines plan is essentially a 3D wireframe model expressed in tabular numerical form — the **offset table**. Modifying the lines plan means modifying these offset coordinates systematically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Methodology

The complete procedure of lines design using an existing lines plan can be decomposed into **seven logical phases**:

### Phase 1 — Selection of the Parent Ship
- Identify existing vessels in the shipyard's portfolio, in classification society records, or in published reference databases (e.g., *Jane's Fighting Ships*, *Lloyd's Register*) whose **mission profile**, **tonnage range**, and **speed regime** closely match the new requirement.
- Selection criteria: similarity of **Cb**, **L/B ratio**, **B/T ratio**, **service category** (tanker, bulker, container, naval).

### Phase 2 — Acquisition and Analysis of Parent Offset Table
- Obtain the **parent offset table** containing coordinates of the hull at discrete stations (typically at every 1/20 of LBP), waterlines, and buttocks.
- Compute the parent ship's **hydrostatic particulars**: ∇, KB, BM, TPC, MCTC, WPA.

### Phase 3 — Target Principal Dimensions and Coefficients
- The owner/operator specifies: required **Deadweight (DWT)**, **cargo capacity**, **service speed (Vs)**, **route draft restrictions**.
- Using the **Deadweight Equation**, principal dimensions are iteratively converged.

### Phase 4 — Choice of Transformation Method
- Decide how to mathematically map the parent form to the new form. Common methods:
  - **(a) Direct Affine Transformation** — uniform or non-uniform scaling along principal axes.
  - **(b) Coefficient-Driven Modification** — target specific Cb, Cp, Cm.
  - **(c) Parametric Interpolation** — blend two parent forms.
  - **(d) Localized Geometric Modification** — append/modify bow bulb, transom, stern profile.

### Phase 5 — Application of Transformations
- Apply the chosen transformation to every offset coordinate in the parent table.
- Generate the new offset table.

### Phase 6 — Fairing and Hydrostatic Verification
- Visually fair the new lines using spline interpolation.
- Compute new hydrostatic properties; iterate until they match the target.

### Phase 7 — Stability and Resistance Validation
- Run **GM** calculations, **GZ curve** generation.
- Conduct **CFD / towing-tank** validation if required.

## 2.2 The Affine Transformation — Mathematical Foundation

The simplest and most widely used transformation is the **3D Affine Transformation**, which scales the parent hull along the three principal axes:

$$x_{\text{new}} = k_x \cdot x_{\text{old}}$$

$$y_{\text{new}} = k_y \cdot y_{\text{old}}$$

$$z_{\text{new}} = k_z \cdot z_{\text{old}}$$

where:
- $k_x = L_{\text{new}} / L_{\text{old}}$
- $k_y = B_{\text{new}} / B_{\text{old}}$
- $k_z = T_{\text{new}} / T_{\text{old}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Affine Alone is Insufficient:** Pure affine scaling preserves the form coefficient (Cb) exactly. To deliberately change Cb (e.g., make the new ship fuller or finer), one must use **non-uniform z-modification** or **interpolation** methods.

## 2.3 Modification of Block Coefficient (Cb) — The "Stretch-Squeeze" Method

When the new design requires a different block coefficient, the half-breadths (y) and/or heights (z) are scaled by a **Cb-modifier factor**:

$$k_{\text{cb}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}}$$

For a **uniform volume change** at the same principal dimensions:

$$y_{\text{new}} = y_{\text{old}} \cdot k_{\text{cb}} \quad ; \quad z_{\text{new}} = z_{\text{old}} \cdot k_{\text{cb}}$$

This gives:

$$\frac{\nabla_{\text{new}}}{\nabla_{\text{old}}} = k_{\text{cb}}^3 = \frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}$$

## 2.4 Two-Form Interpolation Formula (Family Design)

When blending two parent forms (Form A and Form B) with interpolation parameter $t \in [0,1]$:

$$P_{\text{new}} = (1 - t) \cdot P_A + t \cdot P_B$$

For **bilinear interpolation** on a 2D grid (e.g., across stations and waterlines):

$$
P_{\text{new}}(s, t) = (1-s)(1-t) P_{00} + s(1-t) P_{10} + (1-s)t P_{01} + s \cdot t \cdot P_{11}
$$

where $s$ and $t$ are normalized parameters along the two grid directions.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize the following table verbatim. These are the equations examiners expect to see in Part B solutions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient (volumetric fullness) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_p = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L}$ | Prismatic coefficient (longitudinal fullness) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_m = \dfrac{A_m}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship section coefficient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_w = \dfrac{A_w}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane area coefficient | dimensionless |
| 5 | $TPC = \dfrac{A_w}{100} \cdot \rho$ | Tonnes Per Centimeter immersion | t/cm |
| 6 | $k_x = L_{\text{new}} / L_{\text{old}}$ | Affine length scale factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $k_y = B_{\text{new}} / B_{\text{old}}$ | Affine beam scale factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $k_z = T_{\text{new}} / T_{\text{old}}$ | Affine draft scale factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k_{cb} = \sqrt[3]{C_{b,\text{new}} / C_{b,\text{old}}}$ | Cb-modifier cube root | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\nabla_{\text{new}} = C_{b,\text{new}} \cdot L_{\text{new}} \cdot B_{\text{new}} \cdot T_{\text{new}}$ | New displacement volume | m³ |
| 11 | $P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$ | Linear interpolation between forms | — |
| 12 | $DWT = \Delta_{\text{lightship}} - \nabla \cdot \rho$ | Deadweight (Displacement Form) | tonnes |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

This design philosophy is used universally:

- **Commercial Shipping:** Maersk's Triple-E class was derived by affine extension of earlier Emma Maersk class.
- **Naval Architecture:** Indian Navy frigates (Project 17A) are derivative of Project 17 (Shivalik class).
- **Yacht Design:** Benetti, Azimut use family-platform lines to spawn multiple length variants.
- **Fishing Vessels:** ICAR (India) standardized designs are replicated with regional modifications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Affine Transformation Preserves Block Coefficient

**Statement:** Prove that a 3D affine transformation (uniform or non-uniform) along the principal axes preserves the block coefficient $C_b$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Volume in Offset Form:**
The displacement volume is computed by integrating the cross-sectional areas along the length:

$$\nabla = \int_0^L A(x) \, dx$$

where $A(x) = \int_{-y(x)}^{+y(x)} \int_{0}^{z(x,y)} dz \, dy = 2 \int_{0}^{z(x,0)} y(x,z) \, dz$

**Step 2 — Apply Affine Transformations:**
Under scaling, each offset transforms as:

$$x' = k_x x \, ; \quad y' = k_y y \, ; \quad z' = k_z z$$

The new cross-sectional area becomes:

$$A'(x') = \int_{-y'(x')}^{y'(x')} z'(x', y') \, dy' = k_y k_z \int_{-y(x)}^{y(x)} z(x,y) \, dy = k_y k_z A(x)$$

**Step 3 — New Displacement Volume:**

$$\nabla' = \int_0^{L'} A'(x') \, dx' = \int_0^{L} (k_y k_z) A(x) \cdot (k_x \, dx) = k_x k_y k_z \cdot \nabla$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Block Coefficient Expression:**

$$C_b' = \frac{\nabla'}{L' B' T'} = \frac{k_x k_y k_z \cdot \nabla}{(k_x L)(k_y B)(k_z T)} = \frac{k_x k_y k_z \cdot \nabla}{k_x k_y k_z \cdot LBT} = C_b$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \, C_b' = C_b}$$

> **Interpretation:** Pure affine scaling is **Cb-preserving**. To intentionally change Cb, one must deviate from a pure affine scheme — typically by adding a localized Cb-modifier.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cb Modification Through Cubic Root Scaling

**Statement:** Derive the relationship $k_{cb} = (C_{b,\text{new}} / C_{b,\text{old}})^{1/3}$ for changing the block coefficient while keeping principal dimensions constant.

**Step 1 — Fixed Principal Dimensions:** $L, B, T$ remain constant, so $LBT$ is invariant.

**Step 2 — Displacement Ratio from Cb Definition:**

$$\frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}} = \frac{\nabla_{\text{new}} / (LBT)}{\nabla_{\text{old}} / (LBT)} = \frac{\nabla_{\text{new}}}{\nabla_{\text{old}}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Uniform Cubic Scaling to All y and z Offsets:**

$$\nabla_{\text{new}} = \int_0^L \int k_{cb} y(x,z) \cdot k_{cb} z(x,z) \, dy \, dz = k_{cb}^2 \cdot k_{cb} \int y \cdot z \, dy dz = k_{cb}^3 \cdot \nabla_{\text{old}}$$

(Here the integration introduces the third factor $k_{cb}$ from the volume integral in 3D, even though $k_{cb}$ is applied only to y and z.)

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions:**

$$\frac{\nabla_{\text{new}}}{\nabla_{\text{old}}} = k_{cb}^3 = \frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Modifier:**

$$k_{cb} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}}$$

$$\boxed{k_{cb} = \left(\frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}\right)^{1/3}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bilinear Interpolation on Offset Grid

**Statement:** Show how to compute a new offset point from four corner offsets in a parametric grid.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Parametric Grid:**
Consider four parent offsets at corners of a rectangular patch:
- $P_{00}$ at $(x_0, z_0)$ (lower-aft)
- $P_{10}$ at $(x_1, z_0)$ (lower-forward)
- $P_{01}$ at $(x_0, z_1)$ (upper-aft)
- $P_{11}$ at $(x_1, z_1)$ (upper-forward)

**Step 2 — Define Normalized Parameters:**

$$s = \frac{x - x_0}{x_1 - x_0} \quad ; \quad t = \frac{z - z_0}{z_1 - z_0}$$

with $s, t \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 3 — Apply Bilinear Interpolation:**

First, interpolate along $x$ at the two waterlines:

$$P_{A} = (1-s) P_{00} + s P_{10}$$
$$P_{B} = (1-s) P_{01} + s P_{11}$$

Then interpolate along $z$ between the two results:

$$P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$$

**Step 4 — Expand the Composite Expression:**

$$P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) \left[ (1-s) P_{00} + s P_{10} \right] + t \left[ (1-s) P_{01} + s P_{11} \right]$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{new}} = (1-s)(1-t) P_{00} + s(1-t) P_{10} + (1-s)t P_{01} + s \cdot t \cdot P_{11}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A shipyard has an existing bulk carrier with the following particulars:
- $L_{\text{old}} = 150$ m, $B_{\text{old}} = 23$ m, $T_{\text{old}} = 9.5$ m, $C_{b,\text{old}} = 0.78$.

A new bulk carrier is to be designed with:
- $L_{\text{new}} = 180$ m, $B_{\text{new}} = 28$ m, $T_{\text{new}} = 11.0$ m, $C_{b,\text{new}} = 0.82$.

At station $x = 0.4 L_{\text{old}}$ (60 m from AP), the parent offset is $y_{\text{old}} = 10.5$ m, $z_{\text{old}} = 6.2$ m. Compute the new offset $y_{\text{new}}, z_{\text{new}}$.

**Step 1 — Compute Affine Scale Factors:**

$$k_x = \frac{180}{150} = 1.20 \quad ; \quad k_y = \frac{28}{23} = 1.2174 \quad ; \quad k_z = \frac{11.0}{9.5} = 1.1579$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cb Modifier:**

$$k_{cb} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{0.82}{0.78}} = \sqrt[3]{1.05128} = 1.01682$$

**Step 3 — Compute Effective y, z Scale Factors:**

$$k_{y,\text{eff}} = k_y \cdot k_{cb} = 1.2174 \times 1.01682 = 1.2379$$

$$k_{z,\text{eff}} = k_z \cdot k_{cb} = 1.1579 \times 1.01682 = 1.1774$$

**Step 4 — Compute New Offsets:**

$$x_{\text{new}} = 1.20 \times 60 = 72.0 \text{ m (from AP of new ship)}$$

$$y_{\text{new}} = 1.2379 \times 10.5 = 13.00 \text{ m}$$

$$z_{\text{new}} = 1.1774 \times 6.2 = 7.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify New Displacement:**

$$\nabla_{\text{new}} = 0.82 \times 180 \times 28 \times 11.0 = 45\,475.2 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\nabla_{\text{old}} = 0.78 \times 150 \times 23 \times 9.5 = 25\,564.5 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\text{Ratio} = 1.7786 \quad ; \quad k_x k_y k_z \cdot k_{cb}^3 = 1.20 \times 1.2174 \times 1.1579 \times 1.0513 = 1.7787 \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Note:** The small discrepancy (1.7786 vs 1.7787) is due to rounding. The transformation is mathematically consistent.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Mathematical Tool | Risk Level |
|----------|--------------------|-------------------|------------|
| Same form, larger size | Pure Affine Transformation | $k_x, k_y, k_z$ scaling | Low |
| Same size, fuller form | Cb Modifier (Cubic Root) | $k_{cb} = (C_{b,\text{new}}/C_{b,\text{old}})^{1/3}$ | Low |
| Two parents to blend | Linear/Bilinear Interpolation | $P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$ | Medium |
| Add bow bulb to parent | Localized Surface Insertion | Geometric appending + fairing | Medium-High |
| Change stern shape | Localized Region Modification | Section-by-section rescaling | High |
| Family of variants | Parametric Platform Design | Master parametric equations | Low (once built) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lines Design Workflow

The diagram below maps the complete information and decision flow of the lines design process using an existing lines plan, from parent ship selection through final hull form validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Owner Specification] --> B1[Mission Profile DWT Vs Route]
    B1 --> B2[Target Principal Dimensions]
    B2 --> B3[Target Form Coefficients Cb Cp Cm Cw]
    B3 --> C1{Parent Ship Selection}
    C1 --> C2[Search Shipyard Database]
    C2 --> C3[Compare Mission and Form]
    C3 --> C4{Suitable Parent Found?}
    C4 -->|No| C5[Select Best Available Partial Match]
    C4 -->|Yes| D1[Acquire Parent Offset Table]
    C5 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Compute Parent Hydrostatics]
    D2 --> E1[Select Transformation Method]
    E1 --> E2[Method A Pure Affine Scaling]
    E1 --> E3[Method B Cb Modifier]
    E1 --> E4[Method C Two Form Interpolation]
    E1 --> E5[Method D Localized Region Modification]
    E2 --> F1[Generate New Offset Table]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    F1 --> F2[Visual Fairing Using Splines]
    F2 --> G1[Recompute New Hydrostatics]
    G1 --> H1{Targets Achieved?}
    H1 -->|No| H2[Adjust Transformation Parameters]
    H2 --> F1
    H1 -->|Yes| I1[Stability Check GM and GZ]
    I1 --> J1[Resistance and Powering Check]
    J1 --> K1[Class Society Approval]
    K1 --> L1[Finalized Lines Plan]
```

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation of Affine vs Cb Modifier

The figure below contrasts two strategies for transforming a parent hull section. **Node A** represents pure affine scaling (preserves Cb); **Node B** represents Cb-modified scaling (alters Cb).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parent Half Section y_old z_old] --> Q1{Affine Only?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[k_y y_old, k_z z_old]
    Q1 -->|No| R2[k_y k_cb y_old, k_z k_cb z_old]
    R1 --> S1[Section Preserves Cb_original]
    R2 --> S2[Section Has Cb_modified]
    S1 --> T1[New Hull Form]
    S2 --> T1
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Within Offset Table Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Parent Station i] --> U1[Read x_i, y_ij, z_ij]
    U1 --> V1[Apply k_x to x]
    U1 --> V2[Apply k_y k_cb to y]
    U1 --> V3[Apply k_z k_cb to z]
    V1 --> W1[New Station i Coordinates]
    V2 --> W1
    V3 --> W1
    W1 --> X1[Store in New Offset Table]
    X1 --> Y1[Loop Over All Stations]
    Y1 --> Z1[Loop Over All Waterlines]
    Z1 --> AA1[Output Final Offset Table]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** In KTU examinations, students are often expected to draw or describe the **block diagram of the transformation process**. The block diagrams above provide the exact mental template expected by the examiner.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Define "Lines Design Using Existing Lines Plan" and state any **two** advantages of this method over designing a hull from first principles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Lines design using an existing lines plan is a hull form development methodology in which a new vessel's hull form is derived by selecting an existing sea-tested ship (parent ship) and systematically modifying its offset table to meet the new design's principal dimensions, form coefficients, and operational requirements.

**Any Two Advantages (1.5 marks, 0.75 each):**
1. The parent ship has **proven hydrodynamic performance**, eliminating the need for costly trial-and-error model testing at the preliminary stage.
2. **Reduced design risk and development time**, since accumulated shipyard experience with the parent form can be directly leveraged.
3. Lower engineering cost — the parent form's structural, hydrodynamic, and seakeeping characteristics are well-documented.
4. Easier approval from classification societies due to the derivative design lineage.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is a "parent ship"? Explain the role of the form coefficient $C_b$ in lines design using an existing lines plan.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Parent Ship (1 mark):** A parent ship (or mother ship) is an existing, sea-tested vessel whose lines plan serves as the **baseline geometry** from which a new hull form is mathematically or graphically derived.

**Role of Cb (2 marks):** The block coefficient $C_b = \nabla / (LBT)$ quantifies the **volumetric fullness** of the hull. In lines design:
- It is the **primary design driver** linking displacement to principal dimensions.
- A change in $C_b$ requires the application of the **Cb-modifier factor** $k_{cb} = (C_{b,\text{new}} / C_{b,\text{old}})^{1/3}$ to the half-breadth and height offsets.
- It directly influences **resistance, propulsion efficiency, and cargo capacity** of the new vessel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Affine Transformation and Cb Modification**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 3, 14 Marks]**

A shipyard has an existing Panamax bulk carrier (parent ship) with principal particulars:
- $L_{\text{old}} = 200$ m, $B_{\text{old}} = 32.2$ m, $T_{\text{old}} = 12.0$ m
- $C_{b,\text{old}} = 0.79$, $\nabla_{\text{old}} = 61\,200$ m³

A new Capesize bulk carrier is to be designed with:
- $L_{\text{new}} = 250$ m, $B_{\text{new}} = 43.0$ m, $T_{\text{new}} = 14.5$ m
- $C_{b,\text{new}} = 0.83$

**(a) Compute the affine scale factors and the Cb-modifier factor. (7 marks)**

**(b) At station $x = 0.5 L_{\text{old}}$ of the parent ship, the half-breadth offset is $y_{\text{old}} = 15.8$ m at waterline $z_{\text{old}} = 8.0$ m. Determine the new offset $(y_{\text{new}}, z_{\text{new}})$ in the new ship. Also compute the new displacement volume and verify consistency. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Scale Factors and Cb Modifier (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute affine scale factors.

$$k_x = \frac{L_{\text{new}}}{L_{\text{old}}} = \frac{250}{200} = 1.250$$

$$k_y = \frac{B_{\text{new}}}{B_{\text{old}}} = \frac{43.0}{32.2} = 1.3354$$

$$k_z = \frac{T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} = \frac{14.5}{12.0} = 1.2083$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute Cb modifier.

$$k_{cb} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{b,\text{old}}}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{0.83}{0.79}} = \sqrt[3]{1.0506} = 1.01653$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Effective scale factors (combining affine and Cb).

$$k_{y,\text{eff}} = k_y \cdot k_{cb} = 1.3354 \times 1.01653 = 1.3575$$

$$k_{z,\text{eff}} = k_z \cdot k_{cb} = 1.2083 \times 1.01653 = 1.2283$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Physical interpretation.

- $k_{y,\text{eff}} = 1.3575$ means half-breadths are scaled by **~35.75%** to account for both beam increase and added fullness.
- $k_{z,\text{eff}} = 1.2283$ means vertical offsets are scaled by **~22.83%** to account for both draft increase and added fullness.
- $k_{x} = 1.250$ means the new ship is **25% longer** in the longitudinal direction (length is not affected by Cb modification).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Tabulate the four factors.

| Factor | Value | Meaning |
|--------|-------|---------|
| $k_x$ | 1.2500 | Length scaling |
| $k_y$ | 1.3354 | Beam scaling (affine) |
| $k_z$ | 1.2083 | Draft scaling (affine) |
| $k_{cb}$ | 1.0165 | Fullness modifier |
| $k_{y,\text{eff}}$ | 1.3575 | Combined beam + fullness |
| $k_{z,\text{eff}}$ | 1.2283 | Combined draft + fullness |

---

#### Part (b) — New Offset Computation and Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute the new x-position.

$$x_{\text{new}} = k_x \cdot x_{\text{old}} = 1.250 \times 0.5 \times 200 = 125.0 \text{ m from AP}$$

(Equivalently, station 0.5 in the parent becomes station $0.5 \times k_x \cdot L_{\text{old}} / L_{\text{new}} = 0.5 \times 200/250 = 0.4$ in the new ship — this is the standard LBP-normalized station.)

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute new half-breadth and height.

$$y_{\text{new}} = k_{y,\text{eff}} \cdot y_{\text{old}} = 1.3575 \times 15.8 = 21.45 \text{ m}$$

$$z_{\text{new}} = k_{z,\text{eff}} \cdot z_{\text{old}} = 1.2283 \times 8.0 = 9.83 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute new displacement volume.

$$\nabla_{\text{new}} = C_{b,\text{new}} \cdot L_{\text{new}} \cdot B_{\text{new}} \cdot T_{\text{new}}$$

$$\nabla_{\text{new}} = 0.83 \times 250 \times 43.0 \times 14.5 = 129\,396.25 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Verify consistency via transformation.

$$\nabla_{\text{new, predicted}} = k_x k_y k_z \cdot k_{cb}^3 \cdot \nabla_{\text{old}}$$

$$= 1.250 \times 1.3354 \times 1.2083 \times 1.0506 \times 61\,200$$

$$= 2.1144 \times 61\,200 = 129\,401 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The match between **129,396 m³** and **129,401 m³** confirms the transformation is internally consistent (residual error ≈ 0.004% due to rounding).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to apply the Cb-modifier to **both y and z** offsets (1 mark lost per oversight).
> 2. Confusing **affine scale factor** ($k_y, k_z$) with the **effective** scale factor ($k_{y,\text{eff}}, k_{z,\text{eff}}$). Examiners strictly expect both.
> 3. Failing to **verify the displacement volume** at the end — this verification step is worth 1 mark and is often missed.
> 4. Reporting station position without specifying whether it is in the **parent's LBP frame** or the **new ship's LBP frame**.

---

### **Question B — Two-Form Interpolation Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Module 3, 14 Marks — INTERNAL CHOICE]**

A naval architect must design a new frigate by interpolating between two existing parent forms:

- **Parent A (Stealth Frigate):** $L_A = 120$ m, $B_A = 14$ m, $T_A = 4.2$ m, $C_{b,A} = 0.52$, $C_{p,A} = 0.66$
- **Parent B (Conventional Frigate):** $L_B = 125$ m, $B_B = 15$ m, $T_B = 4.5$ m, $C_{b,B} = 0.58$, $C_{p,B} = 0.71$

The interpolation parameter is $t = 0.35$ (closer to the stealth form).

**(a) Compute the principal dimensions and form coefficients of the new design. (7 marks)**

**(b) At a representative station, Parent A has offset $y_A = 6.2$ m, Parent B has $y_B = 6.8$ m. Compute the new offset using linear interpolation. Also briefly explain the physical significance of parameter $t$. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Principal Dimensions and Coefficients (7 marks)

**Step 1 (3.5 marks, 0.5 each):** Apply the linear interpolation formula $P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$ with $t = 0.35$:

$$L_{\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 120 + 0.35 \times 125 = 78.0 + 43.75 = 121.75 \text{ m}$$

$$B_{\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 14 + 0.35 \times 15 = 9.10 + 5.25 = 14.35 \text{ m}$$

$$T_{\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 4.2 + 0.35 \times 4.5 = 2.73 + 1.575 = 4.305 \text{ m}$$

$$C_{b,\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 0.52 + 0.35 \times 0.58 = 0.338 + 0.203 = 0.541$$

$$C_{p,\text{new}} = 0.65 \times 0.66 + 0.35 \times 0.71 = 0.429 + 0.2485 = 0.6775$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute midship coefficient.

$$C_{m,\text{new}} = \frac{C_{b,\text{new}}}{C_{p,\text{new}}} = \frac{0.541}{0.6775} = 0.7985$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Tabulate the new design's particulars.

| Parameter | Parent A | Parent B | New Design (t = 0.35) |
|-----------|----------|----------|-----------------------|
| L (m) | 120.0 | 125.0 | **121.75** |
| B (m) | 14.0 | 15.0 | **14.35** |
| T (m) | 4.2 | 4.5 | **4.305** |
| Cb | 0.52 | 0.58 | **0.541** |
| Cp | 0.66 | 0.71 | **0.6775** |
| Cm | 0.788 | 0.817 | **0.7985** |

---

#### Part (b) — Offset Interpolation and Physical Interpretation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the linear interpolation formula to the offset.

$$y_{\text{new}} = (1 - t) \cdot y_A + t \cdot y_B = 0.65 \times 6.2 + 0.35 \times 6.8$$

$$y_{\text{new}} = 4.03 + 2.38 = 6.41 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Cross-check.

$$y_{\text{new}} = 6.2 + 0.35 \times (6.8 - 6.2) = 6.2 + 0.21 = 6.41 \text{ m} \checkmark$$

**Step 3 (4 marks):** Physical significance of $t$:

- The interpolation parameter $t$ is a **dimensionless blending factor** that determines how much each parent form contributes to the new design.
- $t = 0$ produces Parent A exactly; $t = 1$ produces Parent B exactly.
- $t = 0.35$ (this problem) means the new design inherits **65% of its characteristics from Parent A** (stealth) and **35% from Parent B** (conventional).
- A lower $t$ favors stealth signatures (lower Cb, finer form); a higher $t$ favors seakeeping and payload capacity.
- The parameter $t$ must lie strictly within $[0, 1]$; values outside this range produce extrapolated, potentially unstable forms and are **not recommended**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Using the wrong formula direction — confusing $P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$ with the inverse (1 mark lost).
> 2. Forgetting to convert decimal coefficients to percent form when stating the answer (e.g., $C_b = 0.541$, **not** $54.1\%$).
> 3. Failing to comment on the **physical meaning of $t$** — this is worth 4 marks and is a compulsory KTU requirement.
> 4. Not tabulating the interpolated results — KTU examiners reward **clean tabulated output** with bonus clarity marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 3**

- **Definition (must be verbatim-ready):** Lines design using an existing lines plan is the modification of a **parent ship's offset table** to derive a new hull form, leveraging proven hydrodynamic performance.
- **Parent ship** = baseline vessel whose lines are reused (also called "mother ship").
- **Offset table** = tabulated (x, y, z) coordinates of hull surface at stations, waterlines, and buttocks.
- **Three core transformation methods:**
  1. **Affine Transformation** — preserves $C_b$ (use scale factors $k_x, k_y, k_z$).
  2. **Cb Modifier** — changes $C_b$ (use $k_{cb} = (C_{b,\text{new}}/C_{b,\text{old}})^{1/3}$).
  3. **Two-Form Interpolation** — blends two parents (use $P_{\text{new}} = (1-t) P_A + t P_B$).
- **Key formulas to memorize without error:**
  - $C_b = \nabla / (LBT)$
  - $C_p = \nabla / (A_m L)$
  - $C_m = C_b / C_p$
  - $k_{cb} = (C_{b,\text{new}}/C_{b,\text{old}})^{1/3}$
- **Effective scale factors** (for Cb change + affine change combined):
  - $k_{y,\text{eff}} = k_y \cdot k_{cb}$
  - $k_{z,\text{eff}} = k_z \cdot k_{cb}$
- **Verification step (always include in your answer):** Compute $\nabla_{\text{new}}$ two ways — once from the Cb formula and once from the transformation product — they must agree.
- **Bilinear interpolation formula (for 2D grids):**

$$P_{\text{new}}(s,t) = (1-s)(1-t) P_{00} + s(1-t) P_{10} + (1-s)t P_{01} + s \cdot t \cdot P_{11}$$

- **Common KTU pitfall to avoid:** Mixing up **affine scale factors** with **effective scale factors** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks per occurrence.
- **Real-world examples to cite in answers:** Maersk Triple-E (extended Emma Maersk), Project 17A Indian Navy frigates, Benetti family yachts.
- **Always convert units consistently:** lengths in meters, volumes in m³, coefficients dimensionless.
- **Always tabulate your final numerical results** — KTU examiners reward structured presentation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark Part B question, allocate ~3 marks for the conceptual introduction, ~8 marks for the step-by-step mathematical derivation, and ~3 marks for the verification, tabulation, and physical interpretation. This 3-8-3 distribution matches KTU's valuation rubric precisely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lines Design Using Standard Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lines Design Using Standard Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Standard Series** in marine vessel design refers to a set of systematically derived, statistically averaged, and experimentally validated parent hull forms whose geometric offsets (ordinates) are tabulated as functions of non-dimensional form coefficients. These series enable the naval architect to generate a complete set of ship lines for a new design by interpolating from a known parent form, eliminating the need to start hull form development from scratch.

A **standard series** is essentially a "design catalogue" of pre-tested hull geometries where:
- A base form (called the **parent form**) is chosen, typically corresponding to a specific **block coefficient (C_B)**.
- Offsets of the parent form at every station are non-dimensionalized by ship's principal dimensions.
- By varying C_B, Length-to-Beam ratio (L/B), and Beam-to-Draft ratio (B/T), families of related hull forms are generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Most Common Standard Series (KTU Board-Favorite Topics):**
> 1. **Taylor's Standard Series** (1933, David W. Taylor – USA) — Displacement hulls
> 2. **Series 60** (1960s, Davidson Laboratory – USA) — C_B = 0.60 parent form
> 3. **NPL Series** (1960s, National Physical Laboratory – UK) — Round bilge merchant forms
> 4. **BSRA Series** (1960s–70s, British Ship Research Association – UK) — Wider range, fuller forms
> 5. **Guldhammer Series** (1971) — Restricted water effects

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an architect designing a new house. Instead of drawing every wall, window, and pillar from imagination, you could pick a **"pre-designed standard template"** (say, a 3-bedroom house) and **adjust its length, width, and height** to meet your client's specific plot size. The proportions of rooms, the placement of doors, and the flow of the building remain "optimally tested" — only the **scale** changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **In ship design, the analogy translates as:**
> - **Pre-designed standard template** → Parent hull form (e.g., C_B = 0.60 Series 60)
> - **Adjusting length/width/height** → Varying L/B, B/T, and C_B
> - **Client's plot** → The required ship principal dimensions (L, B, T)
> - **Optimally tested proportions** → Resistance-tested, seakeeping-validated offsets

The brilliance lies in the fact that decades of **model testing and full-scale trials** have already optimized the parent form. The designer inherits this optimization "for free."

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Block Coefficient (C_B) Range** in standard series: **0.48 ≤ C_B ≤ 0.84**
- **L/B Ratio Range** in standard series: **5.5 to 7.5**
- **B/T Ratio Range** in standard series: **2.25 to 3.25**
- **Default Parent Form for Series 60**: **C_B = 0.60**, L/B = 6.5, B/T = 2.5
- **Froude Number (F_n)** range covered: **0.15 to 0.40** (typical merchant ship service speeds)
- **Displacement-Length Ratio (D/∇L³)** related parameter: **∇/L³ coefficient**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Standard series are **statistical averages** of many good designs — they do NOT represent any single existing ship. They are mathematical "averages" of proven geometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Standard Series Exist — The Design Problem

A naval architect faces the following challenge when designing a new ship:
1. Ship resistance and propulsion depend strongly on hull form (lines).
2. Hull form is described by **several hundred offsets** (x, y, z values at each station).
3. Manually designing every offset from scratch is time-consuming and unreliable.
4. Model testing each design is expensive.

**Solution:** Use **standard series** — pre-computed, model-tested hull forms — to jumpstart the design process.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Standard Series

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — Block Coefficient (C_B)** controls the fullness of the form.
> **Pillar 2 — Length-to-Beam Ratio (L/B)** controls slenderness and speed.
> **Pillar 3 — Beam-to-Draft Ratio (B/T)** controls the transverse section shape.

## 2.3 Major Form Coefficients Used in Standard Series

A standard series design uses the following fundamental geometric ratios:

### 2.3.1 Block Coefficient (C_B)

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

where ∇ is the displaced volume. C_B represents the **fineness of the underwater volume**.

### 2.3.2 Prismatic Coefficient (C_P)

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$

where A_M is the area of the midship section. C_P represents the **longitudinal distribution of volume** (how the volume is distributed fore-and-aft).

### 2.3.3 Midship Coefficient (C_M)

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

C_M represents the **fullness of the cross-section** at amidships.

### 2.3.4 Waterplane Coefficient (C_WP)

$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$$

where A_WP is the waterplane area. C_WP represents the **fullness of the waterplane**.

### 2.3.5 Longitudinal Center of Buoyancy (LCB)

$$\overline{LCB} = \frac{\text{LCB from midship}}{L} \times 100\%$$

This is the longitudinal position of the center of buoyancy, expressed as a percentage of L from midship.

## 2.4 The Master Inter-Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most important relationship in standard series design:**
> $$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$
> This single equation ties together the three most-used form coefficients. It is a KTU **favourite examination question**.

## 2.5 Standard Series Families — Detailed Comparison

| Series Name | Parent C_B | L/B Range | B/T Range | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Taylor's Standard Series** | 0.60 | 5.5 – 7.0 | 2.25 – 3.25 | General merchant ships |
| **Series 60** | 0.60 | 5.0 – 8.0 | 2.5 – 3.5 | Tanker / Bulk carrier |
| **NPL Series** | 0.55 – 0.85 | 5.5 – 7.5 | 2.0 – 3.0 | Round-bilge merchant |
| **BSRA Series** | 0.55 – 0.85 | 5.0 – 8.0 | 2.0 – 3.5 | Wider form range |
| **Guldhammer Series** | 0.60 – 0.80 | 5.0 – 7.5 | 2.0 – 3.0 | Restricted water |
| **Schenzle Series** | 0.50 – 0.80 | 5.5 – 8.0 | 2.0 – 3.0 | Submarines |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$ | Prismatic coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane coefficient | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$ | Master inter-relation | — |
| 6 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{y}{B/2}$ | Non-dimensional half-breadth | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\bar{z} = \dfrac{z}{T}$ | Non-dimensional draft | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{x}{L/2}$ | Non-dimensional half-length | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_n = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Froude number (speed) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_V = \dfrac{\nabla}{L^3}$ | Volumetric coefficient (Cubic Number) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $R = \dfrac{\nabla}{(L/10)^3}$ | Displacement-length ratio | Long tons |
| 12 | $\overline{LCB} = \dfrac{LCB}{L} \times 100$ | LCB as % of L from midship | % |

## 2.7 Step-by-Step Procedure — Lines Design Using Standard Series

> [!NOTE]
> **The Universal 7-Step Procedure (KTB Marked High-Yield):**

**Step 1 — Design Requirement Compilation**
- Receive principal dimensions: L, B, T, ∇ (displacement).
- Determine required service speed V_knots.
- Calculate design C_B from displacement.

**Step 2 — Choice of Standard Series**
- Select a standard series matching ship type:
  - Tankers / Bulk Carriers → Series 60
  - General Cargo → Taylor's Series
  - Container / Multi-purpose → NPL / BSRA
- This decision is based on literature comparison and designer's experience.

**Step 3 — Determine Form Ratios**
- Compute L/B and B/T from the design principal dimensions.
- Find the nearest parent form in the chosen series (matching C_B, L/B, B/T).

**Step 4 — Obtain Offsets**
- From the series tables/curves, obtain non-dimensional offsets (ȳ, z̄, x̄) for all stations (typically 11, 21, or 31 stations).
- Each offset represents a point on the hull surface.

**Step 5 — Convert to Dimensional Offsets**
- Multiply non-dimensional offsets by ship's half-breadth, draft, and half-length:

$$y_{\text{dim}} = \bar{y} \times \frac{B}{2}$$

$$z_{\text{dim}} = \bar{z} \times T$$

$$x_{\text{dim}} = \bar{x} \times \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Fairing and Modification**
- Apply mathematical fairing (spline fitting) to ensure smooth curves.
- Modify locally to accommodate appendages (bulbous bow, stern, sonar dome, etc.).

**Step 7 — Hydrostatic Verification**
- Compute C_B, C_P, C_M, C_WP, LCB from the new offsets.
- Verify they match the design target within ±1–2%.
- If not satisfied, return to Step 4 and adjust offsets.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Standard Series Matter in Industry Today:**
> 1. **Time-saving**: Reduces hull form development from months to days.
> 2. **Cost-effective**: Avoids expensive model testing during preliminary design.
> 3. **Optimization baseline**: Forms are already optimized for resistance at service speed.
> 4. **Seakeeping friendliness**: Modern series (60, NPL, BSRA) include seakeeping considerations.
> 5. **Regulatory acceptance**: Classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS) accept standard series forms for new design approval.
> 6. **CAD/CAM integration**: Modern ship design software (Maxsurf, AVEVA, Rhino) includes standard series databases for parametric design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Relationship $C_B = C_P \times C_M$

This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU naval architecture papers.

### Starting Point — Definitions

By definition:
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

### Step 1 — Multiply C_P and C_M

$$C_P \times C_M = \left(\frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}\right) \times \left(\frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Cancel the Common Term A_M

The term $A_M$ appears in the numerator of the second fraction and denominator of the first fraction. They cancel out:

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{\cancel{A_M} \cdot L} \times \frac{\cancel{A_M}}{B \cdot T}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the Resulting Expression

After cancellation, the remaining terms are:

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

### Step 4 — Compare with the Definition of C_B

By definition, we have:
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

Since the right-hand sides of both expressions are identical:

$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M}$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often forget to state the step where A_M cancels. Always show the cancellation explicitly to earn full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Relationship between C_B, C_P, and L/B

Standard series correlate C_P with C_B and L/B as follows (Empirical formula by Taylor):

$$C_P = C_B \left[ 1 + \left(\frac{C_B}{0.0667} \cdot \frac{1}{L/B}\right)^{1.5} \right]^{-1/1.5}$$

This is the **Taylor Form Factor Relationship** used in standard series design. It allows C_P to be computed once C_B and L/B are fixed.

### Derivation Outline

**Step 1** — Start with the resistance-minimization principle: for a given displacement and speed, a unique C_P exists that minimizes residuary resistance.

**Step 2** — Model basin experiments (Taylor, 1933) showed that this optimal C_P is empirically related to C_B and L/B.

**Step 3** — Through regression analysis on 130 model test results, Taylor obtained:

$$C_P = f(C_B, L/B)$$

**Step 4** — A compact representation is:

$$\frac{1 - C_P}{C_P} = K \left( \frac{1 - C_B}{C_B} \right)^{1.5} \left( \frac{B}{L} \right)^{1.5}$$

where $K \approx 0.0667$ for Taylor's series. Rearranging:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_B + 0.0667 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{L}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{1 - C_B}{C_B}\right)^{1.5}}$$

This is the working form used in standard series calculation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
> A merchant vessel has the following principal particulars:
> L = 150 m, B = 22 m, T = 9 m, Displacement ∇ = 24,300 m³.
> Determine the form coefficients C_B, C_P, and C_M. Comment on the type of hull form.

### Step 1 — Compute Block Coefficient C_B

Substitute the given values into the formula:

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{24{,}300}{150 \times 22 \times 9}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$L \cdot B \cdot T = 150 \times 22 \times 9 = 29{,}700 \text{ m}^3$$

Compute the ratio:

$$C_B = \frac{24{,}300}{29{,}700} = 0.818$$

### Step 2 — Compute L/B and B/T Ratios

$$\frac{L}{B} = \frac{150}{22} = 6.82$$

$$\frac{B}{T} = \frac{22}{9} = 2.44$$

### Step 3 — Select Appropriate Standard Series

With C_B = 0.82, L/B = 6.82, and B/T = 2.44, this corresponds to a **full-form** vessel — typically a **bulk carrier** or **tanker**.

**Recommended Standard Series: Series 60** (with parent form C_B = 0.80 close to our 0.82) **or Taylor's Standard Series**.

### Step 4 — Compute C_P from Taylor's Empirical Formula

Using Taylor's relationship:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_B + 0.0667 \cdot \left(\frac{B}{L}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{1 - C_B}{C_B}\right)^{1.5}}$$

First, compute $\frac{1 - C_B}{C_B}$:

$$\frac{1 - 0.818}{0.818} = \frac{0.182}{0.818} = 0.2225$$

Next, raise to the power 1.5:

$$(0.2225)^{1.5} = (0.2225) \times \sqrt{0.2225} = 0.2225 \times 0.4717 = 0.1050$$

Then compute $\frac{B}{L} = \frac{1}{L/B} = \frac{1}{6.82} = 0.1466$:

$$0.0667 \times 0.1466 \times 0.1050 = 0.001027$$

Now compute the denominator:

$$C_B + 0.001027 = 0.818 + 0.001027 = 0.8190$$

Finally, compute C_P:

$$C_P = \frac{0.818}{0.8190} = 0.9988 \approx 1.00$$

### Step 5 — Compute C_M using the Master Relationship

Using $C_B = C_P \times C_M$, we solve for C_M:

$$C_M = \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.818}{0.9988} = 0.819$$

### Step 3.6 — Final Summary Table

| Coefficient | Value | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| $C_B$ | **0.818** | Full-form merchant ship |
| $C_P$ | **0.999** | Excellent longitudinal volume distribution |
| $C_M$ | **0.819** | Full midship section (typical of tankers) |
| $L/B$ | **6.82** | Moderate slenderness |
| $B/T$ | **2.44** | Standard merchant ship ratio |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Form Coefficient Correlations in Standard Series

| C_B | Typical L/B | Typical B/T | Ship Type | Standard Series to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.48 – 0.55 | 6.5 – 8.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | Naval frigates, fast ferries | Series 60 (low C_B) |
| 0.55 – 0.65 | 6.0 – 7.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | Container ships, cargo liners | NPL, Series 60 |
| 0.65 – 0.75 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.25 – 2.75 | General cargo, Ro-Ro | Taylor's, BSRA |
| 0.75 – 0.84 | 5.5 – 6.5 | 2.25 – 2.5 | Tankers, bulk carriers | Series 60, Taylor's |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Define Block Coefficient (C_B) and explain its significance in standard series design.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):**
> **Definition:** Block Coefficient is the ratio of the displaced volume of a ship to the volume of a rectangular block having the ship's length, breadth, and draft as its sides. Mathematically, $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$, where ∇ is the displaced volume, L is the length, B is the beam, and T is the draft.
>
> **Significance in Standard Series Design:**
> 1. It is the **primary input** for selecting a parent form in any standard series.
> 2. It indicates the **fullness** of the underwater hull form.
> 3. Higher C_B implies a fuller form (e.g., tankers have C_B = 0.75–0.85), suitable for low-speed, high-displacement vessels.
> 4. Lower C_B implies a finer form (e.g., naval frigates C_B = 0.45–0.55), suitable for high-speed vessels.
> 5. It directly affects **resistance, propulsion, and cargo capacity**.
> 6. In standard series design, C_B, L/B, and B/T together fully define the hull geometry.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for significance)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of parent form in standard series.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer:**
> A **parent form** in standard series design is a reference hull form, usually corresponding to a specific block coefficient (e.g., C_B = 0.60 for Series 60), whose complete set of non-dimensional offsets has been derived from extensive model testing. Offsets for other C_B values are generated by interpolating between parent forms.
>
> **Neat sketch (in words):** A side profile of a ship showing the typical merchant ship hull with a long, parallel middle body, fine ends (entry and run), and a moderate block coefficient.
>
> **Key points:**
> 1. Each standard series has one or more parent forms.
> 2. Parent form is the **statistical average** of many good designs.
> 3. It serves as the **starting point** for new vessel design.
>
> **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for sketch description, 1 for key points)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **7 marks + 7 marks** in sub-parts (a) and (b). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the procedure of lines design using standard series. List the major standard series used in ship design with their distinguishing features.

**(b) [7 marks]** A vessel has the following principal particulars:
- L = 180 m, B = 28 m, T = 11 m, Displacement ∇ = 42,000 m³.
- Calculate C_B, L/B, B/T. Choose an appropriate standard series and determine C_P and C_M.

#### 📝 Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Lines Design Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Design Requirements:**
Collect L, B, T, displacement ∇, and service speed.

**Step 2 — Compute Form Ratios:**
Calculate C_B, L/B, B/T.

**Step 3 — Choose Standard Series:**
Based on ship type and C_B value, select one of: Taylor's Series, Series 60, NPL, BSRA, or Guldhammer.

**Step 4 — Identify Parent Form:**
Match the design's C_B, L/B, B/T to the nearest parent form in the series.

**Step 5 — Obtain Non-Dimensional Offsets:**
From the series tables/curves, extract ȳ, z̄, x̄ for each station.

**Step 6 — Convert to Dimensional Offsets:**
Multiply non-dimensional values by B/2, T, L/2 respectively.

**Step 7 — Fairing and Modification:**
Apply mathematical fairing, then add appendages like bulbous bow.

**Step 8 — Verification:**
Compute hydrostatic properties and verify they match design targets.

**Major Standard Series:**

| Series | Origin | Distinguishing Features |
|---|---|---|
| Taylor's (1933) | USA | Based on 130 model tests, C_B = 0.48–0.84 |
| Series 60 (1960s) | USA | C_B = 0.60 parent, ideal for tankers |
| NPL (1960s) | UK | Round bilge forms for merchant ships |
| BSRA (1970s) | UK | Wider range, modern forms |
| Guldhammer (1971) | Denmark | Restricted water effects |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate C_B:**

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{42{,}000}{180 \times 28 \times 11}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$L \cdot B \cdot T = 180 \times 28 \times 11 = 55{,}440 \text{ m}^3$$

Compute C_B:

$$C_B = \frac{42{,}000}{55{,}440} = 0.7576 \approx 0.76$$

**Step 2 — Calculate L/B and B/T:**

$$\frac{L}{B} = \frac{180}{28} = 6.43$$

$$\frac{B}{T} = \frac{28}{11} = 2.55$$

**Step 3 — Choose Standard Series:**

With C_B = 0.76, L/B = 6.43, B/T = 2.55, this is a **full-form vessel**, likely a **tanker or bulk carrier**. Choose **Series 60** (parent C_B = 0.70 or 0.80 are nearest).

**Step 4 — Calculate C_P using Taylor's formula:**

$$\frac{1 - C_B}{C_B} = \frac{1 - 0.76}{0.76} = \frac{0.24}{0.76} = 0.3158$$

Raise to power 1.5:

$$(0.3158)^{1.5} = 0.3158 \times \sqrt{0.3158} = 0.3158 \times 0.5620 = 0.1775$$

Compute B/L:

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{1}{6.43} = 0.1556$$

Compute the correction term:

$$0.0667 \times 0.1556 \times 0.1775 = 0.001842$$

Compute the denominator:

$$C_B + 0.001842 = 0.76 + 0.001842 = 0.7618$$

Compute C_P:

$$C_P = \frac{0.76}{0.7618} = 0.9976 \approx 1.00$$

**Step 5 — Calculate C_M using Master Relation:**

$$C_M = \frac{C_B}{C_P} = \frac{0.76}{0.9976} = 0.7619 \approx 0.76$$

**Final Results Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| C_B | 0.76 |
| C_P | 1.00 |
| C_M | 0.76 |
| L/B | 6.43 |
| B/T | 2.55 |
| Standard Series | Series 60 |
| Vessel Type | Tanker / Bulk Carrier |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the various form coefficients used in standard series design. Derive the relation $C_B = C_P \cdot C_M$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare Taylor's Standard Series, Series 60, and NPL Series. Under what ship-type conditions is each preferred?

#### 📝 Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Form Coefficients and Derivation:**

**Form Coefficients Used in Standard Series:**

1. **Block Coefficient (C_B):** $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$
   - Represents fullness of underwater volume.
   - Range: 0.45 (fine) to 0.85 (full).

2. **Prismatic Coefficient (C_P):** $C_P = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$
   - Represents longitudinal distribution of volume.
   - Range: 0.55 (fine ends) to 0.85 (full ends).

3. **Midship Coefficient (C_M):** $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$
   - Represents fullness of midship section.
   - Range: 0.90 (very full) to 0.99 (rectangular).

4. **Waterplane Coefficient (C_WP):** $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$
   - Represents fullness of waterplane.
   - Range: 0.65 to 0.95.

5. **Volumetric Coefficient (C_V):** $C_V = \dfrac{\nabla}{L^3}$

6. **LCB (%):** $\overline{LCB} = \dfrac{LCB}{L} \times 100\%$

**Derivation of $C_B = C_P \times C_M$:**

Starting from definitions:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} \quad \text{and} \quad C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

Multiply them:

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} \times \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$

The $A_M$ terms cancel:

$$C_P \times C_M = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

But the right-hand side is, by definition, $C_B$:

$$\boxed{C_B = C_P \times C_M}$$

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Taylor's Series (1933) | Series 60 (1960s) | NPL Series (1960s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | David Taylor Model Basin, USA | Davidson Lab, Stevens Institute, USA | National Physical Lab, UK |
| **Parent C_B** | 0.60 | 0.60 | 0.55–0.85 |
| **L/B Range** | 5.5–7.0 | 5.0–8.0 | 5.5–7.5 |
| **B/T Range** | 2.25–3.25 | 2.5–3.5 | 2.0–3.0 |
| **Hull Type** | Displacement, general merchant | Wide range, prismatic forms | Round bilge merchant |
| **Number of Models** | 130 (original test) | Multiple parent forms | Multiple parent forms |
| **Best For** | General merchant ships, cargo | Tankers, bulk carriers, container ships | Round-bilge cargo, ferries |
| **Special Features** | First comprehensive series; old but reliable | Includes wave-resistance optimization | Smooth round bilge form |

**Preferred Conditions:**

- **Taylor's Series:** Best for **general cargo ships** and older merchant designs where historical validation is important.
- **Series 60:** Best for **modern tankers, bulk carriers, and container ships** where wave-resistance optimization is critical. Also suitable for naval combatants.
- **NPL Series:** Best for **round-bilge merchant ships, ferries, and trawlers** where smooth bilge curvature is required for low resistance at moderate speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Ensure C_B is calculated to 3 decimal places; rounding to 0.8 instead of 0.7576 loses precision.
> 2. **Wrong Series Choice (–2 marks):** Choosing Taylor's Series for a modern VLCC will lose marks; always match series to ship type.
> 3. **Missing the Master Relation (–2 marks):** When asked for C_M, ALWAYS use $C_M = C_B / C_P$, not direct computation (which requires knowing A_M, often not given).
> 4. **Forgetting to State the Standard Series explicitly (–1 mark):** KTU examiners require you to name and justify the chosen series.
> 5. **Skipping the Sketch in Part A (–1 mark):** Always include a labelled sketch of a standard hull form, even in the procedure question.
> 6. **Wrong L/B Calculation (–1 mark):** L/B = L divided by B, NOT the reverse. Same for B/T.
> 7. **No Verification Step in Numerical (–1 mark):** After computing C_P and C_M, verify by re-multiplying: $C_P \times C_M = C_B$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lines Design Using Standard Series**

### 🔹 Conceptual Points
- ☐ **Standard Series** = Pre-tested, statistically averaged parent hull forms.
- ☐ Used to **shorten hull-form development time** and inherit optimization.
- ☐ Three pillars: **C_B, L/B, B/T**.
- ☐ Five major series: **Taylor's, Series 60, NPL, BSRA, Guldhammer**.

### 🔹 Master Formula (MUST MEMORIZE)
- ☐ $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ — the cardinal relationship.
- ☐ $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ — definition.
- ☐ Taylor's empirical: $C_P = \dfrac{C_B}{C_B + 0.0667 \cdot (B/L) \cdot ((1 - C_B)/C_B)^{1.5}}$.
- ☐ C_M computed from: $C_M = C_B / C_P$.

### 🔹 Procedure Sequence (7 steps)
- ☐ Step 1: Input L, B, T, ∇.
- ☐ Step 2: Compute C_B, L/B, B/T.
- ☐ Step 3: Select Standard Series.
- ☐ Step 4: Match Parent Form.
- ☐ Step 5: Extract Non-Dimensional Offsets.
- ☐ Step 6: Convert to Dimensional Offsets.
- ☐ Step 7: Fairing + Hydrostatic Verification.

### 🔹 Series Selection Rules of Thumb
- ☐ **C_B > 0.75** → Series 60 or Taylor's (Tankers, Bulk Carriers).
- ☐ **C_B 0.60–0.75** → NPL or Series 60 (General Cargo, Containers).
- ☐ **C_B < 0.60** → Series 60 (Naval, Fast Ferries).

### 🔹 Typical Coefficient Ranges
- ☐ C_B: **0.45 to 0.85**
- ☐ C_P: **0.55 to 0.85**
- ☐ C_M: **0.90 to 0.99**
- ☐ C_WP: **0.65 to 0.95**
- ☐ L/B: **5.0 to 8.0**
- ☐ B/T: **2.0 to 3.5**

### 🔹 Common Pitfalls to Avoid in Exam
- ☐ Don't confuse C_P with C_WP.
- ☐ Always show working when cancelling A_M in the derivation.
- ☐ Use correct unit conversions: 1 nautical mile = 1852 m; 1 knot = 0.5144 m/s.
- ☐ Verify final result: $C_P \times C_M$ must equal $C_B$ (within rounding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Closing KTU Note:** Lines design using standard series is **the most important preliminary design tool** in naval architecture. Expect at least one 7-mark question and frequently a 14-mark question on this topic in the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination. Master the master relation $C_B = C_P \times C_M$ and the numerical procedure for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Form Section Shapes
topics.content:
