**(a)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **geometry and key dimensional parameters** of a bulbous bow. Discuss the typical range of bulb length, width, and longitudinal position relative to the forward perpendicular. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A container vessel has the following resistance data at design service speed $V = 20$ knots:
- $R_F = 450$ kN
- $R_{W,bare} = 380$ kN (without bulbous bow)
- After fitting an optimized bulbous bow, $R_{W,bulb} = 304$ kN
- $V = 20$ knots = **10.29 m/s**

Compute:
(i) The percentage reduction in wave-making resistance.
(ii) The effective power saved in kW.
(iii) The percentage reduction in total effective power.
(iv) Comment on the **annual fuel cost savings** assuming 7,000 hours/year of operation at a specific fuel consumption of 195 g/kWh and fuel cost ₹70,000/tonne (HFO). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Geometry and Dimensional Parameters (7 marks):**

A bulbous bow is a streamlined protuberance attached to the forward underwater portion of the hull, projecting forward of the forward perpendicular (FP). The principal dimensional parameters are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (as fraction of $L_{PP}$ or $B$) |
|---|---|---|
| Length of bulb | $L_b$ | $0.05 L_{PP}$ to $0.10 L_{PP}$ |
| Maximum width | $B_b$ | $0.15 B$ to $0.25 B$ |
| Height above keel | $H_b$ | $0.10 D$ to $0.18 D$ (where $D$ = draft) |
| Forward protrusion | $e$ | $0.005 L_{PP}$ to $0.020 L_{PP}$ |
| Longitudinal position of centroid | $x_b$ | Typically 0.01 to 0.05 $L_{PP}$ forward of FP |

**Sketch Description:**

```
   (See schematic in Section 4.3 above)
   - Bulb apex protrudes forward of FP by distance 'e'
   - Bulb cross-section is rounded/ellipsoidal
   - Centerline of bulb aligns with ship's centerline
   - Top of bulb is typically 0.5 to 1.5 m below design waterline
```

The bulb is a hollow (or ballast-filled) welded steel structure, faired smoothly into the hull plating. Its internal volume can be used for **chain lockers, sonar domes, or fresh water tanks** — adding utility beyond hydrodynamics.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Percentage reduction in wave-making resistance:**

$$\Delta R_W = R_{W,bare} - R_{W,bulb} = 380 - 304 = 76 \text{ kN}$$

$$\Delta R_W \% = \frac{76}{380} \times 100 = 20.0\%$$

**(ii) Effective power saved:**

$$\Delta P_E = \Delta R_W \cdot V = 76{,}000 \text{ N} \times 10.29 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\Delta P_E = 782{,}040 \text{ W} \approx 782.0 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction in total effective power:**

Total resistance before:
$$R_{T,1} = R_F + R_{W,bare} = 450 + 380 = 830 \text{ kN}$$

Total resistance after:
$$R_{T,2} = R_F + R_{W,bulb} = 450 + 304 = 754 \text{ kN}$$

Power before:
$$P_{E,1} = 830 \times 10.29 = 8540.7 \text{ kW}$$

Power after:
$$P_{E,2} = 754 \times 10.29 = 7758.7 \text{ kW}$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_E}{P_{E,1}} = \frac{782.0}{8540.7} \times 100 = 9.16\%$$

**(iv) Annual fuel cost savings:**

Annual energy saved:
$$E_{saved} = 782.0 \text{ kW} \times 7000 \text{ h} = 5{,}474{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

Fuel mass saved (at SFC = 195 g/kWh = 0.195 kg/kWh):
$$m_{fuel} = 5{,}474{,}000 \times 0.195 = 1{,}067{,}430 \text{ kg} = 1067.4 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

Cost saved (at ₹70,000/tonne):
$$\text{Cost}_{saved} = 1067.4 \times 70{,}000 = \text{₹ } 7{,}47{,}18{,}000 \approx ₹ 7.47 \text{ crore/year}$$

(Equivalent to roughly **₹74.7 million per year** — a substantial operational saving.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **wave interference principle** by which a bulbous bow reduces wave-making resistance. Derive the **phase condition** for maximum destructive interference in terms of bulb position and ship speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of a **bulbous bow** with a **ram bow** for a high-speed container vessel. Discuss:
(i) The Froude number range of effectiveness for each.
(ii) The relative impact on total resistance.
(iii) The structural and operational trade-offs.
(iv) State with justification which is preferred for a vessel operating at $F_n = 0.32$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Wave Interference Principle and Phase Condition (7 marks):**

When a ship translates at velocity $V$, the free-surface elevation around the hull can be expressed as the superposition of wave systems from various hydrodynamic sources. Following Havelock's source-potential theory, a submerged source of strength $m$ at position $x_s$ generates a wave train on the free surface with dominant wave number:

$$k_0 = \frac{g}{V^2}$$

The wavelength of the transverse wave system is:
$$\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k_0} = \frac{2\pi V^2}{g}$$

The bow shoulder (at $x_{sh}$) generates the primary wave system, and the bulb centroid (at $x_b$, where $x_b < x_{sh}$, i.e., forward of shoulder) generates a secondary wave system with a phase delay:

$$\Delta\phi = k_0 \cdot (x_{sh} - x_b) = \frac{g}{V^2} \cdot (x_{sh} - x_b)$$

For **maximum destructive interference**:
$$\Delta\phi = (2n+1)\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

For first-order cancellation ($n=0$):
$$\frac{g \cdot \Delta x}{V^2} = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta x = \frac{\pi V^2}{g}$$

This **phase condition** dictates the longitudinal separation between bulb centroid and bow shoulder. It is **speed-dependent**, which is why a bulb is "tuned" to a specific design speed.

**Key Insight:** As $V$ changes, the required $\Delta x$ changes quadratically. The physical bulb position is fixed, so the interference condition is satisfied **only at one speed** (the design speed). This is the fundamental reason a bulb is a *speed-tuned* appendage.

**(b) Bulbous Bow vs. Ram Bow Comparison (7 marks):**

**(i) Froude Number Range of Effectiveness:**

| Bow Type | Optimal $F_n$ Range | Effective Band |
|---|---|---|
| Bulbous Bow | $0.20$ to $0.40$ | Narrow (±10% of design $F_n$) |
| Ram Bow | $0.40$ to $0.80$ | Moderate (wider at high $F_n$) |

**(ii) Relative Impact on Total Resistance:**

- **Bulbous Bow:** Reduces $R_W$ by **5%–20%** at design speed. $R_F$ slightly increases due to added wetted surface. Net reduction in $R_T$: 4%–12%.
- **Ram Bow:** Reduces $R_W$ significantly at high $F_n$ by splitting the bow wave and reducing spray generation. Reduction in $R_T$: 3%–8% at high $F_n$, but **increases** $R_T$ at low $F_n$.

**(iii) Structural and Operational Trade-offs:**

| Aspect | Bulbous Bow | Ram Bow |
|---|---|---|
| Structural complexity | Moderate (welded bulb structure) | High (steep stem plate, fine entry) |
| Slamming risk | High if bulb emerges in heavy seas | Moderate to high at high speeds |
| Wetted surface | Increased | Decreased |
| Sensitivity to draft | High (low-draft operation reduces benefit) | Low |
| Cost of construction | Moderate | High |
| Off-design penalty | Significant | Moderate |

**(iv) For $F_n = 0.32$ (Container Vessel):**

A **bulbous bow is clearly preferred** for the following reasons:

1. The design $F_n = 0.32$ falls squarely within the optimal range for bulbous bows ($0.20$–$0.40$).
2. Container vessels operate predominantly at a single design service speed on long ocean voyages — the narrow effective band of a bulb is therefore acceptable.
3. Expected wave resistance reduction of 10%–15% at $F_n = 0.32$ substantially outweighs the marginal benefit a ram bow could provide at this sub-critical Froude number.
4. A ram bow's advantage is realized only at $F_n > 0.40$; at $F_n = 0.32$, its wave-splitting effect is poorly developed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $V^2$ dependence in the phase condition:** A common error is to write $\Delta x = \pi / g$ (dimensionally wrong). Always show $\Delta x = \pi V^2 / g$.
>
> 2. **Choosing the wrong bow for the wrong $F_n$:** Marks are heavily deducted for recommending a ram bow at $F_n = 0.32$. The question specifically tests whether students understand the *band-limited* nature of each bow type.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the frictional resistance increase:** Even when the bulb reduces $R_W$, the added wetted surface area increases $R_F$ slightly. Students who claim "total resistance is reduced by the same percentage as wave resistance" lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist — Bulbous Bow**

- **Definition:** A bulbous bow is a forward-projecting, streamlined, underwater appendage fitted to reduce wave-making resistance through destructive wave interference at the design Froude number.

- **Operating Principle:** Bulb-generated secondary wave (anti-source) destructively interferes with primary bow wave → reduced wave energy radiated → reduced $R_W$.

- **Tuning Equation:** $\Delta x = \pi V_d^2 / g$ — bulb position is **speed-dependent**.

- **Effective Range:** $F_n = 0.20$ to $0.40$ (displacement-type commercial vessels).

- **Benefit Magnitude:** $R_W$ reduction of 5%–20%; total $R_T$ reduction of 4%–12% at design speed; **fuel savings 3%–8% annually**.

- **Key Dimensional Ratios:** $L_b = 0.05$ to $0.10 \, L_{PP}$; $B_b = 0.15$ to $0.25 \, B$; $H_b = 0.10$ to $0.18 \, D$.

- **Disadvantages:** Off-design performance penalty, slamming risk at emergence, increased frictional resistance due to added wetted surface.

- **Power Saving Formula:** $\dfrac{\Delta P_E}{P_{E,1}} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{R_{W,1}}{R_F + R_{W,1}}$ (must account for frictional component).

- **NOT a Universal Solution:** Ineffective at very low $F_n$; detrimental at very high $F_n$ (use ram bow instead).

- **Famous Examples:** VLCC tankers (e.g., *TI Europe*-class), Post-Panamax containerships (Emma Mærsk class), Indian Navy frigates (modified Talwar class).

- **KCU Trend (2024+):** Modern bulbous bow design increasingly uses **CFD optimization** and **form-factor minimization**, moving away from the older Marconi-type symmetric bulbs toward asymmetric, free-form shapes (delta bulbs) for high-Fn slender hulls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Form of Stern. Effect of Hull Form on Ship’s Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Form of Stern. Effect of Hull Form on Ship’s Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Form of Stern — Formal KTU Definition

The **form of stern** of a ship is the geometric shaping of the afterbody (the hull region aft of amidships) as viewed in profile and plan view. It is one of the most decisive design decisions in naval architecture because it controls the **wake distribution**, **eddy-making resistance**, **propulsive efficiency**, and **seakeeping behavior** of a vessel.

The principal KTU-categorised stern forms are:

| Stern Type | Profile Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Cruiser Stern (V-type)** | Convex above waterline, concave below | Fast warships, frigates, high-speed merchant vessels |
| **Transom Stern** | Flat or slightly rounded vertical face aft | Tugboats, fishing boats, planing craft, semi-displacement hulls |
| **Elliptical Stern** | Smooth, symmetric elliptical run | Modern single-screw merchant ships, tankers |
| **Square Stern** | Flat transom, boxy afterbody | Inland vessels, barges, traditional workboats |
| **Canoe Stern** | Long, tapered, pointed run | Sailing vessels, yachts, low-drag forms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The syllabus specifically requires study of the **Cruiser Stern** and **Transom Stern** as the two principal modern forms, plus their effect on resistance, propulsion, and seakeeping.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **swimming freestyle in a pool**. Your hand enters the water cleanly at the front (entry) and **exits smoothly at the back** with fingers trailing. This creates the least disturbance, right? Now imagine pulling your hand out of the water flat-palmed — water splashes everywhere, you lose thrust, and you feel drag.

The **stern of a ship behaves exactly like your hand exit**:
- A **cruiser/elliptical stern** is like the "trailing fingers" — water closes in smoothly behind the propeller, reducing suction resistance and increasing propulsive efficiency.
- A **transom stern** is like the "flat palm" — a flat wall of water must constantly be re-accelerated, which costs energy but provides **lift** for planing/semi-planing craft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wake Field** = the disturbed water that the propeller must "swim" through. A well-formed stern minimises this wake, making the propeller "see" cleaner water and convert engine power into thrust more efficiently.

## 1.3 Hull Form Definition (Broader Context)

**Hull form** refers to the complete three-dimensional geometry of the submerged and emerged portions of the ship, defined by lines on the **Lines Plan (Sheer Plan, Body Plan, Half-Breadth Plan)**. The hull form is parameterised by **form coefficients** (also called **form parameters** or **Hull Form Ratios**) that numerically describe the fullness or fineness of the hull.

The principal form coefficients covered in KTU Module 3 are:

- **Block Coefficient ($C_B$)** — overall fullness of the underwater volume.
- **Prismatic Coefficient ($C_P$)** — longitudinal distribution of area.
- **Midship Section Coefficient ($C_M$)** — transverse fullness at amidships.
- **Waterplane Area Coefficient ($C_{WP}$)** — fullness of the waterplane.
- **Length-to-Beam Ratio ($L/B$)** — slenderness of the hull.
- **Beam-to-Draft Ratio ($B/T$)** — transverse stability indicator.
- **Displacement-to-Length Ratio ($\nabla / L^3$)** — volumetric slenderness.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of sea water**: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Kinematic viscosity of sea water at 15 °C**: $\nu \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Froude Number (dimensionless)**: $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$
- **Reynolds Number (dimensionless)**: $R_n = VL / \nu$
- **Standard ITTC 1978 reference area**: $S = \nabla^{2/3} \cdot (3.432 + 0.305 \cdot L / 1000) + 0.0282 \cdot (L \cdot (B + T))$ in m² (constants in m, ft version differs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Stern Form Analysis

### 2.1.1 Cruiser Stern (V-Type Stern)

The **cruiser stern** has a **double-curvature profile**: a gentle convex curve above the designed waterline and a sharper concave hollow (called the **"squirt"** or **"run"**) below the waterline, terminating at the rudder post.

**Characteristics:**
- Superior **wave-making** behaviour at high Froude numbers.
- Higher **propulsive efficiency** ($Q_{PC}$ or $\eta_D$) due to a thinner, more uniform wake field.
- Better **course-keeping** and directional stability in waves.
- Commonly used for **warships, frigates, corvettes, and fast container vessels**.
- Disadvantage: more complex to fabricate (compound curvature plating).

**Why it works:** The convex upper part sheds the free surface flow smoothly, while the concave lower part **accelerates the flow** approaching the propeller disc, partially recovering pressure and reducing eddy-making losses.

### 2.1.2 Transom Stern

The **transom stern** ends in a **flat or near-vertical plate** above the baseline, creating a "transom" surface.

**Characteristics:**
- Used on **planing, semi-planing, and displacement hulls** (tugs, fishing vessels, patrol boats, SAR craft).
- At low speeds the transom is **immersed** → acts like a blunt appendage, increasing wave resistance.
- At high speeds the transom **emerges** → the hull effectively becomes shorter (shorter dynamic waterline), reducing wetted surface and resistance — this is the **"transom stern hump"** phenomenon.
- Produces a **broad, thick wake** → lower propulsive efficiency for single-screw ships.
- Provides **buoyancy lift** at the stern for semi-planing craft.

**Why it works:** The transom acts as a **planing surface**, generating **dynamic lift** proportional to $V^2$, which raises the stern at high speed and reduces wetted surface area — this is the physical basis of planing.

### 2.1.3 Elliptical Stern

A modern refinement that gives the **wake contour an elliptical shape** (in plan view) at the propeller disc.

**Characteristics:**
- Produces a **rotationally symmetric wake** with low tangential velocities.
- Maximises **Taylor's wake fraction ($w_T$)** consistency for a single screw.
- Used in **tankers, bulk carriers, and large single-screw merchant ships** to optimise propulsive efficiency.
- Hull lines are computer-optimised (CFD-based hull-form design) to give the lowest possible EHP for a given displacement.

### 2.2 Hull Form Coefficients — The Four Pillars

The four primary form coefficients are mathematically related and KTU 2024 expects students to write the **defining integral form** and the **algebraic identity** for each.

#### (a) Block Coefficient $C_B$
$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$
Where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume. $C_B$ is the **most important single number** in resistance estimation. Higher $C_B$ → more cargo capacity but more resistance at high speed.

- **Full-form ships** (tankers, bulk carriers): $C_B \approx 0.80$–$0.85$
- **Fine-form ships** (warships, fast container ships): $C_B \approx 0.50$–$0.65$

#### (b) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$
$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L} = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$
Where $A_M$ is the **midship section area**. $C_P$ describes the **longitudinal distribution of volume**:
- $C_P = 1.0$ → parallel midbody (uniform cross-section)
- $C_P < 1.0$ → ends taper (typical of fine ships)
- $C_P > 1.0$ → impossible (mathematically $C_P$ cannot exceed 1 if $A_M$ is correctly defined)

#### (c) Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$
$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$$
Describes the **transverse fullness** at the midship section.
- $C_M = 1.0$ → rectangular midship
- $C_M \approx 0.90$–$0.98$ for typical merchant ships
- $C_M \approx 0.75$–$0.85$ for fine warships

#### (d) Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$
$$C_{WP} = \frac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$$
Where $A_{WP}$ is the **waterplane area**. $C_{WP}$ controls **wave resistance** and **added resistance in waves**.

#### Key Algebraic Identity (KTU High-Yield)
$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$
This identity is **exam-favourite** — must be memorised.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Froude Number | $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$ | dimensionless | Resistance scaling, hull-speed regime |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $R_n = VL / \nu$ | dimensionless | Frictional resistance scaling |
| 3 | Block Coefficient | $C_B = \nabla / (L \cdot B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | Cargo capacity vs. resistance |
| 4 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_P = C_B / C_M$ | dimensionless | Longitudinal fullness |
| 5 | Midship Coefficient | $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ | dimensionless | Transverse fullness |
| 6 | Waterplane Coeff. | $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L \cdot B)$ | dimensionless | Wave resistance, added resistance |
| 7 | Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L / \nabla^{1/3}$ | m/m | Volumetric fineness |
| 8 | L/B Ratio | $L/B$ | m/m | Slenderness in plan view |
| 9 | B/T Ratio | $B/T$ | m/m | Stability / resistance trade-off |
| 10 | Speed-Length Ratio | $V / \sqrt{L}$ (knots, ft) | — | Hull-speed indicator |
| 11 | Total Resistance | $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{EDDY} + R_{AA}$ | kN | Sum of all resistance components |
| 12 | ITTC Friction Line (1978) | $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$ | dimensionless | Standard frictional reference |
| 13 | Effective Power | $P_E = R_T \cdot V$ | kW | Useful towing power |
| 14 | Thrust Power | $P_T = P_E / \eta_H$ | kW | Power delivered to propeller |
| 15 | Brake Power | $P_B = P_T / \eta_O \cdot \eta_S$ | kW | Engine output power |
| 16 | Froude-Krylov Wave Resist. | $R_W \propto F_n^4$ | kN | Wave-making dominates at high $F_n$ |
| 17 | Frictional Resistance (skin) | $R_F \propto V^{1.83}$ | kN | Skin friction power-law (Schultz-Grigson) |
| 18 | Propulsive Coeff. | $\eta_D = \eta_H \cdot \eta_O$ | dimensionless | Hull + propeller efficiency product |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Table Notation:** All $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) and $\mid$ (conditional) symbols have been replaced with their LaTeX-safe equivalents (\vert, \mid) to preserve markdown table integrity, per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hull form coefficients and stern geometry collectively determine:

1. **Resistance and Required Power** → fuel consumption, range, emissions (EEDI/EEXI compliance under IMO).
2. **Propulsive Efficiency** → wake fraction, thrust deduction, hull efficiency ($\eta_H$).
3. **Seakeeping** → added resistance in waves, motions, slamming.
4. **Maneuverability** → course stability, turning diameter, yaw stability.
5. **Cargo Capacity** → $C_B$ directly translates to deadweight for a given length.
6. **Construction Cost** → fine forms cost more per ton of steel.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Froude Number and Its Physical Meaning

### Step 1: Dimensional Analysis Setup

The wave-making resistance $R_W$ of a ship at speed $V$ depends on:
- Gravity $g$ (creates the wave field)
- Length $L$ (sets the wave pattern scale)
- Density $\rho$ (fluid inertia)
- Speed $V$ (kinematic)

Using **Buckingham $\pi$-theorem**, we can form a dimensionless resistance coefficient that scales with two independent dimensionless groups.

### Step 2: Form the Two Dimensionless Groups

Group 1 — **Froude Number** (gravity / inertia ratio):

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L}}$$

**Derivation logic:** The wave speed in shallow gravity waves is $c = \sqrt{g \cdot \lambda / (2\pi)}$. Setting $V$ proportional to $c$ for a wave of wavelength proportional to $L$ gives $V \sim \sqrt{gL}$, hence the dimensionless ratio $V / \sqrt{gL}$.

Group 2 — **Reynolds Number** (inertia / viscosity ratio):

$$R_n = \frac{V \cdot L}{\nu}$$

**Derivation logic:** Inertia force per unit area $\sim \rho V^2$; viscous force per unit area $\sim \mu V / L = \rho \nu V / L$. Their ratio gives $\rho V^2 / (\rho \nu V / L) = VL / \nu$.

### Step 3: Total Resistance Coefficient Decomposition

The total resistance coefficient (non-dimensionalised by $\frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S$) is decomposed as:

$$C_T = C_F + C_R + C_A + C_{APP}$$

Where:
- $C_F$ — frictional (depends on $R_n$ only)
- $C_R$ — residual (wave + eddy, depends on $F_n$ primarily)
- $C_A$ — correlation allowance (hull roughness, typically $+0.0004$)
- $C_{APP}$ — appendage resistance (rudder, brackets, bilge keels)

### Step 4: Power Conversion Chain

The effective power $P_E$ is the rate of doing work against $R_T$:

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^3 S \cdot C_T$$

**Conversion logic:** $R_T$ has units of Newtons, $V$ has units of m/s, so $R_T \cdot V$ has units of J/s = W (Watts). Multiply by $10^{-3}$ to obtain kiloWatts.

## 3.2 Derivation of Taylor's Wake Fraction and Thrust Deduction

### Step 1: Define Wake Fraction $w_T$

Taylor's wake fraction is the **fractional reduction in propeller inflow velocity** compared to the ship's speed:

$$w_T = \frac{V_A - V}{V}$$

Where $V_A$ is the speed of the ship through still water and $V$ is the speed of water arriving at the propeller disc.

Rearranging:

$$V = V_A (1 - w_T)$$

### Step 2: Define Thrust Deduction $t$

Thrust deduction is the **fractional loss of thrust** to hull pressure forces generated by the propeller:

$$t = \frac{T - R}{T}$$

Where $T$ is total thrust generated and $R$ is the resistance to be overcome.

Rearranging:

$$R = T (1 - t)$$

### Step 3: Hull Propulsive Efficiency

$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w_T}$$

**Conversion logic:** Numerator — fraction of thrust that overcomes resistance. Denominator — fraction of ship speed that reaches the propeller disc. Their ratio is the **hull efficiency** — the KTU gold-standard formula for $\eta_H$.

### Step 4: Taylor's Empirical Wake Fraction for Single-Screw Ships

For single-screw merchant ships, Taylor proposed:

$$w_T = 0.5 \cdot C_B - 0.05$$

**Why it works:** A fuller block ($C_B$ higher) displaces more water and creates a thicker wake, increasing $w_T$. The constant $-0.05$ accounts for the standard slot wake of a single-screw ship.

For example, a tanker with $C_B = 0.82$:
$$w_T = 0.5 \times 0.82 - 0.05 = 0.41 - 0.05 = 0.36$$

For a frigate with $C_B = 0.55$:
$$w_T = 0.5 \times 0.55 - 0.05 = 0.275 - 0.05 = 0.225$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Effective Power from Form Coefficients (Series 60 Example)

The **Series 60** ($C_B = 0.60$) resistance curve gives the wave resistance coefficient as a function of $F_n$:

$$C_W = 0.0024 \cdot F_n^{-3.5} \quad \text{(for Series 60 hull)}$$

### Worked Example: Effective Power Calculation

**Given:**
- $L = 150$ m, $B = 20$ m, $T = 8$ m
- $C_B = 0.60$, $V = 15$ knots
- $S = 3500$ m² (wetted surface)
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- 1 knot $= 0.5144$ m/s

**Step A: Convert speed to SI**
$$V = 15 \times 0.5144 = 7.716 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step B: Compute Froude number**
$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{7.716}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 150}} = \frac{7.716}{38.36} = 0.2012$$

**Step C: Compute Reynolds number**
$$R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{7.716 \times 150}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1157.4}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.726 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step D: ITTC 1978 Friction Line**
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10}(9.726 \times 10^8) - 2)^2}$$
$$\log_{10}(9.726 \times 10^8) = 8.988$$
$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(8.988 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{48.84} = 1.535 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step E: Frictional resistance**
$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.716^2 \times 3500 \times 1.535 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 3500 \times 1.535 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 164,037 \text{ N} = 164.04 \text{ kN}$$

**Step F: Wave resistance coefficient (Series 60)**
$$C_W = 0.0024 \times (0.2012)^{-3.5} = 0.0024 \times 136.3 = 0.3271$$

**Step G: Wave resistance**
$$R_W = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_W = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 3500 \times 0.3271$$
$$= 34{,}929{,}000 \text{ N} = 34{,}929 \text{ kN}$$

**Step H: Total resistance (assuming $C_A = 0.0004$, no appendages)**
$$C_T = C_F + C_R + C_A$$
With $C_R \approx C_W$ for Series 60:
$$C_T = 1.535 \times 10^{-3} + 0.3271 + 4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.3290$$
$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 59.54 \times 3500 \times 0.3290 = 35{,}130{,}000 \text{ N} = 35{,}130 \text{ kN}$$

**Step I: Effective power**
$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 35{,}130{,}000 \times 7.716 = 2.711 \times 10^{8} \text{ W} = 271{,}100 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Interpretation:** The dominant component here is $R_W$ ($C_W$ is ~213 times larger than $C_F$). This illustrates the well-known naval-architecture fact: **at $F_n > 0.20$, wave resistance dominates and hull form optimisation becomes the primary lever to reduce EHP.**

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Stern Type vs. Performance Criterion

| Performance Criterion | Cruiser Stern | Transom Stern | Elliptical Stern |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low wave resistance at high $F_n$ | ★★★★★ | ★★ (low speed only) | ★★★★ |
| Propulsive efficiency (single screw) | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ |
| Course stability | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Maneuverability / turning | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Lift at high speed (planing) | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★ |
| Construction simplicity | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| Fuel efficiency (high speed) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| Suitable for $F_n < 0.20$ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Suitable for $F_n > 0.30$ | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★★★ |
| Common in merchant ships | ★★★ (fast ones) | ★ (tugs only) | ★★★★★ (tankers) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define "form of stern" and explain the difference between a cruiser stern and a transom stern with a sketch.**

**Model Answer:**

The **form of stern** refers to the shape of the afterbody of a ship as viewed in profile and plan view. It is a critical hull-form decision influencing wake field, resistance, and propulsion.

**Cruiser Stern:** Has a **double-curvature profile** with a convex upper region above the waterline and a concave lower region (the "run" or "squirt") below. It produces a **thin, uniform wake** and high propulsive efficiency, making it ideal for **fast warships, frigates, and high-speed merchant vessels**. The double curvature, however, increases **fabrication cost**.

**Transom Stern:** Terminates in a **flat or slightly rounded vertical plate** (the "transom"). It is common on **tugs, fishing vessels, and planing craft**. At low speeds the transom is immersed, adding wave resistance; at high speeds it **emerges**, providing dynamic lift and reducing wetted surface (the **transom hump** effect).

The key trade-off is: **Cruiser = efficient but costly to build; Transom = simple to build but resistance-efficient only at high speed**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the terms (i) Block Coefficient $C_B$ and (ii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$. State the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Block Coefficient $C_B$:** It is the ratio of the **displaced volume $\nabla$** of the ship to the volume of its **rectangular block envelope** (Length × Beam × Draft):

$$C_B = \frac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$$

It is a measure of the **overall fullness** of the underwater hull. Higher $C_B$ indicates a fuller hull (more cargo capacity, more resistance). Typical values:
- Tankers: $0.80$ to $0.85$
- Warships: $0.50$ to $0.65$

**(ii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$:** It is the ratio of the **displaced volume $\nabla$** to the volume formed by the **midship section area** $A_M$ extruded over the length $L$:

$$C_P = \frac{\nabla}{A_M \cdot L}$$

It describes the **longitudinal distribution** of hull volume. $C_P = 1.0$ corresponds to a parallel midbody, while $C_P < 1.0$ indicates tapered ends.

**Relationship (KTU High-Yield):**

$$C_B = C_P \times C_M$$

where $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$ is the midship section coefficient. Hence:

$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Define the following hull form coefficients with sketches and typical value ranges for a bulk carrier:
(i) Block Coefficient $C_B$
(ii) Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$
(iii) Midship Section Coefficient $C_M$
(iv) Waterplane Area Coefficient $C_{WP}$

**(b)** A **Panamax bulk carrier** has the following particulars:
- Length between perpendiculars $L_{PP} = 220$ m
- Beam $B = 32.2$ m
- Design draft $T = 12.0$ m
- Midship section area $A_M = 320$ m²
- Wetted surface area $S = 9500$ m²
- Service speed $V = 14.5$ knots
- Sea water density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Kinematic viscosity $\nu = 1.19 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- 1 knot $= 0.5144$ m/s

Compute:
(i) The Block Coefficient $C_B$ and Prismatic Coefficient $C_P$
(ii) The Froude Number $F_n$ and Reynolds Number $R_n$
(iii) The ITTC 1978 frictional resistance $R_F$
(iv) The Effective Power $P_E$ assuming the Series 60 wave-making coefficient $C_W = 0.0024 \cdot F_n^{-3.5}$ and correlation allowance $C_A = 0.0004$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definitions:** [4 marks, ~1 each]

- **$C_B$:** Already defined above. For bulk carrier: $0.78$ to $0.85$.
- **$C_P$:** Already defined. For bulk carrier: $0.78$ to $0.82$.
- **$C_M$:** $C_M = A_M / (B \cdot T)$. For bulk carrier: $0.94$ to $0.99$ (full mid-section).
- **$C_{WP}$:** $C_{WP} = A_{WP} / (L \cdot B)$. For bulk carrier: $0.78$ to $0.85$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** [10 marks]

**(i) Block and Prismatic Coefficients** [3 marks]

Displaced volume:
$$\nabla = C_B \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T \Rightarrow \text{First find } C_B \text{ from } A_M$$

$$C_M = \frac{A_M}{B \cdot T} = \frac{320}{32.2 \times 12.0} = \frac{320}{386.4} = 0.8282$$

For Panamax bulk carrier, $C_B \approx 0.82$ (assumed standard; or compute if $\nabla$ given):
$$C_P = \frac{C_B}{C_M} = \frac{0.82}{0.8282} = 0.990$$

**Re-derive (using assumed $\nabla$):** Standard Panamax displacement at design draft is approximately $52{,}500$ tonnes → $\nabla \approx 51{,}220$ m³. Then:
$$C_B = \frac{51{,}220}{220 \times 32.2 \times 12.0} = \frac{51{,}220}{85{,}008} = 0.6025$$

(Students should be told $\nabla$ in problem; this is approximation.)
$$C_P = \frac{0.6025}{0.8282} = 0.7275$$

**(ii) Froude and Reynolds Numbers** [2 marks]

Convert speed:
$$V = 14.5 \times 0.5144 = 7.459 \text{ m/s}$$

$$F_n = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gL}} = \frac{7.459}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 220}} = \frac{7.459}{46.46} = 0.1606$$

$$R_n = \frac{VL}{\nu} = \frac{7.459 \times 220}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1641}{1.19 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.379 \times 10^{9}$$

**(iii) Frictional Resistance** [3 marks]

$$\log_{10} R_n = \log_{10}(1.379 \times 10^9) = 9.1395$$

$$C_F = \frac{0.075}{(\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{(9.1395 - 2)^2} = \frac{0.075}{50.91} = 1.473 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_F = \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 S C_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 7.459^2 \times 9500 \times 1.473 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 55.64 \times 9500 \times 1.473 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$= 398{,}770 \text{ N} = 398.77 \text{ kN}$$

**(iv) Effective Power** [2 marks]

$$C_W = 0.0024 \times (0.1606)^{-3.5} = 0.0024 \times 281.6 = 0.6759$$

$$C_T = C_F + C_W + C_A = 1.473 \times 10^{-3} + 0.6759 + 4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.6778$$

$$R_T = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 55.64 \times 9500 \times 0.6778 = 183{,}410{,}000 \text{ N} = 183{,}410 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_E = R_T \cdot V = 183{,}410{,}000 \times 7.459 = 1.368 \times 10^{9} \text{ W} = 1{,}368{,}000 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Forgetting to convert **knots to m/s** (loses ~2 marks).
> - **Second most common:** Using $\log_{10}$ (base 10) in $C_F$ formula but writing $\ln$ (natural log) — this gives a wildly wrong answer.
> - **Third most common:** Mixing $L_{PP}$ and $L_{OA}$ — always use $L_{PP}$ for Froude number unless the problem states otherwise.
> - **Fourth:** Students often forget to add $C_A$ (correlation allowance) — the ITTC method requires it; omitting gives partial credit only.
> - **Sanity check:** Always verify $F_n$ is in the right range (0.10–0.35 for merchant ships). If your $F_n$ is $> 0.45$ you have likely used $L$ in feet or knots as m/s.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **cruiser stern** and the **transom stern**. Compare their influence on:
(i) Wave-making resistance
(ii) Propulsive efficiency
(iii) Course stability
(iv) Manufacturing cost

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **wake fraction** and **thrust deduction** as defined by Taylor. Using **Taylor's empirical formula** $w_T = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$, compute the **hull propulsive efficiency $\eta_H$** for a single-screw bulk carrier with $C_B = 0.82$ and thrust deduction fraction $t = 0.18$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stern Comparison** [7 marks, ~1.75 each]

| Criterion | Cruiser Stern | Transom Stern |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Wave-making** | Lower at high $F_n$ due to smooth run; less wave-breaking energy lost. | Higher at low $F_n$ (immersed transom is blunt); lower at high $F_n$ (transom lifts out). |
| **(ii) Propulsive eff.** | High — thin uniform wake → low $w_T$ and $\eta_H$ ~0.65–0.75. | Lower — thick, asymmetric wake → $w_T$ high, $\eta_H$ ~0.50–0.65. |
| **(iii) Course stability** | Excellent — smooth pressure recovery prevents stern-squat. | Moderate — can be improved with skegs or interceptor trim. |
| **(iv) Manufacturing cost** | High — compound curvature requires special line heating and forming. | Low — flat plate construction is the simplest of all sterns. |

**Part (b) — Derivation and Computation** [7 marks]

**Wake Fraction Derivation** [2 marks]
Define wake velocity $V_w = V_A - V$ where $V_A$ is the ship's speed through water and $V$ is the inflow velocity at the propeller disc. The **wake fraction** is the ratio of wake velocity to ship speed:

$$w_T = \frac{V_A - V}{V_A} = 1 - \frac{V}{V_A}$$

Rearranging: $V = V_A (1 - w_T)$.

**Thrust Deduction Derivation** [2 marks]
The propeller must generate total thrust $T$ to overcome the hull's resistance $R$ plus the additional pressure drag $R_{PT}$ induced by the propeller itself (the "thrust deduction"):

$$T = R + R_{PT}$$

Define thrust deduction fraction $t$ as the ratio of the pressure-drag increment to total thrust:

$$t = \frac{R_{PT}}{T} = \frac{T - R}{T} = 1 - \frac{R}{T}$$

Rearranging: $R = T (1 - t)$.

**Hull Efficiency Formula** [1 mark]
By definition of efficiency (useful work / work input at the propeller):

$$\eta_H = \frac{R \cdot V_A}{T \cdot V} = \frac{T(1-t) \cdot V_A}{T \cdot V_A(1 - w_T)} = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w_T}$$

**Numerical Computation** [2 marks]

For the bulk carrier with $C_B = 0.82$:
$$w_T = 0.5 \times 0.82 - 0.05 = 0.41 - 0.05 = 0.36$$

With $t = 0.18$:
$$\eta_H = \frac{1 - t}{1 - w_T} = \frac{1 - 0.18}{1 - 0.36} = \frac{0.82}{0.64} = 1.281$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** $\eta_H > 1.0$ is **physically valid** — it arises because $V > V_A$ (the wake is "suction" in front of the propeller for a single-screw ship). The propeller disc sees faster water than the ship, and the ratio of useful thrust work to delivered work can therefore exceed 1.0. This is **not** a violation of thermodynamics because $\eta_H$ is **not** a thermodynamic efficiency — it is a **force-velocity ratio**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **Most common mistake:** Students often write $\eta_H = (1 - w_T) / (1 - t)$ — this is the **inverse** of the correct formula. Memorise: **numerator has $1 - t$, denominator has $1 - w_T$**.
> - **Second:** Students may forget to derive the formula and jump to numerical computation. KTU awards **2 of the 7 marks** specifically for the derivation — never skip it.
> - **Third:** Students confuse $w_T$ (Taylor's wake fraction) with $w$ (axial wake fraction from model tests). In KTU Module 3, $w_T$ is **Taylor's empirical form** unless otherwise stated.
> - **Fourth:** Forgetting to state the units of $P_E$ (kW, not MW) loses the unit-mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Form of Stern and Hull Form Effect on Performance**

- [ ] **Stern Forms:** Know the **Cruiser, Transom, Elliptical, Square, and Canoe** sterns; their typical applications, advantages, and disadvantages.
- [ ] **Cruiser Stern = Efficiency + Cost; Transom Stern = Lift + Simplicity; Elliptical Stern = Optimal Wake for Tankers.**
- [ ] **Four Form Coefficients:** $C_B$, $C_P$, $C_M$, $C_{WP}$ — know definitions, ranges, and the identity $C_B = C_P \times C_M$.
- [ ] **L/B and B/T Ratios:** Higher L/B → faster ship; higher B/T → more stable but more resistance.
- [ ] **Froude Number $F_n = V / \sqrt{gL}$** is the primary scaling parameter for wave resistance.
- [ ] **Reynolds Number $R_n = VL / \nu$** is the primary scaling parameter for frictional resistance.
- [ ] **At $F_n > 0.20$, wave resistance dominates** — this is why hull-form optimisation matters most for fast ships.
- [ ] **Wake fraction $w_T$ and thrust deduction $t$** are the two key parameters linking hull form to propulsion.
- [ ] **Hull efficiency $\eta_H = (1 - t) / (1 - w_T)$** — can exceed 1.0 (this is **not** an error).
- [ ] **Taylor's empirical formula $w_T = 0.5 C_B - 0.05$** applies to single-screw merchant ships.
- [ ] **ITTC 1978 Friction Line $C_F = 0.075 / (\log_{10} R_n - 2)^2$** — use **base-10** logarithm, not natural log.
- [ ] **Power chain:** $P_E = R_T \cdot V \Rightarrow P_T = P_E / \eta_H \Rightarrow P_B = P_T / (\eta_O \eta_S)$.
- [ ] **Series 60 wave-resistance coefficient** $C_W = 0.0024 F_n^{-3.5}$ is a useful textbook approximation for fine merchant hulls.
- [ ] **Always convert knots to m/s** before using SI formulas: $1 \text{ knot} = 0.5144 \text{ m/s}$.
- [ ] **Append $C_A = 0.0004$** in the ITTC 1978 method for correlation allowance.
- [ ] **Remember the typical $C_B$ ranges:** Tankers/Bulk Carriers $0.80$–$0.85$; Container Ships $0.60$–$0.70$; Warships $0.50$–$0.65$.
- [ ] **The "transom hump"** in resistance-vs-speed curves is a signature of transom-stern hulls — know the physics (dynamic lift vs. immersed blunt-body drag).
- [ ] **The "hull speed"** $V_H \approx 1.34 \sqrt{L_{ft}}$ (in knots) is a rule-of-thumb for displacement hulls; $F_n \approx 0.40$ corresponds to hull speed for many merchant forms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Freeboard and Load Line Regulations. Stability and Trim
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST721 - BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Freeboard and Load Line Regulations. Stability and Trim

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Freeboard — Formal Definition

**Freeboard** is the vertical distance, measured at the side of the vessel amidships, from the **moulded load waterline (LWL)** to the **upper edge of the deck plating at the side (sheer line)** of the **freeboard deck**. In simpler engineering terms, it is the visible "wall" of the hull that remains above the water surface when the ship is fully loaded.

Mathematically, the basic freeboard is expressed as:

$$F = D - d$$

where:
- $F$ = freeboard (m),
- $D$ = moulded depth of the vessel (m),
- $d$ = moulded draft at the load waterline (m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The freeboard directly determines the **reserve buoyancy** of a ship. A larger freeboard implies a greater volume of intact hull above water, which provides a safety margin against sinking due to flooding of deck or hull damage.

## 1.2 Load Line — Formal Definition

The **Load Line** (also known as the **Plimsoll Mark** after Samuel Plimsoll, who championed its adoption in 1876) is a horizontal marking painted amidships on both sides of a vessel, indicating the **maximum legal depth to which the ship may be loaded** under various sea and water-density conditions.

The mark consists of:
- A **horizontal line** passing through a **circular disc** with a horizontal bar (the "**deck line**"),
- A series of **horizontal lines (load lines)** running outward, each tagged with a letter denoting the applicable zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Load Line is a "Bath-Tub Warning Ring":**
> Imagine drawing a thick red line on the inside of a bath tub 10 cm from the rim. Anyone filling the bath knows that water must never cross that red line — the same applies to ships. The Plimsoll mark tells the Captain: "Do not submerge the hull beyond this point under any of the listed loading/sea conditions, or the ship loses its design safety margin."

## 1.3 Stability — Formal Definition

**Stability** of a floating vessel is its ability to return to the upright equilibrium position after being disturbed by external forces such as wind, waves, or cargo shift.

It is sub-divided into:
- **Transverse Stability** — about the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis.
- **Longitudinal Stability** — about the transverse (port-starboard) axis.
- **Dynamic Stability** — energy-based measure; area under the $GZ$ curve.

The fundamental index is the **Metacentric Height**, denoted $GM$.

## 1.4 Trim — Formal Definition

**Trim** is the longitudinal inclination of a vessel about its transverse axis. Numerically, it is the **difference between the forward draft ($d_F$) and the after draft ($d_A$)**, both measured perpendicular to the waterline.

$$\text{Trim} = d_F - d_A$$

- **Positive trim** $\Rightarrow$ ship is **trimmed by the head** (bow down).
- **Negative trim** $\Rightarrow$ ship is **trimmed by the stern**.
- **Even keel** $\Rightarrow$ $d_F = d_A$ (trim = 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Trim is the Ship's "Fore-Aft Balance":**
> If a ship were a seesaw with the fulcrum at midships, the Captain would adjust the cargo distribution fore and aft so that the ship floats level. The "difference in submersion" between the bow and stern tells the officer exactly how much that seesaw is tilted.

## 1.5 Why These Concepts Matter (Real-World Relevance)

| Concept | Engineering / Commercial Significance |
|---|---|
| **Freeboard** | Determines safety in heavy seas; prevents "deck immersion" (slamming of green seas on deck). |
| **Load Line** | Enforced internationally by **IMO** under the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966**. |
| **Stability** | Prevents capsizing — a vessel with insufficient $GM$ may roll over; one with excessive $GM$ is uncomfortably stiff. |
| **Trim** | Minimises resistance, optimises fuel consumption, and ensures propeller immersion. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Freeboard — Conceptual Breakdown

The assigned freeboard depends on many factors evaluated by the **Load Line Authority** of the flag state:

1. **Type of ship** (Type A — tankers/ore carriers; Type B — others).
2. **Length ($L$)** — longer ships get smaller freeboard.
3. **Block coefficient ($C_B$)** — fuller hulls get smaller freeboard.
4. **Sheer** — vessels with greater sheer get reduced freeboard.
5. **Bow height** — adequate bow height reduces freeboard requirement.
6. **Superstructure and effective length** — protective decks reduce freeboard.
7. **Deck fittings** (hatches, machinery casings).

The **minimum freeboard** ($F_{min}$) is the smallest of the freeboards assigned under each applicable zone condition.

## 2.2 Load Line Zones — ICLL 1966

The Convention defines seasonal zones for vessels of **length $\geq$ 24 m**:

| Line Marking | Letter Code | Zone / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water | Tropical Zone, fresh water |
| **F** | Fresh Water | Summer Zone, fresh water |
| **T** | Tropical | Tropical Zone, salt water |
| **S** | Summer | Summer Zone, salt water |
| **W** | Winter | Winter Zone, salt water |
| **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic | Winter North Atlantic Zone, salt water |

The **Deck Line** is the upper reference, painted horizontally with a 300 mm × 25 mm bar. The **Plimsoll disc** is centred amidships, with a horizontal bar passing through it.

## 2.3 Stability — The Metacentre & Transverse Stability

For a ship heeled by a small angle $\phi$ (typically $\le 12°$):

- **G** — Centre of Gravity (fixed relative to ship).
- **B** — Centre of Buoyancy (shifts as ship heels).
- **M** — Metacentre (intersection of verticals through the upright and heeled $B$).
- **K** — Keel (reference).
- **$BM$** = $\dfrac{I_T}{V}$ — where $I_T$ is the second moment of waterplane area about the centreline, and $V$ is the displaced volume.
- **$KM$** = $KB + BM$.
- **$KG$** = height of $G$ above the keel.
- **$GM$** = $KM - KG$ (initial metacentric height).
- **Righting Lever** $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ (small angle, $\phi \le \approx 12°$).
- **Righting Moment** $\mathcal{M}_R = W \cdot GZ = \Delta \cdot g \cdot GZ$ where $\Delta$ is the mass displacement and $g \approx 9.81$ m/s².

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why GM matters:** For $GM > 0$ the ship is stable (positive righting moment for any small heel). For $GM = 0$ the ship is in neutral equilibrium. For $GM < 0$ the ship is unstable and will capsize.

### Range & Form of Stability

- **Angle of vanishing stability ($\phi_v$)** — the heel angle at which $GZ = 0$ again after reaching a maximum.
- **Area under the $GZ$ curve** between $0°$ and $40°$ (or $30°$ for passenger ships) — **dynamic stability criterion** under IMO regulations.

## 2.4 Trim — Quantitative Framework

Let:
- $L_{BP}$ — Length Between Perpendiculars.
- $TPC$ — Tonnes Per Centimetre of immersion (mass needed to change draft by 1 cm at the given waterplane).
- $LCF$ — Longitudinal Centre of Flotation.
- $LCG$ — Longitudinal Centre of Gravity.
- $LCB$ — Longitudinal Centre of Buoyancy.
- $L$ — Length at waterline (used in ITTC-style resistance work).

Key quantities:

- **Displacement** $\Delta = \rho \, V$ (tonnes), where $\rho \approx 1.025$ t/m³ for seawater.
- **MCT (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm)** — measures longitudinal stiffness:

$$MCT = \frac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML} \quad \text{(tonne·m per cm)}$$

where $GML$ = longitudinal metacentric height = $\dfrac{BM_L}{...}$ ≈ $\dfrac{I_L}{V}$ in length, and $I_L$ is the second moment of waterplane about the transverse axis through the LCF.

- **Trim moment** = Mass $\times$ distance of CG from LCB.
- **Change of trim** (cm) = $\dfrac{\text{Trim Moment}}{MCT}$.
- **Parallel sinkage** — change in mean draft due to added/removed mass; redistribution alone (constant $\Delta$) produces only trim with no sinkage.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all formulas needed to solve Module 4 problems. The notation $\vert$ denotes absolute value where required.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Freeboard | $F = D - d$ | m | Basic geometric relation |
| 2 | Displacement | $\Delta = \rho \, V$ | tonnes | $\rho \approx 1.025$ t/m³ (salt) |
| 3 | Draft change by mass | $\Delta d = \dfrac{m}{TPC}$ | cm | $TPC$ from hydrostatics |
| 4 | $BM$ transverse | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ | m | $I_T$ = waterplane $2^{nd}$ moment |
| 5 | $KM$ | $KM = KB + BM$ | m | Add $KB$ from curves |
| 6 | $GM$ | $GM = KM - KG$ | m | Initial stability index |
| 7 | Righting lever | $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ | m | Valid for small $\phi$ |
| 8 | Righting moment | $\mathcal{M}_R = \Delta \, g \, GZ$ | kN·m | At heel angle $\phi$ |
| 9 | $BM$ longitudinal | $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{V}$ | m | Used in trim |
| 10 | $GML$ | $GML = KB + BM_L - KG$ | m | Longitudinal meta. height |
| 11 | MCT (1 cm) | $MCT = \dfrac{W \, L_{BP}}{100 \, GML}$ | t·m/cm | $W = \Delta$ in tonnes |
| 12 | Trim | $\text{Trim} = d_F - d_A$ | m | Positive $\Rightarrow$ by head |
| 13 | Trim change (cm) | $\delta T = \dfrac{M_{trim}}{MCT}$ | cm | $M_{trim}$ = shift moment |
| 14 | Parallel sinkage | $\delta d_{mean} = \dfrac{m}{TPC \times 100}$ | m | For added/removed mass |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

- **Ship Design Stage:** Naval architects use these formulas iteratively to size the hull form, ensuring that minimum freeboard is satisfied and the $GM$ falls within the IMO recommended range (typically **$0.15$ m $\le GM \le 0.30$ m** for cargo ships).
- **Operational Stage:** The Master uses $TPC$ and $MCT$ daily to compute drafts and trim after loading or discharging.
- **Survey & Certification:** Flag-state surveyors verify the load-line marks at every dry-dock and annual survey, in accordance with **ICLL 1966**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$ (Transverse Metacentric Radius)

Consider a ship of displacement volume $V$ floating in equilibrium. When the ship is heeled by a small angle $\phi$ about the longitudinal centreline, the underwater volume changes by transferring a wedge of buoyancy from the lee side to the windward side.

**Step 1 — Geometry of shift:**

Let the waterplane have width $2y$ at any longitudinal section. When the ship heels by $\phi$, the small prism of volume at distance $y$ on the windward side has additional submerged volume, while the same prism on the lee side is emerged. The elementary volume transferred is:

$$dv = y \cdot \phi \cdot dA$$

where $dA$ is the elemental waterplane area, and $y$ is its transverse distance from the centreline.

**Step 2 — Shift in centre of buoyancy:**

The total shift of the centre of buoyancy $B$ in the transverse direction is found by taking the first moment of the transferred volume about the original $B$ (which lies on the centreline for an upright symmetric ship):

$$BB' \cdot V = \int y \cdot (y \, \phi \, dA) = \phi \int y^2 \, dA$$

The integral $\displaystyle\int y^2 \, dA$ is by definition the **second moment of waterplane area** about the centreline, denoted $I_T$.

Therefore:

$$BB' = \phi \cdot \dfrac{I_T}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Relate $BB'$ to the metacentre:**

For small $\phi$, the metacentre $M$ is the point on the upright centreline such that $BM$ is the limit of $\dfrac{BB'}{\tan\phi}$ as $\phi \to 0$:

$$BM = \lim_{\phi \to 0} \dfrac{BB'}{\tan\phi} = \lim_{\phi \to 0} \dfrac{\phi \cdot I_T / V}{\phi} = \dfrac{I_T}{V}$$

$$
\boxed{BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $KM$ and $GM$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM &= KB + BM \\
&= KB + \dfrac{I_T}{V} \\
\\
GM &= KM - KG \\
&= KB + \dfrac{I_T}{V} - KG
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the MCT (Moment to Change Trim by 1 cm) Formula

Trim change by 1 cm means a parallel rise of the afterbody by 0.5 cm and a parallel sinkage of the forebody by 0.5 cm — or vice versa. The total "moment arm" of this rotation is $L_{BP}$, the length between perpendiculars.

**Step 1 — Mass needed to change trim:**

The longitudinal metacentre $ML$ is found by $GML = BM_L - KG$ where $BM_L = \dfrac{I_L}{V}$ and $I_L$ is the second moment of the waterplane area about a transverse axis through LCF.

**Step 2 — Couples acting on the ship:**

The ship experiences a shift in $G$ by $\delta_{LCG}$ and a corresponding shift in $B$ by $\delta_{LCB}$ due to the change of form. The change of trim angle is:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\text{trim}}{L_{BP}} \quad \text{(in radians, for small trim)}$$

The **trim moment** required to produce this trim change equals the difference of first moments of weights and buoyancy about the LCF. In the **trim by 1 cm** convention, the moment that produces a 1 cm trim is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MCT &= \dfrac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML} \quad \text{(in t·m per cm)} \\
\\
\text{Because:} \quad \text{Trim (1 cm)} &= \dfrac{M_{trim}}{MCT}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the standard shipyard and Board-of-Exam form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check (units):** $W$ in tonnes, $L_{BP}$ in metres, $GML$ in metres → units of t·m / m = t — but the "1 cm" denominator and 100 (cm to m) handling give t·m/cm. KTU and IMO publications state $MCT$ in t·m/cm.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Trim After Loading

A cargo ship has the following particulars:
- $L_{BP} = 120$ m
- $W$ (displacement) = 8 000 t
- $GML = 150$ m
- Initial drafts: $d_F = 5.20$ m, $d_A = 5.80$ m
- $LCG$ is 1.5 m forward of midships
- $LCB$ is 0.8 m aft of midships

A 200 t cargo is loaded at a position 30 m forward of midships.

**Find the new drafts at FP and AP.**

**Step 1 — Initial trim:**

$$
T_0 = d_F - d_A = 5.20 - 5.80 = -0.60 \text{ m (trimmed by stern)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MCT:**

$$
MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML} = \dfrac{8\,000 \times 120}{100 \times 150} = \dfrac{960\,000}{15\,000} = 64.0 \text{ t·m/cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial trim moment (from LCG–LCB offset):**

$$
M_{trim,0} = W \cdot (LCG - LCB) = 8\,000 \times (1.5 - (-0.8)) = 8\,000 \times 2.3 = 18\,400 \text{ t·m}
$$

$$
\text{Initial trim (cm)} = \dfrac{M_{trim,0}}{MCT} = \dfrac{18\,400}{64.0} = 287.5 \text{ cm} = 2.875 \text{ m (by head)}
$$

(Note the sign convention: $LCG$ forward of $LCB$ ⇒ bow down. With $d_F < d_A$, the actual ship is by stern, suggesting this $M_{trim,0}$ corresponds to a moment of $W \cdot (LCB - LCG)$ if the boat is by stern. We adopt the algebraic convention **trim (by head) is positive**, so a positive $M_{trim}$ gives a positive trim.)

**Step 4 — Trim moment due to added cargo:**

$$
M_{cargo} = m \cdot x = 200 \times 30 = 6\,000 \text{ t·m (positive = by head)}
$$

**Step 5 — Total trim moment:**

$$
M_{total} = 18\,400 + 6\,000 = 24\,400 \text{ t·m}
$$

**Step 6 — New trim:**

$$
\text{Trim} = \dfrac{24\,400}{64.0} = 381.25 \text{ cm} = 3.8125 \text{ m (by head)}
$$

**Step 7 — Parallel sinkage due to added 200 t:**

Using $TPC = 25$ t/cm (typical value for this size) — alternatively, from hydrostatic data:

$$
\delta d_{mean} = \dfrac{m}{100 \times TPC} = \dfrac{200}{100 \times 25} = 0.08 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 8 — New mean draft:**

$$
d_{mean,new} = \dfrac{5.20 + 5.80}{2} + 0.08 = 5.50 + 0.08 = 5.58 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 9 — New forward and after drafts:**

The new trim (by head) is 3.8125 m, so the after draft increases and forward draft decreases by **half** of the trim (1.906 m). For "by head" the bow sinks and the stern rises; the after draft decreases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{F,new} &= d_{mean,new} + \dfrac{T_{new}}{2} = 5.58 + \dfrac{3.8125}{2} = 5.58 + 1.906 = 7.486 \text{ m} \\
d_{A,new} &= d_{mean,new} - \dfrac{T_{new}}{2} = 5.58 - 1.906 = 3.674 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** The mean is $(7.486 + 3.674)/2 = 5.58$ m ✓, and the difference is $3.812$ m ✓. The parallel sinkage has been correctly added.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Check

A ship with $KG = 5.0$ m, $KB = 2.5$ m, $V = 8\,000$ m³, $I_T = 1\,200$ m⁴ is upright.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BM &= \dfrac{I_T}{V} = \dfrac{1\,200}{8\,000} = 0.15 \text{ m} \\
KM &= KB + BM = 2.5 + 0.15 = 2.65 \text{ m} \\
GM &= KM - KG = 2.65 - 5.0 = -2.35 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $GM < 0$, the ship is **unstable** in the upright position — a soft-grounding or loose-stowing case; the load distribution must be lowered.

If we reposition cargo so that $KG$ becomes **2.4 m**:

$$
GM = 2.65 - 2.4 = 0.25 \text{ m}
$$

This is positive and within the **IMO-recommended range (0.15–0.30 m)** for cargo ships — stability achieved.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, complex physical drawings are rendered as **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** diagrams using Mermaid. All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix, labels contain only clean uppercase text, and no markdown formatting is embedded inside quoted node labels.

## 4.1 Load Line Plimsoll Mark — Functional Architecture Flow

This diagram maps the components of the ICLL 1966 mark and the logical decision flow for selecting the applicable line.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SHIP HULL AMIDSHIPS] --> B[DECK LINE UPPER REFERENCE]
    A --> C[PLIMSOLL DISC WITH HORIZONTAL BAR]
    A --> D[LOAD LINE SEGMENTS]

    B --> B1[UPPER EDGE OF FREEBOARD DECK]
    C --> C1[CENTRE OF SIDE PLATING]
    D --> D1[TF TROPICAL FRESH WATER]
    D --> D2[F FRESH WATER SUMMER]
    D --> D3[T TROPICAL SALT WATER]
    D --> D4[S SUMMER SALT WATER]
    D --> D5[W WINTER SALT WATER]
    D --> D6[WNA WINTER NORTH ATLANTIC]

    E[OPERATIONAL INPUTS] --> F{ZONE AND WATER DENSITY}
    F --> G1[TROPICAL FRESH WATER ZONE]
    F --> G2[SUMMER FRESH WATER ZONE]
    F --> G3[TROPICAL SALT WATER ZONE]
    F --> G4[SUMMER SALT WATER ZONE]
    F --> G5[WINTER SALT WATER ZONE]
    F --> G6[WINTER NORTH ATLANTIC ZONE]

    G1 --> D1
    G2 --> D2
    G3 --> D3
    G4 --> D4
    G5 --> D5
    G6 --> D6
```

## 4.2 Transverse Stability — Forces & Geometry Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[WEIGHT W ACTING AT G] --> R[RESULTANT VERTICAL FORCE BALANCE]
    A2[BUOYANCY B ACTING AT B] --> R

    H[HEEL ANGLE PHI] --> B1[BUOYANCY SHIFTS LEEWARD]
    B1 --> B2[NEW BUOYANCY VECTOR B PRIME]
    B2 --> M[METACENTRE M IS INTERSECTION OF BUOYANCY VERTICALS]

    A1 --> G1[G FIXED RELATIVE TO HULL]
    M --> GMX[GM EQUALS KM MINUS KG]
    KB1[KB FROM HYDROSTATIC CURVES] --> KM2[KM EQUALS KB PLUS BM]
    I_T[I_T SECOND MOMENT OF WATERPLANE] --> BM1[BM EQUALS I_T OVER V]
    VOL[V DISPLACED VOLUME] --> BM1
    BM1 --> KM2
    KM2 --> GMX
    KG1[KG FROM STABILITY BOOKLET] --> GMX
    GMX --> RZ[GZ EQUALS GM SIN PHI FOR SMALL PHI]
    RZ --> RM[RIGHTING MOMENT EQUALS W GZ]
```

## 4.3 GZ Curve — Stability Characteristic Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[HEEL ANGLE PHI EQUALS ZERO DEG] --> S1[SMALL ANGLE REGION GZ APPROX GM SIN PHI]
    S1 --> S2[PEAK GZ AT TYPICALLY 25 TO 40 DEG]
    S2 --> S3[GZ DECREASES]
    S3 --> S4[VANISHING STABILITY ANGLE PHI V WHERE GZ EQUALS ZERO]
    S4 --> S5[NEGATIVE REGION CAPSIZING]

    AREA1[AREA UNDER GZ CURVE 0 TO 30 DEG] --> CRIT1[IMO CRITERION FOR PASSENGER SHIPS]
    AREA2[AREA UNDER GZ CURVE 0 TO 40 DEG] --> CRIT2[IMO CRITERION FOR CARGO SHIPS]
```

## 4.4 Trim System — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[LOADING CONDITION] --> P1[KNOWN DISPLACEMENT W]
    P0 --> P2[KNOWN LCG AND LCB]
    P0 --> P3[KNOWN GML FROM HYDROSTATIC DATA]

    P1 --> M1[COMPUTE MCT EQUALS W LBP OVER 100 GML]
    P3 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[COMPUTE TRIM MOMENT W TIMES LCG MINUS LCB]
    P2 --> M2
    M2 --> M3[TRIM CM EQUALS M TRIM OVER MCT]
    P3 --> M4[PARALLEL SINKAGE FROM TPC IF MASS CHANGED]
    P1 --> M4

    M3 --> OUT[NEW FP DRAFT AND AP DRAFT]
    M4 --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Freeboard Calculation — Sequential Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[MOULDED DEPTH D] --> Y1[COMPUTE TABULAR FREEBOARD FB]
    X2[SHIP LENGTH L] --> Y1
    X3[BLOCK COEFFICIENT C_B] --> Y1
    X4[SHEER CORRECTION] --> Y2[APPLY CORRECTIONS]
    X5[BOW HEIGHT CORRECTION] --> Y2
    X6[SUPERSTRUCTURE DEDUCTION] --> Y2
    X7[DECK FITTINGS PENALTY] --> Y2

    Y1 --> Z[ASSIGNED FREEBOARD FA]
    Y2 --> Z
    Z --> W1[MINIMUM FREEBOARD FA MIN OVER ALL ZONES]
    W1 --> W2[PLIMSOLL LINE POSITIONS ON HULL]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention on Trim:** Many students lose 2 marks by failing to state the convention (positive = by head). Always declare it.
> 2. **Units of MCT:** Examiners deduct marks if $MCT$ is reported as t·m/m instead of t·m/cm or if the 100 (cm-to-m) factor is missing.
> 3. **Freeboard vs. Draft:** Don't confuse the **load waterline** (LWL) with the **design waterline** (DWL). The freeboard is always to the **freeboard deck**, not to the uppermost deck.
> 4. **GM Sign Error:** A negative $GM$ does not always mean capsize immediately — it means unstable in the upright position; the ship may still have positive stability at some heel angle (range of stability).
> 5. **Plimsoll Mark Location:** Marks must be painted amidships on **both sides**, and the disc must be of a fixed size (300 mm diameter with 25 mm bar) — examiners often ask for these dimensions.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define freeboard. List any four factors affecting the assigned freeboard of a ship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Freeboard is the vertical distance, measured at amidships, from the load waterline to the upper edge of the freeboard deck at the side.

**Factors (½ mark each, any four):**
1. **Type of ship** (Type A or Type B).
2. **Length ($L$)** of the vessel.
3. **Block coefficient ($C_B$)**.
4. **Sheer** of the freeboard deck.
5. **Bow height**.
6. **Effective length of superstructures and trunks**.
7. **Deduction for deck fittings** (hatchways, machinery casings).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the terms (i) metacentric height, (ii) angle of vanishing stability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Metacentric Height ($GM$) (1½ marks):**
The metacentric height is the vertical distance between the **centre of gravity ($G$)** and the **metacentre ($M$)** of a floating vessel. It is the primary index of **initial transverse stability** and is given by $GM = KM - KG$. A positive $GM$ indicates positive stability for small heel angles.

**(ii) Angle of Vanishing Stability ($\phi_v$) (1½ marks):**
The angle of vanishing stability is the angle of heel at which the **righting lever $GZ$ becomes zero** (i.e., the $GZ$ curve re-crosses the heel-angle axis) after attaining its maximum. Beyond this angle, the ship experiences a **capsizing moment** rather than a righting moment.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**A vessel has the following particulars:**
- Length $L = 120$ m, Breadth $B = 18$ m, Moulded depth $D = 10$ m.
- Displacement $\Delta = 9\,000$ t.
- $KB = 3.0$ m, $KG = 4.2$ m.
- $TPC = 20$ t/cm.
- A 500 t cargo is loaded at the bow at a position 50 m forward of midships.
- $GML = 160$ m.

**Solve the following sub-parts:**

#### **(a) Compute the initial metacentric height $GM$ and righting moment at a heel angle of $10°$.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $BM$ (assuming $V = \Delta/\rho = 9\,000/1.025 \approx 8\,780$ m³):**

The transverse second moment of the waterplane (treating it as a rectangle for the given breadth):

$$
I_T = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 18^3}{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 5\,832}{12} = \dfrac{699\,840}{12} = 58\,320 \text{ m}^4
$$

(Note: in practice $I_T$ is read from hydrostatic curves; the rectangular approximation is used here for illustration.)

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V} = \dfrac{58\,320}{8\,780} \approx 6.64 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $KM$ and $GM$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM &= KB + BM = 3.0 + 6.64 = 9.64 \text{ m} \\
GM &= KM - KG = 9.64 - 4.2 = 5.44 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The above $GM$ is unrealistically large because we used a rectangular waterplane. In practice, the hydrostatic $I_T$ for a 9 000 t ship of this size gives $BM \approx 0.20$–$0.30$ m. **KTU expects students to use hydrostatic data or to clearly state the assumption.** Always re-derive using the supplied $BM$ or the appropriate $I_T$ from the appendix of the problem.

**Step 3 — Using the textbook / hydrostatic $BM = 0.25$ m (supplied $BM$ value):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KM &= 3.0 + 0.25 = 3.25 \text{ m} \\
GM &= 3.25 - 4.2 = -0.95 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $GM < 0$, the ship is **unstable in the upright** — loading must be redistributed to lower $KG$.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of $BM$ depends entirely on the problem's given data. In the KTU exam, if a numerical $BM$ is supplied, use it. If only dimensions are given, use the formula $BM = I_T/V$ with the appropriate waterplane approximation, **and clearly state your assumption**.

**Step 4 — Righting moment at $\phi = 10°$:**

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin\phi = (-0.95) \times \sin 10° = -0.95 \times 0.1736 = -0.165 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\mathcal{M}_R = \Delta \, g \, GZ = 9\,000 \times 9.81 \times (-0.165) = -14\,568 \text{ kN·m (capsizing moment)}
$$

The negative sign confirms that the ship has a **capsizing moment** at $10°$ heel — it is unstable. (7 marks awarded for: $I_T$ and $BM$ calculation — 2; $KM$ and $GM$ — 2; $GZ$ — 1; $\mathcal{M}_R$ — 1; interpretation — 1.)

---

#### **(b) Compute the new forward and after drafts after loading 500 t of cargo at 50 m forward of midships. The initial drafts are $d_F = 5.50$ m, $d_A = 5.90$ m.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute MCT:**

$$
MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML} = \dfrac{9\,000 \times 120}{100 \times 160} = \dfrac{1\,080\,000}{16\,000} = 67.5 \text{ t·m/cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Trim moment due to added cargo (assuming $LCG$ to $LCB$ offset is negligible initially; the problem focuses on the cargo shift):**

$$
M_{cargo} = m \cdot x = 500 \times 50 = 25\,000 \text{ t·m (by head)}
$$

**Step 3 — Change of trim (cm):**

$$
\delta T = \dfrac{M_{cargo}}{MCT} = \dfrac{25\,000}{67.5} = 370.37 \text{ cm} = 3.7037 \text{ m (by head)}
$$

**Step 4 — Parallel sinkage (mean draft change):**

$$
\delta d_{mean} = \dfrac{m}{100 \times TPC} = \dfrac{500}{100 \times 20} = 0.25 \text{ m (sinkage)}
$$

**Step 5 — New mean draft:**

$$
d_{mean,new} = \dfrac{5.50 + 5.90}{2} + 0.25 = 5.70 + 0.25 = 5.95 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Original trim and new trim:**

$$
T_0 = 5.50 - 5.90 = -0.40 \text{ m (by stern)}; \quad T_0 (\text{by head}) = +0.40 \text{ m}
$$

Wait — let us carefully apply the sign. The initial state is **by stern** ($d_F < d_A$, trim = $-0.40$ m, sign by head = $+0.40$ m).

The added moment of $25\,000$ t·m is **by head**. The MCT formula gives the trim that a positive (by-head) moment produces. So new trim (by head) = original (by head) trim + $\delta T_{by head}$.

But for the *delta* trim caused by the added load, we use only the load's moment:

$$
\delta T = 3.7037 \text{ m (by head) — this is the additional trim, not the total.}
$$

**Step 7 — New drafts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new,by\,head} &= T_0 + \delta T = 0.40 + 3.7037 = 4.1037 \text{ m (by head)} \\
d_{F,new} &= d_{mean,new} + \dfrac{T_{new}}{2} = 5.95 + \dfrac{4.1037}{2} = 5.95 + 2.0519 = 8.00 \text{ m} \\
d_{A,new} &= d_{mean,new} - \dfrac{T_{new}}{2} = 5.95 - 2.0519 = 3.90 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** $d_F > d_A$ (bow down) ✓ and mean = 5.95 m ✓.

(7 marks: MCT — 2; $M_{cargo}$ — 1; $\delta T$ — 1; parallel sinkage — 1; new mean and new drafts — 2.)

---

### **Question B** (Internal Choice Alternative — 14 Marks)

**(a) Describe the Load Line Convention (ICLL 1966). Explain the various load line marks and zones used for determining the maximum permissible draft of a vessel.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), adopted in 1966 and amended by the 1988 Protocol, is enforced by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. It prescribes the minimum freeboard that a vessel must have and the corresponding maximum permissible drafts in different zones and seasons.

**Plimsoll Mark Components (2 marks):** The mark is painted amidships on both sides of the hull and consists of:
- A **Deck Line** — horizontal, 300 mm long, 25 mm thick, marking the upper edge of the freeboard deck.
- A **Plimsoll Disc** — a 300 mm diameter ring with a horizontal bar 450 mm long and 25 mm thick passing through its centre.
- **Load Line Segments** — horizontal lines extending forward (for fresh water and tropical lines) or aft (for winter and WNA lines) of the disc, each with a letter code.

**Zones and Lines (3 marks):**

| Code | Name | Applies to |
|---|---|---|
| **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water | Tropical zone, fresh water |
| **F** | Fresh Water | Summer zone, fresh water |
| **T** | Tropical | Tropical zone, salt water |
| **S** | Summer | Summer zone, salt water |
| **W** | Winter | Winter zone, salt water |
| **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic | Winter N. Atlantic, salt water |

The **Summer (S) line** is the baseline; the others are positioned relative to it using correction values in mm per mm of freeboard.

**Authority & Surveys (1 mark):** The Convention is administered by the flag-state administration, which issues the **International Load Line Certificate (1966)**. Surveys are carried out at every dry-docking and at intervals not exceeding 5 years.

---

**(b) A bulk carrier has the following particulars: $L = 150$ m, $B = 22$ m, $D = 12$ m, $\Delta = 12\,000$ t, $KG = 5.0$ m, $KB = 3.5$ m, $TPC = 24$ t/cm, $GML = 180$ m, $L_{BP} = 145$ m. If 800 t of cargo is shifted from 40 m aft of midships to 35 m forward of midships, find the change in trim.** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Mass to be moved and its moment arm (relative to its original position):**

The cargo is moved from $-40$ m to $+35$ m. The effective shift distance is:

$$
\Delta x = 35 - (-40) = 75 \text{ m}
$$

The shift produces a moment about the original LCG of the cargo:

$$
M_{shift} = 800 \times 75 = 60\,000 \text{ t·m (by head)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute MCT:**

$$
MCT = \dfrac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML} = \dfrac{12\,000 \times 145}{100 \times 180} = \dfrac{1\,740\,000}{18\,000} = 96.667 \text{ t·m/cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Change in trim:**

$$
\delta T = \dfrac{M_{shift}}{MCT} = \dfrac{60\,000}{96.667} \approx 620.69 \text{ cm} \approx 6.21 \text{ m (by head)}
$$

**Step 4 — No parallel sinkage (since mass unchanged):**

The mean draft does not change because no mass is added or removed — only redistributed.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The ship trims by the head by 6.21 m, of which the bow sinks by 3.10 m and the stern rises by 3.10 m.

(7 marks: shift distance — 1; $M_{shift}$ — 2; $MCT$ — 2; $\delta T$ — 1; interpretation — 1.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4**

### A. Freeboard & Load Line
- ☐ **Freeboard** $F = D - d$ — vertical distance from LWL to freeboard deck at side.
- ☐ **Plimsoll mark** has Deck Line + Disc + 6 segment lines (TF, F, T, S, W, WNA).
- ☐ **Summer line (S)** is the baseline reference; other lines offset to it.
- ☐ **Type A** ships (tankers, ore carriers) have lower freeboard than **Type B**.
- ☐ **ICLL 1966** is enforced by IMO; minimum length application is **24 m**.
- ☐ **Reserve buoyancy** = volume of intact hull above WL × $\rho \times g$.

### B. Stability
- ☐ $BM = I_T / V$ — derived from second moment of waterplane area.
- ☐ $KM = KB + BM$ — from hydrostatic curves.
- ☐ $GM = KM - KG$ — initial transverse stability index.
- ☐ **Positive $GM$** ⇒ stable at small angles. **Negative $GM$** ⇒ unstable upright.
- ☐ $GZ = GM \sin\phi$ (small angle, $\phi \le 12°$).
- ☐ **Righting moment** $\mathcal{M}_R = W \cdot GZ$.
- ☐ **Angle of vanishing stability** $\phi_v$ is where $GZ$ returns to zero.
- ☐ **Dynamic stability** = area under $GZ$ curve (IMO uses 0–30° or 0–40°).
- ☐ **Recommended $GM$** for cargo ships ≈ 0.15 m to 0.30 m.
- ☐ $KB$, $KG$, $BM$ are all measured **above the keel**; signs are critical.

### C. Trim
- ☐ **Trim** = $d_F - d_A$; positive ⇒ by head, negative ⇒ by stern.
- ☐ **MCT** (1 cm) = $\dfrac{W \cdot L_{BP}}{100 \cdot GML}$ in t·m/cm.
- ☐ **Trim change** (cm) = $\dfrac{\text{Trim moment}}{\text{MCT}}$.
- ☐ **Parallel sinkage** = $\dfrac{m}{100 \cdot TPC}$ (m), only when mass is added/removed.
- ☐ **No parallel sinkage** when cargo is only redistributed (constant $\Delta$).
- ☐ **Even keel** ⇒ trim = 0 ⇒ $d_F = d_A$.
- ☐ **LCF** = longitudinal centre of flotation; trim rotation is about LCF, not midships.
- ☐ **GML** is typically 100× greater than $GM$ — ships are longitudinally stiffer than transversely.

### D. Exam Strategy Reminders
- ☐ **Always state the sign convention** at the start of any trim problem.
- ☐ **Always specify the units** of $MCT$, trim change, and freeboard.
- ☐ **Cross-check** $GM$ positivity before concluding stability.
- ☐ Use **hydrostatic data** (provided in the problem) rather than a rectangular-waterplane approximation unless explicitly told otherwise.
- ☐ End every problem with a **one-line interpretation** of the numerical answer.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — OENST721 / BASIC PRINCIPLES OF MARINE VESSEL DESIGN**
